topics.title: Communication Model - Simplex
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Communication Model - Simplex # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Data Communication Model (Foundation) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)** > A **Data Communication Model** is a structured block-level abstraction that describes how digital/information-bearing signals travel from a **source** to a **destination** across a physical or logical medium, while accounting for the inevitable presence of **noise**. The universally accepted reference model of data communication (originally given by *Forouzan* and adopted in KTU modules) consists of **five fundamental functional blocks**: | # | Block | Function | |---|-------|----------| | 1 | **Source** | Originates the data/message (e.g., a person, computer, IoT sensor). | | 2 | **Transmitter** | Converts source data into transmittable signals (e.g., modem, encoder, modulator). | | 3 | **Transmission System** | Carries the signal (e.g., copper wire, optical fibre, RF channel). | | 4 | **Receiver** | Converts received signal back into usable data (e.g., demodulator, decoder). | | 5 | **Destination** | Final recipient of the data (e.g., a person, server, actuator). | > [!NOTE] > **Noise** is a sixth, passive block. It is *not* a designed device but a mathematical abstraction representing any unwanted interference (thermal, EMI, crosstalk, distortion) that corrupts the signal at any point of the transmission system. ## 1.2 Simplex Communication — The Unidirectional Link > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme)** > **Simplex Communication** is a mode of data communication in which the flow of information is **strictly unidirectional** — the signal travels from the sender to the receiver only. The receiver **cannot transmit back**, and the channel is dedicated to a single direction of traffic at all times. ### 🧠 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition) Imagine a **one-way road** connecting City A to City B. Cars can only travel **from A → B**. Even if a car in B wants to honk a reply, there is *no return lane*. Traffic (data) flows in **one fixed direction forever**. Another real-world analogy: a **radio FM broadcast station**. The radio station *only* transmits, your car radio *only* receives. You cannot speak back to the station through the same radio link. Similarly, a **television broadcast**, a **keyboard sending keystrokes to a PC**, and a **computer mouse sending cursor movements to the monitor** are all classic simplex channels. ## 1.3 Why Simplex Matters in KTU Examinations - It is the **simplest and most bandwidth-efficient** mode because the channel is reserved for a single direction. - It is **unreliable for two-way interactive conversations** (no ACK/NACK possible). - It is heavily used in **broadcast, telemetry, and sensor networks**. > [!TIP] > **Memorisation Tip:** "**S**implex = **S**ingle direction only. No return." # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Characterisation of Simplex The simplex mode can be analysed through five key properties: - **Direction of Information Flow:** Strictly **Source → Destination**. No reverse path exists in the channel. - **Number of Devices Involved:** Two functional devices, but only **one acts as a transmitter** at all times, the other as a receiver. (A device can be physically capable of both but is *logically restricted* to one role in this mode.) - **Channel Capacity Allocation:** **100%** of the channel bandwidth is dedicated to the single direction. There is no Time-Division or Frequency-Division splitting. - **Latency Profile:** Very low and deterministic, since there is no contention, no turnaround, and no arbitration. - **Error Recovery:** None within the channel itself. Any error correction must be performed by **upper-layer protocols** using a separate return channel (which converts the system into at least half-duplex). ## 2.2 Comparison Table — Simplex vs Half-Duplex vs Full-Duplex | Property | Simplex | Half-Duplex | Full-Duplex | |----------|---------|-------------|-------------| | Direction | One-way only | Both ways, but **one at a time** | Both ways **simultaneously** | | Return Channel | ❌ None | ✅ Same channel (shared in time) | ✅ Same or separate channels | | Bandwidth Use | 100 % in 1 direction | 100 % shared | 100 % in both directions | | Turnaround Time | 0 (no reversal) | Yes (T_turn) | 0 | | Typical Use | Radio/TV, Keyboard | Walkie-Talkie | Telephone, Ethernet | | Complexity | Lowest | Medium | Highest | ## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Simplex **Why is simplex used at all if it is one-way?** - **Cost:** A simplex channel needs only **one transmitter and one receiver pair** with a single physical medium. No duplexers, no echo cancellers. - **Spectrum Efficiency for Broadcast:** When the same data must reach **millions of receivers** (TV, radio, GPS), simplex is the *only* economically viable mode. - **Reliability in Noisy Industrial Settings:** Many sensor applications (e.g., a temperature sensor reporting to a PLC) only ever need to send data, never receive. Adding a return channel would just invite noise. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Key Metrics > [!NOTE] > Simplex is a *qualitative* topic and does not require heavy quantitative derivation, but the following fundamental data-communication equations govern its throughput and efficiency, and are commonly asked in 3-mark questions. | Symbol / Quantity | Formula | Description / Unit | |-------------------|---------|--------------------| | **Channel Capacity (Nyquist, Noiseless)** | $C = 2B \log_{2}(M)$ | Bits per second (bps); $B$ = bandwidth in Hz, $M$ = signal levels | | **Channel Capacity (Shannon, Noisy)** | $C = B \log_{2}(1 + S/N)$ | bps; $S/N$ = signal-to-noise ratio | | **Bit Rate** | $R = (1/T_b)$ | bps; $T_b$ = time duration of one bit in seconds | | **Baud Rate (Symbol Rate)** | $D = (1/T_s)$ | Symbols/sec; $T_s$ = symbol duration | | **Bits per Symbol** | $r = \log_{2}(M)$ | Dimensionless | | **Bit Rate from Baud** | $R = D \cdot r = D \cdot \log_{2}(M)$ | bps | | **Simplex Throughput** | $\eta_{simplex} = \dfrac{\text{Useful bits}}{\text{Total bits transmitted}} = 1$ | Ratio (100 %) — no ACK overhead | | **Full-Duplex Throughput** | $\eta_{full} = \dfrac{1}{1 + 2\alpha}$ | $\alpha$ = propagation-to-transmission ratio | | **Effective Simplex Delay** | $T_{total} = T_{tx} + T_{prop} + T_{proc}$ | Seconds | ### Engineering Utility - **Nyquist** is used when designing **clean wired simplex links** (RS-232 Tx line, optical sensors). - **Shannon** is used when **noise dominates** — for example, a simplex RF telemetry link in an industrial plant. - Simplex $\eta = 1$ is the reason broadcast engineering prefers it: there is **zero protocol overhead**. # 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation & Worked Logic ## 3.1 Derivation: Effective Data Rate of a Simplex Link Let us rigorously derive the **end-to-end data delivery time** in a pure simplex channel — a standard 7-mark analytical problem in KTU. ### Step 1 — Define the problem We transmit a file of size $F$ bits across a simplex link of bit rate $R$ bps, propagation speed $v$ m/s, and link length $d$ metres. ### Step 2 — Transmission (serialisation) time $$T_{tx} = \frac{F}{R}$$ This is the time for the transmitter to push all $F$ bits onto the medium. ### Step 3 — Propagation time $$T_{prop} = \frac{d}{v}$$ This is the time for the **first bit** to reach the receiver. ### Step 4 — Processing delay at receiver $$T_{proc} = \text{constant} \approx \frac{L_{hdr}}{R}$$ Where $L_{hdr}$ is the receiver-decoding overhead in bits. ### Step 5 — Total delivery time for the **last** bit $$T_{total} = T_{tx} + T_{prop} + T_{proc}$$ ### Step 6 — Effective Throughput (Goodput) $$G = \frac{F}{T_{total}} = \frac{F}{\dfrac{F}{R} + \dfrac{d}{v} + T_{proc}}$$ ### Step 7 — Limiting Case 1: Long file ($F \to \infty$) For a very long message, $T_{tx}$ dominates: $$\lim_{F \to \infty} G = R$$ This confirms that a simplex link achieves **100 % of its raw bit rate** for bulk data. ### Step 8 — Limiting Case 2: Short file ($F \to 0$) For a single-bit message, propagation dominates: $$\lim_{F \to 0} G = \frac{F}{\dfrac{d}{v} + T_{proc}} \to 0$$ The link becomes inefficient for tiny control packets — a structural disadvantage of simplex for interactive use. ## 3.2 Worked Example (KTU Style Numerical) > **Problem:** A simplex telemetry link operates at $R = 9600$ bps, $d = 1000$ km, $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Calculate (i) propagation time, (ii) transmission time of a 1 KB frame, (iii) total delivery time. **Solution:** **Step (i): Propagation time** $$T_{prop} = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{1000 \times 10^{3}}{2 \times 10^{8}} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 5 \text{ ms}$$ **Step (ii): Transmission time** $$F = 1 \text{ KB} = 8 \times 1024 = 8192 \text{ bits}$$ $$T_{tx} = \frac{F}{R} = \frac{8192}{9600} \approx 0.8533 \text{ s}$$ **Step (iii): Total delivery time (ignoring $T_{proc}$)** $$T_{total} = T_{tx} + T_{prop} = 0.8533 + 0.0050 = 0.8583 \text{ s}$$ > [!TIP] > **Observation:** Propagation delay (5 ms) is negligible compared to transmission time (853 ms). For long-distance satellite or deep-space simplex links, this comparison **inverts** — a frequent KTU trick question. ## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Choose Simplex | Application Scenario | Recommended Mode | Justification | |----------------------|------------------|----------------| | One-to-many broadcast (TV, FM) | **Simplex** | Single source, many receivers; no return traffic | | Point-to-point interactive voice (telephone) | Full-Duplex | Both users must talk simultaneously | | Industrial sensor → PLC (one-way) | **Simplex** | Sensor never needs to receive | | Walkie-Talkie military radio | Half-Duplex | Single channel, but "over" replies needed | | Keyboard → CPU | **Simplex** | Keyboard only sends scan codes | | Satellite TV downlink | **Simplex** | High bandwidth one-way distribution | | File Transfer (FTP) | Full-Duplex | ACK packets must flow back | | GPS satellites to receivers | **Simplex** | Receivers are passive listeners | # 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Universal Data Communication Model with Noise ```mermaid flowchart LR src[Source] tx[Transmitter] chan[(Transmission System)] noise{{Noise Source}} rx[Receiver] dst[Destination] src -- data --> tx tx -- modulated signal --> chan noise -. corrupts .-> chan chan -- received signal --> rx rx -- recovered data --> dst classDef dev fill:#E8F0FE,stroke:#1A73E8,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B3D91; classDef chanDev fill:#FEF7E0,stroke:#F9AB00,stroke-width:2px,color:#7A4F00; classDef noiseDev fill:#FCE8E6,stroke:#D93025,stroke-width:2px,color:#7A0E0E; class src,tx,rx,dst dev; class chan chanDev; class noise noiseDev; ``` > **Figure 1:** The 5-block data communication model with noise injection. Every KTU 14-mark question begins with this diagram. ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Simplex Mode ```mermaid flowchart LR devA[Device A
Transmitter Only] chanA[One-Way Channel] devB[Device B
Receiver Only] devA ==>> chanA ==>> devB devB -. no return path .x devA classDef tx fill:#D9EAD3,stroke:#274E13,stroke-width:2px,color:#274E13; classDef rx fill:#CFE2F3,stroke:#0B5394,stroke-width:2px,color:#0B5394; classDef medium fill:#FFF2CC,stroke:#7F6000,stroke-width:2px,color:#7F6000; class devA tx; class devB rx; class chanA medium; ``` > **Figure 2:** Simplex functional architecture. The dotted line with an **X** explicitly visualises the *absence* of a return path. This single diagram has been asked in KTU University Exams. ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Comparative View ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Simp["SIMPLEX"] SA[Tx] -->> SM[One-Way Medium] -->> SB[Rx] end subgraph HD["HALF-DUPLEX"] HA[Tx OR Rx] <-->> HM[Shared Medium] <-->> HB[Rx OR Tx] end subgraph FD["FULL-DUPLEX"] FA[Tx and Rx] <-->> FM[Two-Way Medium] <-->> FB[Rx and Tx] end classDef simpStyle fill:#EAD1DC,stroke:#7B2D8E,stroke-width:1px,color:#3D1B4B; classDef hdStyle fill:#D9EAD3,stroke:#274E13,stroke-width:1px,color:#274E13; classDef fdStyle fill:#CFE2F3,stroke:#0B5394,stroke-width:1px,color:#0B5394; class SA,SB,SM simpStyle; class HA,HB,HM hdStyle; class FA,FB,FM fdStyle; ``` > **Figure 3:** Comparative functional architecture of the three communication modes. Arrows strictly indicate allowed directions of information flow. # 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define Simplex communication. Give two real-world examples.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Simplex communication is a unidirectional mode of data transfer in which the signal flows from the sender to the receiver only, with **no provision for a return path** on the same channel. The entire channel bandwidth is dedicated to the single direction of traffic. **Examples:** 1. **FM Radio / TV broadcast** — the station transmits, listeners only receive. 2. **Keyboard to CPU** — keystrokes are sent to the computer, but no data flows back through the keyboard cable. *(1 mark – definition, 1 mark – characteristic, 1 mark – two examples)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between Simplex and Full-Duplex communication.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Basis | Simplex | Full-Duplex | |-------|---------|-------------| | Direction | Unidirectional | Bidirectional simultaneous | | Return path | Absent | Present | | Channel use | 100 % one way | 100 % both ways | | Example | TV broadcast | Telephone call | *(1 mark per correct contrasting point × 3)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Draw and explain the block diagram of a generalised data communication system. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With suitable diagrams, explain **Simplex**, **Half-Duplex** and **Full-Duplex** modes of communication. Give two examples each. **(7 marks)** #### 🔹 Model Solution for Q-A (a) — Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks) 1. **Source (1 mark):** Origin of data. Example: a person's voice, a sensor. 2. **Transmitter (1 mark):** Converts source data into a form suitable for the medium. Example: microphone + modulator. 3. **Transmission System (1 mark):** Carries the signal. Example: twisted pair, optical fibre, free-space RF. 4. **Receiver (1 mark):** Recovers the original data from the incoming signal. Example: demodulator, speaker. 5. **Destination (1 mark):** Final recipient. Example: a listener, a display. 6. **Noise (1 mark):** External unwanted interference added at any point; represented by a separate block with arrows pointing into the transmission system. 7. **Neat labelled block diagram (1 mark):** As shown in *Figure 1* of SECTION 4. #### 🔹 Model Solution for Q-A (b) — Three Modes Explained (7 marks) - **Simplex (2.5 marks):** One-way, no return. Use *Figure 2* from SECTION 4. Examples: TV broadcast, keyboard. - **Half-Duplex (2.5 marks):** Both stations can transmit, but **not simultaneously**. Channel is shared in time. Use the *Figure 3* HD branch. Example: Walkie-Talkie, CB radio. - **Full-Duplex (2 marks):** Both directions **simultaneously**. Two physical channels or echo-cancellation. Use the *Figure 3* FD branch. Example: Telephone, modern Ethernet switch link. ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** A simplex telemetry channel uses a bandwidth of $B = 4$ kHz and a signal with $M = 16$ discrete levels. Calculate (i) the maximum theoretical bit rate using Nyquist criterion, and (ii) the symbol (baud) rate. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Define **Simplex communication**. State **four** of its key characteristics and explain why it is preferred for **broadcast applications**. **(7 marks)** #### 🔹 Model Solution for Q-B (a) — Numerical (7 marks) **Step 1 — Identify the given data (1 mark):** $B = 4 \text{ kHz} = 4000 \text{ Hz}$, $M = 16$ levels. **Step 2 — Compute $\log_2(M)$ (1 mark):** $\log_{2}(16) = 4 \text{ bits/symbol}$ **Step 3 — Apply Nyquist formula for maximum bit rate (3 marks):** $$C = 2 B \log_{2}(M)$$ $$C = 2 \times 4000 \times 4 = 32000 \text{ bps} = 32 \text{ kbps}$$ **Step 4 — Compute baud rate (2 marks):** $$D = 2B = 8000 \text{ symbols/sec} = 8 \text{ kBaud}$$ (Or equivalently $D = R/r = 32000/4 = 8000$ symbols/s.) #### 🔹 Model Solution for Q-B (b) — Theory (7 marks) 1. **Definition (2 marks):** Simplex is unidirectional data flow; the channel is dedicated from sender to receiver; no reverse path. 2. **Characteristic 1 (1 mark):** Single dedicated direction. 3. **Characteristic 2 (1 mark):** Maximum bandwidth utilisation (100 %). 4. **Characteristic 3 (1 mark):** Cannot support ACK/NACK on the same channel. 5. **Characteristic 4 (1 mark):** Lowest hardware and protocol complexity. **Why preferred for broadcast (Bonus in answer, 1 mark):** One source can reach millions of receivers economically; no need for arbitration, contention, or duplexers; receivers are passive, cheap, and battery-friendly. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Simplex Questions** > 1. **Drawing the block diagram WITHOUT a noise block** — KTU examiners deduct **1–2 marks** for omitting noise, as it is part of the prescribed model. > 2. **Confusing Simplex with Half-Duplex** — stating "both can send and receive" for simplex will fetch **0 marks** for the mode identification. > 3. **Forgetting to state examples** — a 7-mark question on modes explicitly asks for *two examples each*; skipping them costs **1–2 marks**. > 4. **Using `|` in markdown math tables for Nyquist absolute-value-like expressions** — always use `\vert` to avoid KTU answer-sheet rendering errors (this is also why we use `\log_{2}` instead of raw `log2`). > 5. **Not writing units in numericals** — `32000` without `bps` loses 0.5 marks; `5` without `ms` loses 0.5 marks. --- ## 5.4 🧠 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - 🔹 **Data Communication Model:** 5 blocks — **Source, Transmitter, Transmission System, Receiver, Destination** + **Noise**. - 🔹 **Simplex:** **Unidirectional only.** No return path. Channel is 100 % utilised in one direction. - 🔹 **Half-Duplex:** Bidirectional but **one direction at a time** (time-shared). - 🔹 **Full-Duplex:** Bidirectional **simultaneously** (two channels or echo cancellation). - 🔹 **Simplex examples:** TV, FM radio, keyboard, mouse, GPS downlink, telemetry sensors. - 🔹 **Half-Duplex examples:** Walkie-Talkie, CB radio. - 🔹 **Full-Duplex examples:** Telephone, full-duplex Ethernet, mobile voice call. - 🔹 **Nyquist Capacity:** $C = 2B \log_{2}(M)$ bps — used for noiseless wired simplex. - 🔹 **Shannon Capacity:** $C = B \log_{2}(1 + S/N)$ bps — used for noisy wireless simplex. - 🔹 **Bit Rate vs Baud:** $R = D \times \log_{2}(M)$. - 🔹 **Total delay:** $T_{total} = T_{tx} + T_{prop} + T_{proc}$. - 🔹 **Throughput efficiency:** Simplex = 1 (best for one-way bulk); full-duplex = $\frac{1}{1+2\alpha}$. - 🔹 **Exam mantra:** "**S**implex = **S**ingle direction, **N**o return." - 🔹 **Always** include a noise block in any data-communication block diagram. - 🔹 **Always** mention two real-world examples for every mode asked. - 🔹 **Always** use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` inside markdown tables.

topics.title: Half Duplex
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Half Duplex

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Half Duplex Communication

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Half Duplex (HDX) Communication Mode:** A bidirectional data communication mode in which information (data signals) can be transmitted in **both directions** between two communicating stations, but the transmission is restricted to **one direction at a time**. The entire channel capacity is shared alternately between the two stations, meaning Station A can transmit while Station B receives, and vice-versa, but simultaneous two-way transmission is **not permitted** by the system protocol.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework (aligned with Stallings' *Data and Computer Communications* terminology), Half Duplex is formally classified as one of the three primary **transmission modes** of a data link, alongside **Simplex** and **Full Duplex**. The term "half" mathematically reflects the fact that the channel's effective utilization is half of what a Full Duplex system could theoretically achieve under identical bandwidth conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Board Terminology:** Examiners expect students to explicitly mention that Half Duplex systems use a **single physical channel** (or a shared pair of frequencies/wires) with **time-division-based direction reversal**, not two independent channels.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **narrow one-lane wooden bridge** connecting two villages across a river. The bridge is wide enough for exactly **one car at a time**. A car entering from Village A can cross to Village B, but while it is on the bridge, no car from Village B can enter from the opposite end. Once the first car completely crosses, the second car from Village B may begin its journey.

This bridge is your **Half Duplex channel**.

*   **Village A & B** $\rightarrow$ The two Data Terminal Equipment (DTE) stations.
*   **The bridge** $\rightarrow$ The shared transmission medium (copper wire, RF frequency, optical fiber strand).
*   **Direction rule** $\rightarrow$ Either A $\rightarrow$ B OR B $\rightarrow$ A at any given instant, **never both simultaneously**.

A more relatable modern example is a **walkie-talkie** or **CB (Citizens Band) radio**. You press the "Push-To-Talk" (PTT) button to speak, and everyone else on the channel can only listen. When you release the button, someone else can press theirs to reply. The radio channel is shared, and direction is controlled by user action (a simple handshake protocol).

### Standard Metrics Associated With Half Duplex

> [!IMPORTANT]
> * **Effective Channel Capacity:** **50%** of the full theoretical duplex capacity (one direction at a time).
> * **Switchover Latency:** A small but measurable delay (typically **milliseconds**) required to reverse the transmission direction.
> * **Protocol Overhead:** Requires control signals such as **RTS (Request To Send)** and **CTS (Clear To Send)** to manage direction switching.
> * **Common Implementations:** **Walkie-talkies, CB radios, half-duplex Ethernet (10BASE5/10BASE2 with coax), certain satellite uplink/downlink pairs, and RS-485 bus systems.**

---

## 2. Operating Characteristics & Classification

Half Duplex is a hybrid of Simplex and Full Duplex modes. To fully appreciate its behavior, it must be compared with the other two modes:

| Transmission Mode | Direction Capability | Simultaneous? | Channel Usage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Simplex** | One direction only (e.g., A $\rightarrow$ B) | No | Unidirectional |
| **Half Duplex** | Both directions (A $\rightarrow$ B or B $\rightarrow$ A) | No (one at a time) | **Bidirectional, time-shared** |
| **Full Duplex (Duplex)** | Both directions (A $\leftrightarrow$ B simultaneously) | **Yes** | Two independent channels |

Half Duplex is essentially a **time-multiplexed** version of Full Duplex. If the Full Duplex throughput is $T_{FD}$, then the maximum theoretical Half Duplex throughput is $T_{HD} \le \frac{T_{FD}}{2}$ due to the fact that only one direction transmits at any time.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 3. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 3.1 Operational Mechanics — How Half Duplex Actually Works

The half-duplex mode can be understood by dissecting its communication cycle into atomic steps. Let the two stations be denoted as **Station A (Primary)** and **Station B (Secondary)**.

* **Step 1 — Channel Sensing / Carrier Detection:**
  Before any station transmits, it must verify that the shared medium is **idle**. This is the cornerstone of half-duplex discipline. If Station B is currently transmitting, Station A must **wait** and sense the channel until the carrier signal drops. This mechanism is called **CSMA (Carrier Sense Multiple Access)** in data link layer protocols.

* **Step 2 — Direction Request (Optional RTS/CTS Handshake):**
  Many half-duplex systems (notably IEEE 802.11 Wi-Fi and hardware flow-controlled serial links) employ a formal handshake. Station A sends an **RTS (Request To Send)** frame. If Station B is ready and the channel is free, it replies with a **CTS (Clear To Send)**. This guarantees that both ends agree on who is about to transmit.

* **Step 3 — Unidirectional Data Flow:**
  The agreed-upon transmitter (say A) sends a **continuous stream of bits** in one direction. The receiver (B) accepts these bits and may generate **acknowledgment frames (ACKs)** later, but only **after** the current transmission block ends. The full bandwidth of the medium is dedicated to this one direction for the duration of the frame/burst.

* **Step 4 — Direction Turnaround (Switchover):**
  When Station A finishes its transmission, a **propagation delay** and **protocol turnaround time** elapse. During this brief window, the channel is technically idle and direction is "flipped". This is called the **turnaround time** $\tau_{turn}$.

* **Step 5 — Reverse Transmission:**
  Station B can now transmit using the same physical medium, repeating the cycle from Step 3.

* **Why It Works (The Core 'Why'):**
  The shared medium has only **one information pathway**. If two stations tried to transmit simultaneously, their electromagnetic (or optical) signals would **collide** at the physical layer, producing garbled, unintelligible interference. Half Duplex prevents this collision by **temporal segregation** of directions — only one transmitter is ever active.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept — "Why not just use Full Duplex everywhere?"**
> Full Duplex requires **two independent physical channels** (e.g., two wire pairs, two frequency bands, or two fiber strands). Half Duplex is chosen when the deployment cost of a second channel is prohibitive, such as in legacy Ethernet coax (10BASE2/10BASE5), walkie-talkies, or satellite transponder pairs where frequency spectrum is scarce and expensive.

### 3.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the core equations and timing parameters that examiners test for Half Duplex systems. Memorize the symbols, units, and boundary conditions.

| # | Parameter / Concept | Formula / Expression | Unit | Notes & Boundary Conditions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Maximum Half Duplex Throughput | $T_{HD}^{max} = \dfrac{C}{2}$ | **bits/second (bps)** | $C$ is the full-duplex channel capacity. Achievable only with zero turn-around loss. |
| 2 | Effective Half Duplex Throughput | $T_{HD}^{eff} = \dfrac{L \cdot f_{frame}}{T_{frame} + T_{turn}}$ | **frames/second** | $L$ = bits per frame, $f_{frame}$ = payload efficiency, $T_{frame}$ = transmission time, $T_{turn}$ = turn-around time. |
| 3 | Channel Utilization (Efficiency) | $\eta = \dfrac{T_{frame}}{T_{frame} + T_{turn}}$ | **dimensionless (0 to 1)** | Often expressed as a percentage. $\eta \to 1$ as $T_{frame} \gg T_{turn}$. |
| 4 | Turn-around Time (Propagation related) | $T_{turn} \ge 2 \cdot t_{prop}$ | **seconds** | $t_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v}$ where $d$ is link length, $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in copper. |
| 5 | Propagation Delay | $t_{prop} = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | **seconds** | $v$ = signal propagation velocity in the medium. |
| 6 | Transmission Time for a Frame | $T_{frame} = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | **seconds** | $L$ = frame length in bits, $R$ = channel bit rate in bps. |
| 7 | Total Cycle Time (A $\rightarrow$ B then B $\rightarrow$ A) | $T_{cycle} = T_{AB} + T_{turn} + T_{BA} + T_{turn}$ | **seconds** | Sum of both directional transmissions plus both turnarounds. |
| 8 | Bidirectional Throughput | $T_{bi} = \dfrac{2L}{T_{cycle}}$ | **bits/second** | Effective end-to-end data rate considering turnarounds. |
| 9 | Wasted Bandwidth Ratio | $W_{loss} = 1 - \eta = \dfrac{T_{turn}}{T_{frame} + T_{turn}}$ | **dimensionless** | Represents the fraction of time the channel is "silent" during switching. |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Rendering Note for Markdown Tables:** In the table above, the fraction $\dfrac{1}{2}$ and absolute value notations use `\vert` and `\mid` style constructs, **never** the bare vertical pipe `|` character inside the math, to prevent breaking the markdown table cell boundary.

### 3.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Half Duplex is **not an obsolete technology**. It remains critically important in several modern engineering domains:

* **Satellite Communications:** A satellite transponder has a limited **bandwidth** and a single antenna often cannot transmit and receive on the same frequency simultaneously without expensive filters. Therefore, many satellite links (e.g., VSAT networks, early IoT satellite backhaul) operate in half-duplex mode using TDMA (Time Division Multiple Access) to share the transponder.
* **Industrial Bus Systems (RS-485):** Factory automation networks use RS-485, a half-duplex differential signaling standard, because it allows **multiple devices** (up to 32 drivers) to share a single twisted pair, drastically reducing wiring cost.
* **Two-Way Radio (PMR/LMR):** Public safety, military, and amateur radio systems universally use half duplex to conserve scarce RF spectrum.
* **Legacy Ethernet (10BASE2, 10BASE5):** The original Ethernet over coaxial cable was half duplex. It relied on **CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection)** to arbitrate the shared coax bus.
* **Walkie-Talkies & Marine VHF Radio:** Ubiquitous in aviation, marine, and emergency services for decades due to spectrum scarcity and cost.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 4. Step-by-Step Derivation & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** KTU frequently tests the derivation of **Effective Throughput** and **Channel Utilization** for a Half Duplex link. The model solution below is the canonical approach that earns full marks in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

### 4.1 Derivation: Effective Throughput of a Half Duplex Link

**Problem Setup (Standard KTU Style):**
Consider a half-duplex point-to-point link between Station A and Station B. The link has a bit rate of $R$ bps, a one-way propagation delay of $t_p$ seconds, and a fixed turn-around time of $T_{turn}$ seconds. The data to be exchanged consists of frames of $L$ bits each, with negligible ACK size. Determine the effective throughput when Station A sends one frame to Station B and Station B sends one frame back to Station A (a complete bidirectional cycle).

---

**Step 1 — Define the Transmission Time of a Single Frame:**

The time required to push all $L$ bits of one frame onto the physical medium at rate $R$ is given by the fundamental relation:

$$T_{frame} = \frac{L}{R}$$

This is the "active transmission" window during which bits are leaving the transmitter.

---

**Step 2 — Define the Total Cycle Time $T_{cycle}$:**

A complete bidirectional exchange consists of four sequential time intervals:

1.  Station A transmits its frame to B: $T_{frame}$ seconds.
2.  Turn-around delay (direction switch A $\rightarrow$ B): $T_{turn}$ seconds.
3.  Station B transmits its frame to A: $T_{frame}$ seconds.
4.  Turn-around delay (direction switch B $\rightarrow$ A): $T_{turn}$ seconds.

Adding these intervals:

$$T_{cycle} = T_{frame} + T_{turn} + T_{frame} + T_{turn}$$

Grouping like terms:

$$T_{cycle} = 2 T_{frame} + 2 T_{turn}$$

---

**Step 3 — Calculate the Total Useful Data Delivered per Cycle:**

In one complete cycle, the amount of *application-layer useful data* transferred is $2L$ bits (one $L$-bit frame in each direction). Note that the turn-around intervals deliver **zero** useful bits.

$$\text{Useful Data per Cycle} = 2L \text{ bits}$$

---

**Step 4 — Compute the Effective Throughput $T_{eff}$:**

Throughput, by definition, is the amount of useful data delivered per unit of elapsed real time. We divide the useful bits by the total cycle time:

$$T_{eff} = \frac{\text{Useful Data per Cycle}}{T_{cycle}}$$

Substituting the expressions from Steps 2 and 3:

$$T_{eff} = \frac{2L}{2 T_{frame} + 2 T_{turn}}$$

Factoring out 2 from the denominator:

$$T_{eff} = \frac{2L}{2 \left( T_{frame} + T_{turn} \right)}$$

The 2's cancel cleanly:

$$T_{eff} = \frac{L}{T_{frame} + T_{turn}}$$

---

**Step 5 — Substitute $T_{frame} = L/R$ to Express Throughput in terms of $R$:**

$$T_{eff} = \frac{L}{\frac{L}{R} + T_{turn}}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $R$ to clear the fraction:

$$T_{eff} = \frac{L \cdot R}{L + R \cdot T_{turn}}$$

This is the **final, board-ready expression** for the effective throughput of a half-duplex link in bits per second.

---

**Step 6 — Define the Channel Utilization $\eta$:**

The utilization is the fraction of the total cycle time spent in *actual data transmission* versus the *entire cycle*. From Step 2, the active transmission time is $2 T_{frame}$ and the total cycle is $2 T_{frame} + 2 T_{turn}$:

$$\eta = \frac{2 T_{frame}}{2 T_{frame} + 2 T_{turn}} = \frac{T_{frame}}{T_{frame} + T_{turn}}$$

Substituting $T_{frame} = L/R$:

$$\eta = \frac{\frac{L}{R}}{\frac{L}{R} + T_{turn}} = \frac{L}{L + R \cdot T_{turn}}$$

**Boundary Condition Checks:**

* As $T_{turn} \to 0$ (instantaneous switching): $\eta \to 1$ (100% utilization, theoretical maximum).
* As $L \to \infty$ (very large frames): $\eta \to 1$ (frame transmission dominates switching overhead).
* As $L \to 0$ (tiny frames): $\eta \to 0$ (channel is mostly idle during switching — a disastrous design).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** This is precisely why half-duplex satellite and radio systems prefer **large burst transmissions** rather than many small packets — to amortize the $T_{turn}$ penalty.

---

### 4.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Board Question Style)

**Given:**
* Link bit rate $R = 64$ kbps $= 64{,}000$ bps.
* One-way propagation delay $t_p = 20$ ms.
* Turn-around time $T_{turn} = 50$ ms.
* Frame size $L = 1600$ bits.

**Find:** (a) Transmission time, (b) Total cycle time, (c) Effective throughput, (d) Channel utilization.

---

**Solution (a) — Transmission Time:**

$$T_{frame} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{1600 \text{ bits}}{64{,}000 \text{ bps}}$$

$$T_{frame} = 0.025 \text{ s} = 25 \text{ ms}$$

---

**Solution (b) — Total Cycle Time:**

$$T_{cycle} = 2 T_{frame} + 2 T_{turn}$$

$$T_{cycle} = 2(25) + 2(50) = 50 + 100$$

$$T_{cycle} = 150 \text{ ms}$$

---

**Solution (c) — Effective Throughput:**

Using the derived formula:

$$T_{eff} = \frac{L}{T_{frame} + T_{turn}} = \frac{1600}{0.025 + 0.050} = \frac{1600}{0.075}$$

$$T_{eff} = 21{,}333.33 \text{ bps} \approx 21.33 \text{ kbps}$$

**Sanity check:** This is much less than the raw 64 kbps channel rate, reflecting the heavy penalty of the 50 ms turn-around time on a relatively short 1600-bit frame.

---

**Solution (d) — Channel Utilization:**

$$\eta = \frac{T_{frame}}{T_{frame} + T_{turn}} = \frac{25}{25 + 50} = \frac{25}{75} = 0.3333$$

$$\eta = 33.33\%$$

**Interpretation:** Only one-third of the channel's clock time is used for actual data; the remaining two-thirds is wasted in turn-around delays. A larger frame size would dramatically improve this.

---

### 4.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Simplex vs Half Duplex vs Full Duplex

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following decision matrix is a high-frequency favorite in KTU 2-mark and short-answer questions.

| Criterion | Simplex | Half Duplex | Full Duplex |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Direction of Flow** | One-way only | Two-way, **one at a time** | Two-way, **simultaneous** |
| **Channel Count Required** | 1 channel | 1 shared channel | 2 independent channels |
| **Hardware Complexity** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest |
| **Bandwidth Utilization** | **50%** (only one direction used) | Up to **100%** (time-shared) | **100%** (simultaneous) |
| **Collision Risk** | None | High (requires CSMA/CD) | None (channels separated) |
| **Example** | TV broadcast, keyboard $\rightarrow$ CPU | Walkie-talkie, RS-485 bus, 10BASE2 Ethernet | Telephone network, full-duplex Ethernet switches |
| **Use of RTS/CTS** | Not needed | Often used | Not typically needed |
| **Acknowledgment Latency** | Cannot ACK on same channel | ACK sent in reverse phase | Instantaneous ACK on reverse channel |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 5. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 5.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Half Duplex Communication Cycle

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional state machine** of a half-duplex link. It does not attempt a physical schematic of the medium, but it precisely captures the **logical flow** of direction control — which is what KTU examiners reward marks for.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA([Station A])
    nodeB([Station B])
    nodeIdle((Channel IDLE))
    nodeSenseA{A Senses Channel?}
    nodeSenseB{B Senses Channel?}
    nodeTxA[/A Transmits to B/]
    nodeTxB[/B Transmits to A/]
    nodeTurn1{{Turnaround T_turn}}
    nodeTurn2{{Turnaround T_turn}}
    nodeDone([Cycle Complete])

    nodeA --> nodeSenseA
    nodeSenseA -- Idle --> nodeTxA
    nodeSenseA -- Busy --> nodeIdle
    nodeTxA --> nodeTurn1
    nodeTurn1 --> nodeSenseB
    nodeSenseB -- Idle --> nodeTxB
    nodeSenseB -- Busy --> nodeIdle
    nodeTxB --> nodeTurn2
    nodeTurn2 --> nodeDone
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

* `nodeA` and `nodeB` are the two communicating stations.
* The **diamond decision nodes** (`nodeSenseA`, `nodeSenseB`) represent the **CSMA-style channel sensing** logic.
* The **parallelogram nodes** (`nodeTxA`, `nodeTxB`) represent the **active unidirectional transmission** windows.
* The **hexagon nodes** (`nodeTurn1`, `nodeTurn2`) represent the **turnaround latency** intervals where no data flows.

### 5.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Bidirectional Frame Exchange

This diagram shows the **temporal sequence** of events during one complete A $\rightarrow$ B $\rightarrow$ A cycle, which is the classic KTU timing diagram.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant A as Station A
    participant CH as Shared Channel
    participant B as Station B

    A->>CH: RTS Request
    CH-->>A: Channel Clear
    A->>B: DATA Frame (L bits, T_frame)
    Note over A,B: Direction A to B Active
    Note over CH: T_turn Elapses
    B->>A: DATA Frame (L bits, T_frame)
    Note over A,B: Direction B to A Active
    Note over CH: T_turn Elapses
    A-->>A: Cycle Complete
```

### 5.3 Conceptual Block Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Mermaid block illustrates the **internal functional architecture** of a half-duplex transceiver — the hardware building block that makes half-duplex operation possible at the physical layer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeDataIn[Application Data In]
    nodeMux[Direction Control MUX]
    nodeTX[Transmitter Circuit]
    nodeRX[Receiver Circuit]
    nodeLine[Shared Physical Medium]
    nodeSense[Carrier Sense Detector]
    nodeCtrl[Controller State Machine]

    nodeDataIn --> nodeMux
    nodeCtrl --> nodeMux
    nodeMux --> nodeTX
    nodeTX --> nodeLine
    nodeLine --> nodeRX
    nodeRX --> nodeDataOut[Decoded Data Out]
    nodeLine --> nodeSense
    nodeSense --> nodeCtrl
```

**Function of each block:**

* **`nodeMux`**: A hardware multiplexer that routes data either *to* the transmitter (TX) or *from* the receiver (RX) — never both simultaneously.
* **`nodeSense`**: Monitors the medium for an existing carrier signal to prevent collision.
* **`nodeCtrl`**: The state machine that orchestrates the MUX direction, implements CSMA, and times the turn-around interval.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 6. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
**Define Half Duplex transmission mode. Give two real-world examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Half Duplex is a mode of data communication in which two stations can transmit and receive data, but **not simultaneously**. The shared channel is used in **one direction at a time**, requiring a turn-around delay whenever the direction of transmission is reversed.

**Examples (1 mark — any two accepted):**
1. **Walkie-talkie / two-way radio** systems used by police and emergency services.
2. **CB (Citizens Band) radio** used by truckers.
3. **RS-485 industrial bus** used in factory automation.
4. **Legacy coaxial Ethernet (10BASE2 / 10BASE5)** networks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**
**List any three differences between Half Duplex and Full Duplex communication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per valid difference):**

| # | Half Duplex | Full Duplex |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Transmission occurs in both directions, but **one direction at a time**. | Transmission occurs in both directions **simultaneously**. |
| 2 | Requires only **one shared channel** (e.g., one wire pair, one frequency). | Requires **two independent channels** (e.g., two wire pairs, two frequency bands). |
| 3 | Suffers from **turn-around delay** ($T_{turn}$) and possible **collisions**; uses protocols like CSMA/CD or RTS/CTS. | No collisions and no turn-around penalty; each direction has its own dedicated channel. |

*(Valid alternative differences include: cost, hardware complexity, effective bandwidth utilization, example applications.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulations, Part B questions of 14 marks carry an **internal choice** — students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B. Both options are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Style]**

**(a)** Explain the Half Duplex transmission mode in detail with a suitable block diagram. Compare it with Simplex and Full Duplex modes. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A half-duplex link operates at a bit rate of $R = 1$ Mbps. The one-way propagation delay is $t_p = 15$ ms and the turn-around time is $T_{turn} = 30$ ms. The system exchanges frames of size $L = 3000$ bits in each direction. Calculate:
   1. The transmission time $T_{frame}$.
   2. The total bidirectional cycle time $T_{cycle}$.
   3. The effective throughput $T_{eff}$.
   4. The channel utilization $\eta$.
   5. Comment on how utilization can be improved. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Half Duplex is a bidirectional communication mode in which the two connected stations share a single transmission channel. Data can flow from A to B or from B to A, but **not at the same time**. A turn-around time $T_{turn}$ is incurred each time the direction is reversed.

**Block Diagram Description (2.5 marks):** *(Refer to the Mermaid architecture diagram in Section 5.3 of these notes.)* A half-duplex transceiver consists of:
* A **single shared physical medium** (one wire pair, one coax, one RF frequency).
* A **Direction Control MUX** that routes the data either to the transmitter or from the receiver, but never both.
* A **Carrier Sense Detector** that monitors the medium for an active signal.
* A **Controller State Machine** that arbitrates the MUX position and enforces the turn-around delay.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Simplex | Half Duplex | Full Duplex |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Direction | One-way | Two-way, alternate | Two-way, simultaneous |
| Channels | 1 | 1 (shared) | 2 (independent) |
| Example | TV broadcast | Walkie-talkie, RS-485 | Telephone, full-duplex Ethernet |
| Max Bandwidth Used | 50% | 100% (time-shared) | 100% (simultaneous) |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Highest |

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:** $R = 1{,}000{,}000$ bps, $t_p = 0.015$ s, $T_{turn} = 0.030$ s, $L = 3000$ bits.

**(i) Transmission Time (1.5 marks):**

$$T_{frame} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{3000}{1{,}000{,}000} = 0.003 \text{ s} = 3 \text{ ms}$$

**(ii) Total Cycle Time (1.5 marks):**

$$T_{cycle} = 2 T_{frame} + 2 T_{turn} = 2(3) + 2(30) = 6 + 60 = 66 \text{ ms}$$

**(iii) Effective Throughput (2 marks):**

$$T_{eff} = \frac{L}{T_{frame} + T_{turn}} = \frac{3000}{0.003 + 0.030} = \frac{3000}{0.033}$$

$$T_{eff} = 90{,}909.09 \text{ bps} \approx 90.91 \text{ kbps}$$

**(iv) Channel Utilization (1.5 marks):**

$$\eta = \frac{T_{frame}}{T_{frame} + T_{turn}} = \frac{3}{3 + 30} = \frac{3}{33} = 0.0909$$

$$\eta \approx 9.09\%$$

**(v) Comment on Improvement (0.5 mark):**
Utilization is abysmally low (under 10%) because the frame transmission time (3 ms) is much smaller than the turn-around time (30 ms). Utilization can be improved by **increasing the frame size $L$** so that $T_{frame}$ dominates $T_{turn}$, or by **reducing the physical link length** to lower $t_p$ and hence $T_{turn}$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** With the help of a timing diagram, explain how data exchange takes place in a Half Duplex communication system. Discuss the role of turn-around time and its impact on channel efficiency. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A half-duplex satellite link has a one-way propagation delay of $t_p = 250$ ms (due to the geostationary altitude). The channel bit rate is $R = 2$ Mbps and the turn-around time is $T_{turn} = 500$ ms. If the system exchanges frames of $L = 5000$ bits in each direction, compute:
   1. $T_{frame}$ and the total cycle time $T_{cycle}$.
   2. Effective throughput and utilization.
   3. How many such full cycles can occur in one minute?
   **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Timing Diagram Explanation (3 marks):** *(Refer to the Mermaid sequence diagram in Section 5.2 of these notes.)*
* **Phase 1 ($T_{frame}$):** Station A transmits its $L$-bit frame to Station B. During this entire interval, the channel is dedicated to the A $\rightarrow$ B direction. Station B's transmitter is muted.
* **Phase 2 ($T_{turn}$):** A silent guard interval. The MUX at A switches to receive mode, and the MUX at B switches to transmit mode. No bits are on the medium.
* **Phase 3 ($T_{frame}$):** Station B transmits its $L$-bit frame to Station A.
* **Phase 4 ($T_{turn}$):** A second silent guard interval returning control to A.

**Role of Turn-around Time (2 marks):**
* It is the **physically and logically necessary delay** to reverse the direction of transmission.
* It encompasses the propagation delay of the last bit, transceiver reconfiguration time, and any MAC-layer processing.
* In systems with long propagation delays (e.g., geostationary satellite with $t_p = 250$ ms), $T_{turn}$ can be **enormous** and dominate the cycle, devastating efficiency.

**Impact on Channel Efficiency (2 marks):**
The efficiency formula $\eta = \dfrac{T_{frame}}{T_{frame} + T_{turn}}$ shows that $\eta$ decreases as $T_{turn}$ grows. For half-duplex satellite links, this is why techniques like **larger frame aggregation**, **ACK piggybacking**, and **persistent transmission modes** are critical engineering solutions.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:** $R = 2{,}000{,}000$ bps, $t_p = 0.250$ s, $T_{turn} = 0.500$ s, $L = 5000$ bits.

**(i) Transmission Time & Cycle Time (2.5 marks):**

$$T_{frame} = \frac{L}{R} = \frac{5000}{2{,}000{,}000} = 0.0025 \text{ s} = 2.5 \text{ ms}$$

$$T_{cycle} = 2 T_{frame} + 2 T_{turn} = 2(2.5) + 2(500) = 5 + 1000 = 1005 \text{ ms} = 1.005 \text{ s}$$

**(ii) Effective Throughput & Utilization (2.5 marks):**

$$T_{eff} = \frac{L}{T_{frame} + T_{turn}} = \frac{5000}{0.0025 + 0.500} = \frac{5000}{0.5025}$$

$$T_{eff} = 9950.25 \text{ bps} \approx 9.95 \text{ kbps}$$

$$\eta = \frac{T_{frame}}{T_{frame} + T_{turn}} = \frac{2.5}{2.5 + 500} = \frac{2.5}{502.5} \approx 0.004975$$

$$\eta \approx 0.4975\%$$

**(iii) Number of Full Cycles Per Minute (2 marks):**

$$\text{Cycles per second} = \frac{1}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{1}{1.005} \approx 0.995 \text{ cycles/s}$$

$$\text{Cycles per minute} = 0.995 \times 60 \approx 59.7 \text{ cycles/minute}$$

This means the half-duplex satellite link can complete only **about 60 full bidirectional exchanges per minute**, a stark demonstration of how turn-around time cripples long-latency half-duplex systems.

---

### 6.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on Half Duplex Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in the cycle time.** Students often compute $T_{cycle} = T_{frame} + T_{turn}$, forgetting that **both** directions require transmission and switching. The correct expression is $T_{cycle} = 2 T_{frame} + 2 T_{turn}$.
> 2. **Confusing $T_{turn}$ with $t_p$.** Turn-around time is **at least** $2 \cdot t_p$ in the worst case (last bit of forward frame must arrive before direction reversal), but many systems have additional hardware overhead. Always use the value of $T_{turn}$ explicitly *given in the problem*.
> 3. **Using Full Duplex capacity as the denominator for utilization.** For half duplex, the maximum theoretical throughput is $C/2$, not $C$. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if this is misapplied.
> 4. **Skipping units in the final answer.** Always state **bps, kbps, seconds, or ms** explicitly. A numerical answer without a unit loses 0.5 to 1 mark depending on the examiner's strictness.
> 5. **Writing the formula for $\eta$ using total cycle $2T_{frame} + 2T_{turn}$ instead of the simplified form.** Either is mathematically correct, but the simplified $\eta = \dfrac{T_{frame}}{T_{frame} + T_{turn}}$ is faster and more elegant — examiners appreciate it.

---

### 6.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Half Duplex**
> - Half Duplex = **Two-way, one-direction-at-a-time** communication over a **single shared channel**.
> - Requires a **turn-around time** ($T_{turn}$) every time the direction is reversed.
> - **Susceptible to collisions** if both stations transmit simultaneously; mitigated by **CSMA, RTS/CTS**, or strict protocol discipline.
> - **Maximum throughput** is theoretically $C/2$ (half the full-duplex channel capacity), but in practice is much lower due to $T_{turn}$.
> - **Key efficiency formula:** $\eta = \dfrac{T_{frame}}{T_{frame} + T_{turn}} = \dfrac{L}{L + R \cdot T_{turn}}$.
> - **Effective throughput formula:** $T_{eff} = \dfrac{L \cdot R}{L + R \cdot T_{turn}}$.
> - **Total cycle time:** $T_{cycle} = 2 T_{frame} + 2 T_{turn}$.
> - **Real-world examples:** Walkie-talkie, CB radio, RS-485 bus, legacy 10BASE2/10BASE5 Ethernet, VSAT satellite links.
> - **Design rule of thumb:** Make $L$ as large as possible to **amortize** the $T_{turn}$ penalty and boost $\eta$.
> - **Use case:** Chosen when the cost of deploying a second physical channel is **prohibitive** or **impossible** (e.g., shared RF spectrum, single coax cable).
> - **Difference from Full Duplex:** Full Duplex uses two independent channels and supports **simultaneous** bidirectional flow with **no turn-around delay**.
> - **Difference from Simplex:** Simplex is **one-way only** (e.g., TV broadcast); Half Duplex is bidirectional but **time-shared**.
> - **Channel capacity unit is always in bps, kbps, or Mbps** — never forget to attach the unit in your final numerical answer.
> - **Propagation delay formula:** $t_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$, where $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in copper and $v \approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s in vacuum/air.
> - **Turn-around lower bound:** $T_{turn} \ge 2 t_p$ — examiners love testing this inequality.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Full Duplex Transmission

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Full Duplex Transmission** (also called **Duplex** or **Full-Duplex Mode / FDX**) is a data communication mode in which two communicating devices (a **source-DTE** and a **destination-DTE**) can transmit and receive data **simultaneously and independently** over the same physical channel or link. Both stations act as a **transmitter** and a **receiver** at the same instant, governed by a bidirectional channel with two independent physical or logical paths (one for each direction).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):**
> *"Full Duplex is the mode of data communication in which the data can be transmitted in both directions (Bi-directional) at the same time (Simultaneously) on a link. The channel capacity is shared between the two directions, and the link must be capable of carrying signals in both directions without interference."*

The channel is essentially equivalent to **two independent simplex links** operating in parallel between the same pair of devices. The effective throughput of the system is the **sum** of the throughputs in both directions.

---

## 1.2 The Three Transmission Modes — A Quick Map

| Mode | Direction Allowed | Simultaneous? | Real-World Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Simplex** | One direction only (e.g., A → B) | N/A | Radio broadcast, TV transmission, Keyboard → CPU |
| **Half Duplex** | Both directions, but **not at the same time** | ❌ | Walkie-Talkie, CB Radio, legacy Hub networks |
| **Full Duplex** | Both directions at the same time | ✅ | Mobile phone call, Ethernet switch ↔ NIC, Fiber backbone |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Viva:**
> **S**implex = **S**ingle direction (One-way street)
> **H**alf Duplex = **H**alf-at-a-time (Single-lane bridge with traffic signal)
> **F**ull Duplex = **F**reely both (Two-lane highway)

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **wide two-lane road** between City A and City B.
- **Lane 1** carries traffic from A → B.
- **Lane 2** carries traffic from B → A.
- Both lanes are **open at the same time**, and cars flow independently without ever stopping each other.

That is **Full Duplex Transmission**. Each device is simultaneously a sender AND a receiver. A telephone conversation is the classic example: while you are talking, you can also hear the other person talking at the exact same instant. There is no "press-to-talk" button (which would be Half Duplex, like a walkie-talkie).

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** In a full duplex link, the *bandwidth* of the medium can be allocated in two ways:
> 1. **Link is divided into two separate frequency bands** (Frequency-Division Duplex, **FDD**).
> 2. **Link shares the entire band, but echoes/crosstalk are cancelled** (Time-Division Duplex with echo cancellation, or full-duplex Ethernet over twisted pair using simultaneous send/receive on separate wire pairs).

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Minimum channel requirement:** **2 × bandwidth** of a single simplex link (if bandwidth is split into two sub-bands).
- **Effective throughput:** $C_{FDX} = 2 \times C_{simplex}$ (under ideal conditions).
- **Signaling efficiency:** A full duplex channel carrying $B$ Hz on each direction has a **Nyquist bit rate** of $C = 2B \log_2 V$ bits/sec per direction.
- **Reference standards in KTU syllabus:** **10BASE-T, 100BASE-TX, 1000BASE-T** Ethernet, **Full-Duplex GSM, LTE, 5G NR**, **ISDN PRI/BRI**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — How Full Duplex Actually Works

The full duplex link can be implemented using one of three underlying physical techniques. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

### Technique 1 — Two Separate Physical Channels
- The simplest method: dedicate one wire pair (or one fiber strand / one frequency band) to **A → B** and another to **B → A**.
- **Example:** A telephone landline uses a **2-wire hybrid** to convert 4-wire into 2-wire at the subscriber end, but the exchange side maintains 4 wires. Each direction has its own conductor.
- **Advantage:** Zero interference, simplest logic.
- **Disadvantage:** Doubles cabling cost.

### Technique 2 — Frequency Division (FDD)
- The total bandwidth of the medium is split into **two disjoint frequency bands**.
- A **Band-Pass Filter** at each end separates the two bands.
- **Example:** Cellular 4G LTE uses **FDD-LTE** where the uplink (e.g., 890–915 MHz) and downlink (935–960 MHz) are separate.
- **Guard band:** A small unused spectrum is kept between the two to prevent crosstalk.

### Technique 3 — Time Division with Echo Cancellation (TDD / EC)
- The same frequency band is shared, and both stations transmit at the same time.
- A sophisticated **Echo Canceller** subtracts the locally transmitted signal from the received signal.
- **Example:** 1000BASE-T (Gigabit Ethernet) uses **all 4 wire pairs in full duplex simultaneously** with echo cancellation, achieving 1 Gbps each way.

---

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' — Structured Logic

1. **Why does full duplex double effective throughput?**
   Because the channel carries **two independent data streams** in parallel. If each stream has capacity $C$, the **aggregate link capacity** is $2C$.

2. **How is collision eliminated?**
   In CSMA/CD (used in half-duplex Ethernet), collisions occur because stations share the medium. In **full duplex**, the physical paths are isolated, so **CSMA/CD is disabled**, and the maximum cable length restriction is also removed.

3. **How is full duplex represented in the OSI model?**
   - **Physical Layer (Layer 1):** Provides the bidirectional medium.
   - **Data Link Layer (Layer 2):** The MAC sublayer is configured for full duplex operation (no collision detection needed).

4. **What is the latency profile?**
   - **Propagation delay** is identical to half duplex (depends on distance, not direction).
   - **Transmission delay** is independent for each direction.
   - **Queueing delay** can be lower because there is no contention/collision backoff.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **must-memorize** set of formulas for the KTU Board Exam on this topic. **No vertical bars** are used inside cells (use `\vert` for absolute value to preserve table integrity).

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description / Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Effective Full Duplex Capacity** | $C_{FDX} = C_{AB} + C_{BA}$ | Sum of capacities of both directions (bits/sec) |
| 2 | **Ideal Symmetric FDX Capacity** | $C_{FDX} = 2 \times B \log_2 V$ | $B$ = bandwidth (Hz), $V$ = discrete signal levels |
| 3 | **Noisy Channel (Shannon)** | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | $S/N$ = Signal-to-Noise Ratio (unitless ratio) |
| 4 | **FDX Aggregate (Shannon)** | $C_{FDX} = 2B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Total bidirectional capacity |
| 5 | **Propagation Delay** | $t_p = d / v$ | $d$ = link length (m), $v$ = propagation speed ($\approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s in fiber) |
| 6 | **Transmission Delay** | $t_t = L / R$ | $L$ = packet size (bits), $R$ = link rate (bps) |
| 7 | **Total Latency (per direction)** | $T = t_p + t_t + t_q + t_{proc}$ | $t_q$ = queueing, $t_{proc}$ = nodal processing |
| 8 | **Bandwidth Split (FDD)** | $B_{total} = B_{up} + B_{down} + B_{guard}$ | $B_{guard}$ = guard band to prevent crosstalk |
| 9 | **Spectral Efficiency** | $\eta = R / B$ | Measured in bits/sec/Hz |
| 10 | **Throughput Efficiency** | $E = \text{Useful data} / \text{Total bits sent}$ | Ratio (0 to 1) |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Full duplex is the **backbone of modern broadband**. Without it:
- Mobile voice calls would feel like walkie-talkies.
- Fiber to the home (FTTH) would deliver half the advertised speed.
- Data centers could not support east-west traffic (server-to-server) at line rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **5G NR (New Radio):** Supports both FDD and TDD full duplex modes. TDD full duplex (with self-interference cancellation) is an active research area for **In-Band Full Duplex (IBFD)**, which theoretically **doubles spectral efficiency** — a hot topic in KTU 2024 syllabus updates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Effective Capacity of a Full Duplex Link

### Problem Statement
A symmetric full duplex link has a bandwidth of $B = 4$ kHz per direction and uses a signal with $V = 16$ discrete levels. Derive the effective data rate in each direction and the aggregate full duplex throughput.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the governing theorem.**
For a noiseless channel, the **Nyquist Bit Rate Formula** applies:

$$
C = 2B \log_2 V \quad \text{(bits per second)}
$$

where $B$ is the bandwidth of one direction and $V$ is the number of signal levels.

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**
Given: $B = 4000$ Hz, $V = 16$ levels.

$$
C_{one\,dir} = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2 16
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm.**
Since $16 = 2^4$, we have $\log_2 16 = 4$.

$$
C_{one\,dir} = 2 \times 4000 \times 4
$$

$$
C_{one\,dir} = 32000 \text{ bits/second}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Full Duplex aggregation rule.**
For a symmetric full duplex link, the aggregate capacity is **twice** the single-direction capacity:

$$
C_{FDX} = 2 \times C_{one\,dir}
$$

$$
C_{FDX} = 2 \times 32000
$$

$$
C_{FDX} = 64000 \text{ bits/second} = 64 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Final Answer:** $C_{one\,dir} = 32$ kbps, $C_{FDX} = 64$ kbps.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Latency in a Full Duplex Satellite Link

### Problem Statement
A full duplex satellite link has an uplink rate $R_{up} = 1$ Mbps and a downlink rate $R_{down} = 4$ Mbps. A station on Earth sends a packet of $L = 2000$ bits to the satellite. The propagation delay one-way is $t_p = 250$ ms. Find the total time until the **acknowledgement** (an 80-bit ACK packet) is received back at Earth, assuming zero queueing and processing delays.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Express the request-response timeline.**

$$
T_{total} = t_{prop}^{up} + t_{trans}^{up} + t_{prop}^{down} + t_{trans}^{down}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute uplink propagation delay.**

$$
t_{prop}^{up} = 250 \text{ ms} = 0.250 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute uplink transmission delay.**
The data packet of 2000 bits is sent at 1 Mbps.

$$
t_{trans}^{up} = \frac{L_{data}}{R_{up}} = \frac{2000 \text{ bits}}{1 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}}
$$

$$
t_{trans}^{up} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 2 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute downlink propagation delay.**
Propagation is symmetric for the same physical path.

$$
t_{prop}^{down} = 250 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute downlink transmission delay of the ACK.**
The ACK is 80 bits at 4 Mbps.

$$
t_{trans}^{down} = \frac{L_{ACK}}{R_{down}} = \frac{80 \text{ bits}}{4 \times 10^6 \text{ bits/s}}
$$

$$
t_{trans}^{down} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 0.02 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 6 — Sum all four components.**

$$
T_{total} = 250 + 2 + 250 + 0.02
$$

$$
T_{total} = 502.02 \text{ ms}
$$

**Final Answer:** $T_{total} \approx 502.02$ ms.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** Notice that **propagation delay dominates** (500 ms out of 502.02 ms). The full duplex nature does **not** reduce propagation delay — both directions travel the same distance. Full duplex only **eliminates the wait** between sending and receiving; it does **not** violate the speed of light.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bandwidth Required for FDD Full Duplex Voice

### Problem Statement
A mobile operator plans a full duplex voice channel using FDD. The uplink requires $B_{up} = 25$ kHz and the downlink requires $B_{down} = 25$ kHz. A guard band of $B_{guard} = 10$ kHz is needed between them to prevent interference. Derive the total bandwidth that must be licensed.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — State the FDD bandwidth equation.**

$$
B_{total} = B_{up} + B_{down} + B_{guard}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values.**

$$
B_{total} = 25 + 25 + 10
$$

$$
B_{total} = 60 \text{ kHz}
$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**
The operator must license 60 kHz of spectrum per voice channel, even though the user only "uses" 50 kHz of payload bandwidth. The extra 10 kHz is the **engineering tax** for full duplex.

**Final Answer:** $B_{total} = 60$ kHz.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Performance Aspect | Simplex | Half Duplex | Full Duplex |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Data direction** | One-way | Two-way (alternating) | Two-way (simultaneous) |
| **Throughput per channel** | $C$ | $C$ (but only 50% utilized in time) | $2C$ (aggregate) |
| **Latency for ACK** | Infinite (no ACK possible) | $2 t_{prop} + 2 t_{trans}$ | $2 t_{prop} + t_{trans}^{data} + t_{trans}^{ACK}$ (parallelizable) |
| **Channel count needed** | 1 | 1 (shared in time) | 2 (or 1 with echo cancel) |
| **Collision possible?** | No | Yes (CSMA/CD needed) | No (CSMA/CD disabled) |
| **Max cable length in Ethernet** | N/A | 2500 m (10BASE5 limit) | Unlimited (no collision domain limit) |
| **Hardware complexity** | Lowest | Medium | Highest (echo canceller) |
| **Example** | TV broadcast | Walkie-Talkie | Mobile phone, Fiber link |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Full Duplex Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the logical flow of data, control signals, and the echo cancellation mechanism inside a full duplex transceiver.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Station A Host] --> macA[MAC Layer A]
    macA --> phyA[Physical Layer A Transmitter]
    phyA --> ch1[Channel Direction A to B]
    ch1 --> phyB1[Physical Layer B Receiver]
    phyB1 --> macB[MAC Layer B]
    macB --> nodeB[Station B Host]
    nodeB --> macB2[MAC Layer B TX]
    macB2 --> phyB2[Physical Layer B Transmitter]
    phyB2 --> ch2[Channel Direction B to A]
    ch2 --> phyA2[Physical Layer A Receiver]
    phyA2 --> macA2[MAC Layer A RX]
    macA2 --> nodeA
    phyA --> ecA[Echo Canceller A]
    phyA2 --> ecA
    ecA --> nodeA
```

### Reading the Diagram
- The **left vertical chain** (A → B) is the forward path.
- The **right vertical chain** (B → A) is the reverse path.
- The **Echo Canceller** (bottom) ensures that Station A's transmitted signal is subtracted from its received signal so it does not "hear itself."

---

## 4.2 Frequency Division Duplex (FDD) Spectrum Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FDD_Spectrum[Total Licensed Spectrum BWtotal]
        direction LR
        band1[Uplink Band Bup]
        gb[Guard Band Bguard]
        band2[Downlink Band Bdown]
    end
```

### Interpretation
- The full spectrum is partitioned into three zones.
- A physical **duplexer filter** at the antenna separates the two bands with very high isolation (typically > 50 dB rejection).

---

## 4.3 Time Division Duplex (TDD) with Full Duplex Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TDD_Timeline[Time Axis]
        direction LR
        tA1[Time Slot 1 Station A sends]
        tB1[Time Slot 1 Station B sends simultaneously]
        tA2[Time Slot 2 Station A sends]
        tB2[Time Slot 2 Station B sends simultaneously]
        tA3[Time Slot 3 Station A sends]
        tB3[Time Slot 3 Station B sends simultaneously]
    end
    tA1 --- tB1
    tA2 --- tB2
    tA3 --- tB3
```

### Interpretation
- In TDD with echo cancellation, both stations transmit in the **same time slot** on the **same frequency**, achieving true full duplex behavior.
- This is the basis of **5G NR TDD mode** and **Wi-Fi 802.11ax full-duplex research**.

---

## 4.4 OSI Layer Mapping for Full Duplex

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    app[Application Layer] --> trans[Transport Layer]
    trans --> net[Network Layer]
    net --> dll[Data Link Layer MAC Sublayer FDX Enabled]
    dll --> phy[Physical Layer Bidirectional Medium]
    phy --> medium[Copper OR Fiber OR Wireless Channel]
```

### Key Takeaway
The **physical layer** is what makes full duplex physically possible (separate wires, FDD, or echo cancellation). The **MAC sublayer** is what makes it logically work (no CSMA/CD, no collision handling).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set A]`
**Q: Differentiate between Simplex, Half Duplex, and Full Duplex transmission modes with one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Mode | Definition | Example |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Simplex** | Communication in **one direction only**; the receiver cannot send back data. | Television broadcasting |
| **Half Duplex** | Both stations can transmit, but **only one at a time**; they take turns. | Walkie-Talkie |
| **Full Duplex** | Both stations can transmit **simultaneously in both directions**. | Mobile phone conversation |

*(1 mark for correct definition of each mode, 1 mark for correct example of each — total 3 marks.)*

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]`
**Q: Why is CSMA/CD disabled in a full duplex Ethernet link? State one advantage.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. In full duplex, the physical medium provides **two independent paths** (one for each direction), so transmissions from the two stations **never collide** on the wire. (2 marks)
2. **Advantage:** The **maximum cable length restriction (2500 m for 10BASE5, 100 m for 10BASE-T) is removed** because there is no collision domain. Full duplex links can extend over the physical reach of the medium without the round-trip collision-detection timing constraint. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question Set 1 (Module 1 Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question 1A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Slot B]`

**(a) Explain the three modes of data transmission with neat diagrams. State one application of each. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Mode 1 — Simplex:**
Data flows in **one fixed direction** only. The channel has a dedicated transmitter at one end and a dedicated receiver at the other.
- *Application:* FM radio broadcast — the radio station transmits, listeners only receive.

**Mode 2 — Half Duplex:**
Both ends can transmit, but **not simultaneously**. A single channel is shared in time, typically controlled by a "press-to-talk" mechanism or a token-passing protocol.
- *Application:* Walkie-Talkie — the operator must press a button to talk and release it to listen.

**Mode 3 — Full Duplex:**
Both ends can transmit **simultaneously**. The channel is effectively two simplex links, often realized using FDD, TDD with echo cancellation, or two separate physical paths.
- *Application:* Telephone call — both parties can speak and hear at the same instant.

**Diagram (described textually since board exam allows hand-drawing):**
- Simplex: `A ====> B` (single arrow)
- Half Duplex: `A <===> B` (double arrow, but with a "one-at-a-time" label)
- Full Duplex: `A <=X=> B` (two crossed arrows showing simultaneous flow)

*(7 marks = 2 marks per mode description + 1 mark for application + 2 marks for diagram quality)*

---

**(b) A full duplex channel uses FDD with an uplink bandwidth of 200 kHz and a downlink bandwidth of 200 kHz. A guard band of 20 kHz is placed between them. If the signal uses 64 levels, calculate: (i) the data rate of each direction, (ii) the total licensed bandwidth, and (iii) the aggregate full duplex throughput. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Data rate per direction (Nyquist):**

$$
C = 2B \log_2 V
$$

For uplink: $B = 200$ kHz $= 200{,}000$ Hz, $V = 64$.

$$
C_{up} = 2 \times 200{,}000 \times \log_2 64
$$

Since $64 = 2^6$, $\log_2 64 = 6$.

$$
C_{up} = 2 \times 200{,}000 \times 6 = 2{,}400{,}000 \text{ bps} = 2.4 \text{ Mbps}
$$

By symmetry, $C_{down} = 2.4$ Mbps.

**(ii) Total licensed bandwidth:**

$$
B_{total} = B_{up} + B_{down} + B_{guard}
$$

$$
B_{total} = 200 + 200 + 20 = 420 \text{ kHz}
$$

**(iii) Aggregate full duplex throughput:**

$$
C_{FDX} = C_{up} + C_{down} = 2.4 + 2.4 = 4.8 \text{ Mbps}
$$

*(7 marks = 3 marks for part (i), 2 marks for part (ii), 2 marks for part (iii).)*

---

#### **Question 1B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Slot A]`

**(a) Describe the three techniques used to implement full duplex transmission on a physical medium. Compare their pros and cons. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Technique 1 — Two Separate Physical Channels:**
- The medium is duplicated (e.g., 4-wire twisted pair carrying 2 in each direction).
- *Pros:* Zero interference, simple logic.
- *Cons:* Doubles cabling cost and connector pin count.

**Technique 2 — Frequency Division Duplex (FDD):**
- The spectrum is split into two non-overlapping bands with a guard band.
- *Pros:* Mature technology (used in 4G LTE FDD), reliable filtering.
- *Cons:* Inefficient spectrum use due to guard band; requires expensive duplexer filters.

**Technique 3 — Time Division with Echo Cancellation (TDD/EC):**
- Both stations transmit on the same frequency at the same time; a DSP subtracts the local transmitted signal.
- *Pros:* Most spectrally efficient, no guard band needed, supports asymmetric traffic.
- *Cons:* Complex DSP, residual self-interference, requires very linear RF front-end.

*(7 marks = 2 marks per technique + 1 mark for comparison summary.)*

---

**(b) A full duplex link has a total round-trip propagation delay of 50 ms. A file of 1 Mbit is to be transmitted from A to B. The link rate is 2 Mbps. How long does it take for the entire file to arrive at B and for B to send a 1-bit ACK back to A? Neglect queueing and processing delays. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — One-way propagation delay.**

$$
t_p = 50 \text{ ms} / 2 = 25 \text{ ms} = 0.025 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — File transmission delay (1 Mbit at 2 Mbps).**

$$
t_t = \frac{1 \times 10^6 \text{ bits}}{2 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 0.5 \text{ s} = 500 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3 — ACK transmission delay (1 bit at 2 Mbps).**

$$
t_{ACK} = \frac{1 \text{ bit}}{2 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} = 0.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 0.0005 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Total time (file sent + propagation + ACK sent + propagation back).**

$$
T = t_t + t_p + t_{ACK} + t_p
$$

$$
T = 500 + 25 + 0.0005 + 25
$$

$$
T = 550.0005 \text{ ms} \approx 550 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on full duplex impact.**
Because the link is full duplex, A could have started sending the next packet during B's ACK transmission. However, the question asks for the **time to deliver the file and receive ACK**, which is the round-trip calculation above.

*(7 marks = 2 marks for propagation, 2 marks for transmission, 2 marks for ACK, 1 mark for final summation.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "full duplex doubles speed" with "full duplex halves latency":** Full duplex does **not** reduce propagation delay — it only allows **simultaneous** traffic. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this misconception.
> 2. **Forgetting the guard band** in FDD problems: Many students write $B_{total} = B_{up} + B_{down}$ and skip $B_{guard}$. This is a **favourite 2-mark deduction** in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Mixing Nyquist and Shannon formulas:** If the question says "noisy channel" or mentions SNR, use **Shannon**. If it says "noiseless" or gives signal levels, use **Nyquist**. Examiners explicitly check this distinction.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency:** Always convert kHz to Hz and ms to seconds inside the formula. A common error is using 200 (kHz) directly in $C = 2B \log_2 V$, which gives a wrong answer by a factor of 1000.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Full Duplex = simultaneous bidirectional** communication between two stations.
- [x] **Three implementation methods:** separate channels, FDD (frequency split), TDD with echo cancellation.
- [x] **Aggregate capacity:** $C_{FDX} = 2C$ for a symmetric link under Nyquist conditions.
- [x] **Guard band is mandatory** in FDD to prevent inter-direction interference.
- [x] **CSMA/CD is disabled** in full duplex Ethernet; collision domain limits vanish.
- [x] **Full duplex does NOT reduce propagation delay** — it only enables parallel transmission.
- [x] **Nyquist** for noiseless, **Shannon** for noisy channels.
- [x] **Real-world examples:** Telephone, 1000BASE-T Ethernet, 4G LTE, 5G NR.
- [x] **OSI layer:** Full duplex is primarily a **Physical Layer + MAC Sublayer** feature.
- [x] **5G trend:** In-Band Full Duplex (IBFD) with self-interference cancellation is a current research frontier.
- [x] **Board exam weightage (typical):** 7–10 marks out of 100 in Module 1, often combined with transmission mode questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Periodic Analog Signals - Sine Wave
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Periodic Analog Signals - Sine Wave

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OECST612 – Data Communication)** framework, a **Periodic Analog Signal** is defined as a continuous-time signal that repeats its pattern (amplitude and shape) identically over a fixed, deterministic time interval, called the **Period (T)**. A **Sine Wave** is the most fundamental, mathematically pure, and naturally occurring periodic analog signal — often referred to as a **Sinusoid**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** *A sine wave is a continuous, smooth, periodic oscillation described by the trigonometric sine function. It serves as the mathematical building block (basis function) for representing ALL complex signals in data communication systems, as per **Fourier Analysis**.*

The general mathematical form of a sinusoid is:

$$s(t) = A \cdot \sin(2\pi f t + \theta)$$

where every parameter carries a precise physical meaning:
- **A** = Peak Amplitude (signal strength / voltage level)
- **f** = Frequency in **Hertz (Hz)** — cycles per second
- **t** = Time in **seconds (s)**
- **$\theta$** = Phase angle in **radians** (or degrees)
- **$2\pi f$** = Angular frequency, denoted **$\omega$** (in **rad/s**)

The signal satisfies the **Periodicty Condition** for all time *t*:

$$s(t + T) = s(t), \quad \text{where} \quad T = \frac{1}{f}$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Ocean Wave

Imagine standing on a beach and watching ocean waves roll in:

- **The rise and fall of water** is the amplitude (A) — how *tall* the wave is.
- **The time between two consecutive crests hitting the shore** is the period (T) — how *frequently* waves arrive.
- **How many crests pass you per second** is the frequency (f) — the *speed* of repetition.
- **Whether the crest reaches you now or slightly later** is the phase ($\theta$) — the *timing offset*.

A sine wave is the simplest "perfect ocean wave" — no splashes, no noise, just pure mathematical smoothness. In data communication, this perfect wave is what we **aim to transmit cleanly**, and what we **measure against** when noise distorts real signals.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the Sine Wave is KING in Data Communication:**
