> 1. It is the **only periodic signal that preserves its shape** when passed through linear systems.
> 2. Any real-world signal (voice, video, data) can be decomposed into a sum of sine waves using **Fourier's Theorem**.
> 3. Sine waves travel through copper wires, fiber optics, and air with **predictable, mathematically tractable behavior**.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Sine Wave Parameters (At a Glance)

| Property | Symbol | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Instantaneous Amplitude | $s(t)$ | Volts (V) | Signal value at time *t* |
| Peak Amplitude | A | Volts (V) | Maximum displacement from mean |
| Peak-to-Peak Amplitude | $A_{pp}$ | Volts (V) | $A_{pp} = 2A$ |
| Period | T | seconds (s) | Time for one full cycle |
| Frequency | f | Hertz (Hz) | Cycles per second |
| Angular Frequency | $\omega$ | rad/s | Rate of phase change |
| Phase | $\theta$ | radians (rad) | Horizontal shift |
| Wavelength | $\lambda$ | meters (m) | Spatial period of wave |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of a Sine Wave

### Pillar 1 — Amplitude (A)
The **amplitude** represents the signal's energy or strength. In communication systems, higher amplitude means a stronger signal at the receiver, which directly improves the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**.

- **Peak Amplitude (A):** Maximum value of the sine wave above (or below) the time axis.
- **RMS Amplitude ($A_{rms}$):** The "effective" DC-equivalent power value. For a pure sine wave:
$$A_{rms} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707 \cdot A$$

### Pillar 2 — Frequency (f) and Period (T)
These two are **inversely proportional** and form the heart of signal characterization in both the **time domain** and the **frequency domain**.

$$f = \frac{1}{T} = \frac{\omega}{2\pi}$$

- **High frequency** → tightly packed oscillations → short period.
- **Low frequency** → spread out oscillations → long period.

> [!NOTE]
> In **Data Communication**, frequency determines the channel allocation, bandwidth, and the physical medium's ability to carry the signal. The electromagnetic spectrum is divided into bands based on frequency.

### Pillar 3 — Phase ($\theta$)
The **phase** tells us **where the wave starts** at $t = 0$. It is critical when **combining multiple sine waves** (e.g., in modulation or interference analysis).

- A phase shift of $\pi$ (180°) inverts the wave.
- A phase shift of $\pi/2$ (90°) converts sine into cosine.

## 2.2 Time Domain vs. Frequency Domain

A sine wave has two equivalent representations:

1. **Time Domain:** $s(t) = A\sin(2\pi f t + \theta)$ — shows amplitude vs. time.
2. **Frequency Domain:** A single **spectral line** at frequency *f* with height equal to amplitude A — shows amplitude vs. frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Concept:** A pure sine wave in the time domain corresponds to a **single impulse (line)** in the frequency domain. This duality is the foundation of **Fourier Analysis**, which is essential for understanding **bandwidth, harmonics, and signal decomposition**.

## 2.3 Phase Relationships Between Two Sine Waves

For two sinusoids with identical frequency:
$$s_1(t) = A_1 \sin(2\pi f t + \theta_1)$$
$$s_2(t) = A_2 \sin(2\pi f t + \theta_2)$$

The **phase difference** is $\Delta\theta = \theta_1 - \theta_2$.

| Condition | Result | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|
| $\Delta\theta = 0$ | **In-phase** — waves add constructively | Maximum constructive interference |
| $\Delta\theta = \pi$ (180°) | **Anti-phase** — waves cancel | Total destructive interference |
| $\Delta\theta = \pi/2$ (90°) | **Quadrature** | Used in QAM, PSK modulation |
| $\Delta\theta$ arbitrary | **Partial interference** | Phase-shift keying (PSK) |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $s(t) = A\sin(2\pi f t + \theta)$ | General sine wave equation | V |
| 2 | $T = \frac{1}{f}$ | Period from frequency | s |
| 3 | $f = \frac{1}{T}$ | Frequency from period | Hz |
| 4 | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | Angular frequency | rad/s |
| 5 | $A_{pp} = 2A$ | Peak-to-peak amplitude | V |
| 6 | $A_{rms} = \dfrac{A}{\sqrt{2}}$ | RMS amplitude | V |
| 7 | $P = A_{rms}^2 / R$ | Average power across R | W |
| 8 | $\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f}$ | Wavelength (v = wave velocity) | m |
| 9 | $\phi(t) = 2\pi f t + \theta$ | Instantaneous phase | rad |
| 10 | $s(t+T) = s(t)$ | Periodicity condition | — |
| 11 | $\Delta\theta = \theta_1 - \theta_2$ | Phase difference | rad |
| 12 | $E = \int_{0}^{T} s^2(t)\, dt$ | Energy of one period | J |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Telecommunications:** Sine waves are the **carriers** that carry information through modulation (AM, FM, PM, PSK, QAM).
- **Power Systems:** The 50 Hz / 60 Hz AC mains supply is a pure sine wave.
- **Signal Processing:** Filters, FFT analyzers, and oscilloscopes are designed around sine-wave response.
- **Wireless:** Antenna design, propagation, and channel modeling all depend on sinusoidal EM waves.
- **Audio Engineering:** Any musical tone is essentially a sum of sine waves (fundamental + harmonics).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Sine to Cosine (Phase Shift)

We know that:
$$\cos(x) = \sin\!\left(x + \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$$

**Starting Point:** A standard sine wave without phase:
$$s(t) = A\sin(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 1 — Apply the identity** by substituting $x = 2\pi f t$:
$$s(t) = A\cos(2\pi f t - \frac{\pi}{2})$$

**Step 2 — Equate** to general form $A\sin(2\pi f t + \theta)$:
$$A\sin(2\pi f t + \theta) = A\cos(2\pi f t - \frac{\pi}{2})$$

**Step 3 — Solve** for $\theta$:
$$\theta = \frac{\pi}{2} \text{ radians} = 90^\circ$$

> **Conclusion:** A sine wave leads a cosine wave by **+90° (π/2 radians)**, or equivalently, a cosine wave lags a sine wave by **90°**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — RMS Value of a Pure Sine Wave

The **Root Mean Square (RMS)** value represents the equivalent DC voltage that would deliver the same average power to a resistive load.

**Step 1 — Define the squared signal:**
$$s^2(t) = A^2 \sin^2(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 2 — Apply the trigonometric identity:**
$$\sin^2(x) = \frac{1 - \cos(2x)}{2}$$

Therefore:
$$s^2(t) = A^2 \cdot \frac{1 - \cos(4\pi f t)}{2} = \frac{A^2}{2} - \frac{A^2}{2}\cos(4\pi f t)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mean (average) over one full period T:**
Since the average value of $\cos(4\pi f t)$ over a complete period is **zero** (it completes 2 full cycles in time T):

$$\langle s^2(t) \rangle = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} s^2(t)\, dt = \frac{A^2}{2} - 0 = \frac{A^2}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root to obtain RMS:**
$$A_{rms} = \sqrt{\langle s^2(t) \rangle} = \sqrt{\frac{A^2}{2}} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}}$$

$$\boxed{A_{rms} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071 \cdot A}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Average Power Delivered to a Load

Consider a sine wave $s(t) = A\sin(2\pi f t)$ applied across a resistor $R$.

**Step 1 — Instantaneous power** is given by:
$$p(t) = \frac{s^2(t)}{R} = \frac{A^2 \sin^2(2\pi f t)}{R}$$

**Step 2 — Average power** over one period:
$$P_{avg} = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \frac{A^2 \sin^2(2\pi f t)}{R}\, dt = \frac{A^2}{2R}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute** $A = \sqrt{2} \cdot A_{rms}$:
$$P_{avg} = \frac{(\sqrt{2}\,A_{rms})^2}{2R} = \frac{2\,A_{rms}^2}{2R}$$

$$\boxed{P_{avg} = \frac{A_{rms}^2}{R} \quad \text{(Watts)}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Phase Difference and Time Delay Equivalence

A phase shift $\theta$ in a sine wave is equivalent to a time shift $\Delta t$ in the time domain.

**Step 1 — General sine with phase:**
$$s(t) = A\sin(2\pi f t + \theta)$$

**Step 2 — Rewrite** by factoring:
$$s(t) = A\sin\!\left(2\pi f \left[t + \frac{\theta}{2\pi f}\right]\right)$$

**Step 3 — Define the time delay** $\Delta t$:
$$\Delta t = \frac{\theta}{2\pi f} = \frac{\theta}{\omega}$$

$$\boxed{\theta = 2\pi f \cdot \Delta t = \omega \cdot \Delta t}$$

**Engineering Use:** This relationship is **critical** in antenna array design, beamforming, and synchronization circuits, where a known time delay is converted to a phase shift for analysis.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Period to Frequency Conversion (Worked Numerical)

**Problem:** A sine wave completes **one full cycle in 0.5 milliseconds**. Find the frequency and angular frequency.

**Step 1 — Identify** the period:
$$T = 0.5 \text{ ms} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply** $f = 1/T$:
$$f = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 2000 \text{ Hz} = 2 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3 — Compute angular frequency:**
$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 2000 = 4000\pi \text{ rad/s} \approx 12566.37 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 4 — Final sine wave equation:**
$$s(t) = A\sin(4000\pi t + \theta)$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Scenarios

| Scenario | Given | Find | Key Formula Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| Period to Frequency | T = 2 ms | f | $f = 1/T$ → 500 Hz |
| Frequency to Period | f = 1 GHz | T | $T = 1/f$ → 1 ns |
| Wavelength Computation | f = 100 MHz, v = c | $\lambda$ | $\lambda = v/f$ → 3 m |
| RMS to Peak | $V_{rms}$ = 230 V | A | $A = V_{rms}\sqrt{2}$ → 325.27 V |
| Time Delay to Phase | $\Delta t$ = 1 ms, f = 250 Hz | $\theta$ | $\theta = 2\pi f \Delta t$ → $\pi/2$ rad |
| Phase to Time Delay | $\theta$ = $\pi/4$, f = 1 kHz | $\Delta t$ | $\Delta t = \theta/(2\pi f)$ → 125 µs |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Anatomy of a Sine Wave (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Time Variable t] --> B[Angular Frequency Calculator: omega = 2*pi*f]
    B --> C[Phase Modulator: phi = omega*t + theta]
    C --> D[Sine Function Core: sin phi]
    D --> E[Amplitude Scaler: A * sin phi]
    E --> F[Output: s of t]
    P1[Parameter Source A: Peak Amplitude] --> E
    P2[Parameter Source f: Frequency in Hz] --> B
    P3[Parameter Source theta: Phase Shift] --> C
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Time Domain ↔ Frequency Domain Duality

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TD[Time Domain: s of t equals A sin of 2 pi f t plus theta] -->|Fourier Transform FT| FD[Frequency Domain: Spectral Line at f with Height A]
    FD -->|Inverse Fourier Transform IFT| TD
    TD -->|Observation 1: Continuous Oscillation| OBS1[Wave repeats every T seconds]
    FD -->|Observation 2: Discrete Spike| OBS2[Single frequency component only]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Phase Relationship Between Two Sinusoids

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Sinusoid 1: s1 of t equals A1 sin of omega t plus theta1] --> PD[Phase Comparator: delta theta equals theta1 minus theta2]
    S2[Sinusoid 2: s2 of t equals A2 sin of omega t plus theta2] --> PD
    PD --> COND{Check delta theta Value}
    COND -->|delta theta = 0| OUT1[Result: In Phase - Constructive Addition]
    COND -->|delta theta = pi| OUT2[Result: Anti Phase - Total Cancellation]
    COND -->|delta theta = pi by 2| OUT3[Result: Quadrature - Used in QAM and PSK]
    COND -->|delta theta = Any| OUT4[Result: Phase Shift Keying Modulation]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Sine Wave Parameter Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SW[Periodic Analog Signal: Sine Wave] --> AMP[Amplitude Domain]
    SW --> FREQ[Frequency Domain]
    SW --> PHS[Phase Domain]
    AMP --> A1[Peak Amplitude A in Volts]
    AMP --> A2[Peak to Peak Amplitude App equals 2A]
    AMP --> A3[RMS Amplitude Arms equals A divided by sqrt 2]
    FREQ --> F1[Frequency f in Hertz]
    FREQ --> F2[Period T equals 1 over f in seconds]
    FREQ --> F3[Angular Frequency omega equals 2 pi f in rad per s]
    PHS --> P1[Phase Angle theta in radians]
    PHS --> P2[Time Delay delta t equals theta over omega in seconds]
    PHS --> P3[Wavelength lambda equals v over f in meters]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block above maps a parameter to its physical meaning. In the KTU exam, drawing such block diagrams earns you extra marks in the **Application (Apply) and Analysis (Analyze)** Bloom's Taxonomy levels.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define a periodic analog signal. What is the mathematical representation of a sine wave? List any four characteristic parameters.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **periodic analog signal** is a continuous-time signal whose waveform repeats identically after a fixed time interval called the **period (T)**. Mathematically, $s(t + T) = s(t)$ for all $t$.

A sine wave is represented as:
$$s(t) = A\sin(2\pi f t + \theta)$$

The four characteristic parameters are:
1. **Peak Amplitude (A)** — Volts
2. **Frequency (f)** — Hertz
3. **Period (T)** — seconds, where $T = 1/f$
4. **Phase ($\theta$)** — radians

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**State the relationship between frequency, period, and angular frequency. If a sine wave has a period of 2 ms, calculate its frequency and angular frequency.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The relationships are:
$$f = \frac{1}{T}, \quad \omega = 2\pi f = \frac{2\pi}{T}$$

**Given:** $T = 2 \text{ ms} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}$

**Calculations:**
$$f = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 500 \text{ Hz}$$
$$\omega = 2\pi \times 500 = 1000\pi \approx 3141.59 \text{ rad/s}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **RMS value of a pure sine wave**. Show that $A_{rms} = A/\sqrt{2}$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Two sine waves are given as $s_1(t) = 10\sin(100\pi t)$ and $s_2(t) = 10\sin(100\pi t + \pi/3)$. Find the **phase difference, time delay**, and the **resultant signal** $s(t) = s_1(t) + s_2(t)$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**(a) Derivation of RMS value (7 Marks):**

The RMS value is defined as the square root of the mean of the square of the signal over one period:

$$A_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} s^2(t)\, dt}$$

**Step 1:** Write the signal: $s(t) = A\sin(2\pi f t)$

**Step 2:** Square the signal: $s^2(t) = A^2 \sin^2(2\pi f t)$

**Step 3:** Apply the identity $\sin^2(x) = \dfrac{1 - \cos(2x)}{2}$:

$$s^2(t) = \frac{A^2}{2} - \frac{A^2}{2}\cos(4\pi f t)$$

**Step 4:** Integrate over one period T:

$$\int_{0}^{T} s^2(t)\, dt = \frac{A^2}{2}\int_{0}^{T} dt - \frac{A^2}{2}\int_{0}^{T}\cos(4\pi f t)\, dt$$

**Step 5:** The second integral equals zero over a complete period. So:

$$\int_{0}^{T} s^2(t)\, dt = \frac{A^2 \cdot T}{2}$$

**Step 6:** Compute mean and take square root:

$$A_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \cdot \frac{A^2 T}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{A^2}{2}} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}}$$

$$\boxed{A_{rms} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707A}$$

---

**(b) Phase difference and resultant signal (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $s_1(t) = 10\sin(100\pi t)$ and $s_2(t) = 10\sin(100\pi t + \pi/3)$

**Step 1 — Identify the common angular frequency:**
$$\omega = 100\pi \text{ rad/s} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f = \frac{100\pi}{2\pi} = 50 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 2 — Phase difference:**
$$\Delta\theta = \theta_1 - \theta_2 = 0 - \frac{\pi}{3} = -\frac{\pi}{3} \text{ rad} = -60^\circ$$

This means $s_1$ **lags** $s_2$ by 60°, or equivalently, $s_2$ **leads** $s_1$ by 60°.

**Step 3 — Time delay equivalent:**
$$\Delta t = \frac{\Delta\theta}{\omega} = \frac{-\pi/3}{100\pi} = -\frac{1}{300} \text{ s} \approx -3.33 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Resultant signal using identity:**
$$s(t) = A_1\sin(\omega t + \theta_1) + A_2\sin(\omega t + \theta_2)$$

This can be written in the form $R\sin(\omega t + \phi)$ where:

$$R = \sqrt{A_1^2 + A_2^2 + 2A_1 A_2 \cos(\theta_2 - \theta_1)}$$

$$R = \sqrt{10^2 + 10^2 + 2(10)(10)\cos(\pi/3)} = \sqrt{100 + 100 + 200(0.5)} = \sqrt{300} = 10\sqrt{3}$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{A_2 \sin\theta_2}{A_1 + A_2 \cos\theta_2}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{10 \cdot \sin(\pi/3)}{10 + 10\cos(\pi/3)}\right)$$

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{10 \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{10 + 5}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{5\sqrt{3}}{15}\right) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{3}\right) = \frac{\pi}{6}$$

**Final Resultant Signal:**
$$\boxed{s(t) = 10\sqrt{3} \cdot \sin\!\left(100\pi t + \frac{\pi}{6}\right) \approx 17.32 \sin(100\pi t + 30^\circ)}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [Alternative Module Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Define the terms **Wavelength**, **Period**, **Frequency**, and **Phase** for a sinusoidal signal. Establish the relationship between wavelength, frequency, and propagation velocity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A communication channel uses a carrier sine wave of frequency **2.4 GHz** (typical Wi-Fi band). The signal travels at the speed of light ($v = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s). Calculate the **period**, **angular frequency**, and **wavelength** of the carrier. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**(a) Definitions and Wavelength Relationship (7 Marks):**

- **Period (T):** The time duration in seconds required for one complete cycle of the sine wave to occur. Unit: seconds (s).
- **Frequency (f):** The number of complete cycles occurring per second. Unit: Hertz (Hz). Related to period by $f = 1/T$.
- **Phase ($\theta$):** The angular position of the waveform at $t = 0$, indicating a horizontal time shift. Unit: radians.
- **Wavelength ($\lambda$):** The spatial distance covered by one complete cycle of the wave as it propagates through a medium. Unit: meters (m).

**Wavelength-Frequency-Velocity Relationship:**

When a wave travels with velocity $v$, it covers a distance of one wavelength $\lambda$ in time equal to one period $T$. Therefore:

$$v = \frac{\lambda}{T}$$

Substituting $T = 1/f$:
$$\boxed{\lambda = \frac{v}{f}}$$

For electromagnetic waves in free space, $v = c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, so:
$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{f}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Computations for 2.4 GHz Carrier (7 Marks):**

**Given:** $f = 2.4 \text{ GHz} = 2.4 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz}$, $v = c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s

**Step 1 — Period T:**
$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 4.167 \times 10^{-10} \text{ s} = 0.4167 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Angular frequency $\omega$:**
$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 2.4 \times 10^9 = 4.8\pi \times 10^9 \text{ rad/s}$$
$$\omega \approx 1.508 \times 10^{10} \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Wavelength $\lambda$:**
$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2.4 \times 10^9} = 0.125 \text{ m} = 12.5 \text{ cm}$$

**Final Tabulated Results:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Period | T | 0.4167 ns |
| Angular Frequency | $\omega$ | $1.508 \times 10^{10}$ rad/s |
| Wavelength | $\lambda$ | 0.125 m (12.5 cm) |

> **Note:** This is why Wi-Fi routers are designed with antennas of length ~6.25 cm (a quarter-wavelength) — directly derived from the 2.4 GHz sine wave carrier's properties.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Forgetting to convert ms → s, GHz → Hz. This alone costs **1–2 marks** per question.
> 2. **Radian vs. Degree Confusion:** Phase $\theta$ in formulas must be in **radians**, not degrees. Use $\pi/3$ not 60°.
> 3. **RMS vs. Peak Confusion:** $A$ and $A_{rms}$ are **not** the same. Power calculations ALWAYS use $A_{rms}$.
> 4. **Sign of Phase:** A positive $\theta$ means the wave is **advanced** (leading); negative means **delayed** (lagging).
> 5. **Skipping the Derivation Steps:** In 7-mark derivations, KTU expects **every algebraic step shown**. Skipping the integration step in RMS derivation will cost **3–4 marks**.
> 6. **Wavelength Formula:** Students often write $c = f\lambda$ as $f = c\lambda$ — **wrong**. Wavelength is inversely proportional to frequency.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Periodic Analog Signals – Sine Wave":**

- [x] A sine wave has the general form: $s(t) = A\sin(2\pi f t + \theta)$.
- [x] **Period $T = 1/f$** and **angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$** — the two most-tested conversions.
- [x] **RMS value** of a pure sine wave: $A_{rms} = A/\sqrt{2} \approx 0.707A$.
- [x] **Peak-to-Peak** amplitude: $A_{pp} = 2A$.
- [x] **Wavelength–Frequency relation:** $\lambda = v/f$ (for EM waves, $v = c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s).
- [x] **Phase and time delay** are related by: $\theta = \omega \cdot \Delta t$ and $\Delta t = \theta / (2\pi f)$.
- [x] **Time Domain ↔ Frequency Domain:** A pure sine wave maps to a single spectral line.
- [x] **Phase relationships:** $0°$ = in-phase, $180°$ = anti-phase, $90°$ = quadrature.
- [x] **Periodicity condition:** $s(t + T) = s(t)$ for all $t$.
- [x] Sine waves are the **basis functions** for Fourier decomposition of all signals.
- [x] **Two in-phase equal-amplitude sine waves** sum to **double amplitude**; **two anti-phase** sine waves sum to **zero**.
- [x] Always quote **units**: Hz for frequency, s for period, rad for phase, V for amplitude, m for wavelength.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Amplitude
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Amplitude

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Data Communication** (KTU 2024 Scheme, OECST612, Module 1), **Amplitude** is formally defined as the **maximum absolute value of a signal's instantaneous magnitude measured from its mean (equilibrium) position or reference zero level**. It quantifies the strength, intensity, or energy of a signal waveform at any given instant. For a periodic analog signal $s(t)$, amplitude is the peak displacement of the wave from its central axis.

Mathematically, if a sinusoidal signal is represented as:

$$s(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)$$

then the amplitude is the scalar $A$, which is measured in **Volts (V)** for electrical signals, or in corresponding power-equivalent units when referenced to decibels (dB).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1 of Data Communication, amplitude is the foundational signal parameter introduced when classifying analog vs. digital signals. It is one of the three primary signal characteristics, alongside **Frequency** and **Phase**, that are modulated to encode information onto a carrier wave.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding one end of a rope, and the other end is tied to a wall. You move your hand **up and down** to create waves that travel along the rope.

- If you move your hand **only a little** (small up-and-down motion), the waves are tiny → **small amplitude**.
- If you move your hand **vigorously** (large up-and-down motion), the waves are tall → **large amplitude**.

In this analogy:
- The **rope** represents the transmission medium (e.g., copper wire, optical fiber, or air).
- The **height of each wave crest** above the rest position of the rope represents the **amplitude**.
- The number of waves you create per second is the **frequency**.
- The loudness of the signal reaching the receiver is directly tied to amplitude.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** Amplitude simply means "how big is the wave?" A bigger amplitude means a stronger signal, while a smaller amplitude means a weaker signal. In radio communications, a higher amplitude means your voice or music is transmitted louder; in digital data, a change in amplitude can represent binary 1s and 0s.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally adopted in KTU-evaluated data communication problems:

- **Peak Amplitude ($A_p$ or $A_{max}$)** — measured in **Volts (V)**
- **Peak-to-Peak Amplitude ($V_{pp}$)** — measured in **Volts (V)**, defined as $2 \times A_p$
- **Root Mean Square Amplitude ($A_{rms}$)** — measured in **Volts (V)**, used for power calculations
- **Signal Power in decibels:** $P_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P}{P_{ref}}\right)$ where $P_{ref}$ is typically **1 milliwatt (mW)** for dBm, or **1 Watt (W)** for dBW.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference:** Amplitude in dBm uses the reference power $P_{ref} = 1$ mW. The formula is $P(\text{dBm}) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P(\text{mW})}{1\,\text{mW}}\right)$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

A signal in a data communication system is a time-varying electromagnetic or electrical quantity used to convey information. Amplitude describes one critical dimension of this variation. The operational understanding can be broken into the following logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Establish the Reference (Zero) Axis:** Every signal oscillates around a baseline. For an electrical signal transmitted over a wire, the baseline is the DC offset (often **0 V** or ground).
- **Step 2 — Identify the Extreme Value:** Amplitude is the **maximum displacement** from this baseline, regardless of whether the displacement is positive (crest) or negative (trough).
- **Step 3 — Classify the Signal Type:** For a **sinusoidal** (analog) signal, amplitude is constant only when unmodulated. For a **digital** signal (square wave or pulse), amplitude switches between discrete levels (e.g., **+5 V** for binary 1 and **0 V** for binary 0).
- **Step 4 — Apply to Information Encoding:** Varying amplitude over time is the principle behind **Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK)**, a digital modulation technique where binary data is encoded by changing the carrier's amplitude.
- **Step 5 — Relate Amplitude to Power:** Because power is proportional to the square of voltage ($P = V^2 / R$), even a small change in amplitude produces a significant change in transmitted power.

### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step

- **Why a reference axis?** All signal analysis in KTU problems is relative. Without a defined zero, amplitude is meaningless.
- **Why both crest and trough?** A signal can swing symmetrically or asymmetrically. KTU exam questions often test whether students correctly identify **peak**, **peak-to-peak**, and **RMS** values.
- **Why does amplitude matter?** In real channels, amplitude **attenuates** (loses strength) with distance. Engineers must design repeaters/amplifiers to restore amplitude.
- **How is amplitude measured practically?** Using an **oscilloscope** for time-domain visualization, or a **spectrum analyzer** for frequency-domain analysis.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes **every** amplitude-related formula that may appear in KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examinations for OECST612 Module 1.

| Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sinusoidal Signal | $s(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t + \phi)$ | $A$: amplitude (V), $f$: frequency (Hz), $\phi$: phase (rad) | $A > 0$ always; $A$ is non-negative by convention |
| Peak Amplitude | $A_p = \max \vert s(t) \vert$ | Volts (V) | Equal to $A$ for pure sinusoid |
| Peak-to-Peak Amplitude | $V_{pp} = A_{max} - A_{min} = 2A_p$ | Volts (V) | For symmetric signals only |
| RMS Amplitude | $A_{rms} = \frac{A_p}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Volts (V) | Valid for pure sinusoids only |
| Signal Power (DC) | $P = \frac{V^2}{R}$ | $P$: power (W), $V$: voltage (V), $R$: resistance ($\Omega$) | $R$ is the load impedance, typically **50 $\Omega$** in RF systems |
| Signal Power (AC, sinusoidal) | $P = \frac{V_{rms}^2}{R} = \frac{A_p^2}{2R}$ | Watts (W) | For matched load |
| Power in dBm | $P(\text{dBm}) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P(\text{mW})}{1}\right)$ | dBm | Reference: **1 mW** |
| Power in dBW | $P(\text{dBW}) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P(\text{W})}{1}\right)$ | dBW | Reference: **1 W** |
| Voltage in dBV | $V(\text{dBV}) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V}{1\,\text{V}}\right)$ | dBV | Reference: **1 V** |
| Amplitude (dB form) | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)$ | dB | Ratio of two amplitudes |
| Attenuation | $\alpha_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)$ | dB / unit length | Negative value indicates loss |
| Bit Energy from Amplitude | $E_b = A^2 \cdot T_b / R$ | Joules (J) | $T_b$: bit duration (s) |
| SNR | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | dB | Critical for amplitude-based detection |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** Cellular base stations vary the **amplitude of radio waves** to encode voice and data (e.g., 4G/5G QAM modulation).
- **Optical Fiber Communication:** Light pulses sent through fibers have amplitude variations detected by **photodiodes** at the receiver.
- **Audio Engineering:** Microphones convert sound pressure into voltage; the **amplitude of that voltage** is what represents loudness.
- **Seismic & Medical Imaging:** Ultrasound and seismic data rely on analyzing amplitude reflections from tissues or geological layers.
- **Wi-Fi & Bluetooth:** Use amplitude and phase variations across multiple subcarriers (OFDM) to transmit data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall to Avoid:** Students often confuse **amplitude** (V) with **power** (W). Remember: power is proportional to amplitude **squared**. Doubling the amplitude quadruples the power. This is a frequent source of lost marks in numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Peak, Peak-to-Peak, and RMS Amplitude for a Sinusoid

This derivation is essential for KTU problems where you are given one form of amplitude and must convert to another.

**Given:** A pure sinusoidal signal $s(t) = A_p \sin(2\pi f t)$ where $A_p$ is the peak amplitude.

**Step 1 — Identify the Maximum Value:**

By the properties of the sine function, $\sin(\theta)$ achieves its maximum value of **1** at $\theta = \pi/2$, and its minimum of **-1** at $\theta = 3\pi/2$. Therefore:

$$s(t)_{max} = A_p \cdot 1 = A_p$$

$$s(t)_{min} = A_p \cdot (-1) = -A_p$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Peak-to-Peak Amplitude:**

The peak-to-peak amplitude is the difference between the maximum and minimum values of the signal.

$$V_{pp} = s(t)_{max} - s(t)_{min} = A_p - (-A_p) = A_p + A_p = 2A_p$$

**Step 3 — Derive the RMS Amplitude (Mean Square):**

The RMS (Root Mean Square) value is calculated by squaring the signal, finding its mean over one full period $T$, and then taking the square root.

$$A_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T [s(t)]^2 \, dt}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Signal Expression:**

$$A_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T [A_p \sin(2\pi f t)]^2 \, dt}$$

$$A_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{A_p^2}{T}\int_0^T \sin^2(2\pi f t) \, dt}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Trigonometric Identity:**

Using the half-angle identity $\sin^2(\theta) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$A_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{A_p^2}{T}\int_0^T \frac{1 - \cos(4\pi f t)}{2} \, dt}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the Integral:**

The integral of a constant over a period is the constant times the period, and the integral of $\cos(4\pi f t)$ over a complete period is **0**.

$$A_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{A_p^2}{T} \cdot \frac{T}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{A_p^2}{2}}$$

$$A_{rms} = \frac{A_p}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 7 — Decimal Approximation:**

Since $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.4142$:

$$A_{rms} \approx 0.7071 \cdot A_p$$

This means the RMS amplitude of a pure sinusoid is approximately **70.71%** of its peak amplitude.

### 3.2 Derivation: Average Power Delivered by a Sinusoidal Signal

This derivation is critical for KTU problems on dB conversion and signal-to-noise ratio.

**Given:** A sinusoidal voltage $v(t) = A_p \sin(2\pi f t)$ applied across a resistive load $R$.

**Step 1 — Instantaneous Power:**

By Ohm's law, the instantaneous power dissipated in the resistor is:

$$p(t) = \frac{[v(t)]^2}{R} = \frac{A_p^2 \sin^2(2\pi f t)}{R}$$

**Step 2 — Average Power Over One Period:**

The average power is the integral of instantaneous power over one period, divided by the period.

$$P_{avg} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T p(t) \, dt = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T \frac{A_p^2 \sin^2(2\pi f t)}{R} \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Pull Out the Constants:**

Since $A_p$ and $R$ do not depend on time, they can be moved outside the integral.

$$P_{avg} = \frac{A_p^2}{R \cdot T}\int_0^T \sin^2(2\pi f t) \, dt$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral (using previous result):**

From Section 3.1, Step 6:

$$\int_0^T \sin^2(2\pi f t) \, dt = \frac{T}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute and Simplify:**

$$P_{avg} = \frac{A_p^2}{R \cdot T} \cdot \frac{T}{2} = \frac{A_p^2}{2R}$$

**Step 6 — Express in Terms of RMS Voltage:**

Since $V_{rms} = \frac{A_p}{\sqrt{2}}$, we can write:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{V_{rms}^2}{R}$$

This is the standard form used universally in data communication textbooks.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A communication channel transmits a sinusoidal signal with peak amplitude $A_p = 10$ V across a **50 $\Omega$** load. Calculate: (a) the peak-to-peak amplitude, (b) the RMS amplitude, and (c) the average power delivered. Also express the power in dBm.

**Solution (a) — Peak-to-Peak Amplitude:**

$$V_{pp} = 2 A_p = 2 \times 10 = 20 \text{ V}$$

**Solution (b) — RMS Amplitude:**

$$A_{rms} = \frac{A_p}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{10}{1.4142} \approx 7.071 \text{ V}$$

**Solution (c) — Average Power:**

$$P_{avg} = \frac{A_p^2}{2R} = \frac{(10)^2}{2 \times 50} = \frac{100}{100} = 1 \text{ W}$$

**Solution (d) — Power in dBm:**

$$P(\text{dBm}) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P(\text{mW})}{1}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1000) = 30 \text{ dBm}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Amplitude in a Data Communication System

The following diagram illustrates how amplitude is processed from the source through the channel to the receiver, highlighting the key amplitude-related parameters at each stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    srcA[Information Source Voice Data Text] --> encA[Source Encoder Bits Generated]
    encA --> modA[Modulator Carrier Wave with Amplitude A]
    modA --> chanA[Transmission Channel Attenuation alpha]
    chanA --> ampA[Optional Repeater Amplifier Gain G]
    ampA --> demA[Demodulator Amplitude Detected]
    demA --> decA[Destination Decoder Original Info]
    
    srcA -.amplitude characteristic.-> modA
    modA -.ASK OOK QAM.-> chanA
    chanA -.amplitude decay.-> ampA
    ampA -.amplitude restoration.-> demA
    demA -.amplitude threshold.-> decA
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Signal Classification Diagram

This diagram shows how amplitude-based classification differentiates analog and digital signals in a data communication system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sigA[Signal s of t] --> qA{Is amplitude continuous?}
    qA -- YES --> anaA[Analog Signal Example Sine Wave]
    qA -- NO --> qB{How many discrete levels?}
    qB -- TWO --> digA[Digital Binary Signal Levels 0 and A]
    qB -- MORE --> digB[Digital M-ary Signal Multiple Levels]
    
    anaA -.amplitude A.-> anaB[Constant Peak Amplitude]
    digA -.amplitude A.-> digB1[Switches Between 0 and A]
    digB -.amplitude A1 to An.-> digC[Each Level Represents Log2 n Bits]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK) Modulation

This block-level functional architecture shows how amplitude modulation is implemented for digital data transmission.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    binA[Binary Input Data Stream] --> mulA[Multiplier Input]
    oscA[Carrier Oscillator Cosine Wave] --> mulA[Multiplier Carrier]
    mulA --> outA[ASK Modulated Signal s of t equals A times d times Cosine 2 pi f t]
    outA --> chanB[Channel]
    chanB --> detA[Envelope Detector]
    detA --> thrA[Threshold Comparator]
    thrA --> recA[Recovered Binary Data]
    
    binA -.binary 1.-> mulA
    binA -.binary 0.-> mulA
    mulA -.amplitude A on.-> outA
    mulA -.amplitude 0 off.-> outA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU Exams:** When asked to "draw the block diagram of an ASK modulator/demodulator," the above structure is the standard expected answer. Always include the **carrier oscillator**, **multiplier**, and **envelope detector** in your sketches.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**

Define the term **Amplitude** as used in data communication. Differentiate between **peak amplitude** and **RMS amplitude** for a sinusoidal signal.

**Model Answer:**

**Amplitude** is the maximum instantaneous magnitude of a signal measured from its equilibrium (zero reference) level. It represents the strength or intensity of the signal and is measured in Volts (V) for electrical signals.

**Peak Amplitude ($A_p$):** This is the maximum absolute value the signal attains during one cycle. For a pure sinusoid $s(t) = A_p \sin(2\pi f t)$, the peak amplitude is simply $A_p$.

**RMS Amplitude ($A_{rms}$):** The Root Mean Square amplitude is the square root of the mean of the squared signal values over one period. It represents the equivalent DC value that would deliver the same average power.

For a sinusoid, the relationship is:

$$A_{rms} = \frac{A_p}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071 \cdot A_p$$

The RMS value is used in **power calculations**, while peak amplitude is used to specify equipment **voltage limits** (e.g., maximum voltage a circuit can tolerate without damage).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]**

Explain the significance of amplitude in **Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK)**. Why is ASK sensitive to noise?

**Model Answer:**

In **Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK)**, digital binary data is encoded by switching the amplitude of a high-frequency carrier signal between two discrete levels:
- Binary **1** → Carrier transmitted with amplitude $A$ (ON state)
- Binary **0** → Carrier suppressed (amplitude = 0, OFF state)

Mathematically, the ASK signal is:

$$s(t) = \begin{cases} A \cos(2\pi f_c t), & \text{for binary 1} \\ 0, & \text{for binary 0} \end{cases}$$

**Significance:** ASK is one of the simplest digital modulation techniques and is used in low-cost, low-data-rate applications like **optical communication (on-off keying)** and early **modem standards**.

**Noise Sensitivity:** ASK is highly sensitive to channel noise because the information is carried entirely in the amplitude. Any random noise added to the signal alters its amplitude, which can cause the receiver to misinterpret a binary 0 as 1 or vice versa. This is why ASK has the **poorest noise immunity** among PSK, FSK, and ASK. The **bit error rate (BER)** of ASK increases sharply as the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** decreases.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between **peak amplitude**, **peak-to-peak amplitude**, and **RMS amplitude** for a pure sinusoidal signal. State clearly the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A communication system uses a sinusoidal carrier with peak amplitude of **15 V** and frequency **10 kHz** across a load of **75 $\Omega$**. Calculate: (i) RMS voltage, (ii) Average power in Watts, (iii) Power in dBm, and (iv) Power in dBW. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Assumptions:**
- The signal is a **pure sinusoid** of the form $s(t) = A_p \sin(2\pi f t)$.
- The signal is **symmetric** about the zero axis (no DC offset).
- The analysis is performed over **one complete period** $T = 1/f$.

**Derivation of Peak-to-Peak Amplitude:**

The maximum value of the sinusoid is $s(t)_{max} = A_p$ (when $\sin = 1$) and the minimum is $s(t)_{min} = -A_p$ (when $\sin = -1$).

$$V_{pp} = s(t)_{max} - s(t)_{min} = A_p - (-A_p) = 2A_p$$

**Derivation of RMS Amplitude:**

$$A_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T [A_p \sin(2\pi f t)]^2 \, dt}$$

Using $\sin^2(\theta) = \frac{1 - \cos(2\theta)}{2}$:

$$A_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{A_p^2}{T} \cdot \frac{T}{2}} = \frac{A_p}{\sqrt{2}}$$

**Final Relationship Summary:**

$$V_{pp} = 2A_p \quad \text{and} \quad A_{rms} = \frac{A_p}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071 \cdot A_p$$

---

**Model Solution (b):**

Given: $A_p = 15$ V, $f = 10$ kHz, $R = 75 \, \Omega$.

**(i) RMS Voltage:**

$$A_{rms} = \frac{A_p}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{15}{1.4142} \approx 10.607 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Average Power in Watts:**

$$P_{avg} = \frac{A_p^2}{2R} = \frac{(15)^2}{2 \times 75} = \frac{225}{150} = 1.5 \text{ W}$$

**(iii) Power in dBm:**

Convert to milliwatts: $P = 1.5 \text{ W} = 1500$ mW.

$$P(\text{dBm}) = 10 \log_{10}(1500) = 10 \times 3.176 = 31.76 \text{ dBm}$$

**(iv) Power in dBW:**

$$P(\text{dBW}) = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) = 10 \times 0.176 = 1.76 \text{ dBW}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Question]**

**(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the working of an **Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK) modulator and demodulator**. Write the mathematical expression for the ASK signal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concepts of **signal attenuation** and **signal amplification** in a communication channel. A signal with power **20 dBm** is transmitted through a cable that introduces **-3 dB** attenuation. Find the received power in dBm and in milliwatts. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Block Diagram of ASK Modulator:**

```
Binary Data --> Multiplier <-- Carrier Oscillator cos(2 pi f_c t)
                          |
                          v
                  ASK Modulated Signal s(t)
```

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$s(t) = \begin{cases} A \cos(2\pi f_c t), & \text{when binary input} = 1 \\ 0, & \text{when binary input} = 0 \end{cases}$$

This can also be written compactly as:

$$s(t) = b(t) \cdot A \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

where $b(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ is the binary data signal.

**Working Principle:** The carrier oscillator produces a continuous high-frequency cosine wave. The multiplier (a switch/modulator) either allows the carrier to pass (for binary 1) or blocks it (for binary 0). The output is a burst-modulated RF signal.

**Block Diagram of ASK Demodulator:**

```
Received ASK Signal --> Bandpass Filter --> Envelope Detector --> Threshold Comparator --> Recovered Binary Data
```

**Demodulation Principle:** The bandpass filter removes out-of-band noise. The envelope detector (typically a diode detector followed by a low-pass filter) recovers the amplitude envelope, which is then compared to a threshold (e.g., $A/2$) to decide whether the received bit is 1 or 0.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Signal Attenuation:** Attenuation is the **gradual loss of signal amplitude (or power)** as it propagates through a transmission medium. It is caused by resistance in copper wires, scattering in optical fibers, and free-space path loss in wireless channels. It is measured in **dB per unit length**.

**Signal Amplification:** Amplification is the process of **boosting a weakened signal** using electronic devices such as **repeaters** (digital) or **analog amplifiers** (operational amplifiers, RF amplifiers). Amplification introduces **noise** and **distortion**, which is why it is used judiciously.

**Numerical Problem:**

Given: $P_{in} = 20$ dBm, Attenuation = $-3$ dB.

**Received Power in dBm:**

$$P_{out}(\text{dBm}) = P_{in}(\text{dBm}) + \text{Attenuation}(\text{dB}) = 20 + (-3) = 17 \text{ dBm}$$

**Received Power in mW:**

$$P_{out}(\text{mW}) = 10^{P_{out}(\text{dBm})/10} = 10^{17/10} = 10^{1.7} \approx 50.12 \text{ mW}$$

**Verification:** A $-3$ dB attenuation corresponds to **half the power**. Starting from 100 mW (which is 20 dBm), the result should be 50 mW, which matches our calculation of 50.12 mW (minor rounding).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Writing amplitude in Watts instead of Volts, or vice versa. Amplitude is a voltage quantity; power is a derived quantity.
> 2. **dBm vs. dBW mix-up:** dBm uses **1 mW** as reference; dBW uses **1 W**. Forgetting this conversion leads to a **30 dB** error (factor of 1000).
> 3. **Confusing $V_{pp}$ with $V_p$:** Peak-to-peak is **double** the peak for symmetric signals.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in average power formula:** Some students write $P = A^2/R$ instead of $P = A^2/(2R)$. This is the most common KTU valuation pitfall.
> 5. **Treating ASK as noise-immune:** ASK is the **most noise-sensitive** of the basic digital modulation schemes. Writing "ASK is robust against noise" will fetch **zero marks**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Amplitude** = maximum displacement of a signal from its reference/zero level, measured in **Volts (V)**.
- Three forms: **Peak ($A_p$)**, **Peak-to-Peak ($V_{pp} = 2A_p$)**, and **RMS ($A_{rms} = A_p/\sqrt{2}$)**.
- RMS amplitude is used in **power calculations**: $P = V_{rms}^2 / R = A_p^2 / (2R)$.
- Decibel forms: **dBm** (ref. 1 mW), **dBW** (ref. 1 W), **dBV** (ref. 1 V), and the general ratio $20 \log_{10}(A_2/A_1)$.
- Amplitude modulation in digital systems is called **Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK)**, also known as **On-Off Keying (OOK)**.
- ASK is **simple but noise-sensitive**; used in optical fiber and low-cost RF links.
- Channel **attenuation** reduces amplitude; **amplifiers/repeaters** restore it at the cost of added noise.
- **SNR** (Signal-to-Noise Ratio) directly determines the **bit error rate (BER)** in amplitude-based detection.
- For sinusoidal signals, energy per bit $E_b = A^2 T_b / R$, where $T_b$ is the bit duration.
- Remember the **factor of $\sqrt{2}$** between peak and RMS — it is a favorite KTU question topic.
- Always specify the **load impedance $R$** in power calculations; KTU often sets $R = 50 \, \Omega$ or $R = 75 \, \Omega$ for coax cable problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phase
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Phase

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Data Communication**, **Phase** is the position of a waveform relative to a reference point in time, typically expressed in **radians** or **degrees**. For a continuous-time sinusoidal carrier signal used in analog and digital modulation, the general mathematical representation is:

$$s(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$$

where:
- $A$ = Amplitude of the carrier signal (in Volts)
- $f_c$ = Carrier frequency (in **Hertz, Hz**)
- $\phi$ = **Phase** of the carrier signal (in **radians** or **degrees**)
- $2\pi f_c t + \phi$ = Instantaneous phase (in radians)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The **phase** of a signal is the fraction of a complete period elapsed since the last zero-crossing, measured in **radians (0 to 2π)** or **degrees (0° to 360°)**. In data communication, phase forms one of the three primary signal parameters (along with amplitude and frequency) that can be modulated to carry information.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two runners on a **circular 400-metre athletics track**:
- Both runners start at the same line.
- Runner A completes exactly **one full lap** and reaches the start line again — this is a **phase of 0° (or 0 radians)**.
- Runner B is at the **200-metre mark** when Runner A finishes — Runner B is at **half a lap behind**, which is a **phase of 180° (or π radians)**.

If we map the runners' positions to a sinusoidal waveform, the angular position on the circular track directly corresponds to the **phase angle** of the signal at any given moment.

> **[!NOTE]**
> **Real-World Connection:** In telecommunication systems, when you make a phone call or stream a video, the carrier signal carrying your data is a sinusoid. The **phase** of that sinusoid can be deliberately shifted to encode binary 1s and 0s — this is the foundation of **Phase Shift Keying (PSK)**.

### 1.3 Why "Phase" Matters in Data Communication

In digital modulation schemes, **phase** is one of the most reliable signal parameters to manipulate because:
1. **Phase is more robust to noise** than amplitude (noise typically corrupts amplitude first).
2. **Phase allows high data rates** in limited bandwidth (e.g., QPSK carries 2 bits per symbol).
3. **Phase shifts are easy to detect** at the receiver using coherent (synchronous) detection.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Boundary Values:**
> - One full cycle = $2\pi$ radians = $360°$
> - Half cycle = $\pi$ radians = $180°$
> - Quarter cycle = $\pi/2$ radians = $90°$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown: The Anatomy of Phase

The **phase** of a sinusoidal carrier is not a static number — it evolves continuously with time. Let us break down its behavior:

- **Step 1 — Reference Instant ($t = 0$):** The initial phase offset $\phi$ determines where the waveform "starts" on the time axis.
- **Step 2 — Linear Progression:** The instantaneous phase $\theta(t) = 2\pi f_c t + \phi$ increases linearly with time at a rate of $2\pi f_c$ radians per second.
- **Step 3 — Periodic Wrap-Around:** Since a sinusoid is periodic with period $T = 1/f_c$, the phase wraps around every $2\pi$ radians.
- **Step 4 — Phase Difference:** Between two signals of the same frequency, the difference in their phases is **time-invariant** and is what carries information in PSK.

> **Why this matters:** When we transmit a digital bit by changing the **phase** of the carrier, we are essentially choosing one of $M$ possible values of $\phi$ to represent an $M$-ary symbol.

### 2.2 Phase Shift Keying (PSK) — The Key Modulation Scheme

**Phase Shift Keying (PSK)** is a digital modulation technique in which the **phase of the carrier signal is varied** in discrete steps to represent digital data, while the **amplitude and frequency remain constant**.

#### 2.2.1 Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK)

In **BPSK**, the phase is shifted between **two values** separated by $180°$ ($\pi$ radians):
- Binary **'0'** → Phase = $0$ (or $\pi$)
- Binary **'1'** → Phase = $\pi$ (or $0$)

The mathematical representation of a BPSK signal is:

$$s(t) = \begin{cases} +A \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{for binary '1'} \\ -A \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{for binary '0'} \end{cases}$$

Equivalently, this can be compactly written as:

$$s(t) = b(t) \cdot A \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

where $b(t) \in \{+1, -1\}$ represents the binary data.

#### 2.2.2 Quadrature Phase Shift Keying (QPSK)

In **QPSK**, the phase is shifted among **four values** separated by $90°$ ($\pi/2$ radians), allowing **2 bits per symbol**:

| Dibit (Bits) | Phase Shift | In-Phase (I) | Quadrature (Q) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 00 | $0°$ | $+1$ | $0$ |
| 01 | $90°$ | $0$ | $+1$ |
| 10 | $180°$ | $-1$ | $0$ |
| 11 | $270°$ | $0$ | $-1$ |

The QPSK signal is expressed as:

$$s(t) = A \cos\left(2\pi f_c t + \frac{\pi}{4}(2i + 1)\right), \quad i = 0, 1, 2, 3$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all high-yield formulas** for "Phase" and PSK-related problems. Memorize these for the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | General sinusoidal signal | $s(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$ | $\phi$ in radians |
| 2 | Phase–Time conversion | $\phi = 2\pi f_c \cdot \Delta t$ | $\Delta t$ in seconds |
| 3 | Time–Phase conversion | $\Delta t = \dfrac{\phi}{2\pi f_c}$ | Returns time offset |
| 4 | BPSK signal | $s(t) = b(t) \cdot A \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | $b(t) \in \{-1, +1\}$ |
| 5 | QPSK signal | $s(t) = I(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) - Q(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ | $I, Q \in \{-1, +1\}$ |
| 6 | M-ary PSK signal | $s_i(t) = A\cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \dfrac{2\pi i}{M}\right)$ | $i = 0, 1, \dots, M-1$ |
| 7 | Bit rate → Symbol rate (M-PSK) | $R_s = \dfrac{R_b}{\log_2 M}$ | Symbols per second (Baud) |
| 8 | Bandwidth efficiency (M-PSK) | $\eta = \dfrac{\log_2 M}{1 + \alpha}$ | $\alpha$ = roll-off factor |
| 9 | BPSK minimum distance | $d_{\min} = 2\sqrt{E_b}$ | $E_b$ = energy per bit |
| 10 | BPSK theoretical BER | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | Coherent detection |
| 11 | Phase wrapping rule | $\phi_{\text{eff}} = \phi \pmod{2\pi}$ | Always in $[0, 2\pi)$ |
| 12 | In-phase component | $I = A\cos\phi$ | Projects on x-axis |
| 13 | Quadrature component | $Q = A\sin\phi$ | Projects on y-axis |
| 14 | Resultant amplitude from I, Q | $A = \sqrt{I^2 + Q^2}$ | Phasor magnitude |
| 15 | Phase from I, Q | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{Q}{I}\right)$ | Use `atan2(Q, I)` in code |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Phase-based modulation is the **backbone of nearly every modern wireless standard**:
- **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11):** Uses BPSK, QPSK, **16-QAM**, **64-QAM**, **256-QAM**.
- **4G LTE / 5G NR:** High-order **PSK and QAM** for spectral efficiency.
- **Satellite communication (DVB-S2):** Uses QPSK, 8-PSK, 16-APSK.
- **GPS:** Uses BPSK on multiple carrier frequencies.
- **Bluetooth:** Uses **GFSK** (frequency modulation, related concept).

> **Engineering Insight:** Higher-order PSK (e.g., 8-PSK, 16-PSK) packs more bits per symbol but the **minimum distance between constellation points shrinks**, making the system more susceptible to noise.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From a Sinusoid to BPSK Signal

**Given:** A carrier signal $c(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and a binary data stream $b(t) \in \{+1, -1\}$.

**To Derive:** The BPSK modulated signal $s(t)$ and its spectrum.

**Step 1 — Define the binary data signal.**
The digital bit stream is mapped to a bipolar NRZ (Non-Return-to-Zero) waveform:

$$b(t) = \begin{cases} +1 & \text{for binary 1} \\ -1 & \text{for binary 0} \end{cases}$$

This mapping is done so that the average DC value of $b(t)$ is approximately zero for long random bit streams.

**Step 2 — Multiply carrier with data.**
The modulated signal is formed by direct multiplication:

$$s(t) = b(t) \cdot A \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

**Step 3 — Separate the two cases explicitly.**

$$s(t) = \begin{cases} (+1) \cdot A \cos(2\pi f_c t) = +A \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{when bit = 1} \\ (-1) \cdot A \cos(2\pi f_c t) = -A \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{when bit = 0} \end{cases}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the phase change.**

$$+A \cos(2\pi f_c t) = A \cos(2\pi f_c t + 0)$$

$$-A \cos(2\pi f_c t) = A \cos(2\pi f_c t + \pi)$$

> **Conclusion:** BPSK changes the carrier phase by exactly **$\pi$ radians (180°)** between the two bit values. The amplitude $|A|$ remains **constant** — this is what makes BPSK a **constant-envelope** modulation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: QPSK Signal Using In-Phase and Quadrature Components

**Given:** An incoming bit stream is grouped into **pairs (dibits)**. Each dibit is mapped to one of 4 phase values.

**Step 1 — Group incoming bits into dibits.**

$$b_n = \{b_0, b_1\} \quad \text{where } b_0 \text{ is the odd bit, } b_1 \text{ is the even bit}$$

**Step 2 — Split into I (In-Phase) and Q (Quadrature) channels.**

$$I(t) = 2b_0 - 1 \quad \in \{-1, +1\}$$

$$Q(t) = 2b_1 - 1 \quad \in \{-1, +1\}$$

**Step 3 — Construct the QPSK signal using the trigonometric identity.**

Using the identity $\cos(A + B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$:

$$s(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi_i)$$

$$\quad = A[\cos(2\pi f_c t)\cos\phi_i - \sin(2\pi f_c t)\sin\phi_i]$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $I$ and $Q$.**
The in-phase component is $A\cos\phi_i = I(t) \cdot \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}}$ and the quadrature component is $A\sin\phi_i = Q(t) \cdot \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}}$. This gives:

$$s(t) = \frac{A}{\sqrt{2}}\left[I(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) - Q(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)\right]$$

> **Conclusion:** QPSK transmits **two independent bits simultaneously** using two orthogonal carriers ($\cos$ and $\sin$). The signal power is split equally between the I and Q rails, each carrying $\frac{A^2}{2}$ watts.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Bit Error Rate (BER) of BPSK

**Step 1 — Define the noise model.**
At the receiver, the received signal is:

$$r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$$

where $n(t)$ is **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)** with two-sided power spectral density $N_0/2$.

**Step 2 — Coherent detection.**
The received signal is multiplied by a locally generated carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and integrated over one bit duration $T_b$:

$$y = \int_0^{T_b} r(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \, dt = \pm \sqrt{E_b} + \int_0^{T_b} n(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)\, dt$$

**Step 3 — Identify the noise variance.**
The variance of the noise term at the integrator output is:

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{N_0}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Q-function.**
The decision variable is Gaussian with mean $\pm\sqrt{E_b}$ and variance $N_0/2$. The probability of error is:

$$P_e = Q\!\left(\frac{\sqrt{E_b}}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

where $Q(x) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_x^{\infty} e^{-u^2/2} \, du$ is the **Marcum Q-function**.

> **Conclusion:** BPSK has a **BER of $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$**. This is the **lowest BER of any binary modulation scheme** in AWGN — another reason BPSK is the gold standard for low-data-rate, high-reliability links (e.g., deep-space communication).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Phase-Based Modulation Schemes

| Parameter | BPSK | QPSK | 8-PSK | 16-PSK |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Number of phases ($M$) | 2 | 4 | 8 | 16 |
| Bits per symbol ($\log_2 M$) | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
| Phase separation (deg) | 180° | 90° | 45° | 22.5° |
| Symbol rate vs Bit rate | $R_s = R_b$ | $R_s = R_b/2$ | $R_s = R_b/3$ | $R_s = R_b/4$ |
| Bandwidth efficiency | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| Noise immunity | Excellent | Very Good | Good | Poor |
| Minimum distance $d_{\min}$ | $2\sqrt{E_s}$ | $\sqrt{2E_s}$ | $\sqrt{2-\sqrt{2}}\sqrt{E_s}$ | $\approx 0.39\sqrt{E_s}$ |
| BER (approx, coherent) | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | Higher | Highest |
| Typical applications | GPS, deep-space | Satellite, cable modem | DVB-S2, legacy links | Rarely used (QAM preferred) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Concept Map: Phase in a Sinusoidal Signal

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the geometric interpretation of phase in a carrier waveform.

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA["Sinusoidal Carrier Signal"]
    freqA["Frequency f_c determines cycles per second"]
    ampA["Amplitude A determines peak value"]
    phaseA["Phase phi determines starting position on time axis"]
    refA["Reference point t=0 and zero crossing"]
    forwardA["Phase = 0 degrees means cosine starts at peak"]
    shiftA["Phase = 90 degrees means cosine starts at zero"]
    shiftB["Phase = 180 degrees means cosine starts at negative peak"]

    startA --> freqA
    startA --> ampA
    startA --> phaseA
    phaseA --> refA
    refA --> forwardA
    refA --> shiftA
    refA --> shiftB
```

### 4.2 BPSK Transmitter–Receiver Block Diagram

The following diagram shows the high-level functional architecture of a complete BPSK communication link, including modulation, channel, noise addition, and coherent demodulation.

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA["Binary Data Source 0 or 1"]
    nrzA["NRZ Encoder maps 0 to minus 1 and 1 to plus 1"]
    carA["Carrier Oscillator cos of 2 pi f_c t"]
    mulA["Multiplier b of t times A cos of 2 pi f_c t"]
    chanA["AWGN Channel adds noise n of t"]
    recA["Received Signal r of t"]
    locA["Local Carrier cos of 2 pi f_c t"]
    mulB["Multiplier in coherent detector"]
    intA["Integrator over one bit duration T_b"]
    decA["Decision Device threshold at 0 volts"]
    outA["Recovered Binary Output"]

    srcA --> nrzA --> mulA
    carA --> mulA
    mulA --> chanA --> recA
    recA --> mulB
    locA --> mulB
    mulB --> intA --> decA --> outA
```

### 4.3 QPSK Constellation Architecture

The QPSK constellation places symbols uniformly on a circle of radius $A$. Each point represents a unique dibit.

```mermaid
graph TD
    qpskC["QPSK Constellation Diagram"]
    ptA["Point at 0 degrees represents dibit 00"]
    ptB["Point at 90 degrees represents dibit 01"]
    ptC["Point at 180 degrees represents dibit 10"]
    ptD["Point at 270 degrees represents dibit 11"]
    axisI["Horizontal Axis is In Phase I component"]
    axisQ["Vertical Axis is Quadrature Q component"]
    distA["Minimum Euclidean distance between any two points is A times sqrt 2"]

    qpskC --> ptA
    qpskC --> ptB
    qpskC --> ptC
    qpskC --> ptD
    qpskC --> axisI
    qpskC --> axisQ
    qpskC --> distA
```

### 4.4 Phasor Representation of Phase Shift

A **phasor** is a rotating arrow whose angle with the positive x-axis represents the instantaneous phase. The following diagram conceptualizes how a phase shift rotates the phasor.

```mermaid
graph LR
    phsA["Phasor of Length A rotating at Angular Frequency 2 pi f_c"]
    ang0["At time t equals 0 angle is 0 degrees"]
    ang90["After one quarter period angle is 90 degrees"]
    ang180["After half period angle is 180 degrees"]
    ang270["After three quarter period angle is 270 degrees"]
    ang360["After one full period angle returns to 0 degrees"]

    phsA --> ang0 --> ang90 --> ang180 --> ang270 --> ang360
    ang360 --> ang0
```

> **Diagram Note:** The phasor completes one full **$360°$ revolution per period $T = 1/f_c$**. In PSK, we *freeze* the phasor at one of $M$ discrete angles to encode digital data.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define the term **phase** of a sinusoidal signal. Explain the significance of phase in digital modulation schemes.

**Model Answer:**
The **phase** of a sinusoidal signal $s(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$ is the angular displacement $\phi$ (measured in radians or degrees) of the waveform relative to a reference point in time. It determines *where* the waveform starts on the time axis. In digital modulation schemes such as **Phase Shift Keying (PSK)**, the phase of the carrier is varied in discrete steps to encode binary data. For example, in BPSK, the phase is toggled between $0$ and $\pi$ radians to represent bits '0' and '1'. Phase is preferred over amplitude for modulation in noisy channels because it offers superior noise immunity and constant-envelope transmission, making it suitable for power-efficient nonlinear amplifiers. **(3 marks)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** What is **Phase Shift Keying (PSK)**? Differentiate between BPSK and QPSK.

**Model Answer:**
**Phase Shift Keying (PSK)** is a digital modulation technique in which the **phase of the carrier signal** is varied according to the digital input data, while keeping the amplitude and frequency constant. **BPSK (Binary PSK)** uses 2 phase values ($0°$ and $180°$) to represent 1 bit per symbol and offers the best noise immunity. **QPSK (Quadrature PSK)** uses 4 phase values ($0°$, $90°$, $180°$, $270°$) to represent 2 bits per symbol, doubling the spectral efficiency at the cost of reduced noise margin. QPSK is widely used in satellite and wireless systems. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the generation and detection of a **BPSK signal**. Derive the expression for the BPSK waveform. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Generation:**
A BPSK modulator consists of:
1. A **carrier oscillator** producing $c(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
2. A **bipolar NRZ encoder** mapping binary '0' to $-1$ and binary '1' to $+1$, producing $b(t) \in \{-1, +1\}$.
3. A **balanced multiplier** (e.g., a ring modulator) that multiplies $b(t)$ with the carrier.

**Mathematical Derivation:**

$$s(t) = b(t) \cdot A \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

$$\quad = \begin{cases} +A \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{for bit 1} \\ -A \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{for bit 0} \end{cases}$$

Using $\cos(\theta + \pi) = -\cos\theta$, we write:

$$s(t) = A \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \phi_i\right), \quad \phi_i \in \{0, \pi\}$$

**Detection (Coherent Demodulation):**
1. Multiply the received signal $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ by a locally generated carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
2. Integrate the product over one bit duration $T_b$.
3. Compare the integrator output with a threshold of 0 V to decide the bit.

$$y = \int_0^{T_b} r(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t)\, dt = \pm \sqrt{E_b} + \text{noise term}$$

A positive output indicates '1'; a negative output indicates '0'. **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** A BPSK system transmits data at a bit rate of $R_b = 1$ Mbps over an AWGN channel with noise power spectral density $N_0/2 = 10^{-9}$ W/Hz. The transmitted signal energy per bit is $E_b = 10^{-5}$ J. Calculate the **probability of bit error** $P_e$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recall the BPSK BER formula.**

$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the SNR argument.**

$$\frac{2E_b}{N_0} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-5}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = 10^4$$

**Step 3 — Take the square root.**

$$\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}} = \sqrt{10^4} = 100$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the Q-function.**
For large $x$, $Q(x) \approx \dfrac{1}{x\sqrt{2\pi}}\, e^{-x^2/2}$. At $x = 100$:

$$P_e = Q(100) \approx 0 \quad (\text{negligibly small})$$

> **Conclusion:** With $E_b/N_0 = 5000$ (i.e., 37 dB), the BPSK system has an essentially **error-free** link. The bit rate does not directly enter the BER formula — it affects the required **bandwidth** ($BW = R_b$ for BPSK). **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **QPSK modulation scheme** with a constellation diagram. Derive the expression for the QPSK signal in terms of in-phase and quadrature components. How many bits per symbol does QPSK transmit? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:** QPSK uses **four carrier phases** ($0°, 90°, 180°, 270°$) to represent **2 bits per symbol** (dibits). This doubles the data rate compared to BPSK within the same bandwidth.

**Constellation Diagram:** Four equally spaced points on a circle of radius $A$, located at the four axis intersections.

| Dibit | Phase |
|:---:|:---:|
| 00 | $0°$ |
| 01 | $90°$ |
| 10 | $180°$ |
| 11 | $270°$ |

**Derivation:**
The QPSK signal is:

$$s(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi_i), \quad \phi_i \in \left\{0, \frac{\pi}{2}, \pi, \frac{3\pi}{2}\right\}$$

Expanding using $\cos(A+B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$:

$$s(t) = A \cos\phi_i \cos(2\pi f_c t) - A \sin\phi_i \sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

Defining the in-phase component $I = A \cos\phi_i$ and the quadrature component $Q = A \sin\phi_i$:

$$\boxed{s(t) = I(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) - Q(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)}$$

For QPSK, $I, Q \in \left\{+\frac{A}{\sqrt{2}}, -\frac{A}{\sqrt{2}}\right\}$. **QPSK transmits 2 bits per symbol.** **(7 marks)**

---

**(b)** Compare **BPSK, QPSK, and 8-PSK** in terms of (i) bits per symbol, (ii) bandwidth efficiency, (iii) noise immunity, and (iv) one real-world application each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | BPSK | QPSK | 8-PSK |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **(i) Bits/symbol** | 1 | 2 | 3 |
| **(ii) Bandwidth efficiency** | 1 bps/Hz | 2 bps/Hz | 3 bps/Hz |
| **(iii) Noise immunity** | Excellent (best) | Very Good | Moderate |
| **(iv) Application** | GPS, deep-space probes | Satellite TV (DVB-S), LTE | Legacy microwave links, DVB-S2 |

**Key Insight:** As the number of phases $M$ increases, the **bandwidth efficiency** rises logarithmically ($\log_2 M$), but the **Euclidean distance between adjacent constellation points** shrinks, increasing the BER. This is why **16-PSK and higher are rarely used** — QAM (combining amplitude and phase modulation) is preferred for high-order constellations. **(7 marks)**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing degrees with radians:** Always use radians in mathematical formulas ($\pi$, $2\pi$, $\pi/2$). Use degrees only for diagram labels. Mixing them costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the carrier in BPSK signal:** Many students write $s(t) = b(t)$ instead of $s(t) = b(t) \cdot A \cos(2\pi f_c t)$. The carrier is mandatory.
> 3. **Drawing the constellation wrong for QPSK:** Points must be on the **axes** ($0°, 90°, 180°, 270°$) — *not* on the diagonals ($45°, 135°, \dots$). On-axis placement uses Gray coding conventions and is the KTU-recommended diagram.
> 4. **Using BER formula for QPSK without justification:** QPSK has the *same BER as BPSK* in AWGN (per bit), but **only** because the symbol energy $E_s = 2E_b$. Students often quote $Q(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$ which is **incorrect** — the correct argument is $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ per bit, or $Q(\sqrt{2E_s/N_0})$ per symbol.
> 5. **Not drawing block diagrams in Part B:** KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for a clean block diagram in modulation questions. **Always include one.**

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **High-Density Revision Checklist:**
> - **Phase** = angular position of a sinusoid, measured in **radians (0 to 2π)** or **degrees (0° to 360°)**.
> - General signal form: $s(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi)$ — here $\phi$ is the phase.
> - **BPSK:** 2 phases (0, π), 1 bit/symbol, best noise immunity, BER = $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$.
> - **QPSK:** 4 phases (0, π/2, π, 3π/2), 2 bits/symbol, $s(t) = I\cos(2\pi f_c t) - Q\sin(2\pi f_c t)$.
> - **M-PSK symbol rate:** $R_s = R_b / \log_2 M$ (Baud).
> - Higher-order PSK = higher spectral efficiency = lower noise margin (trade-off).
> - **In-phase component:** $I = A\cos\phi$; **Quadrature component:** $Q = A\sin\phi$.
> - Phase to time: $\Delta t = \phi / (2\pi f_c)$.
> - Coherent detection is required for PSK (needs phase-synchronized local oscillator).
> - **Gray coding** is used in practice to map dibits to phases so that adjacent symbols differ by only 1 bit (minimizes BER).
> - QPSK constellation points lie on the **axes** (not diagonals) in the standard KTU convention.
> - Applications: **GPS = BPSK**, **Satellite TV = QPSK**, **5G NR = QPSK/16-QAM/64-QAM**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wavelength
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Wavelength ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the context of **Data Communication** and electromagnetic signal propagation, **Wavelength** (denoted by the Greek letter **lambda, $\lambda$**) is formally defined as the *spatial distance between two successive, identical points (peaks, troughs, or zero-crossings) on a continuous sinusoidal electromagnetic wave, measured along the direction of propagation.* > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Wavelength is a foundational periodic-domain parameter that links **time-domain properties (Frequency, Period)** to the **spatial-domain behavior** of a propagating electromagnetic carrier. It governs antenna sizing, optical fiber channel selection, and bandwidth allocation in WDM systems. The **SI unit of wavelength** is the **meter (m)**. However, in data communication practice, sub-multiples are overwhelmingly used: | Wavelength Regime | Practical Unit | Typical Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Radio / Microwave | cm, mm | Wireless LAN, Satellite uplink | | Infrared / Optical | nanometer (nm) = $10^{-9}$ m | Fiber-optic communication | | Visible light | nm (400–700 nm) | Experimental VLC (Visible Light Communication) | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Speed of Light Constant (in vacuum):** > **$c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s** = $3 \times 10^{5}$ km/s = $3 \times 10^{10}$ cm/s --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a long rope tied to a wall. You shake the free end **up and down** rhythmically. The wavy pattern you see travelling towards the wall has a series of **crests (high points)** and **troughs (low points)**. * The **distance from one crest to the very next crest** is the **wavelength**. * If you shake the rope **faster** (more times per second), the crests are packed closer together → wavelength **decreases**. * If you shake the rope **slowly**, the crests spread apart → wavelength **increases**. > [!NOTE] > **Key Intuitive Takeaway:** Wavelength is essentially the "**length of one complete wave cycle**" as it travels through the medium. Every wave—sound, water, light, radio—has one. For an **electromagnetic wave** travelling through a transmission medium (copper wire, optical fiber, or free space), this same geometric concept applies: wavelength tells us **how physically long one cycle of the carrier signal is** at any given instant. --- ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Metrics (Highlighted in Bold) * **Speed of light in vacuum, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s** * **Speed of light in optical fiber (approx), $v \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s** (refractive index $n \approx 1.5$) * **1 nanometer = $10^{-9}$ m** * **1 micrometer = $10^{-6}$ m** * **Optical fiber communication windows:** 850 nm, 1310 nm, 1550 nm (C-band standard) --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: The Wave Equation Any one-dimensional sinusoidal electromagnetic wave propagating in the +x direction can be expressed as: $$y(x, t) = A \sin\left(2\pi ft - \frac{2\pi x}{\lambda} + \phi\right)$$ Where each symbol carries a precise physical meaning: * **$A$** → Amplitude (signal strength / field intensity) * **$f$** → Frequency in Hertz (cycles per second) * **$t$** → Time in seconds * **$x$** → Position along propagation axis in meters * **$\lambda$** → Wavelength in meters * **$\phi$** → Phase constant (initial offset) * **$2\pi f$** → Angular frequency, $\omega$ * **$\frac{2\pi}{\lambda}$** → Angular wavenumber, $k$ --- ### 2.2 The Fundamental Wave Relationship (The "Why" Behind It) A wave travels a distance of exactly **one wavelength** during the time interval of **one period** ($T = 1/f$). * **"How" it travels:** In time $T$, the wave advances by distance $\lambda$. * **"Why" it matters:** Therefore, average speed = distance / time: $$v = \frac{\lambda}{T} = \lambda f$$ For electromagnetic waves in vacuum, $v = c$, giving the most-cited equation in the entire module: $$\boxed{c = \lambda f}$$ For a medium with refractive index $n$, the wave slows down: $$v = \frac{c}{n} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \lambda_{\text{medium}} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{vacuum}}}{n}$$ The **frequency $f$ never changes** when light enters a new medium — only wavelength and speed do. --- ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | LaTeX Form | Variables & Units | Application / Boundary Condition | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Wave Speed | $v = \lambda f$ | $v$ in m/s, $\lambda$ in m, $f$ in Hz | Universal for all wave types | | 2 | Speed of light in vacuum | $c = \lambda f$ | $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s | EM waves in free space | | 3 | Wavelength from frequency | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | $\lambda$ in m | Computing wavelength from RF carrier | | 4 | Wavelength in a medium | $\lambda_{\text{med}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{\text{vac}}}{n}$ | $n$ = refractive index (dimensionless) | Fiber optics ($n \approx 1.46$–$1.48$) | | 5 | Period relation | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | $T$ in seconds | Time-domain to frequency-domain conversion | | 6 | Wavenumber | $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$ | $k$ in rad/m | Used in phase calculations | | 7 | Optical window conversion | $f(\text{THz}) = \dfrac{c}{\lambda(\mu\text{m})}$ | convenient for 1550 nm | WDM channel spacing | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Condition to Memorize:** Frequency $f$ is an **intrinsic property of the source** and remains invariant. Wavelength $\lambda$ depends on the **propagation medium**. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility in Data Communication 1. **Optical Fiber Communication:** Different wavelengths exhibit different loss characteristics. The 1550 nm window has the lowest attenuation ($\approx 0.2$ dB/km) and is the industry standard for long-haul links. The 1310 nm window has zero chromatic dispersion. 2. **Wavelength Division Multiplexing (WDM):** Multiple independent data streams are transmitted simultaneously on different wavelengths (e.g., 1530 nm, 1531 nm, 1532 nm …) through a single fiber — each wavelength acts as a "virtual wire." 3. **Antenna Engineering:** Antenna physical length is typically a fraction (often $\lambda/2$ or $\lambda/4$) of the carrier wavelength — Wi-Fi 2.4 GHz antenna $\approx 3.1$ cm. 4. **Free-Space Optical (FSO) Communication:** Uses 785 nm / 1550 nm lasers for point-to-point terrestrial links. 5. **Visible Light Communication (Li-Fi):** Uses 380–780 nm wavelength range from LEDs as data carriers. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation #1 — From the Sinusoidal Wave Equation to $v = \lambda f$ We begin with the formal sinusoidal wave equation (snapshot of a wave at position $x$, time $t$): $$y(x, t) = A \sin(kx - \omega t + \phi)$$ **Step 1 — Identify the spatial repeat distance.** For a fixed time $t$, the argument of sine must advance by $2\pi$ to repeat the same waveform value. Thus: $$kx + 2\pi = k(x + \lambda) \quad \Rightarrow \quad k\lambda = 2\pi$$ Solving for $k$: $$k = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}$$ **Step 2 — Identify the temporal repeat interval.** For a fixed position $x$, the wave repeats in time when the argument advances by $2\pi$: $$\omega(t + T) - \omega t = 2\pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega T = 2\pi$$ Solving for $\omega$: $$\omega = \frac{2\pi}{T} = 2\pi f$$ **Step 3 — Compute the wave propagation speed.** Wave speed is the distance one full cycle travels per unit time: $$v = \frac{\text{distance of one cycle}}{\text{time of one cycle}} = \frac{\lambda}{T}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $T = 1/f$:** $$v = \lambda \cdot f$$ **Step 5 — Apply to electromagnetic waves in vacuum ($v = c$):** $$\boxed{c = \lambda f}$$ > This derivation is the bedrock of the entire module. In KTU valuation, missing any one of the four steps will cost you 2–3 marks. --- ### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation #2 — Wavelength Inside an Optical Fiber **Step 1 — Define refractive index.** When light enters a denser medium, its speed decreases. Refractive index quantifies this: $$n = \frac{c}{v_{\text{medium}}}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute $v_{\text{medium}} = \lambda_{\text{med}} f$ and $c = \lambda_{\text{vac}} f$.** Crucially, **frequency $f$ is unchanged** because the source's vibration rate is preserved: $$n = \frac{\lambda_{\text{vac}} f}{\lambda_{\text{med}} f} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{vac}}}{\lambda_{\text{med}}}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for the wavelength in the medium:** $$\boxed{\lambda_{\text{med}} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{vac}}}{n}}$$ **Worked Numerical Example:** A laser source emits at vacuum wavelength 1550 nm.
