The fiber has $n = 1.48$. $$\lambda_{\text{fiber}} = \frac{1550 \text{ nm}}{1.48} \approx 1047.3 \text{ nm}$$ **Step 4 — Frequency check (consistency verification):** $$f = \frac{c}{\lambda_{\text{vac}}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{1550 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 1.935 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz} = 193.5 \text{ THz}$$ This is in the **Infrared-C (IR-C) band** of the EM spectrum — the standard telecom band. --- ### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation #3 — Converting Wavelength to Frequency in Optical Windows Optical engineers prefer to express frequency in **THz** and wavelength in **µm**. **Step 1 — Start with $c = \lambda f$.** **Step 2 — Substitute units:** Let $\lambda$ in $\mu\text{m} = 10^{-6}$ m and $f$ in $\text{THz} = 10^{12}$ Hz: $$3 \times 10^{8} = (\lambda \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (f \times 10^{12})$$ **Step 3 — Simplify:** $$3 \times 10^{8} = \lambda \cdot f \cdot 10^{6}$$ $$\lambda \cdot f = 3 \times 10^{2} = 300$$ **Step 4 — Final compact relation:** $$\boxed{f(\text{THz}) = \frac{300}{\lambda(\mu\text{m})}}$$ **Quick check:** For $\lambda = 1.55\,\mu\text{m}$: $$f = \frac{300}{1.55} \approx 193.55 \text{ THz} \;\checkmark$$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Wavelength vs Frequency (Selected EM Bands) | Band | Wavelength Range | Frequency Range | Data Communication Use | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Extremely Low Freq (ELF) | 100,000 km – 10,000 km | 3–30 Hz | Submarine comms | | VLF (Very Low Freq) | 10 km – 100 km | 3–30 kHz | Submarine navigation | | MF / AM Radio | 100 m – 1000 m | 300 kHz – 3 MHz | AM broadcast | | VHF | 1 m – 10 m | 30–300 MHz | FM radio, analog TV | | UHF | 10 cm – 1 m | 300 MHz – 3 GHz | TV, Wi-Fi 2.4 GHz | | SHF (Microwave) | 1 cm – 10 cm | 3–30 GHz | 5G, Radar, Satellite | | EHF (Millimeter) | 1 mm – 10 mm | 30–300 GHz | 5G mmWave, 60 GHz WPAN | | Infrared | 700 nm – 1 mm | 430 THz – 0.3 THz | Fiber optics, IR remote | | **Fiber-Optic C-band** | **1530–1565 nm** | **191.5–196.0 THz** | **Long-haul DWDM** | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Anatomy of an Electromagnetic Wave (Wavelength Highlighted) ```mermaid graph LR node1[Wave Source
Oscillator] node2[Crest Peak 1
Amplitude A] node3[Trough Valley 1
Amplitude minus A] node4[Zero Crossing
Equilibrium Line] node5[Crest Peak 2
One Wavelength Later] node6[Receiver Antenna
or Photodetector] node7[Direction of
Propagation X] node1 -- generates --> node2 node2 -- distance lambda --> node3 node3 -- distance lambda over 2 --> node4 node4 -- distance lambda over 2 --> node5 node5 -- arrives at --> node6 node2 -.-> node7 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** The labeled span from **Crest Peak 1** to **Crest Peak 2** is one full wavelength $\lambda$. The span from **Crest Peak 1** to **Trough Valley 1** is $\lambda/2$. The span from a **Zero Crossing** (going up) to the **next zero crossing** (going up) is also exactly one wavelength. --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Architecture of Wavelength's Role in Data Communication ```mermaid graph TD nodeA[Data Source
Bits 0 and 1] nodeB[Modulator
Maps Bits to Symbol] nodeC[Carrier Oscillator
Frequency f] nodeD[Wavelength Selector
lambda equals c over f] nodeE[Transmission Medium
Fiber or Free Space] nodeF[Demodulator
Recovers Bits] nodeG[Destination
Receiving System] nodeA -- input bitstream --> nodeB nodeC -- sinusoidal carrier --> nodeB nodeB -- modulated wave --> nodeD nodeD -- selects lambda --> nodeE nodeE -- propagates with lambda --> nodeF nodeF -- recovered bitstream --> nodeG ``` > **Block-Level Insight:** The wavelength is *not chosen arbitrarily* — it is mathematically dictated by the carrier frequency (Step `nodeC → nodeD`). Engineers typically fix the **frequency** (e.g., 193.55 THz for 1550 nm), and the wavelength becomes a derived quantity via $c = \lambda f$. --- ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Fiber-Optic Transmission Windows and Wavelength Choice ```mermaid graph LR node1[Fiber Optic Wavelength Windows] node2[850 nm Window
Short Reach
LAN and Data Center] node3[1310 nm Window
Zero Dispersion
Metro Networks] node4[1550 nm Window
Lowest Attenuation
Long Haul and DWDM] node5[1625 nm Window
Supervisory Channel] node1 --> node2 node1 --> node3 node1 --> node4 node1 --> node5 ``` > **Engineering takeaway:** The 1550 nm band is favored for transcontinental links because silica fiber has its **minimum attenuation** at this wavelength — only ~0.2 dB loss per kilometer, allowing signals to travel 80–100 km between amplifiers. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define wavelength. Write the relationship between wavelength, frequency, and the speed of light.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Wavelength is the spatial distance between two consecutive crests (or two consecutive identical points of equal phase) of a periodic wave, measured along its direction of propagation. It is denoted by $\lambda$ and measured in meters. The fundamental relationship connecting wavelength, frequency, and wave speed is: $$c = \lambda f$$ where $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s is the speed of light in vacuum, $\lambda$ is the wavelength, and $f$ is the frequency in Hz. Rearranging: $\lambda = c / f$. *(3 marks)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **A fiber-optic link operates at 1310 nm. If the fiber core has a refractive index of 1.46, calculate the wavelength of light inside the fiber.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Given: $\lambda_{\text{vac}} = 1310$ nm, $n = 1.46$. Using the medium wavelength formula: $$\lambda_{\text{med}} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{vac}}}{n} = \frac{1310 \text{ nm}}{1.46} \approx 897.26 \text{ nm}$$ The wavelength inside the fiber is approximately **897.26 nm**. *(3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the concept of wavelength. Discuss its role in selecting transmission channels in fiber-optic communication. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An optical source emits light at 1550 nm in vacuum. The fiber has a refractive index of 1.48. Calculate:     (i) The frequency of the optical carrier.     (ii) The wavelength inside the fiber.     (iii) The corresponding frequency in THz. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution to Q-A(a): *Wavelength concept (3 marks):* Wavelength $\lambda$ is the spatial period of a wave — the distance over which the wave's shape repeats once. It is measured from crest-to-crest, trough-to-trough, or any two consecutive in-phase points. Mathematically: $c = \lambda f$, where $c$ is the wave speed and $f$ is the frequency. *Diagram (2 marks — refer Section 4.1).* *Role in fiber-optic channel selection (2 marks):* In fiber-optic communication, **wavelength determines the attenuation and dispersion behavior** of the optical signal. Standard operating windows — 850 nm, 1310 nm, and 1550 nm — are chosen because they correspond to local minima of attenuation in silica glass. The 1550 nm window is preferred for long-distance transmission because it offers the lowest loss (≈0.2 dB/km). In WDM systems, multiple closely-spaced wavelengths (e.g., 100 GHz channel spacing) carry independent data streams over a single fiber, multiplying capacity. ##### Model Solution to Q-A(b): **Given:** $\lambda_{\text{vac}} = 1550$ nm = $1.55 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $n = 1.48$, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. **(i) Frequency of the optical carrier (3 marks):** $$f = \frac{c}{\lambda_{\text{vac}}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{1.55 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.9355 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz} \approx 193.55 \text{ THz}$$ **(ii) Wavelength inside the fiber (2 marks):** $$\lambda_{\text{med}} = \frac{\lambda_{\text{vac}}}{n} = \frac{1550}{1.48} \approx 1047.3 \text{ nm}$$ **(iii) Frequency in THz (2 marks):** $$f = \frac{300}{\lambda(\mu\text{m})} = \frac{300}{1.55} \approx 193.55 \text{ THz}$$ Both methods agree, confirming the source lies in the C-band (1530–1565 nm). --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **(a)** Derive the relationship $c = \lambda f$ for an electromagnetic wave. Explain why frequency remains unchanged but wavelength changes when light passes from vacuum into an optical fiber. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 5G base station operates at 28 GHz. Calculate the wavelength of the carrier signal. Briefly comment on the antenna sizing implications. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution to Q-B(a): *Derivation of $c = \lambda f$ (4 marks):* Starting from the wave equation $y(x,t) = A \sin(kx - \omega t + \phi)$: * Spatial periodicity: $k\lambda = 2\pi \Rightarrow k = 2\pi/\lambda$ * Temporal periodicity: $\omega T = 2\pi \Rightarrow \omega = 2\pi f$ * Wave speed: $v = \text{distance}/\text{time} = \lambda / T = \lambda f$ * For EM waves in vacuum: $v = c$, hence $c = \lambda f$. *Why frequency is invariant (1.5 marks):* The frequency of a wave is determined by the **source** — how many times per second the source oscillates. This oscillation rate is intrinsic and is not affected by the medium. *Why wavelength changes (1.5 marks):* The speed of light in a medium reduces by factor $n$ ($v = c/n$). Since $f$ is fixed but $v$ drops, $\lambda$ must shrink proportionally: $\lambda_{\text{med}} = \lambda_{\text{vac}}/n$. ##### Model Solution to Q-B(b): **Given:** $f = 28$ GHz = $28 \times 10^{9}$ Hz, $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s. **Wavelength calculation (5 marks):** $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{28 \times 10^{9}} = 1.071 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m} \approx 10.71 \text{ mm}$$ **Antenna sizing implications (2 marks):** A typical half-wave dipole antenna has length $L = \lambda/2 \approx 5.36$ mm. This compact size enables **massive MIMO arrays** with hundreds of miniaturized antenna elements to fit on a single base station panel — a key enabler of beamforming and high user-density 5G deployments. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost 2–3 Marks Each:** > > 1. **Unit mismatch:** Writing $\lambda$ in nm while using $c$ in m/s without converting. Always convert to consistent units first (e.g., nm → m by multiplying by $10^{-9}$). > 2. **Confusing $f$ with $\omega$:** Angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$. If a problem gives $\omega$, divide by $2\pi$ first. > 3. **Forgetting the refractive index effect:** Writing $\lambda_{\text{fiber}} = 1550$ nm when the question asks for *the wavelength inside the fiber* — you must divide by $n$. > 4. **Inverting the $c = \lambda f$ formula:** A frequent error is writing $f = c\lambda$. It should be $f = c/\lambda$. > 5. **Skipping the diagram in 7-mark questions:** Even a hand-drawn wavelength sketch (with $\lambda$ labeled) earns 1–2 easy marks. Never skip diagrams in KTU answer sheets. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Wavelength $\lambda$** = spatial period of a wave (crest-to-crest distance), measured in meters. - **Master equation:** $c = \lambda f$, with $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s in vacuum. - **In a medium:** $\lambda_{\text{med}} = \lambda_{\text{vac}} / n$ where $n$ is the refractive index. - **Frequency is invariant** across media; only $\lambda$ and $v$ change. - **Three standard fiber windows:** **850 nm** (LAN), **1310 nm** (zero-dispersion metro), **1550 nm** (lowest loss, long-haul + DWDM). - **Useful quick formula:** $f(\text{THz}) = 300 / \lambda(\mu\text{m})$ — memorize this for fast KTU problem solving. - **WDM** multiplexes many wavelengths (each a "channel") onto a single fiber, dramatically multiplying capacity. - **Antenna size** scales with wavelength: $L \approx \lambda/2$ for a half-wave dipole. Higher $f$ → smaller $\lambda$ → smaller antennas (key for 5G mmWave and Wi-Fi). - **SI unit:** meter (m); practical units in optical comms: **nanometer (nm)** and **micrometer (µm)**. - **Electromagnetic spectrum order (high to low $\lambda$):** Radio → Microwave → Infrared → Visible → UV → X-ray → Gamma. Data comms uses Radio, Microwave, and Infrared. - **Wavenumber $k = 2\pi/\lambda$** appears in advanced phase-velocity and group-velocity derivations. - **Period $T = 1/f$** is the temporal counterpart of wavelength; remember $v = \lambda / T$. ---

topics.title: Time and Frequency Domain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Time and Frequency Domain # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In **Data Communication**, any electrical signal carrying information can be represented and analyzed in two mathematically equivalent, but visually distinct, domains: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Time Domain Representation** > A signal $s(t)$ is plotted as **amplitude (voltage or current) versus time**. It shows *when* the signal changes and *how* it varies instant by instant. The horizontal axis is time $t$ (in seconds), and the vertical axis is instantaneous amplitude $s(t)$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Frequency Domain Representation** > A signal $S(f)$ is plotted as **amplitude (or power) versus frequency**. It shows *what frequency components* make up the signal. The horizontal axis is frequency $f$ (in Hertz), and the vertical axis is the magnitude of each sinusoidal component. The bridge between these two domains is the **Fourier Transform** (for aperiodic signals) and the **Fourier Series** (for periodic signals), both established by **Jean-Baptiste Joseph Fourier** in 1822. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a **smoothie** 🥤. - **Time Domain** = *drinking the smoothie second by second.* You experience the flavor changing with each sip — sometimes sweet, sometimes thick, sometimes thin. You are tasting *the whole mixture as it evolves in time*. - **Frequency Domain** = *the recipe card.* The card lists exactly how much banana, mango, milk, and sugar went into the blender. It tells you the **ingredients** (frequency components) and **how much of each** (amplitudes) — independent of *when* you taste them. Both describe the same smoothie; they are just two different "viewpoints." > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter in Data Communication?** > Data communication systems (modems, fiber optics, wireless links) are designed around **frequency behavior**, not raw time behavior. Bandwidth, channel capacity, attenuation, and noise immunity are all defined in the frequency domain. ## 1.3 Fundamental Metrics (KTU Standard Vocabulary) - **Period ($T$)** — Time taken for one complete cycle, in **seconds (s)**. - **Frequency ($f$)** — Number of cycles per second, in **Hertz (Hz)**. $f = 1/T$. - **Angular Frequency ($\omega$)** — Rate of phase change, in **radians per second (rad/s)**. $\omega = 2\pi f$. - **Bandwidth ($BW$)** — Width of the useful frequency spectrum, in **Hz**. - **DC Component** — The **$0\text{ Hz}$** term; it is the average value of the signal. - **Fundamental Frequency ($f_0$)** — The **lowest** frequency component present. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Two Domains Exist (The "Why") A real-world communication signal is almost never a pure sine wave. It is a **complicated mess of bumps, edges, and transitions** when viewed on an oscilloscope (time domain). To design filters, antennas, amplifiers, and channel equalizers, engineers need to know *which frequencies the signal is wasting its energy on*. The frequency domain answers that directly. ## 2.2 The Fourier Series — For Periodic Signals Any **periodic** signal $s(t)$ with period $T_0$ can be expressed as a sum of sines and cosines: $$s(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\left[a_n \cos(n\omega_0 t) + b_n \sin(n\omega_0 t)\right]$$ The coefficients are calculated by: $$a_0 = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{0}^{T_0} s(t)\,dt$$ $$a_n = \frac{2}{T_0}\int_{0}^{T_0} s(t)\cos(n\omega_0 t)\,dt$$ $$b_n = \frac{2}{T_0}\int_{0}^{T_0} s(t)\sin(n\omega_0 t)\,dt$$ The amplitude-phase form (more useful in comm.): $$s(t) = C_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} C_n \cos(n\omega_0 t + \theta_n)$$ where $C_n = \sqrt{a_n^{2} + b_n^{2}}$ and $\theta_n = -\tan^{-1}(b_n / a_n)$. ## 2.3 The Fourier Transform — For Aperiodic Signals When a signal is **not periodic**, we use the Fourier Transform pair: $$S(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} s(t)\, e^{-j2\pi f t}\, dt$$ $$s(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} S(f)\, e^{+j2\pi f t}\, df$$ ## 2.4 Properties Linking Time and Frequency (Duet of Duals) | Property | Time-Domain Effect | Frequency-Domain Effect | |---|---|---| | **Time Shifting** | $s(t - t_0)$ | $S(f) \cdot e^{-j2\pi f t_0}$ (magnitude unchanged) | | **Frequency Shifting** (Modulation) | $s(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Spectrum shifted to $\pm f_c$ | | **Time Scaling** (Compression) | $s(at)$ | $\frac{1}{\vert a \vert}\,S(f/a)$ — **compression ↔ expansion** | | **Multiplication (Convolution)** | $s_1(t)\cdot s_2(t)$ | $S_1(f) * S_2(f)$ | | **Convolution Theorem** | $s_1(t) * s_2(t)$ | $S_1(f)\cdot S_2(f)$ | ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The table below is the **exam-day cheat sheet** for Module-1 frequency-domain questions. Memorize every row. | # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Period-Frequency Relation | $f = 1/T$ | $T$ in s, $f$ in Hz | | 2 | Angular Frequency | $\omega = 2\pi f$ | rad/s | | 3 | Fundamental Freq. of Periodic Signal | $f_0 = 1/T_0$ | Lowest non-zero frequency | | 4 | $n^{\text{th}}$ Harmonic | $f_n = n f_0$ | Integer multiple of $f_0$ | | 5 | Bandwidth of Periodic Signal | $BW = f_{\max} - f_{\min}$ | $f_{\min} = f_0$ (non-DC case) | | 6 | Fourier Series (Trig form) | $s(t) = a_0 + \sum a_n\cos + \sum b_n\sin$ | $a_0$ is DC value | | 7 | Fourier Series Amplitude | $C_n = \sqrt{a_n^{2} + b_n^{2}}$ | Always $\ge 0$ | | 8 | Fourier Series Phase | $\theta_n = -\tan^{-1}(b_n / a_n)$ | In radians | | 9 | Fourier Transform (Analysis) | $S(f) = \int s(t)\,e^{-j2\pi ft}\,dt$ | Time $\to$ Frequency | | 10 | Inverse Fourier Transform | $s(t) = \int S(f)\,e^{+j2\pi ft}\,df$ | Frequency $\to$ Time | | 11 | Parseval's Power Theorem | $P = \int \vert s(t) \vert^{2} dt = \int \vert S(f) \vert^{2} df$ | Energy conservation | | 12 | Effect of Compression | $s(at) \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{\vert a \vert}S(f/a)$ | **Inversely** related | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip — The "Duality Rule"** always appears in 7-mark sub-questions: > *A signal compact in time is spread out in frequency, and vice versa.* A 1-microsecond pulse occupies a bandwidth of approximately **$1\text{ MHz}$**. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Antenna Design** — Physical size of antenna is tied to wavelength $\lambda = c/f$. - **Channel Bandwidth Allocation** — Telecom regulators (e.g., **TRAI** in India) license frequency bands. - **Filtering** — Low-pass, high-pass, band-pass filters are designed in the frequency domain. - **Modulation** — Shifting baseband spectrum to a higher carrier frequency (e.g., **5G mm-Wave at $28\text{ GHz}$**). - **Spectral Analysis** — Test & measurement instruments like **spectrum analyzers** display $|S(f)|$ directly. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > Every algebraic step is **explicitly** shown. KTU valuation rewards clear intermediate steps, not just the final answer. ## 3.1 Derivation: Fourier Series Coefficients from First Principles **Goal:** Show that $a_n = \frac{2}{T_0}\int_{0}^{T_0} s(t)\cos(n\omega_0 t)\,dt$. **Starting point** — assume the signal can be written as: $$s(t) = a_0 + \sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\left[a_k \cos(k\omega_0 t) + b_k \sin(k\omega_0 t)\right]$$ **Step 1.** Multiply both sides by $\cos(n\omega_0 t)$: $$s(t)\cos(n\omega_0 t) = a_0\cos(n\omega_0 t) + \sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\left[a_k \cos(k\omega_0 t)\cos(n\omega_0 t) + b_k \sin(k\omega_0 t)\cos(n\omega_0 t)\right]$$ **Step 2.** Integrate both sides over one full period $T_0$: $$\int_{0}^{T_0} s(t)\cos(n\omega_0 t)\,dt = a_0\!\int_{0}^{T_0}\!\cos(n\omega_0 t)\,dt + \sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\!\left[a_k\!\int_{0}^{T_0}\!\cos(k\omega_0 t)\cos(n\omega_0 t)\,dt + b_k\!\int_{0}^{T_0}\!\sin(k\omega_0 t)\cos(n\omega_0 t)\,dt\right]$$ **Step 3.** Apply the **orthogonality relations** over $[0, T_0]$: $$\int_{0}^{T_0}\cos(n\omega_0 t)\cos(k\omega_0 t)\,dt = \begin{cases} T_0/2, & n = k \neq 0 \\ 0, & n \neq k \end{cases}$$ $$\int_{0}^{T_0}\sin(n\omega_0 t)\cos(k\omega_0 t)\,dt = 0, \quad \text{(always)}$$ $$\int_{0}^{T_0}\cos(n\omega_0 t)\,dt = 0, \quad \text{for } n \ge 1$$ **Step 4.** All terms in the sum vanish **except** when $k = n$: $$\int_{0}^{T_0} s(t)\cos(n\omega_0 t)\,dt = a_n \cdot \frac{T_0}{2}$$ **Step 5.** Solve for $a_n$: $$\boxed{a_n = \frac{2}{T_0}\int_{0}^{T_0} s(t)\cos(n\omega_0 t)\,dt}$$ A **completely symmetric** derivation (multiply by $\sin(n\omega_0 t)$ instead) gives: $$\boxed{b_n = \frac{2}{T_0}\int_{0}^{T_0} s(t)\sin(n\omega_0 t)\,dt}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Example: Square Wave Fourier Series **Problem:** Find the trigonometric Fourier series of a square wave of amplitude $A$, period $T_0$, defined as $s(t) = A$ for $0 < t < T_0/2$ and $s(t) = -A$ for $T_0/2 < t < T_0$. **Step 1.** Compute the DC term $a_0$: $$a_0 = \frac{1}{T_0}\!\left[\int_{0}^{T_0/2}\!A\,dt + \int_{T_0/2}^{T_0}\!(-A)\,dt\right] = \frac{1}{T_0}\!\left[A\cdot\frac{T_0}{2} - A\cdot\frac{T_0}{2}\right] = 0$$ (No DC component — signal is symmetric about zero.) **Step 2.** Compute $a_n$: $$a_n = \frac{2}{T_0}\!\left[\int_{0}^{T_0/2}\!A\cos(n\omega_0 t)\,dt - \int_{T_0/2}^{T_0}\!A\cos(n\omega_0 t)\,dt\right]$$ Evaluate the first integral: $$\int_{0}^{T_0/2}\!\cos(n\omega_0 t)\,dt = \left[\frac{\sin(n\omega_0 t)}{n\omega_0}\right]_{0}^{T_0/2} = \frac{\sin(n\pi)}{n\omega_0} = 0$$ By the same logic, the second integral also equals **0**. Therefore: $$a_n = 0 \quad \text{for all } n \ge 1$$ **Step 3.** Compute $b_n$: $$b_n = \frac{2}{T_0}\!\left[\int_{0}^{T_0/2}\!A\sin(n\omega_0 t)\,dt - \int_{T_0/2}^{T_0}\!A\sin(n\omega_0 t)\,dt\right]$$ First integral: $$\int_{0}^{T_0/2}\!\sin(n\omega_0 t)\,dt = \left[\frac{-\cos(n\omega_0 t)}{n\omega_0}\right]_{0}^{T_0/2} = \frac{1 - \cos(n\pi)}{n\omega_0} = \frac{1 - (-1)^{n}}{n\omega_0}$$ Second integral: $$\int_{T_0/2}^{T_0}\!\sin(n\omega_0 t)\,dt = \left[\frac{-\cos(n\omega_0 t)}{n\omega_0}\right]_{T_0/2}^{T_0} = \frac{\cos(n\pi) - \cos(2n\pi)}{n\omega_0} = \frac{(-1)^{n} - 1}{n\omega_0}$$ **Step 4.** Substitute back into $b_n$: $$b_n = \frac{2A}{T_0}\!\left[\frac{1 - (-1)^{n}}{n\omega_0} - \frac{(-1)^{n} - 1}{n\omega_0}\right] = \frac{2A}{T_0}\!\left[\frac{2(1 - (-1)^{n})}{n\omega_0}\right]$$ **Step 5.** Simplify using $T_0\omega_0 = 2\pi$: $$b_n = \frac{4A(1 - (-1)^{n})}{T_0 \cdot n\omega_0} = \frac{4A(1 - (-1)^{n})}{2\pi n} = \frac{2A(1 - (-1)^{n})}{n\pi}$$ **Step 6.** Observe that $(1 - (-1)^{n})$ is $0$ when $n$ is even, and $2$ when $n$ is odd. Therefore **only odd harmonics survive**: $$\boxed{s(t) = \frac{4A}{\pi}\!\left[\sin(\omega_0 t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega_0 t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega_0 t) + \cdots\right]}$$ This is the famous result. The **bandwidth** of an ideal square wave is **infinite** — a critical reason why digital signals need bandwidth-limiting channels. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix > [!NOTE] > Use this matrix to pick the right representation in numerical problems. | Signal Type | Time-Domain Shape | Frequency-Domain Shape | Bandwidth | DC Present? | |---|---|---|---|---| | Pure sine $A\sin(\omega t)$ | Single sinusoid | One impulse at $\omega$ | $0$ (line spectrum) | No | | Square wave (symmetric) | Alternating $\pm A$ | Odd harmonics $\propto 1/n$ | Infinite | No | | Triangular wave | Ramp up & down | Odd harmonics $\propto 1/n^{2}$ | Infinite | No | | Single rectangular pulse | Pulse of width $\tau$ | Sinc function $\text{sinc}(f\tau)$ | $\approx 1/\tau$ | Yes (if non-zero mean) | | Periodic impulse train | Dirac comb in time | Dirac comb in frequency | Infinite | Yes | | Random data (NRZ) | Random $\pm V$ | Sinc-shaped envelope | $\approx R_b$ (bit-rate) | Depends on coding | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Dual-Domain Analysis Pipeline The following diagram shows the canonical signal-analysis flow used in every communication engineering lab. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Source Signal s of t] --> B[Time Domain Plot
Amplitude vs Time] A --> C[Fourier Transform
F of omega] C --> D[Frequency Domain Plot
Magnitude vs Frequency] D --> E{Signal Type} E -- Periodic --> F[Fourier Series
Discrete Spectrum] E -- Aperiodic --> G[Fourier Transform
Continuous Spectrum] F --> H[Spectral Analysis
Identify Harmonics] G --> H H --> I[Channel Design
Bandwidth BW] I --> J[Filter Selection
LPF HPF BPF] ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Time ↔ Frequency Duality Visualization ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph T1[TIME DOMAIN VIEW] tA[Pure Sine Wave
Single Frequency Component] tB[Long Duration Pulse
Spectral Width equals 1 over T] tC[Short Duration Pulse
Wide Spectral Spread] tD[Rapidly Varying Signal
Contains High Frequencies] end subgraph F1[FREQUENCY DOMAIN VIEW] fA[Single Spike
Delta Function at f0] fB[Narrow Peak around 0 Hz
Concentrated Spectrum] fC[Wide Flat Spectrum
Many High Frequencies] fD[Spectrum Pushed to High f
Fast Variations] end tA --- fA tB --- fB tC --- fC tD --- fD ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Bandwidth Occupancy in Communication ```mermaid flowchart LR X[Baseband Data Signal] --> Y[Modulator] Y --> Z[Carrier Frequency fc] Z --> W[Channel with Bandwidth BW] W --> V[Demodulator at Receiver] V --> U[Recovered Baseband Data] ``` | Block | Time-Domain Role | Frequency-Domain Role | |---|---|---| | **Baseband Data** | NRZ pulses carrying bits | Spectrum centred at $0$ Hz | | **Modulator** | Multiplies $s(t)$ by $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Shifts spectrum to $\pm f_c$ | | **Channel** | Adds noise and distortion | Filters out frequencies outside BW | | **Demodulator** | Detects envelope / phase | Shifts spectrum back to baseband | > [!NOTE] > The shift between time and frequency representation is the **fundamental reason modulation is needed** — to translate a low-frequency baseband signal up to a frequency band where antennas of practical size can radiate it efficiently. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q1. Differentiate between time domain and frequency domain representation of a signal. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Time Domain | Frequency Domain | |---|---|---| | Independent variable | Time $t$ | Frequency $f$ | | Shows | How amplitude changes with time | Which frequency components exist | | Plotting instrument | Oscilloscope | Spectrum analyzer | | Useful for | Waveform visualization | Bandwidth, filter, and modulation design | | Mathematical tool | $s(t)$ direct expression | Fourier series / transform | > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q2. State Parseval's theorem and write its mathematical form. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Parseval's theorem states that the **total power (or energy)** of a signal computed in the time domain equals the total power computed in the frequency domain. $$P = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert s(t) \vert^{2}\, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert S(f) \vert^{2}\, df$$ This theorem is fundamental in communication because it lets engineers **allocate power across frequency bands** without changing the total transmitted energy. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1 – Internal Choice Pattern]** ### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **(a)** Derive the trigonometric Fourier series representation of a periodic signal. Define each term clearly. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** For a square wave of amplitude $5\text{ V}$ and period $2\text{ ms}$, determine the fundamental frequency and compute the amplitudes of the first three odd harmonics. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to Q-A (a) 1. Any periodic signal $s(t)$ with period $T_0$ can be expressed as the sum of a DC value and harmonically related sines and cosines: $$s(t) = a_0 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\!\left[a_n \cos(n\omega_0 t) + b_n \sin(n\omega_0 t)\right]$$ 2. **$a_0$** = DC component (average value) = $\frac{1}{T_0}\int_{0}^{T_0} s(t)\,dt$ 3. **$a_n$** = cosine coefficient = $\frac{2}{T_0}\int_{0}^{T_0} s(t)\cos(n\omega_0 t)\,dt$ 4. **$b_n$** = sine coefficient = $\frac{2}{T_0}\int_{0}^{T_0} s(t)\sin(n\omega_0 t)\,dt$ 5. **$\omega_0$** = fundamental angular frequency = $\frac{2\pi}{T_0}$ 6. **$n\omega_0$** = frequency of $n^{\text{th}}$ harmonic The series converges to $s(t)$ at all points of continuity and to the **midpoint of discontinuity** at jump points (Dirichlet conditions). #### Model Solution to Q-A (b) Given: $A = 5\text{ V}$, $T_0 = 2\text{ ms} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\text{ s}$. **Step 1 — Fundamental frequency:** $$f_0 = \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = 500\text{ Hz}$$ $$\omega_0 = 2\pi f_0 = 1000\pi\text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 2 — Fourier series of the symmetric square wave (derived in §3.2 above):** $$s(t) = \frac{4A}{\pi}\!\left[\sin(\omega_0 t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3\omega_0 t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5\omega_0 t) + \cdots\right]$$ **Step 3 — Substituting $A = 5\text{ V}$:** $$s(t) = \frac{20}{\pi}\!\left[\sin(1000\pi t) + \frac{1}{3}\sin(3000\pi t) + \frac{1}{5}\sin(5000\pi t) + \cdots\right]$$ **Step 4 — First three odd harmonic amplitudes:** | Harmonic $n$ | Frequency | Amplitude $\frac{4A}{n\pi}$ | Numerical value | |---|---|---|---| | 1 (fundamental) | $500\text{ Hz}$ | $\frac{20}{\pi}$ | $\approx 6.37\text{ V}$ | | 3 | $1500\text{ Hz}$ | $\frac{20}{3\pi}$ | $\approx 2.12\text{ V}$ | | 5 | $2500\text{ Hz}$ | $\frac{20}{5\pi}$ | $\approx 1.27\text{ V}$ | **Final answer:** $f_0 = 500\text{ Hz}$; harmonic amplitudes are $6.37\text{ V}$, $2.12\text{ V}$, and $1.27\text{ V}$ respectively. --- ### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **(a)** Explain the significance of the frequency domain in data communication. Discuss bandwidth and its relation to data rate. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A periodic signal has the following Fourier series coefficients: $a_0 = 2$, $a_1 = 3$, $a_2 = 1$, $b_1 = 4$, $b_2 = 2$, and all higher coefficients are zero. Write the time-domain expression and compute the **bandwidth**. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution to Q-B (a) **Significance of Frequency Domain in Data Communication:** 1. **Channel Characterization** — Real channels (copper wire, optical fibre, wireless) attenuate different frequencies differently. Frequency-domain analysis quantifies this. 2. **Bandwidth Definition** — Bandwidth is the *frequency range* a channel can pass without severe attenuation. It is a **frequency-domain** concept, not a time-domain one. 3. **Modulation & Multiplexing** — FDM (Frequency Division Multiplexing) places multiple signals in non-overlapping frequency bands — possible *only* because of the frequency view. 4. **Filter Design** — LPF, HPF, BPF are specified by their **frequency response** $H(f)$. 5. **Noise Analysis** — Noise is often specified as *Noise Spectral Density* $N_0$ (W/Hz), a frequency-domain quantity. **Bandwidth and Data Rate Relation (Nyquist & Shannon):** - **Nyquist (noiseless channel):** $C = 2B \log_2 M$ bits/s - **Shannon (noisy channel):** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ bits/s where $B$ is bandwidth in Hz, $M$ is signal levels, $S/N$ is signal-to-noise ratio. **Higher bandwidth $\Rightarrow$ higher achievable data rate.** #### Model Solution to Q-B (b) Given coefficients: $a_0 = 2,\ a_1 = 3,\ a_2 = 1,\ b_1 = 4,\ b_2 = 2$, all others zero. **Step 1 — Time-domain expression:** $$s(t) = a_0 + a_1 \cos(\omega_0 t) + a_2 \cos(2\omega_0 t) + b_1 \sin(\omega_0 t) + b_2 \sin(2\omega_0 t)$$ $$\boxed{s(t) = 2 + 3\cos(\omega_0 t) + 4\sin(\omega_0 t) + \cos(2\omega_0 t) + 2\sin(2\omega_0 t)}$$ **Step 2 — Amplitude-phase (compact) form (optional):** For $n = 1$: $C_1 = \sqrt{3^{2} + 4^{2}} = \sqrt{25} = 5$, $\theta_1 = -\tan^{-1}(4/3) \approx -53.13^{\circ}$ For $n = 2$: $C_2 = \sqrt{1^{2} + 2^{2}} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.236$, $\theta_2 = -\tan^{-1}(2/1) \approx -63.43^{\circ}$ $$s(t) = 2 + 5\cos(\omega_0 t - 53.13^{\circ}) + 2.236\cos(2\omega_0 t - 63.43^{\circ})$$ **Step 3 — Bandwidth calculation:** Highest frequency present = $2 f_0$; lowest non-zero frequency = $f_0$. $$BW = f_{\max} - f_{\min} = 2f_0 - f_0 = f_0 = \frac{1}{T_0}\text{ Hz}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Forgetting DC component** — Many students drop $a_0$ in the final expression. Loss: 1 mark. > 2. **Confusing amplitude with RMS** — The Fourier coefficient $C_n$ is the *peak* amplitude, not RMS. RMS = $C_n/\sqrt{2}$. > 3. **Mixing up $f_0$ and $f_c$** — $f_0$ is the fundamental; $f_c$ is the carrier (modulation). These are different concepts. > 4. **Bandwidth miscalculation** — For a baseband signal, $f_{\min} = 0$ Hz; for a bandpass signal, $f_{\min} = f_c - BW/2$. Pick the right one. > 5. **Skipping orthogonality step in derivations** — Always cite the **orthogonality relations** explicitly for full 7 marks in derivations. > 6. **Sign of phase angle** — $\theta_n = -\tan^{-1}(b_n/a_n)$, not $+\tan^{-1}$. Losing the negative sign is a 0.5-mark deduction. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - [ ] **Time Domain** = amplitude vs time; **Frequency Domain** = amplitude vs frequency. - [ ] **Fourier Series** is for **periodic** signals (discrete spectrum); **Fourier Transform** is for **aperiodic** signals (continuous spectrum). - [ ] Fundamental relation: $f = 1/T$ and $\omega = 2\pi f$. - [ ] Bandwidth $BW = f_{\max} - f_{\min}$; for a periodic signal with $N$ harmonics, $BW \approx N \cdot f_0$. - [ ] **Duality Principle:** narrow in time $\Leftrightarrow$ wide in frequency, and vice versa. - [ ] **Parseval's theorem** — total signal power is conserved between the two domains. - [ ] Symmetric square wave has **only odd harmonics** with amplitudes $4A/(n\pi)$. - [ ] Triangular wave has only odd harmonics with amplitudes $8A/(n^{2}\pi^{2})$ (faster decay $\Rightarrow$ less bandwidth). - [ ] Modulation = **frequency shifting** — baseband spectrum moves to $\pm f_c$. - [ ] Channel bandwidth *directly* controls the maximum data rate (Nyquist & Shannon). - [ ] A perfect impulse in time = constant (infinite bandwidth) in frequency. - [ ] A pure DC signal = a single spike at $f = 0$ Hz in the frequency domain. - [ ] **KTU 14-Mark Questions** typically test: (i) Fourier series derivation + harmonic computation OR (ii) frequency-domain significance + bandwidth/data-rate relation. - [ ] **Always** include units (Hz, V, W/Hz) and direction of arrows in any sketch you draw in the exam. - [ ] **Orthogonality of $\sin$ and $\cos$** over one period is the *key lemma* — cite it explicitly in derivations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Mantra for this Module:** > *Draw both plots, label axes with units, write the formula, plug in numbers, and box the answer.* A well-labeled sketch alone can earn 1–2 partial marks even if the algebra fails.

topics.title: Bandwidth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Bandwidth # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Data Communication**, the term **Bandwidth** has two distinct but related technical meanings, and KTU examiners frequently test whether the student understands the difference. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bandwidth (Definition):** The width of the range of frequencies that an electronic signal occupies on a given transmission channel, or the maximum rate at which information can be transmitted over that channel. ### 1.1.1 Analog Bandwidth (Signal Bandwidth) This is the classical meaning inherited from **Analog Signal Processing** and is measured in the frequency domain. > [!NOTE] > **Analog Bandwidth:** The difference between the highest and the lowest frequencies contained in a composite signal, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**. > > $$B_{analog} = f_{max} - f_{min}$$ If $f_{min}$ approaches **0 Hz** (as in baseband signals), the bandwidth is simply equal to the highest frequency component present in the signal. ### 1.1.2 Digital Bandwidth (Channel Capacity) This is the meaning used in modern **Digital Communication** and is measured in the time domain. > [!NOTE] > **Digital Bandwidth (Channel Capacity):** The maximum amount of data (in bits) that can be transmitted over a communication channel per unit time, measured in **bits per second (bps)** or its multiples (**Kbps, Mbps, Gbps**). The two definitions are linked by two fundamental theorems introduced in Module 1: - The **Nyquist Theorem** (for noiseless channels). - The **Shannon–Hartley Theorem** (for noisy channels). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pipe" Model Imagine a **water pipe** carrying data from one point to another: - The **diameter (width) of the pipe** is analogous to **Analog Bandwidth**. A wider pipe can physically pass a wider range of flow rates (frequencies). - The **actual amount of water flowing per second** is analogous to **Digital Bandwidth (throughput)**. - The **roughness / debris inside the pipe** is analogous to **Noise** (lowering the Signal-to-Noise Ratio). Just as a wider, smoother pipe carries more water, a channel with **higher analog bandwidth** and **lower noise** carries **more bits per second**. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics to Remember | Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | |---|---|---| | Analog Bandwidth | $B$ | **Hertz (Hz)** | | Digital Bandwidth / Capacity | $C$ or $R$ | **bits per second (bps)** | | Number of Signal Levels | $M$ | **Dimensionless integer** | | Signal Power | $S$ | **Watts (W)** or **dBm** | | Noise Power | $N$ | **Watts (W)** or **dBm** | | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $S/N$ | **Dimensionless** or **dB** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 1):** Bandwidth is the foundational parameter on which Nyquist and Shannon limits are derived. Mastery of this topic is mandatory before tackling **Encoding, Modulation, and Multiplexing** in later modules. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Bandwidth Matters — The Operational Logic The capacity of any communication link is governed by three fundamental physical realities: 1. **Spectral Availability (B):** A channel can only pass a finite band of frequencies. Higher $B$ means more "room" for the signal. 2. **Signal Granularity (M):** Each symbol sent can represent $\log_2(M)$ bits. More distinct signal levels carry more bits per symbol. 3. **Noise (S/N):** Random disturbances limit how many distinguishable levels can be reliably detected at the receiver. These three realities give rise to two celebrated theorems. ## 2.2 Nyquist Theorem — Noiseless Channel > [!NOTE] > **Nyquist Bit-Rate Theorem:** For a **noiseless** channel, the maximum theoretical data rate is **twice the bandwidth** times the logarithm (base 2) of the number of discrete signal levels. **Why "twice"?** Because of the **Nyquist Sampling Criterion** — a band-limited signal of bandwidth $B$ Hz must be sampled at **at least $2B$** samples per second to be faithfully reconstructed. **Why $\log_2(M)$?** Because with $M$ distinct signal levels, each sample (symbol) can encode $\log_2(M)$ bits of information. ## 2.3 Shannon–Hartley Theorem — Noisy Channel > [!NOTE] > **Shannon–Hartley Theorem:** The maximum theoretical capacity of a **noisy** channel of bandwidth $B$ and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$ is given by $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$. **Why is this important?** It establishes an **absolute upper limit** on information transfer that **no encoding scheme can exceed**, regardless of how clever the modulation is. ## 2.4 The dB Form of Signal-to-Noise Ratio Signal-to-noise ratio is frequently given in decibels. The conversion rule is: $$ \left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{dB} \;=\; 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right) $$ To recover the linear ratio from a dB value, use: $$ \frac{S}{N} \;=\; 10^{\;\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)_{dB}/10} $$ ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Applies To | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Analog Bandwidth | $B = f_{max} - f_{min}$ | $f$ in **Hz** | Signal spectrum | | 2 | Nyquist Capacity (Noiseless) | $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ | $C$ in **bps**, $B$ in **Hz**, $M$ = levels | Ideal channel | | 3 | Shannon Capacity (Noisy) | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | $C$ in **bps**, $B$ in **Hz**, $S/N$ linear | Real channel | | 4 | S/N in dB | $(S/N)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ | dB / dimensionless | Power ratio conversion | | 5 | Bits per Symbol | $\eta = \log_2(M)$ | **bits/symbol** | Digital modulation | | 6 | Spectral Efficiency | $\rho = C/B$ | **bps per Hz** | Channel utilization | | 7 | Throughput vs. Capacity | $T \leq C$ | **bps** | Real systems | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:** Nyquist assumes **zero noise** and is therefore the **best-case** limit. Shannon includes noise and is the **realistic absolute** limit. If a question mentions a "noisy" or "real-world" channel, **always use Shannon**, not Nyquist. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Telephony:** The standard voice channel occupies **$B = 3000$ Hz** (300 Hz to 3400 Hz). This single value underlies every PSTN capacity calculation. - **DSL Broadband:** Uses a wider band (up to ~1 MHz) on copper lines to deliver tens of Mbps. - **Optical Fiber:** Practically unlimited $B$ (THz-scale), but Shannon's limit still applies due to nonlinear noise. - **Satellite & Wireless:** Bandwidth is a regulated, licensed, and expensive resource — Shannon's theorem tells engineers the theoretical ceiling before they invest in infrastructure. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Nyquist Capacity Formula We derive the noiseless maximum bit rate from first principles. **Step 1 — Start with the Sampling Theorem.** A band-limited analog signal with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples **if and only if** the sampling rate $f_s$ is at least twice the highest frequency component. $$ f_s \;\geq\; 2 f_{max} $$ **Step 2 — Treat each sample as a "symbol" on the channel.** If the channel can carry one sample per second at rate $f_s$ (one symbol per second), the **symbol rate** $r$ in symbols/second is: $$ r \;=\; 2 f_{max} \;=\; 2B $$ where $B = f_{max}$ is the bandwidth (taking $f_{min} = 0$ for simplicity, the baseband case). **Step 3 — Encode multiple bits per symbol using $M$ discrete levels.** If each symbol can take on $M$ distinguishable amplitude/phase values, then the number of bits carried by one symbol is: $$ \text{bits/symbol} \;=\; \log_2(M) $$ This is the definition of information content of a symbol with $M$ equally likely values. **Step 4 — Multiply symbol rate by bits per symbol.** The data rate $C$ is the number of bits transmitted per second: $$ C \;=\; r \times \log_2(M) $$ **Step 5 — Substitute $r = 2B$:** $$ \boxed{\,C \;=\; 2B \log_2(M)\ \text{bps}\,} $$ This is the **Nyquist Capacity Formula** for a noiseless channel. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Shannon–Hartley Capacity Formula For a **noisy** channel, derivation begins with Nyquist but incorporates the **distinguishability constraint** imposed by noise. **Step 1 — Start with Nyquist's result.** Even with noise, the maximum number of independent signal levels $M$ that can be **reliably distinguished** in the presence of additive noise is bounded. **Step 2 — Apply the distinguishability limit.** For a channel with signal power $S$ and noise power $N$, the maximum number of distinguishable signal levels grows in proportion to the square root of $S/N$: $$ M \;\propto\; \sqrt{1 + \frac{S}{N}} $$ In the limit (Shannon's result, 1948), one obtains: $$ M_{max} \;=\; \sqrt{1 + \frac{S}{N}} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute into Nyquist.** Plug $M_{max}$ into $C = 2B \log_2(M)$: $$ C \;=\; 2B \log_2\!\left(\sqrt{1 + \frac{S}{N}}\right) $$ **Step 4 — Use the logarithmic identity $\log_2(\sqrt{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\log_2(x)$:** $$ C \;=\; 2B \times \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) $$ **Step 5 — Simplify:** $$ \boxed{\,C \;=\; B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)\ \text{bps}\,} $$ This is the **Shannon–Hartley Theorem** — the absolute information-theoretic ceiling for any communication system. ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Combining Both Theorems > **Problem (KTU-style):** A communication channel has a bandwidth $B = 4$ kHz and a signal-to-noise ratio of $1023$ (linear). Find: > (a) The maximum data rate using Shannon's theorem. > (b) The number of signal levels $M$ that would be required for a Nyquist system to achieve the **same** data rate. ### Solution **Step 1 — Apply Shannon's theorem.** $$ C_{Shannon} \;=\; B \log_2(1 + S/N) $$ $$ C_{Shannon} \;=\; 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 1023) $$ $$ C_{Shannon} \;=\; 4000 \times \log_2(1024) $$ Since $1024 = 2^{10}$, we have $\log_2(1024) = 10$. $$ \boxed{\,C_{Shannon} \;=\; 4000 \times 10 \;=\; 40{,}000\ \text{bps} \;=\; 40\ \text{kbps}\,} $$ **Step 2 — Find $M$ for Nyquist to match this rate.** Set $C_{Nyquist} = C_{Shannon} = 40{,}000$ bps. $$ 40{,}000 \;=\; 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2(M) $$ $$ 40{,}000 \;=\; 8000 \times \log_2(M) $$ $$ \log_2(M) \;=\; \frac{40{,}000}{8000} \;=\; 5 $$ $$ M \;=\; 2^{5} \;=\; 32 $$ $$ \boxed{\,M \;=\; 32\ \text{signal levels}\,} $$ ### Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Theorem | Channel Condition | Correct Theorem | Reason | |---|---|---| | "Noiseless channel" or "ideal channel" | **Nyquist** | $N = 0$, so $S/N \to \infty$, Shannon diverges | | "Noisy channel" with given $S/N$ | **Shannon** | Realistic limit accounts for noise | | Question gives $M$ (number of levels) | **Nyquist** | $M$ appears only in Nyquist's formula | | Question gives $S/N$ in dB or linear | **Shannon** | $S/N$ is Shannon's input | | Mixed (both $M$ and $S/N$ given) | **Compare both** | Use the **minimum** of the two as the binding limit | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** In practice, a real channel's capacity is **bounded above by Shannon** and **bounded below by Nyquist** when $M$ is fixed. The **true achievable rate** sits in between, and the gap represents the implementation penalty of practical coding/modulation schemes. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Map of Bandwidth — Domain Relationship ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeA["BANDWIDTH CONCEPT"] --> nodeB["Analog Domain"] nodeA --> nodeC["Digital Domain"] nodeB --> nodeD["Frequency Range of Signal"] nodeB --> nodeE["Measured in Hertz"] nodeC --> nodeF["Data Throughput Capacity"] nodeC --> nodeG["Measured in Bits per Second"] nodeD --> nodeH["Governed by Nyquist Theorem"] nodeD --> nodeI["Governed by Shannon Hartley Theorem"] nodeF --> nodeJ["Depends on Signal Levels M"] nodeF --> nodeK["Depends on Signal to Noise Ratio"] nodeH --> nodeL["C equals 2B log2 M"] nodeI --> nodeM["C equals B log2 of 1 plus S by N"] nodeL --> nodeN["Noiseless Channel"] nodeM --> nodeO["Noisy Real World Channel"] nodeN --> nodeP["Upper Bound of Noiseless Performance"] nodeO --> nodeP nodeP --> nodeQ["Final Channel Capacity Decision"] nodeJ --> nodeQ nodeK --> nodeQ ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Bandwidth-Limited Channel ```mermaid flowchart LR src["Source
Digital Data"] --> enc["Encoder
Maps bits to M Symbols"] enc --> mod["Modulator
Places Symbols on Carrier"] mod --> chan["Channel
Bandwidth B Hertz
Noise N Watts"] chan --> dem["Demodulator
Recovers Symbols from Carrier"] dem --> dec["Decoder
Recovers Bits from Symbols"] dec --> dst["Destination
Digital Data"] noise["Noise Source
Adds Random Disturbance"] -.-> chan snr["S by N Ratio
Computed at Receiver"] -.-> chan ``` ## 4.3 Decision Flow — Selecting the Right Theorem for a KTU Problem ```mermaid flowchart TD q1{"Does the question
mention noise or S by N?"} -->|YES| shannon["Use Shannon Hartley
C equals B log2 of 1 plus S by N"] q1 -->|NO| q2{"Does the question
give M signal levels?"} q2 -->|YES| nyquist["Use Nyquist
C equals 2B log2 M"] q2 -->|NO| q3["Use analog bandwidth
B equals fmax minus fmin"] shannon --> out["Compute Capacity C in bps"] nyquist --> out q3 --> out ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** The block diagram illustrates that bandwidth $B$ and noise $N$ are **physical channel properties** that exist independently of how the data is encoded. The capacity formulas are simply the **mathematical bridges** that connect these physical properties to the resulting digital throughput. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]` **Define bandwidth. Differentiate between analog bandwidth and digital bandwidth with suitable examples.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Bandwidth refers to either the range of frequencies occupied by a signal on a transmission medium, or the maximum data rate supported by a channel. **Analog Bandwidth (1 mark):** It is the difference between the highest and lowest frequencies of a signal, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**. For example, the human voice occupies a band from **300 Hz to 3400 Hz**, giving an analog bandwidth of $3100$ Hz. **Digital Bandwidth (1 mark):** It is the maximum number of bits that can be transmitted per second, measured in **bits per second (bps)**. For example, a 100 Mbps Ethernet link has a digital bandwidth of $10^8$ bps. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State the Shannon–Hartley theorem and explain the significance of the S/N ratio in determining channel capacity.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Statement (1.5 marks):** The Shannon–Hartley theorem states that the maximum theoretical capacity $C$ of a noisy channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz and signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$ is: $$ C \;=\; B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)\ \text{bps} $$ **Significance of S/N (1.5 marks):** The $S/N$ ratio quantifies the **quality** of the channel. A higher $S/N$ allows more distinguishable signal levels and hence a higher capacity. For example, doubling the bandwidth doubles the capacity, but doubling the $S/N$ does **not** double the capacity — it increases it logarithmically, illustrating the diminishing returns of power increase. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` **(a) [7 Marks]** State and derive the Nyquist bit-rate formula for a noiseless channel. A noiseless channel has a bandwidth of $4$ kHz. Calculate the maximum data rate if the channel uses **(i)** $4$ signal levels and **(ii)** $8$ signal levels. **(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the Shannon–Hartley theorem with its significance. A channel has a bandwidth of $3$ kHz and an S/N ratio of $30$ dB. Determine the maximum theoretical data rate of the channel. --- ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Nyquist Theorem Derivation and Calculation **Derivation (4 marks):** Step 1: From the sampling theorem, a signal of bandwidth $B$ Hz requires a sampling rate of at least $2B$ samples per second for faithful reconstruction. Step 2: Each sample corresponds to one transmitted symbol. Therefore, the symbol rate is $r = 2B$ symbols/second. Step 3: If each symbol can take $M$ distinct levels, the information content of one symbol is $\log_2(M)$ bits. Step 4: Multiplying symbol rate by bits per symbol gives the data rate: $$ C \;=\; r \times \log_2(M) \;=\; 2B \log_2(M) $$ **Calculation (3 marks):** For $B = 4000$ Hz: **Case (i): $M = 4$** $$ C_1 \;=\; 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2(4) \;=\; 8000 \times 2 \;=\; 16{,}000\ \text{bps} $$ **Case (ii): $M = 8$** $$ C_2 \;=\; 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2(8) \;=\; 8000 \times 3 \;=\; 24{,}000\ \text{bps} $$ **Final Result:** $C_1 = 16$ kbps, $C_2 = 24$ kbps. --- #### Part (b) — Shannon–Hartley Theorem and Calculation **Explanation (3 marks):** The Shannon–Hartley theorem gives the **absolute upper limit** of information transfer through a noisy channel. Unlike Nyquist, it does not assume zero noise; instead, it uses the signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$ to quantify the impact of noise. The theorem states: $$ C \;=\; B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) $$ The significance is that **no encoding or modulation scheme can exceed this limit**, regardless of its sophistication. The $S/N$ appears inside the logarithm, indicating that increasing signal power (or reducing noise) yields diminishing returns in capacity. **Calculation (4 marks):** Given $B = 3000$ Hz and $(S/N)_{dB} = 30$ dB. Step 1: Convert $S/N$ from dB to linear: $$ \frac{S}{N} \;=\; 10^{30/10} \;=\; 10^3 \;=\; 1000 $$ Step 2: Apply Shannon's formula: $$ C \;=\; 3000 \times \log_2(1 + 1000) \;=\; 3000 \times \log_2(1001) $$ Step 3: Compute $\log_2(1001)$: $$ \log_2(1001) \;=\; \frac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} \;\approx\; \frac{3.00043}{0.30103} \;\approx\; 9.967 $$ Step 4: Final answer: $$ C \;\approx\; 3000 \times 9.967 \;\approx\; 29{,}900\ \text{bps} \;\approx\; 29.9\ \text{kbps} $$ **Final Result:** $C \approx 29.9$ kbps. --- ### Question B (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024 Scheme]` **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the Shannon–Hartley capacity formula starting from the Nyquist theorem, clearly stating the role of noise in the derivation. **(b) [7 Marks]** A telephone line has a bandwidth of $4$ kHz and a signal-to-noise ratio of $1023$. Calculate the theoretical maximum data rate using: **(i)** Nyquist theorem (assuming $16$ signal levels). **(ii)** Shannon–Hartley theorem. **Compare the two results and state which one represents the realistic bound.** --- ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — Derivation of Shannon–Hartley Formula **Derivation (7 marks):** Step 1: Begin with Nyquist's formula for a noiseless channel: $$ C_{Nyquist} \;=\; 2B \log_2(M) $$ Step 2: In a **noisy** channel, the receiver cannot distinguish all $M$ levels perfectly. The maximum number of **reliably distinguishable** levels is given by: $$ M_{max} \;=\; \sqrt{1 + \frac{S}{N}} $$ This result arises from the geometric spacing of signal levels required to keep them at least one noise standard deviation apart. Step 3: Substitute $M_{max}$ into Nyquist's formula: $$ C_{Shannon} \;=\; 2B \log_2\!\left(\sqrt{1 + \frac{S}{N}}\right) $$ Step 4: Apply the identity $\log_2(\sqrt{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\log_2(x)$: $$ C_{Shannon} \;=\; 2B \times \frac{1}{2}\log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) $$ Step 5: Simplify to obtain the final form: $$ C_{Shannon} \;=\; B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)\ \text{bps} $$ **Role of Noise:** The $S/N$ term captures the **only** place where noise enters the capacity equation. As $N \to 0$ (noiseless limit), the formula diverges, and we recover the Nyquist regime only as a limiting case. --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Comparison Given: $B = 4000$ Hz, $S/N = 1023$ (linear), $M = 16$ for Nyquist. **Part (i) — Nyquist with $M = 16$ (3 marks):** $$ C_{Nyquist} \;=\; 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2(16) \;=\; 8000 \times 4 \;=\; 32{,}000\ \text{bps} \;=\; 32\ \text{kbps} $$ **Part (ii) — Shannon–Hartley (3 marks):** $$ C_{Shannon} \;=\; 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 1023) \;=\; 4000 \times \log_2(1024) \;=\; 4000 \times 10 \;=\; 40{,}000\ \text{bps} \;=\; 40\ \text{kbps} $$ **Comparison (1 mark):** The Shannon limit (40 kbps) is **higher** than the Nyquist rate (32 kbps) computed with $M = 16$. However, Shannon's bound is the **realistic, noise-aware absolute ceiling**, while Nyquist gives a noiseless best-case for a specific $M$. In practice, the achievable rate of a real system is bounded above by Shannon and below by Nyquist (for the chosen $M$). The Nyquist rate of 32 kbps is **achievable** without noise, but in the presence of $S/N = 1023$, the practical ceiling is 40 kbps. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here):** > > 1. **Mixing the two theorems.** Applying Nyquist's formula when the channel is explicitly noisy, or applying Shannon's formula when only $M$ is given. Read the question carefully. > 2. **dB to linear conversion error.** A common mistake is $S/N = 10^{dB}$ instead of $S/N = 10^{dB/10}$. Memorize the factor of 10 in the denominator. > 3. **Forgetting units.** Always convert kHz to Hz (multiply by $10^3$) before plugging into the formula, and state the final answer with correct units (bps, kbps, or Mbps). > 4. **$\log_2$ vs $\log_{10}$ confusion.** Shannon's formula **always** uses $\log_2$ (base 2). If you compute $\log_{10}$ by mistake, the answer will be off by a factor of 3.32. > 5. **Skipping derivation steps in 7-mark sub-parts.** KTU examiners award partial marks for intermediate steps in derivations. Do not jump directly from the problem statement to the final number. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Bandwidth** has **two meanings**: Analog (in Hz) and Digital (in bps). Both are essential for KTU Module 1. - **Analog Bandwidth** $= f_{max} - f_{min}$ (in **Hertz**). - **Digital Bandwidth (Capacity)** is computed using either Nyquist or Shannon, depending on the presence of noise. - **Nyquist Theorem (Noiseless):** $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ bps — used when the question is **ideal** or gives $M$. - **Shannon–Hartley Theorem (Noisy):** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ bps — used when the question is **real** or gives $S/N$. - **dB Conversion:** $(S/N)_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ and $S/N = 10^{(S/N)_{dB}/10}$. - **Bits per symbol** $= \log_2(M)$ — a small but frequently tested sub-formula. - **Sampling Rate** must be at least $2B$ samples/second (Nyquist Sampling Theorem) — this is the "2" factor in Nyquist's bit-rate formula. - **Shannon is the absolute ceiling:** No practical system can exceed it, regardless of coding. - **Diminishing Returns:** Doubling $B$ doubles $C$, but doubling $S/N$ **does not** double $C$ — it increases it only logarithmically. - **Common units:** $1$ kHz $= 10^3$ Hz, $1$ kbps $= 10^3$ bps, $1$ MHz $= 10^6$ Hz, $1$ Mbps $= 10^6$ bps. - **Always state units** in the final answer; KTU examiners deduct marks for missing units. - **Syllabus context:** This topic is the foundation for **Encoding Techniques, Modulation, and Multiplexing** in subsequent modules. Master it before moving ahead. ---

topics.title: Analog & Digital Data and Signals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Analog & Digital Data and Signals ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Data? In the context of **Data Communication**, **Data** refers to entities that convey *meaning* or *information*. Data exists in two fundamental forms: - **Analog Data**: Continuous-valued information whose intensity varies smoothly over time (e.g., human voice, temperature, video captured by traditional cameras). - **Digital Data**: Discrete-valued information represented as a finite set of values, typically binary (0s and 1s) (e.g., text files, computer-stored music, sensor readings converted to integers). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Forouzan Standard):** > *Data* are entities that convey meaning within a system. *Signals* are the electrical or electromagnetic representations of data, used to propagate information across a transmission medium. ### 1.2 What is a Signal? A **Signal** is the physical quantity (electrical voltage, current, light pulses, or radio waves) that carries data from one point to another. The signal is the *carrier*; the data is the *payload*. | Data Form | Typical Signal Used | Example | |-----------|---------------------|---------| | Analog Data | Analog Signal | Microphone output (continuous voltage) | | Analog Data | Digital Signal | Voice sampled and encoded as PCM bits | | Digital Data | Analog Signal | Modem sending bits over a phone line | | Digital Data | Digital Signal | Ethernet using NRZ over copper | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal Letter" Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy: Writing a Letter** > - **Data** is the *thought* or *message* in your head. > - **Signal** is the *ink on paper* — the physical medium that carries your thought. > - If you use a **pen with continuous ink flow** (analogy to analog signal), every subtle pressure change is captured smoothly — this is an *analog* representation. > - If you **type the letter on a keyboard**, each character is one of 26 letters — a *discrete* set of symbols. This is *digital* representation. > - The **postal truck** is the *transmission medium*; the **road conditions** are the *noise*. ### 1.4 Classification of Signals — Master Overview Signals are classified along two primary dimensions: **A. Based on Continuity (Time & Amplitude axis):** 1. **Analog Signal** — Continuous in both time and amplitude. 2. **Digital Signal** — Discrete in both time and amplitude (typically binary pulses). 3. **Discrete Signal** — Discrete in time, continuous in amplitude (e.g., samples of an analog waveform). 4. **Quantized Signal** — Continuous in time, discrete in amplitude. **B. Based on Repetition:** 1. **Periodic Signal** — $s(t+T) = s(t)$ for all $t$, where $T$ is the period. 2. **Aperiodic (Non-periodic) Signal** — Does not repeat at regular intervals. ### 1.5 Why Does KTU Emphasize This Topic? > [!NOTE] > Every protocol, every medium, every modulation technique discussed in later modules (TCP/IP, modulation schemes, multiplexing) is built upon the **fundamental distinction between analog and digital data/signals**. Mastering Module 1 ensures clarity for the rest of the course. ### 1.6 Key Standard Metrics to Remember - **Frequency ($f$)**: Number of signal cycles per second, measured in **Hertz (Hz)**. - **Period ($T$)**: Time taken to complete one full cycle, $T = 1/f$, measured in **seconds (s)**. - **Wavelength ($\lambda$)**: Distance traveled by a wave in one period, $\lambda = v/f$, where $v$ is propagation speed. - **Bandwidth ($B$)**: Width of the range of frequencies a signal (or channel) occupies, measured in **Hz**. - **Bit Rate ($R_b$)**: Number of bits transmitted per second, measured in **bits per second (bps)**. - **Baud Rate (Signal Rate)**: Number of *signal units* (symbols) transmitted per second, measured in **baud**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Analog Signals — Operational Characteristics An **analog signal** $s(t)$ is a continuous function of time with continuous amplitude. Its most important analytical property is that **any periodic analog signal can be decomposed into a sum of pure sinusoids** via the **Fourier Series**. For a periodic signal with period $T$ and fundamental frequency $f_0 = 1/T$: $$s(t) = \frac{A_0}{2} + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} A_n \cos(2\pi n f_0 t) + B_n \sin(2\pi n f_0 t)$$ - $A_0/2$ → **DC component** (average value). - $A_n, B_n$ → **Fourier coefficients** representing amplitude of the $n$-th harmonic. - Each harmonic has frequency $n f_0$. > [!IMPORTANT] > The **Bandwidth** of an analog signal is the difference between its highest and lowest frequency components. Most real signals (e.g., human voice: 300 Hz – 3400 Hz; music: 20 Hz – 20 kHz) have a defined spectral range. ### 2.2 Digital Signals — Bit Interval, Bit Rate, and Levels A **digital signal** is a sequence of discrete voltage pulses. For a binary digital signal: - **Bit Interval ($T_b$)** → Time duration of *one bit* (seconds). - **Bit Rate ($R_b$)** → $R_b = 1/T_b$ (bits per second). - **Bit Length** → Distance the signal travels in one bit interval: $\text{Bit Length} = v \times T_b$, where $v$ is propagation velocity. A digital signal with $L$ discrete amplitude levels can carry $\log_2 L$ bits per signal unit. Thus: - **Bit Rate** $R_b$ vs **Baud Rate** $S$: $$R_b = S \times \log_2 L$$ ### 2.3 Why Convert Between Analog and Digital? - **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Conversion)** involves two steps: **Sampling** + **Quantization**. - **DAC (Digital-to-Analog Conversion)** uses reconstruction filters. > [!NOTE] > **Why go digital?** Digital signals are more **noise-resistant**, easier to **encrypt**, support **error detection/correction**, and allow **time-division multiplexing (TDM)**. These advantages are why modern telecommunications (4G/5G, Ethernet, fiber optics) is overwhelmingly digital. ### 2.4 Transmission Impairities — The Three Enemies of Communication When a signal travels through a medium, it is affected by three impairments: | Impairment | Cause | Effect | |------------|-------|--------| | **Attenuation** | Energy loss over distance / medium resistance | Signal amplitude decreases | | **Distortion** | Different frequency components travel at different speeds | Signal shape changes | | **Noise** | Thermal, induced, crosstalk, impulse noise | Random unwanted signal added | #### 2.4.1 Attenuation Signal power loss, expressed in **decibels (dB)**: $$\text{Attenuation (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$ - $P_1$ → Input power, $P_2$ → Output power. - Negative dB = loss. - Attenuation is compensated by **Amplifiers** (analog) or **Repeaters** (digital). #### 2.4.2 Distortion Caused by **different propagation velocities** for different frequency components. Harmonics arrive at slightly different times, altering waveform shape. #### 2.4.3 Noise Four primary types: 1. **Thermal Noise** — caused by electron agitation; uniform across frequencies. 2. **Induced Noise** — from nearby electromagnetic sources (motors, fluorescent lights). 3. **Crosstalk** — coupling between adjacent wires. 4. **Impulse Noise** — irregular spikes from switching, lightning. **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** $$\text{SNR} = \frac{\text{Average Signal Power}}{\text{Average Noise Power}} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > A **high SNR** means the signal is much stronger than noise → reliable communication. Modern channels target SNR $\geq$ 30 dB. ### 2.5 Data Rate Limits — The Two Fundamental Theorems > [!IMPORTANT] > These two theorems are the **most heavily tested formulas** in KTU Module 1 numerical questions. #### 2.5.1 Nyquist Theorem (Noiseless Channel) For a **noiseless channel** of bandwidth $B$ Hz with $L$ discrete signal levels: $$C = 2B \log_2 L \quad \text{(bps)}$$ - $C$ → Maximum data rate (channel capacity). - $B$ → Bandwidth in Hz. - $L$ → Number of signal levels. #### 2.5.2 Shannon Theorem (Noisy Channel) For a **noisy channel** of bandwidth $B$ Hz with SNR ratio: $$C = B \log_2\left(1 + \text{SNR}\right) \quad \text{(bps)}$$ - $C$ → Theoretical maximum capacity in bps. - $\text{SNR}$ → Signal-to-noise ratio (unitless linear ratio, **not** dB). > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often plug in SNR in dB directly into Shannon's formula. **You MUST first convert** SNR from dB to a linear ratio: > $$\text{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = 10^{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10}$$ ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---------|---------|-------| | 1 | $T = 1/f$ | Period from frequency | s, Hz | | 2 | $\lambda = v/f$ | Wavelength | m | | 3 | $R_b = 1/T_b$ | Bit rate from bit interval | bps, s | | 4 | $R_b = S \cdot \log_2 L$ | Bit rate from baud rate | bps, baud | | 5 | $\text{Attenuation} = 10 \log_{10}(P_2/P_1)$ | Signal power loss | dB | | 6 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(P_s/P_n)$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dB | | 7 | $C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2B \log_2 L$ | Noiseless channel capacity | bps | | 8 | $C_{\text{Shannon}} = B \log_2(1+\text{SNR})$ | Noisy channel capacity | bps | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Telephony**: Uses 4 kHz voice channel; PCM at 64 kbps — derived from Nyquist. - **DSL Internet**: SNR measurement per tone enables **adaptive bit loading** (DMT). - **Wi-Fi (802.11)**: Uses Shannon bounds to choose modulation (BPSK → 1024-QAM) based on SNR. - **Fiber Optics**: Low noise → very high SNR → Shannon allows terabit capacities. - **5G NR**: Massive MIMO + mmWave bandwidth + Shannon-optimized coding. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Attenuation Calculation **Problem:** A signal travels through a medium. Input power is **100 mW**, output power is **0.5 mW**. Find the attenuation in dB. Is this a loss or a gain? **Step 1 — Identify given values.** $P_1 = 100 \text{ mW}$, $P_2 = 0.5 \text{ mW}$. **Step 2 — Recall the attenuation formula.** $$\text{Attenuation (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Substitute the values.** $$\text{Attenuation (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{0.5}{100}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Simplify the ratio.** $$\frac{0.5}{100} = 0.005 = 5 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the logarithm.** $$\log_{10}(0.005) = \log_{10}(5 \times 10^{-3}) = \log_{10}(5) + \log_{10}(10^{-3})$$ $$\log_{10}(5) \approx 0.69897, \quad \log_{10}(10^{-3}) = -3$$ $$\log_{10}(0.005) \approx 0.69897 - 3 = -2.30103$$ **Step 6 — Multiply by 10.** $$\text{Attenuation} = 10 \times (-2.30103) = -23.01 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 7 — Interpret.** Since the value is **negative**, this represents a **signal loss** of approximately **-23 dB** (i.e., the signal lost about 99.5% of its power). > [!NOTE] > Engineers drop the negative sign in casual conversation and say *"23 dB of loss."* But strictly, **attenuation is negative; loss magnitude is positive.** ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Nyquist Capacity (Binary Channel) **Problem:** A noiseless channel has bandwidth **4 kHz**. The signal uses **2 levels (binary)**. Find the maximum data rate. **Step 1 — Identify.** $B = 4000$ Hz, $L = 2$. **Step 2 — Apply Nyquist's formula.** $$C = 2B \log_2 L$$ **Step 3 — Compute $\log_2 2$.** $$\log_2 2 = 1$$ **Step 4 — Substitute and compute.** $$C = 2 \times 4000 \times 1 = 8000 \text{ bps} = 8 \text{ kbps}$$ **Step 5 — Interpret.** A voice channel of 4 kHz with binary signaling can carry **8 kbps** — which is exactly the basis for standard PCM telephony (sampled at 8000 samples/sec, each sample = 8 bits → 64 kbps; the *2B log₂L* result gives the upper bound for *binary* signaling in a 4 kHz channel). ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Shannon Capacity (Noisy Channel) **Problem:** A channel has bandwidth **3 kHz** and SNR of **30 dB**. Find the theoretical maximum data rate using Shannon's theorem. **Step 1 — Identify.** $B = 3000$ Hz, $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 30$. **Step 2 — Convert SNR from dB to linear ratio.** $$\text{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = 10^{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10} = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000$$ > [!WARNING] > Forgetting to convert dB to linear ratio is the **#1 reason students lose marks** in Shannon-based problems. **Step 3 — Apply Shannon's formula.** $$C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$$ **Step 4 — Substitute.** $$C = 3000 \times \log_2(1 + 1000) = 3000 \times \log_2(1001)$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate $\log_2(1001)$.** $$\log_2(1001) = \frac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{3.00043}{0.30103} \approx 9.967$$ **Step 6 — Multiply.** $$C \approx 3000 \times 9.967 \approx 29{,}901 \text{ bps} \approx 29.9 \text{ kbps}$$ **Step 7 — Interpret.** The **Shannon limit** for this channel is ~30 kbps. No coding/modulation scheme can exceed this regardless of how clever the design. ### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Bit Rate vs Baud Rate **Problem:** A signal uses **8 voltage levels**. The baud rate is **2000 symbols/second**. Find the bit rate. **Step 1 — Identify.** $L = 8$, $S = 2000$ baud. **Step 2 — Recall the relationship.** $$R_b = S \times \log_2 L$$ **Step 3 — Compute $\log_2 8$.** $$\log_2 8 = 3 \text{ bits per symbol}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute.** $$R_b = 2000 \times 3 = 6000 \text{ bps} = 6 \text{ kbps}$$ **Step 5 — Interpret.** By using 8 levels (e.g., 8-PSK or 8-QAM modulation), we transmit **3 bits per symbol**, tripling throughput compared to binary signaling at the same baud rate. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Concept-to-Formula Mapping | Concept | Governing Principle | Governing Equation | Common Trap | |---------|--------------------|--------------------|-------------| | Period ↔ Frequency | Reciprocal relation | $T = 1/f$ | Mixing up units (ms vs s) | | Wavelength | Wave physics | $\lambda = v/f$ | Using wrong $v$ (copper vs fiber vs air) | | Channel capacity (clean) | Nyquist | $C = 2B \log_2 L$ | Forgetting factor of 2 | | Channel capacity (noisy) | Shannon | $C = B \log_2(1+\text{SNR})$ | Using SNR in dB without conversion | | Power loss | Logarithmic dB | $10 \log_{10}(P_2/P_1)$ | Sign convention confusion | | Bit rate from baud | Information theory | $R_b = S \log_2 L$ | Confusing bit rate with baud rate | | SNR | Ratio expression | $10 \log_{10}(P_s/P_n)$ | Forgetting to convert to dB | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Diagram 1: Data-to-Signal Mapping (Communication System Block Flow) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Source Data
Analog or Digital] --> B[Source Encoder
Convert Data to Signal] B --> C[Transmitter
Modulation / Encoding] C --> D[Transmission Medium
Channel] D --> E[Receiver
Demodulation / Decoding] E --> F[Destination
Analog or Digital Output] D -.-> G[Noise Source
Attenuation Distortion Crosstalk] G -.-> D ``` > [!NOTE] > This flow is the **universal data communication model** (Shannon-Weaver, 1949). Every communication system — from a phone call to inter-satellite laser links — follows this skeleton. ### 4.2 Diagram 2: Analog vs Digital Signal Waveform Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph A["ANALOG SIGNAL"] A1[Continuous Time Axis] --> A2[Continuous Amplitude Axis] A2 --> A3[Infinitely many states] A3 --> A4[Example: Sine Wave Voice Signal] end subgraph B["DIGITAL SIGNAL"] B1[Discrete Time Axis] --> B2[Discrete Amplitude Axis] B2 --> B3[Finite number of states typically 2] B3 --> B4[Example: NRZ Binary Pulses] end ``` ### 4.3 Diagram 3: Fourier Decomposition of a Periodic Signal ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Periodic Signal
s of t] --> F1[DC Component
A0 over 2] P --> F2[Fundamental
n equals 1 frequency f0] P --> F3[2nd Harmonic
n equals 2 frequency 2f0] P --> F4[3rd Harmonic
n equals 3 frequency 3f0] P --> F5[nth Harmonic
frequency nf0] F1 --> SUM[Sum of Sinusoids
Reconstructs s of t] F2 --> SUM F3 --> SUM F4 --> SUM F5 --> SUM ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > In KTU Module 1, students are expected to recognize that **bandwidth = highest frequency – lowest frequency of a signal's spectrum**. The Fourier concept unlocks this calculation. ### 4.4 Diagram 4: Transmission Impairities Block Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Original Signal sent] --> T[Transmission Medium] T --> A[Attenuation
Power decreases with distance] T --> D[Distortion
Waveform shape changes] T --> N[Noise
Unwanted random energy] A --> R[Received Signal impaired] D --> R N --> R R --> O[Output Signal degraded] ``` ### 4.5 Diagram 5: Nyquist vs Shannon Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Q[Is the channel NOISELESS] -->|Yes| NY[Apply Nyquist
C equals 2B log2 L] Q -->|No| SH[Apply Shannon
C equals B log2 1 plus SNR] NY --> R1[Capacity in bps] SH --> R2[Capacity in bps] R1 --> COM[Compare with actual bit rate] R2 --> COM ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Distinguish between analog and digital data. Give one example for each.* **Model Answer:** **Analog data** are continuous-valued entities that take on any value within a range and vary smoothly over time. The information is represented by infinitely many possible states. *Example:* Human voice (air pressure variation) or temperature recorded by a mercury thermometer. **Digital data** are discrete-valued entities that take on a finite set of values, typically represented in binary form (0s and 1s). *Example:* Text stored in a computer file (ASCII codes) or temperature measured by a digital sensor as integer values. | Parameter | Analog Data | Digital Data | |-----------|-------------|--------------| | Values | Continuous | Discrete | | States | Infinite | Finite | | Storage | Difficult | Easy | | Noise Immunity | Low | High | | Example | Voice | Text file | **(3 marks — 1 mark definition each, 1 mark example/comparison)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define bandwidth. What is the bandwidth of a signal whose spectrum ranges from 200 Hz to 4 kHz?* **Model Answer:** **Bandwidth** is the width of the range of frequencies occupied by a signal or passed by a channel. Mathematically, it is the difference between the highest and lowest frequency components. $$B = f_{\text{highest}} - f_{\text{lowest}}$$ Given $f_{\text{high}} = 4000$ Hz and $f_{\text{low}} = 200$ Hz: $$B = 4000 - 200 = 3800 \text{ Hz} = 3.8 \text{ kHz}$$ **(3 marks — 1 mark definition, 1 mark formula, 1 mark computation)** --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — ESE Module Internal Choice) #### Question A (14 Marks) **Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the three main transmission impairments in a communication system. How are they measured/quantified? **(7 marks)** **(b)** A channel of bandwidth **4 kHz** is used with **16 signal levels**. The SNR is **24 dB**. - (i) Find the maximum data rate using Nyquist's theorem. **(3 marks)** - (ii) Find the maximum data rate using Shannon's theorem. **(4 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Q.A(a):** The three main transmission impairments are: **1. Attenuation:** Attenuation is the **loss of signal power** as it propagates through a medium. It occurs because the medium absorbs or dissipates energy. Attenuation is measured in **decibels (dB)**: $$\text{Attenuation}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right)$$ A negative value indicates power loss. Attenuation increases with distance. To compensate, **amplifiers** (analog) or **repeaters** (digital) are used. **2. Distortion:** Distortion is the **alteration of signal shape** due to different frequency components traveling at different velocities through the medium. This phenomenon is called **dispersion**. In particular, harmonic components of a composite signal may arrive at different phases, causing the received signal to look different from the transmitted one — even though its amplitude may be preserved. **3. Noise:** Noise refers to **unwanted random energy** added to the signal. Common types: - **Thermal noise** (from electron motion; uniform across all frequencies — *white noise*). - **Induced noise** (from external EM sources). - **Crosstalk** (coupling from neighboring wires). - **Impulse noise** (irregular spikes from switching transients/lightning). Quantification is done via **SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio)** in dB: $$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$$ > **[7 marks distributed: 2 marks attenuation, 2 marks distortion, 2 marks noise, 1 mark quantification]** --- **Model Solution to Q.A(b):** **Part (i) — Nyquist Capacity:** Given: $B = 4000$ Hz, $L = 16$. $$C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2B \log_2 L = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2 16$$ $$\log_2 16 = 4$$ $$C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2 \times 4000 \times 4 = 32{,}000 \text{ bps} = 32 \text{ kbps}$$ **Part (ii) — Shannon Capacity:** Given: $B = 4000$ Hz, $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 24$. **Step 1 — Convert SNR to linear ratio:** $$\text{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = 10^{24/10} = 10^{2.4} \approx 251.19$$ **Step 2 — Apply Shannon's formula:** $$C_{\text{Shannon}} = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) = 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 251.19)$$ $$C_{\text{Shannon}} = 4000 \times \log_2(252.19)$$ $$\log_2(252.19) = \frac{\log_{10}(252.19)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{2.4018}{0.3010} \approx 7.978$$ $$C_{\text{Shannon}} \approx 4000 \times 7.978 \approx 31{,}912 \text{ bps} \approx 31.9 \text{ kbps}$$ **Step 3 — Interpretation:** Since $C_{\text{Shannon}} \approx 31.9$ kbps $< C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 32$ kbps, the **practical capacity is bounded by Shannon's theorem at ~31.9 kbps**.
