The noiseless Nyquist capacity is the theoretical *upper* limit, but noise imposes a slightly lower cap. > **[3 + 4 marks: 1 mark formula, 1 mark computation for Nyquist; 1 mark dB conversion, 2 marks Shannon computation, 1 mark interpretation]** --- #### Question B (14 Marks) **Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]** **(a)** With neat block diagrams, explain a digital communication system. List any two advantages of digital over analog communication. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A signal travels through a transmission medium. The input power is **50 mW** and the output power is **0.1 mW**. - (i) Calculate the attenuation in dB. **(3 marks)** - (ii) If the noise power measured at the receiver is **0.01 mW**, calculate the SNR in dB. **(4 marks)** --- **Model Solution to Q.B(a):** A **digital communication system** transmits information as a sequence of discrete symbols (typically binary bits). The block diagram is: **Source** → **Source Encoder** → **Channel Encoder** → **Digital Modulator** → **Channel** → **Digital Demodulator** → **Channel Decoder** → **Source Decoder** → **Destination** **Functions of Each Block:** 1. **Source**: Generates data (e.g., text, audio, video). 2. **Source Encoder**: Converts source data into a compact digital form (e.g., compression, A/D conversion for analog sources). 3. **Channel Encoder**: Adds redundancy for **error detection/correction** (e.g., Hamming, CRC). 4. **Digital Modulator**: Converts digital bits into signal waveforms suitable for the channel (e.g., NRZ, Manchester, QAM). 5. **Channel**: Physical medium (copper, fiber, wireless). 6. **Digital Demodulator**: Recovers bits from received waveform. 7. **Channel Decoder**: Detects/corrects errors using redundancy. 8. **Source Decoder**: Reconstructs the original data form. 9. **Destination**: Final user/device. **Two Advantages of Digital over Analog:** 1. **Noise Immunity**: Digital signals use discrete voltage levels, so small noise perturbations do not change the bit value (decision threshold at midpoint). Analog signals accumulate noise at every stage. 2. **Easy Multiplexing & Encryption**: TDM, encryption, and error correction are natural with discrete bits. Analog requires complex analog circuits. 3. *(Bonus)*: Better **regeneration** via repeaters; efficient storage; integration with computing. > **[7 marks: 3 marks diagram, 2 marks block descriptions, 2 marks advantages]** --- **Model Solution to Q.B(b):** **Part (i) — Attenuation:** Given: $P_{\text{in}} = 50$ mW, $P_{\text{out}} = 0.1$ mW. $$\text{Attenuation}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{0.1}{50}\right)$$ $$\frac{0.1}{50} = 0.002 = 2 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\log_{10}(0.002) = \log_{10}(2) + \log_{10}(10^{-3}) = 0.3010 - 3 = -2.6990$$ $$\text{Attenuation} = 10 \times (-2.6990) = -26.99 \text{ dB} \approx -27 \text{ dB}$$ **Interpretation:** Signal has lost about **27 dB** (a power ratio of ~500:1) during transmission. **Part (ii) — SNR:** Given: $P_{\text{signal}}$ at receiver $\approx P_{\text{out}} = 0.1$ mW, $P_{\text{noise}} = 0.01$ mW. $$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{0.1}{0.01}\right)$$ $$\frac{0.1}{0.01} = 10$$ $$\log_{10}(10) = 1$$ $$\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \times 1 = 10 \text{ dB}$$ **Interpretation:** Signal power is **10 times** the noise power — a relatively low SNR; communication will be unreliable without strong error correction. > **[3 + 4 marks: 1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark answer (i); 1 mark formula, 1 mark ratio, 1 mark log evaluation, 1 mark answer + interpretation (ii)]** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting dB-to-linear conversion** in Shannon's formula. If SNR is 30 dB, use $10^3 = 1000$, not 30. > 2. **Sign convention in attenuation**: Negative dB = loss. Don't write "positive 27 dB attenuation." > 3. **Bit Rate vs Baud Rate**: Many students use the terms interchangeably. They are **different** — bit rate is in bps, baud rate is in symbols/second. They are equal *only* for binary signaling. > 4. **Units mismatch**: Bandwidth must be in Hz, not kHz, when plugging into Nyquist/Shannon formulas. Convert first. > 5. **Nyquist assumes noiseless channel**; **Shannon applies to noisy channel**. Don't mix the two formulas. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 1 — Analog & Digital Data and Signals:** - ✅ **Data** = meaning/information; **Signal** = physical representation of data. - ✅ **Analog data** = continuous; **Digital data** = discrete (e.g., binary). - ✅ **Analog signal** = continuous in time & amplitude; **Digital signal** = discrete in both. - ✅ **Periodic signal**: $s(t+T) = s(t)$; period $T = 1/f$. - ✅ **Fourier Series**: Any periodic signal = sum of sinusoids → defines **bandwidth**. - ✅ **Bandwidth** $B = f_{\max} - f_{\min}$ (Hz). - ✅ **Bit rate** $R_b = 1/T_b$ (bps); **Baud rate** $S$ = symbols/second. - ✅ **Relation**: $R_b = S \log_2 L$, where $L$ = number of signal levels. - ✅ **Three impairments**: Attenuation (power loss), Distortion (shape change), Noise (random energy). - ✅ **Attenuation (dB)** $= 10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{out}}/P_{\text{in}})$. - ✅ **SNR (dB)** $= 10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{signal}}/P_{\text{noise}})$. - ✅ **Nyquist (noiseless)**: $C = 2B \log_2 L$. - ✅ **Shannon (noisy)**: $C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR})$, with SNR in **linear** ratio. - ✅ Always convert **dB → linear** before using Shannon's theorem. - ✅ Digital signals are **noise-immune**, support **encryption**, **error control**, and **multiplexing** — the reasons for the digital revolution. - ✅ The four noise types: **thermal, induced, crosstalk, impulse**. - ✅ Modulation converts digital bits → analog waveforms (and vice versa) to match channel characteristics.

topics.title: Transmission Impairments - Attenuation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transmission Impairments - Attenuation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Academic Definition**
> **Attenuation** is the loss of signal strength (signal power) that occurs as a communication signal propagates through a transmission medium (copper wire, optical fibre, free space, etc.). It is one of the three fundamental **transmission impairments** identified by Forouzan's *Data Communications and Networking* framework, the others being **Distortion** and **Noise**.

Mathematically, attenuation expresses the *reduction* in amplitude/intensity of a signal as a function of distance travelled. The standard unit used in the KTU 2024 syllabus is the **decibel (dB)**, which is a logarithmic ratio and not an absolute unit.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE] **Conceptual Analogy — "The Whispering Tunnel"**
> Imagine shouting a message to a friend standing 500 metres down a long concrete tunnel.
> - **At 10 m:** Your friend hears you clearly.
> - **At 100 m:** The voice is noticeably softer.
> - **At 500 m:** Only the loudest syllables reach them, garbled and faint.
>
> The *energy* of your voice did not vanish — it was *absorbed* by the tunnel walls, scattered by air molecules, and spread out as the wavefront expanded. The medium "ate" some of the signal.
>
> **In a copper cable**, the conductor's resistance converts a small portion of the electrical energy into heat.
> **In an optical fibre**, microscopic impurities and micro-bends scatter the light photons.
> **In free space**, the wavefront spreads geometrically (spreading loss) and is partially absorbed by atmospheric gases.

This is **attenuation** — the gradual weakening of a signal over distance.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Decibel (dB):** Logarithmic unit expressing the ratio between two power (or voltage) levels. It is a *ratio*, not an absolute quantity.
- **dBm:** Absolute power referenced to **1 milliwatt (1 mW)**. Used when absolute signal strength must be reported.
- **dBW:** Absolute power referenced to **1 watt (1 W)**.
- **Attenuation Constant (α):** Measured in **dB per kilometre** (dB/km) for guided media and **dB per metre** for free-space radio.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Syllabus Highlight**
> KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 1) requires students to be able to:
> 1. Express attenuation in **dB** for a given pair of input/output power values.
> 2. Convert between **dB, dBm, and linear power ratios**.
> 3. Discuss the *cause* of attenuation in metallic, optical, and wireless media.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does Attenuation Occur? — The Physics Behind It

The energy of an electromagnetic or electrical signal is progressively lost due to three physical phenomena, depending on the medium:

1. **Conductor Resistance (Copper Media):** As electrons drift through the copper conductor, collisions with the lattice atoms convert a fraction of the signal energy into **thermal energy (heat)**. This is governed by the **Joule Heating Law** ($P = I^2 R$).

2. **Absorption & Scattering (Optical Fibre):** Photons interact with impurities (e.g., OH⁻ ions in silica) and microscopic density variations. This is described by the **Beer–Lambert Law** for optical media.

3. **Free-Space Path Loss (Wireless):** The transmitted wavefront expands spherically; the same energy is distributed over an ever-larger surface area. Atmospheric gases (oxygen, water vapour) and precipitation also cause **absorption and scattering**.

## 2.2 Why Decibels? — The "Why" Behind the Logarithm

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight**
> Signal losses in real systems span **many orders of magnitude**. For example, an optical signal travelling from Earth to a geostationary satellite and back is attenuated by a factor of ~10⁻²⁰.
> Handling such ratios linearly would require either enormous numbers or scientific notation everywhere. The **logarithmic decibel scale compresses** these huge dynamic ranges into small, manageable, additive numbers. **Losses in dB simply add** when signals pass through cascaded media — a property the linear ratio does not possess.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Attenuation in dB (Power) | $A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | **dB**; $P_1$ is input, $P_2$ is output |
| 2 | Attenuation in dB (Voltage) | $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)$ | **dB**; valid when impedance is matched |
| 3 | Power ratio from dB | $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = 10^{A_{dB}/10}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 4 | Absolute Power in dBm | $P_{dBm} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{1\ \text{mW}}\right)$ | **dBm**; reference = 1 mW |
| 5 | Absolute Power in dBW | $P_{dBW} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P}{1\ \text{W}}\right)$ | **dBW**; reference = 1 W |
| 6 | Relation dBm ↔ dBW | $P_{dBW} = P_{dBm} - 30$ | Since $1\ \text{W} = 1000\ \text{mW}$ |
| 7 | Total cascaded attenuation | $A_{total} = A_1 + A_2 + \dots + A_n$ | **dB**; additive property |
| 8 | Attenuation per unit length | $\alpha = \dfrac{A_{dB}}{L}$ | **dB/km** or **dB/m** |
| 9 | Received power | $P_{rx} = P_{tx} \cdot 10^{-A_{dB}/10}$ | Watts; $A_{dB}$ is positive for loss |
| 10 | Free-space path loss (Friis) | $FSPL = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | **dB**; $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz |

> [!IMPORTANT] **Sign Convention (Board-Exam Favourite)**
> - **Positive dB** ⟹ Power *decreased* (loss/attenuation).
> - **Negative dB** ⟹ Power *increased* (gain/amplification).
> Always check whether the formula expects $P_2/P_1$ (loss) or $P_1/P_2$ (gain). The KTU board expects you to mention this convention explicitly.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Optical Submarine Cables:** Repeaters (EDFAs — Erbium-Doped Fibre Amplifiers) are placed every ~80 km because standard single-mode fibre attenuates at ~0.2 dB/km at the 1550 nm window.
- **5G / Satellite Links:** Link budget calculations use cascaded dB values for transmit power, antenna gains, FSPL, and receiver sensitivity to verify that the received power exceeds the noise floor.
- **DSL / Ethernet Cabling:** Cat-6 cable attenuation (~22 dB at 100 m for 100 MHz) determines the **maximum cable run length** specified in IEEE 802.3 (100 m for Gigabit Ethernet).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Linear Attenuation to the Decibel

We start with a continuous transmission medium whose attenuation behaviour is *exponential* — a fundamental result from electromagnetic wave theory (governed by the wave equation with a lossy term).

**Step 1 — Write the exponential power-decay model.**
Assume a signal with initial power $P_1$ travels a distance $L$ in a medium. The power *decays exponentially* with distance:

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot e^{-\alpha L}$$

where $\alpha$ is the medium's *linear attenuation coefficient* (in nepers per metre, Np/m).

**Step 2 — Convert the natural exponent to base 10.**
Using the identity $e^x = 10^{x / \ln 10}$ and $\ln 10 \approx 2.3026$:

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / \ln 10} = P_1 \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 2.3026}$$

**Step 3 — Take the logarithm base 10 of the ratio.**
Rearrange to isolate the exponent:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = 10^{-\alpha L / 2.3026}$$

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\frac{\alpha L}{2.3026}$$

**Step 4 — Define the decibel.**
The *decibel* is defined as 10 times the base-10 logarithm of the power ratio:

$$A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Relate α (Np/m) to the engineering constant.**
Substituting Step 3 into Step 4:

$$A_{dB} = 10 \cdot \left(-\frac{\alpha L}{2.3026}\right) = -4.343 \, \alpha L$$

**Step 6 — Derive the per-unit-length attenuation constant in dB/km.**

$$\boxed{\;\alpha_{dB/km} = \frac{A_{dB}}{L} = 4.343 \cdot \alpha_{Np/m}\;}$$

> [!NOTE] **Why this matters for KTU 2024**
> Many students lose marks because they confuse **Nepers** (a linear, natural-log unit) with **decibels**. The conversion constant **4.343** (which is $10 \cdot \log_{10}(e)$) is frequently asked in Part A.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Examples

### Example 1 — Basic dB Calculation (Board Pattern)

> **Problem:** A signal with input power **100 mW** is measured at the receiver as **0.5 mW** after travelling through 5 km of cable. Find the attenuation in dB and the attenuation per km.

**Solution:**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{0.5\ \text{mW}}{100\ \text{mW}} = 0.005$$

$$A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(0.005)$$

$$= 10 \times (-2.3010)$$

$$= -23.01\ \text{dB}$$

The negative sign indicates loss. Therefore, **Attenuation = 23.01 dB** over 5 km.

$$\alpha = \frac{23.01\ \text{dB}}{5\ \text{km}} = 4.602\ \text{dB/km}$$

---

### Example 2 — dBm Conversion (Board Pattern)

> **Problem:** A transmitter outputs **2 W**. Express this power in dBm and dBW. If a free-space path introduces a loss of 93 dB, find the received power in mW.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Convert 2 W to dBW:**

$$P_{dBW} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2}{1}\right) = 10 \times 0.30103 = 3.01\ \text{dBW}$$

**Part (b) — Convert 2 W to dBm:**

$$P_{dBm} = P_{dBW} + 30 = 3.01 + 30 = 33.01\ \text{dBm}$$

**Part (c) — Received power after 93 dB loss:**

$$P_{rx(dBm)} = 33.01 - 93 = -59.99\ \text{dBm} \approx -60\ \text{dBm}$$

**Convert −60 dBm to mW:**

$$-60 = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{rx}}{1\ \text{mW}}\right)$$

$$10^{-6} = \frac{P_{rx}}{1\ \text{mW}}$$

$$P_{rx} = 10^{-6}\ \text{mW} = 10^{-9}\ \text{W} = 1\ \text{nW}$$

---

### Example 3 — Cascaded Attenuation (Board Pattern)

> **Problem:** A signal passes through three segments: 2 km cable with α₁ = 4 dB/km, 3 km fibre with α₂ = 0.5 dB/km, and a connector with 2 dB loss. Input power is 10 mW. Find the received power.

**Solution:**

$$A_1 = 4 \times 2 = 8\ \text{dB}$$

$$A_2 = 0.5 \times 3 = 1.5\ \text{dB}$$

$$A_3 = 2\ \text{dB}$$

$$A_{total} = 8 + 1.5 + 2 = 11.5\ \text{dB}$$

$$P_{rx} = P_{tx} \cdot 10^{-A_{total}/10} = 10\ \text{mW} \cdot 10^{-1.15}$$

$$= 10 \times 0.07079 = 0.7079\ \text{mW} \approx 708\ \mu\text{W}$$

---

## 3.3 Attenuation Distortion — A Sub-Category

> [!IMPORTANT] **Concept Note**
> **Attenuation Distortion** is the *frequency-dependent* variation of attenuation within a signal's bandwidth. A composite signal (e.g., a square wave) contains many frequency components; if the medium attenuates high frequencies more than low frequencies (typical of copper wires), the received waveform becomes rounded/distorted.
>
> This is measured by transmitting a signal of known frequency at **0 dBm** at the sender and measuring the received power (in dBm) over the channel's operating band. The variation is the **attenuation distortion**.

### Compensation Techniques

- **Equalizers:** Insert a filter with the *inverse* frequency response, boosting the attenuated frequencies back.
- **Loading Coils (older telephone systems):** Inductors added at intervals to flatten the frequency response.
- **Active Repeaters/Amplifiers:** Boost overall signal level (but also amplify noise — a key limitation).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Attenuation in a Communication Link

This diagram maps the functional flow of a signal from source to destination, showing where and how attenuation is introduced.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Source Transmitter] -->|P1 input power| chanA[Transmission Medium Segment 1]
    chanA -->|After A1 dB loss| connA[Connectors and Splices]
    connA -->|After A2 dB loss| chanB[Transmission Medium Segment 2]
    chanB -->|After A3 dB loss| repA[Optional Repeater or Amplifier]
    repA -->|After Gain G dB| chanC[Transmission Medium Segment 3]
    chanC -->|After A4 dB loss| rcvA[Destination Receiver]
    rcvA -->|P2 received power| outA[Output Signal]

    classDef lossBox fill:#ffe1e1,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef gainBox fill:#e1ffe1,stroke:#008000,color:#000
    classDef nodeBox fill:#e1ecff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    class srcA,rcvA,outA nodeBox
    class chanA,chanB,chanC,connA lossBox
    class repA gainBox
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Every red block **adds** dB of loss; the green block (repeater) **adds** dB of gain. The arithmetic sum of all red values minus the green value is the **net link attenuation** in dB.

## 4.2 Frequency-vs-Attenuation Response Curve

This conceptual graph illustrates **attenuation distortion** — different frequencies suffer different amounts of loss in a typical copper medium.

```mermaid
graph LR
    axX[Frequency f in Hz]:::axis --> axY[Attenuation in dB]:::axis
    px1[Low f region: small loss]:::low --> px1
    px2[Mid f region: moderate loss]:::mid --> px2
    px3[High f region: high loss]:::high --> px3

    classDef axis fill:#f0f0f0,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef low fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633,color:#000
    classDef mid fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef high fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    class axX,axY axis
    class px1 low
    class px2 mid
    class px3 high
```

> **Key takeaway:** A medium that *uniformly* attenuates all frequencies equally causes **flat attenuation** (only amplitude loss, no shape distortion). A medium that attenuates frequencies *unequally* causes **attenuation distortion** (shape loss). The first is corrected by amplification; the second requires **equalization**.

## 4.3 Link-Budget Calculation Block (Sequential Steps)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stA[Step 1: Identify Tx power in dBm] --> stB[Step 2: Add Tx antenna gain in dBi]
    stB --> stC[Step 3: Subtract all cable losses in dB]
    stC --> stD[Step 4: Subtract free space path loss in dB]
    stD --> stE[Step 5: Subtract connector and splice losses in dB]
    stE --> stF[Step 6: Add Rx antenna gain in dBi]
    stF --> stG[Step 7: Result equals Rx power in dBm]
    stG --> stH{Is Rx power above receiver sensitivity threshold?}
    stH -->|Yes| okL[Link is viable]
    stH -->|No| failL[Link fails: increase Tx power or use higher gain antenna]

    classDef stepBox fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000
    classDef decBox fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef okBox fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#006633,color:#000
    classDef failBox fill:#ffd6cc,stroke:#990000,color:#000
    class stA,stB,stC,stD,stE,stF,stG stepBox
    class stH decBox
    class okL okBox
    class failL failBox
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define attenuation. What is its unit, and how is it expressed in decibels?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Attenuation is the loss of signal strength (power) as a signal propagates through a transmission medium. It is a type of transmission impairment caused by resistance, absorption, scattering, or free-space spreading. It is expressed in **decibels (dB)** using the formula:

$$A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)\ \text{dB}$$

where $P_1$ is the transmitted (input) power and $P_2$ is the received (output) power. A **positive dB value indicates a loss** in signal power.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between dB and dBm.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | dB (Decibel) | dBm (Decibel-milliwatt) |
|---|---|---|
| Type | **Relative** ratio | **Absolute** power |
| Reference | Ratio of two powers | Referenced to **1 mW** |
| Formula | $10 \log_{10}(P_2 / P_1)$ | $10 \log_{10}(P / 1\ \text{mW})$ |
| Use | Comparing input vs output | Stating actual signal level |
| Example | "Cable has 6 dB loss" | "Transmitter outputs +20 dBm" |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### 🔹 Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for attenuation in decibels. Explain why the decibel is preferred over a linear power ratio in practical communication systems. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A signal of power **50 mW** is transmitted over a **4 km** cable. At the receiver, the measured power is **0.2 mW**. Calculate:
  (i) Total attenuation in dB.
  (ii) Attenuation per km.
  (iii) Received power in dBm.
  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of Attenuation in dB (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Definition of attenuation ratio:**
Attenuation is fundamentally a *ratio* of output power $P_2$ to input power $P_1$:

$$\text{Linear Attenuation Ratio} = \frac{P_2}{P_1}$$

**Step 2 — Limitation of the linear ratio:**
Real communication links may have loss factors ranging from 0.5 to $10^{-20}$. Storing/comparing such numbers linearly is cumbersome.

**Step 3 — Introduce the logarithm:**
The *Bel* is defined as $\log_{10}(P_2 / P_1)$. Because one Bel is too coarse, the **decibel** is used (1 Bel = 10 dB):

$$A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Justification of using dB:**
1. **Compression of range:** $10^{-20}$ loss becomes −200 dB.
2. **Additivity:** Cascaded losses in dB simply add: $A_{total} = A_1 + A_2 + A_3 + \dots$
3. **Perception-friendly:** Human senses (ear, eye) respond logarithmically to stimuli; dB matches perceptual scales.
4. **Standardisation:** All international link-budget calculations use dB; engineers can communicate unambiguously.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $P_1 = 50$ mW, $P_2 = 0.2$ mW, $L = 4$ km.

**(i) Total attenuation:**

$$A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.2}{50}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(0.004)$$

$$= 10 \times (-2.3979) = -23.98\ \text{dB}$$

**Attenuation = 23.98 dB** (positive loss value).

**(ii) Attenuation per km:**

$$\alpha = \frac{23.98\ \text{dB}}{4\ \text{km}} = 5.995\ \text{dB/km} \approx 6\ \text{dB/km}$$

**(iii) Received power in dBm:**

$$P_{rx(dBm)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.2\ \text{mW}}{1\ \text{mW}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(0.2) = -6.99\ \text{dBm}$$

---

### 🔹 Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]`

**(a)** Explain the three main causes of attenuation in (i) copper cables, (ii) optical fibres, and (iii) free-space wireless media. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fibre-optic link uses a laser transmitter of power **5 dBm**. The fibre has attenuation **0.4 dB/km** and is **60 km** long. Two connectors each introduce **0.8 dB** of loss. Compute the received power in dBm and mW. Comment on whether an amplifier is required if the receiver sensitivity is **−30 dBm**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Causes of Attenuation (7 marks):**

| Medium | Cause | Physical Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Copper Cable** | Conductor resistance + dielectric loss | Signal energy converted to heat (Joule heating: $P = I^2 R$); skin effect at high frequencies; leakage through insulation |
| **Optical Fibre** | Absorption + scattering | Impurities (e.g., OH⁻) absorb photons; Rayleigh scattering from microscopic density variations; macro/micro-bends cause radiation loss |
| **Free-Space Wireless** | Path loss + atmospheric absorption | Spherical spreading (FSPL) reduces power density with $d^2$; oxygen and water-vapour resonance absorption; rain/snow scattering |

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:** $P_{tx} = +5$ dBm, $\alpha = 0.4$ dB/km, $L = 60$ km, two connectors @ 0.8 dB each.

**Step 1 — Fibre loss:**

$$A_{fibre} = 0.4 \times 60 = 24\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 2 — Connector loss:**

$$A_{conn} = 2 \times 0.8 = 1.6\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 3 — Total attenuation:**

$$A_{total} = 24 + 1.6 = 25.6\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 4 — Received power in dBm:**

$$P_{rx(dBm)} = 5 - 25.6 = -20.6\ \text{dBm}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mW:**

$$-20.6 = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{rx}}{1\ \text{mW}}\right)$$

$$10^{-2.06} = P_{rx}$$

$$P_{rx} = 0.00871\ \text{mW} = 8.71\ \mu\text{W}$$

**Step 6 — Decision on amplifier:**

Receiver sensitivity = **−30 dBm**. Since received power **−20.6 dBm > −30 dBm**, the signal is **9.4 dB above the sensitivity floor** ⟹ **No amplifier is required**, but the link has only a 9.4 dB **power margin**, so a safety margin for ageing/repairs is recommended.

---

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Sign Error (–2 marks):** Reporting attenuation as −23.98 dB without clarifying "loss = 23.98 dB" is treated as a sign convention blunder.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert to dBm (–1 mark):** dB and dBm are *not interchangeable*. A "6 dB" cable loss is not the same as a "6 dBm" power.
> 3. **Linear ratio errors (–2 marks):** Using voltage formula $20 \log_{10}(V_2/V_1)$ when the problem gives *power* values leads to a wrong answer.
> 4. **Unit mismatch (–1 mark):** Mixing dB/km with km but forgetting to multiply by length is a frequent error.
> 5. **No justification for dB usage (–1 mark):** In Part A derivations, simply writing the formula without explaining *why* the decibel is preferred loses the application mark.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Attenuation = loss of signal power** with distance; measured in **dB** (ratio) or **dBm/dBW** (absolute).
- ✅ **Positive dB ⟹ loss; Negative dB ⟹ gain.** Always state the convention explicitly.
- ✅ Use the **power formula** $A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(P_2/P_1)$; use the **voltage formula** $A_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(V_2/V_1)$ only with matched impedances.
- ✅ **Cascaded losses add in dB** — a property unique to logarithmic units.
- ✅ **1 dBm = 30 dBW** (always remember the +30 / −30 conversion).
- ✅ **Attenuation distortion** is the *frequency-dependent* part; corrected using **equalizers**, not just amplifiers.
- ✅ **Conversion constant** between Nepers and decibels: **1 Np = 8.686 dB**, hence $\alpha_{dB} = 8.686 \cdot \alpha_{Np}$ (equivalently, $4.343 \cdot \alpha$ for *per metre* scaling).
- ✅ **Free-space path loss (Friis):** $FSPL = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ — frequently asked in Part B.
- ✅ Common cable values to memorise: **Coaxial ≈ 3–6 dB/100 m**, **Twisted pair ≈ 6–10 dB/100 m**, **Single-mode fibre ≈ 0.2–0.4 dB/km**.
- ✅ **Link budget = Tx power + Gains − Losses** must yield a received power above the **receiver sensitivity threshold**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Delay Distortion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Delay Distortion ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) **Delay Distortion** (also called **Phase Distortion** or **Dispersion Distortion**) is a form of **linear signal distortion** in a transmission medium in which different frequency components of a composite signal propagate at **different phase velocities**, causing them to arrive at the receiver at **different times**. This results in the spreading (smearing) of the original signal pulse and is a direct consequence of the **frequency-dependent nature of the phase constant $\beta(\omega)$** inside waveguides, optical fibers, and band-limited channels. Mathematically, delay distortion is quantified through the **group delay**: $$ \tau_g(\omega) \;=\; \frac{d\beta(\omega)}{d\omega} \quad \text{[seconds per metre]} $$ A medium where the group delay is constant (i.e., $d\tau_g/d\omega = 0$) is called **non-dispersive**. A medium where $\tau_g$ varies with $\omega$ is called **dispersive**, and it is in dispersive media that delay distortion arises. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Delay Distortion is treated as a transmission-impairment topic under the *Signals & Transmission Media* unit of Module 1. It is conceptually distinct from **Attenuation Distortion** (loss varying with frequency) and **Noise** (unwanted random additions). The KTU examiner frequently asks students to contrast these three impairments. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Marathon Runners Imagine a marathon where all runners start together but each wears shoes of a different design: - Some shoes are designed for slow, steady pacing (low frequencies). - Others are sprint shoes optimized for bursts (high frequencies). - Although they all start at the same instant, the **finish line (receiver)** sees them arriving at **different times**, stretched out over a long interval. The signal pulse is the "starting pack"; the **transmission line is the race track**; and the **frequency-dependent speed is the variable shoe design**. The result at the receiver is a smeared, elongated version of the original pulse — this is delay distortion. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** Even though the *amplitude* of each frequency component is preserved (no attenuation distortion here), the *relative timing* between components is destroyed, which destroys the wave shape of the original message. ### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics | Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | |---|---|---| | $c$ | Speed of light in free space | $3 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s** | | $f_c$ | Cut-off frequency of the waveguide | **Hz** | | $\beta(\omega)$ | Phase (propagation) constant | **rad/m** | | $v_p$ | Phase velocity | **m/s** | | $v_g$ | Group (signal) velocity | **m/s** | | $\tau_g$ | Group delay per unit length | **s/m** | | $\alpha$ | Attenuation constant | **Np/m** or **dB/m** | | $\gamma$ | Propagation constant, $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ | **m$^{-1}$** | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Why Does Delay Distortion Occur? The root cause is the **non-linear relationship between $\omega$ and $\beta$** inside any physically realisable waveguide. Inside a hollow metallic waveguide operating above cut-off: $$ \beta(\omega) \;=\; \frac{\omega}{c}\,\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\omega_c}{\omega}\right)^{2}} $$ Since this square-root term is a **non-linear function of $\omega$**, the slope $d\beta/d\omega$ is not constant. Therefore, the **group delay $\tau_g = d\beta/d\omega$ is frequency-dependent**, and a wideband pulse gets stretched. ### 2.2 The Three Key Velocities 1. **Phase velocity** — the speed of a single-frequency wave's *crests*: $$ v_p \;=\; \frac{\omega}{\beta} $$ 2. **Group velocity** — the speed of the *envelope* (and hence the energy/information) of a narrow-band pulse: $$ v_g \;=\; \frac{d\omega}{d\beta} \;=\; \frac{1}{d\beta/d\omega} $$ 3. **Energy/Front velocity** — never exceeds $c$; the true speed of information in a relativistic sense. > [!NOTE] > **Vital KTU Trap:** For a rectangular waveguide, $v_p > c$ while $v_g < c$, and they satisfy $v_p \cdot v_g = c^{2}$. A common KTU question asks the student to **prove** this relation. Many students incorrectly state that $v_p$ violates relativity; it does not, because no *information* travels at $v_p$ — only the unobservable phase crests do. ### 2.3 How Delay Distortion Manifests in a Pulse Consider a Gaussian-shaped input pulse carrying information. The pulse can be decomposed (Fourier) into a continuous band of frequencies. Each frequency $f_i$ propagates with its own group delay $\tau_g(f_i)$. After travelling length $L$: $$ T_i \;=\; \tau_g(f_i)\cdot L $$ The output pulse is the superposition of all these delayed versions: $$ y(t) \;=\; \int X(f)\,e^{j\,2\pi f\,[t - \tau_g(f)\,L]}\,df $$ Because $\tau_g(f)$ is not constant, the components of the pulse no longer line up, and the output pulse is **broadened and attenuated at the peak**. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $v_p = \omega/\beta$ | Phase velocity definition | | 2 | $v_g = d\omega/d\beta$ | Group velocity definition | | 3 | $v_p \cdot v_g = c^{2}$ | Waveguide energy–phase duality | | 4 | $\beta = (\omega/c)\sqrt{1-(\omega_c/\omega)^{2}}$ | Phase constant inside a hollow waveguide | | 5 | $v_p = c / \sqrt{1-(\omega_c/\omega)^{2}}$ | Phase velocity in waveguide | | 6 | $v_g = c\,\sqrt{1-(\omega_c/\omega)^{2}}$ | Group velocity in waveguide | | 7 | $\tau_g = d\beta/d\omega$ | Group delay per unit length | | 8 | $\tau_g = L / v_g$ | Total group delay over length $L$ | | 9 | $\gamma = \alpha + j\beta$ | Complex propagation constant | | 10 | $\Delta t = (\tau_{g,\max} - \tau_{g,\min})\cdot L$ | Pulse-spreading time over length $L$ | | 11 | $B_{\text{disp}} \approx 1/\Delta t$ | Effective dispersion-limited bandwidth (approx.) | | 12 | $v_p \geq c$, $v_g \leq c$, $v_g v_p = c^{2}$ | Waveguide velocity bounds | > [!IMPORTANT] > **LaTeX Safety Rule:** When writing any expression that needs an absolute-value sign inside a markdown table, **always use `\vert` or `\mid`**, never a literal pipe `|`, to avoid breaking the table's column delimiter. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Optical fibre design** — intermodal and intramodal dispersion both contribute to delay distortion; modern single-mode fibres minimise it by suppressing higher-order modes. - **High-speed digital backplanes** — pulse spreading at multi-Gbps rates forces designers to use pre-emphasis and equalisation. - **Radar & microwave links** — wideband chirped pulses must remain sharp; waveguides are carefully dimensioned so that the operating band is well above cut-off, where $\tau_g$ is nearly constant. - **Digital subscriber lines (DSL)** — copper-pair channels suffer severe delay distortion and require DMT/OFDM modulation with per-tone equalisation. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — General Relation Between $v_p$ and $v_g$ We begin from the definition of phase velocity. **Step 1.** Start with the phase velocity expression. $$ v_p \;=\; \frac{\omega}{\beta} $$ **Step 2.** Solve for the phase constant $\beta$. $$ \beta \;=\; \frac{\omega}{v_p} $$ **Step 3.** Differentiate $\beta$ with respect to $\omega$ on both sides. $$ \frac{d\beta}{d\omega} \;=\; \frac{1}{v_p} \;+\; \omega\,\frac{d}{d\omega}\!\left(\frac{1}{v_p}\right) $$ **Step 4.** Apply the chain rule to the second term. $$ \frac{d}{d\omega}\!\left(\frac{1}{v_p}\right) \;=\; -\frac{1}{v_p^{2}}\,\frac{dv_p}{d\omega} $$ **Step 5.** Substitute Step 4 into Step 3. $$ \frac{d\beta}{d\omega} \;=\; \frac{1}{v_p} \;-\; \frac{\omega}{v_p^{2}}\,\frac{dv_p}{d\omega} $$ **Step 6.** Factor out $1/v_p$ on the right-hand side. $$ \frac{d\beta}{d\omega} \;=\; \frac{1}{v_p}\!\left[\,1 \;-\; \frac{\omega}{v_p}\,\frac{dv_p}{d\omega}\,\right] $$ **Step 7.** Take the reciprocal to obtain the group velocity $v_g = (d\beta/d\omega)^{-1}$. $$ v_g \;=\; \frac{v_p}{1 - \dfrac{\omega}{v_p}\,\dfrac{dv_p}{d\omega}} $$ This is the **general dispersion relation** for any medium. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — $v_g$ and $v_p$ in a Rectangular Hollow Waveguide **Step 1.** Square the waveguide phase-constant equation. $$ \beta^{2} \;=\; \left(\frac{\omega}{c}\right)^{2} \;-\; \left(\frac{\omega_c}{c}\right)^{2} $$ **Step 2.** Multiply by $c^{2}$ to clear denominators. $$ \beta^{2} c^{2} \;=\; \omega^{2} \;-\; \omega_c^{2} $$ **Step 3.** Differentiate both sides with respect to $\omega$ (note $\omega_c$ is a constant of the waveguide geometry). $$ 2\beta\,c^{2}\,\frac{d\beta}{d\omega} \;=\; 2\omega $$ **Step 4.** Solve for $d\beta/d\omega$. $$ \frac{d\beta}{d\omega} \;=\; \frac{\omega}{\beta\,c^{2}} $$ **Step 5.** Take the reciprocal to obtain $v_g$. $$ v_g \;=\; \frac{d\omega}{d\beta} \;=\; \frac{\beta\,c^{2}}{\omega} $$ **Step 6.** Recognise that $\beta/\omega = 1/v_p$, so substitute. $$ v_g \;=\; \frac{c^{2}}{v_p} $$ **Step 7.** Multiply both sides by $v_p$. $$ \boxed{\,v_p \cdot v_g \;=\; c^{2}\,} $$ **Step 8.** Substitute the explicit form of $v_p$ to get $v_g$ explicitly. $$ v_p \;=\; \frac{c}{\sqrt{1 - (\omega_c/\omega)^{2}}} $$ $$ v_g \;=\; c\,\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\omega_c}{\omega}\right)^{2}} $$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pulse Spreading Time Across a Band $[f_1, f_2]$ **Step 1.** Write the group delay per unit length. $$ \tau_g(\omega) \;=\; \frac{d\beta}{d\omega} \;=\; \frac{1}{v_g(\omega)} \;=\; \frac{1}{c\,\sqrt{1 - (\omega_c/\omega)^{2}}} $$ **Step 2.** Evaluate $\tau_g$ at the upper edge $f_2$ of the band. $$ \tau_{g,2} \;=\; \frac{1}{c\,\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f_2)^{2}}} $$ **Step 3.** Evaluate $\tau_g$ at the lower edge $f_1$ of the band. $$ \tau_{g,1} \;=\; \frac{1}{c\,\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f_1)^{2}}} $$ **Step 4.** The total propagation time difference is the difference of the two delays multiplied by the line length $L$. $$ \Delta T \;=\; (\tau_{g,2} - \tau_{g,1})\,L $$ **Step 5.** Substitute the explicit delay expressions. $$ \Delta T \;=\; \frac{L}{c}\left[\frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f_2)^{2}}} \;-\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - (f_c/f_1)^{2}}}\right] $$ This $\Delta T$ is the **pulse-spreading (smearing) time** introduced by delay distortion. The **bandwidth–length product** $B \cdot L$ is roughly limited by the condition $\Delta T \leq T_{\text{bit}}/2$, where $T_{\text{bit}}$ is the bit period. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Transmission Impairments | Property | Attenuation Distortion | Delay Distortion | Noise | |---|---|---|---| | Physical origin | Loss $\alpha(\omega)$ depends on frequency | Phase constant $\beta(\omega)$ is non-linear in $\omega$ | Random thermal/shot/quantisation perturbations | | Effect on amplitude | Yes — different gains per frequency | No — amplitude preserved per component | Yes — adds unwanted amplitude | | Effect on phase/timing | No | Yes — components arrive at different times | Possibly (phase noise) | | Cure | Equalising amplifiers, repeaters | Equaliser with inverse group delay, dispersion-shifted fibre | Shielding, coding, filtering, FEC | | Frequency dependence | $\alpha = \alpha(\omega)$ | $\beta = \beta(\omega)$ | Often broadband / white | | KTU typical weight | 4–6 marks | 7–14 marks | 3–6 marks | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Pulse Spreading due to Delay Distortion The following block diagram traces an input Gaussian pulse through a dispersive waveguide and shows the resulting stretched output, with frequency components f1, f2, f3, f4 experiencing progressively different group delays. ```mermaid flowchart LR node1[INPUT PULSE
Gaussian shape at t=0]:::inNode node2[FOURIER SPLIT
Decompose into f1 f2 f3 f4]:::procNode nodeA[Component f1
Lowest frequency]:::compNode nodeB[Component f2
Low-mid frequency]:::compNode nodeC[Component f3
Mid-high frequency]:::compNode nodeD[Component f4
Highest frequency]:::compNode nodeW[DISPERSIVE WAVEGUIDE
Length L metres]:::wgNode nodeDelay1[Delay tau1 = L over vg of f1]:::dNode nodeDelay2[Delay tau2 = L over vg of f2]:::dNode nodeDelay3[Delay tau3 = L over vg of f3]:::dNode nodeDelay4[Delay tau4 = L over vg of f4]:::dNode nodeSum[FOURIER RECOMBINE
Superposition at receiver]:::procNode nodeOut[OUTPUT PULSE
Broadened and lower peak]:::outNode node1 --> node2 node2 --> nodeA node2 --> nodeB node2 --> nodeC node2 --> nodeD nodeA --> nodeW nodeB --> nodeW nodeC --> nodeW nodeD --> nodeW nodeW --> nodeDelay1 nodeW --> nodeDelay2 nodeW --> nodeDelay3 nodeW --> nodeDelay4 nodeDelay1 --> nodeSum nodeDelay2 --> nodeSum nodeDelay3 --> nodeSum nodeDelay4 --> nodeSum nodeSum --> nodeOut classDef inNode fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef procNode fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef compNode fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef wgNode fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef dNode fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#3730A3,stroke-width:1px,color:#000000 classDef outNode fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#991B1B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Delay-Equalising Receiver ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeTx[Transmitter
Wideband data pulse]:::devA nodeChan[Dispersive Channel
Waveguide or optical fibre]:::devB nodeRxA[Receiver Front End
Amplification and filtering]:::devC nodeEq[Delay Equaliser
Inverse group delay filter]:::devD nodeDec[Decision Circuit
Symbol detection]:::devE nodeOut[Recovered Data
Clean pulse train]:::devF nodeTx --> nodeChan nodeChan --> nodeRxA nodeRxA --> nodeEq nodeEq --> nodeDec nodeDec --> nodeOut classDef devA fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef devB fill:#FECACA,stroke:#B91C1C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef devC fill:#FDE68A,stroke:#A16207,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef devD fill:#C7D2FE,stroke:#4338CA,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef devE fill:#BBF7D0,stroke:#15803D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef devF fill:#F5D0FE,stroke:#86198F,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 ``` ### 4.3 Verbal Schematic — Why Waveguides Suffer Delay Distortion Inside a hollow rectangular waveguide, the electric and magnetic fields cannot be uniform; they form standing-wave patterns across the broad dimension. The boundary conditions imposed by the metallic walls force the dispersion relation: $$ k_{c}^{2} \;=\; \left(\frac{\omega}{c}\right)^{2} \;-\; \beta^{2} $$ Because $k_c$ is **fixed by geometry** and **not** by frequency, $\beta$ must curve away from a straight line as $\omega$ increases above $\omega_c$. This curvature is the **mathematical fingerprint** of delay distortion. The closer $\omega$ is to $\omega_c$, the more curved the $\beta(\omega)$ curve, and the more severe the delay distortion — which is exactly why **waveguide operation is restricted to $f \gg f_c$**. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** *Define delay distortion. Why does it occur inside a hollow metallic waveguide?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Delay distortion is a form of linear signal distortion in which different frequency components of a transmitted signal propagate with different group velocities $v_g$, causing them to arrive at the receiver at different times and producing a smeared output pulse. **Cause (2 marks):** Inside a hollow metallic waveguide, the phase constant $\beta$ is a non-linear function of frequency: $$ \beta(\omega) = \frac{\omega}{c}\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{\omega_c}{\omega}\right)^{2}} $$ Since $d\beta/d\omega$ is not constant, the group delay $\tau_g = d\beta/d\omega$ varies with $\omega$. Therefore, a wideband signal experiences frequency-dependent delay, and the output pulse is broadened. Delay distortion is most severe when the operating frequency is close to the cut-off frequency $\omega_c$ of the dominant mode. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]** *State and prove the relationship between phase velocity and group velocity for a hollow rectangular waveguide.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Statement (1 mark):** For a hollow rectangular waveguide, $v_p \cdot v_g = c^{2}$. **Proof (2 marks):** Starting from the dispersion relation $\beta^{2}c^{2} = \omega^{2} - \omega_c^{2}$, differentiate both sides: $$ 2\beta c^{2}\,\frac{d\beta}{d\omega} = 2\omega \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \frac{d\beta}{d\omega} = \frac{\omega}{\beta c^{2}} $$ Inverting gives $v_g = \beta c^{2}/\omega$. Since $v_p = \omega/\beta$, we have $\beta = \omega/v_p$, hence: $$ v_g = \frac{c^{2}}{v_p}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad v_p\,v_g = c^{2} $$ --- ### 5.2 PART B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) --- #### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Waveguide Dispersion Analysis* > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model]** **Q.A (a)** A rectangular waveguide of broad dimension $a = 5$ cm operates in the dominant $TE_{10}$ mode. The signal bandwidth extends from **5 GHz to 7 GHz**. The cut-off frequency of the mode is $f_c = 3$ GHz. Calculate: (i) the group velocity at the centre frequency, (ii) the group delay per metre at 5 GHz and at 7 GHz, (iii) the pulse-spreading time over a 100 m length of waveguide. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** (i) **Group velocity at the centre frequency $f = 6$ GHz (2 marks)** $$ f_c = 3\text{ GHz},\quad f = 6\text{ GHz} $$ $$ v_g = c\,\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{f_c}{f}\right)^{2}} = (3\times10^{8})\sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{3}{6}\right)^{2}} $$ $$ v_g = 3\times10^{8}\,\sqrt{1 - 0.25} = 3\times10^{8}\,(0.8660) = 2.598\times10^{8}\text{ m/s} $$ (ii) **Group delay per metre at 5 GHz and 7 GHz (3 marks)** At 5 GHz: $$ \tau_{g}(5) = \frac{1}{c\sqrt{1-(3/5)^{2}}} = \frac{1}{3\times10^{8}\,\sqrt{0.64}} = \frac{1}{2.4\times10^{8}} = 4.167\text{ ns/m} $$ At 7 GHz: $$ \tau_{g}(7) = \frac{1}{c\sqrt{1-(3/7)^{2}}} = \frac{1}{3\times10^{8}\,\sqrt{1-0.184}} = \frac{1}{3\times10^{8}\,(0.9035)} = 3.690\text{ ns/m} $$ (iii) **Pulse-spreading time over 100 m (2 marks)** $$ \Delta T = (\tau_{g,5} - \tau_{g,7})\cdot L = (4.167 - 3.690)\times10^{-9}\times100 $$ $$ \Delta T = 0.477\times10^{-9}\times100 = 47.7\text{ ns} $$ --- **Q.A (b)** Explain with a neat block diagram how a delay equaliser is used at the receiver of a digital communication system to overcome delay distortion. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer (7 marks):** A delay equaliser is a **linear filter** whose group delay $\tau_{\text{eq}}(\omega)$ is the **negative** of the channel's group delay $\tau_{\text{ch}}(\omega)$, so that the cascade produces a flat overall group delay. **Block diagram description (3 marks):** `TX → Channel (waveguide / fibre) → Receive Amplifier → Delay Equaliser (all-pass network) → Decision Circuit → Output` **Working principle (2 marks):** The all-pass equaliser is designed such that its transfer function has the form: $$ H_{\text{eq}}(\omega) = e^{-j\phi_{\text{eq}}(\omega)},\qquad \frac{d\phi_{\text{eq}}}{d\omega} = -\tau_{\text{ch}}(\omega) $$ Because the magnitude response is unity, **no amplitude distortion** is added; only the **phase curvature is reversed**, restoring the original pulse shape. **Real-world example (2 marks):** In digital subscriber lines (DSL), per-tone equalisation in the DMT/OFDM receiver performs exactly this function. In optical fibre links, dispersion-compensating fibre (DCF) is inserted to cancel chromatic-dispersion-induced delay distortion. --- #### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Conceptual & Mathematical Treatment* > **[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model]** **Q.B (a)** Starting from the general dispersion relation, derive an expression for the group velocity of a signal propagating through a medium in which the phase velocity varies with frequency. Hence obtain the relation $v_g = v_p/(1 - \frac{\omega}{v_p}\frac{dv_p}{d\omega})$ and discuss its physical significance. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Phase velocity definition. $$ v_p = \frac{\omega}{\beta}\quad\Longrightarrow\quad\beta = \frac{\omega}{v_p} $$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Differentiate $\beta$ w.r.t. $\omega$. $$ \frac{d\beta}{d\omega} = \frac{1}{v_p} - \frac{\omega}{v_p^{2}}\frac{dv_p}{d\omega} $$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Recognise that $v_g = (d\beta/d\omega)^{-1}$, and take the reciprocal. $$ v_g = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{v_p} - \dfrac{\omega}{v_p^{2}}\dfrac{dv_p}{d\omega}} = \frac{v_p}{1 - \dfrac{\omega}{v_p}\dfrac{dv_p}{d\omega}} $$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Physical significance. - When $dv_p/d\omega = 0$ (non-dispersive medium), $v_g = v_p$ and the signal is transmitted without pulse spreading. - When $dv_p/d\omega \neq 0$, $v_g \neq v_p$ and the medium is dispersive, producing **delay distortion**. - In waveguides $dv_p/d\omega < 0$ (since $v_p$ decreases as $\omega$ rises away from $\omega_c$ in a specific regime), giving $v_g < v_p$. **Step 5 (2 marks):** Numerical sanity check using the waveguide result: $v_p\,v_g = c^{2}$ — confirmed by the above formula, since for the waveguide $v_p = c/\sqrt{1-(\omega_c/\omega)^{2}}$, a direct substitution yields $v_g = c\sqrt{1-(\omega_c/\omega)^{2}}$. --- **Q.B (b)** Compare and contrast **attenuation distortion**, **delay distortion**, and **noise** in a communication channel. Why is delay distortion especially problematic in wideband digital communication? **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Comparison (4 marks):** | Impairment | Variable | Effect on signal | Remedy | |---|---|---|---| | Attenuation distortion | $\alpha(\omega)$ | Frequency-dependent loss | Equalising amplifier | | Delay distortion | $\beta(\omega)$ | Frequency-dependent phase/delay | Delay equaliser, dispersion-shifted fibre | | Noise | Random | Adds unwanted random signal | Shielding, coding, FEC | **Why delay distortion is problematic in wideband digital communication (3 marks):** 1. **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI):** In wideband digital signalling, bits are short. A pulse spreading $\Delta T$ comparable to the bit period $T_b$ causes the tail of one symbol to overlap with the next, severely increasing the bit error rate (BER). 2. **Eye-diagram closure:** As $\Delta T$ grows, the eye opening in the receiver's eye diagram closes, reducing noise margin. 3. **Bandwidth–length limit:** The $B \cdot L$ product of the channel is fundamentally capped by delay distortion, so very long-distance or very-high-bit-rate links (e.g. multi-Gbps optical backbones) require dispersion management (DCF, coherent detection, digital signal processing). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Delay Distortion Problems** > 1. **Confusing $v_p$ with $v_g$:** A *very* common error is writing $v_p = c\sqrt{1-(f_c/f)^{2}}$. This is the formula for $v_g$, **not** $v_p$. The square root goes in the *denominator* of $v_p$, not the numerator. **Memorise both forms carefully.** > 2. **Forgetting the factor $L$:** Pulse-spreading time requires the *length* of the channel. Many students compute $\Delta\tau_g$ in seconds-per-metre and stop. The KTU answer key explicitly multiplies by $L$ to get $\Delta T$ in seconds. > 3. **Mis-stating the relativity argument:** $v_p > c$ does *not* violate Einstein's special relativity because information (energy) travels at $v_g$, not $v_p$. Writing "$v_p$ violates relativity" will cost you a mark. > 4. **Mixing up attenuation and delay distortion:** Both are frequency-dependent, but the first affects *amplitude* and the second affects *phase/timing*. Be ready with a one-line distinction if asked. > 5. **Units:** Always express $\tau_g$ in **seconds per metre** (s/m) and $\Delta T$ in **seconds** (s) or **nanoseconds**. Mixing ms and ns without conversion loses marks. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Delay distortion** = frequency-dependent *group delay*; the *phase constant* $\beta$ is a non-linear function of $\omega$. - **Group delay** is defined as $\tau_g = d\beta/d\omega$ — the *time per unit length* taken by the signal envelope. - **Phase velocity** $v_p = \omega/\beta$; **Group velocity** $v_g = d\omega/d\beta$. - For a hollow rectangular waveguide: $v_p\,v_g = c^{2}$. - $v_p > c$ and $v_g < c$; this does **not** violate relativity. - Delay distortion is most severe near the **cut-off frequency** of the dominant mode. - The remedy is a **delay equaliser** (all-pass filter) that flattens $\tau_g(\omega)$ across the signal band. - Compare with **attenuation distortion** (amplitude varies) and **noise** (random addition). - Pulse spreading $\Delta T = (\tau_{g,\max} - \tau_{g,\min})\,L$ — must be much less than the bit period for low-BER digital communication. - Real-world impact: limits the **bandwidth–length product** of optical fibres, waveguides, and copper channels; forces use of OFDM, equalisation, and dispersion-compensating fibre. - **KTU favourite derivations:** (i) general $v_p$–$v_g$ relation, (ii) waveguide $v_p\,v_g = c^{2}$, (iii) pulse spreading across a finite band. - **Numerical practice:** Always quote $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s**, express frequencies in **Hz** (not GHz) when using radian formulas, and convert final delays to **ns** for readability.

topics.title: Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Noise? — Formal KTU Definition

In the context of **Data Communication**, **noise** is defined as any unwanted, random, or deterministic electrical signal that interferes with, distorts, or corrupts the intended information-bearing signal as it propagates through a physical communication medium (channel). 

Mathematically, if the transmitted signal is $s(t)$ and the received signal is $r(t)$, then the simplest model of a noisy channel is:

$$r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$$

where $n(t)$ represents the **additive noise** component. Noise is the primary physical-layer impairment that limits the **channel capacity**, the achievable **bit error rate (BER)**, and the maximum reliable data rate of any communication system.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Noise is the root cause of all information loss in digital systems. Understanding noise is non-negotiable for Module 1, as it directly feeds into concepts like SNR, Shannon Capacity, and modulation.

## 1.2 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants appear repeatedly in noise analysis:

- **Boltzmann Constant** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- **Standard Temperature** $T_0 = 290$ K (17°C) — used as reference for noise figure calculations
- **Room Temperature** $T = 300$ K — used for approximate thermal noise estimates
- **Bandwidth** measured in **Hz**, noise power in **Watts (W)** or **dBm**

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Whispering in a Stadium

Imagine you are sitting in a large **cricket stadium** trying to talk to your friend sitting 50 meters away. The **intended signal** is your voice. The **noise** is the collective sound of 50,000 cheering fans, the PA announcements, and the wind. 

If your friend hears only a faint whisper, the message is **lost in the noise**. If you shout using a **megaphone** (increased signal power), the message cuts through. If you speak slowly and clearly (better modulation, redundancy), the message survives. This is precisely the engineering challenge noise poses — we have only three knobs: **increase signal power**, **increase bandwidth**, or **add redundancy/error-correction codes**.

## 1.4 Why Noise Matters in Engineering

Noise determines the ultimate performance limit of:
- **Wireless systems** (4G/5G, Wi-Fi, satellite) — affected by atmospheric and thermal noise
- **Fiber-optic links** — affected by shot noise and intermodulation
- **Storage media** (HDDs, SSDs) — affected by media noise
- **Audio/video systems** — manifests as hiss, snow, static, hum
- **Radar and sonar** — limits minimum detectable signal

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Insight:** Even a perfect channel with infinite bandwidth would still have a capacity limit imposed by noise, as quantified by **Claude Shannon's** famous theorem (1948).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Noise — The Complete Taxonomy

Noise is broadly classified into two super-categories based on its origin and behavior.

### A. Internal Noise (Generated inside the system)
| # | Noise Type | Cause | Frequency Dependence |
|---|------------|-------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Thermal (Johnson-Nyquist) Noise** | Random motion of electrons in conductors/resistors | Uniform across all frequencies (white) |
| 2 | **Shot Noise** | Discrete nature of charge carriers in diodes/transistors | White, Poisson distributed |
| 3 | **Flicker Noise (1/f Noise)** | Imperfections in conductors, semiconductor traps | Dominant at low frequencies (below ~1 kHz) |
| 4 | **Transit-Time Noise** | Time taken by charge carriers to cross a junction | High frequencies (microwave range) |

### B. External Noise (Originating outside the system)
| # | Noise Type | Source | Typical Effect |
|---|------------|--------|----------------|
| 1 | **Atmospheric Noise** | Lightning, electrical storms | Crackling in AM radio |
| 2 | **Industrial Noise** | Motors, switches, ignition systems | Impulse bursts |
| 3 | **Extraterrestrial (Space) Noise** | Sun, galaxies | Affects satellite links |
| 4 | **Crosstalk** | Coupling between adjacent channels | "Bleed-through" in phone lines |

## 2.2 Deep Dive into Each Noise Type

### 2.2.1 Thermal Noise (Johnson-Nyquist Noise)
Caused by the **thermal agitation of electrons** in a conductor at temperature $T > 0$ K. This is a fundamental, unavoidable consequence of thermodynamics — even with zero applied voltage, electrons move randomly.

**Noise Power Available from a Resistor:**
$$P_n = kTB \text{ (Watts)}$$

**Equivalent Noise Voltage Source** (across a resistor $R$):
$$V_n = \sqrt{4kTBR}$$

**Equivalent Noise Current Source** (through a resistor $R$):
$$I_n = \sqrt{\frac{4kTB}{R}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Note:** Thermal noise is **Gaussian** and **white** (uniform power spectral density). It is additive and unbounded — there's no theoretical limit to its instantaneous value, though it has a well-defined RMS.

### 2.2.2 Shot Noise
Arises from the **discrete particle nature of charge carriers** in semiconductor devices and vacuum tubes. Each charge carrier crossing a potential barrier is a random event.

$$I_{sh}^2 = 2qI_{DC} B$$

where $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C (electron charge), $I_{DC}$ is the DC bias current.

### 2.2.3 Flicker Noise (Pink Noise / 1/f Noise)
Has a power spectral density proportional to $1/f$. Dominant at low frequencies (audio range and below). Cannot be removed by bandwidth reduction alone.

$$S(f) \propto \frac{K}{f}$$

### 2.2.4 Burst Noise (Popcorn Noise)
Sudden, step-like jumps in output at random times, switching between two or more discrete levels. Caused by semiconductor manufacturing defects.

### 2.2.5 White Noise
A theoretical model where power spectral density is **uniform across the entire frequency spectrum** — hence "white" (all colors of light combined). Thermal and shot noise approximate white noise over moderate bandwidths.

### 2.2.6 Colored Noise
- **Pink Noise** — PSD falls as $1/f$
- **Brown/Red Noise** — PSD falls as $1/f^2$ (e.g., thermal noise after RC filtering)
- **Blue Noise** — PSD rises with frequency

### 2.2.7 Intermodulation Noise
Produced when two signals of different frequencies share a **non-linear medium** (like an amplifier operating near saturation). Generates sum and difference frequency components ($f_1 + f_2$, $f_1 - f_2$, $2f_1 - f_2$, etc.) that fall within the desired band.

### 2.2.8 Impulse Noise
Sudden, high-amplitude spikes of short duration caused by external electromagnetic disturbances (lightning, switching transients, electrostatic discharge). Highly disruptive for digital data — a single impulse can wipe out many bits.

## 2.3 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) — The Master Metric

The single most important metric in any communication link:

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}} = \frac{S}{N}$$

In decibels:

$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{S}{N}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Distinction:** Use $10 \log_{10}$ for power ratios. Use $20 \log_{10}$ for voltage or current ratios. This is a common KTU trap.

## 2.4 Noise Figure and Noise Factor

A measure of how much a device **degrades** the SNR of a signal passing through it.

**Noise Factor (linear, dimensionless):**
$$F = \frac{\text{SNR}_{in}}{\text{SNR}_{out}}$$

**Noise Figure (in dB):**
$$NF = 10 \log_{10}(F)$$

For an **ideal noiseless device**: $F = 1$ and $NF = 0$ dB. For **real devices**: $NF > 0$ dB.

**Friis' Formula for Cascaded Stages** (KTU high-yield):
$$F_{total} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2} + \cdots$$

where $G_i$ is the power gain of the $i$-th stage. The **first stage dominates** — design the front-end for low noise!

## 2.5 Effective Noise Temperature

Used especially in satellite and microwave systems. The total noise power is modeled as if produced by a noisy resistor at an effective temperature $T_e$:

$$P_n = kT_e B$$

For a system with noise figure $F$ at reference temperature $T_0 = 290$ K:
$$T_e = T_0 (F - 1)$$

## 2.6 Shannon-Hartley Theorem (Channel Capacity)

The **absolute maximum data rate** achievable over a noisy channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz with SNR = $S/N$:

$$C = B \log_2\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \text{ bits/second}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Golden Fact:** Shannon proved that **error-free** communication is theoretically possible at any rate $R \leq C$, and **impossible** at any rate $R > C$ — regardless of coding or modulation. The capacity $C$ can be increased by increasing **bandwidth** or **signal power**.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Typical Value/Note |
|---|---------|---------|-------|--------------------|
| 1 | Thermal noise power | $P_n = kTB$ | Watts | Proportional to bandwidth |
| 2 | Thermal noise voltage | $V_n = \sqrt{4kTBR}$ | Volts | Across a resistor $R$ |
| 3 | Boltzmann constant | $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ | J/K | Universal constant |
| 4 | Reference temperature | $T_0 = 290$ | K | Used in NF calculation |
| 5 | Shot noise current | $I_{sh} = \sqrt{2qI_{DC}B}$ | Amps | For semiconductor devices |
| 6 | SNR (linear) | $S/N$ | dimensionless | Power ratio |
| 7 | SNR (dB) | $10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ | dB | Use 20 for V or I |
| 8 | Noise Factor | $F = (S/N)_{in}/(S/N)_{out}$ | dimensionless | $\geq 1$ always |
| 9 | Noise Figure | $NF = 10 \log_{10}(F)$ | dB | $\geq 0$ dB always |
| 10 | Friis' formula | $F_{total} = F_1 + \frac{F_2-1}{G_1} + \cdots$ | dimensionless | Cascaded amplifiers |
| 11 | Noise temperature | $T_e = T_0(F-1)$ | K | Satellite/microwave |
| 12 | Shannon Capacity | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | bps | Theoretical max rate |
| 13 | Channel efficiency | $\eta = C/B$ | bps/Hz | Spectral efficiency |
| 14 | Nyquist capacity (noiseless) | $C = 2B \log_2 M$ | bps | For M-level signal |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Low-Noise Amplifiers (LNAs)** in satellite receivers are designed with $NF < 1$ dB to minimize the system's effective noise temperature.
- **Coding Theory (Turbo, LDPC codes)** approach Shannon's limit within 0.1 dB in modern 5G.
- **BER vs. SNR curves** are the standard way to evaluate digital modulation schemes (BPSK, QPSK, QAM) in the presence of **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Thermal Noise Power Equals $kTB$

### Background
Consider a resistor $R$ as the source of thermal noise. The maximum noise power that can be transferred from a source of internal resistance $R$ to a matched load $R$ (i.e., the **available noise power**) is what we seek.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** For a resistor $R$ at absolute temperature $T$, the open-circuit RMS noise voltage is given by Nyquist's theorem (derivable from thermodynamic and quantum mechanical arguments):

$$V_{n,\text{rms}} = \sqrt{4kTBR}$$

**Step 2:** When this noisy resistor is connected to a matched load $R$ (for maximum power transfer), the load receives a fraction $\frac{1}{2}$ of the source's available power (voltage divider effect — half voltage across load, half across source).

**Step 3:** Power delivered to the matched load:

$$P_n = \frac{V_{n,\text{rms}}^2}{R + R} = \frac{4kTBR}{2R} = kTB$$

**Step 4:** Notice the remarkable result: **the resistance $R$ cancels out completely!** This means the available noise power depends only on temperature and bandwidth, not on the resistor's value.

$$\boxed{P_n = kTB \text{ (Watts)}}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> This is a profound result. Any resistor at temperature $T$ delivers the same noise power to a matched load, whether it is $1\ \Omega$ or $1\ \text{M}\Omega$.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Noise Power in a Communication Channel

**Problem:** A receiver operates at room temperature ($T = 300$ K) with a bandwidth $B = 10$ MHz. Find:
(a) The thermal noise power.
(b) The noise voltage across a $50\ \Omega$ input resistance.
(c) The SNR if the received signal power is $S = -90$ dBm.

### Solution

**Part (a):** Use $P_n = kTB$:

$$P_n = (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300 \times (10 \times 10^6)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_n &= 1.38 \times 300 \times 10^{-23+7} \\
&= 414 \times 10^{-16} \\
&= 4.14 \times 10^{-14} \text{ W} \\
&= 4.14 \times 10^{-11} \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to dBm:

$$P_n \text{ (dBm)} = 10 \log_{10}(4.14 \times 10^{-14} \text{ mW} / 1 \text{ mW}) = 10 \log_{10}(4.14 \times 10^{-14})$$

$$\begin{aligned}
&= 10 \times (-13.383) \\
&= -103.83 \text{ dBm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b):** Using $V_n = \sqrt{4kTBR}$:

$$V_n = \sqrt{4 \times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 300 \times (10 \times 10^6) \times 50}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
&= \sqrt{4 \times 4.14 \times 10^{-14} \times 50} \\
&= \sqrt{8.28 \times 10^{-12}} \\
&= 2.877 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V} \\
&\approx 2.88\ \mu\text{V}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (c):** Convert signal power to watts:

$$S = -90 \text{ dBm} = 10^{-90/10} = 10^{-9} \text{ mW} = 10^{-12} \text{ W} = 1 \text{ pW}$$

SNR (linear):

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{10^{-12}}{4.14 \times 10^{-14}} = 24.15$$

SNR in dB:

$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(24.15) = 13.83 \text{ dB}$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Shannon Channel Capacity

**Problem:** A telephone channel has bandwidth $B = 3$ kHz and SNR = 40 dB. Calculate the theoretical maximum data rate. If SNR drops to 20 dB, what is the new capacity?

### Solution

**Part (a):** Convert SNR to linear scale:

$$\text{SNR}_{linear} = 10^{40/10} = 10^4 = 10{,}000$$

Apply Shannon's formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= B \log_2(1 + S/N) \\
&= 3000 \times \log_2(1 + 10{,}000) \\
&= 3000 \times \log_2(10{,}001) \\
&= 3000 \times 13.288 \\
&\approx 39{,}864 \text{ bps} \\
&\approx 39.86 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b):** For SNR = 20 dB:

$$\text{SNR}_{linear} = 10^{20/10} = 100$$

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= 3000 \times \log_2(1 + 100) \\
&= 3000 \times \log_2(101) \\
&= 3000 \times 6.658 \\
&\approx 19{,}975 \text{ bps} \\
&\approx 19.97 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Observation:** A **20 dB drop in SNR** (i.e., signal power reduced by 100x) cuts the channel capacity in half. This shows how powerfully noise limits communication.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Cascaded Noise Figure (Friis' Formula)

**Problem:** A receiver has a low-noise amplifier (LNA) followed by a mixer and an IF amplifier.
- LNA: Gain $G_1 = 20$ dB, NF = 1.5 dB
- Mixer: Gain $G_2 = -6$ dB (loss), NF = 8 dB
- IF Amp: Gain $G_3 = 30$ dB, NF = 4 dB

Find the overall noise figure.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Convert all gains and noise figures to linear scale.

$$G_1 = 10^{20/10} = 100, \quad F_1 = 10^{1.5/10} = 1.4125$$

$$G_2 = 10^{-6/10} = 0.2512, \quad F_2 = 10^{8/10} = 6.3096$$

$$G_3 = 10^{30/10} = 1000, \quad F_3 = 10^{4/10} = 2.5119$$

**Step 2:** Apply Friis' formula:

$$F_{total} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2}$$

**Step 3:** Compute each term:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{total} &= 1.4125 + \frac{6.3096 - 1}{100} + \frac{2.5119 - 1}{100 \times 0.2512} \\
&= 1.4125 + \frac{5.3096}{100} + \frac{1.5119}{25.12} \\
&= 1.4125 + 0.0531 + 0.0602 \\
&= 1.5258
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to dB:

$$NF_{total} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5258) = 1.83 \text{ dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Key Insight:** The LNA's noise figure of 1.5 dB dominates the system. If the LNA weren't there first, the mixer's 8 dB NF would dominate. This is why the LNA is **always** placed at the front-end.

## 3.5 Summary of Key Derivations

| Derivation | Starting Point | Key Result | Physical Meaning |
|------------|---------------|------------|------------------|
| Available Noise Power | $V_n^2/(2R)$ matched load | $P_n = kTB$ | Bandwidth-limited noise floor |
| Shannon Capacity | Information theory | $C = B\log_2(1+S/N)$ | Absolute rate limit |
| Friis' Formula | SNR degradation through chain | $F_{tot} = F_1 + (F_2-1)/G_1 + \cdots$ | First-stage dominance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
