# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]
**Define thermal noise. Derive an expression for the thermal noise power generated by a resistor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Thermal noise, also called Johnson-Nyquist noise, is the random electrical noise generated by the thermal agitation of charge carriers (electrons) in a conductor or resistor at any temperature above absolute zero.
- **Derivation (2 marks):** For a resistor $R$ at temperature $T$ K, the open-circuit RMS noise voltage across a bandwidth $B$ Hz is given by Nyquist's formula: $V_n = \sqrt{4kTBR}$. When connected to a matched load $R_L = R$ for maximum power transfer, the load receives $P_n = V_n^2/(2R + 2R) = kTB$. Hence, the available thermal noise power is $P_n = kTB$ watts, independent of the resistance value.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - June 2023]
**Explain the significance of Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) in a communication system. How is it expressed in dB?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Significance (2 marks):** SNR is the ratio of the desired signal power to the unwanted noise power at a given point in the system. It quantifies the **clarity and quality** of the received signal. A higher SNR means cleaner reception, lower bit error rate, and the ability to support higher data rates. SNR is the single most important link-quality metric — it directly determines Shannon channel capacity, modulation scheme selection, and achievable BER.
- **dB Expression (1 mark):** $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$. (Use $20 \log_{10}$ for voltage or current ratios.)

## 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the sources of noise in a communication system. Classify them into internal and external noise with examples.

**(b) [7 marks]** A receiver operating at $T = 290$ K has a bandwidth of 4 kHz. If the received signal power is $4 \times 10^{-18}$ W, calculate:
   (i) The thermal noise power in watts and dBm.
   (ii) The SNR in linear scale and dB.
   (iii) The Shannon channel capacity in kbps.

#### Model Solution to (a) [7 marks]
The block diagram of a communication system showing noise sources was covered in **Section 4.2** above. Key points to mention:
1. **Source of information** — generates data.
2. **Transmitter** — modulates the data onto a carrier.
3. **Channel** — physical medium (cable, fiber, wireless) — **dominant noise location**.
4. **Receiver** — demodulates; amplifier noise adds here.
5. **Destination** — receives recovered data.

**Classification of noise (with examples):**
- **Internal noise** (generated inside the system):
  - *Thermal noise* — from resistors, transmission lines
  - *Shot noise* — in BJTs, diodes, photodetectors
  - *Flicker noise* — in MOSFETs at audio frequencies
  - *Transit-time noise* — at microwave frequencies
- **External noise** (enters from outside):
  - *Atmospheric* — lightning, electrical storms
  - *Industrial* — motors, fluorescent lamps, ignition
  - *Space/cosmic* — sun, galactic radiation
  - *Crosstalk* — coupling from adjacent wires/channels

#### Model Solution to (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Thermal noise power (3 marks):**

$$P_n = kTB = (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 290 \times (4 \times 10^3)$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_n &= 1.38 \times 290 \times 4 \times 10^{-20} \\
&= 1600.8 \times 10^{-20} \\
&= 1.6008 \times 10^{-17} \text{ W} \\
&\approx 1.6 \times 10^{-17} \text{ W}
\end{aligned}$$

In dBm:

$$P_n \text{(dBm)} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-17}}{10^{-3}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(1.6 \times 10^{-14})$$

$$= 10 \times (\log_{10} 1.6 + \log_{10} 10^{-14}) = 10 \times (0.204 - 14) = 10 \times (-13.796) = -137.96 \text{ dBm}$$

**(ii) SNR (2 marks):**

$$\text{SNR}_{linear} = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-18}}{1.6 \times 10^{-17}} = 0.25$$

$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(0.25) = 10 \times (-0.602) = -6.02 \text{ dB}$$

> **Important:** A negative SNR means the noise is **stronger** than the signal — communication is impossible without additional measures (such as coding or signal processing gain).

**(iii) Channel capacity (2 marks):**

$$C = B \log_2(1 + S/N) = 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 0.25) = 4000 \times \log_2(1.25)$$

$$C = 4000 \times 0.3219 = 1287.7 \text{ bps} \approx 1.29 \text{ kbps}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 marks]** What is meant by noise figure and noise factor? A receiver consists of three cascaded stages with the following parameters:
- Stage 1: $G_1 = 12$ dB, $NF_1 = 2$ dB
- Stage 2: $G_2 = 20$ dB, $NF_2 = 6$ dB  
- Stage 3: $G_3 = 30$ dB, $NF_3 = 10$ dB

Calculate the overall noise figure of the receiver using Friis' formula.

**(b) [7 marks]** State and explain the Shannon-Hartley theorem. A communication channel has a bandwidth of 20 kHz. If the maximum allowed data rate is 100 kbps, calculate the minimum SNR required (in dB) for error-free transmission.

#### Model Solution to (a) [7 marks]

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Noise Factor (F)** is the ratio of input SNR to output SNR of a device. It is a dimensionless number $\geq 1$, with $F = 1$ for an ideal noiseless device.
- **Noise Figure (NF)** is the same ratio expressed in decibels: $NF = 10 \log_{10}(F)$.

**Conversions to linear (2 marks):**

$$G_1 = 10^{1.2} = 15.85, \quad F_1 = 10^{0.2} = 1.585$$

$$G_2 = 10^{2.0} = 100, \quad F_2 = 10^{0.6} = 3.981$$

$$G_3 = 10^{3.0} = 1000, \quad F_3 = 10^{1.0} = 10$$

**Friis' Formula Application (2 marks):**

$$F_{total} = F_1 + \frac{F_2 - 1}{G_1} + \frac{F_3 - 1}{G_1 G_2}$$

$$F_{total} = 1.585 + \frac{3.981 - 1}{15.85} + \frac{10 - 1}{15.85 \times 100}$$

$$= 1.585 + \frac{2.981}{15.85} + \frac{9}{1585}$$

$$= 1.585 + 0.1881 + 0.00568 = 1.779$$

**Convert to dB (1 mark):**

$$NF_{total} = 10 \log_{10}(1.779) = 2.50 \text{ dB}$$

#### Model Solution to (b) [7 marks]

**Statement of Shannon-Hartley Theorem (3 marks):**
The Shannon-Hartley theorem states that the theoretical maximum data rate (channel capacity $C$ in bits/second) that can be transmitted over a communication channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz in the presence of additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN) with signal power $S$ and noise power $N$ is:

$$C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$$

This represents the **upper limit** on the rate of error-free communication. No coding or modulation scheme can exceed this rate.

**Calculation of minimum SNR (4 marks):**

Given: $B = 20$ kHz = 20,000 Hz, $C = 100$ kbps = 100,000 bps.

Rearranging Shannon's formula:

$$\frac{C}{B} = \log_2(1 + S/N)$$

$$\frac{100{,}000}{20{,}000} = 5 = \log_2(1 + S/N)$$

$$2^5 = 1 + S/N \implies 32 = 1 + S/N$$

$$S/N = 31$$

Converting to dB:

$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(31) = 10 \times 1.491 = 14.91 \text{ dB}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Minimum SNR} \approx 14.91 \text{ dB}}$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Mixing 10 log and 20 log:** Use $10 \log_{10}$ for power/dB, but $20 \log_{10}$ for voltage/current ratios. Many students incorrectly use 10 for voltage — losing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the "+1" inside the log:** Some students write $C = B \log_2(S/N)$. The "1 +" is essential and distinguishes Shannon's formula from the simpler (incorrect) form.
> 3. **In Friis' formula:** Students often compute $F_1 + F_2 + F_3$ directly, forgetting to divide subsequent terms by the cumulative product of gains. This produces wrong answers and full mark loss.
> 4. **Unit confusion:** Convert bandwidth to Hz (not kHz) and powers to Watts (not dBm) before plugging into formulas.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Noise**

- ✅ **Thermal noise** is the most fundamental and unavoidable noise. Formula: $P_n = kTB$. Independent of resistance.
- ✅ **Boltzmann constant** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K. **Reference temperature** $T_0 = 290$ K.
- ✅ **Shot noise** arises from discrete charge carriers; formula $I_{sh}^2 = 2qI_{DC}B$.
- ✅ **Flicker (1/f) noise** dominates at low frequencies (audio range).
- ✅ **SNR (dB)** = $10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ for power; $20 \log_{10}$ for V or I.
- ✅ **Noise Factor** $F = (S/N)_{in}/(S/N)_{out} \geq 1$. **Noise Figure** = $10 \log_{10}(F)$ in dB $\geq 0$.
- ✅ **Friis' formula** for cascaded systems: $F_{tot} = F_1 + (F_2-1)/G_1 + (F_3-1)/(G_1 G_2) + \cdots$. **First stage dominates.**
- ✅ **Effective noise temperature** $T_e = T_0(F-1)$ — used in satellite and microwave link design.
- ✅ **Shannon Capacity** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ is the **theoretical maximum** error-free bit rate.
- ✅ **Bandwidth-power tradeoff:** For a fixed capacity, doubling bandwidth allows halving the signal power.
- ✅ **White noise** has uniform PSD; **colored noise** (pink, brown) has frequency-dependent PSD.
- ✅ **Impulse noise** is the worst enemy of digital data — a single spike can cause many bit errors.
- ✅ For negative SNR, communication is theoretically still possible but requires very clever coding (e.g., spread spectrum).
- ✅ Remember to convert all values to **linear (not dB)** form before applying Friis' formula algebraically.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Rate Limits - Noiseless Channel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Rate Limits - Noiseless Channel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In a **Noiseless Channel**, the fundamental physical constraint on the maximum achievable data rate is established by **Nyquist's Theorem (Nyquist Bit Rate Criterion)**, published by **Henry Nyquist in 1924**. The theorem formally states:

> *For a noiseless transmission channel of finite bandwidth $B$ Hertz, the maximum data rate (channel capacity) that can be reliably transmitted is given by:*

$$C = 2B \log_{2} V \quad \text{bits/second (bps)}$$

Where:
- $C$ = Maximum channel capacity / data rate (in **bits per second**)
- $B$ = Channel bandwidth (in **Hertz**)
- $V$ = Number of discrete signal levels (voltage levels, frequency levels, or phase shifts) used for encoding
- The logarithmic base **2** gives the result in **bits**, because we are dealing with binary digital data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The factor **"2B"** is directly derived from the **Nyquist Sampling Theorem**, which states that a band-limited signal of bandwidth $B$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed if sampled at a rate of at least **2B samples per second** (i.e., the **Nyquist Rate**).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **two-lane highway**:
- The **bandwidth ($B$)** represents the **width of the highway** (number of lanes). A wider road allows more cars to pass per hour.
- The **signal levels ($V$)** represent the **types of vehicles allowed** on the road — bicycles, cars, buses, trucks (e.g., 4 levels means each vehicle can carry one of 4 different "logos" or "colors" representing 2 bits of data).
- The **maximum data rate ($C$)** is the **total number of useful passenger-seats that cross a checkpoint per second**.

If you only have bicycles (2 levels = 1 bit each), you get fewer bits per vehicle. If you allow trucks carrying 8 different cargo labels ($V=8$, which is $\log_2 8 = 3$ bits per truck), you get 3× the data per vehicle — but the road width (bandwidth) still fundamentally caps the total vehicle throughput.

### Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Nyquist Sampling Rate:** $f_s = 2B$ samples/second
- **Information per signal level:** $\log_2 V$ bits
- **Standard audio-grade channel:** $B = 4000$ Hz (telephony)
- **Typical LAN Ethernet bandwidth:** $B = 10$ MHz to $1$ GHz range
- **Binary signaling baseline ($V=2$):** Data rate = $2B$ bps (this is the absolute floor reference)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Noiseless"?** A truly noiseless channel is an *idealization*. Real channels always have thermal noise, crosstalk, and interference. The Noiseless Channel formula gives the **upper-bound theoretical maximum** — useful as a benchmark, but real-world capacity is governed by **Shannon's Theorem** (for noisy channels), which is covered as a separate topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown

The Nyquist Bit Rate formula is not arbitrary; it is the consequence of **two independent physical constraints** acting together:

**Step 1 — The Sampling Limit (Time Domain Constraint)**
- A signal of bandwidth $B$ Hz contains frequency components from $0$ to $B$ Hz.
- By the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**, to capture all information without loss, the signal must be sampled at least **2B times per second**.
- This sets the **maximum number of signal transitions (symbols) per second** = $2B$.

**Step 2 — The Information per Symbol (Encoding Constraint)**
- Each transmitted symbol (voltage level) can carry information.
- If there are $V$ discrete levels, then each level can be distinguished from the others, encoding $\log_2 V$ bits of information.
- This is because with $V$ levels, we need $\log_2 V$ binary digits to represent each level uniquely.

**Step 3 — Multiplying the Two Constraints**
- Total bits per second = (symbols per second) × (bits per symbol)
- Therefore: $C = 2B \times \log_2 V$

### Why Each Variable Matters

- **$B$ (Bandwidth):** A *physical* property of the medium. Governed by the conductor, antenna design, or fiber-optic characteristics. Doubling bandwidth doubles the data rate (linearly).
- **$V$ (Signal Levels):** An *engineering choice* of the transmitter. Doubling $V$ increases the data rate **logarithmically**, not linearly. Hence, increasing levels gives diminishing returns.

### Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Modem Design:** Engineers designing modems (e.g., V.90, ADSL) use this formula to determine how many discrete phase/amplitude levels they can use.
- **Fiber-Optic Networks:** Used to compute the maximum throughput of a fiber with given bandwidth.
- **Storage Media:** Helps calculate the maximum write-rate of a magnetic/optical medium.
- **Digital Audio:** Explains why CD audio uses 44,100 samples/sec for a 22,050 Hz audio bandwidth ($2 \times 22{,}050$).

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Unit** | **Meaning** | **Equation / Bound** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Channel Capacity (Noiseless) | $C$ | bits/second (bps) | Maximum data rate | $C = 2B \log_2 V$ |
| Bandwidth | $B$ | Hertz (Hz) | Frequency range of channel | $B = f_{\text{high}} - f_{\text{low}}$ |
| Number of Signal Levels | $V$ | Dimensionless integer | Discrete symbols per sample | $V \geq 2$ |
| Bits per Signal Level | — | bits | Information per symbol | $\log_2 V$ |
| Symbol Rate (Baud) | $S$ | baud | Signal changes per second | $S = 2B$ |
| Binary Baseline Capacity | $C_{\text{bin}}$ | bps | When $V = 2$ | $C = 2B$ |
| Information per Level | $I$ | bits | Information content | $I = \log_2 V$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Bound:** A noiseless channel's capacity grows **linearly with bandwidth** but only **logarithmically with signal levels**. Therefore, doubling bandwidth is *always* more effective than doubling the number of signal levels beyond a reasonable point.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 3.1 — From Sampling Theorem to Nyquist Bit Rate

We derive the formula by combining the **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** with **information theory**.

**Given:** A noiseless channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz, using $V$ discrete signal levels.

**Step 1 — Maximum Sampling Rate**
According to the Nyquist Sampling Theorem, to reconstruct a band-limited signal of bandwidth $B$ Hz without aliasing or loss, the sampling frequency $f_s$ must satisfy:

$$f_s \geq 2B$$

**Step 2 — Number of Samples per Second (Symbol Rate)**
In a digital communication system, each sample corresponds to one transmitted symbol. Therefore, the maximum symbol rate (baud rate) is:

$$S = 2B \quad \text{baud (symbols/second)}$$

**Step 3 — Information per Symbol**
If the channel uses $V$ discrete levels, the number of bits required to uniquely identify one level out of $V$ is given by the **information-theoretic definition** of a base-2 logarithm:

$$\text{Bits per symbol} = \log_2 V$$

Derivation of this step: With $V$ levels, we need binary strings of minimum length $k$ such that $2^k \geq V$. Hence, $k = \lceil \log_2 V \rceil$, and for large $V$, the ceiling approaches $\log_2 V$.

**Step 4 — Total Data Rate**
Multiply the symbol rate by the bits per symbol:

$$C = (\text{symbols per second}) \times (\text{bits per symbol})$$

$$C = 2B \times \log_2 V$$

$$\boxed{C = 2B \log_2 V \quad \text{bits per second}}$$

This completes the derivation.

---

### Worked Numerical Example 3.1 — Binary Signaling on Telephone Channel

**Problem:** A noiseless 4 kHz channel uses binary signaling ($V = 2$). Calculate the maximum data rate.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad & B = 4000 \text{ Hz}, \quad V = 2 \\
\text{Formula:} \quad & C = 2B \log_2 V \\
\text{Substituting:} \quad & C = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2 2 \\
& C = 8000 \times 1 \\
& C & = 8000 \text{ bps} = 8 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The maximum data rate is **8 kbps** — exactly the original PCM voice channel rate used in early digital telephony (which is precisely why this is the canonical example in textbooks).

---

### Worked Numerical Example 3.2 — Multi-Level Signaling

**Problem:** A noiseless channel of 6 MHz bandwidth uses 8 signal levels. Find the maximum data rate.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad & B = 6 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}, \quad V = 8 \\
\text{Formula:} \quad & C = 2B \log_2 V \\
\text{Computing log term:} \quad & \log_2 8 = 3 \text{ bits/level} \\
\text{Substituting:} \quad & C = 2 \times 6 \times 10^6 \times 3 \\
& C = 36 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} \\
& C & = 36 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The maximum data rate is **36 Mbps**.

---

### Worked Numerical Example 3.3 — Reverse Problem (Finding Required Levels)

**Problem:** A noiseless 20 kHz channel must support a data rate of 256 kbps. How many signal levels are required?

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad & B = 20{,}000 \text{ Hz}, \quad C = 256{,}000 \text{ bps} \\
\text{Formula:} \quad & C = 2B \log_2 V \\
\text{Solving for } \log_2 V: \quad & \log_2 V = \frac{C}{2B} \\
& \log_2 V = \frac{256{,}000}{2 \times 20{,}000} \\
& \log_2 V = \frac{256{,}000}{40{,}000} \\
& \log_2 V = 6.4 \\
\text{Solving for } V: \quad & V = 2^{6.4} \\
& V \approx 84.45
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $V$ must be an integer and we cannot have a fraction of a level, we must round up to $V = 85$ levels (or to the next power of 2 for clean binary encoding, $V = 128$, giving $\log_2 128 = 7$ bits/level).

**Result:** A minimum of **$V \approx 85$ discrete levels** is required.

---

### Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Scenario** | **Bandwidth $B$ (Hz)** | **Levels $V$** | **Bits/Level $\log_2 V$** | **Capacity $C$ (bps)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Voice-grade PCM (Binary) | 4,000 | 2 | 1 | 8,000 |
| Voice-grade (4-level) | 4,000 | 4 | 2 | 16,000 |
| TV Channel (Binary) | 6,000,000 | 2 | 1 | 12,000,000 |
| TV Channel (8-level) | 6,000,000 | 8 | 3 | 36,000,000 |
| Fiber-Optic Link | 1,000,000,000 | 16 | 4 | 8,000,000,000 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight from the matrix:** Observe that moving from $V=2$ to $V=8$ on the 6 MHz TV channel **triples** the capacity (12 Mbps → 36 Mbps) without needing any extra bandwidth. This is precisely the principle exploited by modern **QAM (Quadrature Amplitude Modulation)** schemes in cable broadband and Wi-Fi.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 4.1 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Nyquist Capacity Computation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[Channel Bandwidth B in Hz] --> mul1[Multiplier]
    inputB[Sampling Theorem Factor 2] --> mul1
    inputC[Number of Signal Levels V] --> log1[Log base 2 operation]
    log1 --> bits[Bits per Level log2 V]
    mul1 --> symrate[Symbol Rate 2B baud]
    bits --> mul2[Final Multiplier]
    symrate --> mul2
    mul2 --> output[Maximum Data Rate C in bps]
    output --> app1[Modem Design]
    output --> app2[Fiber Optic Links]
    output --> app3[Audio CD Systems]
    output --> app4[Digital TV Broadcast]
```

**Interpretation:** This block diagram traces the *information flow* through the Nyquist formula. The two independent physical inputs (bandwidth $B$ and number of levels $V$) are processed through separate sub-paths and combined only at the final multiplication stage — visually reinforcing the multiplicative independence of the time-domain and encoding-domain constraints.

---

### Diagram 4.2 — Decision Flow for Applying Nyquist vs. Shannon Theorem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Given Communication Channel] --> q1{Is the channel NOISELESS?}
    q1 -->|Yes| nyq[Use Nyquist Theorem]
    q1 -->|No| sha[Use Shannon Theorem]
    nyq --> f1[Formula C = 2B log2 V]
    f1 --> out1[Upper bound on capacity]
    sha --> f2[Formula C = B log2 of 1 plus SNR]
    f2 --> out2[Capacity with noise penalty]
    out1 --> compare[Compare both bounds for real channel analysis]
    out2 --> compare
    compare --> end[Final Design Decision]
```

**Interpretation:** This is a **decision-flow schematic** that a KTU student should memorize. The Nyquist bound is *always* applicable (even on noisy channels as a noisiest-case upper limit), but Shannon's bound is tighter and more realistic for real systems. The diagram helps the student pick the right tool for the right problem statement in the exam.

---

### Diagram 4.3 — Effect of Signal Levels on Data Rate (Visual Intuition)

```mermaid
graph LR
    L2[V = 2 levels 1 bit per symbol] --> R2[Capacity = 2B bps]
    L4[V = 4 levels 2 bits per symbol] --> R4[Capacity = 4B bps]
    L8[V = 8 levels 3 bits per symbol] --> R8[Capacity = 6B bps]
    L16[V = 16 levels 4 bits per symbol] --> R16[Capacity = 8B bps]
    L32[V = 32 levels 5 bits per symbol] --> R32[Capacity = 10B bps]
```

**Interpretation:** Visualizes the *logarithmic growth* of capacity as $V$ increases linearly. Note that going from $V=2$ to $V=4$ doubles the rate, but going from $V=16$ to $V=32$ (same doubling of levels) only adds $2B$ to the rate — demonstrating **diminishing returns** of higher-level encoding.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State Nyquist's theorem for a noiseless channel and define each term in the formula with its unit.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Nyquist's theorem for a noiseless channel gives the maximum theoretical data rate (channel capacity) that can be transmitted without error. The formula is:

$$C = 2B \log_2 V \quad \text{bits/second}$$

Where:
- **$C$** = Maximum channel capacity, measured in **bits per second (bps)**. (1 mark)
- **$B$** = Channel bandwidth in **Hertz (Hz)**. (1 mark)
- **$V$** = Number of discrete signal levels used to represent data (dimensionless integer). (1 mark)
- $\log_2 V$ = Number of bits carried by each signal level.

> **Examiner's Note:** Award full marks only if all three variables and their units are explicitly stated.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is the factor $2B$ (and not just $B$) used in the Nyquist formula for a noiseless channel? Mention the underlying principle.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The factor $2B$ arises from the **Nyquist Sampling Theorem**, which states that a band-limited signal of maximum frequency $B$ Hz must be sampled at **at least twice its highest frequency component** to be reconstructed without loss of information. (2 marks)

Therefore, the maximum number of independent signal samples (symbols) that can be transmitted per second through a channel of bandwidth $B$ is $2B$ samples/second. Each sample represents one symbol, so the maximum **symbol rate (baud rate)** of the channel is $2B$ baud. (1 mark)

This is why the time-domain factor is $2B$ and not $B$ — sampling must occur at twice the bandwidth to capture all signal variations.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the Nyquist bit rate formula $C = 2B \log_2 V$ for a noiseless channel, starting from the Nyquist Sampling Theorem. Explain the meaning of each term. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A noiseless channel has a bandwidth of 3.5 kHz.
  (i) Calculate the maximum data rate when binary signaling is used.
  (ii) How many signal levels are required to achieve a data rate of 70 kbps on the same channel?
  (iii) If the channel bandwidth is doubled to 7 kHz while keeping $V=2$, what is the new data rate? Comment on the relationship between bandwidth and data rate. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the Nyquist Sampling Theorem:
A band-limited signal of bandwidth $B$ Hz must be sampled at a rate $f_s \geq 2B$ samples/second to enable perfect reconstruction.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Identify the symbol rate:
In digital transmission, each sample carries one symbol. Hence, the maximum symbol rate is:
$$S = 2B \quad \text{baud (symbols/second)}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute bits per symbol:
If $V$ discrete levels are used, the number of bits needed to distinguish one level is:
$$\text{Bits per symbol} = \log_2 V$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Combine into the capacity formula:
$$C = \text{Symbol Rate} \times \text{Bits per Symbol}$$

**Step 5 (2 marks):** Final formula and term explanation:
$$C = 2B \log_2 V \quad \text{bits/second}$$

Where:
- $C$ → Channel capacity in **bps**
- $B$ → Bandwidth in **Hz**
- $V$ → Number of discrete signal levels
- $2B$ → Maximum symbol rate from sampling theorem
- $\log_2 V$ → Information per symbol in bits

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problems (7 Marks)

**(i) Binary signaling ($V = 2$) on 3.5 kHz channel: (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& B = 3500 \text{ Hz}, \quad V = 2 \\
& C = 2 \times 3500 \times \log_2 2 \\
& C = 7000 \times 1 \\
& C = 7000 \text{ bps} = 7 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** Maximum data rate = **7 kbps**.

---

**(ii) Required levels for 70 kbps: (3 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& B = 3500 \text{ Hz}, \quad C = 70{,}000 \text{ bps} \\
& \log_2 V = \frac{C}{2B} = \frac{70{,}000}{7000} = 10 \\
& V = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ levels}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $V = \mathbf{1024}$ **discrete signal levels** are required (each carrying 10 bits).

---

**(iii) Doubled bandwidth with $V = 2$: (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& B = 7000 \text{ Hz}, \quad V = 2 \\
& C = 2 \times 7000 \times \log_2 2 \\
& C = 14{,}000 \text{ bps} = 14 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** Doubling the bandwidth (3.5 kHz → 7 kHz) while keeping the signal levels constant *exactly doubles* the data rate (7 kbps → 14 kbps). This confirms the **linear relationship** between bandwidth and capacity in a noiseless channel: $C \propto B$ when $V$ is held constant.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice, 14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a clear diagram, explain the concept of a noiseless channel. Discuss the two fundamental physical constraints that combine to give the Nyquist bit rate formula. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Consider three different noiseless channels with the following specifications:
  - Channel 1: $B = 4$ kHz, $V = 2$
  - Channel 2: $B = 4$ kHz, $V = 8$
  - Channel 3: $B = 8$ kHz, $V = 2$

  (i) Compute the maximum data rate for each channel.
  (ii) Which channel offers the highest data rate? Justify.
  (iii) What does this comparison tell you about the relative effectiveness of increasing bandwidth vs. increasing signal levels? **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Conceptual Explanation (7 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):** A **noiseless channel** is an idealized transmission medium in which there is zero thermal noise, zero interference, and zero distortion — the only limiting factor is the channel's **bandwidth**.

**The Two Physical Constraints (4 marks):**

1. **Time-Domain (Sampling) Constraint:** Governed by the Nyquist Sampling Theorem. A channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz can carry at most $2B$ independent signal changes per second. This is the symbol rate ceiling.
2. **Encoding-Domain (Information) Constraint:** Governed by the number of distinguishable signal levels $V$ the transmitter can generate. Each level can encode $\log_2 V$ bits.

**Combining the Constraints (1 mark):**
$$\text{Total Data Rate} = (\text{Constraint 1}) \times (\text{Constraint 2}) = 2B \times \log_2 V$$

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** The student should sketch the flow: $B$ → 2B (Sampling block) → × $\log_2 V$ (Encoder block) → $C$ (Output).

> **Note:** A Mermaid block diagram similar to Diagram 4.1 in Section 4 of these notes may be reproduced by the student for full credit.

---

#### Part (b) — Comparative Analysis (7 Marks)

**(i) Capacity calculation for each channel: (3 marks — 1 mark each)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Channel 1:} \quad & B = 4000 \text{ Hz}, \quad V = 2 \\
& C_1 = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2 2 = 8000 \times 1 = 8000 \text{ bps} \\
\textbf{Channel 2:} \quad & B = 4000 \text{ Hz}, \quad V = 8 \\
& C_2 = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2 8 = 8000 \times 3 = 24{,}000 \text{ bps} \\
\textbf{Channel 3:} \quad & B = 8000 \text{ Hz}, \quad V = 2 \\
& C_3 = 2 \times 8000 \times \log_2 2 = 16{,}000 \times 1 = 16{,}000 \text{ bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Highest data rate: (2 marks)**

**Channel 2** offers the highest data rate at **24 kbps**, beating Channel 3 (16 kbps) and Channel 1 (8 kbps). 

Justification: Channel 2 uses the same bandwidth as Channel 1 but triples $V$, gaining $\log_2 8 = 3$ bits per symbol. The triple is more than enough to outperform Channel 3's bandwidth doubling (which only adds a factor of 2). However, in general, **linear bandwidth increase is more powerful than logarithmic level increase** for large gains.

**(iii) Engineering Insight: (2 marks)**

- Increasing **bandwidth** scales capacity **linearly** ($C \propto B$).
- Increasing **signal levels** scales capacity **logarithmically** ($C \propto \log_2 V$).
- For *small* enhancements (e.g., going from 2 to 8 levels), increasing $V$ can be more cost-effective than increasing $B$ (which may require new hardware/media).
- For *large* enhancements, bandwidth expansion is more impactful because bandwidth gives linear returns while levels give logarithmic returns.
- **Real-world trade-off:** Higher $V$ requires better Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) for the receiver to distinguish levels — this is the bridge to Shannon's theorem on noisy channels.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit omission:** Students often write $C = 2B \log_2 V$ without stating the unit "**bits per second**" — this costs 0.5 to 1 mark.
> 2. **Log base confusion:** A frequent error is using $\log_{10}$ instead of $\log_2$ for binary data. The base **must be 2** because we are measuring information in **bits**. Shannon's formula on noisy channels also uses base 2.
> 3. **Confusing Nyquist with Shannon:** On a noisy channel, applying Nyquist's noiseless formula gives an *unachievable* upper bound. Students must recognize when the question implies a noisy channel and switch to Shannon's formula.
> 4. **Forgetting to round up $V$:** When solving reverse problems, $V$ must be an integer — round up, never down, because rounding down violates the capacity requirement.
> 5. **Mixing baud and bps:** Baud = symbols/sec = $2B$. bps = bits/sec = $2B \log_2 V$. Confusing them is a guaranteed mark loss.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Nyquist Theorem (1924)** applies to **noiseless** channels and gives the maximum data rate as $C = 2B \log_2 V$ bps.
- ✅ The **$2B$** factor comes from the **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** — a signal of bandwidth $B$ must be sampled at least $2B$ times/sec to be perfectly reconstructed.
- ✅ The **$\log_2 V$** factor encodes the information per symbol in bits; $V$ is the number of **discrete signal levels** (a transmitter design choice).
- ✅ Capacity grows **linearly** with bandwidth but **logarithmically** with signal levels.
- ✅ For $V=2$ (binary), the formula reduces to $C = 2B$ — a useful quick check.
- ✅ Telephone channel: $B = 4$ kHz, $V = 2$ → $C = 8$ kbps (the original PCM standard).
- ✅ The Nyquist bound is an **idealized upper limit** — real channels have noise, governed by **Shannon's Theorem**.
- ✅ The **unit of bandwidth** is **Hertz (Hz)**; the **unit of capacity** is **bits per second (bps)**; **$V$ is dimensionless**.
- ✅ Symbol rate (baud) = $2B$; Bit rate (bps) = $2B \log_2 V$.
- ✅ In a noiseless channel, there is **no error** — the formula gives the **maximum possible**, and this maximum is **achievable in practice** for an ideal noiseless medium.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nyquist Bandwidth
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nyquist Bandwidth

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Nyquist Bandwidth — Foundational Concepts

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the framework of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for Data Communication, the **Nyquist Bandwidth** refers to the theoretical maximum data transmission rate (channel capacity) achievable over a **noiseless communication channel** of finite bandwidth. It was derived by **Harry Nyquist** in 1928 as a direct consequence of the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**.

For a channel of bandwidth **B (Hz)** transmitting **M discrete signal levels** (symbols) per signaling interval, the **maximum bit rate** $C$ is given by the **Nyquist Bit Rate Formula**:

$$C = 2 B \log_{2}(M) \;\; \text{bits/second}$$

where:
- **C** = Channel capacity (bit rate) in **bits per second (bps)**
- **B** = Bandwidth of the channel in **Hertz (Hz)**
- **M** = Number of distinct signal levels (discrete symbols) used
- **2B** = Maximum number of signal changes per second (twice the bandwidth)
- **$\log_2(M)$** = Number of bits carried by each signal level

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Nyquist's theorem assumes a **noise-free** channel. It gives the **upper theoretical limit** for symbol transmission. The presence of noise is handled separately by **Shannon's Channel Capacity Theorem** (Hartley–Shannon law).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Highway Lanes & Cars" Model

Imagine a **highway** as your communication channel:
- The **width of the highway** = **Bandwidth (B)**. A wider road fits more cars per second.
- The **type of vehicle** = **Signal levels (M)**. A sedan (2 levels — binary) carries fewer passengers than a bus (multiple levels — M-ary signaling).
- **Nyquist's rule** says: *"On a noiseless highway of width B, you can change lanes at most 2B times per second, and each vehicle can carry $\log_2(M)$ passengers."*

So doubling the bandwidth OR increasing the signal levels (more sophisticated modulation) boosts the data rate — but only **mathematically** in a noise-free environment. In reality, **noise always exists**, which is why **Shannon's theorem** is the practical bound.

## 1.3 Why Nyquist Bandwidth Matters in Data Communication

- It is the **theoretical benchmark** for all digital transmission systems (e.g., Ethernet, DSL, fiber optics).
- It explains why **higher-order modulation schemes** (QAM-64, QAM-256) pack more data into the same bandwidth.
- It is the **first step** in analyzing link capacity before considering noise (Shannon).
- It is **directly examinable** in KTU Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition:** If the channel uses only **2 signal levels** ($M = 2$, i.e., binary signaling), the formula simplifies to $C = 2B$ bps. This is the **classical Nyquist rate** for binary transmission.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 1: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Assumptions of Nyquist's Theorem

To apply the Nyquist formula correctly, the following **assumptions** must hold:

1. **Noise-free channel** — No random signal distortion from thermal noise, crosstalk, or interference.
2. **Ideal low-pass channel** — Perfect rectangular frequency response from 0 to B Hz.
3. **Perfect synchronization** — Sender and receiver clock alignment is exact (no ISI).
4. **Equal symbol intervals** — Each signal level occupies the same time slot T = 1/(2B).
5. **M-ary signaling** — Signal levels are equally spaced and equally probable.

## 2.2 Logical Breakdown — "Why 2B?"

The **"2"** in $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ arises from the **Nyquist Sampling Theorem**, which states:

> A band-limited signal of bandwidth **B Hz** can be completely reconstructed from its samples **only if** sampled at a rate $\geq 2B$ samples per second.

In a digital channel, **each symbol transition** corresponds to **one sample interval**. Hence:
- Number of independent signal changes per second = $2B$
- Each change conveys $\log_2(M)$ bits
- Total bit rate = $2B \times \log_2(M)$ bps

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Boundary Case |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ | **Nyquist Bit Rate (Noiseless Capacity)** | bps | All cases |
| 2 | $C = 2B$ | Special case: Binary ($M=2$) | bps | $\log_2(2)=1$ |
| 3 | $r = \log_2(M)$ | **Bits per signal level** | bits/symbol | $M=2^r$ |
| 4 | $T = \dfrac{1}{2B}$ | **Symbol duration** | seconds | Nyquist interval |
| 5 | $f_s = 2B$ | **Nyquist sampling rate** | samples/sec | Min. sampling |
| 6 | $M = 2^{C/2B}$ | Signal levels for target rate | dimensionless | Integer only |
| 7 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (for Shannon) | dB | Used in $C = B\log_2(1+S/N)$ |
| 8 | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | **Shannon Capacity (Noisy)** | bps | Compare with Nyquist |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Do NOT confuse** $C = 2B\log_2(M)$ (Nyquist, noiseless) with $C = B\log_2(1+S/N)$ (Shannon, noisy). KTU frequently tests the **distinction** between the two.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DSL Modems:** Use Nyquist's principle to fit more bits per symbol (DMT modulation).
- **Fiber Optic Links (OTU, OTN):** Use M-ary PAM/QAM to maximize capacity over limited bandwidth.
- **Wi-Fi (802.11ax):** Adaptive modulation up to 1024-QAM leverages higher M to push closer to Nyquist limit.
- **Audio CD (44.1 kHz sampling):** Built directly on Nyquist sampling theorem for 20 kHz audio bandwidth.
- **5G NR:** Massive MIMO and high-order QAM (up to 256-QAM) operate close to the Nyquist bound in clean RF conditions.

## 2.5 Key Comparisons Students Must Memorize

| Aspect | Nyquist Theorem | Shannon Theorem |
|--------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Channel** | Noiseless | Noisy |
| **Formula** | $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ |
| **Considers** | Bandwidth & signal levels | Bandwidth & SNR |
| **Limiting factor** | Signal level engineering | Noise floor |
| **Year / Originator** | Nyquist, 1928 | Shannon, 1948 |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 1: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Workouts

## 3.1 Derivation: From Sampling Theorem to Nyquist Bit Rate

We begin with the **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem**, which states that a band-limited analog signal of maximum frequency **$f_{\max}$** Hz must be sampled at least at:

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{\max}
$$

Let the **channel bandwidth** be $B = f_{\max}$. The **minimum sampling rate** (Nyquist rate) is:

$$
f_s = 2B \;\; \text{samples/second}
$$

Each sample corresponds to **one signaling interval** during which a symbol is transmitted. The **symbol duration** $T$ is therefore:

$$
T = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{2B} \;\; \text{seconds/symbol}
$$

If the channel uses $M$ discrete signal levels, then by the **information-theoretic encoding principle**, each symbol carries:

$$
\text{bits per symbol} = \log_2(M) \;\; \text{bits}
$$

Hence the **bit rate** (channel capacity) is:

$$
C = \frac{\text{bits per symbol}}{\text{time per symbol}} = \frac{\log_2(M)}{T}
$$

Substituting $T = 1/(2B)$:

$$
C = \frac{\log_2(M)}{1/(2B)} = 2B \log_2(M)
$$

This completes the **derivation** of the Nyquist bit rate formula. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between $M$ and $r$ (Bits per Level)

Starting from $C = 2B \log_2(M)$, divide both sides by $2B$:

$$
\frac{C}{2B} = \log_2(M)
$$

Apply $\exp_2$ to both sides (i.e., $2^{(\cdot)}$ to both sides):

$$
2^{C/2B} = M
$$

Let $r = C/(2B)$ denote the **bits per signal level**. Then:

$$
M = 2^{r}
$$

For **M to be physically realizable**, $M$ must be a **positive integer** $\geq 2$, which constrains $r$ to specific discrete values.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick Question Alert:** Students often forget that $M$ must be a power of 2 in pure Nyquist signaling. If a problem gives a non-power-of-2 $M$, it is either a **trick** or implies **non-uniform** (probabilistic) signaling.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Board Style)

### **Example 1 — Binary Channel (M = 2)**

**Problem:** A channel has a bandwidth of **4 kHz**. Using binary signaling ($M = 2$), find the maximum bit rate.

**Solution:**

Given: $B = 4000$ Hz, $M = 2$.

Apply the Nyquist formula:

$$
C = 2B \log_2(M)
$$

Substitute values:

$$
C = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2(2)
$$

Since $\log_2(2) = 1$:

$$
C = 2 \times 4000 \times 1 = 8000 \;\; \text{bps}
$$

**Answer:** The maximum bit rate is **8 kbps**.

---

### **Example 2 — M-ary Channel (M = 8)**

**Problem:** A noiseless channel of **6 kHz** bandwidth uses **8 signal levels**. Compute the channel capacity and the bit duration.

**Solution:**

Given: $B = 6000$ Hz, $M = 8$.

Bits per symbol:

$$
\log_2(8) = 3 \;\; \text{bits/symbol}
$$

Channel capacity:

$$
C = 2 \times 6000 \times 3 = 36000 \;\; \text{bps} = 36 \;\; \text{kbps}
$$

Symbol duration:

$$
T = \frac{1}{2B} = \frac{1}{2 \times 6000} = 83.33 \;\; \mu s
$$

**Answer:** $C = 36$ kbps; $T = 83.33$ $\mu$s.

---

### **Example 3 — Reverse Problem: Find Required M**

**Problem:** A **3 kHz** channel must support **24 kbps**. How many signal levels are needed (assuming noiseless conditions)?

**Solution:**

Given: $B = 3000$ Hz, $C = 24000$ bps.

Rearrange Nyquist formula:

$$
C = 2B \log_2(M) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \log_2(M) = \frac{C}{2B}
$$

Substitute:

$$
\log_2(M) = \frac{24000}{2 \times 3000} = \frac{24000}{6000} = 4
$$

Therefore:

$$
M = 2^{4} = 16 \;\; \text{signal levels}
$$

**Answer:** The system requires **16 discrete signal levels** (16-QAM equivalent).

---

### **Example 4 — Doubling Bandwidth vs. Doubling Signal Levels**

**Problem:** A channel initially operates at $B = 4$ kHz with $M = 4$. Compare the new capacity if (i) bandwidth is doubled to 8 kHz, and (ii) signal levels are doubled to $M = 8$.

**Solution (i):** Double bandwidth, keep $M = 4$:

$$
C_1 = 2 \times 8000 \times \log_2(4) = 16000 \times 2 = 32000 \;\; \text{bps}
$$

**Solution (ii):** Double $M$ from 4 to 8, keep $B = 4$ kHz:

$$
C_2 = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2(8) = 8000 \times 3 = 24000 \;\; \text{bps}
$$

**Observation:** Doubling bandwidth gives **more capacity** than doubling signal levels — because bandwidth scales the bit rate **linearly** while signal levels scale **logarithmically**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why optical systems aggressively pursue **wider bandwidth** (WDM, DWDM) rather than just higher-order QAM.

---

### **Example 5 — Combining Nyquist & Shannon (Comparative)**

**Problem:** A channel has $B = 4$ kHz, $M = 16$, and $\text{SNR} = 30$ dB. Compare the Nyquist limit vs. the Shannon limit.

**Solution — Nyquist Limit:**

$$
C_{\text{Nyq}} = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_2(16) = 8000 \times 4 = 32000 \;\; \text{bps}
$$

**Solution — Shannon Limit:**

Convert SNR: $30 \text{ dB} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N) \Rightarrow S/N = 10^{3} = 1000$.

$$
C_{\text{Shan}} = 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 1000) = 4000 \times \log_2(1001)
$$

Compute: $\log_2(1001) \approx 9.967$.

$$
C_{\text{Shan}} \approx 4000 \times 9.967 \approx 39868 \;\; \text{bps}
$$

**Observation:** Shannon's limit (≈ 39.87 kbps) > Nyquist's limit (32 kbps), confirming Nyquist is the **tighter bound** in noise-limited channels. Real systems must satisfy:

$$
C \leq \min\!\left(2B \log_2 M, \; B \log_2(1+S/N)\right)
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 1: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Nyquist Channel Capacity Model

The diagram below illustrates the functional flow of a communication system governed by Nyquist's capacity constraint.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Data Source] --> enc[Encoder M-ary Signaling]
    enc --> chn[Noiseless Channel Bandwidth B Hz]
    chn --> dec[Decoder Symbol Detector]
    dec --> dst[Data Sink]
    enc -.Capacity Limit.-> nyt[Nyquist C equals 2B log2 M]
    chn -.2B transitions per second.-> nyt
    nyt -.Constrains.-> enc
```

**Interpretation of the Block Diagram:**

- **Data Source → Encoder:** Source bits are grouped into blocks of $\log_2(M)$ bits and mapped to one of $M$ signal levels.
- **Noiseless Channel:** The channel is idealized with bandwidth $B$; it allows up to $2B$ signal changes per second.
- **Nyquist Constraint (dashed link):** The capacity formula $C = 2B\log_2(M)$ acts as a **design ceiling** linking encoder and channel.
- **Decoder → Data Sink:** The receiver samples at $2B$ Hz and maps detected symbols back to bits.

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Nyquist vs. Shannon Selection

Use this flowchart to decide which formula applies in a KTU problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Channel Specification Given] --> B{Is Noise Mentioned?}
    B -- No --> C[Use Nyquist C equals 2B log2 M]
    B -- Yes --> D{Is M or SNR Given?}
    D -- M given only --> E[Use Nyquist formula as upper bound]
    D -- SNR given only --> F[Use Shannon C equals B log2 of 1 plus S over N]
    D -- Both M and SNR given --> G[Take Minimum of Nyquist and Shannon]
    G --> H[Actual Capacity equals min of 2B log2 M and B log2 1 plus S over N]
    C --> I[Final Answer in bps]
    E --> I
    F --> I
    H --> I
```

**Reading the Flowchart:**

1. **No noise mentioned** → straight to Nyquist.
2. **Noise present, only SNR** → straight to Shannon.
3. **Noise present, both M and SNR** → actual capacity is the **minimum** of the two (system is limited by the more restrictive bound).
4. Always report the final answer in **bits per second (bps)**.

## 4.3 Concept Map — Nyquist Theorem's Logical Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    smp[Nyquist Sampling Theorem] --> smprate[Min Sample Rate equals 2B]
    smprate --> symdur[Symbol Duration T equals 1 over 2B]
    symdur --> cap[Channel Capacity C equals 2B log2 M]
    siglvl[Number of Signal Levels M] --> cap
    bw[Channel Bandwidth B] --> cap
    cap --> app1[Ethernet and DSL]
    cap --> app2[5G NR and WiFi 6]
    cap --> app3[Fiber Optic OTN]
    cap --> comp[Compare with Shannon Limit]
    shan[Shannon Theorem] --> comp
```

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs in the Mermaid diagrams above are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (`src`, `enc`, `chn`, `dec`, `dst`, `nyt`, `smp`, `smprate`, `symdur`, `cap`, `siglvl`, `bw`, `comp`, `shan`, `app1`, `app2`, `app3`) to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards. Labels contain only uppercase alphanumeric text and standard ASCII characters — no bold, italics, or HTML formatting inside quoted strings.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Module 1: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1.** State the **Nyquist Channel Capacity Theorem** and write its formula. Define each term.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Nyquist Channel Capacity Theorem** states that the maximum data rate of a **noiseless channel** of finite bandwidth $B$ using $M$ discrete signal levels is given by:

$$
C = 2B \log_2(M) \;\; \text{bits/second}
$$

where:
- $C$ = Maximum bit rate (channel capacity) in bps.
- $B$ = Bandwidth of the channel in Hz.
- $M$ = Number of distinct signal levels (symbols).
- $\log_2(M)$ = Number of bits represented by each signal level.

The theorem is based on the assumption of a **noise-free** channel and a **band-limited** signal. *(Full marks for the formula, the term definitions, and the noise-free assumption.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** A noiseless channel has a bandwidth of **5 kHz**. Calculate the maximum bit rate when (i) binary signaling is used, and (ii) **16 signal levels** are used.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given: $B = 5000$ Hz.

**(i) Binary signaling** ($M = 2$):

$$
C_1 = 2 \times 5000 \times \log_2(2) = 2 \times 5000 \times 1 = 10000 \;\; \text{bps}
$$

**(ii) 16 signal levels** ($M = 16$):

$$
C_2 = 2 \times 5000 \times \log_2(16) = 2 \times 5000 \times 4 = 40000 \;\; \text{bps}
$$

**Final Answer:** $C_1 = 10$ kbps, $C_2 = 40$ kbps. *(1.5 marks each sub-part.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 | ESE Module 1]**

### ✦ **Question A — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Derive the **Nyquist bit rate formula** $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ starting from the **Nyquist Sampling Theorem**. Clearly state all assumptions. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A **3.5 kHz** channel is used to transmit a digital signal. The system uses **32 signal levels**.  
    (i) Find the maximum data rate achievable.  
    (ii) If the bandwidth is increased to **7 kHz** while keeping $M = 32$, what is the new capacity?  
    (iii) How many signal levels would be required to achieve **70 kbps** at the original 3.5 kHz bandwidth? **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) Derivation of Nyquist Bit Rate Formula** *(7 marks)*

**Step 1 — Start with Sampling Theorem:**  
A band-limited signal of maximum frequency $f_{\max}$ must be sampled at:

$$
f_s \geq 2 f_{\max} \;\; \text{samples/second}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify channel bandwidth:**  
In a channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz, the highest frequency that can be passed is $f_{\max} = B$. Hence the minimum sampling rate is:

$$
f_s = 2B
$$

**Step 3 — Symbol duration:**  
Each sample corresponds to one signaling interval. The duration of one symbol is:

$$
T = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{2B} \;\; \text{seconds}
$$

**Step 4 — Bits per symbol:**  
If the channel uses $M$ equally probable, distinct signal levels, each symbol conveys:

$$
\text{bits per symbol} = \log_2(M)
$$

**Step 5 — Combine to obtain bit rate:**  
The bit rate $C$ equals bits per symbol divided by time per symbol:

$$
C = \frac{\log_2(M)}{T} = \frac{\log_2(M)}{1/(2B)}
$$

$$
\therefore \;\; C = 2B \log_2(M) \;\; \text{bps} \quad \blacksquare
$$

**Assumptions (must be stated):**
- Channel is **noise-free**.
- Channel is **ideal low-pass** with rectangular frequency response.
- **No intersymbol interference (ISI)**.
- Signal levels are **equiprobable and equally spaced**.

---

**(b) Numerical Computations** *(7 marks)*

Given: $B = 3500$ Hz, $M = 32$.

**(i) Maximum data rate at 3.5 kHz, M = 32:**

$$
\log_2(32) = 5 \;\; \text{bits/symbol}
$$

$$
C = 2 \times 3500 \times 5 = 35000 \;\; \text{bps} = 35 \;\; \text{kbps}
$$

**(ii) New capacity with B = 7 kHz, M = 32:**

$$
C_{\text{new}} = 2 \times 7000 \times 5 = 70000 \;\; \text{bps} = 70 \;\; \text{kbps}
$$

**Observation:** Doubling the bandwidth doubles the capacity (linear scaling).

**(iii) Required $M$ for $C = 70$ kbps at $B = 3.5$ kHz:**

$$
\log_2(M) = \frac{C}{2B} = \frac{70000}{2 \times 3500} = \frac{70000}{7000} = 10
$$

$$
M = 2^{10} = 1024 \;\; \text{signal levels}
$$

**Final Answer:** (i) 35 kbps, (ii) 70 kbps, (iii) $M = 1024$.

---

### ✦ **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** Explain the difference between the **Nyquist Channel Capacity Theorem** and the **Shannon Channel Capacity Theorem**. Under what conditions is Nyquist's formula a more restrictive bound than Shannon's? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A channel has bandwidth **B = 5 kHz** and operates in a **noisy environment with SNR = 1023** (linear, not dB).  
    (i) Compute the **Shannon capacity limit**.  
    (ii) If the system designer wants to use a modulation scheme with **M = 8 signal levels**, calculate the **Nyquist limit**.  
    (iii) Determine the **achievable data rate** and identify the **bottleneck theorem**. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Nyquist vs. Shannon** *(7 marks)*

| Aspect | Nyquist Theorem | Shannon Theorem |
|--------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Channel Model** | Noiseless | Noisy |
| **Formula** | $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ |
| **Parameters** | Bandwidth + signal levels | Bandwidth + SNR |
| **Originator / Year** | Harry Nyquist, 1928 | Claude Shannon, 1948 |
| **Use Case** | Theoretical upper bound on symbol rate | Practical capacity limit under noise |

**When Nyquist is more restrictive:**  
Nyquist's formula is the tighter (more restrictive) bound when:

$$
2B \log_2(M) < B \log_2(1 + S/N)
$$

This typically happens when:
- The **modulation order is low** (small $M$),
- The **SNR is high** (Shannon's bound becomes very large).

In such scenarios, the **channel cannot support** the modulation scheme's symbol rate, regardless of SNR.

**Engineering implication:** Real systems must satisfy:

$$
C_{\text{actual}} \leq \min\!\left(2B \log_2 M, \; B \log_2(1+S/N)\right)
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Computations** *(7 marks)*

Given: $B = 5000$ Hz, $S/N = 1023$, $M = 8$.

**(i) Shannon Capacity:**

$$
C_{\text{Shan}} = B \log_2(1 + S/N) = 5000 \times \log_2(1 + 1023)
$$

$$
\log_2(1024) = 10
$$

$$
C_{\text{Shan}} = 5000 \times 10 = 50000 \;\; \text{bps} = 50 \;\; \text{kbps}
$$

**(ii) Nyquist Limit with M = 8:**

$$
\log_2(8) = 3
$$

$$
C_{\text{Nyq}} = 2 \times 5000 \times 3 = 30000 \;\; \text{bps} = 30 \;\; \text{kbps}
$$

**(iii) Achievable Rate & Bottleneck:**

$$
C_{\text{actual}} = \min(30,000, \; 50,000) = 30000 \;\; \text{bps}
$$

**Bottleneck:** **Nyquist's theorem** is the binding constraint here. The channel's symbol-level capacity is limiting, not the noise. To increase throughput, the designer must either **increase bandwidth** or **use higher-order modulation** (e.g., $M = 16$ or more).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** Many students write bandwidth in **kHz** but forget to convert to **Hz** before plugging into $C = 2B\log_2(M)$. A 5 kHz channel **must** be entered as **5000 Hz**. Lost marks: **1–2 per occurrence**.
>
> 2. **Misapplying Shannon to Noiseless Scenarios:** If the problem does **not mention** SNR or noise, do **NOT** use $C = B\log_2(1+S/N)$. Use only the Nyquist formula.
>
> 3. **Non-integer $M$:** If your calculation gives a non-integer $M$ (e.g., $M = 12.7$), the system **cannot** use that level count. Either round **up** to a power of 2 (e.g., $M = 16$) and accept overshoot, or round **down** ($M = 8$) and recalculate. KTU expects you to **comment on the trade-off**.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the "2" in 2B:** A common careless error is to write $C = B \log_2(M)$ instead of $C = 2B \log_2(M)$. The factor of **2** comes from sampling at twice the bandwidth. Marks deducted: typically **half-credit (≈ 1 mark)**.
>
> 5. **Not Stating Assumptions:** KTU valuation key gives **1–2 marks** specifically for listing the assumptions (noise-free, ideal channel, no ISI). Skipping them is a free-mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Nyquist Bandwidth**

- **Core Formula:** $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ — memorize verbatim, including the **factor of 2**.
- **Binary special case:** When $M = 2$, $C = 2B$ bps.
- **Reverse formula:** $M = 2^{C/2B}$ — useful for "find required signal levels" problems.
- **Symbol duration:** $T = 1/(2B)$ seconds — useful when problems give bit duration or signaling rate.
- **Sampling rate:** $f_s = 2B$ samples/second — the **Nyquist rate** for a band-limited signal.
- **Bits per symbol:** $r = \log_2(M)$ — must be a positive integer for standard M-ary signaling.
- **Assumption set:** Noise-free, ideal channel, no ISI, equiprobable levels — **always state** in 7+ mark answers.
- **Nyquist vs. Shannon:** Nyquist is the **symbol-rate bound**; Shannon is the **noise-rate bound**. Real capacity = **minimum** of the two.
- **Unit discipline:** Convert kHz → Hz, kbps → bps before computation.
- **M must be an integer ≥ 2**, and (in standard schemes) a power of 2.
- **Doubling B doubles C; doubling M adds only $\log_2(M)$ factor** — bandwidth scaling is more powerful.
- **KTU Hot Topics:** Numerical problems combining Nyquist + Shannon, derivation of the formula, comparison of theorems, reverse computation of $M$ or $B$.
- **Diagram must include:** sampling theorem → symbol duration → bit rate chain (as shown in Section 4.3).
- **Examiner loves:** "Compare and contrast Nyquist vs. Shannon" — practice this as a 7-mark answer.
- **Common trap:** Writing $C = B \log_2(M)$ (missing the 2). **Never forget the 2.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noisy Channel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Noisy Channel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📡 MODULE 1: NOISY CHANNEL — Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Noisy Channel** is a transmission medium in a data communication system in which the received signal differs from the transmitted signal due to the addition of unwanted random disturbances called **noise**. Mathematically, if the transmitted signal is $s(t)$ and the noise is $n(t)$, then the received signal is:
> $$r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$$

In the context of KTU's outcome-based framework, a noisy channel is a probabilistic medium where the probability of a bit error $P_e$ is non-zero. Every practical physical medium (copper wire, optical fiber, radio link) is inherently a **noisy channel** due to thermal agitation of electrons, electromagnetic interference, and physical imperfections.

> [!NOTE]
> **Channel Types as per KTU Module 1:**
> - **Noiseless Channel** — Theoretical ideal; capacity governed by Nyquist bit rate.
> - **Noisy Channel** — Practical medium; capacity governed by Shannon's capacity formula.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy — The Whispering Game in a Stadium 🎤**
> Imagine a person (the **sender**) whispering a secret message to another person (the **receiver**) inside a crowded football stadium. The whisper ($s(t)$) is the original signal. The crowd noise — fans shouting, horns, vendors yelling — represents the **noise** $n(t)$. The friend hears the whisper **plus** the crowd noise, so the message received ($r(t)$) is slightly garbled. The louder the crowd (higher noise power $N$), the harder it is to understand the message (lower channel capacity $C$). This is exactly what happens on a noisy communication channel: the **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** determines the maximum data rate you can reliably achieve.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Boltzmann's Constant | $k$ | $\mathbf{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}}$ |
| Standard Temperature | $T_0$ | $\mathbf{290 \text{ K}}$ |
| Thermal Noise Floor | $N$ | $\mathbf{kTB}$ (Watts) |
| SNR Threshold (Telephony) | $S/N$ | $\mathbf{\approx 1000 \text{ (30 dB)}}$ |
| Shannon Limit (Theoretical Max) | $C$ | $\mathbf{B \log_2(1 + S/N) \text{ bits/sec}}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Noise Model — How a Noisy Channel Works

The transmitted signal $s(t)$ does not arrive cleanly. The system model is:

**Step 1 — Source Generation:** The transmitter produces a signal $s(t)$ with power $S$.

**Step 2 — Channel Corruption:** The channel adds **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)** with power spectral density $N_0/2$, giving noise power $N = N_0 B$, where $B$ is the bandwidth in Hz.

**Step 3 — Reception:** The receiver observes $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$.

**Step 4 — Detection:** The receiver attempts to estimate $s(t)$ from $r(t)$ using statistical decision rules (e.g., Maximum Likelihood detection).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is noise inevitable?**
> The **H theorem of thermodynamics** proves that any physical medium at a temperature $T > 0$ K contains random thermal motion of charge carriers. This generates an irreducible **thermal noise floor** of $N = kTB$ watts — you cannot engineer this away, only design around it.

## 2.2 Types of Noise (High-Yield for KTU)

- **Thermal Noise (Johnson-Nyquist Noise):** Caused by thermal agitation of electrons in conductors. Power = $\mathbf{kTB}$ watts. Uniformly distributed across all frequencies (white).
- **Intermodulation Noise:** Produced when signals of different frequencies share a medium and cause sum/difference frequency components.
- **Crosstalk:** Unwanted coupling between adjacent channels (e.g., between twisted pairs).
- **Impulse Noise:** Sudden, sharp spikes from electromagnetic interference (lightning, switching transients). Dominant error source in digital systems.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula Table for Noisy Channel Problems (KTU 2024)**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N = kTB$ | Thermal noise power | Watts (W) |
| 2 | $N = N_0 B$ | Noise power using PSD $N_0$ | Watts (W) |
| 3 | $\text{SNR} = S/N$ | Linear signal-to-noise ratio | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ | SNR in decibels | Decibels (dB) |
| 5 | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | **Shannon Channel Capacity** | bits/second (bps) |
| 6 | $C = B \log_2(1 + 10^{(\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10)})$ | Shannon capacity using dB SNR | bits/second (bps) |
| 7 | $C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2B \log_2 M$ | Noiseless channel capacity (Nyquist) | bits/second (bps) |
| 8 | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2\,\text{SNR}}\right)$ | Probability of bit error (BPSK) | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $F = \text{SNR}_{\text{in}} / \text{SNR}_{\text{out}}$ | Noise figure of a system | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $E_b/N_0 = \text{SNR} \cdot (B/R)$ | Energy per bit to noise PSD ratio | Dimensionless |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** When SNR is given in dB, **always convert to linear scale** using $S/N = 10^{(\text{dB}/10)}$ before plugging into Shannon's formula. Using dB directly inside $\log_2$ is the #1 mark-losing error in KTU exams.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The noisy channel model is the **foundation of every digital communication standard**: 4G/5G cellular, Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11), fiber-optic long-haul links, and satellite communication. Shannon's 1948 theorem tells engineers the **theoretical upper bound** on data rate — modern coding schemes (LDPC, Turbo codes, Polar codes) push practical systems within ~0.5 dB of this limit.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Shannon's Channel Capacity Theorem

**Starting Premise (Hartley-Shannon Law, 1948):**
A channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz with additive white Gaussian noise has a maximum reliable data rate $C$ given by:

$$C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) \quad \text{bits/second}$$

**Exhaustive Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Define signal energy and noise energy over symbol duration $T$.**
The signal energy per symbol is $E_s = S \cdot T$, and the noise energy per symbol is $N_0 = N/B$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_s &= S \cdot T \\
N_0 &= \frac{N}{B}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Express the number of distinguishable signal levels.**
For a band-limited channel of bandwidth $B$, the Nyquist sampling theorem gives $2B$ independent samples per second. With $M$ distinguishable amplitude levels, the number of distinct symbols per second is $2B \log_2 M$.

$$
R = 2B \log_2 M
$$

**Step 3 — Determine $M$ from SNR.**
The difference between the smallest distinguishable signal levels is set by the noise amplitude. With peak signal $V_{\max}$ and $M$ levels, the step size is $\Delta = V_{\max}/M$. For reliable detection, we need $\Delta \geq V_{\text{noise}}$, so:

$$
M^2 \leq 1 + \frac{S}{N}
$$

(Reasoning: Signal power $S \propto V_{\max}^2$ and noise power $N \propto V_{\text{noise}}^2$. Setting $V_{\text{noise}} = V_{\max}/M$ and substituting yields $M^2 \leq 1 + S/N$.)

**Step 4 — Take the base-2 logarithm and multiply by $2B$ samples/sec.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\max} &= 2B \log_2\!\left(\sqrt{1 + \frac{S}{N}}\right) \\
R_{\max} &= 2B \cdot \frac{1}{2} \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \\
R_{\max} &= B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Result (Shannon Capacity):**

$$
\boxed{\,C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \;\text{bits/second}\,}
$$

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A channel has bandwidth $B = 4$ kHz and SNR = 30 dB. Find the channel capacity.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear scale:

$$
\frac{S}{N} = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000
$$

Step 2 — Apply Shannon's formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= B \log_2(1 + S/N) \\
C &= 4000 \times \log_2(1 + 1000) \\
C &= 4000 \times \log_2(1001) \\
C &= 4000 \times 9.967 \\
C &\approx 39{,}870 \text{ bps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $C \approx 39.87$ kbps.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Noiseless vs. Noisy Channel

| Parameter | Noiseless Channel (Nyquist) | Noisy Channel (Shannon) |
|---|---|---|
| Governing Equation | $C = 2B \log_2 M$ | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ |
| Depends on SNR? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Depends on Levels $M$? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (absorbed into SNR) |
| Practical Use | Theoretical upper bound | Real system design limit |
| Achievable? | Yes, with perfect signaling | Only as limit; never exactly |
| Effect of Doubling $B$ | Doubles $C$ | Doubles $C$ (same) |
| Effect of Doubling $S/N$ | No effect (fixed $M$) | $C$ increases by $B \log_2 3$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Noisy Channel Communication Model (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcNodeA["Source s of t"] --> encNodeB["Transmitter Encoder"]
    encNodeB --> modNodeC["Modulator Maps to Signal"]
    modNodeC --> chanNodeD["Noisy Channel Adds n of t"]
    chanNodeD --> demNodeE["Receiver Demodulator"]
    demNodeE --> decNodeF["Decoder Decodes bits"]
    decNodeF --> dstNodeG["Destination r of t approx s of t"]
    noiseSrcZ["Noise Source n of t Thermal EMI Crosstalk"] -.-> chanNodeD
    style chanNodeD fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style noiseSrcZ fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:1px
```

**Interpretation:** The **Noisy Channel** block (highlighted in red) is the focal point of Module 1. The dashed yellow input shows the **Noise Source** corrupting the signal during transmission. The receiver's job is to recover $s(t)$ from the corrupted observation $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$.

## 4.2 Channel Capacity vs. SNR Behavior Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Input B fixed 4 kHz"] --> calcB["Convert SNR dB to linear S over N"]
    calcB --> calcC["Compute log base 2 of 1 plus S over N"]
    calcC --> calcD["Multiply by B to get C in bps"]
    calcD --> outLow["If SNR 0 dB to 10 dB C is low kbps range"]
    calcD --> outMid["If SNR 20 dB to 30 dB C is moderate tens of kbps"]
    calcD --> outHigh["If SNR greater than 40 dB C is high greater than 100 kbps"]
    calcD --> noteShannon["Shannon Bound Cannot Be Exceeded No Matter How Advanced Coding"]
    style noteShannon fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation:** This flowchart encapsulates the analysis pipeline for any KTU problem on the **Noisy Channel** — convert, compute logarithm, scale, and interpret the result in context of the Shannon bound.

## 4.3 Noise Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["Channel Noise"]
    rootA --> branchB["Thermal Noise kTB constant across frequency"]
    rootA --> branchC["Intermodulation Nonlinear mixing of signals"]
    rootA --> branchD["Crosstalk Coupling between adjacent channels"]
    rootA --> branchE["Impulse Noise Sudden spikes lightning or switching"]
    branchB --> detailB1["Dominant at low frequencies and high temperatures"]
    branchC --> detailC1["Caused by faulty multiplexing or amplifiers"]
    branchD --> detailD1["Common in twisted pair and PCB traces"]
    branchE --> detailE1["Main error source in digital telephone lines"]
    style rootA fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a noisy channel. Mention any two types of noise affecting data communication.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **noisy channel** is a transmission medium in which the received signal $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ differs from the transmitted signal $s(t)$ due to the addition of unwanted disturbances $n(t)$. **(1 mark)**

Two types of noise:

1. **Thermal Noise:** Random noise generated by the thermal agitation of electrons in a conductor, with power $N = kTB$ watts. It is present across all frequencies and is unavoidable. **(1 mark)**

2. **Impulse Noise:** Sudden, high-amplitude short-duration spikes caused by external electromagnetic disturbances (lightning, switching of heavy electrical equipment). It is the major source of bit errors in digital communication. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the Shannon-Hartley theorem for channel capacity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement:** The Shannon-Hartley theorem states that the maximum theoretical data rate (channel capacity) of a noisy channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz with signal-to-noise ratio $S/N$ is given by: **(2 marks)**

$$
C = B \log_2(1 + S/N) \quad \text{bits/second}
$$

**Explanation:** This theorem, proved by Claude Shannon in 1948, establishes the **theoretical upper limit** on the rate at which information can be transmitted over a noisy channel with **arbitrarily low error probability**, provided sufficiently powerful error-correcting codes are used. The capacity increases linearly with bandwidth $B$ and logarithmically with $(1 + S/N)$. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 1 Choice 1] (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Derive the Shannon-Hartley theorem for the capacity of a noisy channel. State all assumptions. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. The channel is band-limited to $B$ Hz.
2. The noise is **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)** with two-sided power spectral density $N_0/2$.
3. The transmitter can use any number of signal levels $M$.

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Nyquist Sampling:** A band-limited signal of bandwidth $B$ can be uniquely represented by $2B$ samples per second. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Number of Distinct Symbols:** If each sample can take one of $M$ amplitude levels, the data rate in bits/second is:

$$
R = 2B \log_2 M
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Reliable Detection Limit:** The receiver can distinguish two signal levels only if they differ by more than the RMS noise voltage. With peak signal $V_{\max}$ and $M$ levels, the spacing is $\Delta = V_{\max}/M$. For reliable detection, $\Delta \geq \sigma_{\text{noise}}$, giving:

$$
M^2 \leq 1 + \frac{V_{\max}^2}{\sigma_{\text{noise}}^2} = 1 + \frac{S}{N}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 4 — Combine:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 2B \log_2 M \\
&\leq 2B \cdot \log_2 \sqrt{1 + S/N} \\
&= B \log_2(1 + S/N)
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{C = B \log_2(1 + S/N) \;\text{bits/second}}
$$

**(1 mark)**

---

#### **(b) A noisy channel has a bandwidth of 3 kHz and an SNR of 40 dB. Calculate: (i) the channel capacity, and (ii) the minimum SNR (in dB) required to double this capacity. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Channel Capacity Calculation:**

Step 1 — Convert SNR to linear scale: **(1 mark)**

$$
\frac{S}{N} = 10^{40/10} = 10^4 = 10{,}000
$$

Step 2 — Apply Shannon's formula: **(2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= B \log_2(1 + S/N) \\
C &= 3000 \times \log_2(1 + 10{,}000) \\
C &= 3000 \times \log_2(10{,}001) \\
C &= 3000 \times 13.288 \\
C &\approx 39{,}863 \text{ bps} \approx 39.86 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $C \approx 39.86$ kbps. **(1 mark)**

**(ii) Minimum SNR to Double Capacity:**

To double $C$, we need $\log_2(1 + S/N)_{\text{new}} = 2 \log_2(1 + 10{,}000)$, so:

$$
1 + (S/N)_{\text{new}} = (1 + 10{,}000)^2 = 10{,}001^2 \approx 10^8
$$

**(2 marks)**

$$
(S/N)_{\text{new}} \approx 10^8
$$

In dB:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(10^8) = 80 \text{ dB}
$$

**Answer:** Minimum SNR required = **80 dB**. **(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Always express $C$ in **bits per second (bps)**, not bits per symbol. **(−1 mark if wrong)**
> 2. **Logarithm Base:** KTU strictly requires $\log_2$ (base 2) to get the result in **bits**. Using $\log_{10}$ or $\ln$ without conversion is a **−2 mark** penalty.
> 3. **dB vs. Linear:** Failing to convert SNR from dB to linear form before substituting is the **most common error** — full −2 marks.
> 4. **Writing only the final answer:** Examiners award **step marks**; show all intermediate steps for full credit.
> 5. **Forgetting the "+1" inside the log:** The formula is $\log_2(1 + S/N)$, not $\log_2(S/N)$. Missing the 1 costs **−1 mark**.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 1 Choice 2] (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE**

#### **(a) Explain the four major types of noise in a data communication channel. Compare their effects on analog vs. digital signals. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Thermal Noise (1.5 marks)**
- **Cause:** Random thermal motion of electrons in conductors.
- **Power:** $N = kTB$ — directly proportional to bandwidth $B$ and temperature $T$.
- **Effect on Analog:** Adds a continuous hissing background; degrades SNR of voice/music.
- **Effect on Digital:** Causes random bit errors; one of the irreducible error sources.

**2. Intermodulation Noise (1.5 marks)**
- **Cause:** Nonlinearity in transmitters, multiplexers, or amplifiers causing signals of frequencies $f_1$ and $f_2$ to produce sum $(f_1 + f_2)$ and difference $(f_1 - f_2)$ components.
- **Effect on Analog:** Creates unwanted whistles and tones overlapping the original signal.
- **Effect on Digital:** Produces intersymbol interference (ISI) and burst errors.

**3. Crosstalk (1.5 marks)**
- **Cause:** Unwanted electromagnetic coupling between adjacent signal-carrying channels (e.g., nearby twisted pairs in a cable bundle).
- **Effect on Analog:** You can faintly hear another conversation ("crosstalk").
- **Effect on Digital:** Increases the bit error rate, especially at high frequencies.

**4. Impulse Noise (2.5 marks)**
- **Cause:** Sudden, irregular spikes from external sources — lightning, power-line switching, faulty electrical equipment.
- **Characteristics:** High amplitude, short duration, unpredictable timing.
- **Effect on Analog:** Sudden clicks/pops — generally tolerable for voice.
- **Effect on Digital:** **Most damaging** — can wipe out entire frames of data in a few microseconds. Dominates BER statistics in digital telephone lines.

---

#### **(b) A communication channel has bandwidth 8 kHz and is observed to support a maximum data rate of 48 kbps reliably. If the signal power is 20 mW, determine: (i) the noise power in the channel, and (ii) the noise power spectral density $N_0$. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $B = 8$ kHz $= 8000$ Hz, $C = 48$ kbps $= 48{,}000$ bps, $S = 20$ mW $= 0.02$ W.

**(i) Finding the Noise Power $N$:**

Step 1 — Use Shannon's formula rearranged for SNR: **(1 mark)**

$$
C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)
$$

$$
48{,}000 = 8000 \times \log_2(1 + S/N)
$$

Step 2 — Solve for the log term: **(1 mark)**

$$
\log_2(1 + S/N) = \frac{48{,}000}{8000} = 6
$$

Step 3 — Take $2^6$ to remove the logarithm: **(1 mark)**

$$
1 + \frac{S}{N} = 2^6 = 64
$$

Step 4 — Solve for $S/N$: **(1 mark)**

$$
\frac{S}{N} = 64 - 1 = 63
$$

Step 5 — Compute $N$: **(1 mark)**

$$
N = \frac{S}{63} = \frac{0.02}{63} \approx 3.175 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W} = 317.5 \;\mu\text{W}
$$

**Answer:** $N \approx 317.5 \,\mu\text{W}$. **(0.5 mark)**

**(ii) Finding the Noise PSD $N_0$:**

Step 1 — Use $N = N_0 B$: **(1 mark)**

$$
N_0 = \frac{N}{B} = \frac{3.175 \times 10^{-4}}{8000}
$$

Step 2 — Compute: **(1 mark)**

$$
\boxed{N_0 \approx 3.97 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/Hz} = 39.7 \text{ nW/Hz}}
$$

**(0.5 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Warning — Question B Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Algebraic slip at $2^6 = 64$:** Many students write $2^6 = 32$ — verify your powers of two.
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract 1:** The result of $2^6$ is $1 + S/N$, not $S/N$. This is a **−1 mark** error.
> 3. **Unit mismatch:** $N_0$ must be in **W/Hz**, not W. Always divide by $B$ explicitly.
> 4. **Expressing the answer only in scientific notation:** Show the calculation in two forms (W and µW / nW) for full marks.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Noisy Channel (Module 1)**

- ✅ **Noisy Channel Definition:** $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ — received signal = transmitted + noise.
- ✅ **Thermal Noise Formula:** $N = kTB$ (Boltzmann constant $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K).
- ✅ **SNR Conversion:** $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ (linear to dB).
- ✅ **Shannon Capacity (THE key formula):** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ in **bits/second**.
- ✅ **Nyquist Capacity (Noiseless):** $C = 2B \log_2 M$ in **bits/second** (no $S/N$ dependence).
- ✅ **Doubling Capacity:** Requires squaring the SNR (e.g., 10 dB → 40 dB to double capacity once).
- ✅ **Four Noise Types:** Thermal, Intermodulation, Crosstalk, Impulse.
- ✅ **Impulse Noise is the dominant digital error source.**
- ✅ **Shannon Limit is a bound, not an achievable rate** — practical systems use codes (LDPC, Turbo) to approach it.
- ✅ **Common KTU Trap:** Always convert dB → linear before substituting into $\log_2$.
- ✅ **Common KTU Trap #2:** Logarithm must be base 2 for results in **bits**; use base $e$ only if the answer is in **nats**.
- ✅ **Noise Figure $F$:** $F = \text{SNR}_{\text{in}} / \text{SNR}_{\text{out}} \geq 1$ (ideal = 1).
- ✅ **Energy per bit to noise ratio:** $E_b/N_0 = \text{SNR} \cdot (B/R)$ — crucial in digital link budget analysis.

---

**End of KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Notes — Module 1: Noisy Channel**

topics.title: Shannon's Capacity Formula
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
