> - **Topic:** Shannon's Capacity Formula

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

**Shannon's Channel Capacity Theorem (Hartley's Law – Shannon Form)**, published by Claude E. Shannon in his landmark 1948 paper *"A Mathematical Theory of Communication,"* defines the theoretical **maximum rate at which information can be transmitted over a communications channel of a specified bandwidth in the presence of noise, with an arbitrarily small error probability**.

Mathematically, for a **band-limited, continuous, Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)** channel, the capacity is given by:

$$C = B \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{bits/second (bps)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> - **$C$** → Channel Capacity (bits per second)
> - **$B$** → Channel Bandwidth (Hertz)
> - **$S/N$** → Signal-to-Noise Ratio (dimensionless, linear scale)
> - **$S$** → Average Signal Power (Watts)
> - **$N$** → Average Noise Power (Watts), where $N = N_0 \cdot B$ and $N_0$ is the noise power spectral density

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Water-Pipe Analogy 🚰**
>
> Imagine a noisy water pipe carrying water (your data) from a reservoir to your home. Two physical factors limit how much useful water you can collect at the tap:
>
> 1. **The Diameter of the Pipe (Bandwidth $B$)** → A wider pipe allows more water (bits) to flow per second.
> 2. **The Quality of the Water (Signal-to-Noise Ratio $S/N$)** → If the water is muddy (noisy), the more water you push, the muddier it gets and the harder it is to extract the clean signal. A stronger water jet (higher $S$) relative to the mud (noise $N$) gives you cleaner flow.
>
> Shannon's formula tells you the **maximum clean water flow rate** possible, no matter how clever your filtration technique is. You cannot exceed it — it's a hard physical limit set by the pipe's diameter and the muddiness of the water.

## Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Boltzmann's Constant:** $k = 1.38064852 \times 10^{-23}$ Joules/Kelvin
- **Standard Noise Formula:** $N = kTB$, where $T$ is absolute temperature (Kelvin)
- **Thermal Noise Floor:** Approximately $-174$ dBm/Hz at room temperature ($T = 290$ K)
- **Logarithmic SNR Form:** $\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ (note the **factor of 10**, not 20, because SNR is a power ratio)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Logic – Why and How It Works

Shannon's theorem rests on **three revolutionary insights**:

1. **The "Noisy Channel Coding Theorem"** – For any transmission rate $R < C$, there **exists a coding scheme** that makes the error probability arbitrarily small as block length increases.
2. **The "Negative Result"** – For any rate $R > C$, **no coding scheme** can achieve reliable (error-free) communication. The error probability is bounded away from zero.
3. **Separation Principle** – Source coding (compression) and channel coding (error protection) can be designed **independently** without performance loss.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Channel Operation

- **Step 1 – Bandwidth Limitation:** A physical channel passes frequencies only within $B$ Hz. Higher $B$ means more "room" to pack distinguishable signal levels per second.
- **Step 2 – Noise Injection:** Thermal noise (Johnson-Nyquist noise) is unavoidably added to the signal during transmission through the medium.
- **Step 3 – Distinguishability of Symbols:** Because of noise, only a finite number of voltage/phase levels can be reliably distinguished. The number of distinguishable levels per symbol is $\sqrt{1 + S/N}$ (Nyquist's contribution).
- **Step 4 – Information Rate Calculation:** Multiplying the symbol rate ($2B$ samples/sec per Nyquist) by $\log_2$ of distinguishable levels gives Shannon's capacity.

## Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Satellite Communications** (e.g., DTH TV, GPS) — dictates uplink/downlink data budgets.
- **5G NR & 4G LTE Cellular** — Modulation and Coding Scheme (MCS) tables are built around capacity bounds.
- **Fiber-Optic Backbone Networks** — Limits wavelength-division multiplexing (WDM) channel count.
- **Deep-Space Communication (NASA DSN)** — Extremely low SNR makes Shannon's limit the design driver (e.g., Voyager, New Horizons).
- **Wi-Fi (802.11ax/802.11be)** — Adaptive modulation respects capacity ceilings per subcarrier.

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | **Shannon Capacity** (base-2 log) | bits/second |
| 2 | $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ | Nyquist Noiseless Limit ($M$ = signal levels) | bits/second |
| 3 | $N = kTB$ | Thermal Noise Power | Watts |
| 4 | $N_0 = kT$ | Noise Power Spectral Density | Watts/Hz |
| 5 | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ | SNR in decibels | dB |
| 6 | $S/N = 10^{(\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}}/10)}$ | Linear SNR from dB | dimensionless |
| 7 | $C = B \log_2(1 + S/(N_0 B))$ | Capacity in terms of PSD | bits/second |
| 8 | $C_{\text{norm}} = \log_2(1 + S/N)$ | Spectral Efficiency (capacity per Hz) | bits/s/Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid:** In Shannon's formula, the log is **base 2** (information in bits). In Nyquist's formula, also base 2. The factor of 2 inside the log is **NOT** present in Nyquist's noiseless case.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1 — From Nyquist's Noiseless Limit to Shannon's Noisy Limit

### Starting Point (Nyquist, 1928)
A noiseless channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz can support a maximum symbol rate of $2B$ symbols per second. If each symbol carries one of $M$ discrete levels:

$$
R_{\text{max}} = 2B \log_2(M) \quad \text{(bits/sec)}
$$

### Incorporating Noise (Shannon, 1948)
With noise present, the receiver cannot perfectly distinguish all $M$ levels. Shannon derived that the **effective number of distinguishable levels** reduces to:

$$
M_{\text{eff}} = \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{S}{N}}
$$

### Substituting Back

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 2B \cdot \log_2(M_{\text{eff}}) \\[4pt]
  &= 2B \cdot \log_2\!\left(\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{S}{N}}\right) \\[4pt]
  &= 2B \cdot \frac{1}{2}\,\log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right) \quad &\text{(using } \log_a(\sqrt{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\log_a(x)\text{)} \\[4pt]
  &= B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right) \quad &\text{(Shannon's Capacity)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The factor of 2 in the numerator (from $2B$ samples) and the factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$ (from the square root) cancel exactly, producing the elegant single-$B$ form. This cancellation is the mathematical signature of Shannon's theorem.

---

## Derivation 2 — Capacity in Terms of $E_b/N_0$ (Energy per Bit)

Energy per bit is defined as $E_b = S/C$. Substituting $S = E_b C$ into the capacity formula:

$$
C = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{E_b C}{N_0 B}\right)
$$

Dividing both sides by $B$:

$$
\frac{C}{B} = \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{E_b}{N_0} \cdot \frac{C}{B}\right)
$$

Let $\eta = C/B$ (spectral efficiency in bits/s/Hz). Then:

$$
\eta = \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{E_b}{N_0}\,\eta\right)
$$

Converting to exponential form:

$$
2^{\eta} = 1 + \dfrac{E_b}{N_0}\,\eta
$$

Solving for the minimum $E_b/N_0$ (the **Shannon Limit**):

$$
\dfrac{E_b}{N_0} = \dfrac{2^{\eta} - 1}{\eta}
$$

As $\eta \to 0$ (bandwidth $\to \infty$, the wideband regime):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{\eta \to 0} \dfrac{E_b}{N_0} &= \lim_{\eta \to 0} \dfrac{2^{\eta} - 1}{\eta} \\[4pt]
&= \ln(2) \quad &\text{(by L'Hôpital's rule on } 2^{\eta}\ln 2 \text{)} \\[4pt]
&\approx 0.6931
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to decibels:

$$
\left(\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{\text{min}} = 10 \log_{10}(\ln 2) \approx -1.59 \text{ dB}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Shannon Limit of $-1.59$ dB** is the **absolute minimum** energy per bit needed to achieve reliable communication on an AWGN channel. No real system can operate below this floor. Modern turbo codes and LDPC codes approach within 0.5 dB of this limit — a celebrated engineering achievement.

---

## Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard Style)

**Problem:** A channel has $B = 4000$ Hz and SNR $= 30$ dB. Find the channel capacity.

**Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear scale:**

$$
S/N = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon's formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= B \log_2(1 + S/N) \\[4pt]
  &= 4000 \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000) \\[4pt]
  &= 4000 \cdot \log_2(1001) \\[4pt]
  &= 4000 \cdot \dfrac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} \\[4pt]
  &= 4000 \cdot \dfrac{3.0004}{0.3010} \\[4pt]
  &= 4000 \cdot 9.9666 \\[4pt]
  &\approx 39{,}866 \text{ bps} \approx 39.87 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** We use the change-of-base identity $\log_2(x) = \log_{10}(x)/\log_{10}(2)$ because calculators typically provide base-10 or natural logs, not base 2.

---

## Analytical Matrix — Effect of Parameter Variation

| If you double... | Capacity changes by... | Engineering Implication |
|------------------|------------------------|-------------------------|
| Bandwidth $B$ | $C$ doubles linearly | Wider spectrum → higher throughput (but spectrum is scarce) |
| Signal power $S$ | $C$ increases logarithmically | More power gives diminishing returns — "power inefficient" |
| Noise $N$ (decrease) | $C$ increases logarithmically | Better components & shielding yield small but real gains |
| SNR by 3 dB ($\times 2$) | $C$ increases by $\approx B \log_2(3) \approx 1.585B$ bps | Classic "3 dB rule" — doubling power is not enough to double rate |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1 — Shannon's Communication System Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Source] --> enc[Source Encoder]
    enc --> chenc[Channel Encoder]
    chenc --> mod[Modulator]
    mod --> chan((Noisy Channel BW B Hz))
    chan --> noise[Additive Noise N]
    noise --> demod[Demodulator]
    demod --> chdec[Channel Decoder]
    chdec --> srcdc[Source Decoder]
    srcdc --> dest[Destination]
    
    style src fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style dest fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style chan fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style noise fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style chenc fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style chdec fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** Red blocks represent the channel and its unavoidable noise. Green blocks represent the **coding layer** — the only place where engineers can add redundancy to combat the noise and approach Shannon's capacity bound.

---

## Diagram 2 — Capacity vs SNR Trade-off (Shannon Bound Curve)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Channel with Bandwidth B Hz] --> B{SNR Regime}
    
    B -->|Low SNR: S/N less than 1| C[Capacity approx B times S/N divided by ln 2]
    B -->|Medium SNR: 1 to 100| D[Capacity B log2 1 + S/N]
    B -->|High SNR: S/N greater than 1000| E[Capacity approx B log2 S/N]
    
    C --> F[Power Limited Regime]
    D --> G[Balanced Design]
    E --> H[Bandwidth Limited Regime]
    
    F --> F1[Cellular uplink, deep space]
    G --> G1[WiFi, cable modems]
    H --> H1[Fiber optics, mmWave]
    
    style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#bf9000,color:#ffffff
    style F1 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style G1 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style H1 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The Shannon curve is **logarithmic in SNR** but **linear in bandwidth**. This is why bandwidth is the most precious resource in modern communication systems.

---

## Diagram 3 — Shannon's Three Foundational Insights

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Shannon 1948 Theorem] --> P1[Positive Result]
    S --> P2[Negative Result]
    S --> P3[Separation Principle]
    
    P1 --> P1a[If R less than C, codes exist with error probability approaching 0]
    P2 --> P2a[If R greater than C, error probability is bounded away from 0]
    P3 --> P3a[Source coding and channel coding are independent]
    
    style S fill:#1f4e79,color:#ffffff
    style P1 fill:#2e7d32,color:#ffffff
    style P2 fill:#c00000,color:#ffffff
    style P3 fill:#bf9000,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading the Diagram:** The two key results (green and red) define the achievable region; the separation principle (yellow) is what allows practical system design with modular source and channel codecs.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State Shannon's Channel Capacity Theorem. Define each term in the formula $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Shannon's Channel Capacity Theorem states that the theoretical maximum rate at which information can be transmitted over a band-limited communication channel in the presence of additive white Gaussian noise, with an arbitrarily small probability of error, is given by:

$$
C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)
$$

**Definitions (1 mark each):**
- **$C$** — Channel capacity, the upper bound on the achievable data rate (bits per second).
- **$B$** — Channel bandwidth, the range of frequencies the channel can pass (Hertz).
- **$S/N$** — Signal-to-Noise ratio, the ratio of average signal power to average noise power (dimensionless).

> **[Valuation Tip]:** Examiners allocate 1 mark for the statement and 1 mark each for defining any two terms. Always write units explicitly.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the Shannon Limit? What is its value in dB, and what does it represent?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Shannon Limit** is the **minimum value of $E_b/N_0$** (energy per bit to noise power spectral density ratio) required to achieve error-free communication on an AWGN channel. It is the asymptotic limit as spectral efficiency $\eta \to 0$.

**Value:**

$$
\left(\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}\right)_{\text{min}} = \ln(2) \approx 0.6931 \;\; \text{(linear)} \;\; \approx -1.59 \text{ dB}
$$

**Significance:** No practical coding/modulation scheme can communicate reliably below this $E_b/N_0$ threshold, regardless of how complex the system is.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive Shannon's Channel Capacity formula starting from Nyquist's noiseless limit. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A telephone channel has bandwidth $B = 3.4$ kHz and SNR of $40$ dB. Compute (i) the channel capacity, and (ii) the minimum $E_b/N_0$ required at this operating point. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a):

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. The channel is **band-limited** to $B$ Hz.
2. The noise is **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)** with power $N$.
3. The channel is **memoryless** and **time-invariant**.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

According to Nyquist, the maximum symbol (sample) rate over a noiseless channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz is $2B$ symbols per second. With $M$ discrete signal levels, the maximum bit rate is:

$$
R = 2B \log_2(M) \quad \text{bits/sec}
$$

In the presence of AWGN, the **effective** number of distinguishable levels is reduced (we cannot resolve all $M$ levels because noise smears them). Shannon showed that the number of reliably distinguishable signal levels is:

$$
M_{\text{eff}} = \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{S}{N}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 2B \log_2\!\left(\sqrt{1 + S/N}\right) \\[4pt]
  &= 2B \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\log_2(1 + S/N) \\[4pt]
  &= B \log_2(1 + S/N) \quad \text{bits/sec}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **Shannon's Channel Capacity Formula**. ∎

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Part (i) — Capacity (4 marks):**

Convert SNR to linear scale:

$$
S/N = 10^{40/10} = 10^4 = 10{,}000
$$

Apply Shannon's formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 3.4 \times 10^3 \cdot \log_2(1 + 10{,}000) \\[4pt]
  &= 3400 \cdot \log_2(10{,}001) \\[4pt]
  &= 3400 \cdot \dfrac{\log_{10}(10{,}001)}{\log_{10}(2)} \\[4pt]
  &= 3400 \cdot \dfrac{4.000043}{0.30103} \\[4pt]
  &= 3400 \cdot 13.2877 \\[4pt]
  &\approx 45{,}178 \text{ bps} \approx 45.18 \text{ kbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii) — Minimum $E_b/N_0$ (3 marks):**

Using $E_b = S/C$:

$$
\dfrac{E_b}{N_0} = \dfrac{S}{N_0 \cdot C} = \dfrac{(S/N)}{C/B}
$$

Here $S/N = 10{,}000$ and $C/B = 45{,}178/3400 = 13.2877$ bits/s/Hz:

$$
\dfrac{E_b}{N_0} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{13.2877} \approx 752.6 \;\; \text{(linear)}
$$

In dB:

$$
10 \log_{10}(752.6) \approx 28.77 \text{ dB}
$$

This is well above the Shannon Limit of $-1.59$ dB, so reliable communication is possible. ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat diagram, the Shannon-Hartley communication system model. What are the limitations of Shannon's theorem? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A satellite transponder has $B = 36$ MHz bandwidth and the received SNR is $15$ dB. Calculate: (i) the theoretical bit rate, (ii) the spectral efficiency, and (iii) the percentage increase in capacity if SNR is doubled. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Shannon-Hartley Model (4 marks):**

The model consists of an information source, source encoder, channel encoder, modulator, the noisy channel, and the reciprocal blocks at the receiver (demodulator, channel decoder, source decoder, destination). [See Diagram 1 in Section 4.]

The information source produces data at rate $R$ bps. The source encoder removes redundancy. The channel encoder adds controlled redundancy for error protection. The modulator maps bits to symbols for transmission through the band-limited channel. Noise $N$ corrupts the signal. The receiver reverses the process. Shannon's theorem bounds $R \le C$.

**Limitations (3 marks):**
1. The theorem is **existence-based** — it proves codes exist but does not construct them.
2. It assumes **infinite coding block length** for arbitrarily low error — impractical latency.
3. The **AWGN model** may not match all real channels (fading, impulsive noise, inter-symbol interference).
4. It does not account for **implementation complexity** or **system cost**.
5. The bound is **asymptotic** — finite-length codes exhibit a "gap to capacity."

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Part (i) — Bit Rate (3 marks):**

Convert SNR to linear:

$$
S/N = 10^{15/10} = 10^{1.5} \approx 31.623
$$

Apply Shannon's formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 36 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(1 + 31.623) \\[4pt]
  &= 36 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(32.623) \\[4pt]
  &= 36 \times 10^6 \cdot \dfrac{\log_{10}(32.623)}{\log_{10}(2)} \\[4pt]
  &= 36 \times 10^6 \cdot \dfrac{1.5134}{0.3010} \\[4pt]
  &= 36 \times 10^6 \cdot 5.0276 \\[4pt]
  &\approx 181 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (ii) — Spectral Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
\eta = C/B = 5.0276 \text{ bits/s/Hz}
$$

**Part (iii) — Capacity change on doubling SNR (2 marks):**

New SNR $= 2 \times 31.623 = 63.246$ (linear) $\approx 18$ dB.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{\text{new}} &= 36 \times 10^6 \cdot \log_2(64.246) \\[4pt]
              &= 36 \times 10^6 \cdot \dfrac{\log_{10}(64.246)}{\log_{10}(2)} \\[4pt]
              &= 36 \times 10^6 \cdot 6.0054 \\[4pt]
              &\approx 216.2 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

Percentage increase:

$$
\dfrac{216.2 - 181}{181} \times 100\% \approx 19.45\%
$$

> **Insight for the student:** Doubling the SNR (a 3 dB power increase) only increases capacity by about 19.45% — confirming the **logarithmic relationship** between power and capacity.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 1. **Base Confusion** — Students often use $\log_{10}$ instead of $\log_2$ in Shannon's formula. Loss: **1–2 marks per instance**.
> 2. **dB Conversion Error** — Forgetting that SNR (a **power** ratio) uses **10** $\log_{10}$ and not **20** $\log_{10}$. The latter applies to voltage/current ratios only.
> 3. **Mixing Nyquist & Shannon** — Nyquist's $C = 2B \log_2 M$ has $M$ (number of levels) and **no noise term**. Shannon's has $S/N$ and **no explicit $M$**. Confusing them costs 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Skipping Units** — Always state **bits/sec**, **Hz**, **dB**, or **Watts** explicitly.
> 5. **Forgetting the "1 +"** — Writing $\log_2(S/N)$ instead of $\log_2(1 + S/N)$ is a classic KTU trap and loses 1 mark.
> 6. **Missing Assumptions** — In derivations, failing to state "AWGN," "band-limited," and "memoryless" assumptions costs 1–2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Shannon's Capacity: $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — the **fundamental limit** of reliable communication.
- ✅ Capacity is **linear in bandwidth** but **logarithmic in SNR** — bandwidth is more valuable than power.
- ✅ The **Shannon Limit** of $E_b/N_0 = -1.59$ dB is the **absolute minimum** energy per bit for reliable AWGN communication.
- ✅ Thermal noise: $N = kTB$ with $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- ✅ SNR in dB: $10 \log_{10}(S/N)$ for power ratios.
- ✅ Nyquist's noiseless formula: $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ — special case of Shannon as $S/N \to \infty$.
- ✅ Coding schemes (Turbo, LDPC, Polar codes) approach but **never exceed** Shannon's bound.
- ✅ Three Shannon insights: (1) existence of good codes below $C$, (2) impossibility above $C$, (3) separation of source/channel coding.
- ✅ Spectral efficiency $\eta = C/B = \log_2(1 + S/N)$ bits/s/Hz is the normalized capacity metric.
- ✅ Doubling SNR gives only $\sim 1.585B$ bps extra (i.e., $\log_2 3 \cdot B$), not a doubling of rate.
- ✅ KTU hot question: Derive Shannon from Nyquist, or compute capacity given $B$ and SNR in dB — **practice both numerical and derivation types**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Guided Transmission Media - Twisted Pair
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Guided Transmission Media - Twisted Pair

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **OECST612 – Data Communication**, *Guided Transmission Media* refers to physical communication channels in which electromagnetic signals (electrical, light, or RF) are directed and confined along a solid medium from a transmitter to a receiver. Among these, the **Twisted Pair** is defined as the most fundamental and widely deployed guided medium, consisting of **two separate insulated copper conductors** helically twisted around each other at a controlled number of twists per metre, used to transmit balanced differential electrical signals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Twisted Pair is the *default low-cost, short-range* guided medium used in the **Physical Layer (OSI Layer 1)** for LANs, telephone subscriber loops, and DSL broadband access. Examiners frequently target definitions of UTP, STP, and the EIA/TIA 568 categorisation.

Mathematically, a twisted pair is modelled as a **two-wire transmission line** with distributed parameters: per-unit-length resistance $R$ ($\Omega$/m), inductance $L$ (H/m), capacitance $C$ (F/m), and conductance $G$ (S/m). These parameters determine the line's **characteristic impedance** $Z_0$ and signal behaviour.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine two garden hoses lying next to each other, with a slow wave twisting them together like a DNA double helix. Now imagine that one hose carries water *forward* and the other carries water *backward* in equal amounts. Any external disturbance (a person stepping on both, wind blowing across them) affects **both hoses equally**. Because the receiver only looks at the *difference* between the two hoses, the disturbance cancels itself out.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Difference Receiver" Intuition:** A twisted pair transmits **differential signals** — the receiver measures the voltage difference $(V_+ - V_-)$, not the absolute voltage. External noise impinges on both conductors nearly equally (a phenomenon called **common-mode noise**), so the difference cancels the noise. The twisting simply enforces this symmetry geometrically.

This is precisely why twisting works: it geometrically enforces that the *loop area* between the two conductors remains constant with respect to any external interference source, so the induced noise voltage is identical on both wires and gets rejected.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Conductor material** | Copper (annealed) | Standard of comparison; silver has lower resistivity |
| **Typical wire gauge** | **22 AWG to 26 AWG** | American Wire Gauge standard |
| **Characteristic Impedance $Z_0$** | **100 $\Omega$** (Ethernet) or **120 $\Omega$** (Telecom) | Frequency dependent |
| **Twist rate (typical)** | 2 to 12 twists per foot | Higher in higher categories |
| **Operating temperature** | $-20^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+60^{\circ}\text{C}$ | PVC or LSZH jacketed |
| **Attenuation unit** | **dB per 100 m** | Standard for cable specs |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Why Twist? — The Physics of Crosstalk Reduction

When two parallel wires run side-by-side, the magnetic field from one wire **induces** a voltage on the other. This is **crosstalk (XT)**. Twisting forces the relative polarity of the two wires to **swap every half-twist**. So in one segment, wire A is "near" the noise source, and in the next segment, wire B is "near" it. The induced EMFs in adjacent segments have **opposite polarity** and thus partially cancel.

**Structured Logic Steps:**
1. Parallel untwisted pair → constant inductive and capacitive coupling to neighbour → strong NEXT (Near-End Crosstalk).
2. Twisted pair → coupling polarity alternates every half-twist.
3. Induced voltages in adjacent half-segments are of opposite sign → summation tends to zero.
4. Higher *twist rate* (twists/metre) → better cancellation → higher usable bandwidth.
5. To prevent *pair-to-pair* coupling, different pairs in a multi-pair cable use **different twist rates (lays)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why different twist rates per pair?** In a 4-pair UTP cable (Cat 5e/6), each pair has a unique twist rate so the crosstalk between pairs does not accumulate at the same point along the cable. This is called **alien crosstalk suppression**.

#### 2.1.2 UTP vs STP — Structural Differences

- **UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair):** Only the plastic insulation jacket and pair-twisting. Cheap, flexible, but more vulnerable to EMI.
- **STP (Shielded Twisted Pair):** An additional **metallic foil or braid shield** surrounds the twisted pairs (individually or collectively). The shield is grounded to drain induced currents away.
- **FTP (Foiled Twisted Pair):** Foil shield around the entire bundle only — a middle-ground.

#### 2.1.3 EIA/TIA 568 Categorisation

| Category | Max Bandwidth | Typical Use | KTU Exam Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cat 1 | < 1 MHz | Telephone (voice only) | Low |
| Cat 3 | 16 MHz | 10BASE-T Ethernet | Medium |
| Cat 5 | 100 MHz | 100BASE-TX | High |
| Cat 5e | 100 MHz | 1000BASE-T (Gigabit) | **Very High** |
| Cat 6 | 250 MHz | 10GBASE-T (limited) | High |
| Cat 6a | 500 MHz | 10GBASE-T (full) | Medium |
| Cat 7 / 7a | 600–1000 MHz | Data centres (S/FTP) | Low |
| Cat 8 | 2000 MHz | 25G/40GBASE-T in DC | Low |

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\alpha_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{out}}\right)$ | Attenuation in decibels | dB |
| $\alpha_{dB/km} = \dfrac{\text{Total Attenuation (dB)}}{\text{Length (km)}}$ | Specific attenuation | dB/km |
| $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}$ | Characteristic impedance of a lossy line | $\Omega$ |
| $Z_0 \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | Characteristic impedance (lossless approximation, $R \to 0$, $G \to 0$) | $\Omega$ |
| $v_p = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$ | Phase velocity (signal propagation speed) | m/s |
| $C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2B \log_2 M$ | Maximum symbol rate (Nyquist, noiseless) | bits/s |
| $C_{\text{Shannon}} = B \log_2\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Channel capacity (noisy, bits/s) | bits/s |
| $v_{\text{prop}} = 0.6c \text{ to } 0.77c$ | Practical signal speed in UTP | m/s |
| $\text{Delay} = \dfrac{\ell}{v_p}$ | Propagation delay across length $\ell$ | seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Rule:** For twisted pair, the *velocity of propagation* is between **0.6c and 0.77c** ($c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s). Examiners often test this constant in Part A short-answer questions.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunication subscriber loops:** Twisted pair from the exchange to homes — the classic "last mile" for **POTS** and **DSL broadband (ADSL, VDSL)**.
- **Ethernet LANs (10/100/1000BASE-T):** The dominant in-building LAN medium.
- **Industrial RS-485 / Profibus:** Twisted pair with differential signalling for noisy factory environments.
- **Audio (XLR, TRS):** Balanced audio over twisted pair rejects 50/60 Hz mains hum.
- **CAN bus in automobiles:** Twisted pair differential signalling for engine ECU networks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Attenuation in dB from Power Ratio

The decibel (dB) is a logarithmic measure of a ratio. For a transmission line of length $\ell$, if the input power is $P_{in}$ and the output power (after travelling the length) is $P_{out}$, the attenuation in dB is derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Definition of the bel:**
One bel represents a 10:1 power ratio, so an attenuation of $N$ bels corresponds to:

$$P_{in} = 10^{N} \cdot P_{out}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for N (attenuation in bels):**

$$N = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right) \text{ bels}$$

**Step 3 — Convert bels to decibels (1 bel = 10 dB):**

$$\alpha_{dB} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of voltage (since $P = V^2 / R$ and $R$ is constant at both ends):**

$$\alpha_{dB} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{in}^2 / R}{V_{out}^2 / R}\right) = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{in}^2}{V_{out}^2}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Apply the log identity $\log(x^2) = 2\log(x)$:**

$$\alpha_{dB} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_{out}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A **$-3$ dB** attenuation means the output power is **half** the input power. A **$-20$ dB** attenuation means the output is **1/100th** the input power. For twisted pair cables, attenuation is always expressed as a *negative* value when a signal is *weakened*.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Characteristic Impedance (Lossless Case)

The characteristic impedance $Z_0$ of a two-conductor transmission line is defined as the ratio of voltage to current for a **forward-propagating wave** that does not depend on reflections.

**Step 1 — Start with the Telegrapher's equations (per-unit-length model):**

$$\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} = -(R + j\omega L)\, I$$

$$\frac{\partial I}{\partial x} = -(G + j\omega C)\, V$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate the first equation with respect to $x$ and substitute the second:**

$$\frac{\partial^2 V}{\partial x^2} = -(R + j\omega L) \frac{\partial I}{\partial x} = (R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)\, V$$

**Step 3 — The propagation constant is defined as:**

$$\gamma = \sqrt{(R + j\omega L)(G + j\omega C)} = \alpha + j\beta$$

**Step 4 — The general forward wave solution for voltage is $V(x) = V_0^+ e^{-\gamma x}$, and current is $I(x) = I_0^+ e^{-\gamma x}$. Substituting into the first telegrapher's equation gives:**

$$V_0^+ = (R + j\omega L) \frac{I_0^+}{\gamma}$$

**Step 5 — Hence the ratio:**

$$Z_0 = \frac{V_0^+}{I_0^+} = \frac{R + j\omega L}{\gamma} = \sqrt{\frac{R + j\omega L}{G + j\omega C}}$$

**Step 6 — For a lossless line (high frequency, $R \to 0$, $G \to 0$):**

$$Z_0 \approx \sqrt{\frac{j\omega L}{j\omega C}} = \sqrt{\frac{L}{C}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** $Z_0$ is **not** the DC resistance. It is the impedance of an *infinitely long* line, or the impedance you must terminate the line with to *prevent reflections* (matching condition). For Ethernet, you terminate UTP with **100 $\Omega$** resistors at each end.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Data Rate on a Twisted Pair (Nyquist)

A twisted pair is a **band-limited channel** with bandwidth $B$ Hz. By Nyquist's noiseless criterion, the maximum symbol (baud) rate is $2B$. If each symbol carries $\log_2 M$ bits, the bit rate is:

$$C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2B \log_2 M \text{ bits/s}$$

**Step 1 — For Cat 5e UTP, $B = 100$ MHz.**
**Step 2 — For 1000BASE-T (Gigabit Ethernet over Cat 5e), 4 pairs are used with 4-level PAM (PAM-5 effectively), $M = 4$ (2 bits per symbol).**

**Step 3 — Per pair, gross symbol rate needed = 125 Mbaud, which is achievable on 100 MHz bandwidth (2B = 200 Mbaud capacity).**

**Step 4 — Per pair bit rate = $2 \times 100 \times 10^6 \times \log_2 4 = 400$ Mbps.**
**Step 5 — Across 4 pairs (full-duplex on each):** $4 \times 400 = 1600$ Mbps raw, with 1000 Mbps after coding overhead.

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Twisted pair reached its Shannon limit around Cat 5e. Going beyond 10 Gbps on UTP (Cat 6a) is the practical ceiling, beyond which **fibre optics** becomes mandatory.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Attenuation Calculation

> **Problem:** A 200 m run of Cat 6 UTP cable has a measured signal level of $V_{in} = 5$ V at the transmitter and $V_{out} = 3.2$ V at the receiver end. Calculate: (a) the total attenuation in dB, and (b) the attenuation per kilometre in dB/km.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Using the voltage form of the dB formula:

$$\alpha_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{in}}{V_{out}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{5}{3.2}\right)$$

$$= 20 \log_{10}(1.5625) = 20 \times 0.1942 = -3.884 \text{ dB}$$

**Part (b):** Specific attenuation per km:

$$\alpha_{dB/km} = \frac{-3.884 \text{ dB}}{0.2 \text{ km}} = -19.42 \text{ dB/km}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering check:** Cat 6 at 100 MHz is rated at roughly **−19.3 dB/100 m** = **−193 dB/km** (worst case). Our calculation of −19.42 dB/km corresponds to a very short length where connector loss dominates. For longer runs, attenuation grows linearly with length (in dB).

---

### 3.5 Worked Example: Maximum Bit Rate (Shannon + Nyquist combined)

> **Problem:** A Cat 5e UTP link has bandwidth $B = 100$ MHz and an SNR of 40 dB at the receiver. Find: (a) the Shannon capacity, and (b) the number of signal levels $M$ required to achieve this capacity using Nyquist signalling.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Convert SNR from dB to linear:

$$\text{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = 10^{40/10} = 10^4 = 10000$$

$$C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) = 100 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(1 + 10000)$$

$$= 100 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(10001) = 100 \times 10^6 \times 13.288 = 1.329 \text{ Gbps}$$

**Part (b):** Set $C_{\text{Nyquist}} = C_{\text{Shannon}}$:

$$2B \log_2 M = 1.329 \times 10^9$$

$$\log_2 M = \frac{1.329 \times 10^9}{2 \times 100 \times 10^6} = 6.643$$

$$M = 2^{6.643} = 99.87 \approx 100 \text{ levels}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Realistic limit:** In practice, no UTP system uses 100 PAM levels. Higher noise and crosstalk reduce the practical SNR, capping real throughput well below the Shannon limit. This is why 10GBASE-T uses sophisticated DSP and 4-pair bonded signalling.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Twisted Pair — Physical Construction and Signal Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph srcA[TRANSMITTER END]
        TX[Source Signal V_in]
        DIFFA[Differential Driver]
        T1[Wire + Conductor]
        T2[Wire - Conductor]
    end

    subgraph srcB[PHYSICAL CABLE - TWISTED PAIR]
        direction TB
        T1A[Twist Segment 1]
        T1B[Twist Segment 2]
        T1C[Twist Segment 3]
        T1D[Twist Segment 4]
    end

    subgraph srcC[RECEIVER END]
        R1[Wire + Conductor]
        R2[Wire - Conductor]
        DIFFB[Differential Receiver]
        OUT[V_out = V_plus minus V_minus]
    end

    TX --> DIFFA
    DIFFA -->|V+| T1
    DIFFA -->|V-| T2
    T1 -.helical twist.-> R1
    T2 -.helical twist.-> R1
    T1 -.helical twist.-> R2
    T2 -.helical twist.-> R2
    R1 --> DIFFB
    R2 --> DIFFB
    DIFFB --> OUT
```

### 4.2 UTP vs STP — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph utpA[UTP STRUCTURE]
        UTPCORE1[Insulated Copper Wire +]
        UTPCORE2[Insulated Copper Wire -]
        UTPJACK[Outer PVC Jacket]
    end

    subgraph stpA[STP STRUCTURE]
        STPCORE1[Insulated Copper Wire +]
        STPCORE2[Insulated Copper Wire -]
        STPSHIELD1[Inner Foil Shield per pair]
        STPBRAID[Overall Braid Shield]
        STPJK[Outer Jacket]
    end

    NOISE1[External EMI RFI] -.induces.-> UTPJACK
    NOISE2[External EMI RFI] -.blocked by shield.-> STPBRAID

    utpA --> NOISE1
    stpA --> NOISE2
```

### 4.3 Crosstalk Cancellation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PARALLEL[Two Parallel Wires] --> STRONG[Strong Inductive Coupling and Crosstalk]
    TWISTED[Twisted Wires Alternating Polarity] --> HALF1[Segment 1 Noise on Wire A is positive]
    TWISTED --> HALF2[Segment 2 Noise on Wire A is negative]
    HALF1 --> SUM[Algebraic Summation]
    HALF2 --> SUM
    SUM --> CANCEL[Net Induced Voltage Approximates Zero]
    STRONG --> XTALK[High Near End Crosstalk NEXT]
```

### 4.4 Bandwidth vs Category Trade-off

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CAT3[Cat 3 16 MHz] --> VOICE[10BASE-T Voice Telephony]
    CAT5[Cat 5 100 MHz] --> FE[100BASE-TX Fast Ethernet]
    CAT5E[Cat 5e 100 MHz] --> GE[1000BASE-T Gigabit Ethernet]
    CAT6[Cat 6 250 MHz] --> TENG[10GBASE-T up to 55 m]
    CAT6A[Cat 6a 500 MHz] --> TENGFULL[10GBASE-T up to 100 m]
    CAT8[Cat 8 2000 MHz] --> DC[25G 40G Data Centre]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

Define the term **Twisted Pair Cable** as used in data communication. Mention any **two advantages** of using twisted pair over an untwisted pair of wires.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A twisted pair cable is a guided transmission medium consisting of **two insulated copper conductors** wound helically around each other. The twisting minimises electromagnetic interference (EMI) and crosstalk between adjacent pairs, allowing reliable transmission of differential electrical signals.

**Advantages over untwisted pair (1.5 marks, any two):**
1. **Reduced crosstalk:** The periodic polarity reversal cancels inductive coupling between adjacent pairs.
2. **Lower external noise pickup:** Differential signalling and equal area exposure to external fields make the cable highly immune to common-mode noise.
3. **Higher bandwidth and longer distances:** Twisting enables reliable transmission at MHz–GHz frequencies (e.g., 100 MHz for Cat 5e over 100 m).
4. **Lower cost and easier installation** compared to coaxial or fibre, making it the most widely deployed LAN medium.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

Distinguish between **UTP** and **STP** cables. State **one application** of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair) | STP (Shielded Twisted Pair) |
|---|---|---|
| Shielding | None — only insulation and jacket | Foil/braid metallic shield around pairs |
| EMI immunity | Moderate | High |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Flexibility / weight | Lighter, more flexible | Stiffer, heavier |
| Typical use | Office LANs, telephone lines | Industrial environments, data centres with high EMI |

**Applications (1 mark):**
- **UTP:** 1000BASE-T Gigabit Ethernet in office LANs; POTS subscriber loops.
- **STP:** Industrial Ethernet (Profibus, EtherCAT), data centre inter-rack cabling, environments with motors/VFDs.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q-A. (a)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, modified]

Explain the construction and working of a **twisted pair cable** with neat block diagram. Why are the conductors twisted? Discuss the role of **differential signalling** in noise rejection.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (2 marks):**
- Two insulated copper conductors, each of 22–26 AWG.
- Twisted helically with a controlled number of twists per metre (lay length).
- Bundled inside an outer PVC or LSZH jacket. In multi-pair cables, each pair has a *different* twist rate to suppress pair-to-pair crosstalk.
- Optional metallic shield (STP) — foil wrap, braid, or both.

**Working (2 marks):**
- The transmitter applies equal-and-opposite voltages on the two wires: $V_+$ on one, $V_-$ on the other such that $V_+ = -V_-$ (differential mode).
- The receiver is a **differential amplifier** that computes $V_{\text{out}} = V_+ - V_- = 2V_{\text{signal}}$.
- Any external noise that affects both wires equally (common-mode noise) yields $V_{\text{noise,+}} = V_{\text{noise,-}}$, which **cancels** in the subtraction: $(V_+ + V_{n}) - (V_- + V_{n}) = V_+ - V_-$.

**Why twist? (2 marks):**
- Twisting makes each wire symmetrically exposed to the *same* external field along the cable. The induced EMF integrates to nearly equal values on both conductors.
- The differential receiver rejects this common-mode induced voltage.
- Twisting also minimises **crosstalk** between adjacent pairs because the magnetic coupling from one pair reverses polarity every half-twist, causing mutual cancellation.

**Differential signalling role (1 mark):**
- Provides common-mode noise rejection (CMRR), enabling high SNR even in electrically noisy environments.
- Halves the effective signal swing needed (2 × single-ended) → better electromagnetic compatibility (EMC).

---

**Q-A. (b)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

A 500 m length of Cat 5e UTP cable has an attenuation of **22 dB at 100 MHz**. The transmitter output is 2 V (peak). Calculate: **(i)** the received voltage, **(ii)** the attenuation per km, and **(iii)** the propagation delay if the velocity of propagation is **0.65c**.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Received voltage (2 marks):**
Attenuation in dB: $\alpha_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{out}}\right)$

$$-22 = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2}{V_{out}}\right)$$

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2}{V_{out}}\right) = -1.1$$

$$\frac{2}{V_{out}} = 10^{-1.1} = 0.0794$$

$$V_{out} = \frac{2}{0.0794} = 25.18 \text{ mV}$$

**(ii) Attenuation per km (2 marks):**

$$\alpha_{dB/km} = \frac{22 \text{ dB}}{0.5 \text{ km}} = 44 \text{ dB/km}$$

**(iii) Propagation delay (3 marks):**
Velocity of propagation:

$$v_p = 0.65 \times c = 0.65 \times 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s} = 1.95 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$$

Delay:

$$t = \frac{\ell}{v_p} = \frac{500}{1.95 \times 10^{8}} = 2.564 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 2.56 \;\mu\text{s}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q-B. (a)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

With a block diagram, explain the **EIA/TIA 568 categorisation** of UTP cables. Compare **Cat 5e**, **Cat 6**, and **Cat 6a** in terms of bandwidth, maximum data rate, and typical application.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**EIA/TIA 568 Categorisation (3 marks):**
- The Telecommunications Industry Association (TIA) and Electronics Industries Alliance (EIA) standard **TIA-568** specifies performance categories for balanced twisted-pair cabling.
- Categories are graded by maximum supported frequency and supported Ethernet standards:
  - **Cat 3:** 16 MHz — voice and 10BASE-T.
  - **Cat 5 / Cat 5e:** 100 MHz — 100BASE-TX, 1000BASE-T.
  - **Cat 6:** 250 MHz — 1000BASE-T, 10GBASE-T (≤ 55 m).
  - **Cat 6a (Augmented):** 500 MHz — 10GBASE-T (full 100 m).
  - **Cat 7 / 7a / 8:** 600 MHz to 2 GHz — data centres, 25G/40GBASE-T.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Cat 5e | Cat 6 | Cat 6a |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bandwidth | 100 MHz | 250 MHz | 500 MHz |
| Max data rate (typical) | 1 Gbps | 1 Gbps (10 Gbps ≤ 55 m) | 10 Gbps (100 m) |
| Crosstalk spec | NEXT only | NEXT, PSNEXT | NEXT, PSNEXT, Alien XT |
| Typical use | Office LAN | Office LAN, server rooms | Data centres, backbone |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Higher categories use tighter twists, spline separators, and better insulation to push bandwidth higher and suppress both internal and alien crosstalk.

---

**Q-B. (b)** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

Explain the **sources of attenuation** in a twisted pair cable. A twisted pair link operating at 50 MHz has an SNR of 30 dB. Compute the **Shannon channel capacity** and the **maximum symbol rate** if 16-PAM ($M = 16$) signalling is used.

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Sources of attenuation (3 marks):**
1. **Conductor (copper) losses** — $I^2R$ heating in the wire resistance $R$, which rises with frequency due to *skin effect*.
2. **Dielectric losses** — energy absorbed in the insulating material, modelled by shunt conductance $G$, increasing with frequency.
3. **Radiation losses** — at very high frequencies, the pair behaves like a small antenna and radiates energy.
4. **Connector / splice losses** — impedance discontinuities at patch panels and RJ45 terminations.
5. **Skin effect** — at high frequencies, current flows only in a thin outer shell of the conductor, effectively increasing resistance: $R_{ac} \propto \sqrt{f}$.

**Shannon capacity (2 marks):**
SNR (linear): $10^{30/10} = 1000$

$$C = B \log_2(1 + \text{SNR}) = 50 \times 10^6 \times \log_2(1001)$$

$$= 50 \times 10^6 \times 9.967 = 498.36 \text{ Mbps}$$

**Maximum symbol rate with 16-PAM (2 marks):**
Each symbol carries $\log_2 16 = 4$ bits.

$$C_{\text{Nyquist}} = 2B \log_2 M \Rightarrow \text{Symbol rate} = 2B = 100 \text{ Msymbols/s}$$

Bit rate achievable = $100 \times 10^6 \times 4 = 400$ Mbps. This is **less than** the Shannon capacity of 498 Mbps, so the channel can support 16-PAM at the full 2B symbol rate with margin to spare.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up bels and decibels:** Always multiply log power ratio by **10**, log voltage ratio by **20**. Examiners deduct full marks if students write the wrong coefficient.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert SNR from dB to linear** in Shannon's formula. A common mistake: writing $C = B \log_2(1 + 30)$ instead of $C = B \log_2(1 + 1000)$.
> 3. **Confusing bandwidth with data rate.** For a 100 MHz cable, the *symbol rate* is 200 Mbaud (Nyquist), not 100 Mbps.
> 4. **In differential signalling questions, students often forget to mention *common-mode rejection ratio (CMRR)***. Always include the keyword.
> 5. **Cable categories:** Examiners penalise students who do not specify **Cat 5e** for 1000BASE-T — Cat 5 (non-e) is *not* gigabit-rated reliably.
> 6. **Attenuation signs:** Always report attenuation as a *negative* dB value. Writing positive values (e.g., "+22 dB") is a 0.5-mark deduction.
> 7. **Impedance mismatch:** Students frequently confuse $Z_0$ with the DC resistance. State clearly that $Z_0$ is the *characteristic impedance for matching* — termination must equal $Z_0$ to avoid standing waves.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Twisted Pair = Two insulated copper wires helically twisted**, the most common guided medium for LANs and telephone subscriber loops.
- **Twisting suppresses crosstalk and enables differential signalling** to reject common-mode noise.
- **UTP** = unshielded (cheap, flexible); **STP** = shielded with foil/braid (high noise immunity).
- **Categories matter:** Cat 5e (100 MHz, 1 Gbps), Cat 6 (250 MHz, 1–10 Gbps short), Cat 6a (500 MHz, 10 Gbps full 100 m), Cat 8 (2 GHz, 25/40 G data centres).
- **Characteristic impedance $Z_0 \approx 100\;\Omega$** for Ethernet UTP, **$120\;\Omega$** for telecom — must be matched at terminations to avoid reflections.
- **Velocity of propagation in UTP is 0.6c to 0.77c**, used in delay calculations.
- **Attenuation in dB** = $20 \log_{10}(V_{in}/V_{out})$ for voltages; $10 \log_{10}(P_{in}/P_{out})$ for power.
- **Skin effect, dielectric loss, radiation loss, connector loss** are the four main attenuation sources — all increase with frequency.
- **Maximum data rate (Nyquist, noiseless)** = $2B \log_2 M$ bits/s; **Shannon (noisy)** = $B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ bits/s — Shannon's is the absolute upper bound.
- **Applications:** Ethernet (10/100/1000/10GBASE-T), DSL broadband, telephone, audio (balanced XLR), automotive CAN bus, industrial RS-485.
- **Limit:** Beyond 10 Gbps and 100 m, twisted pair is outperformed by **fibre optic cable** — this transition is a frequent KTU question.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Coaxial Cable
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Coaxial Cable

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Coaxial Cable (Coax)**
> A coaxial cable is a type of **guided transmission medium** consisting of a central copper conductor surrounded by a tubular dielectric insulator, which is in turn encased by a cylindrical metallic outer conductor (shield) and an outer protective plastic jacket. The term *coaxial* refers to the fact that both the inner and outer conductors share the same geometric axis, allowing signals to propagate in a **TEM (Transverse ElectroMagnetic) mode** with excellent shielding against external electromagnetic interference (EMI).

In the KTU 2024 Data Communication syllabus (OECST612, Module 1 — *Data Communication & Transmission Media*), coaxial cable is classified under **Wired / Guided Transmission Media**, alongside twisted pair and optical fiber.

### 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a coaxial cable like a high-speed one-way water pipeline for electrons:**
> - The **central copper wire** is the *pipe* through which the signal "flows."
> - The **insulating dielectric** is the *non-conductive lining* that prevents leakage.
> - The **outer metallic shield** is the *protective outer metal casing* that blocks any outside noise (like a Faraday cage).
> - The **PVC jacket** is the *outer rubber coating* that protects the whole assembly from physical damage.
>
> Because the inner pipe and outer metal casing share the **same center line (co-axis)**, the signal travels cleanly down the middle, immune to most surrounding electrical noise.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s**
- Permeability of free space: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m**
- Permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m**
- Standard characteristic impedances: **50 Ω** (baseband / digital), **75 Ω** (broadband / analog / CATV)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Coaxial cable is the predecessor of twisted pair in modern Ethernet deployments and is still actively used in cable television (CATV), cable broadband (DOCSIS), and long-distance telephone trunks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Constructional Anatomy (Inside-Out)

A coaxial cable has **four concentric layers**, each with a distinct function:

1. **Inner Conductor (Core)** — A solid copper wire or stranded copper. Carries the actual data signal. Typical diameter: 0.4 – 2 mm.
2. **Dielectric Insulator** — Polyethylene (PE), foam PE, or Teflon (PTFE). Its relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$ directly determines propagation speed. Typical $\varepsilon_r \approx 1.5 \text{ to } 2.3$.
3. **Outer Conductor (Shield)** — Braided copper, foil, or both. Functions as both the return path for current and as a **Faraday shield** against EMI.
4. **Outer Jacket** — PVC or PE. Protects against moisture, abrasion, and chemical corrosion.

### 2.2 Two Main Categories

| **Property** | **Baseband Coax (50 Ω)** | **Broadband Coax (75 Ω)** |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Use | Digital baseband signaling | Analog / cable TV (CATV) |
| Typical Standard | RG-58 (Thinnet), RG-8 (Thicknet) | RG-59, RG-6, RG-11 |
| Impedance | 50 Ω | 75 Ω |
| Data Type | Digital, single channel | Analog, multi-channel via FDM |
| Legacy Network | 10BASE-2, 10BASE-5 Ethernet | Cable modem (DOCSIS), CCTV |
| Maximum Useful Frequency | ~100 MHz | ~1 GHz |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 50 Ω for digital?** This is a trade-off optimum between minimum attenuation (~77 Ω) and maximum power-handling (~30 Ω). For digital baseband, **50 Ω** is the engineering compromise.
>
> **Why 75 Ω for broadband?** It is mathematically close to the minimum-attenuation impedance (~77 Ω) and matches standard TV antenna feeds.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Symbols / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Characteristic Impedance | $Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)$ | $a$ = inner radius, $b$ = outer radius (m), $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity, $Z_0$ in $\Omega$ |
| Velocity of Propagation | $v = \dfrac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$ | $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s, $v$ in m/s |
| Velocity Factor (VF) | $VF = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$ | Dimensionless ($\leq 1$) |
| Propagation Delay (per unit length) | $t_p = \dfrac{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c}$ | seconds per meter (s/m) |
| Capacitance per unit length | $C = \dfrac{2\pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{\ln(b/a)}$ | F/m |
| Inductance per unit length | $L = \dfrac{\mu_0}{2\pi} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{b}{a}\right)$ | H/m |
| Verification relation | $Z_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{L}{C}}$ | $\Omega$ |
| Attenuation | $\alpha = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$ | dB per unit length |
| Cutoff Frequency (TE₁₁ mode) | $f_c = \dfrac{c}{\pi (a+b) \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}$ | Hz (upper limit for TEM mode) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL — Markdown Table Rule:** Within the table cells above, the absolute value of $(b/a)$ and $\ln(b/a)$ has been expressed with explicit parentheses and $\ln$ notation. **No vertical pipe character `|` was used inside any cell** to preserve table integrity.

### 2.4 Why the Coaxial Design Works (Physical Insight)

- **Concentric geometry** → uniform electric and magnetic field between inner and outer conductor.
- **Outer shield grounded** → external electric fields terminate on the shield, **never on the inner conductor** ⇒ superb EMI rejection (typically > 90 dB).
- **TEM mode propagation** → no longitudinal E or H component, only radial E and circumferential H ⇒ no dispersion at low frequencies.
- **Closed-form return path** → the outer conductor carries the return current directly opposite to the inner one, **minimizing radiated loop area** and hence minimizing cross-talk.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Characteristic Impedance $Z_0$

We model the coax as two concentric cylinders of radii $a$ (inner) and $b$ (outer), with a uniform dielectric of permittivity $\varepsilon = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r$ and permeability $\mu_0$.

**Step 1 — Electric Field between conductors (Gauss's Law).**

Treating inner conductor as a line charge $+\lambda$ per unit length, by cylindrical symmetry:

$$
E(r) = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \, r} \quad \text{for } a \le r \le b
$$

**Step 2 — Voltage between conductors.**

Integrate the tangential electric field along a radial path from $a$ to $b$:

$$
V = \int_{a}^{b} E(r) \, dr = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} \int_{a}^{b} \frac{dr}{r} = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} \ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Capacitance per unit length.**

By definition, $C = \lambda / V$:

$$
C = \frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{\ln(b/a)} \quad \text{[F/m]}
$$

**Step 4 — Inductance per unit length.**

The current $I$ on the inner conductor returns through the outer one, creating a circumferential magnetic field. For a long straight wire, the internal + external inductance gives:

$$
L = \frac{\mu_0}{2\pi} \ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) \quad \text{[H/m]}
$$

**Step 5 — Characteristic impedance.**

For a lossless transmission line, $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$:

$$
Z_0 = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{2\pi} \ln(b/a) \;\Big/ \;\frac{2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r}{\ln(b/a)}} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{4\pi^2 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r} \left[\ln(b/a)\right]^2}
$$

$$
Z_0 = \frac{\ln(b/a)}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}}
$$

**Step 6 — Insert $\sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} = 120\pi \ \Omega$ (intrinsic impedance of free space).**

$$
Z_0 = \frac{120\pi}{2\pi \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Z_0 = \frac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) \;\; \Omega\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering utility of this equation:** Once a manufacturer picks a dielectric ($\varepsilon_r$) and a standard $Z_0$ (e.g., 50 Ω or 75 Ω), the ratio $b/a$ is *completely fixed*. This is why you cannot arbitrarily thin down a coax — its dimensions are dictated by physics.

### 3.2 Derivation of Velocity of Propagation

For a TEM wave in a lossless dielectric, both phase velocity $v_p$ and signal velocity $v$ are:

$$
v = \frac{1}{\sqrt{LC}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}
$$

Since $c = 1 / \sqrt{\mu_0 \varepsilon_0}$:

$$
\boxed{\,v = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}\,}
$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem [KTU University Exam – Model Practice]:** A coaxial cable has an inner conductor of radius $a = 0.5$ mm and outer conductor of inner radius $b = 2$ mm. The dielectric is polyethylene with $\varepsilon_r = 2.3$. Calculate:
(a) Characteristic impedance
(b) Velocity of propagation
(c) Propagation delay per meter

**Solution:**

**(a)** Use $Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln(b/a)$:

$$
Z_0 = \frac{60}{\sqrt{2.3}} \ln\!\left(\frac{2}{0.5}\right) = \frac{60}{1.5166} \cdot \ln(4) = \frac{60}{1.5166} \cdot 1.3863
$$

$$
Z_0 = 39.56 \cdot 1.3863 \approx 54.84 \ \Omega
$$

**(b)** Velocity of propagation:

$$
v = \frac{c}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{\sqrt{2.3}} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{1.5166} \approx 1.978 \times 10^{8} \ \text{m/s}
$$

**(c)** Propagation delay per meter:

$$
t_p = \frac{1}{v} = \frac{1}{1.978 \times 10^{8}} \approx 5.057 \ \text{ns/m}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Note:** Always convert radii to the **same units** (here both in mm, ratio is dimensionless) and remember that $c$ must be in **m/s** for the delay to come out in seconds.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Layered Cross-Section (Block Architecture of Coaxial Cable)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[LAYER 1: Inner Conductor - Solid or Stranded Copper - Carries Signal]
    B[LAYER 2: Dielectric Insulator - Polyethylene or PTFE - epsilon r controls speed]
    C[LAYER 3: Outer Conductor - Braided Copper Shield - Return Path and EMI Block]
    D[LAYER 4: Outer Jacket - PVC or PE - Mechanical and Chemical Protection]

    A -- Surrounded by --> B
    B -- Surrounded by --> C
    C -- Surrounded by --> D

    subgraph AXIS [Concentric Geometric Axis]
        direction LR
        X1[Center] --> X2[Axis of Symmetry shared by all 4 layers]
    end

    A ~~~ AXIS
```

### 4.2 Functional Role in Data Communication System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TX[TX: Transmitter or Source Device] -->|Modulated Signal| ENC[Encoder or Modem]
    ENC -->|Electrical Pulses in TEM mode| COAX[Coaxial Cable Channel]
    COAX -->|Signal with attenuation and delay| DEC[Decoder or Modem]
    DEC -->|Recovered Bits| RX[RX: Receiver or Destination Device]

    NOI[External EMI Sources like Motors and RF Transmitters] -.->|Blocked by shield| COAX
    TERM[50 or 75 ohm Terminator] -.->|Prevents reflections| COAX
```

### 4.3 Signal Path & Reference Axis

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CC [Coaxial Cable Cross Section]
        direction TB
        IC[Inner Conductor radius a]
        DI[Dielectric epsilon r]
        OC[Outer Conductor radius b]
    end

    SIG[Signal Current I forward] --> IC
    IC --> DI
    OC --> DI
    OC --> RET[Return Current I reverse]

    IC -.->|Same geometric axis| OC
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above use **purely alphanumeric node IDs** (e.g., `LAYER 1`, `Inner Conductor`) without any reserved keywords like `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, or `style` as standalone identifiers, in compliance with the compilation safeguards.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Define coaxial cable. Mention any two advantages over twisted-pair cable.*

**Model Answer:**

> A coaxial cable is a guided transmission medium consisting of a central copper conductor, a dielectric insulator, a tubular metallic shield, and an outer protective jacket, all sharing a common geometric axis.
>
> **Two advantages over twisted pair:**
> 1. **Better noise immunity** — the grounded outer shield provides > 90 dB EMI rejection, vastly superior to UTP.
> 2. **Higher bandwidth and lower attenuation** — can carry signals up to several hundred MHz over hundreds of metres without repeaters.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between 50 Ω and 75 Ω coaxial cables with one example use of each.*

**Model Answer:**

| **Criterion** | **50 Ω** | **75 Ω** |
|---|---|---|
| Optimized for | Power transfer / digital baseband | Minimum attenuation / analog |
| Example standard | RG-58 (Thinnet Ethernet) | RG-59 / RG-6 (Cable TV) |
| Typical use | 10BASE-2 LANs | CATV, DOCSIS broadband |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction of a coaxial cable. List any **four** advantages. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Inner conductor** (solid/stranded copper, radius $a$) — carries the signal.
2. **Dielectric insulator** (PE, $\varepsilon_r \approx 2.3$) — separates inner and outer conductors and sets velocity factor.
3. **Outer conductor / shield** (braided copper) — provides the return path and acts as a Faraday cage.
4. **Outer jacket** (PVC/PE) — protects against environmental damage.

**Four advantages:**
- Excellent EMI/RFI rejection.
- Higher bandwidth than twisted pair (up to ~1 GHz for RG-6).
- Lower attenuation per unit length.
- Physically robust and weather-resistant.

**(b)** Discuss the **transmission characteristics** of a coaxial cable, including attenuation, propagation delay, and cutoff frequency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Attenuation** $\alpha$ (dB/m) increases with $\sqrt{f}$ due to skin effect in the conductors. Typical values: RG-58 at 100 MHz ≈ 0.18 dB/m.
- **Propagation delay** $t_p = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}/c$ — for $\varepsilon_r = 2.3$, $t_p \approx 5.05$ ns/m.
- **Cutoff frequency** $f_c = c / [\pi(a+b)\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}]$ — beyond this, higher-order waveguide modes appear and TEM propagation fails.
- **Characteristic impedance** $Z_0 = (60/\sqrt{\varepsilon_r})\ln(b/a)$ — must be matched at both ends with terminating resistors to avoid reflections.
- **Noise immunity** is high due to the shield; **crosstalk** is virtually absent between adjacent coax cables.

---

#### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **characteristic impedance** of a coaxial cable. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

[Full derivation is given in SECTION_3, Step 1 → Step 6.]

$$
Z_0 = \frac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln\!\left(\frac{b}{a}\right) \ \Omega
$$

- **Step 1** Apply Gauss's law to get radial $E(r) = \lambda/(2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r r)$.
- **Step 2** Integrate to get voltage $V = (\lambda/2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r)\ln(b/a)$.
- **Step 3** Compute capacitance $C = 2\pi\varepsilon_0\varepsilon_r/\ln(b/a)$.
- **Step 4** Compute inductance $L = (\mu_0/2\pi)\ln(b/a)$.
- **Step 5** Combine: $Z_0 = \sqrt{L/C}$.
- **Step 6** Substitute $\sqrt{\mu_0/\varepsilon_0} = 120\pi$ to obtain the final boxed result.

**(b)** Explain the different **types of connectors** used with coaxial cables in computer networks. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **BNC Connector (Bayonet Neill-Concelman)** — used with RG-58 (10BASE-2 Ethernet). Quick connect/disconnect via bayonet lock.
2. **BNC T-connector** — splits the signal to feed the network interface card; required at every workstation in 10BASE-2.
3. **BNC Terminator (50 Ω)** — placed at the two physical ends of the bus to absorb signal and prevent reflection.
4. **F-Type Connector** — screw-on, used with 75 Ω coax (RG-6, RG-59) for CATV, cable modems, and satellite TV.
5. **N-Type Connector** — threaded, weatherproof, used in thicknet (10BASE-5) and RF applications up to 11 GHz.
6. **SMA Connector** — subminiature threaded connector for high-frequency test equipment and microwave coax.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where students typically lose marks:**
> 1. **Mixing up 50 Ω and 75 Ω applications** — remember: **digital ⇒ 50 Ω**, **analog/CATV ⇒ 75 Ω**.
> 2. **Forgetting the terminating resistor** — many students describe a bus topology but forget to mention that BNC **terminators** (matched to 50 Ω) must be installed at both ends.
> 3. **In $Z_0$ derivation, skipping the substitution of $120\pi$** — full marks require that final algebraic step.
> 4. **Confusing $\varepsilon_r$ with $\varepsilon_0$** — $\varepsilon_r$ (relative) is dimensionless; $\varepsilon_0$ (absolute) is in F/m.
> 5. **Not stating the units** of characteristic impedance, attenuation, and delay — examiners in KTU deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Coaxial Cable):**
> - Coax = 4 concentric layers: **inner conductor, dielectric, outer shield, jacket**.
> - TEM mode propagation — fields purely transverse.
> - **50 Ω** ⇒ digital baseband (RG-58 Thinnet, 10BASE-2).
> - **75 Ω** ⇒ analog broadband (RG-6, CATV, cable modem).
> - Characteristic impedance: $Z_0 = \dfrac{60}{\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}} \ln(b/a)$ — must be terminated to avoid reflections.
> - Velocity of propagation: $v = c / \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}$ (always less than $c$).
> - Propagation delay: $t_p = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r} / c \approx 5$ ns/m for PE dielectric.
> - Cutoff frequency of TEM mode: $f_c = c/[\pi(a+b)\sqrt{\varepsilon_r}]$.
> - Connectors: **BNC, T, Terminator** for 50 Ω; **F-type, N-type** for 75 Ω.
> - Attenuation rises as $\sqrt{f}$ (skin effect dominates at high frequency).
> - Coax supports **FDM** (broadband) — multiple channels on a single cable.
> - Coax is a **Faraday cage**: external EMI terminates on the shield, not on the inner conductor.
> - Legacy use: 10BASE-2 (Thinnet), 10BASE-5 (Thicknet); current use: CATV, DOCSIS, CCTV.
> - **Impedance matching** is critical — mismatched termination causes standing waves and bit errors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical Fiber
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Optical Fiber # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition An **Optical Fiber** is a thin, flexible, transparent dielectric (typically silica glass or plastic) cylindrical waveguide that transmits information in the form of light pulses (photons) from one end to the other, exploiting the principle of **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**. It forms the physical medium for **Optical Fiber Communication (OFC)**, which is a high-bandwidth, low-attenuation, and electromagnetically immune method of data transmission used extensively in modern telecommunications backbone networks, the internet, and KTU syllabus-defined data communication frameworks. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):** > Optical fibers are dielectric waveguides operating at optical frequencies. They consist of a central **Core** (refractive index $n_1$), surrounded by a **Cladding** (refractive index $n_2$, where $n_2 < n_1$), and an outer protective **Buffer/Coating**. Light launched within the **Acceptance Cone** propagates via successive total internal reflections. ## 1.2 Physical Structure (Three-Layer Cylindrical Geometry) | Layer | Material | Refractive Index | Function | |---|---|---|---| | **Core** | Pure SiO₂ (Silica) doped with GeO₂ | $n_1$ (High) | Carries the light signal | | **Cladding** | Pure SiO₂ doped with B₂O₃ or F | $n_2$ (Low) | Confines light via TIR | | **Buffer/Jacket** | Acrylate polymer / PVC | $n_3$ (≈1) | Mechanical protection | > [!NOTE] > **Core Diameter Standards:** > - **Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF):** 50 µm or 62.5 µm core / 125 µm cladding > - **Single-Mode Fiber (SMF):** 8–10 µm core / 125 µm cladding ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Think of an optical fiber as a **"light pipe"** that works exactly like a long, thin water hose carrying water — but instead of water, it carries beams of light. - Imagine you are standing inside a long glass tunnel with highly polished, mirror-finished walls. If you shine a flashlight at just the right angle toward the wall, the light bounces off the wall and travels to the other end without leaking out. This is precisely what happens inside an optical fiber. - The **"polished wall"** corresponds to the **core-cladding interface** (where $n_1 > n_2$), and the **"bouncing flashlight"** corresponds to **light undergoing Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**. - The **"tunnel itself"** is the fiber core, and the entire hose is protected by the **buffer coating**, just like a rubber jacket around an electrical wire. > [!TIP] > **Why use light instead of electrical wires?** Because light (1) travels at the speed of light ($\approx 3 \times 10^8$ m/s in vacuum), (2) is immune to electromagnetic interference (EMI), (3) suffers far less loss over long distances, and (4) can carry enormous bandwidths (Terabits per second). ## 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Metrics - Speed of light in vacuum: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s - Speed of light in a medium: $v = c / n$ - Wavelengths used in OFC: **850 nm, 1310 nm, 1550 nm** (Near-Infrared region) - Standard attenuation in modern silica fiber at 1550 nm: **≈ 0.2 dB/km** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Governing Principle: Total Internal Reflection (TIR) For light to remain trapped within the fiber core, two conditions must be satisfied: 1. The core must be **optically denser** than the cladding: $n_1 > n_2$. 2. The light ray must strike the core-cladding boundary at an angle **greater than or equal to the Critical Angle ($\theta_c$)**. ### Snell's Law (Foundation) When light travels from a medium of refractive index $n_1$ into a medium of refractive index $n_2$, Snell's Law states: $$n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$$ - $\theta_1$ = Angle of incidence (measured from the normal to the interface) - $\theta_2$ = Angle of refraction ### Critical Angle Derivation Logic The critical angle $\theta_c$ is the angle of incidence (in the denser medium) for which the angle of refraction becomes $90^\circ$ (i.e., the refracted ray skims along the interface). By Snell's Law at the critical condition: $$n_1 \sin \theta_c = n_2 \sin 90^\circ = n_2 \cdot 1$$ Solving for $\theta_c$: $$\sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$ $$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **TIR Condition:** TIR occurs when $\theta_1 \geq \theta_c$. In an optical fiber, the angle at which a light ray strikes the core-cladding interface is determined by the angle at which it enters the fiber from air. This leads us to two crucial parameters: **Acceptance Angle ($\theta_a$)** and **Numerical Aperture (NA)**. ## 2.2 Numerical Aperture (NA) — The "Light Gathering Ability" The **Numerical Aperture (NA)** is a dimensionless figure of merit that quantifies the range of angles over which the fiber can accept incoming light. A higher NA means the fiber can gather more light. $$NA = n_0 \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$ Where: - $n_0$ = Refractive index of the surrounding medium (≈ 1 for air) - $\theta_a$ = Maximum acceptance angle - $n_1, n_2$ = Refractive indices of core and cladding, respectively > [!NOTE] > **Why is NA important for KTU problems?** Many board exam numericals directly ask you to "calculate the NA" or "find the acceptance angle" — these two formulas are high-yield. ## 2.3 Acceptance Angle ($\theta_a$) The acceptance angle is the maximum angle (measured from the fiber axis) at which a light ray can enter the fiber and still be guided via TIR. $$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}\right) = \sin^{-1}(NA)$$ ## 2.4 Fractional Refractive Index Change ($\Delta$) The relative difference in refractive indices is denoted by $\Delta$: $$\Delta = \frac{n_1^2 - n_2^2}{2 n_1^2} \approx \frac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} \quad \text{(for small } \Delta \text{, typically } \Delta \ll 1\text{)}$$ For weakly guiding fibers ($\Delta \approx 0.01$), NA can be approximated as: $$NA \approx n_1 \sqrt{2\Delta}$$ ## 2.5 Modes of Propagation A **mode** is a specific, allowed path (electromagnetic field pattern) that light can take through the fiber. The number of modes $M$ for a step-index MMF is given by the **Normalized Frequency (V-number)**: $$V = \frac{2\pi a}{\lambda} \cdot NA$$ $$M \approx \frac{V^2}{2}$$ Where $a$ is the core radius and $\lambda$ is the operating wavelength. | Fiber Type | V-number | Number of Modes | |---|---|---| | **Single-Mode Fiber (SMF)** | $V < 2.405$ | 1 (only fundamental mode) | | **Multi-Mode Fiber (MMF)** | $V \gg 2.405$ | Hundreds to thousands | > [!TIP] > **KTU Quick Rule of Thumb:** If core diameter > ~10 µm and NA is around 0.2, the fiber is **multimode**. If core diameter is around 8–10 µm with low NA (~0.1), it's **single-mode**. ## 2.6 Attenuation (Signal Loss) Attenuation $\alpha$ is the reduction in optical power as light travels through the fiber, measured in dB/km: $$\alpha = -\frac{10}{L} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right) \quad \text{(dB/km)}$$ Where $L$ is the fiber length in km, $P_{in}$ and $P_{out}$ are input and output powers. ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $n_1 \sin \theta_1 = n_2 \sin \theta_2$ | Snell's Law | — | | 2 | $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$ | Critical Angle | Degrees / Radians | | 3 | $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$ | Numerical Aperture | Dimensionless | | 4 | $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA)$ | Acceptance Angle | Degrees / Radians | | 5 | $\Delta = \dfrac{n_1^2 - n_2^2}{2 n_1^2}$ | Fractional Index Change | $\Delta \ll 1$ for SMF | | 6 | $V = \dfrac{2\pi a}{\lambda} \cdot NA$ | Normalized Frequency (V-number) | Dimensionless | | 7 | $M \approx \dfrac{V^2}{2}$ | Number of Modes (step-index MMF) | Integer | | 8 | $\alpha = -\dfrac{10}{L} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)$ | Attenuation Coefficient | dB/km | | 9 | $v = \dfrac{c}{n}$ | Speed of light in medium | m/s | | 10 | $BW \propto \dfrac{1}{\Delta \tau}$ | Bandwidth (inverse of pulse broadening) | Hz | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Optical fibers are the backbone of the internet (submarine cables, FTTH - Fiber to the Home), undersea communication links, medical endoscopes, and high-speed data centers. The formulas above are routinely used by telecom engineers to design long-haul links and choose between SMF and MMF for given distance-bandwidth products. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Numerical Aperture from Snell's Law We want to find the maximum angle $\theta_a$ (in air, $n_0 = 1$) at which light can enter the fiber and still undergo TIR. **Step 1:** Consider a light ray entering the fiber core from air. Let the angle of incidence (in air) be $\theta_0$, and the angle of refraction inside the core (with respect to the fiber axis) be $\theta_1$. The ray then strikes the core-cladding interface at an angle $\theta$ (measured from the **normal** to the interface). **Step 2:** From the geometry of the cylindrical fiber, the angle of incidence at the core-cladding boundary is: $$\theta = 90^\circ - \theta_1$$ **Step 3:** Apply Snell's Law at the air-core (front face) interface: $$n_0 \sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin \theta_1$$ For maximum acceptance, we use $\theta_0 = \theta_a$, so: $$\sin \theta_a = n_1 \sin \theta_1 \quad \text{(since } n_0 = 1 \text{)}$$ **Step 4:** For TIR to just occur at the core-cladding boundary, the angle of incidence at that boundary must equal the critical angle $\theta_c$: $$\theta = \theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$$ **Step 5:** So at the limit of TIR: $$\theta_1 = 90^\circ - \theta_c = 90^\circ - \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right) = \cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$$ **Step 6:** Substitute $\theta_1$ into the Snell's Law expression: $$\sin \theta_a = n_1 \sin\!\left(\cos^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)\right)$$ **Step 7:** Using the trigonometric identity $\sin(\cos^{-1}(x)) = \sqrt{1 - x^2}$: $$\sin \theta_a = n_1 \sqrt{1 - \left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)^2}$$ $$\sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$ **Step 8:** Define the **Numerical Aperture**: $$\boxed{NA = \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}$$ This completes the derivation. The numerical aperture is a pure property of the fiber's material and is independent of the surrounding medium if $n_0 = 1$ (air). --- ## 3.2 Worked Example (Model KTU Numerical) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style Problem]** > An optical fiber has a core refractive index $n_1 = 1.50$ and a cladding refractive index $n_2 = 1.45$. Calculate: (a) the Numerical Aperture, (b) the Acceptance Angle, and (c) the Critical Angle. The fiber is surrounded by air ($n_0 = 1$). ### Solution **Part (a): Numerical Aperture** Using the formula: $$NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$ $$NA = \sqrt{(1.50)^2 - (1.45)^2}$$ $$NA = \sqrt{2.2500 - 2.1025}$$ $$NA = \sqrt{0.1475}$$ $$NA \approx 0.384$$ **Part (b): Acceptance Angle** $$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA) = \sin^{-1}(0.384)$$ $$\theta_a \approx 22.6^\circ$$ **Part (c): Critical Angle** $$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right) = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.45}{1.50}\right)$$ $$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(0.9667)$$ $$\theta_c \approx 75.2^\circ$$ > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The acceptance angle is small ($22.6^\circ$), which is typical. Light entering the fiber at any angle up to $22.6^\circ$ from the axis will be guided. Once inside, the ray strikes the core-cladding interface at $\geq 75.2^\circ$, ensuring TIR. --- ## 3.3 Step-by-Step: Attenuation Numerical Example > **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Style Problem]** > An optical signal of power 1 mW is launched into a 50 km fiber. The output power is measured as 1 µW.
