Calculate the attenuation coefficient $\alpha$ in dB/km. **Step 1:** Identify inputs: - $P_{in} = 1 \text{ mW} = 10^{-3} \text{ W}$ - $P_{out} = 1 \text{ µW} = 10^{-6} \text{ W}$ - $L = 50 \text{ km}$ **Step 2:** Apply the attenuation formula: $$\alpha = -\frac{10}{L} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)$$ **Step 3:** Compute the power ratio: $$\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{10^{-6}}{10^{-3}} = 10^{-3}$$ **Step 4:** Evaluate the log: $$\log_{10}(10^{-3}) = -3$$ **Step 5:** Substitute: $$\alpha = -\frac{10}{50} \cdot (-3) = \frac{30}{50}$$ $$\boxed{\alpha = 0.6 \text{ dB/km}}$$ This value indicates a moderately lossy fiber (modern silica fibers achieve ~0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm). --- ## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix | Parameter | Symbol | Typical SMF Value | Typical MMF Value | KTU Question Likelihood | |---|---|---|---|---| | Core diameter | $2a$ | 8–10 µm | 50 / 62.5 µm | High | | NA | $NA$ | 0.10 – 0.14 | 0.20 – 0.30 | Very High | | Attenuation (1550 nm) | $\alpha$ | 0.2 dB/km | 2.5 dB/km (850 nm) | High | | Bandwidth-distance | $B \cdot L$ | > 1000 GHz·km | 200–500 MHz·km | Medium | | Cost | — | Higher (laser source) | Lower (LED source) | Low | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Optical Fiber — Layered Structure and Light Ray Propagation ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph OUTER["PROTECTIVE OUTER LAYERS"] jacketNode["BUFFER JACKET
Acrylate Polymer or PVC
Mechanical Protection"] coatNode["PRIMARY COATING
UV Cured Acrylate
Reduces Microbending Loss"] end subgraph CLAD["CLADDING LAYER"] cladNode["CLADDING
Doped Silica SiO2 plus B2O3
Refractive Index n2 LOWER
Outer Diameter 125 micrometre"] end subgraph CORE["CORE LAYER"] coreNode["CORE
Pure SiO2 plus GeO2
Refractive Index n1 HIGHER
Diameter 8 to 50 micrometre
LIGHT PROPAGATES HERE"] end subgraph RAYS["LIGHT RAY GEOMETRY"] axisNode["FIBER AXIS
Central Reference Line"] incidentNode["INCIDENT RAY
Enters at Acceptance Angle theta_a
from air with n0 equals 1"] tirlNode["TIR AT INTERFACE 1
Ray strikes boundary
Angle greater than theta_c
TOTAL INTERNAL REFLECTION"] tirmNode["TIR AT INTERFACE 2
Second bounce
Continues propagation"] emergeNode["EMERGENT RAY
Exits at far end
Carries data pulses"] end jacketNode --> coatNode coatNode --> cladNode cladNode --> coreNode coreNode --> axisNode incidentNode --> tirlNode tirlNode --> tirmNode tirmNode --> emergeNode ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** - The **CORE** (innermost, $n_1$) is where light physically travels. - The **CLADDING** (middle, $n_2 < n_1$) provides the refractive index discontinuity that causes TIR. - The **COATING + JACKET** (outermost) provide mechanical and environmental protection, but do not guide light. - The lower block shows the **zig-zag propagation path** of a meridional light ray undergoing successive total internal reflections. --- ## 4.2 Optical Fiber Communication System — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR srcNode["DATA SOURCE
Voice Video or Computer
Electrical Signal Input"] --> encNode["ELECTRICAL ENCODER
PCM or Analog Modulation
Converts Data to Bitstream"] encNode --> eoNode["E O CONVERTER
Electrical to Optical
LED or LASER DIODE
850 1310 1550 nm"] eoNode --> fiberNode["OPTICAL FIBER CHANNEL
Core plus Cladding plus Jacket
Guides Light via TIR
Attenuation alpha dB per km"] fiberNode --> oeNode["O E CONVERTER
Optical to Electrical
Photodiode APD or PIN"] oeNode --> decNode["ELECTRICAL DECODER
Amplifier and Demodulator
Recovers Original Data"] decNode --> dstNode["DATA DESTINATION
End User or Network Node"] fiberNode -.->|"Loss Mechanisms"| lossNode["ATTENUATION
ABSORPTION
SCATTERING
BENDING LOSSES
DISPERSION"] ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** - The architecture follows the **Source → Encoder → Transmitter → Channel → Receiver → Decoder → Destination** flow used universally in KTU's data communication syllabus. - The **E/O** and **O/E** blocks are critical: LEDs are used for MMF (cheaper, broader spectrum), while **Laser Diodes** are used for SMF (narrow linewidth, higher bandwidth). - The dashed annotation highlights the **loss mechanisms** that the attenuation formula quantifies. --- ## 4.3 Step-Index vs Graded-Index Fiber — Modal Behavior ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SIF["STEP INDEX FIBER MMF"] sifCore1["Core Layer n1 Constant"] --> sifBounce1["Sharp TIR Bounces"] sifBounce1 --> sifBounce2["Different Path Lengths"] sifBounce2 --> sifResult1["INTERMODAL DISPERSION HIGH
Lower Bandwidth"] end subgraph GIF["GRADED INDEX FIBER MMF"] gifCore1["Core Index n1r Decreases Radially"] --> gifCurve1["Continuous Refraction Bending"] gifCurve1 --> gifSpeed1["Faster at Edges Slower at Center"] gifSpeed1 --> gifResult1["EQUAL TRAVEL TIME
Higher Bandwidth"] end sifResult1 -.->|"Compare"| gifResult1 ``` **Diagram Interpretation:** - In **Step-Index MMF**, all rays travel at the same speed but take different path lengths, leading to **intermodal dispersion** (pulse spreading). - In **Graded-Index MMF**, the refractive index is highest at the center and decreases parabolically outward, causing rays to continuously bend. Rays traveling near the edge move faster (lower $n$), compensating for their longer path — resulting in near-equal transit times and significantly higher bandwidth. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define Numerical Aperture. [3 Marks]** **[Simulated KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** The Numerical Aperture (NA) of an optical fiber is a dimensionless quantity that measures the fiber's ability to collect and accept incoming light. It is defined as the sine of the maximum acceptance angle $\theta_a$ when the fiber is in air ($n_0 = 1$): $$NA = \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$ where $n_1$ and $n_2$ are the refractive indices of the core and cladding, respectively. A higher NA indicates a larger light-gathering capacity, which is critical for efficient coupling of light from a source (LED or laser) into the fiber. --- ### **Question 2: What is Total Internal Reflection? Mention the conditions required. [3 Marks]** **[Simulated KTU University Exam - June 2024]** **Model Answer:** Total Internal Reflection (TIR) is the optical phenomenon in which a light ray traveling in a denser medium is completely reflected back into the same medium at the interface with a less dense medium, with no transmission of light into the second medium. **Two conditions for TIR:** 1. Light must travel from an optically **denser medium to a rarer medium** (i.e., $n_1 > n_2$). 2. The angle of incidence at the interface must be **greater than or equal to the critical angle** $\theta_c$, where: $$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{n_2}{n_1}\right)$$ TIR is the fundamental principle that allows optical fibers to confine and guide light over long distances with minimal loss. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### **Question A (Choice 1):** > **[Simulated KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | 14 Marks]** > **(a)** Derive an expression for the Numerical Aperture of a step-index optical fiber in terms of the refractive indices of the core and cladding. **[7 Marks]** > **(b)** An optical fiber has $n_1 = 1.52$ and $n_2 = 1.48$. Find (i) Numerical Aperture, (ii) Acceptance Angle, (iii) Critical Angle, and (iv) Fractional Refractive Index Difference $\Delta$. **[7 Marks]** ### **Model Solution:** #### **Part (a) — Derivation of NA** **Step 1:** Consider a meridional light ray entering the fiber core from air at an angle $\theta_0$ to the fiber axis. **Step 2:** By Snell's Law at the air-core interface (front face): $$n_0 \sin \theta_0 = n_1 \sin \theta_1$$ For maximum acceptance, $\theta_0 = \theta_a$ (acceptance angle), so: $$\sin \theta_a = n_1 \sin \theta_1 \quad \text{(since } n_0 = 1\text{)} \quad \text{...(1)}$$ **Step 3:** From the geometry of the cylindrical fiber, the angle $\theta$ at which the ray strikes the core-cladding boundary is: $$\theta = 90^\circ - \theta_1 \quad \text{...(2)}$$ **Step 4:** For TIR to just occur, $\theta = \theta_c$, the critical angle: $$n_1 \sin \theta_c = n_2 \Rightarrow \sin \theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1} \quad \text{...(3)}$$ **Step 5:** From (2) at the limit: $$\theta_1 = 90^\circ - \theta_c$$ **Step 6:** Substituting into (1): $$\sin \theta_a = n_1 \sin(90^\circ - \theta_c) = n_1 \cos \theta_c = n_1 \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \theta_c}$$ **Step 7:** Using (3): $$\sin \theta_a = n_1 \sqrt{1 - \frac{n_2^2}{n_1^2}} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$ $$\boxed{NA = \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}}$$ This is the standard KTU expression for Numerical Aperture. #### **Part (b) — Numerical Computation** **Given:** $n_1 = 1.52$, $n_2 = 1.48$ **(i) Numerical Aperture:** $$NA = \sqrt{(1.52)^2 - (1.48)^2} = \sqrt{2.3104 - 2.1904} = \sqrt{0.1200}$$ $$NA \approx 0.3464$$ **(ii) Acceptance Angle:** $$\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(0.3464) \approx 20.27^\circ$$ **(iii) Critical Angle:** $$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{1.48}{1.52}\right) = \sin^{-1}(0.9737) \approx 76.65^\circ$$ **(iv) Fractional Refractive Index Difference:** $$\Delta = \frac{n_1^2 - n_2^2}{2 n_1^2} = \frac{0.1200}{2 \times (1.52)^2} = \frac{0.1200}{4.6208}$$ $$\Delta \approx 0.02597 \approx 2.6\%$$ --- ### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative Internal Choice):** > **[Simulated KTU University Exam - June 2024 | 14 Marks]** > **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the optical fiber communication system. List the key advantages of optical fiber communication over conventional copper-based communication. **[7 Marks]** > **(b)** An optical fiber of length 30 km is supplied with 200 µW of optical power at the input. The output power measured is 4 µW. Determine: (i) the total signal loss in dB, (ii) the attenuation coefficient in dB/km. **[7 Marks]** ### **Model Solution:** #### **Part (a) — Block Diagram and Advantages** **Block Diagram (already detailed in Section 4.2 of these notes):** A complete OFC system consists of: 1. **Input/Source:** Data (voice/video/digital) → electrical signal 2. **Encoder:** Converts to digital bitstream (PCM) 3. **E/O Converter:** LED (850 nm, MMF) or Laser Diode (1310/1550 nm, SMF) 4. **Optical Fiber Channel:** Core + Cladding + Jacket, with TIR guiding 5. **O/E Converter:** Photodiode (PIN or APD) converts light back to electrical 6. **Decoder/Amplifier:** Recovers original data 7. **Destination** **Key Advantages (minimum 4 required for full marks):** - **Enormous Bandwidth:** THz range vs MHz for copper - **Low Attenuation:** 0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm vs ~20 dB/km for coax - **EMI Immunity:** Light is unaffected by electromagnetic noise - **Security:** Difficult to tap without detection - **Small Size & Lightweight:** Silica fiber is thin and light - **No Crosstalk:** Optical isolation between fibers #### **Part (b) — Attenuation Numerical** **Given:** $P_{in} = 200 \text{ µW} = 200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}$, $P_{out} = 4 \text{ µW} = 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W}$, $L = 30 \text{ km}$ **(i) Total Signal Loss in dB:** $$\text{Loss} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{4 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(50)$$ $$\text{Loss} = 10 \times 1.699 = 16.99 \text{ dB} \approx 17 \text{ dB}$$ **(ii) Attenuation Coefficient in dB/km:** $$\alpha = \frac{\text{Total Loss}}{L} = \frac{16.99 \text{ dB}}{30 \text{ km}}$$ $$\boxed{\alpha \approx 0.566 \text{ dB/km}}$$ This attenuation is consistent with silica fiber operating in the 850 nm window (typical MMF band). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing Angle of Incidence with Fiber Axis:** Students often forget that the critical angle $\theta_c$ is measured from the **normal** to the interface, NOT from the fiber axis. The fiber axis geometry uses the complementary angle $90^\circ - \theta_c$. Drawing the diagram clearly is essential. > > 2. **Forgetting $n_0 = 1$ for Air:** When a problem states "fiber in air," set $n_0 = 1$ from the start. Some students leave it as a variable and lose the mark for the final numerical answer. > > 3. **Attenuation Sign Error:** The attenuation formula uses $P_{out}/P_{in}$ (output over input), which is **less than 1**, so the log is **negative**. The leading negative sign in $\alpha = -\frac{10}{L}\log_{10}(P_{out}/P_{in})$ ensures a **positive** $\alpha$. Forgetting the negative sign is a frequent error. > > 4. **Numerical Aperture ≠ Acceptance Angle:** NA is a number (e.g., 0.38), while $\theta_a$ is an angle (e.g., 22.6°). Don't interchange them in the answer sheet. > > 5. **Step-Index vs Graded-Index Confusion:** For 7-mark derivation questions, state explicitly whether you are using a **step-index** fiber (uniform $n_1$, sharp TIR) or **graded-index** (parabolic profile, continuous refraction). KTU examiners deduct marks for ambiguity. > > 6. **Missing Units:** Always state the unit (dB/km, degrees, µm). A numerically correct answer without units may lose 0.5–1 mark. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **High-Density Revision Bullets — Memorize Before Exam:** - [ ] **Optical Fiber = Core + Cladding + Buffer.** Core has higher RI ($n_1$), cladding has lower RI ($n_2$). Buffer is for mechanical protection only. - [ ] **Guiding Principle = Total Internal Reflection (TIR).** Two conditions: (1) $n_1 > n_2$, (2) angle of incidence $\geq \theta_c$. - [ ] **Critical Angle Formula:** $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2 / n_1)$. **Always in degrees for KTU answers.** - [ ] **Numerical Aperture:** $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$. Dimensionless. High NA = more light collected. - [ ] **Acceptance Angle:** $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA)$. Maximum entry angle from air. - [ ] **Fractional Index Difference:** $\Delta = (n_1^2 - n_2^2) / (2n_1^2)$. Small for weakly guiding fibers. - [ ] **V-number (Normalized Frequency):** $V = (2\pi a / \lambda) \cdot NA$. Determines if fiber is single-mode ($V < 2.405$) or multi-mode ($V > 2.405$). - [ ] **Attenuation Formula:** $\alpha = -\frac{10}{L}\log_{10}(P_{out}/P_{in})$ dB/km. Modern silica at 1550 nm: **0.2 dB/km**. - [ ] **Three Transmission Windows:** 850 nm (MMF, short range), 1310 nm (zero dispersion), 1550 nm (lowest loss, long haul). - [ ] **SMF vs MMF:** SMF has 8–10 µm core, single mode, laser source, long distance, high bandwidth. MMF has 50/62.5 µm core, many modes, LED source, short distance, lower bandwidth. - [ ] **Step-Index vs Graded-Index:** Step-index = uniform $n_1$, sharp TIR, high intermodal dispersion. Graded-index = parabolic profile, continuous refraction, lower dispersion, higher bandwidth. - [ ] **Key Advantages:** Huge bandwidth, low loss, EMI immunity, security, lightweight, no crosstalk. - [ ] **Key Disadvantages:** Fragile, expensive connectors/tools, requires precise alignment, difficult to splice. - [ ] **Always draw a ray diagram** in TIR/NA derivation questions — KTU examiners reward diagram clarity. - [ ] **Mention $n_0 = 1$ (air) explicitly** in the first line of any NA/acceptance angle derivation. - [ ] **Attenuation is positive** in dB/km — watch the sign in the formula.

topics.title: Unguided Media - Radio Waves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Unguided Media - Radio Waves ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Unguided Media** refers to the physical transmission medium in data communication systems where signals are propagated through the atmosphere, outer space, or water without any physical conductor to constrain and guide the electromagnetic waves from the source to the destination. Radio waves form the most widely deployed subset of unguided media, occupying the portion of the electromagnetic spectrum roughly between **3 Hz** and **300 GHz**. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, **Radio Wave Transmission** is formally defined as the wireless propagation of electromagnetic energy generated by a transmitter antenna, characterized by oscillating electric ($E$) and magnetic ($H$) fields that are mutually perpendicular to each other and to the direction of propagation, traveling at the speed of light in free space. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** > Unguided media is broadly classified into three propagation categories: > 1. **Radio Waves** (3 Hz – 300 GHz) > 2. **Microwave Terrestrial & Satellite** (1 GHz – 300 GHz, sub-band of radio) > 3. **Infrared & Light Waves** (300 GHz – 900 THz and beyond) > > *This note focuses exclusively on Radio Waves.* ### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine dropping a **stone into a still pond of water**. The ripples spread outward in every direction, unconstrained by walls. A boat floating anywhere on the pond can "sense" the vibration and react to it. This is precisely how radio waves work: - The **stone** is the **transmitter antenna** (which is electrically excited). - The **pond surface** is the **free-space medium** (atmosphere, vacuum, or air). - The **ripples** are the **electromagnetic wavefronts** radiating outward. - The **boat** is the **receiver antenna** that intercepts the wave and decodes the information. > Unlike the pond ripple, radio waves do not need a medium like air to travel — they can propagate through the **vacuum of space** because they are self-sustaining oscillations of electric and magnetic fields. ### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following are the cardinal constants and parameters associated with radio wave propagation: - **Speed of light in free space:** $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \; \text{m/s}$ - **Permeability of free space:** $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \; \text{H/m}$ - **Permittivity of free space:** $\epsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \; \text{F/m}$ - **Intrinsic impedance of free space:** $\eta_0 \approx 377 \; \Omega$ (also called the wave impedance) - **Frequency Range for Radio Waves:** **3 Hz to 300 GHz** - **Wavelength Range:** **1 mm to 100,000 km** > [!NOTE] > **Why Radio Waves?** > Radio frequencies penetrate buildings, can travel long distances (especially HF sky-wave), bend around obstacles via diffraction, and can be generated efficiently using simple antennas — making them ideal for broadcast, mobile, maritime, and amateur (HAM) communication. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of Radio Wave Generation and Reception The transmission and reception of radio waves follow a deterministic chain of physical and electronic events: - **Step 1 — Baseband Signal Generation:** The information source (voice, data, video) produces a low-frequency electrical signal. - **Step 2 — Modulation:** This baseband signal is used to vary (modulate) one of the high-frequency carrier wave's parameters — **Amplitude (AM)**, **Frequency (FM)**, or **Phase (PM)**. - **Step 3 — RF Amplification:** The modulated carrier is amplified by a Power Amplifier (PA) to a level suitable for radiation. - **Step 4 — Radiation by Transmit Antenna:** A time-varying current in the transmit antenna accelerates charges, launching coupled $E$-field and $H$-field disturbances into free space at the speed of light. - **Step 5 — Propagation Through Channel:** The wave interacts with the ionosphere, troposphere, ground, and obstacles, undergoing attenuation, reflection, refraction, scattering, and diffraction. - **Step 6 — Reception by Receive Antenna:** The receive antenna intercepts a small fraction of the radiated energy, inducing a tiny voltage that is filtered, amplified, and demodulated. - **Step 7 — Information Recovery:** The demodulator extracts the original baseband signal for the destination device. > **Why Each Step Matters:** Modulation (Step 2) allows low-frequency information to be "carried" over long distances. Without it, a voice signal (~3 kHz) could not be efficiently radiated because its wavelength would be ~100 km — far larger than any practical antenna. ### 2.2 Radio Frequency Band Classification (ITU Standard) The International Telecommunication Union (ITU) divides radio waves into bands based on **frequency** and corresponding **propagation behavior**: | Band Name | Frequency Range | Wavelength Range | Primary Use Case | Propagation Mode | |---|---|---|---|---| | **VLF (Very Low Frequency)** | **3 kHz – 30 kHz** | **100 km – 10 km** | Submarine communication, navigation | Ground wave | | **LF (Low Frequency)** | **30 kHz – 300 kHz** | **10 km – 1 km** | AM long-wave broadcast, RFID | Ground wave | | **MF (Medium Frequency)** | **300 kHz – 3 MHz** | **1 km – 100 m** | AM broadcast, maritime | Ground + Sky wave | | **HF (High Frequency)** | **3 MHz – 30 MHz** | **100 m – 10 m** | Shortwave broadcast, amateur radio | **Sky wave (ionospheric)** | | **VHF (Very High Frequency)** | **30 MHz – 300 MHz** | **10 m – 1 m** | FM radio, TV, aviation | Line-of-sight | | **UHF (Ultra High Frequency)** | **300 MHz – 3 GHz** | **1 m – 100 mm** | Mobile, GPS, Wi-Fi, TV | Line-of-sight | | **SHF (Super High Frequency)** | **3 GHz – 30 GHz** | **100 mm – 10 mm** | Satellite, radar, 5G | Line-of-sight | | **EHF (Extremely High Frequency)** | **30 GHz – 300 GHz** | **10 mm – 1 mm** | mmWave 5G, radio astronomy | Line-of-sight | ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $c = f \cdot \lambda$ | Wave speed = frequency × wavelength | m/s | | 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength from frequency | m | | 3 | $f = \dfrac{c}{\lambda}$ | Frequency from wavelength | Hz | | 4 | $T = \dfrac{1}{f}$ | Time period of oscillation | s | | 5 | $E = h \cdot f$ | Energy of a single photon (Planck) | Joules | | 6 | $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu_0}{\epsilon_0}} \approx 377 \; \Omega$ | Intrinsic impedance of free space | Ohms | | 7 | $P_r = \dfrac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^2}{(4\pi d)^2}$ | Friis Transmission Equation (received power) | Watts | | 8 | $FSPL_{(dB)} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | Free Space Path Loss (d in km, f in MHz) | dB | | 9 | $FSPL_{(dB)} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi}{c}\right)$ | Path loss in SI units (d in m, f in Hz) | dB | | 10 | $A_{eff} = \dfrac{G \cdot \lambda^2}{4\pi}$ | Effective aperture of an antenna | m² | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Exam Tip:** KTU examiners **love** the Friis equation and the Free Space Path Loss (FSPL) formula. Memorize the constant **32.44** (for km/MHz) and the structure of $20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$. ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility Radio wave technology underpins: - **AM/FM Broadcasting:** Long-range audio distribution using MF and VHF bands. - **Maritime & Aeronautical Communication:** HF and VHF for ship-to-shore and aircraft-to-tower links. - **Cellular Networks (2G/3G/4G/5G):** UHF and SHF bands for mobile broadband. - **Amateur (HAM) Radio:** HF experimentation for global communication via sky-wave. - **Public Safety & Defense:** Dedicated VHF/UHF channels for police, military, emergency services. - **IoT & Smart Agriculture:** Sub-GHz ISM bands (e.g., 433 MHz, 868 MHz, 915 MHz) for low-power long-range sensors. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Wavelength from Frequency The most fundamental relationship in radio wave analysis links the speed of light ($c$), frequency ($f$), and wavelength ($\lambda$). **Starting Point:** $$c = f \cdot \lambda$$ **Step 1 — Isolate Wavelength:** We divide both sides by $f$ to express wavelength in terms of frequency. $$\frac{c}{f} = \frac{f \cdot \lambda}{f}$$ This simplifies to: $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the Numerical Value of $c$:** In free space, $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \; \text{m/s}$. Plugging in: $$\lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{f} \; \text{meters}$$ > **Logic Note:** This equation reveals an inverse relationship — as frequency increases, wavelength decreases. Higher frequency waves are "tighter" oscillations, which is why UHF antennas can be small (cm-scale) while VLF antennas span kilometers. **Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical):** *Problem:* Find the wavelength of a 100 MHz FM radio signal. **Step 3 — Apply the Formula:** $$\lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \; \text{m/s}}{100 \times 10^{6} \; \text{Hz}}$$ **Step 4 — Simplify Numerator and Denominator:** $$\lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{10^{8}} \; \text{m}$$ **Step 5 — Final Result:** $$\boxed{\lambda = 3 \; \text{meters}}$$ This matches the well-known fact that a **quarter-wave monopole antenna for 100 MHz FM is approximately 75 cm long** — a useful KTU exam cross-check. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Free Space Path Loss (FSPL) in Decibels The Friis Transmission Equation in linear form is: $$P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^2}{(4\pi d)^2}$$ **Step 1 — Isolate the Path Loss Term:** The path loss $L$ is defined as the ratio of transmitted to received power (assuming unity gains), rearranged as: $$L = \frac{P_t}{P_r} = \frac{(4\pi d)^2}{\lambda^2}$$ **Step 2 — Express in Decibels:** $$L_{(dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{(4\pi d)^2}{\lambda^2}\right]$$ **Step 3 — Apply the Logarithm Identity** $\log_{10}(a^2) = 2\log_{10}(a)$: $$L_{(dB)} = 10 \left[2 \log_{10}(4\pi d) - 2 \log_{10}(\lambda)\right]$$ $$L_{(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(4\pi d) - 20 \log_{10}(\lambda)$$ **Step 4 — Substitute $\lambda = c/f$:** $$L_{(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(4\pi d) - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{c}{f}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Split the Second Logarithm:** $$L_{(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(4\pi d) - 20 \log_{10}(c) + 20 \log_{10}(f)$$ **Step 6 — Convert to Convenient Units (km and MHz):** When distance $d$ is in km and frequency $f$ is in MHz, the constants collapse to give the **engineering-friendly form**: $$FSPL_{(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$$ > **Logic Note:** The constant **32.44** originates from $20\log_{10}(4\pi \times 10^{3} \times 3 \times 10^{8}/10^{6})$. Verify mentally during exam prep. **Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical):** *Problem:* Compute the free space path loss for $d = 10$ km and $f = 100$ MHz. **Step 7 — Plug in Values:** $$FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(10) + 20 \log_{10}(100) + 32.44$$ **Step 8 — Evaluate Each Logarithm:** $$20 \log_{10}(10) = 20 \times 1 = 20 \; \text{dB}$$ $$20 \log_{10}(100) = 20 \times 2 = 40 \; \text{dB}$$ **Step 9 — Sum:** $$FSPL = 20 + 40 + 32.44$$ $$\boxed{FSPL = 92.44 \; \text{dB}}$$ ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Radio Wave Propagation Modes | Feature | **Ground Wave** | **Sky Wave (Ionospheric)** | **Line-of-Sight (LOS)** | |---|---|---|---| | **Operating Frequencies** | VLF, LF, MF (up to ~2 MHz) | HF (3 – 30 MHz) | VHF, UHF, SHF, EHF (>30 MHz) | | **Mechanism** | Wave hugs Earth's curvature via diffraction & ground conductivity | Wave refracts/bounces off ionized ionosphere layers (D, E, F, F2) | Direct straight-line travel from Tx to Rx antenna | | **Range** | Up to ~1000 km over land/sea | Up to ~4000 km per hop; multi-hop for global | Limited by horizon + antenna height (typically <100 km terrestrial) | | **Key Limitation** | High attenuation over dry terrain; unusable at HF+ | Ionospheric variability (sunspots, day/night) | Requires clear Fresnel zone; blocked by terrain | | **Typical Application** | AM broadcast, maritime, navigation | Shortwave broadcast, HAM radio, long-distance military | FM radio, TV, cellular, Wi-Fi, satellite | | **Antenna Type** | Vertical monopole (large) | Horizontal/vertical dipoles, rhombic | Yagi-Uda, parabolic dish, patch | | **KTU 2024 Weightage** | Medium (theory) | **High** (numerical & conceptual) | **High** (most modern systems) | > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The choice of propagation mode is dictated by **frequency** — a direct consequence of the interaction between wavelength and Earth's atmospheric/ionospheric structure. This is a guaranteed KTU conceptual question. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Electromagnetic Spectrum Classification for Radio Waves The following block diagram maps the ITU-standard radio frequency band hierarchy used in data communication. ```mermaid graph TD A[RADIO WAVES
3 Hz to 300 GHz] --> B[VLF + LF + MF
3 kHz to 3 MHz] A --> C[HF BAND
3 MHz to 30 MHz] A --> D[VHF BAND
30 MHz to 300 MHz] A --> E[UHF BAND
300 MHz to 3 GHz] A --> F[SHF BAND
3 GHz to 30 GHz] A --> G[EHF BAND
30 GHz to 300 GHz] B --> B1[Ground Wave
AM Broadcast, Navigation] C --> C1[Sky Wave
Shortwave, HAM Radio] D --> D1[Line of Sight
FM Radio, TV, Aviation] E --> E1[Line of Sight
Mobile 4G, Wi-Fi, GPS] F --> F1[Line of Sight
Satellite, Radar, 5G] G --> G1[Line of Sight
mmWave 5G, Radio Astronomy] ``` ### 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Radio Communication System This diagram traces the signal flow from information source to destination in a typical unguided radio link. ```mermaid graph LR src[Information Source
Voice, Data, Video] --> mod[Modulator
AM or FM or PM] mod --> pa[Power Amplifier
Boosts RF Signal] pa --> txant[Transmit Antenna
Radiates EM Wave] txant --> channel[Channel
Free Space, Ionosphere, Ground] channel --> rxant[Receive Antenna
Intercepts Wave] rxant --> lna[Low Noise Amplifier] lna --> demod[Demodulator
Recovers Baseband] demod --> dest[Destination Device
Speaker, Display, CPU] ``` ### 4.3 Propagation Mode Decision Flow This decision flow illustrates how the operating frequency selects the appropriate propagation mode. ```mermaid graph TD start[Start: Choose Operating Frequency] --> q1{Frequency less than 3 MHz?} q1 -- Yes --> gw[GROUND WAVE
VLF, LF, MF] q1 -- No --> q2{Frequency between 3 and 30 MHz?} q2 -- Yes --> sw[SKY WAVE
HF Ionosphere] q2 -- No --> los[LINE OF SIGHT
VHF, UHF, SHF, EHF] gw --> app1[Use Vertical Monopole Antenna] sw --> app2[Use Dipole or Rhombic Antenna] los --> app3[Use Yagi, Dish, or Patch Antenna] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Interpretation Note for Students:** Each path in the Mermaid diagrams represents a real engineering decision made by a radio system designer. The "channel" block in the second diagram is the only unguided component — everything else is conventional electronic hardware. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions are concise, definition-oriented, and expect a 3–4 sentence answer supported by a key formula or diagram. --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define unguided media. List any two advantages of radio wave propagation over guided media.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** *Unguided media is a transmission medium in which data-carrying electromagnetic waves are broadcast through the atmosphere or free space without any physical conductor.* *Two advantages of radio wave propagation:* 1. **Mobility:** Users can communicate on the move (mobile phones, vehicles, ships) without physical cables. 2. **Long-distance coverage:** Sky-wave and satellite links can span continents and oceans, far beyond the reach of copper or fiber. 3. *(Bonus point)* Cost-effective for sparsely populated areas where laying cable is uneconomical. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is the wavelength of a radio signal at 1 GHz? State the formula used.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** *Formula:* $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ *Substitution:* $$\lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \; \text{m/s}}{1 \times 10^{9} \; \text{Hz}} = 0.3 \; \text{m}$$ $$\boxed{\lambda = 0.3 \; \text{meters} = 30 \; \text{cm}}$$ This explains why UHF antennas for 1 GHz systems (such as GPS L1) are typically a few centimeters in length. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions carry 14 marks split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each** (e.g., part (a) and part (b)). You must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q.A.** **(a)** Explain the three modes of radio wave propagation. Compare their frequency ranges and typical applications. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The three modes of radio wave propagation are: 1. **Ground Wave (Surface Wave) Propagation:** - Operates in the **VLF, LF, and MF** bands (3 kHz – 3 MHz). - The wave follows the curvature of the Earth due to diffraction and the conductive properties of the ground. - **Applications:** AM long-wave and medium-wave broadcasting, maritime MF communication, and submarine VLF communication. - *Limitation:* Severe attenuation over dry, rocky terrain; range reduces drastically at higher frequencies. 2. **Sky Wave (Ionospheric) Propagation:** - Operates in the **HF** band (3 – 30 MHz). - The wave is refracted back to Earth by ionized layers of the upper atmosphere (D, E, F1, F2). - **Applications:** Shortwave international broadcast, amateur (HAM) radio, military over-the-horizon radar (OTHR). - *Limitation:* Highly variable with solar activity (sunspot cycle) and time of day (D-layer absorption during daytime). 3. **Line-of-Sight (LOS) Propagation:** - Operates in **VHF, UHF, SHF, and EHF** bands (30 MHz – 300 GHz). - The wave travels in a straight line from the transmit antenna to the receive antenna. - **Applications:** FM radio, terrestrial TV, cellular mobile networks (2G/3G/4G/5G), Wi-Fi, satellite communication, GPS, radar. - *Limitation:* Limited by the radio horizon; requires unobstructed Fresnel zone. *(Summary table on board: Frequency vs. Range vs. Application — 2 marks)* --- **Q.A. (b)** A transmitter radiates **50 W** of power at **900 MHz** with an antenna gain of **10 dB**. The receiver has an antenna gain of **3 dB** and is located **25 km** away. Calculate the received power using the Friis transmission equation. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 — Convert dB gains to linear scale:** $$G_t = 10^{10/10} = 10^{1} = 10$$ $$G_r = 10^{3/10} \approx 1.995 \approx 2$$ **Step 2 — Compute the Wavelength:** $$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{900 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{1}{3} \; \text{m} \approx 0.333 \; \text{m}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the Friis Transmission Equation:** $$P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \lambda^2}{(4\pi d)^2}$$ **Step 4 — Substitute Numerical Values:** $$P_r = \frac{50 \times 10 \times 2 \times (0.333)^2}{(4\pi \times 25 \times 10^{3})^2}$$ **Step 5 — Simplify Numerator:** $$50 \times 10 \times 2 \times 0.111 = 111.0 \; \text{W} \cdot \text{m}^2$$ **Step 6 — Simplify Denominator:** $$(4\pi \times 25000)^2 = (314159.27)^2 \approx 9.87 \times 10^{10}$$ **Step 7 — Final Calculation:** $$P_r \approx \frac{111.0}{9.87 \times 10^{10}} \approx 1.12 \times 10^{-9} \; \text{W}$$ $$\boxed{P_r \approx 1.12 \; \text{nW}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q.B. (a)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the electromagnetic spectrum and the position of radio waves within it. List the ITU frequency bands. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The electromagnetic (EM) spectrum is the continuum of all possible electromagnetic radiation, ordered by frequency/wavelength. From lowest to highest frequency: **Radio Waves** (3 Hz – 300 GHz) → **Microwaves** → **Infrared** → **Visible Light** → **Ultraviolet** → **X-rays** → **Gamma rays** **ITU Radio Frequency Bands:** | Band | Frequency | Wavelength | |---|---|---| | VLF | 3 – 30 kHz | 100 – 10 km | | LF | 30 – 300 kHz | 10 – 1 km | | MF | 300 kHz – 3 MHz | 1 km – 100 m | | HF | 3 – 30 MHz | 100 – 10 m | | VHF | 30 – 300 MHz | 10 – 1 m | | UHF | 300 MHz – 3 GHz | 1 m – 100 mm | | SHF | 3 – 30 GHz | 100 – 10 mm | | EHF | 30 – 300 GHz | 10 – 1 mm | *Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the spectrum structure.* (Mention the diagram in the exam answer — examiners reward visualization.) --- **Q.B. (b)** Derive the Free Space Path Loss formula and compute the FSPL (in dB) for a **5 km** link operating at **2.4 GHz**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Derivation (Recap from Section 3.2):** Starting from Friis equation and assuming unity gains: $$FSPL_{(dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$$ where $d$ is in **km** and $f$ is in **MHz**. **Numerical Computation:** **Step 1 — Identify Inputs:** $$d = 5 \; \text{km}, \quad f = 2.4 \; \text{GHz} = 2400 \; \text{MHz}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the Formula:** $$FSPL = 20 \log_{10}(5) + 20 \log_{10}(2400) + 32.44$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the Logarithms:** $$20 \log_{10}(5) = 20 \times 0.69897 \approx 13.98 \; \text{dB}$$ $$20 \log_{10}(2400) = 20 \times 3.3802 \approx 67.60 \; \text{dB}$$ **Step 4 — Sum:** $$FSPL = 13.98 + 67.60 + 32.44$$ $$\boxed{FSPL \approx 114.02 \; \text{dB}}$$ **Interpretation:** This is why Wi-Fi (2.4 GHz) signals are weak beyond ~50 m indoors — the path loss exceeds 100 dB quickly. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:** > > 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** if you mix up km/MHz versus m/Hz in the FSPL formula. Always state the units you are using before substituting. > 2. **dB vs. Linear Confusion:** Antenna gains in the problem may be given in dBi or dBd — convert to linear scale using $10^{G_{dB}/10}$ before plugging into Friis equation. > 3. **Propagation Mode Blunder:** Many students wrongly state that *all* radio waves travel by LOS. Always qualify your answer with the frequency range. > 4. **Missing Diagram Marks:** A question asking for "neat diagram" without one loses **at least 1–2 marks**. Always sketch the propagation mode diagram (Section 4.3) for full credit. > 5. **Skipping Units in Final Answer:** Writing $P_r = 1.12$ without specifying nW or dBm loses 0.5 mark. KTU examiners are strict on dimensional consistency. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Unguided media** transmits signals through free space; **radio waves** are the lowest-frequency subset (3 Hz – 300 GHz). - The cardinal relation is $c = f\lambda$ with $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. - **Three propagation modes:** Ground wave (VLF/LF/MF), Sky wave (HF), Line-of-sight (VHF+). - **HF sky wave** is the only mode enabling intercontinental communication without satellites. - **Friis equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \lambda^2 / (4\pi d)^2$ — valid in the far-field region. - **FSPL formula (km, MHz):** $20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ dB. - **Intrinsic impedance of free space:** $\eta_0 \approx 377 \; \Omega$. - Path loss **increases by 6 dB** every time distance or frequency is **doubled** (from the $20\log$ form). - **Antenna size** is typically $\lambda/4$ (monopole) or $\lambda/2$ (dipole) — smaller at higher frequencies. - **ITU bands:** VLF, LF, MF, HF, VHF, UHF, SHF, EHF — memorize the boundaries. - **Modern applications:** Cellular (UHF/SHF), Wi-Fi (2.4/5/6 GHz UHF/SHF), GPS (1.575 GHz UHF), 5G mmWave (EHF). - **Kerala-specific relevance:** VHF/UHF towers dot the Western Ghats for FM/TV/cellular coverage; HF amateur operators in Kerala regularly exploit sky-wave for DX (long-distance) contacts during equinox seasons.

topics.title: Terrestrial Microwave
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Terrestrial Microwave

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
**Terrestrial Microwave Communication** is a point-to-point line-of-sight (LOS) wireless transmission technology that uses highly directional parabolic antennas operating in the **microwave frequency band (typically 1 GHz to 300 GHz)** to relay electromagnetic signals between fixed ground-based stations, repeaters, and towers. It serves as a backbone medium in modern telecommunication networks, providing high-capacity, long-distance digital and analog data transfer without the need for physical cables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme - Module 1):** Terrestrial Microwave falls under the "Guided and Unguided Media" portion of the syllabus. Students must understand frequency allocations, antenna principles, path loss behaviour, and the role of repeaters.

### Key Frequency Bands Used

| Band Designation | Frequency Range | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|
| L-Band | **1 – 2 GHz** | Mobile, GPS, telemetry |
| S-Band | **2 – 4 GHz** | Radar, satellite uplinks, microwave ovens |
| C-Band | **4 – 8 GHz** | Long-haul terrestrial microwave, satellite TV |
| X-Band | **8 – 12 GHz** | Radar, military comms |
| Ku-Band | **12 – 18 GHz** | DBS-TV, VSAT |
| K-Band | **18 – 27 GHz** | Radar, experimental |
| Ka-Band | **27 – 40 GHz** | High-capacity 5G backhaul |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are standing on top of a hill with a powerful flashlight, trying to signal your friend on another hill **15 km away**. You cannot see them clearly because of haze, but if both of you use **focused, high-power flashlights aimed precisely at each other**, the beam travels in a straight line and reaches the destination. **Terrestrial microwave works in exactly the same way** — it uses tightly focused electromagnetic "beams" shot between tall towers. The hills represent elevated towers, the focused beam represents the directional microwave signal, and the clear straight path between them is the **line-of-sight (LOS)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** Terrestrial microwave = Invisible flashlight beam between two tall towers carrying data, voice, or video.

### Salient Features
- Operates in **licensed frequency bands** (government-allocated).
- Requires **clear line-of-sight (LOS)** — no solid obstacles allowed.
- Uses **highly directional antennas** (parabolic dishes, horn antennas).
- Supports **bandwidths of tens to hundreds of MHz**, enabling multi-Mbps to Gbps data rates.
- Repeaters are placed typically every **30 – 50 km** to overcome path loss.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** Microwave = "**M**ountains **I**n **C**ompass **R**ange **O**f **W**aves" — microwaves need tall, elevated towers because they travel in straight lines and cannot bend over mountains or buildings.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 System Architecture (Block-Level View)
A terrestrial microwave link comprises the following functional blocks:

1. **Multiplexer / Demultiplexer** — combines/separates multiple voice/data channels onto the microwave carrier.
2. **Modulator / Demodulator** — converts baseband signals to passband (and vice versa) using FDM, TDM, or modern QAM/QPSK schemes.
3. **Up-Converter / Down-Converter** — shifts the modulated signal to the microwave RF range (typically 4–12 GHz for traditional long-haul links).
4. **High-Power Amplifier (HPA)** — boosts RF power before transmission.
5. **Waveguide / Coaxial Feed** — connects the transmitter to the antenna with minimal loss.
6. **Parabolic Dish Antenna** — focuses RF energy into a narrow, high-gain beam.
7. **Free-Space Propagation Medium** — the LOS atmospheric path.
8. **Receiver End (Mirror Image)** — low-noise amplifier, down-converter, demodulator, demultiplexer.

### 2.2 Line-of-Sight (LOS) Propagation Criteria
For reliable operation, the path between the transmitting and receiving antennas must satisfy:

- **Fresnel Zone Clearance:** At least **60% of the first Fresnel zone** must be free of obstructions.
- **Maximum Earth Curvature Sag (k = 4/3 effective earth radius):** Path clearance must account for atmospheric refraction.
- **Antenna Heights:** Towers are typically 30–100 m high; rugged terrain may demand taller towers.

### 2.3 Antenna Gain and Effective Aperture
The gain of a parabolic dish antenna is given by:

$$G = \frac{4 \pi A_e}{\lambda^2} = \eta \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^2$$

Where:
- $A_e$ = effective aperture (m²)
- $\lambda$ = wavelength (m)
- $D$ = dish diameter (m)
- $\eta$ = aperture efficiency (typically **0.55 – 0.65** for parabolic dishes)

### 2.4 Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) — Core Equation
When a signal propagates through free space (or near-free-space atmosphere) over a distance $d$, the loss in signal strength is:

$$FSPL = \left(\frac{4 \pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2$$

In decibels, for $d$ in **kilometres** and $f$ in **MHz**, this simplifies to the **KTU-favourite form**:

$$FSPL\,(dB) = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{MHz})$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $FSPL = (4\pi d / \lambda)^2$ | Free-space path loss (linear) | Ratio (unitless) |
| 2 | $FSPL\,(dB) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f)$ | FSPL in dB (d in km, f in MHz) | dB |
| 3 | $G = \eta(\pi D / \lambda)^2$ | Parabolic antenna gain | dBi (numeric) |
| 4 | $G_{dBi} = 10\log_{10}(G)$ | Gain in dB isotropic | dBi |
| 5 | $A_e = G\lambda^2 / 4\pi$ | Effective antenna aperture | m² |
| 6 | $\lambda = c / f$ | Wavelength from frequency | m |
| 7 | $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL$ | Received power (Friis equation) | dBm / dBW |
| 8 | $r_n = 547 \sqrt{n \cdot d_1 \cdot d_2 / f_{GHz} \cdot d}$ | Fresnel zone radius | m |
| 9 | $N_{rep} = D_{total} / D_{hop}$ | Number of repeaters in a link | Integer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants for Memorization:**
> - Speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
> - For $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz, the constant **32.44** is used. If $d$ is in m, the constant is **−27.55**. If $d$ is in km and $f$ in GHz, the constant is **92.44**.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications
- **Cellular Backhaul:** Connecting cellular towers to the core network (especially in 4G/5G mmWave small cells).
- **Long-Distance Telephony:** Replacing or supplementing fibre in remote or hilly terrain.
- **Broadcast TV Relay:** Point-to-point distribution of broadcast content.
- **Disaster Recovery Networks:** Rapid deployment when fibre is damaged.
- **Railway & Highway Signalling:** Continuous communication along transport corridors.

### 2.7 Common Impairments in Terrestrial Microwave

1. **Attenuation** — signal weakening due to distance, rain, fog, and atmospheric gases.
2. **Free-Space Loss** — fundamental geometric spreading loss.
3. **Multipath Fading** — signal arrives via multiple paths (ground reflection, atmospheric layers), causing constructive/destructive interference.
4. **Rain Attenuation** — significant above **10 GHz** (Ku/Ka bands); raindrops absorb and scatter microwaves.
5. **Refraction & Ducting** — atmospheric layers bend the signal, sometimes causing it to overshoot or fall short of the receiving antenna.
6. **Obstruction Loss** — buildings, trees, hills penetrating the Fresnel zone.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Free-Space Path Loss

We begin with the fundamental Friis transmission equation. Consider a transmitting isotropic radiator (a hypothetical antenna that radiates equally in all directions) with power $P_t$. At a distance $d$, the power is distributed uniformly over the surface of an imaginary sphere of radius $d$.

**Step 1: Power Flux Density at Distance d**
The total radiated power $P_t$ is spread over the surface area of a sphere of radius $d$:

$$S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \quad \text{(W/m}^2\text{)}$$

This is the power flux density (Poynting vector magnitude) at distance $d$.

**Step 2: Effective Aperture of the Receiving Antenna**
A real receiving antenna intercepts a portion of this power. The amount of power captured depends on the antenna's effective aperture $A_e$:

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t \cdot A_e}{4\pi d^2}$$

**Step 3: Substitute Aperture in Terms of Gain**
From antenna theory, the effective aperture is related to gain by:

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

Substituting into the received power expression:

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot G_r \lambda^2}{(4\pi d)^2} = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$

This is the **Friis transmission equation in linear form**.

**Step 4: Isolate the Path Loss Term**
For an isotropic-isotropic link ($G_t = G_r = 1$), the path loss is defined as the ratio $P_t / P_r$:

$$L_p = \frac{P_t}{P_r} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2$$

This is the **Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)** in linear scale.

**Step 5: Convert to Decibels**

$$L_p\,(dB) = 10\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)$$

**Step 6: Substitute $\lambda = c/f$**

$$L_p\,(dB) = 20\log_{10}\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)$$

**Step 7: Evaluate the Constant $4\pi/c$**
$c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. Therefore:

$$\frac{4\pi}{c} = \frac{4\pi}{3 \times 10^8} \approx 4.189 \times 10^{-8}$$

So:

$$L_p\,(dB) = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 20\log_{10}(4.189 \times 10^{-8})$$

The constant term evaluates to approximately **−147.55** when $d$ is in metres and $f$ is in Hz.

**Step 8: Convert Units to km and MHz (KTU Standard)**
To use $d$ in km ($10^3$ m) and $f$ in MHz ($10^6$ Hz):

$$L_p\,(dB) = 20\log_{10}(d_{km} \times 10^3) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz} \times 10^6) + 20\log_{10}(4.189 \times 10^{-8})$$

Expanding each logarithmic term:

$$= 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 60 + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz}) + 120 - 147.55$$

$$= 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$$

$$\boxed{L_p\,(dB) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reasoning Check:** Higher frequency → higher path loss (signals at higher frequency attenuate more). Greater distance → higher path loss. This matches physical intuition.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

**Problem:** A microwave link operates at **6 GHz** with a hop distance of **40 km**. Compute the free-space path loss in dB. If the transmit power is **+30 dBm**, transmit antenna gain is **+40 dBi**, and receive antenna gain is **+38 dBi**, calculate the received power. Assume no other losses.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute FSPL**

$$FSPL = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(40) + 20\log_{10}(6000)$$

Compute each term:

$$20\log_{10}(40) = 20 \times 1.6021 = 32.04 \text{ dB}$$

$$20\log_{10}(6000) = 20 \times 3.7782 = 75.56 \text{ dB}$$

Sum:

$$FSPL = 32.44 + 32.04 + 75.56 = 140.04 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2: Compute Received Power (Friis Equation in dB)**

$$P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL$$

$$P_r = 30 + 40 + 38 - 140.04 = -32.04 \text{ dBm}$$

Converting to milliwatts:

$$P_r = 10^{(-32.04/10)} \text{ mW} \approx 0.625 \text{ μW}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Insight for Students:** This is a typical KTU problem. The negative dBm result indicates the received signal is extremely weak — this is why **high-gain antennas** and **low-noise amplifiers (LNAs)** are essential in microwave links.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Performance Trade-offs

| Parameter | Increase Effect on FSPL | Engineering Trade-off |
|---|---|---|
| Distance $d$ ↑ | $20\log_{10}(d)$ ↑ → Higher loss | Need more repeaters, taller towers |
| Frequency $f$ ↑ | $20\log_{10}(f)$ ↑ → Higher loss | More bandwidth available, but shorter hops needed |
| Antenna size $D$ ↑ | $G$ ↑ → Compensates for loss | Larger dishes → Higher wind load, cost |
| Rain rate ↑ | Rain attenuation ↑ | Use lower band (C-band < 8 GHz) for high-rainfall regions |
| Hop count ↑ | Cumulative noise & distortion | More repeaters → Higher cost, more failure points |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Terrestrial Microwave Link

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[Voice Channel 1] --> mux
    src2[Data Channel 2] --> mux
    src3[Video Channel 3] --> mux
    mux[Multiplexer FDM or TDM] --> mod[Modulator QAM or PSK]
    mod --> up[Up Converter to RF]
    up --> hpa[High Power Amplifier]
    hpa --> feed[Waveguide Feed]
    feed --> txAntenna[Tx Parabolic Dish 3m to 30m]
    txAntenna -->|Free Space LOS| prop{Atmospheric Channel 30 to 50 km}
    prop --> rxAntenna[Rx Parabolic Dish High Gain]
    rxAntenna --> lna[Low Noise Amplifier LNA]
    lna --> down[Down Converter to IF]
    down --> demod[Demodulator]
    demod --> demux[Demultiplexer]
    demux --> out1[Recovered Voice]
    demux --> out2[Recovered Data]
    demux --> out3[Recovered Video]
    repeater[Repeater Tower placed every 30 to 50 km] -. Back-to-Back Tx-Rx .-> prop
```

### 4.2 Hop-by-Hop Link Diagram (Long-Haul Microwave Chain)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Station A Originating Terminal] -->|Hop 1| R1[Repeater 1 Intermediate Tower]
    R1 -->|Hop 2| R2[Repeater 2 Intermediate Tower]
    R2 -->|Hop 3| R3[Repeater 3 Intermediate Tower]
    R3 -->|Hop N| B[Station B Destination Terminal]
    cls[Fresnel Zone Clearance >= 60 percent] -. Path Constraint .-> A
    cls -. Path Constraint .-> R1
    cls -. Path Constraint .-> R2
```

### 4.3 Frequency Band Hierarchy (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Terrestrial Microwave Link Design] --> q1{Rainfall Region?}
    q1 -->|Low less than 500 mm per year| q2{Long Haul more than 40 km?}
    q1 -->|High more than 1500 mm per year| ku[Use C-Band 4 to 8 GHz Minimal Rain Fade]
    q2 -->|Yes| cband[Use C-Band 4 to 8 GHz Lowest Path Loss]
    q2 -->|No| kuband[Use Ku-Band 12 to 18 GHz Smaller Dishes]
    ku --> kuband2[Optional Ka-Band 26 to 40 GHz for 5G Backhaul]
    kuband --> kuband2
```

### 4.4 Antenna Geometry (Parabolic Reflector)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    feedPoint[Feed Horn at Focal Point F] -->|Spherical Wavefront| dish[Parabolic Reflector Diameter D Focal Length f]
    dish -->|Collimated Plane Wave| beam[Directional Beam Beamwidth theta]
    beam -->|Hits Target| target[Receiving Antenna 30 to 50 km away]
    dishShape[Shape Equation y squared equals 4fx] -. Geometry .-> dish
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Parabolic?** A parabola has the unique property that **all rays from the focal point reflect parallel to the axis**, creating a tightly collimated beam. This is why parabolic dishes produce the **highest antenna gain per unit area** — a critical property for long-distance microwave links.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Terrestrial Microwave Communication. List any four advantages.**

**Model Answer:**

Terrestrial Microwave Communication is a line-of-sight (LOS) wireless transmission technique that uses **highly directional antennas** to send and receive microwave-frequency signals (typically 1 GHz – 40 GHz) between fixed ground stations, towers, and repeaters. It forms the wireless backbone for telephone, data, and video networks.

**Four Advantages:**
1. **No right-of-way required** — unlike cables, it can cross mountains, rivers, and restricted terrain.
2. **High data rates** — supports tens to hundreds of Mbps per channel.
3. **Cost-effective for long distances** — cheaper than laying fibre in remote areas.
4. **Rapid deployment** — towers and dishes can be installed within weeks, not months.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of repeaters in a terrestrial microwave link. Why is antenna height critical?**

**Model Answer:**

**Role of Repeaters:** As microwave signals travel through the atmosphere, they suffer **free-space path loss**, **atmospheric attenuation**, and **terrain obstruction**. Over long distances, the signal becomes too weak to be detected reliably. A **repeater** is a tower-mounted station placed at intervals of typically 30 – 50 km that:
- Receives the weak signal via a high-gain dish.
- Amplifies it (using a low-noise amplifier).
- Re-transmits it on the same frequency (back-to-back) or on a different frequency to the next hop.

Repeaters effectively "refresh" the signal, allowing the link to span hundreds or thousands of kilometres.

**Why Antenna Height is Critical:** Microwaves travel in **straight lines** (quasi-optical propagation). For LOS to be maintained, the antennas must be elevated high enough to clear:
- The **Earth's curvature** (visible horizon).
- **Terrain obstacles** such as buildings, trees, and hills.
- The **Fresnel zone** must remain at least 60% clear of obstructions.

Higher towers = longer LOS distance = fewer repeaters required, reducing cost and complexity.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **KTU Note:** Each Part B question is split into **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**. You must answer **either Question A or Question B** in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### (a) Explain the main components of a terrestrial microwave communication system with the help of a block diagram. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

A terrestrial microwave communication system consists of the following essential components, arranged sequentially from input to output:

1. **Input Source** — Provides the baseband signal: voice, data, or video.

2. **Multiplexer** — Combines multiple baseband channels using **FDM** (Frequency Division Multiplexing) or **TDM** (Time Division Multiplexing) for efficient transmission.

3. **Modulator** — Imposes the baseband signal onto an Intermediate Frequency (IF) carrier using techniques like **AM, FM, PSK, or QAM**.

4. **Up-Converter (Mixer + Local Oscillator)** — Translates the IF signal to the microwave RF frequency (e.g., 4 GHz, 6 GHz, 11 GHz).

5. **High-Power Amplifier (HPA)** — Boosts the RF signal power to a level suitable for long-distance transmission (typically +30 to +40 dBm).

6. **Waveguide / Coaxial Feed** — Carries the RF signal from the equipment room to the antenna with minimal loss. At microwave frequencies, **waveguides** (hollow metallic tubes) are preferred over coaxial cables to reduce losses.

7. **Transmitting Antenna (Parabolic Dish)** — Converts the guided RF energy into a **highly directional free-space wave**, with gain typically 30 – 50 dBi.

8. **Free-Space Propagation** — The LOS path between transmitter and receiver (atmosphere, with possible effects from rain, fog, refraction).

9. **Receiving Antenna** — Captures the incoming wave and feeds it to the receiver. Gain similar to the Tx antenna.

10. **Low-Noise Amplifier (LNA)** — The first stage in the receiver; amplifies the weak incoming signal while adding minimal noise.

11. **Down-Converter** — Translates the received RF back to IF for processing.

12. **Demodulator** — Recovers the baseband signal from the IF carrier.

13. **Demultiplexer** — Separates the multiplexed channels back into individual voice/data/video streams.

14. **Output Sinks** — Deliver the recovered signals to their respective destinations.

**Block Diagram Flow:**
`Source → MUX → Mod → Up-Conv → HPA → Waveguide → Tx Antenna → [Free Space] → Rx Antenna → LNA → Down-Conv → Demod → DEMUX → Sink`

---

#### (b) A microwave link operating at 6 GHz has a hop distance of 50 km. Calculate:
(i) The free-space path loss in dB.
(ii) The received power if $P_t = +35$ dBm, $G_t = +42$ dBi, $G_r = +38$ dBi.
(iii) The wavelength of the signal.
(7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Free-Space Path Loss:**

$$FSPL\,(dB) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$$

Substituting $d = 50$ km, $f = 6000$ MHz:

$$20\log_{10}(50) = 20 \times 1.6990 = 33.98 \text{ dB}$$

$$20\log_{10}(6000) = 20 \times 3.7782 = 75.56 \text{ dB}$$

$$FSPL = 32.44 + 33.98 + 75.56 = \boxed{141.98 \text{ dB}}$$

**(ii) Received Power:**

$$P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL$$

$$P_r = 35 + 42 + 38 - 141.98 = \boxed{-26.98 \text{ dBm}}$$

Converting to watts:

$$P_r = 10^{(-26.98/10)} \text{ mW} \approx 2.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mW} = 2 \text{ μW}$$

**(iii) Wavelength:**

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}}{6 \times 10^9 \text{ Hz}} = \boxed{0.05 \text{ m} = 5 \text{ cm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** A very common mistake students make is using **32.44 + 20log(d) + 20log(f)** but entering $f$ in Hz instead of MHz, which gives an answer that is **120 dB too high**. Always verify unit consistency **before** writing the final number. Also, do not confuse **dBi** (gain relative to isotropic) with **dBd** (gain relative to a dipole); $dBd = dBi - 2.15$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### (a) Explain the different frequency bands used in terrestrial microwave communication. Discuss the trade-off between frequency, path loss, and rainfall attenuation. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Terrestrial microwave communication utilizes a wide spectrum, broadly divided into the following bands:

**1. C-Band (4 – 8 GHz)**
- Lowest path loss among practical microwave bands.
- Excellent propagation in adverse weather; minimal rain attenuation.
- Used for **long-haul, high-availability links** (40 – 80 km hops).
- Drawback: requires **larger dish antennas** (3 – 5 m diameter) for adequate gain.

**2. Ku-Band (12 – 18 GHz)**
- Moderate path loss.
- **Susceptible to rain fade** above 15 GHz, especially in tropical regions (KTU context: Kerala has high rainfall).
- Allows **smaller dishes** (1 – 1.8 m).
- Widely used for **VSAT, DTH TV, and enterprise links**.

**3. Ka-Band (26 – 40 GHz)**
- High path loss, but **enormous bandwidth** available (multi-GHz).
- Significant rain attenuation — requires **adaptive coding and modulation (ACM)**.
- Used in **5G fronthaul/backhaul** and satellite communications.
- Very small dishes suffice (0.3 – 0.6 m).

**Trade-off Matrix:**

| Frequency | Path Loss | Rain Attenuation | Antenna Size | Available Bandwidth |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| C-Band (4 GHz) | Lowest | Negligible | Large | Limited |
| Ku-Band (12 GHz) | Moderate | Noticeable | Medium | Moderate |
| Ka-Band (28 GHz) | High | Severe | Small | Very High |

**Engineering Trade-off:** Lower frequencies (C-band) are preferred for **long-haul, rain-prone regions** like Kerala. Higher frequencies (Ka-band) are chosen for **short-haul, high-capacity** applications where small cells or rapid deployment matters.

---

#### (b) Derive the expression for free-space path loss starting from the Friis transmission equation. A link at 12 GHz has a free-space loss of 140 dB. Find the corresponding distance. (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:** [See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation. The final result is:]

$$\boxed{FSPL\,(dB) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})}$$

**Numerical Problem:**

Given: $f = 12$ GHz $= 12000$ MHz, $FSPL = 140$ dB. Find $d$.

Substituting into the FSPL equation:

$$140 = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(12000)$$

Computing the frequency term:

$$20\log_{10}(12000) = 20 \times 4.0792 = 81.58 \text{ dB}$$

Rearranging:

$$20\log_{10}(d) = 140 - 32.44 - 81.58 = 25.98 \text{ dB}$$

$$\log_{10}(d) = \frac{25.98}{20} = 1.299$$

$$d = 10^{1.299} = \boxed{19.95 \text{ km} \approx 20 \text{ km}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Q B-b):** In derivation questions, students often skip the **definition of effective aperture** and the **substitution step** where $A_e = G_r \lambda^2 / 4\pi$. Examiners specifically look for these algebraic transitions. **Memorizing only the final formula loses 2–3 marks**; you must show the logical path. Also, remember to **state the value of the speed of light** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s when computing the constant **32.44**.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Terrestrial Microwave (Module 1)**

- ⭐ **Definition:** LOS, point-to-point, microwave-frequency (1–300 GHz) wireless communication using directional antennas.
- ⭐ **Core Formula (must memorize):** $FSPL\,(dB) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$
- ⭐ **Friis Equation:** $P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - FSPL$ (all in dB)
- ⭐ **Antenna Gain:** $G = \eta(\pi D / \lambda)^2$; $\eta \approx 0.55$–$0.65$
- ⭐ **Wavelength:** $\lambda = c/f = 3 \times 10^8 / f$
- ⭐ **Repeater spacing:** typically **30 – 50 km** for C-band links.
- ⭐ **Fresnel zone clearance:** at least **60%** of first zone must be clear.
- ⭐ **Frequency Bands:** C (4–8 GHz) → low loss; Ku (12–18 GHz) → moderate; Ka (26–40 GHz) → high loss, high bandwidth.
- ⭐ **Higher frequency** ⇒ more path loss + more rain attenuation + smaller antenna.
- ⭐ **Path loss increases by 6 dB** when distance doubles (or when frequency doubles).
- ⭐ **Major impairments:** Free-space loss, rain attenuation, multipath fading, refraction, obstruction loss.
- ⭐ **Applications:** Cellular backhaul, long-haul telephony, broadcast TV relay, disaster-recovery links, 5G fronthaul.
- ⭐ **Units to watch:** **dBi** (isotropic) vs **dBd** (dipole); dBi = dBd + 2.15.
- ⭐ **C-band for Kerala's monsoon-prone region** — KTU examiners love this application context.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Exam Strategy:** Always carry the constant **32.44** in your mental "formula bank" along with the conditions (d in km, f in MHz). For 14-mark derivations, present the **logical flow** clearly: Flux Density → Effective Aperture → Friis Equation → FSPL → Decibel Form → Unit Conversion. This structured flow is what examiners reward.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Satellite Microwave
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Satellite Microwave

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Satellite Microwave Communication** is a specialized form of wireless data transmission that utilizes the microwave frequency spectrum (**300 MHz to 300 GHz**) and an artificial Earth-orbiting satellite as a **repeater / relay station** to establish communication links between widely separated terrestrial terminals. The transmitting Earth station uplinks a modulated RF carrier to the satellite, which receives, amplifies, frequency-translates, and re-broadcasts the signal back to one or more receiving Earth stations (downlink).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A satellite acts as a **bent-pipe transponder** — it neither originates nor terminates the user traffic; it simply forwards it between distant points on Earth, effectively overcoming the curvature-of-Earth and line-of-sight (LOS) limitations of terrestrial microwave links.

The International Telecommunication Union (ITU) has allocated specific sub-bands within the microwave region for Fixed Satellite Services (FSS), Mobile Satellite Services (MSS), and Broadcasting Satellite Services (BSS). The most commonly used commercial bands are:
- **L-band (1–2 GHz)** — Mobile satellite (e.g., Inmarsat)
- **S-band (2–4 GHz)** — MSS / NASA / weather radar
- **C-band (4–8 GHz)** — FSS, uplink **6 GHz**, downlink **4 GHz**
- **X-band (8–12 GHz)** — Military / radar
- **Ku-band (12–18 GHz)** — DTH TV, VSAT
- **Ka-band (26–40 GHz)** — Modern broadband (e.g., Starlink, Jupiter-2)
- **V-band (40–75 GHz)** — Future 5G backhaul

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Cosmic Mirror"**
> Imagine standing in a deep valley and trying to communicate with a friend on a distant mountaintop. The Earth's curvature and intervening hills block your view — shouting won't work, and a ground-based radio tower can't see the other peak either. Now imagine a **helicopter hovering at 36,000 km above the valley** carrying a giant signal mirror. You shout (uplink) to the helicopter, it reflects (re-transmits) your message downward, and your friend hears you clearly. The satellite is this "cosmic mirror" — it sees the entire visible Earth from its altitude and rebroadcasts the signal where ground infrastructure cannot reach.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Constant / Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of light (vacuum) | $c$ | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** |
| Geostationary orbital radius | $r_{GEO}$ | **42,164 km** from Earth's center |
| Geostationary altitude | $h_{GEO}$ | **35,786 km** above mean sea level |
| Geostationary orbital period | $T_{GEO}$ | **23 h 56 m 4 s** (1 sidereal day) |
| Earth equatorial radius | $R_E$ | **6,378 km** |
| Boltzmann's constant | $k$ | **1.3806 × 10⁻²³ J/K** = **–228.6 dBW/K/Hz** |
| Minimum slant range (GEO) | $d_{min}$ | **~35,786 km** (at zenith, 0° elevation) |
| Maximum slant range (GEO, 0° elev.) | $d_{max}$ | **~41,754 km** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of a Satellite Link

A satellite communication system comprises three primary segments:

1. **Space Segment** — The satellite bus (structure, power, thermal, attitude control) + the **payload** (transponders, antennas, TWTA/SSPA amplifiers).
2. **Ground Segment** — Earth stations (HUB, VSATs, gateways, TT&C).
3. **User Segment** — End-user terminals (set-top boxes, mobile handsets, broadband modems).

### 2.1.1 Satellite Orbits (Classified by Altitude)

| Orbit Type | Altitude | Period | Round-Trip Delay | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **LEO** (Low Earth Orbit) | 160 – 2,000 km | ~90 min | **~10–40 ms** | Starlink, Iridium, Earth observation |
| **MEO** (Medium Earth Orbit) | 2,000 – 35,786 km | ~2–12 hr | **~50–150 ms** | GPS, O3b mPOWER |
| **GEO** (Geostationary Earth Orbit) | 35,786 km | 23h 56m | **~240–280 ms (one-way)** | DTH TV, VSAT, C-band telephony |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why GEO is special:** A satellite at the correct altitude of **35,786 km** above the equator, orbiting in the same direction and angular speed as Earth's rotation, *appears stationary* to a ground observer. This permits fixed-dish pointing and avoids Doppler-induced frequency shifts.

### 2.1.2 Frequency Translation in a Transponder
The satellite cannot transmit and receive on the same frequency simultaneously (would cause self-oscillation/feedback). Therefore, the **LNA → Frequency Mixer → HPA (TWTA/SSPA)** chain translates the uplink band to the downlink band. The shift is typically:
- **C-band:** Uplink **6 GHz** → Downlink **4 GHz** (Δf ≈ 2225 MHz for 36 MHz transponder bandwidth)
- **Ku-band:** Uplink **14 GHz** → Downlink **12 GHz**
- **Ka-band:** Uplink **30 GHz** → Downlink **20 GHz**

## 2.2 The Link Equation: From Earth to Earth

The **received power** at any point in the link is determined by the **Friis Transmission Equation** combined with system gains/losses:

$$P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{fs} - L_{other} \quad \text{(all in dB)}$$

### 2.2.1 Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL)
For an isotropic radiator, the power density dilutes over a sphere of area $4\pi d^2$. After antenna gains are factored, the one-way path loss is:

$$L_{fs} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$$

In decibel form, the **two KTU-favorite forms** are:

$$L_{fs}\ (\text{dB}) = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}})$$

$$L_{fs}\ (\text{dB}) = 92.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{GHz}}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering intuition:** FSPL grows by **+6 dB every time you double either the distance or the frequency**. This is why a GEO satellite at 36,000 km in Ka-band is enormously more challenging than a Ku-band LEO at 600 km.

### 2.2.2 Antenna Gain
A parabolic dish of effective aperture $A_e$ has gain:

$$G = \frac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^{2}} = \eta \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$$

where $D$ is the dish diameter, $\lambda$ the wavelength, and $\eta$ the **aperture efficiency** (typically 0.55–0.70 for a prime-focus parabola).

### 2.2.3 EIRP (Equivalent Isotropically Radiated Power)
EIRP consolidates the transmitter's RF output and antenna gain into a single figure of merit:

$$EIRP\ (\text{dBW}) = P_t\ (\text{dBW}) + G_t\ (\text{dBi}) - L_{f,\text{feedline}}\ (\text{dB})$$

### 2.2.4 Figure of Merit — G/T
The **receive-side figure of merit** (ground station sensitivity):

$$\frac{G}{T}\ (\text{dB/K}) = G_r\ (\text{dBi}) - 10\log_{10}(T_{\text{sys}})$$

where $T_{\text{sys}}$ is the system noise temperature in Kelvin, combining antenna sky noise, LNA noise, and feed losses.

### 2.2.5 Carrier-to-Noise Ratio
The single most important link-quality metric:

$$\frac{C}{N_0} = EIRP + \frac{G}{T} - L_{fs} - k - L_{\text{other}}$$

where $k$ = **–228.6 dBW/K/Hz** (Boltzmann). With $C/N_0$ in dB·Hz, the in-band C/N for a carrier of bandwidth $B$ is:

$$\frac{C}{N} = \frac{C}{N_0} - 10\log_{10}(B)$$

### 2.2.6 Bit Energy to Noise Spectral Density
Required to achieve a target $P_b$ (BER) on a digital link:

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = \frac{C}{N_0} - 10\log_{10}(R_b)$$

where $R_b$ is the bit rate in bits/second.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula (Linear) | Formula (dB form) | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Free-Space Path Loss | $L_{fs} = (4\pi d / \lambda)^{2}$ | $32.45 + 20\log f_{\text{MHz}} + 20\log d_{\text{km}}$ | dB |
| 2 | Antenna Gain | $G = 4\pi A_e / \lambda^{2}$ | $10\log(4\pi A_e / \lambda^{2})$ | dBi |
| 3 | EIRP | $EIRP = P_t G_t$ | $P_t\ (\text{dBW}) + G_t\ (\text{dBi})$ | dBW |
| 4 | Received Power | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r / L_{fs}$ | $EIRP + G_r - L_{fs}$ | dBW |
| 5 | System Noise Temp. | $T_{sys} = T_{ant} + T_{LNA}$ | — | K |
| 6 | Figure of Merit | $G/T = G_r / T_{sys}$ | $G_r - 10\log T_{sys}$ | dB/K |
| 7 | C/N₀ | $C/N_0 = EIRP + G/T - L - k$ | sum of dB terms | dB·Hz |
| 8 | C/N (in-band) | $C/N = (C/N_0) / B$ | $C/N_0 - 10\log B$ | dB |
| 9 | $E_b/N_0$ | $E_b/N_0 = (C/N_0) / R_b$ | $C/N_0 - 10\log R_b$ | dB |
| 10 | Slant Range (GEO) | $\sqrt{R_E^{2} + r^{2} - 2R_E r \cos \theta}$ | — | km |
| 11 | Round-Trip Delay | $t = 2d / c$ | — | s |
| 12 | Kepler 3rd Law | $T^{2} = (4\pi^{2}/\mu) a^{3}$ | — | s² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In all exam answers, the absolute-value / divisor strokes in formulas like $\lambda^{2}$ or $G/T$ must be written using `\vert` or `\mid`, never the bare pipe `\vert` symbol that breaks Markdown tables.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **VSAT (Very Small Aperture Terminal):** 0.6–2.4 m dishes for bank ATMs, retail POS, rural telephony.
- **DTH Television:** Direct-to-home Ku/Ka broadcasting to millions of receivers.
- **GPS:** MEO constellation providing nanosecond timing and 3-D positioning.
- **Starlink / OneWeb:** LEO mega-constellations delivering <50 ms latency broadband.
- **Disaster Recovery:** When terrestrial fiber/microwave is destroyed, satellite links restore command-and-control within hours.
- **Maritime & Inflight Connectivity:** Coverage where no tower can reach.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Free-Space Path Loss (Friis Equation)

### Step 1 — Start with an isotropic radiator
A point source radiating $P_t$ watts uniformly spreads its energy over a sphere of radius $d$. The power flux density (Poynting vector magnitude) at distance $d$ is:

$$S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^{2}} \quad \text{[W/m}^2\text{]}$$

**Logic:** The sphere's surface area is $4\pi d^{2}$; dividing the total power by this area gives the watts arriving per square meter of wavefront.

### Step 2 — Capture by a receive antenna of effective aperture $A_e$
Power delivered to the receiver's feed is the flux density multiplied by the antenna's effective collecting area:

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t A_e}{4\pi d^{2}}$$

**Logic:** Only the portion of the wavefront intercepted by the antenna's physical area can be delivered to the LNA.

### Step 3 — Introduce antenna gain via the fundamental relation
For any antenna, gain and effective aperture are linked by:

$$G = \frac{4\pi A_e}{\lambda^{2}} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^{2}}{4\pi}$$

**Logic:** A larger aperture collects more energy, but the relationship is wavelength-dependent because a larger dish has a narrower beam and therefore higher directional gain.

### Step 4 — Substitute $A_e$ into the received power equation

$$\begin{aligned}
P_r &= \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^{2}} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^{2}}{4\pi} \\
&= P_t \, G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** Re-arrange the terms; isolate the loss factor.

### Step 5 — Identify the path-loss factor

$$L_{fs} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; P_r = \frac{P_t G_t G_r}{L_{fs}}$$

**Logic:** The factor multiplying $P_t G_t G_r$ is the *ratio* by which radiated power is attenuated by the time it reaches the receiver. Inverting it gives the *gain*; inverting-and-inverting gives the **loss**.

### Step 6 — Convert to dB

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{fs}\ (\text{dB}) &= 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2} \\
&= 20\log_{10}(4\pi) + 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(\lambda)
\end{aligned}$$

**Logic:** $10\log(x^{2}) = 20\log x$.

### Step 7 — Insert the wavelength-to-frequency relation $\lambda = c/f$

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{fs} &= 20\log_{10}(4\pi c) + 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(f) \\
&= 20\log_{10}(4\pi \cdot 3\times 10^{8}) + 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(f)
\end{aligned}$$

When $d$ is in **km** and $f$ is in **MHz** ($f_{\text{MHz}} = f \cdot 10^{-6}$):

$$L_{fs} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$$

**Logic:** The constant 32.45 dB emerges from $20\log_{10}(4\pi \cdot 3 \times 10^{8}) - 60 = 32.44\ldots \approx 32.45$, where the –60 dB accounts for the unit conversions from metres to km and from Hz to MHz.

## 3.2 Derivation: Slant Range to a GEO Satellite

### Step 1 — Use the law of cosines on the Earth-center / Earth-station / Satellite triangle
The slant range $d$ is the side opposite the angle $\theta$ (Earth-central angle) in a triangle with sides $R_E$ (Earth's radius) and $r$ (orbital radius), where $r = R_E + h$:

$$d^{2} = R_E^{2} + r^{2} - 2 R_E r \cos \theta$$

### Step 2 — When the satellite is at the zenith ($\theta = 0°$, $\cos\theta = 1$)

$$d_{min} = r - R_E = h = 35{,}786\ \text{km}$$

### Step 3 — When the elevation angle $E$ is 0° (just above horizon)
The Earth-central angle becomes $\theta = 90° - E$ for the satellite triangle, but the standard relationship for the 0°-elevation slant range is:

$$\begin{aligned}
d_{max} &= \sqrt{R_E^{2} + r^{2} - 2 R_E r \cos(90° + E)} \\
&\text{At } E=0°: \cos(90°) = 0 \\
d_{max} &= \sqrt{R_E^{2} + r^{2}} \approx 41{,}754\ \text{km}
\end{aligned}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Round-Trip Propagation Delay for GEO

$$t_{RT} = \frac{2 d_{sl}}{c}$$

**Step-by-step numerical evaluation for a satellite at zenith (35,786 km):**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{RT} &= \frac{2 \times 35{,}786{,}000\ \text{m}}{3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}} \\
&= \frac{71{,}572{,}000}{300{,}000{,}000} \\
&= 0.2386\ \text{s} \\
&\approx 238.6\ \text{ms}
\end{aligned}$$

For a satellite at 0° elevation (slant range ≈ 41,754 km):

$$t_{RT} = \frac{2 \times 41{,}754{,}000}{3 \times 10^{8}} \approx 278.4\ \text{ms}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world consequence:** The **~240 ms GEO one-way delay** is the reason echo cancellers and TCP-window tuning are mandatory in satellite data networks. LEO constellations reduce this to <50 ms, making VoIP and gaming feasible.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-style Link Budget

**Problem:** A Ku-band VSAT uplink operates at **14 GHz** with $P_t = 20$ W, transmit dish diameter 1.8 m, efficiency 0.6. Slant range = 37,500 km. Receive Earth station G/T = 30 dB/K. Find EIRP, FSPL, and C/N₀.

### Step 1 — Transmit gain

$$\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{14 \times 10^{9}} = 0.02143\ \text{m} \\
G_t &= 10\log_{10}\!\left[\eta \left(\frac{\pi D}{\lambda}\right)^{2}\right] \\
&= 10\log_{10}\!\left[0.6 \cdot \left(\frac{\pi \cdot 1.8}{0.02143}\right)^{2}\right] \\
&= 10\log_{10}\!\left[0.6 \cdot (263.9)^{2}\right] \\
&= 10\log_{10}(41{,}800) \approx 46.2\ \text{dBi}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — EIRP

$$P_t = 10\log_{10}(20) = 13.01\ \text{dBW}$$
$$EIRP = 13.01 + 46.2 = 59.21\ \text{dBW}$$

### Step 3 — Free-Space Path Loss

$$L_{fs} = 92.45 + 20\log_{10}(14) + 20\log_{10}(37{,}500)$$
$$= 92.45 + 22.92 + 91.48 = 206.85\ \text{dB}$$

### Step 4 — Carrier-to-Noise Density
