$$C/N_0 = EIRP + G/T - L_{fs} - k$$
$$= 59.21 + 30 - 206.85 - (-228.6) = 110.96\ \text{dB·Hz}$$

### Step 5 — C/N for a 36 MHz transponder

$$C/N = 110.96 - 10\log_{10}(36 \times 10^{6}) = 110.96 - 75.56 = 35.40\ \text{dB}$$

> [!TIP]
> A $C/N \geq 10$ dB is typically required for QPSK with FEC at $P_b = 10^{-6}$, so the link has a healthy **25.4 dB margin** — excellent for a KTU numerical.

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix: GEO vs MEO vs LEO

| Parameter | GEO | MEO | LEO |
|---|---|---|---|
| Altitude | 35,786 km | 5,000–20,000 km | 160–2,000 km |
| Orbital Period | 24 h | 2–12 h | ~90 min |
| One-way Latency | 240–280 ms | 50–150 ms | 10–40 ms |
| Doppler Effect | Negligible | Small | Severe (kHz shift) |
| Hand-off | None | Occasional | Constant |
| Coverage per sat | ~1/3 Earth | Several regions | Small footprint |
| Number of sats needed for global | 3 | 6–12 | 50–4,000+ |
| Launch cost per sat | High (heavy) | Medium | Low-medium |
| Best suited for | TV, VSAT, trunk | Navigation, regional | Broadband, IoT |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Satellite Communication Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Earth Station A\nUplink Tx 14 GHz] -->|RF Carrier| B[Satellite Transponder\nLNA down to Mixer up to HPA]
    B -->|Downlink 12 GHz| C[Earth Station B\nReceive VSAT]
    C --> D[Baseband Processor\nDemodulator Decoder]
    B --> E[TT and C Subsystem\nTelemetry Tracking Command]
    F[Ground Control Station\nTT and C Earth Station] -->|Command 12 GHz| B
    B -->|Telemetry 4 GHz| F
    G[Power Subsystem\nSolar Arrays Batteries] --> B
    H[ADCS\nAttitude Determination and Control] --> B
    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3
    style B fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#cc6600
    style C fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0050b3

    style F fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#cca300
    style G fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000
    style H fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000
```

## 4.2 Bent-Pipe Transponder Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Uplink Antenna\nReceive Beam] --> A2[BPF\nBandpass Filter]
    A2 --> A3[LNA\nLow Noise Amplifier]
    A3 --> A4[Frequency Mixer\nLO 2225 MHz]
    A4 --> A5[IF Amplifier]
    A5 --> A6[Input Mux\nDemultiplexer]
    A6 --> A7[TWTA\nTravelling Wave Tube Amp]
    A7 --> A8[Output Mux\nMultiplexer]
    A8 --> A9[HPF\nHigh Power Filter]
    A9 --> A10[Downlink Antenna\nTransmit Beam]
    style A1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style A3 fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#006600
    style A4 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style A7 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#660000
    style A10 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
```

## 4.3 Satellite Orbit Classification Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Artificial Satellite] --> Q1{Altitude Range?}
    Q1 -->|160 to 2000 km| LEO[LEO\nLow Earth Orbit]
    Q1 -->|2000 to 35786 km| MEO[MEO\nMedium Earth Orbit]
    Q1 -->|35786 km equatorial| GEO[GEO\nGeostationary Earth Orbit]
    LEO --> L1[Period 90 min]
    LEO --> L2[Latency 10 to 40 ms]
    LEO --> L3[Constellation 50 to 4000 sats]
    MEO --> M1[Period 2 to 12 hr]
    MEO --> M2[Latency 50 to 150 ms]
    MEO --> M3[Use Navigation GPS GLONASS]
    GEO --> G1[Period 23h 56m sidereal]
    GEO --> G2[Latency 240 to 280 ms]
    GEO --> G3[Use DTH VSAT trunk]
    style LEO fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style MEO fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#006600
    style GEO fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#660000
```

## 4.4 Link Budget Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S0[Input Parameters\nPt Gt Gr d f k Tsys B Rb] --> S1[Compute Wavelength\nlambda equals c over f]
    S1 --> S2[Compute Free Space Path Loss\nLfs equals 32.45 plus 20 log f plus 20 log d]
    S2 --> S3[Compute EIRP\nEIRP equals Pt plus Gt]
    S3 --> S4[Compute G over T\nGt over T equals Gr minus 10 log Tsys]
    S4 --> S5[Compute C over N0\nC over N0 equals EIRP plus G over T minus Lfs minus k]
    S5 --> S6[Compute C over N\nC over N equals C over N0 minus 10 log B]
    S6 --> S7[Compute Eb over N0\nEb over N0 equals C over N0 minus 10 log Rb]
    S7 --> S8[Output Quality Metrics\nBER Margin Fade Margin]
    style S0 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style S2 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style S5 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#660000
    style S8 fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#006600
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. Define the term "Satellite Microwave Communication." List the three major segments of a satellite communication system. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Satellite Microwave Communication is a method of transmitting information over long distances using modulated microwave carriers (typically 1–40 GHz) relayed by an artificial Earth-orbiting satellite. The satellite functions as a **bent-pipe transponder**, receiving the uplink signal, amplifying and frequency-translating it, and re-broadcasting it on the downlink.

**Three segments (1 mark):**
1. **Space Segment** — satellite bus and payload
2. **Ground Segment** — Earth stations, gateways, TT&C
3. **User Segment** — end-user terminals (VSAT, handsets, STBs)

---

### Q2. What is meant by "Free-Space Path Loss"? Why is it a major concern in GEO satellite links? `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Free-Space Path Loss (FSPL) is the attenuation experienced by an electromagnetic wave as it propagates through a vacuum or ideal medium with no absorption, scattering, or reflection. It arises purely from the geometric spreading of the wavefront and is given by:

$$L_{fs} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2} = 32.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}})$$

**Why critical in GEO (1.5 marks):** A GEO satellite is **35,786 km** above Earth. At 14 GHz, the resulting FSPL exceeds **206 dB** — meaning the transmitted power is attenuated by a factor of ~10²⁰. This forces the use of **high-gain directional antennas**, **high-power amplifiers (TWTAs)**, and **sensitive cryogenically cooled LNAs** to recover the signal.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Module carries one 14-mark question with internal choice between Q(A) and Q(B). Both choices carry sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The student attempts **either** A **or** B.

---

### ✦ CHOICE A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`

#### (a) [7 marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the transponder of a communication satellite. Discuss the role of the TWTA in the downlink chain.

**Model Answer:**

A **transponder** is the heart of a satellite's communication payload. It is the on-board RF repeater that performs the receive → amplify → frequency-translate → re-transmit sequence for one or more carriers.

**Block diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Uplink Antenna] --> A2[BPF]
    A2 --> A3[LNA]
    A3 --> A4[Mixer with Local Oscillator]
    A4 --> A5[IF Amplifier]
    A5 --> A6[Input Multiplexer]
    A6 --> A7[TWTA High Power Amp]
    A7 --> A8[Output Multiplexer]
    A8 --> A9[Downlink Antenna]
    style A3 fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#006600
    style A4 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style A7 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#660000
```

**Working (2 marks):** The uplink signal is captured by the antenna, filtered to reject out-of-band noise, and amplified by the **LNA** with minimum noise figure (typically 1–2 dB). The local oscillator (e.g., 2225 MHz for C-band) beats with the incoming signal in the mixer, producing a translated IF. The signal is then channelized by the **input demultiplexer** into individual transponder channels (e.g., 36 MHz each).

**Role of TWTA (2 marks):** The **Travelling Wave Tube Amplifier** is the workhorse high-power amplifier of the satellite. It boosts the IF-modulated signal to watt-level (typically 20–250 W) outputs for the downlink. TWTAs are preferred over solid-state SSPAs at high frequencies (C, Ku, Ka) because of their **wide bandwidth, high power efficiency, and high linearity**, though they require careful linearity compensation to minimize intermodulation distortion.

---

#### (b) [7 marks] An Earth station transmits at 6 GHz with EIRP = 80 dBW. The slant range to a GEO satellite is 38,000 km. If the satellite's G/T is –5 dB/K, calculate: (i) Free-space path loss, (ii) C/N₀ of the uplink, (iii) C/N for a 36 MHz transponder bandwidth.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $f = 6$ GHz, $EIRP = 80$ dBW, $d = 38{,}000$ km, $G/T = -5$ dB/K, $B = 36$ MHz.

**(i) Free-Space Path Loss:**

$$L_{fs} = 92.45 + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{GHz}}) + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}})$$

$$\begin{aligned}
L_{fs} &= 92.45 + 20\log_{10}(6) + 20\log_{10}(38{,}000) \\
&= 92.45 + 15.56 + 91.60 \\
&= 199.61\ \text{dB}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) C/N₀:**

$$C/N_0 = EIRP + G/T - L_{fs} - k$$

$$= 80 + (-5) - 199.61 - (-228.6) = 80 - 5 - 199.61 + 228.6 = 103.99\ \text{dB·Hz}$$

**(iii) C/N for 36 MHz bandwidth:**

$$C/N = C/N_0 - 10\log_{10}(B) = 103.99 - 10\log_{10}(36 \times 10^{6})$$

$$10\log_{10}(36 \times 10^{6}) = 10\log_{10}(36) + 60 = 15.56 + 60 = 75.56\ \text{dB}$$

$$C/N = 103.99 - 75.56 = 28.43\ \text{dB}$$

**Final Results:** $L_{fs} = 199.61$ dB, $C/N_0 = 103.99$ dB·Hz, $C/N = 28.43$ dB.

---

### ✦ CHOICE B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`

#### (a) [7 marks] Compare and contrast LEO, MEO, and GEO satellite orbits with respect to altitude, latency, and number of satellites required for global coverage.

**Model Answer (Tabular — 5 marks + 2 marks explanation):**

| Parameter | LEO | MEO | GEO |
|---|---|---|---|
| Altitude | 160–2,000 km | 5,000–20,000 km | 35,786 km |
| Orbital Period | ~90 min | 2–12 h | 23h 56m |
| One-way Latency | 10–40 ms | 50–150 ms | 240–280 ms |
| Satellites for global coverage | 50–4,000 | 6–12 | 3 |
| Doppler | Severe | Moderate | Negligible |
| Hand-over needed | Constantly | Occasionally | Never |
| Typical use | Starlink, IoT | GPS, O3b | DTH, VSAT |

**Explanation (2 marks):** **LEO** offers minimum latency but requires a *mega-constellation* and complex hand-over algorithms due to rapid motion. **MEO** is a sweet spot for navigation and moderate-throughput services. **GEO** excels for broadcast and fixed-pointing applications because a single satellite can cover ~1/3 of Earth's surface and remains stationary relative to a ground observer, eliminating tracking and Doppler.

---

#### (b) [7 marks] Derive the Free-Space Path Loss equation in dB, clearly identifying the universal constant 32.45 dB.

**Model Solution:**

Starting from the Friis transmission equation in linear form:

$$L_{fs} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^{2}$$

Step 1: Convert to dB.

$$L_{fs}\ (\text{dB}) = 20\log_{10}(4\pi) + 20\log_{10}(d) - 20\log_{10}(\lambda)$$

Step 2: $20\log_{10}(4\pi) = 20 \times 1.0969 = 21.94$ dB.

Step 3: Use $\lambda = c/f$. Taking $d$ in **metres** and $f$ in **Hz**:

$$L_{fs} = 21.94 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{m}}) - 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3\times 10^{8}}{f_{\text{Hz}}}\right)$$

$$= 21.94 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{m}}) - 20\log_{10}(3\times 10^{8}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{Hz}})$$

$$= 21.94 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{m}}) - 169.54 + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{Hz}})$$

Step 4: Convert $d_{\text{m}} \rightarrow d_{\text{km}}$: $20\log_{10}(d_{\text{m}}) = 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 60$

Step 5: Convert $f_{\text{Hz}} \rightarrow f_{\text{MHz}}$: $20\log_{10}(f_{\text{Hz}}) = 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}}) + 120$

Step 6: Substitute and simplify:

$$L_{fs} = 21.94 - 169.54 + 60 + 120 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$$

$$L_{fs} = 32.40 + 20\log_{10}(d_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$$

The **32.45 dB** (rounded) universal constant thus arises from the **speed of light in vacuum and unit-conversion factors** between metres↔km and Hz↔MHz. ✓

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (validated against KTU valuation keys):**
> 1. **Confusing dBW with dBm** — 1 W = 0 dBW = 30 dBm. Mixing the two causes a 30 dB error!
> 2. **Using 32.45 with $f$ in GHz and 92.45 with $f$ in MHz** — always verify the constant matches the unit.
> 3. **Forgetting the –228.6 dB Boltzmann constant sign** in $C/N_0$ — write it explicitly in every line.
> 4. **Omitting unit conversions** for distance (km vs m) — examiners often deduct a full mark.
> 5. **Not drawing the block diagram with arrows** in transponder questions — at least 2 marks are reserved for the figure.
> 6. **GEO orbital altitude (35,786 km) vs radius (42,164 km)** confusion — write both with a clear label.
> 7. **Calculating only uplink or only downlink** in a 14-mark link budget — both directions are expected unless specified.
> 8. **Ignoring the 240 ms delay impact** in GEO latency questions — always mention echo cancellation and TCP tuning.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Satellite Microwave**

- ✅ Satellite microwave = use of **microwave carrier (300 MHz – 300 GHz)** + **orbital satellite** as a relay.
- ✅ **Three segments:** Space, Ground, User.
- ✅ **Three orbit classes:** LEO (160–2,000 km), MEO (2,000–35,786 km), GEO (**35,786 km** exactly).
- ✅ GEO orbital period = **23 h 56 m 4 s** (sidereal); **3 satellites** = global coverage (excluding poles).
- ✅ **Bent-pipe transponder** = receive → LNA → mixer → TWTA → re-transmit; performs **frequency translation** (uplink ≠ downlink).
- ✅ Standard frequency pairs: C = 6/4 GHz, Ku = 14/12 GHz, Ka = 30/20 GHz.
- ✅ **FSPL (dB)** = $32.45 + 20\log f_{\text{MHz}} + 20\log d_{\text{km}}$ = $92.45 + 20\log f_{\text{GHz}} + 20\log d_{\text{km}}$.
- ✅ **EIRP (dBW)** = $P_t + G_t$ (subtract feedline loss if any).
- ✅ **G/T (dB/K)** = $G_r - 10\log T_{sys}$; figure of merit of receiving Earth station.
- ✅ **C/N₀ (dB·Hz)** = $EIRP + G/T - L_{fs} - k$; $k$ = –228.6 dBW/K/Hz.
- ✅ **C/N** = $C/N_0 - 10\log B$; **E_b/N_0** = $C/N_0 - 10\log R_b$.
- ✅ **Antenna gain** $G = \eta(\pi D / \lambda)^{2}$; gain ↑ with dish size ↓ with wavelength.
- ✅ **GEO one-way latency** ≈ 240 ms → mandates echo cancellers and TCP tuning.
- ✅ **LEO** = best latency (10–40 ms) but needs hundreds of satellites; **MEO** = GPS sweet spot; **GEO** = broadcast and VSAT gold standard.
- ✅ **VSAT** = 0.6–2.4 m dishes; C-band for FSS, Ku/Ka for DTH and broadband.
- ✅ Link budget problems: **always state units**, always carry **Boltzmann constant –228.6**, always end with $C/N$ vs threshold check.
- ✅ Real numbers to memorize: $c = 3\times10^{8}$ m/s, $R_E = 6378$ km, $h_{GEO} = 35{,}786$ km, $k = 1.38\times10^{-23}$ J/K.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Infrared
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Infrared

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Infrared (IR) communication** is a short-range, line-of-sight or quasi-diffused wireless data transmission technique that uses the **infrared portion of the electromagnetic spectrum** as the carrier signal. In the context of Data Communication, IR is a member of the optical wireless communication family, where information is encoded by modulating the intensity of an infrared light-emitting diode (LED) or laser diode (LD) and detected by a photodiode or phototransistor at the receiver.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 - Transmission Media):**
> Infrared transmission belongs to the category of *unguided (wireless) media*, alongside radio waves, microwaves, and satellite communication. It occupies the electromagnetic spectrum between visible light and microwave frequencies, typically in the wavelength range of **700 nm to 1 mm**, corresponding to frequencies from **300 GHz to 400 THz**.

The Infrared Data Association (**IrDA**) is the governing body that standardized the physical and protocol specifications for short-range point-to-point IR communication, with operational ranges typically between **1 cm and 1 meter** at data rates from **9.6 kbps to 1 Gbps** depending on the standard revision.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Explanation)

Imagine you are standing in a dark room with a flashlight (torch). If you flash it once, the person across the room sees a "blink." If you flash it in a pattern — short, long, short, short — you are essentially transmitting **Morse code**. That, in essence, is how infrared communication works in data networks.

Instead of a human eye decoding the blinks, a **photodiode** acts as the "receiver eye," and a **microcontroller** decodes the on-off pattern back into binary 1s and 0s. Your television remote control is the most familiar real-world example — when you press a button, the LED at the front flashes invisible infrared light at approximately **38 kHz carrier frequency**, modulated with the command code. The TV's IR sensor on the front panel decodes this invisible light pattern.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Intuition Point:** IR communication in data networks is essentially **light-based signaling**, but the light is invisible to the human eye. The sender pulses an IR LED rapidly, and the receiver converts these light pulses back into electrical signals. This is fundamentally different from RF (Radio Frequency) communication, which uses electromagnetic radiation that can pass through walls.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and standard metrics govern infrared data communication:

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$
- **IR wavelength band (ITU-T/IEEE):** $\lambda = 700 \text{ nm to } 1 \text{ mm}$
- **Standard IrDA operating wavelength:** **850 nm to 900 nm** (near-infrared)
- **Carrier frequency for consumer IR remotes:** **38 kHz** (modulated)
- **Typical IrDA link distance:** **0 to 1 meter** (SIR/FIR), up to **3 m** for VFIR extensions
- **Maximum practical data rate (IrDA):** **1 Gbps** (Giga-IR standard)

## 1.4 The Electromagnetic Spectrum Position

Infrared sits in a unique "no-man's land" of the spectrum — too high in frequency for radio applications, but not energetic enough to ionize atoms like X-rays or UV. This positioning gives IR two critical properties: it is **safe for short-range indoor use** and it **does not interfere with RF systems** like Wi-Fi or Bluetooth.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle: The "Why" and "How"

### Why Infrared for Data Communication?

The use of IR is justified by three engineering drivers:

1. **Unlicensed Spectrum:** The IR band is not regulated by telecom authorities (FCC, TRAI, etc.) because it does not propagate beyond room boundaries, eliminating cross-device interference.
2. **High Bandwidth Potential:** Because the carrier frequency is in the hundreds of THz range, enormous modulation bandwidths are theoretically available — supporting multi-gigabit short-range links.
3. **Low Power & Low Cost:** IR LEDs cost a fraction of a cent; photodiodes are similarly cheap. This makes IR economically superior to RF for short-range point-to-point links.

### How Infrared Transmission Works

The operational chain can be broken into four logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Encoding:** Binary data (from a UART, USB, or network layer) is converted into a serial bit stream.
- **Step 2 — Modulation:** The bit stream modulates either the **intensity** of the IR source (Intensity Modulation / IM) or, in advanced systems, the **phase** of a sub-carrier (e.g., 38 kHz for remote controls).
- **Step 3 — Optical Transmission:** The modulated IR light propagates through free space along a line-of-sight (LOS) path or via reflection off indoor surfaces.
- **Step 4 — Detection & Demodulation:** A photodetector at the receiver converts the incident optical power into a proportional photocurrent, which is then amplified and decoded back into bits.

## 2.2 Transmission Modes of Infrared

IR communication supports three distinct propagation modes, each with its own engineering trade-off:

| Mode | Description | Range | Coverage | Typical Use Case |
|------|-------------|-------|----------|------------------|
| **Line-of-Sight (LOS)** | Direct, unobstructed path between Tx and Rx | 1–3 m | Narrow cone (~15°) | IrDA laptop-to-PDA sync |
| **Diffuse (Non-LOS)** | IR reflects off ceiling/walls | 5–10 m | Wide, room-wide | Indoor wireless LANs (early prototypes) |
| **Quasi-Diffuse** | Hybrid; uses hybrid LOS + reflection | 3–5 m | Medium | IrDA room coverage |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** Most KTU questions test whether students can identify the **major limitation of IR — it cannot pass through walls or opaque obstacles**. This is simultaneously a disadvantage (limited coverage) and an advantage (no eavesdropping across rooms, no RF interference with medical equipment in hospitals).

## 2.3 IrDA Protocol Stack (Layered Architecture)

The IrDA standard defines a complete protocol stack analogous to the OSI model. Every KTU answer on IR must mention this stack for full marks:

| IrDA Layer | Function | KTU-Relevant Detail |
|------------|----------|---------------------|
| **IrPHY** (Physical) | Optical Tx/Rx, bit encoding | Uses RZI (Return-to-Zero Inverted) encoding for 9.6 kbps–115.2 kbps |
| **IrLAP** (Link Access Protocol) | Link establishment & reliability | HDLC-based, half-duplex |
| **IrLMP** (Link Management Protocol) | Multiplexing multiple services | Allows multiple logical channels over one link |
| **Tiny TP** | Flow control & segmentation | Optional transport layer |
| **IrCOMM / IrOBEX** | Application emulation | Emulates serial/parallel ports; file transfer |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** Vertical pipes `\vert` or `\mid` are used in all mathematical expressions to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. Do not use the raw `|` symbol inside table cells.

| Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---------|-------------|----------------|
| $c = \lambda \cdot \nu$ | Wave equation relating speed, wavelength, and frequency | $c$ in m/s, $\lambda$ in m, $\nu$ in Hz |
| $E = h \cdot \nu = \frac{h \cdot c}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | $E$ in Joules, $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s |
| $P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | Free-space optical Friis equation | $P$ in Watts, $d$ in meters, $\lambda$ in meters |
| $P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot A_r \cdot \cos(\theta)}{\pi \cdot d^2}$ | LOS optical power received (Lambertian source) | All in SI units, $A_r$ = receiver aperture area |
| $\text{SNR} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{signal}}}{P_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio in dB | Dimensionless ratio, result in dB |
| $t_{\text{link}} = \frac{D}{R_b}$ | Link setup time (proportional to data) | $D$ in bits, $R_b$ in bps |
| $\text{BER} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 \cdot E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | Bit Error Rate for OOK modulation | $E_b$ = energy/bit, $N_0$ = noise spectral density |

## 2.5 Engineering Applications

- **Consumer Electronics:** TV/air-conditioner remote controls, set-top box IR extenders.
- **Computing Peripherals:** Laptop-to-mobile IrDA file transfer (largely replaced by Bluetooth, but the technology is conceptually identical).
- **Medical & Industrial:** Hospital wireless sensor networks where RF interference with pacemakers is unacceptable.
- **Optical Wireless LANs:** Research prototypes for indoor Gbps WLANs using diffuse IR.
- **Automotive:** In-cabin entertainment and diagnostic port communication.
- **Security Systems:** IR motion sensors and curtain-beam intruder detection.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Converting IR Wavelength to Carrier Frequency

This derivation is the most frequently tested calculation in KTU Module 1. Given the wavelength of an IR source, students must compute the corresponding carrier frequency.

**Problem Setup:** An IrDA transmitter uses an LED with peak emission wavelength $\lambda = 870 \text{ nm}$. Compute the corresponding frequency in THz and verify whether it falls in the standard IR band.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Starting from the universal wave equation:

$$c = \lambda \cdot \nu$$

Rearranging to solve for frequency $\nu$:

$$\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda}$$

Substituting the known values — speed of light $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ and $\lambda = 870 \text{ nm} = 870 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$:

$$\nu = \frac{3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}}{870 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}}$$

$$\nu = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{8.7 \times 10^{-7}} \text{ Hz}$$

$$\nu = 0.3448 \times 10^{15} \text{ Hz}$$

$$\nu = 3.448 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz} = 344.8 \text{ THz}$$

**Conversion Logic:** We inverted the fraction by moving the $10^{-9}$ from the denominator to the numerator, making the exponent $+9$. Then we performed the division $3 / 8.7 \approx 0.3448$, and the powers of 10 yielded $10^{8+9} = 10^{17}$, giving a final result of $3.448 \times 10^{14}$ Hz, which converts to **344.8 THz** by dividing by $10^{12}$.

**Conclusion:** Since $344.8 \text{ THz}$ lies within the IR band of $300 \text{ GHz to } 400 \text{ THz}$, the LED qualifies as a standard near-IR source. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Photon Energy of an IR Photon

**Problem Setup:** For the same 870 nm LED, compute the energy of a single emitted photon in electron-volts (eV).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Starting from the Planck-Einstein relation:

$$E = h \cdot \nu = \frac{h \cdot c}{\lambda}$$

Substituting constants: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$, $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$, $\lambda = 870 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$:

$$E = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34}) \cdot (3 \times 10^{8})}{870 \times 10^{-9}}$$

Computing the numerator first:

$$h \cdot c = 6.626 \times 3 \times 10^{-34+8} = 19.878 \times 10^{-26} = 1.9878 \times 10^{-25} \text{ J·m}$$

Now dividing by $\lambda$:

$$E = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{870 \times 10^{-9}} \text{ J}$$

$$E = \frac{1.9878}{870} \times 10^{-25+9} \text{ J}$$

$$E = 0.002285 \times 10^{-16} \text{ J}$$

$$E = 2.285 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$$

**Converting to electron-volts** using the conversion $1 \text{ eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$:

$$E = \frac{2.285 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \text{ eV}$$

$$E \approx 1.426 \text{ eV}$$

**Conversion Logic:** We multiplied $h$ and $c$ first to simplify, then performed the division. The exponent arithmetic: $-25 - (-9) = -16$ in the exponent step, then we moved the decimal to get a clean scientific notation. The eV conversion uses the standard $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J per eV factor.

**Engineering Insight:** A photon energy of ~1.43 eV confirms the 870 nm LED operates safely in the **non-ionizing** region (ionization threshold is ~10 eV), making it biologically safe for human exposure at the low powers used in IrDA links.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Received Power Using the Lambertian LOS Model

**Problem Setup:** An IrDA transmitter emits $P_t = 100 \text{ mW}$ of optical power uniformly within a cone of half-angle $\theta = 15°$. The receiver has a circular photodetector of diameter $D = 1 \text{ cm}$, placed at distance $d = 0.5 \text{ m}$ along the axis. Compute the received optical power $P_r$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

For a Lambertian source aligned along the axis of an ideal receiver, the received power follows the geometric line-of-sight equation:

$$P_r = \frac{P_t \cdot A_r \cdot \cos(\theta)}{\pi \cdot d^2}$$

Because the receiver is on-axis, $\cos(\theta) = \cos(0) = 1$. The receiver aperture area is:

$$A_r = \pi \cdot \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 = \pi \cdot \left(\frac{0.01}{2}\right)^2$$

$$A_r = \pi \cdot (0.005)^2 = \pi \cdot 2.5 \times 10^{-5} = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$

Substituting into the main equation:

$$P_r = \frac{(100 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (7.854 \times 10^{-5}) \cdot 1}{\pi \cdot (0.5)^2}$$

Computing the numerator:

$$\text{Numerator} = 0.1 \cdot 7.854 \times 10^{-5} = 7.854 \times 10^{-6} \text{ W·m}^2$$

Computing the denominator:

$$\text{Denominator} = \pi \cdot 0.25 = 0.7854 \text{ m}^2$$

Therefore:

$$P_r = \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-6}}{0.7854} = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W} = 10 \text{ µW}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The numerator is in units of W·m² (watts times square meters), and the denominator is in m², so the m² cancels, leaving watts — confirming dimensional consistency. The numerical ratio $7.854 / 0.7854 = 10$ gives a clean answer of 10 µW, which is well within the sensitivity range of standard Si photodiodes (typical NEP around 1 nW).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Study of IR vs Other Wireless Media

This matrix is the **single most important comparison table** for KTU Module 1 examination questions.

| Parameter | Infrared (IR) | Radio (RF) | Microwave | Visible Light |
|-----------|---------------|------------|-----------|---------------|
| Frequency Band | 300 GHz – 400 THz | 3 kHz – 300 GHz | 1 GHz – 300 GHz | 400 – 790 THz |
| Wavelength | 700 nm – 1 mm | 1 mm – 100 km | 1 mm – 30 cm | 380 – 700 nm |
| Propagation | LOS / Diffuse | Omnidirectional | LOS, highly directional | LOS, Diffuse |
| Passes through walls? | **No** | **Yes** | Partially (with attenuation) | **No** |
| Security (eavesdropping) | High (room-confined) | Low (sniffable beyond walls) | Medium | High |
| Data Rate Capability | Up to 1 Gbps (IrDA-GigaIR) | Up to 10 Gbps (Wi-Fi 6E) | Up to Gbps (mmWave 5G) | Up to multi-Gbps (Li-Fi) |
| License Required | **No** | Mostly Yes (ISM bands exception) | Yes (highly regulated) | No |
| Susceptible to sunlight? | **Yes (high)** | Low | Low | **Yes (very high)** |
| Typical Range | < 10 m | < 100 m | Up to km | < 10 m |
| Cost of Components | **Very Low** | Medium | High | Low to Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When a question asks "Why IR is preferred in hospitals or aircraft cabins," the answer must mention **two properties simultaneously**: (1) it does not interfere with sensitive RF equipment, and (2) it is contained within the room for security.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IrDA Protocol Stack — Layered Architecture

The following block diagram illustrates the IrDA protocol stack and its mapping to the OSI reference model. This is a frequently asked question in KTU exams (worth 7 marks when asked as a "describe" question).

```mermaid
graph TD
    app1["User Application"]
    app2["IrOBEX File Transfer"]
    app3["IrCOMM Serial Emulation"]
    layer4["Tiny TP Transport"]
    layer3["IrLMP Multiplexer"]
    layer2["IrLAP Link Access"]
    layer1["IrPHY Optical Transceiver"]
    osi7["OSI Layer 7 Application"]
    osi4["OSI Layer 4 Transport"]
    osi3["OSI Layer 3 Network"]
    osi2["OSI Layer 2 Data Link"]
    osi1["OSI Layer 1 Physical"]

    app1 --> app2 --> app3
    app3 --> layer4
    layer4 --> layer3
    layer3 --> layer2
    layer2 --> layer1
    layer1 --> osi1
    osi1 --> osi2
    osi2 --> osi3
    osi3 --> osi4
    osi4 --> osi7

    style app1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style app2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style app3 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style layer4 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style layer3 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style layer2 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style layer1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The stack flows vertically from the application (top) down to the physical optical transceiver (bottom). The right column shows the OSI layers each IrDA layer corresponds to. Note that IrLMP acts as a multiplexer — a single physical link can carry multiple logical sessions simultaneously (e.g., file transfer + printing).

## 4.2 IR Transmitter and Receiver Block Diagram

The block-level functional architecture of a typical IrDA communication system is shown below.

```mermaid
graph LR
    src["Data Source UART USB"] --> enc["Encoder RZI NRZ Pulse Shaping"]
    enc --> mod["Modulator 38 kHz Subcarrier"]
    mod --> led["IR LED Driver 850 nm to 900 nm"]

    led --> air["Free Space Channel LOS Diffuse Reflective"]
    air --> pd["Photodiode PIN or Avalanche"]

    pd --> amp["Transimpedance Amplifier"]
    amp --> filt["Bandpass Filter 38 kHz"]
    filt --> demod["Demodulator Envelope Detector"]
    demod --> dec["Decoder RZI to NRZ"]
    dec --> dst["Data Sink UART USB"]

    style src fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style enc fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style mod fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style led fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style air fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style pd fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style amp fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style filt fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style demod fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style dec fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style dst fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The left side (yellow and red nodes) is the transmitter chain, the center (blue node) is the propagation channel, and the right side (purple and yellow) is the receiver chain. The IR LED operates in the **850–900 nm** near-infrared window because silicon photodiodes have peak quantum efficiency at these wavelengths, maximizing receiver sensitivity.

## 4.3 Transmission Mode Comparison Diagram

The three transmission modes are visualized as a single functional architecture flow, showing how the propagation path differs.

```mermaid
graph TD
    tx["IR Transmitter LED"]

    tx --> los1["Direct Line of Sight Beam"]
    tx --> diff1["Diffuse Reflection from Ceiling Walls Floor"]
    tx --> quas1["Hybrid Quasi Diffuse LOS plus Reflection"]

    los1 --> rx1["Receiver Pointed Directly at TX"]
    diff1 --> rx2["Receiver Anywhere in Room"]
    quas1 --> rx3["Receiver on Wall or Desk"]

    rx1 --> out1["Range 1 to 3 m High Speed"]
    rx2 --> out2["Range 5 to 10 m Lower Speed"]
    rx3 --> out3["Range 3 to 5 m Balanced"]

    style tx fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style los1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style diff1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style quas1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style rx1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style rx2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style rx3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style out1 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style out2 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style out3 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the Diagram:** The diagram shows how the same transmitter can serve receivers in three different configurations. In **LOS mode** (green), the link is fast but rigidly aligned. In **diffuse mode** (gold), the link is flexible but suffers multipath-induced dispersion, limiting data rate. **Quasi-diffuse** (orange) is the practical compromise used in indoor optical LANs.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Define Infrared transmission. State two advantages and two disadvantages of using infrared waves for data communication.**

**Model Answer:**

Infrared (IR) transmission is a wireless communication technique that uses electromagnetic waves in the frequency range of **300 GHz to 400 THz** (wavelength 700 nm to 1 mm) to carry data through free space. It belongs to the optical wireless communication family and is standardized by the Infrared Data Association (IrDA).

**Advantages:**
1. **High security** — IR does not pass through walls, so the signal is confined to a single room, preventing eavesdropping from outside.
2. **No licensing required** — The IR band is unregulated, so it can be used freely without spectrum allocation.
3. **Low cost** — IR LEDs and photodiodes are extremely cheap (fractions of a cent), making it economically attractive for short-range links.
4. **No RF interference** — Does not interfere with sensitive radio equipment, making it safe for hospitals and aircraft.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Strictly line-of-sight** — Cannot pass through walls, ceilings, or opaque obstacles, severely limiting mobility.
2. **Susceptible to sunlight** — Solar IR radiation acts as noise, degrading link quality in outdoor or bright indoor environments.
3. **Short range** — Typically limited to under 10 m, much shorter than RF alternatives.
4. **Low data rate** — Standard IrDA caps at 115.2 kbps (SIR) to 4 Mbps (FIR), though newer standards push to Gbps.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: What is the IrDA protocol stack? List its layers in order from top to bottom.**

**Model Answer:**

The IrDA (Infrared Data Association) protocol stack is a layered set of standards that defines how devices communicate over infrared links. From top to bottom, the layers are:

1. **IrPHY (Physical Layer)** — Defines optical, electrical, and encoding specifications.
2. **IrLAP (Link Access Protocol)** — Establishes and maintains the reliable link, based on HDLC.
3. **IrLMP (Link Management Protocol)** — Multiplexes multiple logical connections over a single physical link.
4. **Tiny TP (Tiny Transport Protocol)** — Provides flow control and segmentation (optional).
5. **IrCOMM / IrOBEX** — Application-layer services; IrCOMM emulates serial/parallel ports, while IrOBEX handles object (file) exchange.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain in detail the Infrared Data Communication system. Discuss its transmission modes, IrDA protocol stack, and compare infrared with radio wave transmission.**

#### Part (a) — Infrared System and Transmission Modes (7 Marks)

**Infrared System Overview:**

An IR data communication system consists of three blocks: **transmitter**, **free-space channel**, and **receiver**. The transmitter uses an IR LED (typically 850–900 nm) modulated by the data signal. The receiver employs a silicon photodiode (PIN or APD) that converts incident optical power into a proportional photocurrent. The signal is then amplified, filtered (often at 38 kHz sub-carrier for noise rejection), and demodulated to recover the original data.

**Transmission Modes:**

Infrared supports three propagation modes:

- **Line-of-Sight (LOS):** Direct, unobstructed path between transmitter and receiver. The most common mode in IrDA links. Range: 1–3 m. High data rates (up to 1 Gbps in Giga-IR) are achievable.
- **Diffuse:** IR radiates from a wide-angle source and reflects off ceilings and walls before reaching the receiver. Provides room-wide coverage (5–10 m) at the cost of reduced data rate due to multipath dispersion.
- **Quasi-Diffuse (Hybrid):** Combines LOS and reflective paths. A focused beam aims at a reflective spot on the ceiling; the receiver captures the reflected cone. Range: 3–5 m, balanced throughput.

#### Part (b) — IrDA Stack and IR vs RF Comparison (7 Marks)

**IrDA Protocol Stack:** *(Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1 for visual aid.)*

The stack is layered from the physical up to the application:
- **IrPHY** — Optical transceiver and bit-level encoding (RZI for SIR mode).
- **IrLAP** — Link establishment, frame structure, and error recovery; uses HDLC framing.
- **IrLMP** — Multiplexes multiple service channels over one link.
- **Tiny TP** — Optional transport for flow control.
- **IrCOMM / IrOBEX** — Application emulation for serial ports and file transfer.

**Comparison: IR vs RF Transmission**

| Parameter | Infrared | Radio Frequency |
|-----------|----------|-----------------|
| Spectrum | Unregulated, free | Mostly licensed |
| Wall penetration | **No** | **Yes** |
| Security | High (room-confined) | Lower (sniffable) |
| Susceptibility to sunlight | **High** | Low |
| Typical range | 1–10 m | Up to 100 m |
| Data rate (legacy) | 115 kbps to 4 Mbps | 1 Mbps to 10 Gbps |
| Cost | Very low | Moderate to high |
| RF interference with medical devices | **None** | **Possible** |
| Mobility support | Poor (LOS required) | Excellent |

**Conclusion:** IR is preferred where **security, low cost, and RF silence** are critical (e.g., hospital wireless monitors, TV remotes). RF is preferred where **mobility, range, and wall penetration** matter (e.g., Wi-Fi, cellular).

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Q: With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an Infrared transmitter and receiver. Derive the relationship between carrier frequency and wavelength, and calculate the frequency of an 870 nm IR LED.**

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram and Operation (7 Marks)

**Transmitter Block Diagram:** *(Refer to the diagram in Section 4.2 for visual aid.)*

The transmitter chain consists of:
1. **Data Source** (UART / USB / microcontroller port) producing serial bits.
2. **Encoder** that converts NRZ data to RZI (Return-to-Zero Inverted) for IrDA-SIR mode.
3. **Modulator** that mixes the data with a 38 kHz sub-carrier (for remote-control applications) or applies baseband intensity modulation (for IrDA).
4. **LED Driver Circuit** that amplifies the modulated signal to drive the IR LED with currents of 50–500 mA.
5. **IR LED** (GaAs or AlGaAs, 850–900 nm) that converts electrical pulses to optical pulses.

**Receiver Block Diagram:**
1. **Photodiode** (Si PIN or APD) that generates photocurrent proportional to incident IR.
2. **Transimpedance Amplifier** that converts the small photocurrent (~µA) into a usable voltage.
3. **Bandpass Filter** centered at 38 kHz (for remote-control) or matched to the data bandwidth.
4. **Demodulator / Envelope Detector** that recovers the baseband data.
5. **Decoder** that converts RZI back to NRZ and presents bits to the host UART.

**Operation:** When a logic '1' is sent, the LED is pulsed (modulated); for '0', the LED is off. At the receiver, only modulated light triggers the demodulator, providing excellent rejection of ambient sunlight and DC room lighting.

#### Part (b) — Derivation of Frequency-Wavelength Relation (7 Marks)

**Step 1:** All electromagnetic waves, including infrared, obey the universal wave equation:

$$c = \lambda \cdot \nu$$

where $c$ is the speed of light, $\lambda$ is the wavelength, and $\nu$ is the frequency.

**Step 2:** Rearranging to express frequency in terms of wavelength:

$$\nu = \frac{c}{\lambda}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$ and $\lambda = 870 \text{ nm} = 870 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$:

$$\nu = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{870 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$\nu = \frac{3 \times 10^{8}}{8.7 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.3448 \times 10^{15} \text{ Hz}$$

$$\boxed{\nu \approx 3.448 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz} = 344.8 \text{ THz}}$$

**Step 4:** Verifying the result lies in the IR band: 300 GHz < 344.8 THz < 400 THz ✓

**Engineering Interpretation:** The 344.8 THz carrier is ~10,000× higher than the 38 kHz sub-carrier used in remote controls. This extreme carrier frequency is precisely what allows IR systems to support high data rates — the modulation bandwidth is a small fraction of an enormous carrier, giving plenty of headroom.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Students frequently write $870 \text{ nm}$ directly into $c = \lambda \nu$ without converting to meters, leading to numerically wrong answers. **Always convert nm → m by multiplying by $10^{-9}$.**
> 2. **Confusing SIR/FIR/MIR/VFIR data rates:** SIR = 9.6 kbps to 115.2 kbps; MIR = 576 kbps to 1.152 Mbps; FIR = 4 Mbps; VFIR = 16 Mbps. Writing them in random order costs marks.
> 3. **Mixing up layers:** Tiny TP sits **above** IrLMP, not below. IrLAP is **always** below IrLMP.
> 4. **Forgetting the wavelength range:** The KTU answer must explicitly state **700 nm to 1 mm** or **300 GHz to 400 THz**. Vague statements like "high frequency" earn partial credit only.
> 5. **Not mentioning the application relevance:** A purely theoretical answer without linking to a real-world use case (TV remote, IrDA, hospital networks) loses the "application-level" 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

Use this high-density checklist for last-minute revision before the KTU exam:

- ✅ **Wavelength range of IR:** 700 nm to 1 mm
- ✅ **Frequency range of IR:** 300 GHz to 400 THz
- ✅ **Standard IrDA operating wavelength:** 850–900 nm (near-IR)
- ✅ **Consumer remote control sub-carrier:** 38 kHz
- ✅ **IrDA maximum practical speed:** 1 Gbps (GigaIR standard)
- ✅ **Transmission modes:** LOS, Diffuse, Quasi-Diffuse
- ✅ **IrDA Stack (top to bottom):** IrPHY → IrLAP → IrLMP → Tiny TP → IrCOMM/IrOBEX
- ✅ **Key equations:** $c = \lambda \nu$, $E = h\nu$, Lambertian LOS power formula
- ✅ **Standard speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- ✅ **Planck's constant:** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- ✅ **Photon energy of 870 nm IR:** ~1.43 eV (safe, non-ionizing)
- ✅ **IR cannot pass through walls** — this is the central feature for security
- ✅ **IR is susceptible to sunlight** — use 38 kHz sub-carrier to filter ambient noise
- ✅ **IR vs RF:** IR is contained, secure, cheap, RF-interference-free; RF is mobile, long-range, wall-penetrating
- ✅ **Key applications:** TV remotes, IrDA sync, hospital wireless, automotive, indoor optical LAN
- ✅ **Encoding used by IrDA-SIR:** Return-to-Zero Inverted (RZI), 3/16 pulse width
- ✅ **Receiver element:** Si photodiode (PIN or APD), peak sensitivity near 900 nm
- ✅ **Link range:** < 1 m for SIR/FIR; up to 3 m for extended mode; up to 10 m for diffuse
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireless Propagation - Ground Wave Propagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Wireless Propagation - Ground Wave Propagation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ground Wave Propagation (Surface Wave Propagation)** is the mode of electromagnetic wave propagation in which the radio wave travels along the curvature of the Earth by hugging its surface, supported by the conductive earth itself. The wave induces currents in the ground, which in turn bend (or "diffract") the wavefront downward, allowing the signal to follow the Earth's curvature beyond the line-of-sight horizon.

According to KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, ground wave propagation is a **tropospheric-surface mechanism** that dominates in the **Very Low Frequency (VLF)**, **Low Frequency (LF)**, and **Medium Frequency (MF)** bands — typically from **30 kHz to 2 MHz**. It is the *primary* mechanism used by **AM broadcast radio (530 kHz – 1700 kHz)**, maritime communication, and long-range navigation (LORAN-C).

The ground wave consists of three coupled components:
1. **Direct Wave** – travels in a straight line from Tx to Rx.
2. **Reflected Wave** – bounces off the Earth's surface.
3. **Surface Wave** – the ground-bound, diffracted component that is unique to this mode.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — Surface Wave:** The portion of the wave that is guided along the conducting surface of the Earth. Without a conducting earth (e.g., in space or over a perfect insulator), surface wave propagation does not exist.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hugging the Floor" Intuition

Imagine a **roomba (robotic vacuum)** gliding along the floor of your house. Even when the floor curves gently (a slight bend in the path), the roomba sticks to it because of its surface sensors and physical contact. It doesn't fly in a straight line; it *follows* the contour.

A **ground wave** behaves in a remarkably similar way:
- The **earth's surface** acts as the "floor."
- The **induced ground currents** act as the "sensors," keeping the wave tethered.
- As the earth curves away, the wave **tilts downward** and continues to hug the surface.

If the floor were **perfectly smooth ice** (a perfect dielectric, σ = 0), the roomba would slide off — analogous to a wave escaping into space. If the floor were **sticky rubber** (high conductivity σ, like seawater), the roomba holds on tightly — analogous to **low attenuation** and long-range ground wave propagation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used Throughout This Module**
> - $\eta_0 = 120\pi \approx 377\ \Omega$ → Intrinsic impedance of free space
> - $c = 3 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$ → Speed of light in vacuum
> - $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{F/m}$ → Permittivity of free space
> - $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}\ \text{H/m}$ → Permeability of free space

## 1.3 Why Ground Wave Propagation Matters

| Property | Significance |
| :--- | :--- |
| Bypasses line-of-sight | Communication over the horizon without satellites |
| Independent of ionosphere | Reliable day/night, all-weather performance |
| Limited by frequency | Usable only up to ~2 MHz practically |
| Highly terrain-dependent | Seawater > flat earth > hilly terrain |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism — The Three-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Radiation and Ground Coupling**
A vertically polarized transmitting antenna (typically a monopole) radiates electromagnetic energy. The **vertical polarization** is critical because the **electric field must be parallel to the ground** to induce conduction currents in the Earth's surface.

**Step 2 — Current Induction in the Earth**
The time-varying electric field of the wave **penetrates the upper layers of the soil/sea** and forces free electrons to oscillate. These oscillating charges create their own secondary electromagnetic field, which is **180° out of phase** with the primary wave in the direction of propagation above the surface.

**Step 3 — Wavefront Tilting (Tilting the Wave Downward)**
This phase opposition effectively **rotates the wavefront**, tilting the originally vertical E-field into a **forward-tilted elliptical polarization**. The tilted wavefront now propagates along a path that matches the Earth's curvature. This phenomenon is called **wavefront tilt** or the **Banos-Wesley effect** in modern literature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sommerfeld's Insight (1909):** The ground wave is a *bound* wave. Its existence depends entirely on the energy exchange between the wave above the surface and the lossy dielectric below it. Without losses in the ground, no tilting occurs.

## 2.2 Factors Affecting Ground Wave Propagation

| Factor | Effect on Propagation | Engineering Implication |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Frequency (f)** | Higher f → shorter λ → more attenuation | Ground wave usable only below ~2 MHz |
| **Earth Conductivity (σ)** | Higher σ → lower attenuation | Seawater (σ ≈ 4 S/m) gives 100× range vs dry soil (σ ≈ 10⁻³ S/m) |
| **Earth Permittivity (ε_r)** | Affects phase velocity in the lossy medium | Wet soil: ε_r ≈ 30; Dry soil: ε_r ≈ 4 |
| **Distance (d)** | Field strength falls as 1/d in free space, faster in ground wave | Practical AM broadcast range: 50–200 km over land |
| **Antenna Height** | Higher Tx → stronger direct component, but surface wave is mostly independent | For MF AM, λ/4 vertical monopole is standard |
| **Terrain Roughness** | Hilly terrain scatters and absorbs surface wave | Flat coastal areas give maximum ground wave range |

## 2.3 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they form the backbone of any KTU numerical on this topic.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Wavelength | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | m |
| 2 | Intrinsic impedance of free space | $\eta_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu_0}{\varepsilon_0}} = 120\pi$ | $\Omega$ |
| 3 | Intrinsic impedance of Earth | $\eta_g = \sqrt{\dfrac{j\omega\mu_0}{\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon}}$ | $\Omega$ (complex) |
| 4 | Numerical distance | $\rho = \dfrac{\pi d}{\lambda} \cdot \cos\theta$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Attenuation factor (Sommerfeld) | $A = \dfrac{2 + 0.3\rho}{2 + \rho + 0.6\rho^2}$ | dimensionless, $\vert A \vert \leq 1$ |
| 6 | Ground-wave field strength (Norton) | $E = \dfrac{E_0}{d} \cdot A$ | V/m |
| 7 | Free-space field strength | $E_0 = \dfrac{\sqrt{30 \cdot P_t \cdot G_t}}{d}$ | V/m |
| 8 | Surface wave tilt angle | $\tan\psi = \dfrac{\omega\varepsilon}{\sigma}$ | rad |
| 9 | Loss tangent of ground | $\tan\delta = \dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}$ | ratio |
| 10 | Phase constant in Earth | $\beta_g = \omega\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon}\sqrt{\dfrac{\sqrt{1 + \tan^2\delta} + 1}{2}}$ | rad/m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **When to use vertical bars in formulas:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX (NOT the raw `|` symbol), so the markdown table parser doesn't break.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **AM Radio Broadcasting (535 kHz – 1705 kHz):** The reason you can listen to All India Radio (AIR) stations hundreds of kilometers away is ground wave propagation. Skywave (ionospheric) propagation supplements it at night, but during the day, **ground wave is the only mechanism**.
2. **Maritime & Aeronautical NDB (Non-Directional Beacons):** Operate in 190–535 kHz range using ground wave for reliable navigation.
3. **Submarine Communication (VLF, 3–30 kHz):** Ground wave can even penetrate a few meters into seawater, allowing one-way communication with submerged submarines.
4. **Time Signal Stations (e.g., WWVB at 60 kHz):** National time references use ground wave for continental coverage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Surface Wave Tilt and the Loss Tangent

**Starting Point:** A plane electromagnetic wave traveling in free space strikes the lossy earth surface. The tangential E-field must be continuous across the boundary, but the conductivity of the ground forces a phase shift.

Let the intrinsic impedance of the lossy ground be:

$$
\eta_g = \sqrt{\frac{j\omega\mu_0}{\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon}}
$$

**Step 1 — Separate the numerator and denominator into magnitude and phase.**

We rewrite the complex conductivity as $\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon$. The intrinsic impedance becomes:

$$
\eta_g = \sqrt{\frac{j\omega\mu_0}{\sigma + j\omega\varepsilon}}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the loss tangent.**

The loss tangent characterizes how "lossy" the medium is:

$$
\tan\delta = \frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}
$$

This gives us a clean way to compare conduction current versus displacement current in the soil.

**Step 3 — Use the identity for the tilt angle.**

The wavefront tilt angle $\psi$ of the E-field relative to the vertical satisfies:

$$
\tan\psi = \frac{\omega\varepsilon}{\sigma} = \frac{1}{\tan\delta} = \cot\delta
$$

**Interpretation:** For **good conductors** (e.g., seawater, σ → ∞), $\tan\delta \to \infty$, hence $\psi \to 0$. The wavefront remains nearly vertical, hugging the surface tightly. For **poor conductors** (dry sand, σ → 0), $\psi \to 90°$, meaning the wave tilts forward excessively and detaches from the surface.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Attenuation Factor (Sommerfeld's Approximation)

This is the **most important derivation for KTU numerical problems**.

**Step 1 — Define numerical distance.**

The numerical distance is a dimensionless parameter:

$$
\rho = \frac{\pi d}{\lambda} \cdot \cos\theta
$$

where $d$ is the propagation distance, $\lambda$ is the wavelength, and $\theta$ is the angle measured from the surface.

**Step 2 — Express $\cos\theta$ in terms of ground parameters.**

For a lossy earth with complex refractive index, the angle becomes complex. Substituting the loss tangent:

$$
\cos\theta = \sqrt{\frac{\tan\delta}{j + \tan\delta}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the magnitude of the attenuation factor.**

Sommerfeld showed that the field strength of the ground wave relative to free space is:

$$
A = \frac{2 + 0.3\rho}{2 + \rho + 0.6\rho^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply limiting cases.**

$$
\lim_{\rho \to 0} A = 1 \quad \text{(small distance, behaves like free space)}
$$

$$
\lim_{\rho \to \infty} A \approx \frac{0.3}{\rho} \quad \text{(large distance, field falls as } 1/\rho^2 \text{)}
$$

**Step 5 — Final field strength equation.**

$$
E_{ground} = \frac{E_0}{d} \cdot A = \frac{\sqrt{30 P_t G_t}}{d} \cdot \frac{2 + 0.3\rho}{2 + \rho + 0.6\rho^2}
$$

where $P_t$ is the transmitted power (W) and $G_t$ is the gain of the transmitting antenna.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** An AM broadcast station operates at **f = 1 MHz** with $P_t = 10$ kW and a quarter-wave monopole antenna ($G_t = 1.64$). The ground has conductivity $\sigma = 10^{-2}$ S/m and $\varepsilon_r = 10$. Compute the surface wave tilt and the ground-wave field strength at $d = 100$ km.

**Step 1 — Compute wavelength.**

$$
\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{1 \times 10^6} = 300\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute loss tangent.**

$$
\tan\delta = \frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon} = \frac{10^{-2}}{2\pi \times 10^6 \times 10 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}}
$$

$$
\tan\delta = \frac{10^{-2}}{5.563 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 17.97
$$

**Step 3 — Compute wavefront tilt.**

$$
\psi = \cot^{-1}(17.97) \approx 3.18^{\circ}
$$

The wavefront is tilted by only ~3.2°, indicating moderate coupling to the ground.

**Step 4 — Compute free-space field strength at 100 km.**

$$
E_0 = \frac{\sqrt{30 \times 10^4 \times 1.64}}{100 \times 10^3} = \frac{\sqrt{4.92 \times 10^5}}{10^5} = \frac{701.4}{10^5} \approx 7.01\ \text{mV/m}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute numerical distance.**

$$
\rho \approx \frac{\pi \times 10^5}{300} \times 0.236 \approx 247
$$

**Step 6 — Compute attenuation factor.**

$$
A = \frac{2 + 0.3(247)}{2 + 247 + 0.6(247)^2} = \frac{76.1}{36837} \approx 2.07 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 7 — Final ground-wave field strength.**

$$
E_{ground} = E_0 \times A = 7.01\ \text{mV/m} \times 2.07 \times 10^{-3} \approx 14.5\ \mu\text{V/m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A field of ~14.5 μV/m at 100 km is on the borderline of AM receiver sensitivity (~50 μV/m for good reception). To cover 100 km reliably, the station would need higher power or a more conductive ground path (e.g., coastal location).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Dominant Propagation | Key Equation | Practical Limit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| d < 10 km, f < 2 MHz | Ground wave | $E = E_0 A$ | Range set by Tx power |
| 10 km < d < 500 km, f < 2 MHz | Ground + Sky wave (night) | $E = E_0 A + E_{sky}$ | Fading due to interference |
| d > 500 km, f < 30 kHz | Ground wave only (VLF) | $\lambda$ very large | Used for submarine comms |
| f > 2 MHz, any d | Sky wave / LOS | Different mechanism | Ground wave is negligible |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: The Three-Component Mechanism of Ground Wave Propagation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TX[TX ANTENNA VERTICAL MONOPOLE] --> RAD[RADIATES EM WAVE]
    RAD --> DIR[DIRECT WAVE LINE OF SIGHT COMPONENT]
    RAD --> REF[REFLECTED WAVE EARTH BOUNDARY]
    RAD --> SURF[SURFACE WAVE GROUND COUPLED]

    DIR --> RX1[RX ANTENNA]
    REF --> RX1
    SURF --> RX1

    SURF --> IND[CURRENTS INDUCED IN SOIL]
    IND --> TILT[WAVEFRONT TILTING]
    TILT --> CURV[WAVE FOLLOWS EARTH CURVATURE]
    CURV --> RX1

    TX1[GROUND PARAMETERS] --> CON[CONDUCTIVITY SIGMA]
    TX1 --> PER[PERMITTIVITY EPSILON]
    CON --> IND
    PER --> IND

    RX1 --> EFS[RESULTANT FIELD STRENGTH E AT RX]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Functional Architecture: Ground Wave Attenuation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT PT GT AND F] --> B[COMPUTE WAVELENGTH LAMBDA]
    B --> C[COMPUTE LOSS TANGENT TAN DELTA]
    C --> D[COMPUTE NUMERICAL DISTANCE RHO]
    D --> E[LOOKUP OR COMPUTE ATTENUATION FACTOR A]
    A --> F[COMPUTE FREE SPACE E0]
    F --> G[FINAL FIELD E = E0 TIMES A]
    E --> G
    G --> H[OUTPUT FIELD STRENGTH AT RX]
```

## 4.3 Frequency Band Allocation Map (Textual Schematic)

| Band | Frequency Range | Dominant Mode | Typical Service |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| VLF | 3 – 30 kHz | **Ground wave (primary)** | Submarine comms |
| LF | 30 – 300 kHz | **Ground wave (primary)** | Navigation, time signals |
| MF | 300 kHz – 2 MHz | **Ground wave + Sky wave (night)** | AM broadcast |
| HF | 2 – 30 MHz | Sky wave (ionospheric) | Shortwave radio |
| VHF and above | > 30 MHz | Line-of-sight | FM, TV, mobile |

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Concept:** Imagine the Earth's surface as a stretched elastic sheet. Low-frequency ground waves "stick" to the sheet even when it curves downward. High-frequency waves slip off because they oscillate too fast for the surface currents to track them.

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Effect of Ground Conductivity on Range

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[SEAWATER SIGMA 4 S/M] --> R1[RANGE UP TO 1000 KM AT 1 MHZ]
    G2[WET SOIL SIGMA 10 TO 2] --> R2[RANGE UP TO 300 KM AT 1 MHZ]
    G3[DRY SOIL SIGMA 10 TO 3] --> R3[RANGE UP TO 100 KM AT 1 MHZ]
    G4[ROCKY TERRAIN SIGMA 10 TO 4] --> R4[RANGE LESS THAN 50 KM AT 1 MHZ]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** "**S**eawater, **W**et, **D**ry, **R**ocky" — range decreases in this order. Just remember **"S W D R = longer to shorter"** and you will never forget the conductivity hierarchy.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Define ground wave propagation. Why is it limited to frequencies below approximately 2 MHz?**

**Model Answer:**

Ground wave propagation is the mode of electromagnetic wave propagation in which the radio wave travels along the curvature of the Earth, guided by currents induced in the conducting surface. It consists of direct, reflected, and surface wave components.

The 2 MHz upper limit arises because:
1. At higher frequencies, the **skin depth in soil** $\delta = \sqrt{2/(\omega\mu\sigma)}$ becomes very small, so the wave cannot penetrate the ground sufficiently to establish guiding currents.
2. The **wavefront tilt** becomes ineffective as frequency rises, and the wave detaches from the surface.
3. Above 2 MHz, **ionospheric reflection (sky wave)** and **line-of-sight** become the dominant modes, making ground wave negligible.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Explain the role of vertical polarization in ground wave propagation.**

**Model Answer:**

Ground wave propagation requires the electric field of the wave to be **parallel to the Earth's surface** so that it can induce conduction currents in the lossy ground. A vertically polarized antenna (such as a quarter-wave monopole) produces an E-field with a strong horizontal component at the surface, enabling this coupling.

Horizontal polarization, in contrast, has its E-field parallel to the surface but its **H-field vertical**, which does not couple to ground currents effectively. Therefore, **vertical polarization is mandatory for ground wave propagation** in the MF and LF bands.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully!**
> - Students often **confuse ground wave with sky wave**. Markers deduct 2 marks for this.
> - The **attenuation factor A is NOT optional** in numericals. Writing only $E = E_0/d$ without A loses 3 marks.
> - Units must be consistent: distance in **meters**, frequency in **Hz**, power in **watts** in the Norton equation.
> - Always state the **polarization assumption** when using the ground wave formula.
> - The **loss tangent** must be computed *before* using the Sommerfeld approximation; skipping it loses 2 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Derive the expression for the ground wave field strength using the Sommerfeld attenuation factor. State clearly the assumptions made.

**Model Answer:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Earth is a smooth, homogeneous, isotropic lossy dielectric.
2. Transmitting antenna is a short vertical monopole at the surface.
3. Propagation is over a flat or gently curved surface (radius >> λ).
4. Frequency is low enough that surface wave is the dominant mode.

**Derivation:**

The free-space field strength at distance $d$ from an isotropic radiator with power $P_t$ and gain $G_t$ is:

$$
E_0 = \frac{\sqrt{30 P_t G_t}}{d}
$$

This is derived from the Poynting vector $S = E_0^2 / \eta_0$, equating it to $P_t G_t / (4\pi d^2)$, and solving for $E_0$.

When the wave travels over a lossy ground, the field is reduced by the **Sommerfeld attenuation factor** $A$, which is a function of the numerical distance $\rho$:

$$
\rho = \frac{\pi d}{\lambda} \cos\theta
$$

Sommerfeld solved the boundary-value problem for a vertical electric dipole over a lossy half-space and obtained:

$$
A = \frac{2 + 0.3\rho}{2 + \rho + 0.6\rho^2}
$$

Combining both:

$$
\boxed{E_{ground} = \frac{\sqrt{30 P_t G_t}}{d} \cdot \frac{2 + 0.3\rho}{2 + \rho + 0.6\rho^2}}
$$

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** An AM transmitter at 1 MHz radiates 20 kW with a gain of 1.64. The ground has $\sigma = 2 \times 10^{-2}$ S/m and $\varepsilon_r = 8$. Compute the field strength at 80 km. Comment on the result.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Wavelength:**
$$
\lambda = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{10^6} = 300\ \text{m}
$$

**Step 2 — Loss tangent:**
$$
\tan\delta = \frac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-2}}{2\pi \times 10^6 \times 8 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12}} = \frac{2 \times 10^{-2}}{4.45 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 44.9
$$

**Step 3 — $\cos\theta$ for numerical distance:**
$$
\cos\theta = \sqrt{\frac{\tan\delta}{j + \tan\delta}} \approx \sqrt{\frac{44.9}{j + 44.9}} \approx 0.999
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical distance:**
$$
\rho = \frac{\pi \times 80\,000}{300} \times 0.999 \approx 837.6
$$

**Step 5 — Attenuation factor:**
$$
A = \frac{2 + 0.3 \times 837.6}{2 + 837.6 + 0.6 \times 837.6^2} = \frac{253.3}{421\,820} \approx 6.0 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 6 — Free-space field:**
$$
E_0 = \frac{\sqrt{30 \times 20\,000 \times 1.64}}{80\,000} = \frac{\sqrt{9.84 \times 10^5}}{80\,000} = \frac{991.97}{80\,000} \approx 12.4\ \text{mV/m}
$$

**Step 7 — Final field strength:**
$$
E_{ground} = 12.4\ \text{mV/m} \times 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \approx 7.44\ \mu\text{V/m}
$$

**Comment:** The field is below the standard AM receiver sensitivity (~50 μV/m), so coverage at 80 km over this ground would be marginal. Increasing power or improving ground conductivity (e.g., coastal location) would help.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With neat diagrams, explain the three components of the ground wave and discuss how the surface wave is guided along the Earth.

**Model Answer:**

**The Three Components:**

1. **Direct Wave:** Travels in a straight line from Tx to Rx above the Earth's surface. Dominant at short distances and higher frequencies.

2. **Reflected Wave:** Bounces off the Earth's surface according to Snell's law. Phase changes by 180° for a denser medium (typical ground), causing partial cancellation with the direct wave.

3. **Surface Wave (Ground Wave):** The unique component that hugs the Earth's curvature. It is a *bound wave* that exists only in the presence of a lossy ground.

**Guidance Mechanism:**

When the EM wave reaches the lossy surface, it induces conduction currents. These currents are 180° out of phase with the displacement current in the air. The vector sum produces a tilted E-field that:
- Has a forward-pointing horizontal component, allowing the wave to "lean into" the curvature.
- Continuously regenerates along the path as long as the ground has finite conductivity.

The process is sustained because energy is continuously exchanged between the wave above the surface and the lossy dielectric below, with the ground acting as a slow-wave guiding structure.

**Diagram Reference:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the Mermaid schematic.

---

**Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Compare ground wave propagation with sky wave propagation in terms of frequency range, mechanism, reliability, and typical applications.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Ground Wave | Sky Wave |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Frequency range** | 30 kHz – 2 MHz | 2 MHz – 30 MHz |
| **Medium** | Earth surface (ground currents) | Ionosphere (reflection/refraction) |
| **Mechanism** | Surface wave guided by lossy ground | Reflection from ionized layers (D, E, F) |
| **Day/Night reliability** | Same day and night | Highly variable (D layer disappears at night) |
| **Distance covered** | 50 – 1000 km | 100 – 12,000 km (multi-hop) |
| **Fading** | Minimal | Severe (selective, multipath) |
| **Antenna** | Vertical monopole (LF/MF) | Horizontal or vertical dipole (HF) |
| **Typical applications** | AM broadcast, NDB, VLF submarine comms | Shortwave radio, international broadcast, ham radio |
| **Limitation** | High attenuation at higher f; poor over dry/rocky terrain | Ionospheric storms, blackouts, skip-zone issues |

**Conclusion:** Ground wave is preferred for **reliable, short-to-medium range, all-weather** communication, while sky wave is used for **long-range, HF international** communication.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1, Ground Wave Propagation**

- **Definition:** Ground wave = wave that hugs the Earth via surface currents.
- **Frequency range:** **VLF (3–30 kHz), LF (30–300 kHz), MF (300 kHz – 2 MHz)**.
- **Polarization:** **Vertical** is mandatory.
- **Three components:** Direct + Reflected + Surface.
- **Key equation:** $\boxed{E = \dfrac{\sqrt{30 P_t G_t}}{d} \cdot \dfrac{2 + 0.3\rho}{2 + \rho + 0.6\rho^2}}$
- **Loss tangent:** $\tan\delta = \dfrac{\sigma}{\omega\varepsilon}$.
- **Numerical distance:** $\rho = \dfrac{\pi d}{\lambda} \cos\theta$.
- **Conductivity ranking (best to worst):** **Seawater > Wet soil > Dry soil > Rocky terrain**.
- **Sommerfeld condition:** Ground wave requires **lossy ground**; perfect dielectric gives no surface wave.
- **Practical limit:** ~2 MHz above which ground wave becomes negligible.
- **Applications:** AM radio, NDB navigation, VLF submarine comms, time signal stations.
- **Limitation:** Attenuation increases with frequency and decreases with conductivity.
- **Wavefront tilt:** Angle $\psi$ = $\cot^{-1}(\tan\delta)$; small for good conductors.
- **Comparison mnemonic:** "Ground = stable but short range; Sky = long range but unreliable."

> [!TIP]
> **Final Viva Tip:** If asked "Why does AM radio use vertical antennas?" — answer: "Vertical polarization couples to ground currents, enabling ground wave propagation, which is the dominant mode in the MF band."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sky Wave Propagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sky Wave Propagation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Sky Wave Propagation** (also termed as *Ionospheric Propagation*) is the mechanism by which electromagnetic waves radiated from a transmitting antenna at angles **above the horizontal (i.e., directed toward the upper atmosphere)** are progressively refracted back to the Earth's surface by the ionized layers of the atmosphere known as the **Ionosphere**. This mode of propagation is the dominant mechanism for medium-frequency (MF) and high-frequency (HF) radio communication over long distances ranging from approximately **30 km to 4000 km**, and forms the historical backbone of transcontinental shortwave radio broadcasting, maritime communication, and aeronautical services.

The phenomenon is fundamentally governed by the interaction between the propagating electromagnetic wave and the **free electrons** present in the ionospheric plasma. The higher the frequency of the transmitted wave, the deeper the wave penetrates into the ionosphere; conversely, lower frequencies are reflected at lower altitudes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Sky wave propagation is grouped under "Wave Propagation" along with ground wave and space wave propagation. Examiners expect students to clearly distinguish the *mechanism of refraction*, identify the *reflecting layer*, and compute *critical frequency / MUF / skip distance* using the standard formulas.

## 1.2 The Ionosphere — The Reflecting Medium

The **Ionosphere** is the region of the Earth's upper atmosphere, extending from approximately **60 km to 400 km** above the surface, that contains a sufficient concentration of free electrons and ions to affect the propagation of radio waves. The ionization is primarily produced by **solar ultraviolet (UV) and X-ray radiation** striking the gas molecules.

| Layer | Approximate Altitude (km) | Existence Time | Key Behavior |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **D Layer** | 60 – 90 | Daytime only | Absorbs LF/MF; **vanishes at night** |
| **E Layer** | 100 – 150 | Daytime (strong), night (residual sporadic-E) | Reflects HF waves up to ~10 MHz |
| **F1 Layer** | 150 – 250 | Daytime only | Merges with F2 at night |
| **F2 Layer** | 250 – 400 | Persists day & night | Primary reflecting layer for HF communication |

> [!NOTE]
> The D layer, despite being weakly ionized, plays a **critical role in atmospheric absorption** — this is why distant HF reception is *better at night* when the D layer disappears and absorption losses plummet.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Skipping Stone on a Water Gradient"

Imagine throwing a flat stone so that it skips across the surface of a lake. As the stone moves over regions of water with *progressively changing density* (e.g., warmer surface water above colder deep water), its path bends downward until it momentarily touches the surface and "skips" upward again.

The sky wave behaves identically:
- The **transmitting antenna** is the "thrower."
- The **ionosphere** is the "gradient medium" (electron density increases with altitude).
- The **wave's velocity slows down** as it enters a denser plasma, causing **continuous refraction**.
- The wave eventually bends so much that it returns to Earth — a **total internal reflection analogue**.
- After striking Earth, a **ground-reflected wave** may bounce back into the ionosphere, enabling **multi-hop sky wave propagation** over intercontinental distances.

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU exam, remember the single most important fact: *Sky waves are NOT reflected in the geometrical-optics sense*. They are **refracted back continuously** by a medium whose refractive index *gradually decreases* with altitude.

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Speed of light in vacuum:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s**
- **Refractive index of free space:** $n_0 = 1$
- **Effective frequency range for sky wave:** **2 MHz – 30 MHz (HF band)**
- **Useful range:** **30 km – 4000 km** per single hop
- **Maximum electron density altitude:** ~300 km (F2 peak)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Ionospheric Refraction — The "Why" and the "How"

### The "Why": Why does the wave bend?

When an electromagnetic wave enters a region containing **free electrons**, the electrons are forced to oscillate at the wave's frequency. This oscillation **re-radiates** a secondary wave that interferes with the incident wave, modifying the **phase velocity**. The cumulative effect is a **reduction in refractive index** of the medium.

The refractive index of the ionospheric plasma (neglecting Earth's magnetic field) is given by **Appleton's relation** in its simplified form:

$$n = \sqrt{1 - \frac{81 \cdot N}{f^2}}$$

where:
- $n$ = refractive index of the ionized layer (dimensionless, $n < 1$)
- $N$ = electron density per cubic meter (electrons/m³)
- $f$ = operating frequency in Hz
- $81$ = constant derived from $\frac{N e^2}{4 \pi^2 m \varepsilon_0}$ where $e$ is electron charge and $m$ is electron mass

### The "How": How does bending occur?

As the wave ascends, the electron density $N$ **increases** with altitude. Consequently, the refractive index $n$ **decreases** (becomes smaller than 1). The wave front progressively **tilts**, just as a light beam bends when entering a less dense optical medium — the trajectory curves back toward Earth.

**Refraction continues until the wave reaches a level where $N$ is maximum.** Above that peak, the wave would start to refract *away* again; but because of Earth's curvature and the geometry, the wave instead **turns back** to Earth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The wave never actually reaches the level of maximum electron density. It is **bent back** before that. This is the foundation of the **Virtual Height** concept (covered in §3).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

The following table is the **exam-ready formula bank** for Sky Wave Propagation problems.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Refractive Index of Ionosphere | $n = \sqrt{1 - \frac{81\,N}{f^2}}$ | $N$ in $\text{el/m}^3$, $f$ in Hz | $n < 1$ in ionized medium |
| 2 | Critical Frequency | $f_c = 9\,\sqrt{N_{max}}$ | $N_{max}$ in $\text{el/m}^3$, $f_c$ in Hz | Vertical incidence; **most important formula** |
| 3 | MUF (Max Usable Frequency) | $MUF = \dfrac{f_c}{\cos\theta} = f_c \cdot \sec\theta$ | $\theta$ = angle of incidence at ionosphere | **Secant Law** |
| 4 | Virtual Height | $h' = \dfrac{d_{skip}}{2\,\tan\Delta}$ | $d$ in km, $\Delta$ in radians | $h' >$ true height |
| 5 | Skip Distance | $d_{skip} = 2\,h'\,\tan\Delta$ | $h'$ in km, $\Delta$ in radians | Minimum receivable distance |
| 6 | Optimum Working Frequency (OWF) | $OWF \approx 0.85 \times MUF$ | — | Practical operating frequency |
| 7 | Secant Law Derivation | $MUF = f_c \cdot \sec\theta$ | $\theta$ at the layer | Applies for oblique incidence |
| 8 | Maximum Electron Density | $N_{max} = \dfrac{f_c^{\,2}}{81}$ | $f_c$ in Hz | Inversion of (2) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pipe:** KTU examiners routinely test the conversion of $f_c$ (in Hz) into MHz. Always remember: $f_c \text{(MHz)} = 9\,\sqrt{N_{max}} \times 10^{-6}$? **No** — keep $f_c$ in Hz if $N$ is in $\text{el/m}^3$, OR convert consistently. The simplest exam approach: use $f_c \text{(Hz)} = 9\sqrt{N_{max}}$ where $N_{max}$ is in $\text{el/m}^3$.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of Sky Wave Propagation

- **Long-distance broadcasting** — BBC, Voice of America, All India Radio shortwave services.
- **Maritime and aeronautical HF communication** — over oceans where no repeater infrastructure exists.
- **Military strategic communication** — robust, infrastructure-free long-range links.
- **Amateur (HAM) radio** — intercontinental contacts in the HF band.
- **Time signal stations** (e.g., WWV, ATA) for calibration.

Modern alternatives (satellites, fiber optics) have reduced dependence on sky waves, but the ionospheric channel remains a **vital backup** during disasters and wartime when satellites may be denied.
