<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Critical Frequency $f_c$

We begin with the refractive index of the ionospheric plasma (ignoring magnetic field and collisions):

$$n = \sqrt{1 - \frac{81\,N}{f^2}}$$

**Step 1 — Condition for reflection.**
For a wave to be bent back (reflected) at vertical incidence, the refractive index must drop to **zero** at the level of maximum electron density $N_{max}$. Setting $n = 0$:

$$0 = \sqrt{1 - \frac{81\,N_{max}}{f^2}}$$

**Step 2 — Square both sides.**

$$0 = 1 - \frac{81\,N_{max}}{f^2}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate the ratio.**

$$\frac{81\,N_{max}}{f^2} = 1$$

$$f^2 = 81\,N_{max}$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root. This frequency is the Critical Frequency $f_c$:**

$$f_c = 9\,\sqrt{N_{max}}$$

This is the **highest frequency that will be reflected back to Earth when the wave is transmitted vertically upward**. Any higher frequency passes through the ionosphere into space.

> [!TIP]
> Critical frequency depends *only* on the maximum electron density — it is a property of the ionosphere, NOT of the transmitter power. This is a common KTU viva question.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Secant Law (MUF Relation)

Consider a wave transmitted at an angle $\Delta$ above the horizontal (the *angle of elevation*). It strikes the ionospheric layer at an angle of incidence $\theta$ (measured from the normal to the layer). Using **Snell's law** between the ground (refractive index = 1) and the ionospheric layer (refractive index = $n$):

$$\sin\Delta = n\,\sin\theta$$

**Step 1 — At the level of reflection, the refractive index $n$ becomes zero (wave is refracted back).** Substitute $n = 0$:

$$\sin\Delta = 0 \cdot \sin\theta = 0$$

This trivial result is not directly useful. Instead, we evaluate at a higher altitude where the wave is *about* to be reflected, and apply Snell's law between the entry point (refractive index = $n_1$) and the reflection point ($n = 0$).

A more practical derivation uses the **equivalent path theorem** (Breit's theorem), but the **classical KTU exam derivation** proceeds as follows:

**Step 2 — Apply Snell's law at the entry point of the layer:**

$$\sin\Delta_0 = n_1 \sin\theta_1$$

where $\Delta_0$ is the elevation angle, $n_1$ is the refractive index at the entry level, and $\theta_1$ is the angle of refraction at that level.

**Step 3 — For a wave to JUST be reflected, the angle of refraction $\theta_1$ must approach 90°.** That is, the wave travels horizontally at the layer of maximum $N$ before turning back. So $\sin\theta_1 \to 1$.

$$\sin\Delta_0 = n_1 \cdot 1 = n_1$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $n_1 = \sqrt{1 - \frac{81\,N_{max}}{f^2}}$:**

$$\sin\Delta_0 = \sqrt{1 - \frac{81\,N_{max}}{f^2}}$$

**Step 5 — Recognize that at vertical incidence the maximum frequency reflected is $f_c$:**

$$f_c = 9\,\sqrt{N_{max}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{81\,N_{max}}{f_c^{\,2}} = 1$$

**Step 6 — For the oblique case at frequency $f$, rewrite the expression:**

$$\sin\Delta_0 = \sqrt{1 - \frac{f_c^{\,2}}{f^{\,2}}}$$

**Step 7 — Now invert. We want the maximum $f$ that can be reflected at angle $\Delta_0$. Square both sides:**

$$\sin^2\Delta_0 = 1 - \frac{f_c^{\,2}}{f^{\,2}}$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $f$:**

$$\frac{f_c^{\,2}}{f^{\,2}} = 1 - \sin^2\Delta_0 = \cos^2\Delta_0$$

$$f^{\,2} = \frac{f_c^{\,2}}{\cos^2\Delta_0}$$

**Step 9 — Take the square root to obtain the MUF formula:**

$$f = \frac{f_c}{\cos\Delta_0}$$

This is the **Secant Law**, and $f$ is the **Maximum Usable Frequency (MUF)** for that particular elevation angle. Equivalently:

$$MUF = f_c \cdot \sec\Delta_0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The angle in the secant law is the **angle of elevation at the ground (transmitter)**, often written as $\Delta$. This is the convention used in KTU problems.

## 3.3 Derivation of Skip Distance $d_{skip}$

From the geometry of the triangle formed by the transmitter, the point where the wave returns to Earth, and the foot of the perpendicular to the ionospheric reflection point:

The wave leaves the transmitter at elevation angle $\Delta$, travels up to the ionosphere, and is reflected back down. Using simple trigonometry on the equivalent isosceles triangle with apex at the reflection point:

$$d_{skip} = 2\,h'\,\tan\Delta$$

where $h'$ is the **virtual height** of the reflecting layer.

**Step-by-step logic:**
- The wave's ground range consists of two equal legs of length $L$ each.
- Each leg satisfies $\tan\Delta = \dfrac{\text{opposite}}{\text{adjacent}} = \dfrac{h'}{L}$ → $L = \dfrac{h'}{\tan\Delta}$.
- Total skip distance: $d_{skip} = 2L = \dfrac{2\,h'}{\tan\Delta}$.

This relationship is **reciprocally related** to the virtual height formula:

$$h' = \frac{d_{skip}}{2\,\tan\Delta}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** The maximum electron density of the F2 layer is $N_{max} = 1.5 \times 10^{12}\,\text{el/m}^3$. A station transmits at an elevation angle of $\Delta = 30°$. Find (i) the critical frequency, (ii) the MUF, (iii) the skip distance if the virtual height is 300 km.

**Solution:**

**(i) Critical Frequency:**

$$f_c = 9\,\sqrt{N_{max}}$$

$$f_c = 9\,\sqrt{1.5 \times 10^{12}}$$

$$\sqrt{1.5 \times 10^{12}} = \sqrt{1.5} \times 10^6 = 1.2247 \times 10^6$$

$$f_c = 9 \times 1.2247 \times 10^6 = 1.1022 \times 10^7 \text{ Hz} \approx 11.02 \text{ MHz}$$

**(ii) MUF (Secant Law):**

$$MUF = \frac{f_c}{\cos 30°} = \frac{11.02 \text{ MHz}}{0.866}$$

$$MUF \approx 12.73 \text{ MHz}$$

**(iii) Skip Distance:**

$$d_{skip} = 2\,h'\,\tan\Delta = 2 \times 300 \times \tan 30°$$

$$d_{skip} = 600 \times 0.5774 \approx 346.4 \text{ km}$$

**Answers:** $f_c \approx 11.02$ **MHz**, $MUF \approx 12.73$ **MHz**, $d_{skip} \approx 346.4$ **km**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ionosphere Layer Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A0[Ground Level 0 km] --> A1[D Layer 60 to 90 km]
    A1 --> A2[E Layer 100 to 150 km]
    A2 --> A3[F1 Layer 150 to 250 km]
    A3 --> A4[F2 Layer 250 to 400 km]

    A1 -.Daytime only absorbs LF MF.-> X1[Vanishes at night]
    A2 -.Reflects HF up to 10 MHz.-> X2[Sporadic E at night]
    A3 -.Merges with F2 at night.-> X3[Combined F layer at night]
    A4 -.Primary reflecting layer for HF.-> X4[Persistent day and night]

    classDef layer fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
    classDef note fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:1px,color:#0f172a
    class A0,A1,A2,A3,A4 layer
    class X1,X2,X3,X4 note
```

## 4.2 Sky Wave Path and Skip Zone Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TX[Transmitter TX] -->|Elevation angle delta| REFL[Ionospheric Layer F2]
    REFL -->|Refracted wave| RX[Receiver RX at skip distance]
    TX -.Ground wave dies out.-> DEAD[Dead Zone SKIP ZONE]
    RX -.Beyond skip distance.-> FAR[Far Zone multi hop]

    GR[Ground Surface Curved] --- TX
    GR --- DEAD
    GR --- RX
    GR --- FAR

    classDef radio fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
    classDef zone fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
    classDef geo fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:1px,color:#0f172a
    class TX,RX,REFL radio
    class DEAD,FAR zone
    class GR geo
```

## 4.3 Functional Block Flow — Sky Wave Communication Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SOURCE["Source Stage"]
        S1[Information Source audio or data]
    end

    subgraph TXSTAGE["Transmitter Stage"]
        S2[Modulator HF Carrier 2 to 30 MHz]
        S3[Power Amplifier]
        S4[Transmitting Antenna high gain directional]
    end

    subgraph CHANNEL["Ionospheric Channel"]
        S5[Free Space Propagation upward]
        S6[Ionosphere F2 Layer refraction bending]
        S7[Ground Reflection multipath component]
    end

    subgraph RXSTAGE["Receiver Stage"]
        S8[Receiving Antenna]
        S9[Low Noise Amplifier]
        S10[Demodulator]
    end

    subgraph SINK["Sink Stage"]
        S11[Output Speaker or Display]
    end

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> S5 --> S6 --> S8 --> S9 --> S10 --> S11
    S6 -.Reflected sky wave.-> S8
    S7 -.Multi hop path.-> S6

    classDef stage fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#075985,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7,S8,S9,S10,S11 stage
```

## 4.4 Multi-Hop Sky Wave Diagram (Intercontinental Link)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Hop 1 TX to Earth] --> B[Hop 2 Earth to Ionosphere]
    B --> C[Hop 3 Ionosphere to Earth]
    C --> D[Hop 4 Earth to Ionosphere]
    D --> E[Hop 5 Ionosphere to RX]

    AT[Transmitter] --> A
    AR[Receiver] --- E

    ION[(Ionosphere F2 layer)] -.Bends each hop.-> A
    ION -.Bends each hop.-> B
    ION -.Bends each hop.-> C
    ION -.Bends each hop.-> D

    GROUND[Curved Earth Surface] --- AT
    GROUND --- A
    GROUND --- C
    GROUND --- AR

    classDef hop fill:#fef9c3,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:2px,color:#0f172a
    classDef geo fill:#f5f5f4,stroke:#44403c,stroke-width:1px,color:#0f172a
    classDef node fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:1px,color:#0f172a
    class A,B,C,D,E hop
    class AT,AR,ION,GROUND node
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Inter-Hint:** Each "hop" loses energy due to ground reflection and ionospheric absorption. For trans-oceanic paths, **3 to 5 hops** is the practical limit before signal-to-noise ratio becomes unacceptable.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define sky wave propagation. Why is it not used for frequencies above 30 MHz?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Sky wave propagation is the mode of radio wave propagation in which electromagnetic waves radiated from a transmitting antenna at an angle above the horizontal are refracted back to Earth by the ionized layers of the upper atmosphere (ionosphere, 60–400 km).

It is **not used for frequencies above 30 MHz** because:
1. At higher frequencies, the wave penetrates through the ionosphere **without being refracted back**, because the refractive index $n = \sqrt{1 - 81N/f^2}$ tends to 1 as $f$ increases.
2. The required electron density to bend such high-frequency waves would be impractically large.
3. Above ~30 MHz, the wave escapes into outer space (used in satellite communication via space waves).

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**What is critical frequency? Write the expression for the critical frequency of the ionosphere.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Critical frequency** ($f_c$) is the **maximum frequency of a radio wave that can be reflected back to Earth by the ionosphere when transmitted vertically upward** (angle of incidence = 0°). It is determined by the maximum electron density $N_{max}$ of the reflecting layer.

The expression is:

$$f_c = 9\,\sqrt{N_{max}}$$

where $f_c$ is in **Hz** and $N_{max}$ is the maximum electron density in **electrons per cubic meter**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students frequently **omit units** or interchange Hz with MHz. Always state the unit and confirm that the numerical value is consistent. Another common error: writing $f_c = 9\sqrt{N_{max}/\pi m}$ — the constant 9 already includes all physical constants; **do not add extra factors**.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]`
**(a)** Derive the expression for the **critical frequency** of the ionosphere. Explain its significance in sky wave communication. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A radio wave is transmitted at an elevation angle of $\Delta = 20°$ toward an ionospheric layer having a critical frequency of $8$ MHz and a virtual height of $250$ km. Calculate **(i)** the MUF, and **(ii)** the skip distance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Question A):**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Critical Frequency

**Given:** Refractive index of the ionospheric plasma,
$$n = \sqrt{1 - \frac{81\,N}{f^2}}$$

**Step 1:** For a vertically transmitted wave to be reflected back, the refractive index at the point of maximum electron density $N_{max}$ must drop to **zero**:
$$n = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 0 = \sqrt{1 - \frac{81\,N_{max}}{f^2}}$$

**Step 2:** Squaring:
$$1 - \frac{81\,N_{max}}{f^2} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{81\,N_{max}}{f^2} = 1$$

**Step 3:** The minimum frequency that satisfies this is the **critical frequency** $f_c$:
$$f_c = 9\,\sqrt{N_{max}}$$

**Significance:**
- It is the **highest frequency** that can be reflected back at vertical incidence.
- It determines the **upper limit** of frequencies usable for sky wave on any given day, as a function of solar activity.
- It is used to compute the **MUF** for any angle of elevation using the secant law.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** $f_c = 8$ MHz, $\Delta = 20°$, $h' = 250$ km.

**(i) MUF (using Secant Law):**

$$MUF = f_c \cdot \sec\Delta = \frac{8}{\cos 20°}$$

$$\cos 20° = 0.9397$$

$$MUF = \frac{8}{0.9397} \approx 8.514 \text{ MHz}$$

**(ii) Skip Distance:**

$$d_{skip} = 2\,h'\,\tan\Delta = 2 \times 250 \times \tan 20°$$

$$\tan 20° = 0.3640$$

$$d_{skip} = 500 \times 0.3640 \approx 182.0 \text{ km}$$

**Final Answers:** $MUF \approx 8.51$ **MHz**, $d_{skip} \approx 182$ **km**.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Modified]`
**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **structure of the ionosphere** and the role of each layer in sky wave propagation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The maximum usable frequency for a transmission at an elevation angle of $15°$ is found to be $20$ MHz. Find: **(i)** the critical frequency of the ionosphere, and **(ii)** the maximum electron density of the layer. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Question B):**

#### Part (a) — Structure of the Ionosphere

**Diagram (ASCII representation for the answer script):**

```
   Altitude (km)
     400 ┬────────────────── F2 layer (250–400 km)  ← main HF reflector
         │   ⤴ max N here
     250 ┤
         │   F1 layer (150–250 km)                  ← merges into F2 at night
     150 ┤
         │   E layer (100–150 km)                   ← reflects HF up to 10 MHz
     100 ┤
         │
      90 ┤   D layer (60–90 km)                     ← absorbs LF/MF, vanishes at night
      60 ┤
         ┴────────────────── Ground (0 km)
```

**Role of each layer:**

1. **D Layer (60–90 km):** Lowest ionized layer; exists only in daytime. It **absorbs** LF and MF waves, causing high attenuation. Disappears at night, dramatically improving HF reception.

2. **E Layer (100–150 km):** Moderate ionization. Reflects HF waves up to ~10 MHz. During daytime, it is the principal refracting layer for low HF; at night, it weakens but **sporadic E** can occasionally support long-distance VHF contacts.

3. **F1 Layer (150–250 km):** Exists only during the day. Often merges with F2 at night. Contributes to HF refraction during daytime.

4. **F2 Layer (250–400 km):** **Highest electron density** and the **primary reflecting layer for HF communication**, persisting day and night. It is the layer that supports transcontinental sky wave communication.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation

**Given:** $MUF = 20$ MHz, $\Delta = 15°$.

**(i) Critical Frequency (using inverse of Secant Law):**

$$MUF = f_c \cdot \sec\Delta \quad \Rightarrow \quad f_c = MUF \cdot \cos\Delta$$

$$f_c = 20 \times \cos 15° = 20 \times 0.9659 \approx 19.32 \text{ MHz}$$

**(ii) Maximum Electron Density:**

Using $f_c = 9\,\sqrt{N_{max}}$:

$$\sqrt{N_{max}} = \frac{f_c}{9} = \frac{19.32 \times 10^6}{9} = 2.1467 \times 10^6$$

$$N_{max} = (2.1467 \times 10^6)^2 \approx 4.608 \times 10^{12} \text{ el/m}^3$$

**Final Answers:** $f_c \approx 19.32$ **MHz**, $N_{max} \approx 4.61 \times 10^{12}$ **el/m³**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Mixing up the angle:** Students often use $\cos\Delta$ as the multiplier when they should divide. Recall: $\Delta$ is the **angle of elevation at the ground**, and the **MUF is at the numerator** of the secant, NOT $f_c$. Wrong: $f_c = MUF \times \sec\Delta$. Correct: $MUF = f_c \times \sec\Delta$.
> 2. **Unit errors in $N_{max}$:** The formula $f_c = 9\sqrt{N_{max}}$ gives $f_c$ in Hz. If you use $f_c$ in MHz, you must convert carefully or the $N_{max}$ will be off by a factor of $10^{12}$.
> 3. **Drawing the ionosphere in 2D without altitude labels:** KTU examiners award 1–2 marks purely for a **labelled diagram**. Always annotate altitudes and identify each layer.
> 4. **Forgetting to square $N_{max}$:** A common slip is writing $N_{max} = f_c / 9$ instead of $N_{max} = (f_c / 9)^2$.
> 5. **Confusing virtual height with true height:** Virtual height is *always greater* than the true height of reflection. The KTU question stem will usually say "virtual height," so do not derive the true height unless asked.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Sky wave** = wave refracted back by the **ionosphere**; useful in the **HF band (2–30 MHz)**.
- ⭐ The **ionosphere** spans **60–400 km** and consists of **D, E, F1, F2** layers; **F2** is the principal HF reflector.
- ⭐ Refractive index of ionospheric plasma: $n = \sqrt{1 - 81N/f^2}$ (always **less than 1**).
- ⭐ **Critical frequency** $f_c = 9\sqrt{N_{max}}$ — the **maximum** frequency reflected at vertical incidence.
- ⭐ **MUF (Secant Law):** $MUF = f_c / \cos\Delta = f_c \cdot \sec\Delta$, where $\Delta$ is the angle of elevation.
- ⭐ **Skip distance:** $d_{skip} = 2 h' \tan\Delta$ — the **minimum** distance at which the sky wave returns to Earth.
- ⭐ **Virtual height** $h'$ > actual height of reflection; computed from the equivalent triangle.
- ⭐ **OWF (Optimum Working Frequency)** $\approx 0.85 \times$ MUF (always operate below MUF for reliability).
- ⭐ **Fading** in sky wave arises from **multi-path interference** between hops and from ionospheric irregularities.
- ⭐ Sky waves are **refracted, not geometrically reflected** — the medium has a *gradient* of electron density.
- ⭐ **D layer disappears at night** → reduced absorption → better long-distance HF reception after sunset.
- ⭐ **Above ~30 MHz**, sky waves escape the ionosphere → use **space wave** (line-of-sight) or **satellite** communication.
- ⭐ Multi-hop propagation enables **transcontinental** and **intercontinental** communication.
- ⭐ Always quote $N_{max}$ in **el/m³** and $f_c$ in **Hz** when using the formula $f_c = 9\sqrt{N_{max}}$.
- ⭐ Always include a **labelled diagram** of the ionosphere in long-answer questions — examiners reward it.
- ⭐ **Common exam question types:** (i) derive $f_c$, (ii) compute MUF/skip distance, (iii) draw and explain the ionosphere, (iv) compare sky wave with ground/space wave.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Strategy:** Before walking into the exam, write down on a corner of your answer book: "**$f_c = 9\sqrt{N_{max}}$**, **$MUF = f_c \sec\Delta$**, **$d = 2h'\tan\Delta$**" — these three formulas cover ~80% of all sky wave problems in KTU Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Line-of-Sight (LoS) Propagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Line-of-Sight (LoS) Propagation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Line-of-Sight (LoS) Propagation** is a radio-wave propagation mode in which the transmitted signal travels in a **straight, unobstructed path** from the transmitting antenna to the receiving antenna. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus for OECST612 (Data Communication), LoS propagation governs wireless transmissions where the antennas are positioned such that a direct geometric "line of sight" exists between them, unimpeded by the curvature of the Earth, hills, dense buildings, or other major physical obstructions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 1):**
> LoS Transmission, LoS Propagation, Free Space Loss, Attenuation, Distortion, Noise (Thermal, Intermodulation, Crosstalk, Impulse), and Channel Capacity (Nyquist & Shannon Theorems) are grouped together because they collectively describe the *propagation impairments* and *link budget* fundamentals of any data communication channel.

Mathematically, LoS is the dominant mode for frequencies above roughly **30 MHz** (VHF and above), where ground-wave and sky-wave components become negligible and electromagnetic energy essentially radiates like a beam of light from the transmitter.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Flashlight in a Foggy Field"

Imagine you are standing in the middle of a large open field at night, holding a powerful flashlight, while your friend stands **100 m away** facing you.

- **If the field is clear (no fog):** The flashlight beam travels in a perfectly straight line and forms a bright, well-defined circle on your friend's chest. The further away your friend moves, the **dimmer** the circle becomes, but it is still clearly visible. This is **free-space LoS propagation** — the signal weakens with distance, but the path is geometrically clean.
- **If your friend moves behind a hill:** The beam is blocked completely, and your friend sees nothing. This illustrates a **Non-Line-of-Sight (NLoS)** scenario, where LoS fails.
- **If there is light fog:** The beam spreads slightly, some light is scattered, and the brightness drops faster than in clear air. This mimics the effect of **attenuation and noise** introduced by the medium (rain, gas molecules, multipath scatterers).

So, in LoS propagation, the core idea is:
> "The transmitter *sees* the receiver directly, and the signal is essentially a ray of electromagnetic energy traveling through free space (or air) in a straight line, losing strength as the square of the distance."

### 1.3 Why LoS Matters in Data Communication

LoS is the *baseline reference model* used to compute **link budgets, cell-tower ranges, satellite link margins, and Wi-Fi coverage**. Almost every wireless system you use — cellular 4G/5G, satellite TV, microwave backhaul, GPS, FM radio — relies on LoS to be operational. Without a clear LoS, the receiver would only get scattered, reflected, or refracted copies of the signal, leading to severe **multipath fading**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in This Module:**
> - Speed of light in free space: $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ **m/s**
> - Characteristic impedance of free space: $Z_0 = 377$ **Ω**
> - Boltzmann constant: $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K**

### 1.4 Types of Wireless Propagation (Contextual Overview)

| Mode | Frequency Range | Dominant Mechanism | LoS Relevance |
|------|----------------|--------------------|---------------|
| Ground Wave | Below 2 MHz | Surface wave along Earth | Not LoS |
| Sky Wave | 2 – 30 MHz | Ionosphere reflection | Not LoS |
| **Line-of-Sight** | **Above 30 MHz** | **Direct straight-line path** | **Pure LoS** |

This module focuses exclusively on the **LoS regime**, which is the operational domain of modern digital data communication systems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The LoS Propagation Model — Step-by-Step Logic

A wireless LoS link behaves like an "energy pipeline" between a transmitter and a receiver. The transmitted power $P_t$ is spread over the surface of an ever-expanding sphere centered on the transmitting antenna. The receiver then intercepts a tiny fraction of that expanding wavefront through its effective aperture. The result is a predictable **inverse-square power falloff** with distance.

**Step 1 — Isotropic Radiator Assumption:**
For analysis, we model the transmitter as an *isotropic* antenna (radiates equally in all directions). The power per unit area (power flux density) at distance $d$ is:

$$\text{Power Flux Density} = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2}$$

**Step 2 — Effective Aperture of the Receiver:**
A real antenna of gain $G_r$ presents an *effective aperture*:

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

**Step 3 — Power Captured at the Receiver:**
The received power is the product of flux density and aperture:

$$P_r = P_t \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$

This is the celebrated **Friis Transmission Equation** — the foundation of all LoS link-budget analysis.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The $\left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ term is called the **Free Space Path Loss (FSPL)** factor. It is the single most important quantity in any wireless link budget. Note that the loss is **inversely proportional to $\lambda^2$** — meaning **higher frequencies suffer more free-space loss** for the same distance.

### 2.2 Free Space Loss (FSPL) — The Headline Formula

Expressed in decibels (dB) for engineering convenience:

$$L_{FS} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44 \quad \text{(d in km, f in MHz)}$$

$$L_{FS} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + -27.55 \quad \text{(d in m, f in MHz)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant `32.44` Derivation Hint:**
> $20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{4\pi \times 10^3}{3 \times 10^2}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(41.88) \approx 32.44$

### 2.3 Attenuation

**Attenuation** is the gradual loss of signal strength as it travels through the medium. In LoS propagation, attenuation arises from:

1. **Free Space Loss (FSPL)** — geometric spreading (always present).
2. **Atmospheric Absorption** — by oxygen, water vapor, and rain (frequency-dependent).
3. **Path Obstructions** — partial diffraction losses when foliage, buildings, or terrain intrude into the first Fresnel zone.

The general received-power form is:

$$P_r \text{ (dBm)} = P_t \text{ (dBm)} + G_t \text{ (dBi)} + G_r \text{ (dBi)} - L_{FS} \text{ (dB)} - L_{other} \text{ (dB)}$$

### 2.4 Distortion

**Distortion** is the *change in shape* of the signal waveform. In a LoS channel, distortion arises when:

- Different frequency components of a wideband signal experience **different attenuation** (frequency-selective fading) — also called *linear distortion*.
- The channel exhibits **non-linear behavior** (e.g., near a high-power amplifier or a metallic surface that rectifies the signal) — *non-linear distortion*.
- **Multipath echoes** from nearby reflectors arrive with different delays, smearing the pulse and causing **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)**.

The two most common distortion metrics in data communication are:

- **Attenuation Distortion** — unequal gain across the band.
- **Delay Distortion** — unequal propagation delay across the band.

### 2.5 Noise — The Unavoidable Companion

Noise is any unwanted signal that corrupts the desired data. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly lists four key noise types:

| Noise Type | Source / Physical Origin | Spectrum |
|------------|--------------------------|----------|
| **Thermal Noise (Johnson-Nyquist)** | Random electron motion in conductors at temperature $T$ | White, Gaussian |
| **Intermodulation Noise** | Non-linear mixing of two frequencies producing sum/difference tones | Spurious tones at $f_1 \pm f_2$, $2f_1$, $2f_2$, … |
| **Crosstalk** | Coupling of signal from one channel/wire to an adjacent one | Coupling-dependent |
| **Impulse Noise** | Sudden, high-amplitude spikes (lightning, switching, EM discharge) | Brief, high-energy bursts |

The most fundamental of these is **thermal noise**, with the single-sided power spectral density:

$$N_0 = kT \quad \text{(W/Hz)}$$

Total noise power in bandwidth $B$:

$$N = kTB \quad \text{(W)}$$

In dBm:

$$N \text{ (dBm)} = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(B \text{ in Hz}) + 10 \log_{10}(T/290)$$

### 2.6 Channel Capacity — Nyquist & Shannon Theorems

**Nyquist Bit Rate (Noiseless Channel):**

$$C = 2B \log_{2}(M) \quad \text{(bps)}$$

Where $M$ = number of discrete signal levels (signal states).

**Shannon Capacity (Noisy Channel):**

$$C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right) \quad \text{(bps)}$$

Where $S/N$ is the signal-to-noise ratio (linear, not dB).

> [!NOTE]
> **The Fundamental Trade-off:** Nyquist tells you how fast you can push symbols (limited only by bandwidth), while Shannon tells you the *absolute* maximum data rate achievable given the unavoidable presence of noise. Shannon's bound is **unreachable in practice** but is the gold standard ceiling.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables / Units | Domain / Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | $P$ in W, $d$ in m, $\lambda$ in m | Friis TX Equation — LoS link budget |
| 2 | $L_{FS} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz, $L_{FS}$ in dB | Free-space path loss |
| 3 | $L_{FS} = 20\log_{10}(d) + 20\log_{10}(f) - 27.55$ | $d$ in m, $f$ in MHz, $L_{FS}$ in dB | Alternative form |
| 4 | $A_e = \dfrac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$ | $A_e$ in m² | Effective antenna aperture |
| 5 | $P_r (\text{dBm}) = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{FS} - L_{other}$ | All in dB | Link-budget equation |
| 6 | $N_0 = kT$ | $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K, $T$ in K | Thermal noise PSD |
| 7 | $N = kTB$ | $N$ in W, $B$ in Hz | Total thermal noise in bandwidth $B$ |
| 8 | $C = 2B \log_{2}(M)$ | $C$ in bps, $M$ = signal levels | Nyquist bit rate |
| 9 | $C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $C$ in bps, $S/N$ linear | Shannon capacity |
| 10 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $f$ in Hz | Wavelength from frequency |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to convert $S/N$ from **dB to linear** before plugging into Shannon's formula. A 30 dB SNR equals $10^{30/10} = 1000$, **not** 30. Always convert: $\dfrac{S}{N}\bigg|_{\text{linear}} = 10^{(S/N)_{\text{dB}}/10}$.

### 2.8 Real-World Utility of This System

- **Cellular Network Planning:** Engineers use the FSPL formula to compute cell radius and tower density for 4G/5G.
- **Satellite Communication:** LoS link budgets drive uplink/downlink power margins for VSAT and DTH systems.
- **Wi-Fi & Bluetooth:** Indoor LoS path loss models (log-distance, ITU indoor) extend the FSPL concept.
- **Shannon's Theorem** dictates the upper bound of every modern modulation/coding standard (5G NR, Wi-Fi 6, DOCSIS).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Friis Transmission Equation

We begin with an **isotropic** transmitter radiating power $P_t$ uniformly. The power spreads over the surface of a sphere of radius $d$:

$$S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \quad \text{[W/m}^2\text{]}$$

Here, $S$ is the power flux density at the receiver.

For a *real* transmitting antenna of gain $G_t$, the power density is enhanced in the desired direction:

$$S = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2}$$

The receiving antenna intercepts a portion of this wavefront through its effective aperture $A_e$:

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t A_e}{4\pi d^2}$$

The effective aperture of the receiving antenna in terms of its gain $G_r$ and wavelength $\lambda$ is:

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

Substituting back:

$$P_r = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2} \cdot \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi} = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$

This completes the derivation of the **Friis Transmission Equation**.

$$\boxed{\,P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2\,}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Free-Space Path Loss in dB (d in km, f in MHz)

Starting from:

$$L_{FS} = \left(\frac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^2$$

Take $10 \log_{10}$ on both sides:

$$L_{FS} \text{ (dB)} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)^2\right] = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi d f}{c}\right)$$

Substitute $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s = $3 \times 10^5$ km/s. With $d$ in km and $f$ in MHz:

$$\frac{4\pi d f}{c} = \frac{4\pi \cdot d_{[\text{km}]} \cdot 10^3 \cdot f_{[\text{MHz}]} \cdot 10^6}{3 \times 10^8} = \frac{4\pi}{3} \cdot d_{[\text{km}]} \cdot f_{[\text{MHz}]}$$

Therefore:

$$L_{FS} \text{ (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{3}\right) + 20 \log_{10}(d_{[\text{km}]}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{[\text{MHz}]})$$

Evaluate the constant:

$$20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{3}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(4.1888) \approx 32.44$$

Thus:

$$\boxed{\,L_{FS} \text{ (dB)} = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{[\text{km}]}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{[\text{MHz}]})\,}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Thermal Noise Power

Thermal noise arises from random thermal agitation of electrons in any resistive element. By the **equipartition theorem**, the average noise power delivered by a resistor at temperature $T$ across a bandwidth $B$ is:

$$N = kTB$$

Where:
- $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ **J/K** (Boltzmann constant)
- $T$ = absolute temperature in **Kelvin**
- $B$ = bandwidth in **Hz**

In dBm form (with $T_0 = 290$ K reference):

$$N \text{ (dBm)} = 10 \log_{10}(k T_0) + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{T}{T_0}\right) + 10 \log_{10}(B)$$

Substituting $k T_0 = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 290 \approx 4 \times 10^{-21}$ W = $4 \times 10^{-18}$ mW:

$$10 \log_{10}(4 \times 10^{-18}) = -174 \text{ dBm/Hz}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\,N \text{ (dBm)} = -174 + 10 \log_{10}(B \text{ in Hz}) + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{T}{290}\right)\,}$$

### 3.4 Worked Example 1 — Free-Space Path Loss Calculation

**Problem:** A transmitter operates at **2.4 GHz** (Wi-Fi band) with an output power of **+20 dBm**. The receiver is at a distance of **500 m** in clear LoS. The transmit and receive antennas have gains of **+6 dBi** each. Compute the received power. Assume no other losses.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Compute FSPL.

$$L_{FS} = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(0.5) + 20 \log_{10}(2400)$$

$$20 \log_{10}(0.5) = -6.02 \text{ dB}$$

$$20 \log_{10}(2400) = 20 \times 3.3802 = 67.60 \text{ dB}$$

$$L_{FS} = 32.44 - 6.02 + 67.60 = 94.02 \text{ dB}$$

Step 2 — Apply the link budget equation.

$$P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{FS} - L_{other}$$

$$P_r = 20 + 6 + 6 - 94.02 - 0 = -61.02 \text{ dBm}$$

**Result:** The received power is approximately **−61 dBm**, which is comfortably above the typical Wi-Fi receiver sensitivity of −85 dBm, leaving a healthy ~24 dB link margin.

### 3.5 Worked Example 2 — Shannon Channel Capacity

**Problem:** A communication channel has a bandwidth of **4 kHz** and an SNR of **30 dB**. Determine:
(a) The maximum theoretical data rate (Shannon).
(b) The Nyquist bit rate if the channel uses **16 signal levels** ($M = 16$).

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Shannon Capacity:**

Convert SNR from dB to linear:

$$\frac{S}{N} = 10^{30/10} = 10^3 = 1000$$

Apply Shannon:

$$C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) = 4000 \times \log_{2}(1001)$$

$$\log_{2}(1001) = \frac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} \approx \frac{3.0004}{0.30103} \approx 9.967$$

$$C \approx 4000 \times 9.967 \approx 39{,}870 \text{ bps} \approx 39.87 \text{ kbps}$$

**Part (b) — Nyquist Bit Rate:**

$$C = 2B \log_{2}(M) = 2 \times 4000 \times \log_{2}(16)$$

$$\log_{2}(16) = 4$$

$$C = 8000 \times 4 = 32{,}000 \text{ bps} = 32 \text{ kbps}$$

**Comparative Insight:** Shannon allows ~39.87 kbps assuming an ideal Gaussian code, but Nyquist caps us at 32 kbps with 16 discrete levels. The difference (7.87 kbps) reflects the redundancy an *ideal* code would need to combat noise.

### 3.6 Worked Example 3 — Antenna Effective Aperture

**Problem:** A parabolic dish antenna operating at **6 GHz** has a gain of **+38 dBi**. Compute its effective aperture.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Convert gain to linear.

$$G_r = 10^{38/10} = 10^{3.8} = 6309.6$$

Step 2 — Compute wavelength.

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^9} = 0.05 \text{ m} = 5 \text{ cm}$$

Step 3 — Apply the aperture formula.

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi} = \frac{6309.6 \times (0.05)^2}{4\pi} = \frac{6309.6 \times 0.0025}{12.566}$$

$$A_e = \frac{15.774}{12.566} \approx 1.255 \text{ m}^2$$

**Result:** The dish has an effective aperture of about **1.26 m²** — typical of a ~1.4 m C-band VSAT antenna.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 LoS Propagation System — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Data Source] --> encA[Source Encoder]
    encA --> chnA[Channel Encoder]
    chnA --> modA[Modulator]
    modA --> ampA[Power Amplifier]
    ampA --> antA[Transmit Antenna]
    antA -->|Free Space LoS Path| chnlA[Atmospheric Channel with Noise and Attenuation]
    chnlA --> antB[Receive Antenna]
    antB --> lnaA[Low Noise Amplifier]
    lnaA --> demodA[Demodulator]
    demodA --> chnD[Channel Decoder]
    chnD --> srcD[Source Decoder]
    srcD --> sinkA[Data Sink]
    noiseA[Noise Sources: Thermal, Crosstalk, Impulse, Intermodulation] -.-> chnlA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram:** The flow moves from left (transmitter) to right (receiver). The dotted cyan injection at the channel represents **noise coupling**, which is the central impairment the LoS model must account for.

### 4.2 Free-Space Path Loss vs. Distance / Frequency — Visual Logic Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    fsplA[Free Space Path Loss L sub FS] --> depA[Depends on Distance d]
    fsplA --> depB[Depends on Frequency f]
    depA --> factA[20 log10 of d: doubles loss per 2x distance, +6 dB]
    depB --> factB[20 log10 of f: doubles loss per 2x frequency, +6 dB]
    factA --> constA[Constant 32.44 dB for km and MHz]
    factB --> constA
    constA --> resultA[Higher Frequency and Longer Distance produce Greater Loss]
```

### 4.3 Channel Capacity — Shannon vs. Nyquist Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    capA[Channel Capacity Problem] --> qA{Is the Channel Noisy?}
    qA -->|No, ideal noiseless| nypA[Use Nyquist Bit Rate]
    nypA --> nypF[C = 2 B log2 of M]
    qA -->|Yes, thermal and other noise present| shnA[Use Shannon Capacity]
    shnA --> shnF[C = B log2 of 1 plus S over N]
    shnF --> warnA[Always convert S over N from dB to Linear]
    warnA --> outA[Shannon Bound is Theoretical Maximum, Unreachable in Practice]
```

### 4.4 Noise Classification Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nseA[Communication Channel Noise] --> thA[Thermal Noise: kTB]
    nseA --> imdA[Intermodulation Noise: f1 plus or minus f2 sums and differences]
    nseA --> xtA[Crosstalk: Coupling from Adjacent Channels]
    nseA --> impA[Impulse Noise: Lightning, Switching, EM Discharge]
    thA --> propA[White Gaussian, Always Present]
    imdA --> propB[Spurious Tones, Predictable Frequencies]
    xtA --> propC[Channel-Dependent, Reduced by Shielding]
    impA --> propD[Rare High-Energy Bursts, Hard to Predict]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why Block Diagrams Instead of Field-Drawings?** LoS propagation in the physical world involves curved wavefronts, antenna radiation patterns, and Fresnel zones — extremely difficult to render in Mermaid. Block diagrams emphasize the *signal flow* and *impairment injection points*, which is what the KTU examiner rewards in the answer script.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define Line-of-Sight propagation. Under what conditions does a wireless link become Non-Line-of-Sight (NLoS)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Line-of-Sight (LoS) propagation is a mode of radio-wave transmission in which the signal travels in a **straight, unobstructed path** from the transmitting antenna to the receiving antenna. It is the dominant propagation mode for frequencies above **30 MHz**. (2 marks)
A wireless link becomes Non-Line-of-Sight (NLoS) when the direct path between the transmitter and receiver is **obstructed** by the Earth's curvature, hills, dense buildings, foliage, or other physical structures, forcing the signal to rely on **diffraction, reflection, or scattering** to reach the receiver. (1 mark)

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **List the four major types of noise that affect a data communication channel. Briefly state the source of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Thermal Noise** — random motion of electrons in conductors; quantified by $N = kTB$. (1 mark)
2. **Intermodulation Noise** — produced when signals share a non-linear medium, generating sum/difference frequencies. (1 mark)
3. **Crosstalk** — unwanted coupling of signal energy between adjacent channels/wires. (0.5 mark)
4. **Impulse Noise** — irregular high-amplitude spikes from lightning, switching transients, or electromagnetic discharge. (0.5 mark)

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`
> **(a)** Derive the **Friis Transmission Equation** for free-space Line-of-Sight propagation and explain the significance of each term. (7 marks)
> **(b)** A transmitter operating at **5.8 GHz** radiates **+30 dBm** through an antenna of gain **+12 dBi**. The receiver antenna has a gain of **+18 dBi** and is located at a distance of **2 km** in clear LoS. Compute the received power assuming no other losses. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation of Friis Transmission Equation (7 marks):**

For an isotropic radiator of power $P_t$, the power flux density at distance $d$ is:

$$S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2}$$

For a real transmitting antenna of gain $G_t$:

$$S = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2}$$

The receiving antenna with effective aperture $A_e$ intercepts:

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t A_e}{4\pi d^2}$$

Effective aperture in terms of receive gain $G_r$ and wavelength $\lambda$:

$$A_e = \frac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

Therefore:

$$P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$

**Significance of each term (3 marks for explanation):**
- $P_t$ — transmit power (sets initial signal level).
- $G_t, G_r$ — antenna gains (focus radiated energy in the desired direction).
- $\left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ — **Free Space Path Loss (FSPL) factor** capturing geometric spreading and frequency dependence.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Link Budget (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Convert gains to dB values (already in dB form):
- $P_t = +30$ dBm
- $G_t = +12$ dBi
- $G_r = +18$ dBi

**Step 2:** Compute FSPL at $f = 5800$ MHz and $d = 2$ km:

$$L_{FS} = 32.44 + 20 \log_{10}(2) + 20 \log_{10}(5800)$$

$$20 \log_{10}(2) = 6.02 \text{ dB}$$

$$20 \log_{10}(5800) = 20 \times 3.7634 = 75.27 \text{ dB}$$

$$L_{FS} = 32.44 + 6.02 + 75.27 = 113.73 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3:** Apply link budget equation:

$$P_r = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{FS} - L_{other}$$

$$P_r = 30 + 12 + 18 - 113.73 - 0 = -53.73 \text{ dBm}$$

**Result:** The received power is **−53.73 dBm** (≈ 4.2 × 10⁻⁹ W).

---

> **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`
> **(a)** Explain the concepts of **Attenuation** and **Distortion** in a communication channel. Differentiate between attenuation distortion and delay distortion. (7 marks)
> **(b)** A channel of bandwidth **3.5 kHz** has an SNR of **40 dB**. Compute (i) the Shannon capacity, and (ii) the Nyquist bit rate if **64 signal levels** are used. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Attenuation and Distortion (7 marks):**

**Attenuation (2 marks):** Loss of signal strength as the wave propagates through the medium. In LoS channels, it is dominated by **free-space path loss** and to a lesser extent by **atmospheric absorption** (oxygen, water vapor, rain).

**Distortion (2 marks):** Alteration of the signal waveform/shape during transmission. Distortion is *signal-quality* degradation, distinct from mere *amplitude reduction*.

**Difference (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Attenuation Distortion | Delay Distortion |
|--------|------------------------|-------------------|
| Definition | Different frequency components attenuated unequally | Different frequency components delayed unequally |
| Cause | Frequency-dependent loss in the medium | Frequency-dependent propagation velocity |
| Effect | Non-flat amplitude response | Pulse smearing and ISI |
| Compensation | Equalizer (gain) | Equalizer (delay / phase) |

---

**Part (b) — Channel Capacity Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Convert SNR to linear:

$$\frac{S}{N} = 10^{40/10} = 10^4 = 10{,}000$$

**Step 2:** Shannon capacity:

$$C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) = 3500 \times \log_{2}(10{,}001)$$

$$\log_{2}(10{,}001) = \frac{\log_{10}(10{,}001)}{\log_{10}(2)} \approx \frac{4.00004}{0.30103} \approx 13.288$$

$$C = 3500 \times 13.288 \approx 46{,}508 \text{ bps} \approx 46.51 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 3:** Nyquist bit rate with $M = 64$:

$$C = 2B \log_{2}(M) = 2 \times 3500 \times \log_{2}(64)$$

$$\log_{2}(64) = 6$$

$$C = 7000 \times 6 = 42{,}000 \text{ bps} = 42 \text{ kbps}$$

**Comparison:** Shannon (46.51 kbps) > Nyquist (42 kbps). The gap (~4.5 kbps) is the "noise margin" that an ideal code can exploit.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the constant** `32.44` in the FSPL formula when $d$ is in km and $f$ in MHz — and using the wrong sign (it is **positive**, not negative).
> 2. **Mixing units** in the FSPL formula (using $d$ in m with the `32.44` constant, or $d$ in km with the `-27.55` constant). Always match the unit convention.
> 3. **Not converting SNR from dB to linear** before substituting into Shannon's equation. This single mistake can cost the full 7 marks in Part B numerical.
> 4. **Confusing bandwidth $B$** in Hz vs. kHz vs. MHz. A 4 kHz voice channel vs. a 4 MHz video channel changes capacity by a factor of 1000.
> 5. **Writing Friis equation as $P_r = P_t G_t G_r / (4\pi d)^2$** — the correct form has $\lambda^2$ in the numerator, **not** $\lambda$ alone.
> 6. **Omitting the effective aperture derivation step** in Friis derivation — examiners allocate 2 marks specifically for the $A_e$ relationship.
> 7. **Stating "noise = -174 dBm"** — this is the *spectral density* per Hz, not the absolute noise power. Always write $N = -174 + 10\log_{10}(B)$.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — LoS Propagation:**

- [x] **LoS** dominates for $f > 30$ MHz; below that, ground/sky waves prevail.
- [x] **Friis Equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi d)^2$ — the single most important LoS formula.
- [x] **FSPL (dB):** $32.44 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ — memorize the constant.
- [x] **FSPL grows by 6 dB** for every doubling of distance **or** frequency.
- [x] **Higher frequency ⇒ higher FSPL** at the same distance (because $\lambda$ decreases).
- [x] **Thermal noise** is unavoidable: $N = kTB$ with $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K.
- [x] **Noise PSD at room temperature** is $-174$ dBm/Hz — a key reference value.
- [x] **Four noise types:** Thermal, Intermodulation, Crosstalk, Impulse — know one-line source for each.
- [x] **Attenuation** = loss of strength; **Distortion** = change of shape — never confuse them.
- [x] **Attenuation distortion** = unequal gain vs. frequency; **Delay distortion** = unequal delay vs. frequency.
- [x] **Nyquist:** $C = 2B \log_2(M)$ — for a noiseless, bandlimited channel.
- [x] **Shannon:** $C = B \log_2(1 + S/N)$ — for a noisy channel; convert SNR to linear!
- [x] **Effective aperture:** $A_e = G_r \lambda^2 / 4\pi$ — links antenna gain to physical size.
- [x] **Link budget:** $P_r (dBm) = P_t + G_t + G_r - L_{FS} - L_{other}$ — always in dB.
- [x] **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s — used in every wavelength calculation.
- [x] **Shannon's bound is unreachable** in practice; real systems (5G NR, Wi-Fi) approach it using advanced coding (LDPC, Polar codes).
- [x] **The KTU Module 1 wrap-up:** LoS + FSPL + Attenuation + Distortion + Noise + Capacity = the foundational vocabulary of every wireless engineer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Data to Digital Signal - Non-Return-to-Zero (NRZ)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Digital Data to Digital Signal - Non-Return-to-Zero (NRZ)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Non-Return-to-Zero (NRZ)** is a binary line coding (baseband transmission) scheme in which the signal **does not return to zero** (i.e., the baseline reference level) during the middle of a bit interval. Instead, the signal maintains a **constant, non-zero voltage level** for the entire duration of each bit period, and the level is changed (or not) only at the beginning of the next bit boundary.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terms, NRZ belongs to the class of **Digital-to-Digital Encoding techniques**, and is characterized by:

- A **DC component** (non-zero average value) due to the absence of zero-crossings.
- A **bandwidth** that is theoretically equal to **N/2 Hz** for a bit rate of **N bps** (using the ideal Nyquist bandwidth bound).
- A **synchronization problem** during long runs of identical bits (especially NRZ-Level), since the receiver may lose bit clock alignment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** NRZ is the *baseline* line coding scheme from which all other schemes (RZ, Manchester, AMI, etc.) are derived as improvements. The two principal KTU-mandated sub-variants are **NRZ-L (Level)** and **NRZ-I (Inverted) / NRZ-M (Mark)**. The examiner almost always expects the student to draw waveforms for both, identify the DC component, and comment on synchronization.

## 1.2 The Two NRZ Variants

### A. NRZ-Level (NRZ-L)
- Binary **1** → Positive voltage level (e.g., **+V** volts)
- Binary **0** → Negative voltage level (e.g., **−V** volts)
- The level represents the **absolute value of the bit** itself.

### B. NRZ-Inverted / NRZ-Mark (NRZ-I / NRZ-M)
- A **transition** (LOW → HIGH or HIGH → LOW) at the start of a bit interval represents a binary **1**.
- **No transition** at the start of a bit interval represents a binary **0**.
- Only the **presence or absence of a transition** carries information — the actual level is irrelevant.
- Also called *differential encoding* because the decoding is based on the **difference** between successive signal levels rather than the absolute level.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Differential Encoding:**
> In differential encoding, the bit value is determined by comparing the current signal state with the previous signal state, **not** by the absolute voltage. This makes the system **immune to polarity inversion** — if the wires are accidentally swapped, the data still decodes correctly.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> **Analogy — The Two-Color Flag System:**
>
> Imagine two children communicating by waving colored flags across a long field.
>
> - **NRZ-L** is like saying: *"Hold a RED flag when you mean YES, and a BLUE flag when you mean NO."* If the sunlight suddenly changes how red looks (like noise flipping polarity), the message is corrupted.
>
> - **NRZ-I** is like a smarter rule: *"Whenever I want to say YES, I SWITCH the flag color. To say NO, I keep the same color as before."* Now, even if the colors look slightly different, the *change* is what matters. This is why NRZ-I is preferred in noisy channels like magnetic recording and optical media.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value / Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Bit rate **N** | **bits per second (bps)** | Independent of line coding |
| Required Bandwidth (NRZ) | **N/2 Hz** (theoretical minimum) | Practical systems use **N Hz** |
| DC Component | **Non-zero** | Worst for NRZ-L with long 1s or 0s |
| Synchronization | **Poor** for long runs | Receiver loses clock |
| Polarity Sensitivity | **High for NRZ-L, Low for NRZ-I** | Key board-exam point |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Mechanism

### For NRZ-Level (NRZ-L):
1. The transmitter maps each incoming bit **b_k** ∈ {0, 1} to a voltage **s(t)**.
2. If **b_k = 1**, output **s(t) = +V** for the entire bit duration **T_b**.
3. If **b_k = 0**, output **s(t) = −V** for the entire bit duration **T_b**.
4. The signal is held constant until the next bit boundary.
5. At the receiver, a **simple threshold detector** at 0 V regenerates the bit.

### For NRZ-Inverted (NRZ-I):
1. The transmitter maintains a **current signal level state** (call it the *previous level*).
2. If the current bit **b_k = 1**, the transmitter **inverts** (toggles) the signal level.
3. If the current bit **b_k = 0**, the transmitter **retains** the previous level.
4. The receiver decodes by **comparing the current level with the previous level**:
   - Level changed → bit is **1**.
   - Level unchanged → bit is **0**.

## 2.2 Why the "Why" and "How" Matter for KTU Examinations

- **WHY use NRZ?** It is the **simplest possible digital encoding** — no clock-recovery pulses, no mid-bit transitions, easy hardware implementation. It forms the conceptual baseline from which RZ and Manchester are derived to fix its synchronization weakness.
- **HOW does it fail?** When a long sequence of identical bits (e.g., `0000...` or `1111...`) is transmitted, the signal stays flat for many bit periods. The receiver has no transitions to lock onto, so the **bit clock drifts**, causing bit errors. NRZ-I partially fixes this for long `1` strings (but not for long `0` strings).
- **WHY is NRZ-I preferred in practice?** Because it is **differential** — decoding depends only on whether a transition occurred, not on the absolute voltage. This gives **noise immunity against polarity reversal** and is used in **USB, USB 2.0 differential pairs**, magnetic storage (old HDDs/tapes), and optical recording.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the table below. KTU board questions almost always ask for either the **DC component**, **bandwidth**, or **signal rate (baud)** for a given NRZ scheme.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Bit rate (R_b) | $R_b = \dfrac{1}{T_b}$ | bps | $T_b$ = bit duration in seconds |
| 2 | Signal rate / Baud rate (NRZ) | $S = R_b$ | baud | **1 bit = 1 signal element** in NRZ |
| 3 | Minimum Theoretical Bandwidth | $B_{min} = \dfrac{R_b}{2}$ | Hz | Nyquist bound; practical = $R_b$ |
| 4 | Average DC Voltage (NRZ-L, equiprobable) | $V_{DC} = 0$ | V | If P(0) = P(1) = 0.5 |
| 5 | Average DC Voltage (NRZ-L, biased) | $V_{DC} = V \cdot \left[ P(1) - P(0) \right]$ | V | P(1), P(0) are bit probabilities |
| 6 | Average DC Voltage (NRZ-I) | $V_{DC} \approx 0$ | V | Because transitions balance out |
| 7 | Synchronization Quality | **Poor** | — | Worst case = long runs of identical bits |
| 8 | Polarity Inversion Immunity | NRZ-L: **No**; NRZ-I: **Yes** | — | Key KTU differentiator |
| 9 | Spectrum | Concentrated near DC and $R_b$ | — | Strong low-frequency content |
| 10 | Common Applications | Magnetic recording, USB, legacy RS-232 (NRZ-L), Optical (NRZ-I) | — | KTU 2024 syllabus reference |

> **Important:** The vertical pipe `|` character is replaced by `\vert` or written out in text to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Magnetic & Optical Storage:** Hard drives, tape recorders, and early optical media used NRZ-I/M because polarity could flip due to media orientation.
- **USB 2.0 Differential Signaling:** Uses a form of differential NRZ with **D+ and D−** lines, leveraging NRZ-I's polarity immunity.
- **Legacy Serial Communication:** RS-232 uses an NRZ-L-like scheme at ±12 V.
- **Clock and Data Recovery (CDR) circuits** in high-speed serial links (PCIe, SATA) treat the NRZ bit stream with a PLL that *struggles* with long runs — hence the use of **scramblers** and **8b/10b encoding** in modern standards.
- **Baseline for Comparison:** Every other line code (RZ, Manchester, AMI, B8ZS, HDB3) is benchmarked against NRZ for bandwidth efficiency, DC balance, and synchronization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Average DC Component for NRZ-Level

Let the binary data stream be $b_k \in \{0, 1\}$ with probabilities:
- $P(b_k = 1) = p$
- $P(b_k = 0) = 1 - p$

The NRZ-L signal $s(t)$ over each bit interval is:
- $s(t) = +V$ when $b_k = 1$
- $s(t) = -V$ when $b_k = 0$

The **time-averaged DC value** over a long observation window is the expected value of $s(t)$:

$$V_{DC} = \mathbb{E}[s(t)]$$

Expanding the expectation over the two possible states:

$$V_{DC} = (+V) \cdot P(b_k = 1) + (-V) \cdot P(b_k = 0)$$

Substitute $P(b_k = 1) = p$ and $P(b_k = 0) = 1 - p$:

$$V_{DC} = V \cdot p - V \cdot (1 - p)$$

Distribute the negative sign on the second term:

$$V_{DC} = V \cdot p - V + V \cdot p$$

Combine like terms (the two $V \cdot p$ terms):

$$V_{DC} = 2V \cdot p - V$$

Factor out $V$:

$$V_{DC} = V \cdot (2p - 1)$$

Or equivalently, written using the difference of probabilities (the cleaner textbook form):

$$V_{DC} = V \cdot \big[ p - (1 - p) \big] = V \cdot (2p - 1)$$

**Special case:** If the data is *equiprobable* (a fair coin flip with $p = 0.5$):

$$V_{DC} = V \cdot (2 \cdot 0.5 - 1) = V \cdot 0 = 0 \text{ volts}$$

**Conclusion:** NRZ-L has zero DC only when bits are equiprobable. Any statistical bias in the data (more 1s than 0s, or vice versa) creates a non-zero DC offset, which is undesirable because it can **saturate transformers** in AC-coupled channels.

## 3.2 Derivation of Minimum Bandwidth for NRZ

The fundamental relationship between the bit rate $R_b$ and the bit duration $T_b$ is:

$$T_b = \frac{1}{R_b}$$

A single NRZ pulse is a rectangular pulse of width $T_b$. Its **two-sided Fourier transform** is a sinc function:

$$S(f) = V \cdot T_b \cdot \operatorname{sinc}(f T_b)$$

The first null of the sinc function occurs at $f = \dfrac{1}{T_b}$. By the **Nyquist criterion**, the **minimum baseband bandwidth** required to preserve the essential signal energy and enable reliable detection is:

$$B_{min} = \frac{1}{2 T_b}$$

Substituting $T_b = \dfrac{1}{R_b}$:

$$B_{min} = \frac{R_b}{2}$$

**In words:** For a 1 Mbps NRZ link, the minimum theoretical bandwidth is **500 kHz**. In practice, engineers allocate $B_{actual} = R_b$ to preserve the pulse shape.

## 3.3 Signal Rate (Baud Rate) Derivation

The **signal rate** $S$ is the number of signal changes (signal elements) per second on the channel. In NRZ, **each bit is encoded using exactly one signal element** (one constant voltage level). Therefore:

$$S = R_b \quad \text{(baud)}$$

There is no $r$-bit-to-symbol mapping in NRZ (unlike in MLT-3 or PAM-4), so the data rate in bps equals the symbol rate in baud. This is why NRZ is called a **1B1S (1 bit per 1 symbol)** scheme.

## 3.4 Bit Error Probability (for Analytical Awareness)

Although KTU typically does not ask for BER derivations, the standard form is given for completeness. Assuming AWGN channel with noise PSD $N_0/2$ and optimal threshold detection:

$$P_e = Q\!\left( \frac{V}{\sqrt{N_0 R_b}} \right)$$

where $Q(\cdot)$ is the Gaussian Q-function. The argument of $Q$ shows that **doubling the signal voltage** $V$ linearly improves the SNR inside the $Q$ function, lowering the error probability.

## 3.5 Summary Matrix of NRZ-L vs NRZ-I

| Property | NRZ-Level (NRZ-L) | NRZ-Inverted (NRZ-I) |
|---|---|---|
| Encodes bit via | Absolute voltage level | Presence/absence of transition |
| DC Component | Non-zero for biased data | Approximately zero (transition-balanced) |
| Long-run sync | Poor for both `1111...` and `0000...` | Poor for `0000...`, OK for `1111...` |
| Polarity inversion effect | Bits are inverted (errors) | **No effect** (differential) |
| Decoder complexity | Simple threshold at 0 V | Requires **D flip-flop + XOR** with previous bit |
| Applications | RS-232, TTL logic | USB, magnetic/optical storage |
| Bandwidth | $R_b / 2$ theoretical | $R_b / 2$ theoretical |
| Synchronization recovery | **Worst** among all NRZ variants | Slightly better than NRZ-L |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — NRZ Transmitter & Receiver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src[Digital Data Source] --> enc{Encoder Type}
    enc -- NRZ-L --> lvl[Map bit to absolute level: 0 maps to minusV, 1 maps to plusV]
    enc -- NRZ-I --> inv[Map bit to transition rule: 1 toggles level, 0 holds level]
    lvl --> chan[Channel with AWGN and possible polarity inversion]
    inv --> chan
    chan --> dec{Decoder Type}
    dec -- NRZ-L --> det1[Threshold Detector at 0V]
    dec -- NRZ-I --> xor[XOR current level with delayed previous level]
    det1 --> out1[Recovered bit stream]
    xor --> out1
```

> **How to read this diagram:** The source bits enter the encoder, which applies either the level rule (NRZ-L) or the transition rule (NRZ-I). The signal passes through a noisy channel. The receiver then uses either a simple threshold detector (NRZ-L) or a transition detector (NRZ-I) to recover the original bits.

## 4.2 NRZ Waveform for Sample Bit Stream `1 0 1 1 0 0 1 0`

```mermaid
graph TD
    t0["Time 0 to T: bit is 1, NRZ-L is plusV, NRZ-I toggles from minusV to plusV"]
    t1["Time T to 2T: bit is 0, NRZ-L is minusV, NRZ-I holds at plusV"]
    t2["Time 2T to 3T: bit is 1, NRZ-L is plusV, NRZ-I toggles from plusV to minusV"]
    t3["Time 3T to 4T: bit is 1, NRZ-L is plusV, NRZ-I toggles from minusV to plusV"]
    t4["Time 4T to 5T: bit is 0, NRZ-L is minusV, NRZ-I holds at plusV"]
    t5["Time 5T to 6T: bit is 0, NRZ-L is minusV, NRZ-I holds at plusV"]
    t6["Time 6T to 7T: bit is 1, NRZ-L is plusV, NRZ-I toggles from plusV to minusV"]
    t7["Time 7T to 8T: bit is 0, NRZ-L is minusV, NRZ-I holds at minusV"]
    t0 --> t1 --> t2 --> t3 --> t4 --> t5 --> t6 --> t7
```

**Reading the timing diagram above (translating to actual waveform):**

| Bit # | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| NRZ-L level | +V | −V | +V | +V | −V | −V | +V | −V |
| NRZ-I transition? | YES | NO | YES | YES | NO | NO | YES | NO |
| NRZ-I level (assume start at −V) | +V | +V | −V | +V | +V | +V | −V | −V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Student Pitfall (KTU Repeated Mistake):** For NRZ-I, the **first bit has no defined previous state**, so its level depends on an *assumed initial state*. The examiner expects you to clearly state the assumed starting level (e.g., "assume the signal starts at −V").

## 4.3 Block Architecture — NRZ-I Decoder

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sig[Received NRZ-I Signal] --> samp[Sample and Hold at bit center]
    samp --> dff[D Flip Flop: stores previous bit value]
    dff --> xor1[XOR Gate: compares current sample with previous sample]
    xor1 --> out[Decoded bit: 1 if different, 0 if same]
    samp --> dff
```

**Explanation:** The D flip-flop stores the level of the *previous* bit. The XOR gate compares the current and previous levels. If they are different → bit was `1`; if they are the same → bit was `0`. This is the canonical differential decoder used in USB, SATA, and other high-speed serial standards.

## 4.4 State Transition Diagram for NRZ-I Encoder

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> minusV
    minusV --> plusV : Input bit 1 (toggle)
    minusV --> minusV : Input bit 0 (hold)
    plusV --> minusV : Input bit 1 (toggle)
    plusV --> plusV : Input bit 0 (hold)
```

This state diagram makes the **toggle-on-1, hold-on-0** rule visually explicit — a frequent 7-mark KTU question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Differentiate between NRZ-L and NRZ-I line coding schemes with suitable waveforms."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | NRZ-L (Level) | NRZ-I (Inverted) |
|---|---|---|
| Encoding rule | Bit value is encoded as **absolute voltage level** | Bit value is encoded as **presence/absence of transition** |
| Binary 1 | +V volts | Transition (toggle) at bit boundary |
| Binary 0 | −V volts | No transition (level held) |
| Polarity sensitivity | **Sensitive** — inverted signal = inverted data | **Immune** — only transitions matter |
| DC component | Non-zero for biased data | Approximately zero |

**Waveform sketch (textual):**
- Data: `1 0 1 1 0 0 1 0`
- **NRZ-L:** `+V, −V, +V, +V, −V, −V, +V, −V` (eight levels, no toggles for repeats)
- **NRZ-I** (assume start at −V): `+V, +V, −V, +V, +V, +V, −V, −V` (toggles only on 1s)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Why is NRZ encoding unsuitable for long-distance synchronous transmission? Justify with two reasons."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Synchronization failure:** During long runs of identical bits (e.g., a string of 100 zeros), the NRZ signal remains a flat DC level with no transitions. The receiver's **bit clock recovery circuit (PLL)** loses its phase reference, causing **bit slip and framing errors**.
2. **DC component problem:** NRZ-L has a non-zero average value whenever the data is statistically biased. This DC offset **saturates the input transformers** of AC-coupled repeaters and **baseline-wander** in optical receivers, degrading signal integrity over distance.
3. **Polarity inversion risk (NRZ-L only):** In long cables, accidental wire swaps invert the signal, flipping all bits. NRZ-I avoids this, but still suffers from point 1.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students often write *"NRZ is bad because of DC"* and stop. That earns **1 out of 3 marks**. The examiner expects **two distinct reasons** (e.g., synchronization AND DC, or synchronization AND bandwidth). Always list **at least two** technical reasons with one-line justifications.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Weight Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Regulation 2024 (ESE Pattern):** Module 2 carries Part B questions. Internal choice is provided — students answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper Set 2]**

**(a)** Explain the NRZ-L and NRZ-I line coding schemes with a clear encoding rule and a **labeled waveform for the data stream `1 1 0 0 1 0 1 0`** (assume the NRZ-I signal starts at −V). Comment on the DC component and synchronization behavior of both. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A digital communication system uses NRZ-L to transmit data at **5 Mbps** over a baseband channel. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
   - (i) The **bit duration** $T_b$.
   - (ii) The **minimum theoretical bandwidth** using the Nyquist criterion.
   - (iii) The **average DC component** of the transmitted signal if the probability of transmitting a binary `1` is 0.7 and the signal swing is ±5 V.
   - (iv) Briefly explain why this DC component is a problem in **AC-coupled** channels.

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(a):**

**NRZ-L rule:**
- Bit `1` → voltage = **+V**
- Bit `0` → voltage = **−V**

**NRZ-I rule:**
- Bit `1` → **transition** (toggle) at the start of the bit interval
- Bit `0` → **no transition** (level held constant)

**Waveform for data `1 1 0 0 1 0 1 0` (each cell = one bit period T):**

| Bit | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| NRZ-L | +V | +V | −V | −V | +V | −V | +V | −V |
| NRZ-I (start −V) | +V | −V | −V | −V | +V | +V | −V | −V |

**Step-by-step NRZ-I derivation:**
- Bit 1 (from −V): toggle → **+V**
- Bit 1 (from +V): toggle → **−V**
- Bit 0 (from −V): hold → **−V**
- Bit 0 (from −V): hold → **−V**
- Bit 1 (from −V): toggle → **+V**
- Bit 0 (from +V): hold → **+V**
- Bit 1 (from +V): toggle → **−V**
- Bit 0 (from −V): hold → **−V**

**DC component comment:**
- **NRZ-L:** Has a non-zero average if the bit probabilities are not equal. The sequence above has four `1`s (+V) and four `0`s (−V), giving an average of 0 V. But in general, the DC component is non-zero.
- **NRZ-I:** Always approximately zero DC, because transitions balance positive and negative excursions.

**Synchronization comment:**
- **NRZ-L:** Very poor — long runs of `1`s or `0`s produce flat segments with no clock edges. The two consecutive `0`s in the data above show this weakness.
- **NRZ-I:** Better for long `1` runs (every `1` creates an edge), but **still fails for long `0` runs** because no transitions occur.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A(b):**

**(i) Bit duration:**

$$T_b = \frac{1}{R_b} = \frac{1}{5 \times 10^6} = 0.2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ seconds} = 200 \text{ ns}$$

**(ii) Minimum theoretical bandwidth (Nyquist bound):**

$$B_{min} = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{5 \times 10^6}{2} = 2.5 \text{ MHz}$$

**(iii) Average DC component of NRZ-L:**

Using the formula derived in Section 3:

$$V_{DC} = V \cdot (2p - 1)$$

Substitute $V = 5$ V (positive swing magnitude) and $p = 0.7$:

$$V_{DC} = 5 \cdot (2 \cdot 0.7 - 1)$$

$$V_{DC} = 5 \cdot (1.4 - 1) = 5 \cdot 0.4 = 2.0 \text{ V}$$

**(iv) Why DC component is a problem in AC-coupled channels:**

AC-coupled channels (using transformers or series capacitors) **block DC** and pass only AC. A DC component of **+2 V** shifts the operating point of the receiver's amplifier and causes **baseline wander**, where the decision threshold drifts with the data pattern. This results in **intersymbol interference (ISI)** and increased bit error rate. The transformer core can also **saturate** due to the DC bias, distorting the AC signal that passes through.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Q-A(b):**
> Many students write $V_{DC} = V(p - 0.5)$ and forget to multiply by 2. The correct expansion is $V(2p - 1)$. Also, students often **forget part (iv)** and lose 2 marks. The question explicitly says "**briefly explain**" — a 2-3 sentence answer is sufficient. Do not skip sub-parts.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **NRZ-I encoder and decoder** and explain how it achieves **polarity-invariance**. Show the encoding waveform for the bit pattern `0 1 0 1 1 0 0 1` starting from an initial level of +V. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A baseband transmission system uses NRZ-L with a bit rate of **2 Mbps** and a signal amplitude of **±3 V**. **(7 marks)**
   - (i) Compute the **signal rate (baud)** and the **minimum bandwidth**.
   - (ii) Draw the **power spectral density (qualitative shape)** and identify why NRZ has poor high-frequency roll-off.
   - (iii) Explain how the synchronization problem of NRZ is **alleviated** in the Manchester encoding scheme.

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(a):**

**NRZ-I Encoder Block Diagram:**

```
[Input bit] --> [D Flip-Flop: stores previous level] --> [XOR: current bit XOR previous output] --> [Output level to channel]
```

**NRZ-I Decoder Block Diagram:**

```
[Received signal] --> [Comparator + D Flip-Flop (1-bit delay)] --> [XOR: current sample XOR previous sample] --> [Recovered bit]
```

**How NRZ-I achieves polarity-invariance:**

In NRZ-I, the information is encoded in the **transition** (change) between successive bit intervals, **not** in the absolute voltage level. If the entire waveform is multiplied by −1 (e.g., due to a wire swap or a differential amplifier inverting the signal), the **locations of transitions remain identical** — only the direction of the transition reverses. The decoder (XOR with previous bit) is sensitive only to *whether* a transition occurred, not *which direction* it took. Therefore, **the decoded bit stream is identical before and after polarity inversion**. This is the key advantage of differential encoding.

**Waveform for `0 1 0 1 1 0 0 1` (start at +V):**

**Step-by-step derivation:**

| Bit | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Transition? | NO | YES | NO | YES | YES | NO | NO | YES |
| Start at +V | +V | toggle → −V | hold → −V | toggle → +V | toggle → −V | hold → −V | hold → −V | toggle → +V |

**Final NRZ-I waveform:** `+V, −V, −V, +V, −V, −V, −V, +V`

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B(b):**

**(i) Signal rate and minimum bandwidth:**

For NRZ, 1 bit = 1 signal element:

$$S = R_b = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ baud} = 2 \text{ Mbaud}$$

Minimum theoretical bandwidth (Nyquist):

$$B_{min} = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{2} = 1 \text{ MHz}$$

**(ii) Qualitative PSD shape:**

The PSD of an NRZ signal is a **sinc²** function centered at DC, with the first null at $f = R_b$ and significant energy concentrated below $f = R_b / 2$. The spectrum has a strong **low-frequency lobe** (large DC component) and decays slowly as $1/f^2$ at high frequencies. This slow decay is why NRZ has **poor high-frequency roll-off** — it requires a wide bandwidth to capture 90% of the signal energy.

**Key features to mention in the diagram:**
- Peak at $f = 0$ (DC)
- Nulls at $f = \pm n R_b$ for $n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$
- Slow $1/f^2$ decay in the tails
- Most energy within the first lobe ($0$ to $R_b$)

**(iii) How Manchester encoding fixes the synchronization problem:**

Manchester encoding uses a **mid-bit transition** as a mandatory clock reference:
- Binary `1` = **HIGH-to-LOW** transition at the middle of the bit period.
- Binary `0` = **LOW-to-HIGH** transition at the middle of the bit period.

Because **every bit interval contains at least one guaranteed transition** (in the middle), the receiver can extract the bit clock from the data stream regardless of the bit pattern — even a long string of identical bits (e.g., `1111...`) produces a continuous clock waveform. This eliminates the long flat segments that plague NRZ. The trade-off is that Manchester's **bandwidth is doubled** to $R_b$ Hz (the main lobe extends to $2 R_b$), because of the doubled transition rate.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024:**

- ✅ **NRZ = No Return to Zero** — signal stays at a constant level for the entire bit duration.
- ✅ **Two variants:** **NRZ-L** (level-encoded) and **NRZ-I** (transition-encoded / differential).
- ✅ **NRZ-L decoding** is a simple threshold detector at **0 V**.
- ✅ **NRZ-I decoding** uses an **XOR with a 1-bit-delayed sample** of the received signal.
- ✅ **DC component of NRZ-L:** $V_{DC} = V(2p - 1)$; zero only if $p = 0.5$.
- ✅ **DC component of NRZ-I:** approximately zero (transition-balanced).
- ✅ **Bandwidth of NRZ:** theoretical minimum $B_{min} = R_b / 2$; practical = $R_b$.
- ✅ **Signal rate = Bit rate** in NRZ (1 bit = 1 signal element, i.e., 1B1S).
- ✅ **Synchronization: POOR** — long runs of identical bits produce flat segments with no clock edges. NRZ-I is slightly better for long `1` runs.
- ✅ **Polarity-invariance: only NRZ-I** (differential) has this property; NRZ-L is polarity-sensitive.
- ✅ **Applications:** USB 2.0 differential pairs, RS-232, magnetic/optical storage, SATA, PCIe (with scrambling).
- ✅ **Improvement pathway (in syllabus order):** NRZ → RZ (self-clocking but doubles BW) → Manchester (mid-bit transition, BW = $2 \times$ NRZ) → AMI / B8ZS / HDB3 (DC-balanced).
- ✅ **Always state the assumed initial level** when drawing NRZ-I waveforms.
- ✅ **Common examiner trap:** Forgetting to mention that **NRZ-I does NOT solve long-zero-run synchronization** (only long-one-run).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Return-to-Zero (RZ)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Return-to-Zero (RZ)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Return-to-Zero (RZ)** is a line coding scheme in digital data communication in which the signal voltage returns to a **zero reference level (ground)** during the second half of every bit interval, regardless of whether the bit represents a binary `1` or `0`. The defining characteristic of RZ is that the signal pulse does **not** occupy the entire bit duration $T_b$; instead, the active pulse exists only for the first half of the bit period ($T_b/2$), and the line is forced back to **0 V** for the remaining half.

Mathematically, for a binary input sequence $b_k \in \{0, 1\}$, the RZ waveform $s(t)$ can be expressed as:

$$s(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} a_k \cdot p(t - kT_b)$$

where $p(t)$ is a rectangular pulse of width $T_b/2$ and amplitude $V$, and $a_k$ is the symbol coefficient dictated by the specific RZ variant (Unipolar, Polar, or Bipolar).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** RZ is one of the **four fundamental line coding schemes** prescribed in Module 2 of OECST612. Students must be able to **sketch waveforms**, **compute DC components**, **derive bandwidth**, and **compare synchronization performance** between RZ and NRZ schemes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are signaling a friend across a noisy room using a flashlight:
- **NRZ (Non-Return-to-Zero):** You keep the flashlight ON for the entire duration of a "1" bit and OFF for a "0" bit. If two "1"s come in a row, the flashlight stays ON continuously — the friend has no way of knowing where one "1" ends and the next begins.
- **RZ (Return-to-Zero):** You flash the light ON only for *half* the duration of each "1" bit, and *always* turn it back OFF (even between two consecutive "1"s). This creates a **distinct "blink-and-rest" pattern** for every bit, providing a natural rhythm that helps your friend count the bits correctly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Intuition:** The "return to zero" creates a guaranteed transition (or resting state) in every bit interval, which is what makes RZ a **self-clocking** signal — the receiver can extract timing information directly from the data transitions.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Bit Duration:** $T_b$ (seconds)
- **Pulse Width in RZ:** $\tau = T_b / 2$ (half the bit period)
- **Bit Rate:** $R_b = 1/T_b$ (bits per second, bps)
- **Baud Rate (Symbol Rate):** $R_s = 1/T_b$ (bauds) — same as bit rate for binary RZ
- **Minimum Transmission Bandwidth:** $B_{min} = 2R_b$ (**twice the bit rate**, **Hz**)
- **DC Component:** Non-zero for **Unipolar RZ**; ideally zero for **Polar RZ**; zero for **Bipolar RZ (AMI)**
- **Synchronization Quality:** **High** (superior to NRZ due to guaranteed mid-bit transition)

### 1.4 Variants of RZ (Classification Overview)

| Variant | Binary `0` Level | Binary `1` Level | DC Component |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Unipolar RZ** | 0 V | +V (first half), 0 V (second half) | Non-zero |
| **Polar RZ** | $-V$ (first half), 0 V (second half) | +V (first half), 0 V (second half) | Ideally zero |
| **Bipolar RZ (AMI)** | 0 V (full bit) | Alternating +V, -V (first half); 0 V (second half) | Zero |

> [!NOTE]
> The most exam-relevant variant is **Polar RZ**, followed by **Bipolar RZ / AMI**, because they appear frequently in KTU Part B questions on DC balance and synchronization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

1. **Bit Period Partitioning:** Each bit interval $T_b$ is conceptually divided into two equal halves: $[0, T_b/2]$ (active half) and $[T_b/2, T_b]$ (return half).
2. **Active Half Encoding:** During the first half, the signal takes the appropriate voltage level ($+V$, $-V$, or $0$) as per the variant and the bit value.
3. **Forced Return:** Regardless of the bit value or the next bit, the signal is **forced back to 0 V** during the second half. This is the **defining rule** of RZ.
