<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reconstruction of $x(t)$ from Its Samples (Whittaker–Shannon Formula)

**Goal:** Show mathematically that $x(t)$ can be perfectly recovered from $\{x(nT_s)\}$ using an ideal low-pass filter.

**Step 1 — Sampling as multiplication by an impulse train.**

Let $x(t)$ be a bandlimited signal with $X(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > f_m$. The sampled signal is:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \delta_{T_s}(t) = x(t) \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}\delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 2 — Pulling sample values out of the impulse.**

Since the delta function "picks" the value of $x(t)$ at $t = nT_s$:

$$x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 3 — Take the Fourier Transform.**

Using the modulation property and the impulse train's Fourier series (where $\delta_{T_s}(t) \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k}\delta(f - kf_s)$):

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - kf_s)$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Nyquist condition.**

If $f_s \geq 2f_m$, the replicas $X(f-kf_s)$ **do not overlap**. An ideal LPF of bandwidth $f_m$ and gain $T_s$ extracts only the baseband copy $X(f)$:

$$H(f) = \begin{cases} T_s, & \vert f \vert \leq f_m \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 5 — Inverse Fourier Transform yields the reconstruction formula.**

$$x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\frac{\sin\!\big(\pi(t - nT_s)/T_s\big)}{\pi(t - nT_s)/T_s}$$

This is the celebrated **Whittaker–Shannon Interpolation Formula**, with $\text{sinc}$ kernels perfectly reconstructing every point of $x(t)$. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Derivation of SQNR for a Uniform Mid-Tread Quantizer

**Step 1 — Quantization Error Model.**

Let the quantizer output be $x_q = Q(x)$ and define the error $e = x_q - x$. For uniform mid-tread quantization with $L = 2^n$ symmetric levels:

$$-\frac{\Delta}{2} \leq e \leq +\frac{\Delta}{2}$$

Assume $e$ is **uniformly distributed** in $[-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$. The probability density function is:

$$p(e) = \frac{1}{\Delta}, \quad e \in [-\Delta/2, \Delta/2]$$

**Step 2 — Compute mean square error (noise power).**

$$N_q = E[e^2] = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \, p(e)\, de = \frac{1}{\Delta}\int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \, de$$

Evaluate the integral:

$$\int_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} e^2 \, de = \frac{e^3}{3}\bigg|_{-\Delta/2}^{\Delta/2} = \frac{(\Delta/2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-\Delta/2)^3}{3} = \frac{2(\Delta/2)^3}{3} = \frac{\Delta^3}{12}$$

Therefore:

$$N_q = \frac{1}{\Delta}\cdot\frac{\Delta^3}{12} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

**Step 3 — Compute signal power for a full-scale sinusoid.**

For $x(t) = A_{max}\sin(2\pi f t)$, signal power is:

$$S = \frac{A_{max}^2}{2}$$

Since $A_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta \cdot 2^n}{2}$ (full-scale peak equals $L/2$ steps $\times \Delta$):

$$S = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\Delta \cdot 2^n}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{\Delta^2 \cdot 4^n}{8}$$

**Step 4 — Compute SQNR (ratio form).**

$$SQNR = \frac{S}{N_q} = \frac{\Delta^2 \cdot 4^n / 8}{\Delta^2 / 12} = \frac{12 \cdot 4^n}{8} = \frac{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}{2}$$

Converting to decibels:

$$SQNR_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}{2}\right) = 10\log_{10}(1.5) + 20n\log_{10}(2)$$

$$= 1.76 + 6.02n \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Typical KTU Numerical Problem

**Problem:** An analog signal $x(t)$ has bandwidth $f_m = 4$ kHz. It is sampled at $f_s = 10$ kHz and quantized using an 8-bit uniform quantizer with peak-to-peak range of $16$ V.
**(a)** Verify the Nyquist criterion.
**(b)** Compute the bit rate $R_b$ and minimum transmission bandwidth.
**(c)** Compute SQNR in dB.

### (a) Nyquist Verification

$$f_s = 10{,}000 \text{ Hz} \geq 2f_m = 8{,}000 \text{ Hz} \quad \checkmark$$

Sampling rate exceeds the Nyquist rate, so **no aliasing** will occur.

### (b) Bit Rate and Bandwidth

$$R_b = n \cdot f_s = 8 \times 10{,}000 = 80{,}000 \text{ bps} = 80 \text{ kbps}$$

Minimum transmission bandwidth (using NRZ polar line coding):

$$B_{min} = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{80{,}000}{2} = 40 \text{ kHz}$$

### (c) SQNR Computation

$$SQNR_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02n = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 8 = 1.76 + 48.16 = 49.92 \text{ dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Insight:** Always show three things clearly: (1) the Nyquist check, (2) the units of $R_b$ and $B$, and (3) the formula substitution. Examiners award marks for **structured presentation** in long derivations.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State the Nyquist Sampling Theorem. What is the consequence of violating it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Statement (2 marks):** A bandlimited analog signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency $f_s \geq 2f_m$. The rate $2f_m$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**.
>
> **Consequence of Violation (1 mark):** If $f_s < 2f_m$, the spectral replicas of $x(t)$ overlap, producing **aliasing** — high-frequency components appear as false low-frequency components. This information loss is **irreversible** and cannot be removed by any filter.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define aliasing. A sinusoidal signal $x(t) = 5\sin(2\pi \cdot 900t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 1000$ Hz. Find the aliased frequency.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Aliasing is the phenomenon in which a high-frequency component of a signal, when sampled below the Nyquist rate, appears as a lower-frequency signal at the output. The aliased frequency is computed as:
>
> $$f_a = \vert f - f_s \vert = \vert 900 - 1000 \vert = 100 \text{ Hz}$$
>
> **Verification (1 mark):** Since $f_s = 1000 < 2f = 1800$ Hz, the sampling is sub-Nyquist and aliasing is confirmed. The reconstructed signal will appear as $5\sin(2\pi \cdot 100t)$ instead of the original $900$ Hz tone.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the **Whittaker–Shannon interpolation formula** showing that a bandlimited signal can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples. Discuss the role of the **anti-aliasing filter** in the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An audio signal of bandwidth $f_m = 15$ kHz is sampled at $f_s = 48$ kHz and quantized using a **16-bit uniform quantizer** with a peak-to-peak range of $10$ V. Compute:
    (i) The quantization step size $\Delta$
    (ii) The PCM bit rate $R_b$
    (iii) The SQNR in dB
    (iv) The minimum transmission bandwidth using NRZ polar coding. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

### (a) Derivation of Whittaker–Shannon Formula + Anti-Aliasing Filter (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Sampling operation:**
The sampled signal is $x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\delta(t - nT_s)$.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Fourier Transform of the sampled signal:**
Using the duality of impulse train, we obtain:

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - kf_s)$$

**Step 3 (1 mark) — Apply Nyquist condition:**
If $f_s \geq 2f_m$, the spectral replicas do not overlap. The baseband copy $X(f)$ is isolated by passing $X_s(f)$ through an **ideal low-pass filter** $H(f)$ of bandwidth $f_m$ and gain $T_s$.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Inverse Fourier Transform:**

$$x(t) = T_s \int_{-f_m}^{f_m} X_s(f) e^{j2\pi f t}\, df = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$$

which yields the **Whittaker–Shannon formula**:

$$\boxed{x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\frac{\sin\!\big(\pi(t - nT_s)/T_s\big)}{\pi(t - nT_s)/T_s}}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark) — Role of Anti-Aliasing Filter (AAF):**
The AAF is an analog low-pass filter placed **before** the sampler that bandlimits $x(t)$ to $f_m \leq f_s/2$. Without the AAF, any out-of-band noise or harmonics above $f_s/2$ would alias back into the baseband and corrupt the signal **irreversibly**.

**Step 6 (1 mark) — Practical reconstruction filter:**
At the receiver, a low-pass filter with cutoff $f_m$ reconstructs the analog signal from the demodulated PAM.

**Step 7 (1 mark) — Conclusion:**
This proves that under the Nyquist condition, no information is lost and **perfect reconstruction** is mathematically possible using ideal sinc interpolation.

---

### (b) Numerical Problem Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $f_m = 15$ kHz, $f_s = 48$ kHz, $n = 16$ bits, $V_{pp} = 10$ V.

**Step (i) — Quantization Step Size (2 marks):**

Number of levels $L = 2^n = 2^{16} = 65{,}536$.

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{pp}}{L} = \frac{10}{65{,}536} = 1.526 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V} = 152.6 \text{ μV}$$

**Step (ii) — PCM Bit Rate (1 mark):**

$$R_b = n \cdot f_s = 16 \times 48{,}000 = 768{,}000 \text{ bps} = 768 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step (iii) — SQNR (2 marks):**

$$SQNR_{dB} = 1.76 + 6.02n = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 16 = 1.76 + 96.32 = 98.08 \text{ dB}$$

**Step (iv) — Minimum Bandwidth (2 marks):**
For NRZ polar line coding:

$$B_{min} = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{768{,}000}{2} = 384{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 384 \text{ kHz}$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – July 2024 — Internal Choice]**

**(a)** Compare **Natural Sampling** and **Flat-Top Sampling** in detail. Explain the **aperture effect** and how an **equalizing filter** compensates for it. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **PCM system**. A signal $x(t)$ has $f_m = 4$ kHz. Find the **minimum sampling rate**, and if encoded with $n = 8$ bits, compute the **bit duration** and **transmission bandwidth** (NRZ). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

### (a) Natural vs Flat-Top Sampling + Aperture Effect (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks) — Natural Sampling:**
In natural sampling (also called "chopper sampling"), the analog signal $x(t)$ is multiplied by a **pulse train** $p(t)$ with rectangular pulses of width $\tau < T_s$. The output pulses **retain the natural shape** of $x(t)$ under each pulse window.

$$x_{ns}(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t)$$

- **Pros:** Spectrum is theoretically clean; no aperture effect.
- **Cons:** Output amplitudes vary within each pulse, making the sample value **ambiguous** to a quantizer.

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Flat-Top Sampling (Sample-and-Hold):**
The sampled value is **held constant** for the entire sampling period using a **zero-order hold (ZOH)** circuit (capacitor + MOSFET switch).

$$x_{ft}(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s)\left[u(t-nT_s) - u(t-nT_s-T_s)\right]$$

- **Pros:** Each sample has a stable amplitude, ideal for A/D conversion.
- **Cons:** Spectrum has a **sinc roll-off** — the **aperture effect**.

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Aperture Effect:**
Multiplication in time by a rectangular pulse of width $T_s$ corresponds to **convolution** in frequency with a $\text{sinc}$ function:

$$X_{ft}(f) = X_s(f) \cdot T_s\,\text{sinc}(fT_s)e^{-j\pi f T_s}$$

The $\text{sinc}(fT_s)$ factor attenuates high frequencies, distorting the recovered signal.

**Step 4 (1 mark) — Equalizing Filter:**
A **compensating filter** with transfer function:

$$H_{eq}(f) = \frac{\pi f T_s}{\sin(\pi f T_s)} \cdot \frac{1}{T_s}$$

is placed after demodulation to **undo** the sinc roll-off and restore flat frequency response.

---

### (b) PCM Block Diagram + Numerical (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks) — PCM Block Diagram:**

A PCM system consists of:
1. **Anti-Aliasing Filter** (LPF at transmitter)
2. **Sampler** (PAM generation)
3. **Quantizer** (n-bit resolution)
4. **Encoder** (binary PCM output)
5. **Channel** (digital transmission medium)
6. **Decoder** at receiver (binary → quantized levels)
7. **Reconstruction LPF** at receiver (PAM → analog)

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Minimum Sampling Rate:**
For no aliasing, by Nyquist:

$$f_s^{min} = 2f_m = 2 \times 4{,}000 = 8{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 8 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Bit Duration:**
Bit rate is $R_b = n \cdot f_s = 8 \times 8{,}000 = 64{,}000$ bps.

$$T_b = \frac{1}{R_b} = \frac{1}{64{,}000} = 15.625 \text{ μs}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks) — Transmission Bandwidth (NRZ):**

$$B_{min} = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{64{,}000}{2} = 32{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 32 \text{ kHz}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always write Hz, V, s, bps — examiners check units explicitly. Lose 0.5–1 mark per missing unit.
> 2. **Wrong formula in SQNR:** Using $\frac{3 \cdot 2^{2n}}{2}$ for general case but **not** $1.76 + 6.02n$ for sinusoidal. The two are equivalent, but **state which signal model you assume**.
> 3. **Confusing alias frequency formula:** $f_a = \vert f - f_s \vert$ is for single-tone aliasing. For multi-tone signals, fold via $f_a = f \mod f_s$ (modular arithmetic).
> 4. **Skipping the Nyquist verification:** Always **prove** $f_s \geq 2f_m$ before solving the rest of the problem — this carries 1–2 marks in numericals.
> 5. **Bandwidth factor confusion:** NRZ polar → $B = R_b/2$; Unipolar NRZ / RZ → $B = R_b$; Manchester → $B = R_b$. State the line coding.
> 6. **Sampling without anti-aliasing filter:** Examiners will deduct marks if your block diagram omits the AAF.
> 7. **Mixing sampling theorem with quantization:** Sampling is in **time domain**, quantization is in **amplitude domain** — they are independent stages.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Module 2):**
>
> ✅ **Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2f_m$ — foundation of all ADC.
> ✅ **Nyquist Rate:** $f_N = 2f_m$. Nyquist Interval = $1/(2f_m)$.
> ✅ **Aliasing:** Occurs when $f_s < 2f_m$; irreversible; prevented by **anti-aliasing LPF**.
> ✅ **Three Sampling Types:** Ideal impulse, Natural (chopper), Flat-Top (sample-and-hold).
> ✅ **Aperture Effect:** sinc roll-off in flat-top sampling; compensated by **equalizing filter** $H_{eq}(f) = \text{sinc}^{-1}(fT_s)/T_s$.
> ✅ **Quantization:** $L = 2^n$ levels, step $\Delta = V_{pp}/L$, error $\in [-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$.
> ✅ **SQNR:** $1.76 + 6.02n$ dB (sinusoidal, full-scale) — **6 dB per bit rule**.
> ✅ **PCM Bit Rate:** $R_b = n \cdot f_s$.
> ✅ **PCM Bandwidth:** $R_b/2$ (NRZ polar) or $R_b$ (bipolar).
> ✅ **Delta Modulation:** Max slope condition $\Delta/T_s \geq \vert dx/dt \vert_{max}$; **granular noise** + **slope overload**.
> ✅ **Companding:** μ-law (USA/Japan), A-law (Europe) — improves SQNR for speech.
> ✅ **Standard Frequencies:** 8 kHz (telephony), 44.1 kHz (CD), 48 kHz (studio).
> ✅ **Master Equation:** $x(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s)\,\text{sinc}\big((t-nT_s)/T_s\big)$ — Whittaker–Shannon formula.
> ✅ **Always state line coding** when computing bandwidth — examiners reward this discipline.

---

**END OF MODULE 2 NOTES — SAMPLING THEOREM**

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pulse Code Modulation (PCM)** is the standard method of digitizing an analog baseband signal (such as audio, voice, or video) by representing each sample of the signal as a discrete binary codeword. It is a **sampling-quantization-encoding** process that converts a continuous-amplitude, continuous-time (CACT) signal into a discrete-amplitude, discrete-time (DADT) digital bitstream suitable for digital transmission, storage, and processing.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (OECST612 – Module 2)**, PCM is the foundational waveform-coding technique studied before delta modulation and differential PCM. It is formally described in the textbook by **B.P. Lathi** and **Simon Haykin** under the topic of "Pulse Code Modulation" within the broader chapter on "Digital Transmission of Analog Signals."

### The Three Sequential Building Blocks of PCM

1. **Sampling** — The continuous-time analog signal $x(t)$ is sampled at uniform intervals $T_s$ to obtain a discrete-time signal $x(nT_s)$. The **Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem** states:
$$f_s \geq 2 f_m \quad \text{(Nyquist Criterion)}$$
where $f_m$ is the highest frequency component of $x(t)$ and $f_s = 1/T_s$ is the sampling frequency.

2. **Quantization** — Each sample amplitude is approximated (rounded) to the nearest of $L = 2^n$ discrete levels. This is an **irreversible** process that introduces **quantization error** $e_q$.

3. **Encoding** — Each quantized level is mapped to a unique $n$-bit binary codeword (typically Natural Binary Code or Gray Code). The output is a serial bitstream.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Term to Remember:** PCM is the *first true* digital modulation scheme. PAM (Pulse Amplitude Modulation) is analog; PCM is digital because each sample is represented by a discrete code.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to **describe the color of a sunset to a friend who is color-blind**. You cannot send them the actual continuous color spectrum. Instead, you do the following:

- **Sampling** ≈ Taking a photograph of the sky every second (you "sample" reality at discrete moments).
- **Quantization** ≈ Looking at the photo and assigning it a label from a fixed palette of 256 named colors (Red, Crimson, Vermillion…). The real shade is *almost* Vermillion, so you call it Vermillion — losing a tiny bit of accuracy.
- **Encoding** ≈ Writing down a numerical index for each color (e.g., Vermillion = "11011001") and sending that code over WhatsApp.

The receiver reads the code, looks up the color in the same palette, and displays it. The picture is not *perfect*, but it is *good enough* — and crucially, it is **digital**, so it can be copied, encrypted, compressed, and transmitted without degradation.

**PCM does exactly this for electrical signals** like voice or music. The microphone produces a continuous voltage; the PCM system "snapshots" it, rounds it to a finite set of levels, and emits a binary pattern.

> [!TIP]
> **Why PCM matters in real engineering:**
> - **Telephony:** The 64 kbps standard for voice (G.711) is 8-bit PCM sampled at 8 kHz.
> - **Audio CDs:** 16-bit linear PCM at 44.1 kHz (LPCM stereo).
> - **Digital telephony trunks (TDM):** PCM is the building block of T1/E1 carrier systems.

---

## 1.3 System-Level Classification

| Property | Value / Class |
|---|---|
| Signal type in | Analog (continuous) |
| Signal type out | Digital (bits) |
| Modulation family | Pulse + Digital (Waveform Coding) |
| Reversible? | **No** (quantization is lossy) |
| Bit rate formula | $R_b = n f_s$ bits/sec |
| Channel bandwidth (ideal) | $B_T = R_b / 2$ Hz |

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### Step 1 — Sampling
- The analog signal $x(t)$ is multiplied by an impulse train $p(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - kT_s)$.
- Output is a sampled signal $x_s(t) = \sum x(kT_s)\,\delta(t - kT_s)$.
- **Aliasing condition:** If $f_s < 2 f_m$, spectral overlap destroys the original signal. An **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** of cutoff $f_m$ Hz must precede the sampler.

### Step 2 — Quantization
- The dynamic range of the input (say $-V$ to $+V$) is divided into $L = 2^n$ equal (uniform) intervals of step size:
$$\Delta = \frac{2V}{L} = \frac{2V}{2^n}$$
- Each sample is rounded to the midpoint of its interval. The **quantization error** $e_q = x_q - x$ is bounded:
$$-\frac{\Delta}{2} \leq e_q \leq +\frac{\Delta}{2}$$
- Quantization error behaves like **uniformly distributed white noise** in the signal band, with mean-square value (quantization noise power):
$$N_q = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

### Step 3 — Encoding
- Each quantized level is represented by a unique $n$-bit binary word.
- Common codes: **Natural Binary (NBC)**, **Folded Binary Code (FBC)**, **Gray Code**.
- Folded Binary is preferred in PCM because sign-bit errors (MSB flips) cause smaller magnitude errors — used in standard telephone PCM (μ-law/A-law in companded systems).

### Step 4 — Regeneration at Receiver
- A **regenerative repeater** detects each bit, cleans it, and re-transmits. This is why PCM is virtually immune to noise on the channel.
- The decoder (D/A) reconstructs the quantized levels, and a **reconstruction low-pass filter** (cutoff $f_m$) interpolates the staircase output back into an analog waveform.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula** that KTU examiners have historically tested in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions on PCM.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Nyquist sampling rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ | Hz |
| 2 | Sampling interval | $T_s = 1/f_s$ | seconds |
| 3 | Number of quantization levels | $L = 2^n$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Quantization step (uniform) | $\Delta = (V_{max} - V_{min})/L$ | volts |
| 5 | Quantization noise power | $N_q = \Delta^2 / 12$ | watts (normalized to $1\,\Omega$) |
| 6 | Signal power (full-scale sinusoid) | $S = V^2 / 2$ | watts |
| 7 | **SQNR (Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio)** | $\mathrm{SQNR} = 1.76 + 6.02\,n$ | **dB** |
| 8 | Bit rate at PCM output | $R_b = n \cdot f_s$ | bits/second (bps) |
| 9 | Transmission bandwidth (ideal) | $B_T = R_b / 2$ | Hz |
| 10 | Channel bandwidth (practical, NRZ) | $B_T = R_b$ | Hz |
| 11 | Output voltage dynamic range | $V_{pp} = L \cdot \Delta$ | volts peak-to-peak |
| 12 | Quantization error bound | $\vert e_q \vert \leq \Delta/2$ | volts |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Escape Rule:** The table above uses no vertical pipes inside cell text. In LaTeX, when writing $\vert e_q \vert$ or $\vert x \vert$ inside tables, **always** write `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the KTU note-rendering pipeline.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Telecommunications:** The entire Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN) backbone carries 8-bit PCM at 8 kHz, giving the famous **64 kbps DS0 channel**.
- **Audio Engineering:** Compact Discs (CDs) store 16-bit linear PCM at 44.1 kHz, producing 705.6 kbps per channel of uncompressed digital audio.
- **Telemetry & Spacecraft:** NASA's deep-space probes transmit PCM telemetry because of its noise immunity across interplanetary distances.
- **VoIP:** Modern VoIP codecs (G.711) are direct descendants of μ-law/A-law companded PCM from the 1960s.

> [!NOTE]
> The **6.02 dB-per-bit rule** is one of the most tested derivations in KTU ESE papers. It originates from the fact that doubling the number of levels (i.e., adding one bit) quadruples the SQNR power ratio, which is $10 \log_{10}(4) \approx 6.02$ dB.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the SQNR Formula

We will derive the **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio** in decibels for a full-scale sinusoidal input, which is the single most important result in KTU PCM problems.

### Setup
- Consider a sinusoidal input $x(t) = A \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ with peak amplitude $A$ and total dynamic range $\pm A$ volts.
- The quantizer uses $L = 2^n$ uniform levels. The step size is therefore:
$$\Delta = \frac{2A}{L} = \frac{2A}{2^n} = A \cdot 2^{1-n}$$

### Step 1 — Compute the Quantization Noise Power $N_q$

Assume the quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2,\,+\Delta/2]$. Its mean-square value is:

$$N_q = E[e_q^2] = \int_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2} e_q^2 \,\frac{1}{\Delta}\, de_q$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$N_q = \frac{1}{\Delta}\left[\frac{e_q^3}{3}\right]_{-\Delta/2}^{+\Delta/2}$$

$$N_q = \frac{1}{\Delta}\left[\frac{(\Delta/2)^3}{3} - \frac{(-\Delta/2)^3}{3}\right]$$

$$N_q = \frac{1}{\Delta}\cdot \frac{2}{3}\cdot\frac{\Delta^3}{8} = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

This is the **average noise power** delivered to a $1\,\Omega$ load. Hence, the result $N_q = \Delta^2 / 12$.

### Step 2 — Compute the Signal Power $S$

For a sinusoid $x(t) = A\cos(\omega t)$, the average power (across $1\,\Omega$) is:

$$S = \overline{x^2(t)} = \frac{A^2}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Form the Signal-to-Noise Power Ratio

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = \frac{S}{N_q} = \frac{A^2 / 2}{\Delta^2 / 12} = \frac{A^2}{2}\cdot\frac{12}{\Delta^2} = \frac{6 A^2}{\Delta^2}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute the Step Size

Recall $\Delta = A \cdot 2^{1-n}$. Therefore $\Delta^2 = A^2 \cdot 2^{2-2n} = A^2 \cdot 4 \cdot 2^{-2n}$.

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = \frac{6 A^2}{A^2 \cdot 4 \cdot 2^{-2n}} = \frac{6}{4}\cdot 2^{2n} = \frac{3}{2}\cdot 2^{2n}$$

$$\mathrm{SNR}_{\text{linear}} = 1.5 \cdot 4^{n}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to Decibels

Using $10 \log_{10}$:

$$\mathrm{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10 \log_{10}(4^n)$$

$$\mathrm{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10 n \log_{10}(4)$$

Now evaluate the constants:

$$10 \log_{10}(1.5) = 1.7609 \approx 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

$$10 \log_{10}(4) = 10 \times 0.60206 = 6.0206 \approx 6.02 \text{ dB}$$

Therefore, the final closed-form result is:

$$\boxed{\mathrm{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 1.76 + 6.02\,n}$$

This is the celebrated **"6 dB per bit" rule** for uniform PCM, and it is the formula KTU examiners expect verbatim in Part B solutions.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem:** A voice signal has maximum frequency $f_m = 4$ kHz. It is sampled at $f_s = 8$ kHz and quantized with $n = 8$ bits per sample.
Find:
(a) The bit rate at the PCM output.
(b) The minimum transmission bandwidth (ideal channel).
(c) The output SQNR in dB.
(d) The number of quantization levels and step size if the signal range is $\pm 4$ V.

**Solution (a):**

$$R_b = n \cdot f_s = 8 \times 8000 = 64{,}000 \text{ bps} = 64 \text{ kbps}$$

**Solution (b):**

$$B_T = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{64{,}000}{2} = 32{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 32 \text{ kHz}$$

**Solution (c):**

$$\mathrm{SQNR} = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 8 = 1.76 + 48.16 = 49.92 \text{ dB} \approx 50 \text{ dB}$$

**Solution (d):**

$$L = 2^n = 2^8 = 256 \text{ levels}$$

The total dynamic range is $V_{pp} = 4 - (-4) = 8$ V. So:

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{pp}}{L} = \frac{8}{256} = 0.03125 \text{ V} = 31.25 \text{ mV}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Increasing $n$

| $n$ (bits) | $L = 2^n$ | SQNR (dB) | Step $\Delta$ (for $\pm 4$ V) | Bit rate at 8 kHz |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | 16 | 25.84 dB | 0.5 V | 32 kbps |
| 6 | 64 | 37.88 dB | 0.125 V | 48 kbps |
| 8 | 256 | 49.92 dB | 31.25 mV | 64 kbps |
| 10 | 1024 | 61.96 dB | 7.81 mV | 80 kbps |
| 12 | 4096 | 74.00 dB | 1.95 mV | 96 kbps |
| 16 | 65,536 | 98.08 dB | 0.122 mV | 128 kbps |

This table makes the **engineering trade-off** explicit: more bits → higher SQNR but linearly higher bit rate. KTU frequently asks for the "minimum $n$ needed for a target SQNR," solved as:

$$n \geq \frac{\mathrm{SQNR}_{\text{req}} - 1.76}{6.02}$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PCM Transmitter — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analog Input Signal x of t] --> B[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter Cutoff f m]
    B --> C[Sampler Impulse Train at f s]
    C --> D[Quantizer L equals 2 to n levels]
    D --> E[n bit Binary Encoder]
    E --> F[Parallel to Serial Converter]
    F --> G[Digital Bitstream to Channel]

    style A fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1
    style B fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309
    style C fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309
    style D fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d
    style E fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d
    style F fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d
    style G fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d
```

**Explanation of the flow:**

- **Anti-Aliasing LPF** (Block B): A real-world signal has infinite bandwidth; the LPF limits it to $f_m$ so that Nyquist's criterion is not violated.
- **Sampler** (Block C): Implemented as a sample-and-hold (S/H) circuit followed by an A/D converter in modern hardware.
- **Quantizer** (Block D): Maps continuous amplitude to one of $L$ levels.
- **Encoder** (Block E): Issues the $n$-bit code.
- **P/S Converter** (Block F): Serializes the parallel bits for transmission.

---

## 4.2 PCM Receiver — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Received Digital Bitstream] --> I[Serial to Parallel Converter]
    I --> J[n bit Binary Decoder]
    J --> K[Sample and Hold Reconstruction]
    K --> L[Reconstruction Low Pass Filter Cutoff f m]
    L --> M[Analog Output Signal y of t]
    I -.-> N[Bit Clock Recovery]

    style H fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d
    style I fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d
    style J fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d
    style K fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d
    style L fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309
    style M fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1
    style N fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1
```

**Note:** The receiver also contains a **bit-clock recovery** circuit (Block N) to keep synchronization. The Regenerative Repeater (not shown separately) is interspersed along the channel and is the reason PCM is noise-immune.

---


# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students often write the **Nyquist rate** as $f_s = f_m$ instead of $f_s = 2 f_m$. The correct inequality is $f_s \geq 2 f_m$, not equality. **Lose 1 mark** for this slip.
> 2. Do not confuse **SQNR** (which is $1.76 + 6.02n$) with the general **SNR** of a noisy channel. PCM's "noise" is purely quantization noise, not channel noise.
> 3. When asked for the **minimum transmission bandwidth**, the answer is $R_b/2$ for an ideal noiseless channel, and $R_b$ for a practical NRZ line code. Read the wording of the question carefully.
> 4. Always specify units: bit rate is in **bps**, bandwidth in **Hz**, SQNR in **dB**.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define Pulse Code Modulation. List its main advantages over analog modulation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** PCM is a digital modulation technique in which an analog signal is sampled, quantized, and encoded into a binary bitstream for transmission. Each sample is represented by a discrete $n$-bit codeword chosen from $L = 2^n$ levels.

**Three advantages (1 mark each):**
1. **Noise immunity** — Regenerative repeaters restore the original digital waveform, eliminating accumulated channel noise.
2. **Ease of encryption and signal processing** — Bits can be encrypted, compressed, error-corrected, and time-multiplexed.
3. **Long-distance reliability** — Unlike analog, signal quality does not degrade with distance since each repeater restores the bits perfectly.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Series-1]**
**State the Nyquist sampling theorem. What is the consequence of violating it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (2 marks):** *"A band-limited signal of maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. The minimum rate $f_s = 2 f_m$ is called the **Nyquist rate**."*

**Consequence (1 mark):** If $f_s < 2 f_m$, the spectral replicas of the sampled signal overlap, producing **aliasing** — high-frequency content folds back into the baseband and the original signal cannot be recovered, even with an ideal reconstruction filter.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE rules, every Part B (14-mark) question provides an **internal choice**: the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are provided below with full sub-parts and step-by-step model solutions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — PCM System Design Problem**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the working of a PCM system with a neat block diagram of transmitter and receiver. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A sinusoidal signal of peak amplitude 4 V and frequency 4 kHz is sampled at 8 kHz and quantized using a uniform 10-bit quantizer.
  Compute:
  (i) Number of quantization levels
  (ii) Quantization step size
  (iii) SQNR in dB
  (iv) Output bit rate
  (v) Minimum ideal transmission bandwidth. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a) — 7 marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the full PCM system showing the Anti-Aliasing Filter → Sampler → Quantizer → Encoder → Line Coder on the TX side, and Line Decoder → Regenerative Repeater → Decoder → Reconstruction LPF on the RX side (refer to Section 4.3 of these notes).

**Working (4 marks — distribute as 1 mark per step):**

1. **Sampling:** The analog signal $x(t)$ is multiplied by an impulse train at $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. The output is a discrete-time signal $x(nT_s)$.
2. **Quantization:** Each sample amplitude is approximated to the nearest of $L = 2^n$ levels. The step size is $\Delta = (V_{max}-V_{min})/L$. This step is **lossy** and introduces quantization error.
3. **Encoding:** Each quantized level is mapped to an $n$-bit binary code (Natural Binary or Folded Binary). The output is a serial bitstream at $R_b = n f_s$ bps.
4. **Reception & Reconstruction:** The receiver decodes the bits back to quantized levels and a low-pass reconstruction filter (cutoff $f_m$) reconstructs the analog waveform. Regenerative repeaters along the channel cancel accumulated noise.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b) — 7 marks

Given: $A = 4$ V, $f_m = 4$ kHz, $f_s = 8$ kHz, $n = 10$ bits.

**(i) Quantization levels (1 mark):**
$$L = 2^n = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ levels}$$

**(ii) Step size (2 marks):**
The signal swings from $-A = -4$ V to $+A = +4$ V, so $V_{pp} = 8$ V.
$$\Delta = \frac{V_{pp}}{L} = \frac{8}{1024} = 0.0078125 \text{ V} = 7.8125 \text{ mV}$$

**(iii) SQNR in dB (2 marks):**
$$\mathrm{SQNR} = 1.76 + 6.02 \times n = 1.76 + 6.02 \times 10 = 1.76 + 60.20 = 61.96 \text{ dB}$$

**(iv) Output bit rate (1 mark):**
$$R_b = n \cdot f_s = 10 \times 8000 = 80{,}000 \text{ bps} = 80 \text{ kbps}$$

**(v) Minimum ideal transmission bandwidth (1 mark):**
$$B_T = \frac{R_b}{2} = \frac{80{,}000}{2} = 40{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 40 \text{ kHz}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — SQNR & Quantization Theory**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Series-2]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio (SQNR) in dB for a uniform PCM system. State clearly the assumptions used. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A PCM system must achieve an SQNR of at least 50 dB. Determine:
  (i) The minimum number of bits per sample $n$.
  (ii) If the input signal bandwidth is 5 kHz, the Nyquist rate and the resulting bit rate.
  (iii) If a companding technique with $\mu = 255$ (μ-law) is applied, by what factor is the bit rate reduced for the same SQNR? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a) — 7 marks

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Input is a full-scale sinusoid of amplitude $A$ (peak).
2. Quantizer is **uniform** with $L = 2^n$ levels and step size $\Delta = 2A/L$.
3. Quantization error $e_q$ is uniformly distributed in $[-\Delta/2, +\Delta/2]$ and uncorrelated with the signal (the **Bennett integral assumption**).

**Derivation (5 marks):**

The signal power is:

$$S = \frac{A^2}{2}$$

The quantization noise power is:

$$N_q = \frac{\Delta^2}{12}$$

Therefore:

$$\mathrm{SNR} = \frac{S}{N_q} = \frac{A^2 / 2}{\Delta^2 / 12} = \frac{6 A^2}{\Delta^2}$$

Substituting $\Delta = 2A / 2^n$:

$$\mathrm{SNR} = \frac{6 A^2}{4 A^2 / 4^n} = \frac{3}{2}\cdot 4^n = 1.5 \cdot 2^{2n}$$

Converting to decibels:

$$\mathrm{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 10 n \log_{10}(4) = 1.76 + 6.02 n$$

$$\boxed{\mathrm{SQNR}_{\text{dB}} = 1.76 + 6.02\,n}$$

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Minimum $n$ for 50 dB SQNR (2 marks):**
$$n \geq \frac{50 - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{48.24}{6.02} = 8.012$$

Rounding up, $n_{\min} = 9$ bits per sample.

**(ii) Bit rate for 5 kHz signal (2 marks):**
$$f_s = 2 f_m = 2 \times 5000 = 10{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 10 \text{ kHz}$$
$$R_b = n f_s = 9 \times 10{,}000 = 90 \text{ kbps}$$

**(iii) Companding improvement (3 marks):**

In a companded PCM system, the effective SQNR for low-amplitude signals is improved by approximately $Q$ dB, where for μ-law:
$$Q_{\mu} \approx \frac{6.02}{\ln(1 + \mu)}\cdot \ln(\mu) \quad \text{(at low signal levels)}$$

For $\mu = 255$:
$$Q_{\mu} \approx 25 \text{ to } 30 \text{ dB improvement}$$

A practical engineering estimate is that companding **saves 2 to 4 bits per sample** for voice signals. Hence, the same 50 dB SQNR could be achieved with $n = 9 - 3 = 6$ bits (a conservative estimate).

The bit rate reduces from 90 kbps to approximately $6 \times 10 = 60$ kbps, a reduction factor of $90/60 = 1.5\times$ (i.e., the bandwidth is $1.5$ times smaller).

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking note:** KTU examiners accept the simplified textbook statement *"companding saves ~2 bits"* and award 3 marks if the student clearly mentions **non-uniform quantization**, the **logarithmic compression characteristic**, and computes the resulting bit rate.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print this before the exam.**

- **PCM = Sampling + Quantization + Encoding.** Memorize this triplet in order.
- **Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$. The minimum $f_s = 2 f_m$ is the Nyquist rate.
- **Number of levels:** $L = 2^n$ where $n$ is bits per sample.
- **Step size (uniform):** $\Delta = V_{pp} / L$.
- **Quantization noise power:** $N_q = \Delta^2 / 12$.
- **SQNR in dB:** $\boxed{1.76 + 6.02\,n}$ — every additional bit adds ~6 dB of SQNR.
- **Bit rate:** $R_b = n f_s$.
- **Ideal bandwidth:** $B_T = R_b / 2$.
- **Practical NRZ bandwidth:** $B_T = R_b$.
- **Quantization error bound:** $\vert e_q \vert \leq \Delta/2$.
- **PCM is irreversible / lossy** — quantization destroys information.
- **Companding (μ-law / A-law)** improves SQNR for low-amplitude signals at the cost of slightly worse high-amplitude fidelity.
- **Folded Binary Code** is used in telephony because MSB errors become small-magnitude errors.
- **Anti-aliasing LPF** must be placed **before** the sampler.
- **Reconstruction LPF** must be placed **after** the decoder.
- **Regenerative repeaters** make PCM virtually noise-immune over long distances.
- **Standard telephony:** 8 kHz sampling, 8 bits/sample → **64 kbps** DS0 channel.
- **Audio CD:** 44.1 kHz, 16 bits → 705.6 kbps per channel (LPCM stereo).
- **Minimum $n$ for target SQNR:** $n = \lceil (S_{req} - 1.76)/6.02 \rceil$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three formulas that appear in 80% of KTU PCM problems:**
> 1. $\mathrm{SQNR} = 1.76 + 6.02\,n$
> 2. $R_b = n f_s$
> 3. $\Delta = V_{pp} / 2^n$
>
> Master these and you will score full marks on the numerical portions of Part B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Delta Modulation (DM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Delta Modulation (DM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Delta Modulation (DM)** is a **single-bit** predictive differential pulse coding technique in which each sample of the input analog message signal $m(t)$ is encoded as a single binary digit ($\mathbf{+1}$ or $\mathbf{-1}$) representing the **sign of the difference** between the current sample and its predicted (locally reconstructed) value, using a fixed step size $\sigma$ (or $\Delta$).

Mathematically, the transmitted bit at the $k^{th}$ instant is:

$$b_k = \text{sgn}\big[m(kT_s) - m_q((k-1)T_s)\big]$$

where $m(kT_s)$ is the current input sample, $m_q((k-1)T_s)$ is the previous reconstructed (predicted) sample held by the local accumulator, and $T_s = 1/f_s$ is the sampling interval.

DM is essentially a **1-bit Differential PCM (DPCM)** system that oversamples the input signal to exploit the strong correlation between successive samples.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** DM is positioned in Module 2 as a *predictive/hybrid analog-to-digital conversion* scheme, contrasting directly with PCM and DPCM covered in earlier module units.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **blindfolded hiker trying to trace the outline of a mountain ridge** while wearing shoes with a fixed-size lift on the heel. At every step, the hiker can only choose to either:

- **Step UP** by exactly one fixed lift-height $\Delta$ (transmit a $+1$), OR
- **Step DOWN** by exactly one fixed lift-height $\Delta$ (transmit a $-1$).

The hiker compares the **actual ground altitude** with the **estimated altitude from the previous step** and decides the direction of the next step. A series of such "$\pm\Delta$" steps traces a **staircase waveform** that approximates the true mountain profile. Too small a step → the hiker cannot climb steep slopes (slope overload). Too large a step → the staircase oscillates noisily on flat terrain (granular noise). This is exactly the operating principle of DM.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Checklist

| Term | Meaning | Notation |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Step size** | Fixed quantization increment of the 1-bit quantizer | $\sigma$ or $\Delta$ (in Volts) |
| **Sampling rate** | Rate at which the input is sampled; DM requires $f_s \gg$ Nyquist rate | $f_s$ |
| **Staircase waveform** | Reconstructed approximation at receiver | $m_q(t)$ |
| **Slope overload** | Distortion when input changes faster than the staircase can follow | — |
| **Granular noise** | Random error when step size is too large for slowly varying regions | — |
| **Predictor** | A 1-sample delay (pure accumulator structure in basic DM) | $z^{-1}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Working Principle (Bulleted Logic)

The DM system operates as an oversampled **tracking encoder-decoder pair**:

1. **Sampling:** The continuous-time message $m(t)$ is sampled at a very high rate $f_s$ (typically 5–10 times the Nyquist rate $2W$).
2. **Comparison:** Each sample $m(kT_s)$ is compared with the previous reconstructed sample $m_q((k-1)T_s)$ stored in the local accumulator.
3. **1-bit Quantization:** The comparator output is hard-limited to produce:
   - $b_k = \mathbf{+1}$ (i.e., $+ \Delta$) if $m(kT_s) > m_q((k-1)T_s)$ → signal is rising
   - $b_k = \mathbf{-1}$ (i.e., $- \Delta$) if $m(kT_s) \leq m_q((k-1)T_s)$ → signal is falling
4. **Transmission:** Only the polarity bit $b_k \in \{+1, -1\}$ is transmitted (1 bit/sample).
5. **Local Reconstruction at Tx:** The accumulator adds $\pm\Delta$ to the previous stored value, producing $m_q(kT_s)$ for the next comparison.
6. **Receiver Decoding:** The receiver is an identical accumulator that adds $\pm\Delta$ to its own previous value, generating the same staircase waveform $m_q(t)$.
7. **Low-pass Filtering:** An LPF smooths the staircase to approximate the original message.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The transmitter and receiver are **structurally identical** accumulators. As long as both start with the same initial value and receive the same bit stream, they produce identical staircase outputs — **no side-information (step size) needs to be transmitted**. This is the defining elegance of DM.

## 2.2 The Two Cardinal Imperfections

| Imperfection | Cause | Effect | Remedy |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Slope Overload** | $\vert dm(t)/dt \vert_{max} > \Delta \cdot f_s$ | Staircase cannot climb fast enough; **systematic distortion** | Increase $\Delta$ or $f_s$ |
| **Granular Noise** | $\Delta$ is too large for slowly varying $m(t)$ | Random $\pm\Delta$ jitter on flat segments | Decrease $\Delta$ |

This creates a **fundamental trade-off**: $\Delta$ must be small to limit granular noise but large enough to prevent slope overload.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **must-memorize high-yield set** for the KTU board exam. Master every entry.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary/Condition |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Transmitted bit | $b_k = \text{sgn}\big[m(kT_s) - m_q((k-1)T_s)\big]$ | dimensionless | $b_k \in \{+1, -1\}$ |
| 2 | Reconstructed sample | $m_q(kT_s) = m_q((k-1)T_s) + b_k \cdot \Delta$ | Volts | accumulator recursion |
| 3 | Slope overload avoidance | $\left \vert \dfrac{dm(t)}{dt} \right \vert_{max} \leq \Delta \cdot f_s$ | V/s | necessary condition |
| 4 | Step size (sinusoidal) | $\Delta \geq \dfrac{2\pi f A_m}{f_s}$ | Volts | minimum to avoid slope overload |
| 5 | Granular noise variance | $N_q = \sigma_q^{\,2} = \dfrac{\Delta^2}{3}$ | $V^2$ | uniform error pdf over $[-\Delta, +\Delta]$ |
| 6 | Signal power (sine) | $P_s = \dfrac{A_m^{\,2}}{2}$ | $V^2$ | RMS power of sinusoid |
| 7 | Output SNR (linear) | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{P_s}{N_q} = \dfrac{3 A_m^{\,2}}{2 \Delta^{2}}$ | dimensionless | ratio |
| 8 | Output SNR (dB) | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{3 A_m^{\,2}}{2 \Delta^{2}}\right)$ | dB | logarithmic form |
| 9 | SNR in terms of $f_s$ | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{3}{8\pi^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{f_s^{\,2}}{f^{2}}$ | dimensionless | substituting eq. (4) into eq. (7) |
| 10 | Granular noise PSD | $S_{n}(f) = \dfrac{\Delta^{2}}{6 f_s}$ | $V^{2}/Hz$ | assumed white within message BW |
| 11 | Output SNR (with LPF) | $\text{SNR}_o = \dfrac{3}{8\pi^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{f_s^{\,3}}{f^{2} f_m}$ | dimensionless | $f_m$ = message bandwidth |
| 12 | ADM step update | $\Delta_k = \Delta_{k-1} \cdot \vert b_k \vert \cdot K$ | Volts | $K$ = adaptation constant |

> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** DM/ADM codecs are deployed in **military secure voice (CVSD — Continuously Variable Slope Delta Modulation)**, **overloaded POTS speech scramblers**, **low-cost IoT sensor telemetry**, and **Apollo-era NASA telemetry links** where bit-rate economy outweighs fidelity. Modern descendants include **Sigma-Delta ($\Sigma\Delta$) ADCs** in audio codecs and high-precision instrumentation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Slope Overload Condition for a Sinusoidal Input

**Given:** Input message $m(t) = A_m \sin(2\pi f t)$, sampling rate $f_s$, step size $\Delta$.

**Goal:** Find the minimum step size $\Delta_{min}$ that prevents slope overload.

**Step 1 — Compute the maximum slope of the message signal.**
The instantaneous slope is:

$$S(t) = \frac{dm(t)}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\big[A_m \sin(2\pi f t)\big] = 2\pi f A_m \cos(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 2 — Identify the peak slope.**

Since $\cos(2\pi f t)$ oscillates between $-1$ and $+1$, the magnitude of the slope is bounded by:

$$\left \vert S(t) \right \vert = 2\pi f A_m \, \vert \cos(2\pi f t) \vert \;\leq\; 2\pi f A_m$$

Therefore, the **maximum slope** is:

$$\left \vert \frac{dm(t)}{dt} \right \vert_{max} = 2\pi f A_m$$

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum staircase slope that the DM encoder can produce.**
In one sampling interval $T_s = 1/f_s$, the staircase can rise by at most one full step $\Delta$. Hence the **maximum staircase tracking slope** is:

$$S_{track,\,max} = \frac{\Delta}{T_s} = \Delta \cdot f_s$$

**Step 4 — Impose the slope-overload avoidance condition.**
To track the input without systematic lag, the encoder's tracking slope must be **at least** the input's maximum slope:

$$\Delta \cdot f_s \;\geq\; \left \vert \frac{dm(t)}{dt} \right \vert_{max} = 2\pi f A_m$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the minimum permissible step size.**

$$\boxed{\;\Delta \;\geq\; \Delta_{min} = \frac{2\pi f A_m}{f_s}\;}$$

> **Conversion Logic Note:** The factor $2\pi f$ comes from the derivative of sine; the factor $A_m$ is the amplitude; the factor $1/f_s$ converts the per-sample step into a slope (V/s). This is the **first equation every KTU examiner expects to see derived.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Granular Noise Power

**Given:** The quantization error $e(t) = m(t) - m_q(t)$ is bounded as $\vert e(t) \vert \leq \Delta$ and is assumed to be **uniformly distributed** over $[-\Delta, +\Delta]$.

**Goal:** Compute the mean-square error (variance) of $e(t)$.

**Step 1 — State the uniform probability density function.**

$$p(e) = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2\Delta}, & -\Delta \leq e \leq +\Delta \\[6pt] 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 2 — Verify the PDF is normalized.**

$$\int_{-\Delta}^{+\Delta} \frac{1}{2\Delta}\, de = \frac{1}{2\Delta}\big[e\big]_{-\Delta}^{+\Delta} = \frac{1}{2\Delta}(2\Delta) = 1 \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mean-square value (variance).**

$$N_q = E[e^{2}] = \int_{-\Delta}^{+\Delta} e^{2} \cdot p(e)\, de = \int_{-\Delta}^{+\Delta} e^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2\Delta}\, de$$

$$N_q = \frac{1}{2\Delta}\left[\frac{e^{3}}{3}\right]_{-\Delta}^{+\Delta} = \frac{1}{2\Delta}\left(\frac{\Delta^{3}}{3} - \frac{(-\Delta)^{3}}{3}\right) = \frac{1}{2\Delta}\left(\frac{2\Delta^{3}}{3}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\;N_q = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{3}\;}$$

> **Conversion Logic Note:** The factor 1/3 emerges from the cubic term in the antiderivative. This is the **standard uniform-quantizer result** and is the KTU board's expected noise-power expression.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Output Signal-to-Noise Ratio

**Step 1 — Signal power for a sinusoid.**
A sinusoid $A_m \sin(2\pi f t)$ has RMS value $A_m/\sqrt{2}$, so:

$$P_s = \left(\frac{A_m}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{2} = \frac{A_m^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the SNR expression.**

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{P_s}{N_q} = \frac{A_m^{2}/2}{\Delta^{2}/3} = \frac{3 A_m^{2}}{2 \Delta^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Eliminate $\Delta$ using the slope-overload result $\Delta = 2\pi f A_m / f_s$.**

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{3 A_m^{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta^{2}} = \frac{3 A_m^{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{f_s^{2}}{(2\pi f A_m)^{2}} = \frac{3 A_m^{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{f_s^{2}}{4\pi^{2} f^{2} A_m^{2}}$$

Cancel $A_m^{2}$:

$$\boxed{\;\text{SNR} = \frac{3 f_s^{2}}{8\pi^{2} f^{2}}\;}$$

**Step 4 — Express in dB (frequently asked).**

$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3}{8\pi^{2}}\right) + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{f_s}{f}\right) \approx -14.2 + 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{f_s}{f}\right) \text{ dB}$$

> **Physical Interpretation:** SNR improves as $\mathbf{6 \; dB}$ for every **doubling of the sampling rate** $f_s$ (because $f_s^{2}$ is in the numerator). This is the **$6\,dB/octave$ rule for DM** — a key KTU exam result.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Output SNR After Receiver Low-Pass Filtering

When the granular noise is modelled as **white** with two-sided PSD $S_n(f) = \Delta^{2}/(6 f_s)$ (V²/Hz), and the LPF passes only $|f| \leq f_m$ (message bandwidth), the **in-band** noise power is:

$$N_{in} = \int_{-f_m}^{+f_m} S_n(f)\, df = 2 f_m \cdot \frac{\Delta^{2}}{6 f_s} = \frac{\Delta^{2} f_m}{3 f_s}$$

Using the slope-overload equality $\Delta = 2\pi f A_m / f_s$:

$$N_{in} = \frac{1}{3 f_s} \cdot \left(\frac{2\pi f A_m}{f_s}\right)^{2} f_m = \frac{4 \pi^{2} f^{2} A_m^{2} f_m}{3 f_s^{3}}$$

Then:

$$\text{SNR}_o = \frac{P_s}{N_{in}} = \frac{A_m^{2}/2}{\,4 \pi^{2} f^{2} A_m^{2} f_m / (3 f_s^{3})\,} = \frac{3 A_m^{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{3 f_s^{3}}{4\pi^{2} f^{2} A_m^{2} f_m}$$

$$\boxed{\;\text{SNR}_o = \frac{3 f_s^{3}}{8\pi^{2} f^{2} f_m}\;}$$

> **Conversion Logic Note:** Note the exponent jump from $f_s^{2}$ to $f_s^{3}$ — the additional $f_s$ in the numerator arises because the LPF **shrinks** the in-band noise proportionally to $1/f_s$. This is a **9 dB/octave** improvement in $f_s$ after LPF integration — a classic KTU follow-up question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Delta Modulation Transmitter — Functional Block Architecture

The following block diagram captures the closed-loop tracking structure of the DM encoder. Note the **comparator-driven 1-bit quantizer** and the **local accumulator** that closes the prediction loop.

```mermaid
graph LR
    sigA["INPUT MESSAGE m(t)"] --> smpB["SAMPLER at fs"]
    smpB --> cmpC["COMPARATOR SUBTRACTOR"]
    fbD["FEEDBACK PREDICTION SIGNAL"] --> cmpC
    cmpC --> qntE["1-BIT HARD LIMITER + DELTA"]
    qntE --> outF["TRANSMITTED BIT STREAM bk"]
    qntE --> accG["ACCUMULATOR SUMMER PLUS DELAY"]
    accG --> fbD
    accG --> monH["LOCAL STAIRCASE mq(t)"]
```

**Read-Order for Students:** Signal enters from the left → sampled → compared with the previous staircase value held in the accumulator → quantized to $\pm\Delta$ → emitted as the bit stream **and** fed back to update the accumulator. The accumulator's output is simultaneously the predicted value (sent back to the comparator) and a local monitoring replica.

---

## 4.2 Delta Modulation Receiver — Functional Block Architecture

The receiver is intentionally identical to the transmitter's accumulator chain, plus a smoothing low-pass filter.

```mermaid
graph LR
    rxA["RECEIVED BIT STREAM bk"] --> sftB["SIGN RECOVERY PLUS MINUS DELTA"]
    sftB --> accC["ACCUMULATOR SUMMER PLUS DELAY"]
    accC --> lpfD["LOW PASS FILTER LPF"]
    lpfD --> outE["RECONSTRUCTED MESSAGE mhat t"]
    accC --> fbF["FEEDBACK DELAYED COPY"]
    fbF --> accC
```

**Read-Order:** The bit stream drives a $\pm\Delta$ pulse generator → accumulator reconstructs the staircase → LPF smooths the staircase to recover the analog message.

---

## 4.3 Adaptive Delta Modulation (ADM) Block Diagram

ADM is the **high-yield extension** frequently asked as a 7-mark sub-part. The block diagram includes a **step-size controller** that observes the bit history and adjusts $\Delta$ adaptively.

```mermaid
graph LR
    inA["INPUT m(t)"] --> smpB["SAMPLER fs"]
    smpB --> cmpC["COMPARATOR"]
    fbE["ADAPTIVE PREDICTION"] --> cmpC
    cmpC --> qntD["1-BIT QUANTIZER PLUS MINUS DELTAK"]
    qntD --> txF["OUTPUT bk"]
    qntD --> ctlG["STEP SIZE CONTROLLER"]
    ctlG --> accH["ADAPTIVE ACCUMULATOR"]
    accH --> fbE
    accH --> outI["RECONSTRUCTED mq(t)"]
```

**Controller Logic (Song's Algorithm — KTU favorite):**
- If $b_k = b_{k-1} = b_{k-2} = \dots$ (consecutive identical polarity) → **slope is too steep** → **increase $\Delta$** (typically $\Delta_k = K \cdot \Delta_{k-1}$ with $K > 1$).
- If bit stream **alternates** $\pm 1, \mp 1, \pm 1, \dots$ → **region is flat** → **decrease $\Delta$** (typically $\Delta_k = \Delta_{k-1} / K$).

> **Why ADM Matters:** ADM simultaneously solves the slope-overload vs. granular-noise dilemma by **adapting** the step size to local signal statistics. CVSD (Continuously Variable Slope DM) is a 1-bit ADM used in Bluetooth, military radios, and NASA mission communications.

---

## 4.4 Conceptual Staircase-Waveform Schematic

The following ASCII representation illustrates the input sinusoid (smooth) and the DM staircase (jagged) tracking it. Use this mental image to recall slope-overload and granular-noise situations.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph TM["TIME DOMAIN BEHAVIOR"]
        dir1["REGION A FLAT LOW SLOPE CAUSES GRANULAR NOISE BOUNCING PLUS MINUS DELTA"]
        dir2["REGION B STEEP POSITIVE SLOPE SLOPE OVERLOAD STAIRCASE LAGS BELOW m t"]
        dir3["REGION C STEEP NEGATIVE SLOPE SLOPE OVERLOAD STAIRCASE LAGS ABOVE m t"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Tip:** In **Region A** (flat), the staircase "ping-pongs" by $\pm\Delta$ around the true signal — this is granular noise. In **Regions B and C** (steep), the staircase cannot catch up to the rapidly changing sinusoid — this is slope overload. A well-designed DM must keep Region A and Region B/C durations small.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*
> **Define Delta Modulation. Why is it called a 1-bit DPCM system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Delta Modulation is a differential pulse code modulation technique in which only **one bit per sample** is transmitted, representing the sign of the difference between the current sample and the locally predicted (previous reconstructed) sample, using a fixed step size $\Delta$.

It is called a **1-bit DPCM system** because:
- **(1 mark)** It is fundamentally a DPCM scheme that exploits correlation between adjacent samples and transmits only the prediction error.
- **(1 mark)** The error is quantized to **2 levels only** ($+\Delta$ for positive error, $-\Delta$ for negative error), which is encoded using a **single bit** per sample ($+1$ or $-1$).
- **(1 mark)** Thus the code-word length is $n=1$, giving a bit rate of $R_b = f_s$ bits/s — half or less than that of standard PCM.

---

> **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*
> **Differentiate between slope overload error and granular noise in Delta Modulation. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Slope Overload | Granular Noise |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Cause** | Step size $\Delta$ too small (or $f_s$ too low) to track rapid signal changes | Step size $\Delta$ too large for slowly varying or flat signal regions |
| **Nature of error** | Systematic, directional — staircase consistently lags the signal | Random — staircase ping-pongs $\pm\Delta$ around the true value |
| **Equation trigger** | $\vert dm/dt \vert_{max} > \Delta \cdot f_s$ | $e(t)$ oscillates uniformly over $[-\Delta, +\Delta]$ |
| **Remedy** | Increase $\Delta$ or oversample (raise $f_s$) | Decrease $\Delta$ |
| **Trade-off** | Both are **conflicting** — ADM is the unified solution | — |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2023]*

> **A(a).** Draw the block diagram of a Delta Modulation system (transmitter and receiver) and explain its working with the help of relevant equations. *(7 marks)*
>
> **A(b).** A sinusoid of amplitude $A_m = 2\;\text{V}$ and frequency $f = 500\;\text{Hz}$ is applied to a DM system operating at $f_s = 32\;\text{kHz}$. Determine:
> - (i) the minimum step size $\Delta$ required to avoid slope overload,
> - (ii) the granular noise power $N_q$,
> - (iii) the output signal-to-noise ratio in dB.
> *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — A(a) [7 marks]

**Block diagrams (2 marks):** Draw the transmitter and receiver as shown in SECTION 4.1 and 4.2. (Receivers are full credit only if the LPF after the accumulator is explicitly shown.)

**Working explanation (5 marks):**

1. **(1 mark)** Input $m(t)$ is sampled at $f_s \gg 2W$ to obtain $m(kT_s)$.
2. **(1 mark)** The comparator computes the error $e_k = m(kT_s) - m_q((k-1)T_s)$ where $m_q((k-1)T_s)$ is the previous reconstructed sample from the accumulator.
3. **(1 mark)** The 1-bit quantizer outputs:
$$b_k = \begin{cases} +1, & e_k > 0 \\ -1, & e_k \leq 0 \end{cases}$$
4. **(1 mark)** The accumulator updates via $m_q(kT_s) = m_q((k-1)T_s) + b_k \cdot \Delta$.
5. **(1 mark)** The bit stream $b_k$ is transmitted. At the receiver, an identical accumulator reconstructs the staircase, which is then low-pass filtered to recover $\hat{m}(t)$.

#### Model Solution — A(b) [7 marks]

**Given:** $A_m = 2\;\text{V}$, $f = 500\;\text{Hz}$, $f_s = 32\;\text{kHz}$.

**(i) Minimum step size to avoid slope overload (2 marks):**

Using $\Delta_{min} = 2\pi f A_m / f_s$:

$$\Delta_{min} = \frac{2\pi \times 500 \times 2}{32{,}000} = \frac{2000\pi}{32{,}000} = \frac{\pi}{16} \approx 0.1963 \;\text{V}$$

**(ii) Granular noise power (2 marks):**

$$N_q = \frac{\Delta^{2}}{3} = \frac{(0.1963)^{2}}{3} = \frac{0.03854}{3} \approx 0.01285 \;\text{V}^{2}$$

**(iii) Output SNR in dB (3 marks):**

$$\text{SNR} = \frac{3 A_m^{2}}{2 \Delta^{2}} = \frac{3 \times 4}{2 \times 0.03854} = \frac{12}{0.07708} \approx 155.7$$

$$\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(155.7) \approx 21.92 \;\text{dB}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students **forget to convert** the SNR linear ratio to dB when the question asks for SNR "in dB". This alone costs 1 mark. Also, students often use $A_m$ instead of $A_m^{2}/2$ for signal power — that is the **second most common** mark-loss in this question type.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]*

> **B(a).** Derive the expression for the minimum step size required to avoid slope overload in a Delta Modulation system. Assume a sinusoidal input of amplitude $A_m$ and frequency $f$. State the slope-overload condition clearly. *(7 marks)*
>
> **B(b).** With a neat block diagram, explain the principle of operation of **Adaptive Delta Modulation (ADM)**. How does ADM overcome the slope-overload vs. granular-noise trade-off present in standard DM? *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — B(a) [7 marks]

**Full derivation as in SECTION 3.1** (5 marks), broken as:

1. **(1 mark)** Write the input as $m(t) = A_m \sin(2\pi f t)$.
2. **(1 mark)** Compute $dm/dt = 2\pi f A_m \cos(2\pi f t)$.
3. **(1 mark)** Identify $\vert dm/dt \vert_{max} = 2\pi f A_m$.
4. **(1 mark)** Identify the maximum staircase tracking slope as $\Delta f_s$.
5. **(1 mark)** State and solve the inequality $\Delta f_s \geq 2\pi f A_m \Rightarrow \Delta_{min} = 2\pi f A_m / f_s$.

**Slope-overload condition (2 marks):** Slope overload occurs when $\vert dm/dt \vert_{max} > \Delta f_s$. The condition to **avoid** it is $\Delta f_s \geq 2\pi f A_m$.

#### Model Solution — B(b) [7 marks]

**Block diagram (2 marks):** As in SECTION 4.3 — the ADM transmitter must explicitly include a **step-size controller** observing the bit stream and feeding a variable $\Delta_k$ into the accumulator.

**Principle of operation (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** In ADM, the step size is **not fixed** — it varies with time as $\Delta_k$.
- **(1 mark)** The controller examines the recent bit pattern. If consecutive bits are identical (e.g., $+1, +1, +1$), the signal is varying steeply in one direction → **slope-overload imminent** → $\Delta_k$ is **increased** (e.g., $\Delta_k = K \Delta_{k-1}$, $K > 1$).
- **(1 mark)** If bits alternate (e.g., $+1, -1, +1, -1$), the signal is nearly flat → **granular noise imminent** → $\Delta_k$ is **decreased** (e.g., $\Delta_k = \Delta_{k-1} / K$).

**How ADM overcomes the trade-off (2 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** Standard DM cannot pick a $\Delta$ that simultaneously prevents slope overload (needs large $\Delta$) and limits granular noise (needs small $\Delta$).
- **(1 mark)** ADM **adapts** $\Delta$ to local signal characteristics — large $\Delta$ on steep slopes, small $\Delta$ on flat regions — thereby resolving the conflict and providing **higher SNR** for the same average bit rate (1 bit/sample).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Two specific mistakes lose marks here:
> 1. Writing $\Delta_k = K \cdot \Delta_{k-1}$ without explaining **what $K$ is** (you must state $K > 1$ is the adaptation constant, typically $1.5$ for CVSD).
> 2. Forgetting to mention the **receiver must be adaptive too** — the receiver needs the same adaptation rule applied to its local accumulator, otherwise synchronization is lost. Examiners frequently deduct 1 mark if the receiver's adaptive nature is not mentioned.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read Twice Before the Exam**

- [ ] **DM is 1-bit DPCM** — transmits only the **sign** of the prediction error using a fixed step $\Delta$.
- [ ] **Bit rate** $R_b = f_s$ bits/s (vs. $n f_s$ for $n$-bit PCM).
- [ ] **Slope-overload condition:** $\Delta f_s \geq \vert dm/dt \vert_{max}$.
- [ ] **Sinusoidal $\Delta_{min}$:** $\Delta = 2\pi f A_m / f_s$.
- [ ] **Granular noise power:** $N_q = \Delta^{2} / 3$ (uniform-error model).
- [ ] **Output SNR (pre-LPF):** $\text{SNR} = 3 A_m^{2} / (2 \Delta^{2}) = 3 f_s^{2} / (8\pi^{2} f^{2})$.
- [ ] **Output SNR (post-LPF):** $\text{SNR}_o = 3 f_s^{3} / (8\pi^{2} f^{2} f_m)$.
- [ ] **6 dB/octave** rule in $f_s$ for basic SNR; **9 dB/octave** after LPF integration.
- [ ] **Tx and Rx are identical accumulators** — no step-size side information required in standard DM.
- [ ] **ADM** uses variable $\Delta_k$ adapted to bit history; solves the slope/granular trade-off.
- [ ] **CVSD** is the famous 1-bit ADM used in Bluetooth (older profiles) and military radios.
- [ ] **Sigma-Delta ($\Sigma\Delta$) modulators** are the modern descendants — used in audio ADCs of phones and DACs of high-fidelity equipment.
- [ ] **Always express SNR in dB** when the question requests it: $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(\text{SNR}_{linear})$.
- [ ] **Watch the units** — $f_s$ in Hz, $A_m$ in V, $\Delta$ in V, $N_q$ in $V^{2}$.

> **Final Mantra for KTU Board:** *"A Delta Modulator is a one-bit blindfolded hiker tracing a mountain with a fixed-step ladder — give him an adaptive ladder (ADM) and he traces any mountain with minimum error."* 🥾⛰️

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Digital Data to Analog Signal - Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Digital Data to Analog Signal - Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK)**, also known as **On-Off Keying (OOK)**, is a fundamental form of **digital-to-analog modulation** in which the amplitude of a high-frequency sinusoidal carrier signal is systematically varied in direct proportion to the discrete digital data stream, while the frequency and phase of the carrier remain strictly constant.

Mathematically, the carrier signal is represented as:

$$s_c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi_c)$$

In ASK modulation, the instantaneous amplitude $A_c$ is switched between two discrete values depending on the input binary symbol:
- **Binary `1` (Mark):** Carrier transmitted with full amplitude $A_c$
- **Binary `0` (Space):** Carrier amplitude is set to zero (or a minimum value)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** ASK is the simplest and earliest form of digital modulation. It is a special case of **Binary Amplitude Shift Keying (BASK)**. Despite its bandwidth inefficiency, it is heavily tested in KTU examinations because it is the conceptual foundation for understanding PSK, FSK, and QAM.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a dark room with a **flashlight** communicating with a friend outside:
- When you want to say **"1"**, you turn the flashlight **ON** (light visible = carrier present).
- When you want to say **"0"**, you turn the flashlight **OFF** (no light = carrier absent).
- The **frequency of the flickering** does not change; only the **presence or absence of light** (amplitude) changes.

This is exactly how ASK works. The carrier is a continuous sine wave (the flashlight), and the digital bits toggle its amplitude (ON/OFF). The receiver simply checks: *"Is the carrier present? If yes → 1. If no → 0."*

## 1.3 Classification of ASK

| Variant | Description | Application |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| **BASK / OOK** | 2 amplitude levels (0 and A) | Simple optical/RF links |
| **MASK (M-ary ASK)** | More than 2 amplitude levels (0, A, 2A, 3A...) | High-speed wireline (PAM) |
| **Coherent ASK** | Requires phase reference at receiver | Better BER performance |
| **Non-Coherent ASK** | Uses envelope detection | Simple, cheap receivers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** The transmission bandwidth of an ASK signal is given by **B_T = 2 × B**, where B is the baseband bandwidth of the digital signal. For a bit duration of $T_b$ seconds, **B_T = 2/T_b Hz**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic Breakdown

The generation and detection of an ASK signal follow a structured pipeline:

### 2.1.1 Modulation (Transmitter Side)
1. **Binary Source** generates a Non-Return-to-Zero (NRZ) data stream $m(t)$, where $m(t) \in \{0, 1\}$.
2. **Product Modulator (Multiplier)** multiplies the NRZ data with the high-frequency carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
3. **Output Signal** $s(t)$ is produced, where the carrier envelope follows the digital pattern.

### 2.1.2 Demodulation (Receiver Side)
ASK can be detected using two distinct methods:

**A. Coherent (Synchronous) Detection:**
1. The received ASK signal is multiplied by a locally generated carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
2. The product is passed through a **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** to extract the baseband.
3. A **Decision Device (Threshold Comparator)** samples the LPF output at the bit center and decides between 0 and 1.

**B. Non-Coherent (Envelope) Detection:**
1. The received ASK signal is passed through an **Envelope Detector** (diode + RC circuit).
2. The detector output is a DC level proportional to the carrier amplitude.
3. A **Threshold Detector** decides: presence of envelope → `1`, absence → `0`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** Non-coherent detection is simpler and cheaper but has a higher Bit Error Rate (BER) compared to coherent detection. The choice depends on the trade-off between **cost** and **performance**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates all KTU-board-tested formulas for ASK. Critical reminder: Vertical pipes `|` are **never** used inside table cells to prevent markdown parsing errors. The `\vert` macro is used instead.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Mathematical Expression | Units / Typical Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | ASK Signal (General) | $s(t) = A_c \cdot m(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts (V) | $m(t)$ is binary NRZ data |
| 2 | Carrier Signal | $s_c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts (V) | $f_c$ is carrier frequency |
| 3 | Signal for Binary `1` | $s_1(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Volts (V) | Full carrier present |
| 4 | Signal for Binary `0` | $s_0(t) = 0$ | Volts (V) | No carrier (OOK) |
| 5 | Bit Rate | $R_b = \dfrac{1}{T_b}$ | bits/second (bps) | $T_b$ is bit duration |
| 6 | Baud Rate (Symbol Rate) | $R_s = R_b$ (for binary ASK) | bauds | Binary modulation → 1 bit/symbol |
| 7 | Transmission Bandwidth | $B_T = 2 \cdot B = \dfrac{2}{T_b} = 2 R_b$ | Hertz (Hz) | Twice the baseband BW |
| 8 | Spectral Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{R_b}{B_T} = 0.5$ | bits/sec/Hz | Inherently inefficient |
| 9 | Average Transmitted Power | $P_t = \dfrac{A_c^2}{4}$ (for OOK, equiprobable) | Watts (W) | Assuming 50% ones |
| 10 | Energy per Bit | $E_b = P_t \cdot T_b$ | Joules (J) | Energy efficiency metric |
| 11 | Coherent BER | $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{2N_0}}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{4N_0}}\right)$ | Dimensionless | $N_0$ is noise PSD |
| 12 | Non-Coherent BER | $P_e = \dfrac{1}{2} e^{-E_b/2N_0}$ | Dimensionless | Envelope detection |
| 13 | Modulation Index (BASK) | $\mu = \dfrac{A_{\max} - A_{\min}}{A_{\max} + A_{\min}}$ | 0 to 1 | $\mu = 1$ for standard OOK |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

ASK is the modulation backbone of several critical real-world systems:

- **Optical Communication:** Infrared remote controls (TVs, ACs) use OOK at 38 kHz carrier.
- **RFID Systems:** Passive RFID tags backscatter a CW signal using ASK.
- **Amateur Radio:** Morse code transmission historically used OOK (Continuous Wave keying).
- **Early Modems:** Low-speed dial-up modems (e.g., Bell 202) used ASK at 1200 bps.
- **Pulsed RF Transmitters:** Simple telemetry and beacon systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Mathematical Expression of the ASK Signal

### Step 1: Define the Binary Data Stream
Let the input digital data be a unipolar NRZ waveform $m(t)$, where $m(t) = 1$ for a binary `1` and $m(t) = 0$ for a binary `0`.

### Step 2: Define the Carrier Signal
The high-frequency sinusoidal carrier is:

$$s_c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

### Step 3: Apply Modulation
The ASK modulator performs a multiplication of the data signal with the carrier. Thus:

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot s_c(t)$$

### Step 4: Substitute the Carrier
Plugging in the carrier expression:

$$s(t) = m(t) \cdot A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

### Step 5: Final Compact Form
For a single bit interval $nT_b \le t \le (n+1)T_b$, this becomes:

$$s(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{if bit} = 1 \\ 0 & \text{if bit} = 0 \end{cases}$$

This is the canonical BASK/OOK waveform.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Transmission Bandwidth of ASK

### Step 1: Identify the Baseband Spectrum
The baseband NRZ data $m(t)$ has a **sinc-squared** power spectral density centered at DC. The main lobe extends from $f = -1/T_b$ to $f = +1/T_b$.

### Step 2: Apply the Modulation Theorem
Multiplication by $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ in the time domain corresponds to a **frequency shift** by $\pm f_c$ in the frequency domain. This is the **Frequency Shifting Property of the Fourier Transform**:

$$m(t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \dfrac{1}{2}\left[M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c)\right]$$

### Step 3: Compute the New Band Extent
Each shifted copy of the baseband spectrum $M(f)$ occupies a bandwidth of $2/T_b$ (from $-1/T_b$ to $+1/T_b$ around its center frequency).

The ASK spectrum has two symmetric sidebands centered at $+f_c$ and $-f_c$. The **positive-frequency sideband** spans from:

$$f_{\text{lower}} = f_c - \dfrac{1}{T_b} \quad \text{to} \quad f_{\text{upper}} = f_c + \dfrac{1}{T_b}$$

### Step 4: Calculate the Total Bandwidth
The total transmission bandwidth is:

$$B_T = f_{\text{upper}} - f_{\text{lower}} = \left(f_c + \dfrac{1}{T_b}\right) - \left(f_c - \dfrac{1}{T_b}\right)$$

$$\boxed{B_T = \dfrac{2}{T_b} = 2 R_b \text{ Hz}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Bit Error Rate (BER) for Coherent ASK

### Step 1: Set Up the Detection Problem
Assume the received signal in a noisy AWGN channel is:

$$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t) \quad \text{for } i \in \{0, 1\}$$

where $n(t)$ is zero-mean white Gaussian noise with PSD $N_0/2$.

### Step 2: Optimal Threshold for Equiprobable Binary
Since the two signal levels are $0$ and $A_c$, the optimal decision threshold is at half the peak amplitude:

$$V_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{A_c}{2}$$

### Step 3: Define the Decision Rule
- If $r > A_c/2$ → decide `1`
- If $r \le A_c/2$ → decide `0`

### Step 4: Express the Energy per Bit
The energy of the `1` symbol over a bit duration is:

$$E_b = \int_0^{T_b} A_c^2 \cos^2(2\pi f_c t)\, dt = \dfrac{A_c^2 T_b}{2}$$

The energy of the `0` symbol is **zero**.

### Step 5: Compute the Symbol Error Probability
For a matched filter receiver, the noise at the decision instant has variance $\sigma^2 = N_0/2$. The probability of error is:

$$P_e = P(\text{sent 1, decided 0}) \cdot P(1) + P(\text{sent 0, decided 1}) \cdot P(0)$$

Assuming equiprobable bits ($P(0) = P(1) = 0.5$):

$$P_e = \dfrac{1}{2} P\!\left(n > \dfrac{A_c}{2}\right) + \dfrac{1}{2} P\!\left(n \le -\dfrac{A_c}{2}\right)$$

### Step 6: Use the Q-Function
For Gaussian noise with zero mean and variance $\sigma^2 = N_0/2$:

$$P_e = \dfrac{1}{2} Q\!\left(\dfrac{A_c/2}{\sigma}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2} Q\!\left(\dfrac{A_c/2}{\sqrt{N_0/2}}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2} Q\!\left(\dfrac{A_c}{\sqrt{2N_0}}\right)$$

### Step 7: Express in Terms of $E_b/N_0$
From Step 4, $A_c = \sqrt{2E_b/T_b}$. Substituting:

$$\dfrac{A_c}{\sqrt{2N_0}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2E_b/T_b}}{\sqrt{2N_0}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0 T_b}}$$

For a bit-rate normalized channel where $T_b = 1$ (i.e., matched filter normalization):

$$\boxed{P_e^{\text{coh}} = \dfrac{1}{2} Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{2N_0}}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative form commonly used in textbooks:** Some texts (like Proakis & Salehi) express the coherent ASK BER as $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/2N_0}\right)$ because they define the signal energy differently. **Always check the convention in your reference.**

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Non-Coherent (Envelope) Detection BER

### Step 1: Envelope Detector Output
For non-coherent detection, the receiver computes the envelope $V(t) = \vert r(t) \vert$ using a diode-RC circuit.

### Step 2: Ricean and Rayleigh Distributions
- If `1` is sent: Envelope follows a **Ricean distribution** with parameter $A_c^2/2$.
- If `0` is sent: Envelope follows a **Rayleigh distribution** (no signal, only noise).

### Step 3: Compute the Error Probability
After integration over the bit duration and threshold comparison at $V_{\text{th}} = A_c/2$:

$$P_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\int_{A_c/2}^{\infty} v \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{v^2}{N_0}\right) dv + \dfrac{1}{2}\int_0^{A_c/2} \dfrac{v}{\sigma_n^2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{v^2}{2\sigma_n^2}\right) dv$$

### Step 4: Simplify the Integral
Evaluating these integrals yields the closed-form result:

$$\boxed{P_e^{\text{non-coh}} = \dfrac{1}{2} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_b}{2N_0}\right)}$$

This is approximately 3 dB worse than coherent detection at low BER.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example: Bandwidth Calculation

**Problem:** A binary data stream of 10 Mbps is transmitted using BASK. Calculate the transmission bandwidth and spectral efficiency.

### Given
- Bit rate $R_b = 10 \times 10^6$ bps

### Solution
Step 1: Since BASK is binary, the symbol rate equals the bit rate:
$$R_s = R_b = 10^7 \text{ bauds}$$

Step 2: Transmission bandwidth:
$$B_T = 2 R_b = 2 \times 10^7 = 20 \text{ MHz}$$

Step 3: Spectral efficiency:
$$\eta = \dfrac{R_b}{B_T} = \dfrac{10^7}{2 \times 10^7} = 0.5 \text{ bits/sec/Hz}$$

**Inference:** ASK uses **twice** the bandwidth required by the baseband signal and achieves a maximum spectral efficiency of only **0.5 bits/sec/Hz**, which is why it is rarely used in bandwidth-constrained modern systems.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: ASK Transmitter (Modulator)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the signal flow in a **BASK transmitter**. Because waveform shapes are difficult to render in Mermaid, a **functional block-level architecture** is used to map the interaction between the data source, modulator, and the output signal conditioning.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Binary Data Source] --> B[NRZ Encoder]
    B --> C[Multiplier Modulator]
    D[Local Oscillator: Ac cos 2 pi fc t] --> C
    C --> E[Bandpass Filter]
    E --> F[Channel: AWGN plus H]
    F --> G[Receiver Input]
```

**Signal Flow Explanation:**
- The **Binary Data Source** (e.g., a microcontroller UART output) generates random `0`s and `1`s.
- The **NRZ Encoder** maps the bits to voltage levels (e.g., +V for `1`, 0V for `0`) to create the baseband waveform $m(t)$.
- The **Multiplier Modulator** (e.g., an MC1496 balanced modulator IC) multiplies $m(t)$ with the carrier.
- The **Local Oscillator** generates the high-frequency carrier $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
- The output $s(t)$ is filtered to remove harmonics and transmitted over the **AWGN Channel**.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: ASK Receiver (Coherent Demodulator)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Received Signal r of t] --> B[Multiplier]
    C[Local Carrier: cos 2 pi fc t] --> B
    B --> D[Low-Pass Filter]
    D --> E[Sampler at T_b]
    E --> F[Threshold Comparator: Vth = Ac/2]
    F --> G[Decision Output: 0 or 1]
    G --> H[Bit Stream Reconstruction]
```

**Demodulation Sequence:**
- The received noisy ASK signal is multiplied by a **synchronized local carrier** (requiring a Phase-Locked Loop or Costas loop in practice).
- A **Low-Pass Filter** (integrate-and-dump or matched filter) extracts the baseband envelope.
- A **Sampler** captures the LPF output at the optimal sampling instant (center of the bit period).
- A **Threshold Comparator** decides whether the sample corresponds to a `0` or `1`.

## 4.3 Signal Waveform Representation (Schematic)

Below is a textual representation of the time-domain ASK waveform. The vertical lines indicate carrier cycles; the envelope follows the binary data.

```
Binary Data:    1     0     1     1     0     0     1
                 _____       _____ _____             _____
                /     \     /     \     \           /     \
   Carrier:  __/       \___/       \_____\_________/       \___
                _____             _____                     _____
               |     |           |     |                   |     |
   ASK Out:  __|     |___________|     |___________________|     |__
```
