```
       (Amplitude follows the binary pattern)
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- When data = `1`, the ASK output is a full-amplitude carrier burst.
- When data = `0`, the output is zero (silence).
- The **envelope** of the ASK signal is a replica of the digital data.

## 4.4 Spectral Representation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Baseband NRZ Spectrum: sinc squared shape centered at 0 Hz] --> B[After Modulation: Two sinc squared lobes at plus and minus fc]
```

- The **main lobe** of the ASK spectrum is centered at $\pm f_c$.
- The **null-to-null bandwidth** of the main lobe is $2/T_b$.
- **Side-lobe levels** decay as $1/f^2$, which causes out-of-band interference in multi-channel systems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why ASK is Bandwidth-Inefficient:** The two sidebands (Upper Sideband and Lower Sideband) carry **redundant information**. Single Sideband ASK (SSB-ASK) can halve the bandwidth, but this is rarely implemented in practice due to synchronization complexity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
Define Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK). Why is it also called On-Off Keying (OOK)?

**Model Answer:**

**Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK):** It is a digital modulation technique in which the amplitude of the sinusoidal carrier is varied in accordance with the digital data signal, while the frequency and phase of the carrier remain constant.

The mathematical expression is:

$$s(t) = A_c \cdot m(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

where $m(t)$ is the binary data stream (0 or 1).

**Why called OOK:** In the simplest and most common form of ASK (BASK), the amplitude is switched between two values: a non-zero value $A_c$ for binary `1` and **zero** for binary `0`. Since the carrier is literally turned **ON** when transmitting a `1` and **OFF** when transmitting a `0`, the technique is called **On-Off Keying (OOK)**. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
State and briefly explain the transmission bandwidth requirement for an ASK system transmitting data at a bit rate of $R_b$ bps.

**Model Answer:**

The transmission bandwidth of an ASK signal is:

$$B_T = 2R_b \text{ Hz}$$

**Explanation:** When a baseband NRZ data signal of bit rate $R_b$ is multiplied by the carrier, its spectrum is shifted to $\pm f_c$. The main spectral lobe has a null-to-null width equal to twice the baseband bandwidth ($2B$). Since for NRZ, $B = R_b = 1/T_b$, the total ASK transmission bandwidth becomes $B_T = 2R_b$.

**Inference:** ASK requires **twice** the baseband bandwidth, making it bandwidth-inefficient compared to BPSK. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – July 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the generation and detection (coherent) of a Binary Amplitude Shift Keying (BASK) signal. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BASK system transmits data at a bit rate of 1 Mbps over an AWGN channel. The received signal has an amplitude $A_c = 1$ mV, and the noise PSD is $N_0/2 = 10^{-9}$ W/Hz. Calculate:
   1. The transmission bandwidth.
   2. The bit energy $E_b$.
   3. The probability of error using coherent detection. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Generation (Modulation):**
A BASK signal is generated by multiplying the binary NRZ data $m(t)$ with the carrier $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$ using a balanced modulator (e.g., MC1496). For bit `1`, the modulator output is a full-amplitude carrier; for bit `0`, the output is zero. A bandpass filter removes higher harmonics.

**Detection (Coherent Demodulation):**
The received signal $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ is multiplied by a locally generated carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ that is **phase-synchronized** with the transmitter carrier. The product is:

$$r(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t) = A_c m(t) \cos^2(2\pi f_c t) + n(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

Using the identity $\cos^2(x) = [1 + \cos(2x)]/2$:

$$= \dfrac{A_c m(t)}{2} + \dfrac{A_c m(t)}{2}\cos(4\pi f_c t) + n(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

A **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** removes the high-frequency $2f_c$ term, leaving the baseband signal $A_c m(t)/2$ plus low-frequency noise. A **sampler** captures the LPF output at $t = T_b/2$, and a **threshold comparator** (threshold = $A_c/4$) decides the bit value.

**Block Diagram (Coherent Receiver):**

```
r(t) → [×] → [LPF] → [Sampler] → [Comparator] → Output bits
        ↑
   Local cos(2πf_c t)
```

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $R_b = 10^6$ bps, $A_c = 10^{-3}$ V, $N_0/2 = 10^{-9}$ W/Hz.

**Step 1: Transmission Bandwidth**
$$B_T = 2R_b = 2 \times 10^6 = 2 \text{ MHz}$$

**Step 2: Bit Energy**
$$T_b = \dfrac{1}{R_b} = 10^{-6} \text{ s}$$
$$E_b = \dfrac{A_c^2 T_b}{2} = \dfrac{(10^{-3})^2 \times 10^{-6}}{2} = \dfrac{10^{-12}}{2} = 5 \times 10^{-13} \text{ J}$$

**Step 3: Noise Spectral Density**
$$N_0 = 2 \times 10^{-9} \text{ W/Hz}$$

**Step 4: Compute $E_b/N_0$**
$$\dfrac{E_b}{N_0} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-13}}{2 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5: Probability of Error (Coherent)**
Using the formula:

$$P_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{2N_0}}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{2}}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{1.25 \times 10^{-4}}\right)$$

$$\sqrt{1.25 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 0.01118$$

$$P_e = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(0.01118) \approx \dfrac{1}{2}(1 - 0.0126) \approx 0.494$$

**Conclusion:** The error probability is very high (~49%) because the noise power is large compared to signal energy. A practical system would require either **higher signal amplitude** or a **lower noise environment**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Compare coherent and non-coherent detection of ASK. Discuss the trade-off involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the transmission bandwidth of a BASK signal and explain why ASK is considered bandwidth-inefficient. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Parameter | Coherent Detection | Non-Coherent Detection |
|---|---|---|
| **Local Carrier Required** | Yes, phase-synchronized | No, uses envelope detector |
| **Complexity** | High (needs PLL/Costas loop) | Low (diode + RC circuit) |
| **BER Performance** | Better: $P_e = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\left(\sqrt{E_b/2N_0}\right)$ | Worse: $P_e = \frac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/2N_0}$ |
| **Cost** | Higher | Lower |
| **Synchronization** | Critical (phase + frequency) | Not required |
| **Application** | High-performance digital links | Simple telemetry, IR remote controls |

**Trade-off:** Coherent detection offers superior noise immunity (~3 dB gain at low BER) at the cost of complex receiver circuitry. Non-coherent detection sacrifices ~3 dB of SNR efficiency in exchange for simpler, cheaper hardware. The choice depends on whether the system is **cost-sensitive** (use non-coherent) or **performance-critical** (use coherent).

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Derivation of Bandwidth:**

The baseband NRZ data signal $m(t)$ has a sinc-squared power spectral density with first null at $f = \pm 1/T_b = \pm R_b$.

After multiplication with the carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$, the spectrum is shifted to $\pm f_c$:

$$S(f) = \dfrac{1}{2}\left[M(f - f_c) + M(f + f_c)\right]$$

The positive-frequency main lobe extends from $f_c - R_b$ to $f_c + R_b$. The total null-to-null transmission bandwidth is:

$$B_T = (f_c + R_b) - (f_c - R_b) = 2R_b$$

**Why ASK is Bandwidth-Inefficient:**

1. **Spectral efficiency** is only $\eta = R_b/B_T = 0.5$ bits/sec/Hz.
2. The **two sidebands (USB and LSB)** carry the same information — one sideband is redundant.
3. For the same bit rate, ASK requires **twice** the bandwidth of baseband transmission.
4. Advanced schemes like **SSB-ASK** or **vestigial sideband** could halve this, but they are rarely implemented.
5. Modern systems prefer **BPSK or QPSK**, which achieve the same bit rate with **half** the bandwidth of ASK.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the threshold voltage:** Many students write the decision threshold as $A_c$ instead of $A_c/2$. The threshold must be the **midpoint** between the two signal levels (0 and $A_c$).
>
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in bandwidth:** Writing $B_T = R_b$ instead of $B_T = 2R_b$ is a very common error. Always derive it from the spectrum shifting theorem.
>
> 3. **Wrong BER formula:** The Q-function and erfc arguments differ between **coherent** and **non-coherent** ASK. Mixing them up will cost 2–3 marks instantly. Memorize both forms.
>
> 4. **Not defining $N_0$ vs $N_0/2$:** Some texts use $N_0/2$ as the two-sided PSD, while others use $N_0$ as the one-sided. State the convention at the start of your answer.
>
> 5. **Confusing ASK with AM:** ASK is a **digital** modulation; the modulating signal is binary, not analog. Examiners specifically check this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for ASK — Must Memorize Before Exam:**

- **Full Form:** Amplitude Shift Keying (ASK), also called On-Off Keying (OOK).
- **Core Idea:** Carrier amplitude switches between $A_c$ (for `1`) and $0$ (for `0`).
- **Signal Equation:** $s(t) = A_c \cdot m(t) \cdot \cos(2\pi f_c t)$.
- **Bandwidth Formula:** $B_T = 2R_b$ (twice the bit rate).
- **Spectral Efficiency:** $\eta = 0.5$ bits/sec/Hz (very low).
- **Coherent BER:** $P_e = \frac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/2N_0}\right)$.
- **Non-Coherent BER:** $P_e = \frac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/2N_0}$.
- **Detection Methods:** Coherent (multiplier + LPF) and Non-Coherent (envelope detector).
- **Coherent is 3 dB better** than non-coherent at low BER.
- **Threshold Voltage:** $V_{\text{th}} = A_c/2$ for equiprobable bits.
- **Baud Rate = Bit Rate** for binary ASK.
- **Disadvantages:** Bandwidth-inefficient, sensitive to noise, susceptible to amplitude fading.
- **Advantages:** Simplest modulator/demodulator, low-cost hardware, used in IR remotes, RFID, OOK telemetry.
- **Variants:** BASK (binary), MASK (M-ary), Coherent vs Non-Coherent, SSB-ASK.
- **Comparison to remember:** ASK is to AM what PSK is to PM — both are digital modulations of an analog process.
- **Practical Uses:** TV remote controls (38 kHz IR carrier), RFID backscatter, amateur CW Morse keying, Bell 202 modem (1200 bps).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency Shift Keying (FSK)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Frequency Shift Keying (FSK) # Module 2: Frequency Shift Keying (FSK) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Frequency Shift Keying (FSK)** is a digital modulation scheme in which the **frequency** of a sinusoidal carrier signal is discretely varied between two (or more) predefined frequencies in accordance with the binary (or M-ary) information stream, while the **amplitude** and **phase** of the carrier are kept constant. In its most common form, **Binary Frequency Shift Keying (BFSK)** assigns: - A higher frequency $f_1$ (called the **mark frequency**) to represent binary digit **'1'**. - A lower frequency $f_0$ (called the **space frequency**) to represent binary digit **'0'**. The general mathematical representation of a BFSK signal is: $$ s(t) = \begin{cases} A_c \cos(2\pi f_1 t + \phi_1), & \text{for binary '1'} \\ A_c \cos(2\pi f_0 t + \phi_0), & \text{for binary '0'} \end{cases} $$ where $A_c$ is the carrier amplitude, $f_1$ and $f_0$ are the two distinct carrier frequencies, and $\phi_1, \phi_0$ are the initial phase angles. > [!NOTE] > **Standard KTU 2024 Terminology Note:** > - **Coherent FSK (CFSK):** Phase continuity is maintained between symbols; usually produced by a single oscillator whose frequency is switched. > - **Non-Coherent FSK (NCFSK):** Phase is allowed to change arbitrarily between symbols; usually produced by switching between two independent oscillators. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Two-Tone Whistle" Imagine you are standing at a railway station and need to signal two distinct messages to a friend across the platform: - A **high-pitched whistle** → means **"COME HERE"** (binary 1). - A **low-pitched whistle** → means **"STAY THERE"** (binary 0). The **loudness (amplitude)** of your whistle does not change, and you don't care about the exact moment you start blowing (phase). Your friend is trained to listen only for the **pitch (frequency)**, not the loudness or the precise starting instant. This is exactly how **FSK** works — the receiver ignores amplitude and phase noise and listens only for shifts in frequency. > [!TIP] > **Why use FSK?** FSK is highly **resilient to amplitude noise and fading**, which makes it ideal for low-cost radio links, RFID tags, pagers, caller-ID systems, and early modem standards (like Bell 202 operating at 1200 bps). ### 1.3 Important Physical & Standard Metrics The following constants and metrics must be remembered: - **Nominal carrier frequency** $f_c$ is the arithmetic mean: $f_c = \dfrac{f_1 + f_0}{2}$. - **Frequency deviation** $\Delta f = \dfrac{\vert f_1 - f_0 \vert}{2}$. - **Minimum frequency spacing for orthogonal signaling** is $\vert f_1 - f_0 \vert = \dfrac{R_b}{2}$ (where $R_b$ is the bit rate). - **Carson's Rule bandwidth** is the standard metric used by KTU examiners for FSK bandwidth estimation. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic — How FSK Works (Structured Steps) 1. **Input binary stream** enters the modulator. The serial bits are buffered in pairs or single bits depending on whether BFSK or MFSK is used. 2. **Frequency selector logic** routes the data to a **Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO)** or a **Direct Digital Synthesizer (DDS)** whose output frequency is determined by the input bit voltage. 3. **Carrier generation:** The oscillator outputs a sinusoid whose instantaneous frequency equals $f_1$ when bit = 1 and $f_0$ when bit = 0. 4. **Channel transmission:** The signal travels through an AWGN (Additive White Gaussian Noise) channel, which primarily perturbs amplitude and phase. 5. **Receiver front-end:** A bandpass filter (BPF) centered around $f_1$ and another BPF centered around $f_0$ isolate the two possible tone energies. 6. **Energy detection / coherent correlation:** Envelope detectors (for non-coherent) or matched filters (for coherent) measure the energy in each frequency bin over one bit duration. 7. **Decision device:** The bit corresponding to the frequency with the **larger received energy** is declared as the transmitted bit. > [!NOTE] > **Why 'Coherent vs Non-Coherent' matters in KTU problems:** > Coherent FSK requires a **local carrier reference** at the receiver (synchronization), giving a probability of error $P_e = Q\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$. Non-coherent FSK does not need phase reference, giving $P_e = \dfrac{1}{2} \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_b}{2N_0}\right)$, which is slightly worse but simpler and cheaper. --- ## 2.2 Types of FSK — A Structured View | Type | Symbol Mapping | Demodulator | Relative BER | Typical Use | |------|----------------|-------------|---------------|-------------| | **BFSK (Coherent)** | 1→f₁, 0→f₀ | Matched filter / correlator | Lower | High-fidelity radio | | **BFSK (Non-Coherent)** | 1→f₁, 0→f₀ | Envelope detector | Slightly higher | Pagers, RFID | | **MFSK (M-ary)** | log₂M bits → one of M frequencies | Bank of M matched filters | Much lower (for M>2) | HF radio, modems | | **MSK (Minimum Shift Keying)** | Continuous phase FSK with h=0.5 | Coherent | Optimal BFSK | GSM, CDPD | | **GMSK** | Gaussian-filtered MSK | Coherent | Excellent spectral efficiency | GSM cellular | --- ## 2.3 The Modulation Index — The Most Tested KTU Parameter The **modulation index** $h$ (also called the **deviation ratio**) of an FSK signal is defined as: $$ h = \frac{\vert f_1 - f_0 \vert}{R_b} = \frac{2 \Delta f}{R_b} $$ - $R_b$ = bit rate (bits per second). - $\Delta f$ = peak frequency deviation from the carrier. **KTU Critical Threshold Values:** | Condition | Modulation Index | Spectral Property | KTU Exam Hint | |-----------|------------------|-------------------|----------------| | $h = 0.5$ | **Minimum Shift Keying (MSK)** | Most compact spectrum, continuous phase | "Compute min. bandwidth" | | $h = 1.0$ | **Wideband FSK** | Two clear spectral lobes | Common in textbook problems | | $h \gg 1$ | **Coarse FSK** | Almost orthogonal tones | Used in simple radio control | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam Lifesaver) | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $s(t) = A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t \pm \pi h (t)]$ | Instantaneous phase of FSK | Volts | | 2 | $\Delta f = \dfrac{\vert f_1 - f_0 \vert}{2}$ | Peak frequency deviation | Hz | | 3 | $h = \dfrac{\vert f_1 - f_0 \vert}{R_b}$ | Modulation (deviation) index | Dimensionless | | 4 | $BW_{\min} = \vert f_1 - f_0 \vert + R_b$ | Minimum Nyquist bandwidth (orthogonal BFSK) | Hz | | 5 | $BW = 2(\Delta f + R_b) = 2\Delta f + 2R_b$ | Carson's Rule for FSK bandwidth | Hz | | 6 | $BW = 2R_b + 2(hR_b) = R_b(2 + 2h)$ | Equivalent form of Carson's Rule | Hz | | 7 | $P_e(\text{CFSK}) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | BER for coherent BFSK | Dimensionless | | 8 | $P_e(\text{NCFSK}) = \dfrac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_b}{2N_0}\right)$ | BER for non-coherent BFSK | Dimensionless | | 9 | $P_e(\text{MFSK}) \approx \dfrac{M-1}{2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_b \log_2 M}{2N_0}\right)$ | Approx. BER for non-coherent MFSK | Dimensionless | | 10 | $\vert f_1 - f_0 \vert = \dfrac{n R_b}{2},\; n=1,2,3,\ldots$ | Orthogonality condition (minimum separation) | Hz | | 11 | $f_c = \dfrac{f_1 + f_0}{2}$ | Nominal carrier (center) frequency | Hz | | 12 | $S_{FSK}(f) \propto \dfrac{1}{4}\!\left[\delta(f-f_1) + \delta(f+f_1) + \delta(f-f_0) + \delta(f+f_0)\right]$ | Ideal PSD (impulse-train for random data) | W/Hz | > [!WARNING] > **Notation Trap for KTU Valuators:** The vertical pipe `\|` is used here for absolute value (e.g., $\vert f_1 - f_0 \vert$) only inside the math block — never inside a markdown table cell directly, to avoid breaking the table renderer. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Caller ID (Bell 202 modem):** 1200 bps FSK between 1200 Hz and 2200 Hz. - **AM Broadcast Subcarriers:** 57 kHz RBDS uses FSK for digital radio. - **Paging Systems (POCSAG / FLEX):** Use 4-level FSK at 512/1200/2400 bps. - **RF Remote Controls (Garage doors, toys):** Simple low-cost NCFSK. - **Deep-Space Telemetry:** Coherent FSK with very narrow tone spacing for power efficiency. - **FSK over optical fiber:** Coherent optical FSK for high-capacity DWDM links. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mathematical Model of BFSK Signal **Step 1 — Start with the binary information signal.** Let the binary bit stream be $b(t) \in \{+1, -1\}$ with bit duration $T_b = 1/R_b$. **Step 2 — Define the two carrier frequencies.** The mark and space frequencies are chosen symmetrically about the carrier $f_c$: $$ f_1 = f_c + \Delta f \quad \text{and} \quad f_0 = f_c - \Delta f $$ **Step 3 — Express the BFSK as a frequency-modulated sinusoid.** A unified mathematical expression using a single continuous-phase sinusoid is: $$ s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[ 2\pi f_c t + 2\pi \Delta f \int_{0}^{t} b(\tau)\,d\tau + \phi_0 \right] $$ **Step 4 — Verify that the instantaneous frequency matches.** The instantaneous angular frequency is the time derivative of the phase: $$ \omega_i(t) = \frac{d}{dt}\!\left[ 2\pi f_c t + 2\pi \Delta f \int_{0}^{t} b(\tau)\,d\tau + \phi_0 \right] $$ $$ \omega_i(t) = 2\pi f_c + 2\pi \Delta f \cdot b(t) $$ **Step 5 — Convert angular frequency to ordinary frequency.** $$ f_i(t) = f_c + \Delta f \cdot b(t) $$ $$ f_i(t) = \begin{cases} f_c + \Delta f = f_1, & \text{if } b(t) = +1 \\ f_c - \Delta f = f_0, & \text{if } b(t) = -1 \end{cases} $$ This confirms the unified FSK equation generates exactly two tones, one for each bit value. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Transmission Bandwidth (Carson's Rule) **Step 1 — Identify the highest frequency component.** The highest frequency present in the FSK spectrum occurs during a long run of 1s, generating $f_1$. Similarly, the lowest is $f_0$. **Step 2 — Carson's Rule definition.** Carson's Rule states that approximately **98% of the total power** of an FM signal is contained within a bandwidth: $$ BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) $$ where $f_m$ is the highest modulating frequency. For a binary NRZ signal of bit rate $R_b$, the fundamental frequency of the modulating baseband is $R_b / 2$. However, treating the digital bit as a square wave with sharp transitions, the practical highest baseband frequency is $R_b$. **Step 3 — Substitute into Carson's Rule.** $$ BW = 2(\Delta f + R_b) $$ **Step 4 — Express in terms of modulation index $h = 2\Delta f / R_b$ → $\Delta f = h R_b / 2$.** $$ BW = 2\!\left( \frac{h R_b}{2} + R_b \right) = 2 R_b \left( \frac{h}{2} + 1 \right) = R_b (2 + h) $$ Wait — let me recheck the coefficient convention (this is a common KTU trap): $$ BW = 2(\Delta f + R_b) = 2\Delta f + 2R_b = hR_b + 2R_b = R_b (h + 2) $$ **Final result:** $$ \boxed{\, BW_{FSK} = R_b\,(h + 2) = 2(\Delta f + R_b) \,} $$ **Step 5 — Special case: MSK where $h = 0.5$.** $$ BW_{MSK} = R_b (0.5 + 2) = 2.5\, R_b $$ This matches the well-known result that MSK has a main-lobe bandwidth of $1.5 R_b$ and total bandwidth $2.5 R_b$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Probability of Error for Coherent BFSK **Step 1 — Define the signal space.** Two equiprobable signals $s_1(t)$ and $s_0(t)$ of energy $E_b$ are transmitted over an AWGN channel with two-sided PSD $N_0 / 2$. **Step 2 — Orthogonality condition.** The signals are orthogonal if $\int_0^{T_b} s_1(t) s_0(t)\,dt = 0$. This holds exactly when $\vert f_1 - f_0 \vert = n R_b / 2$ for integer $n$. **Step 3 — The matched filter outputs.** The decision variable after correlation is a Gaussian random variable: $$ r = \int_0^{T_b} r(t) s_1(t)\,dt - \int_0^{T_b} r(t) s_0(t)\,dt $$ **Step 4 — Mean and variance of $r$.** - If $s_1$ is sent: mean of $r$ is $E_b$, variance is $N_0 E_b$. - If $s_0$ is sent: mean of $r$ is $-E_b$, variance is $N_0 E_b$. **Step 5 — Compute probability of error.** $$ P_e = P(r < 0 \mid s_1 \text{ sent}) = Q\!\left( \frac{E_b}{\sqrt{N_0 E_b}} \right) = Q\!\left( \sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}} \right) $$ **Final result:** $$ \boxed{\, P_e(\text{CFSK}) = Q\!\left( \sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}} \right) \approx \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi (E_b/N_0)}} e^{-E_b/2N_0} \,} $$ This is **3 dB worse than BPSK** at high SNR. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Probability of Error for Non-Coherent BFSK For non-coherent detection (envelope detector), there is no phase reference. The decision variable is the **difference in envelope detector outputs**. Following the standard derivation (referenced in any digital comms text): **Step 1 — Envelope outputs $r_1$ and $r_0$ follow Rician and Rayleigh distributions** when $s_1$ is sent, respectively. **Step 2 — Decision rule:** Declare "1" if $r_1 > r_0$, else "0". **Step 3 — Integrating the joint density** (steps omitted in shorthand, but standard textbook result): $$ P_e = \frac{1}{2} \int_0^\infty r_1 \, e^{-(r_1^2 + 2E_b/N_0)/2} I_0(r_1 \sqrt{2E_b/N_0}) \, e^{-r_1^2/2}\, dr_1 $$ **Step 4 — Evaluating the integral** (using Marcum Q-function identity): $$ \boxed{\, P_e(\text{NCFSK}) = \frac{1}{2} \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_b}{2 N_0} \right) \,} $$ This is about **1 dB worse than coherent FSK** at $P_e = 10^{-4}$, but is dramatically simpler to implement. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Quick-Reference for KTU) | Metric | BPSK | Coherent BFSK | Non-Coherent BFSK | MFSK (M=4) | |--------|------|---------------|-------------------|------------| | BER expression | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | $Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | $\dfrac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/2N_0}$ | $\dfrac{3}{2}e^{-E_b/N_0}$ | | Bandwidth efficiency | **Highest** (1 bps/Hz) | Low (0.5 bps/Hz) | Low (0.5 bps/Hz) | Very low (0.25 bps/Hz) | | Power efficiency | **Best** | Good | Moderate | Best for high M | | Phase coherence required | Yes | Yes | **No** | No | | Complexity | Moderate | Moderate | **Lowest** | High | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — FSK Modulator and Demodulator ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Binary Data
b(t)] --> B[Bit Clock
Timing] B --> C{FSK Modulator} D[Oscillator Bank
f1 and f0] --> C C --> E[Channel
AWGN plus h] E --> F[Bandpass Filter 1
f1] E --> G[Bandpass Filter 2
f0] F --> H[Envelope Detector 1
or Matched Filter] G --> I[Envelope Detector 2
or Matched Filter] H --> J[Comparator and
Decision Device] I --> J J --> K[Recovered Binary
Data r(t)] style A fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,stroke-width:1px style K fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#2a7,stroke-width:1px style C fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:1px style J fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#1f4e8c,stroke-width:1px ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **binary data** is fed into the **FSK Modulator** together with two reference tones ($f_1, f_0$). - The modulated signal is corrupted by **AWGN** in the channel. - At the receiver, **two parallel bandpass filters** separate the energy at $f_1$ and $f_0$. - The **comparator** decides which tone has higher energy and outputs the corresponding bit. --- ## 4.2 FSK Transmitter (Direct Carrier Switching Method) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Binary Input b t] --> B[Switch Logic] B -->|bit equals 1| C[Oscillator 1
f1] B -->|bit equals 0| D[Oscillator 2
f0] C --> E[Summing Junction and
Amplifier] D --> E E --> F[Output s t
FSK Signal] style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0 style F fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32 style E fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100 ``` This **non-coherent FSK generator** uses two independent oscillators. The output has **discontinuous phase** at bit transitions, which broadens the spectrum. --- ## 4.3 FSK Receiver — Non-Coherent (Envelope Detection) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Received FSK
plus noise] --> B[BPF at f1] A --> C[BPF at f0] B --> D[Envelope Detector 1] C --> E[Envelope Detector 2] D --> F[Sample and Hold 1] E --> G[Sample and Hold 2] F --> H[Comparator] G --> H H -->|output 1 if upper larger| I[Decision Output 1] H -->|output 0 if lower larger| J[Decision Output 0] style A fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style I fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132 style J fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132 ``` --- ## 4.4 FSK Receiver — Coherent (Correlation / Matched Filter) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Received FSK
plus noise] --> B[Multiplier 1
x cos 2 pi f1 t] A --> C[Multiplier 2
x cos 2 pi f0 t] B --> D[Integrator 1
over Tb] C --> E[Integrator 2
over Tb] D --> F[Sampler 1 at t = Tb] E --> G[Sampler 2 at t = Tb] F --> H[Comparator] G --> H H --> I[Bit Decision Output] style A fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24 style I fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132 style H fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404 ``` **Note:** This coherent receiver needs a **local oscillator** that is **phase-synchronized** with the transmitter — a major implementation challenge justifying the use of MSK/GMSK in practice. --- ## 4.5 Conceptual FSK Time-Domain Waveform ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph BIN["Binary Input"] A1[0] --> A2[1] --> A3[0] --> A4[1] --> A5[0] end subgraph FSK["FSK Output s t"] B1[Low freq
f0 tone] --> B2[High freq
f1 tone] --> B3[Low freq
f0 tone] --> B4[High freq
f1 tone] --> B5[Low freq
f0 tone] end A1 -.-> B1 A2 -.-> B2 A3 -.-> B3 A4 -.-> B4 A5 -.-> B5 style BIN fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33 style FSK fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#1f4e8c ``` This **state-transition diagram** shows that as the input bit changes, the **output frequency toggles** between the two pre-defined tones — the defining behavior of FSK. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### Question 1 — Definition Type `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Q: Define Frequency Shift Keying (FSK). With a neat block diagram, explain the generation of BFSK signal using the frequency modulation method.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark) Definition:** FSK is a digital modulation technique in which the frequency of the carrier signal is varied in accordance with the digital data signal, while the amplitude and phase remain constant. - **(1 mark) Mathematical form:** For BFSK, the transmitted signal is $s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_1 t)$ for bit '1' and $s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ for bit '0'. - **(1 mark) Block diagram explanation:** A binary data stream is applied to a **Voltage-Controlled Oscillator (VCO)**. The VCO output frequency is switched between $f_1$ and $f_0$ based on whether the input is '1' or '0'. The two frequencies are derived from a single carrier $f_c$ by frequency deviation $\pm \Delta f$. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "FSK is modulation in which frequency changes" without mentioning the constant amplitude and phase. KTU examiners will deduct **1 mark** for this omission. Always state all three parameters: *amplitude constant, phase constant, frequency varied.* --- ### Question 2 — Comparison Type `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Q: Compare coherent and non-coherent detection of BFSK in terms of probability of error and complexity.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark) Coherent BFSK:** Uses a local carrier reference synchronized to the received signal. Probability of error: $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)$. - **(1 mark) Non-Coherent BFSK:** Uses envelope detectors, no phase reference needed. Probability of error: $P_e = \dfrac{1}{2} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_b}{2N_0}\right)$. - **(1 mark) Comparison:** Coherent FSK has better BER performance (~1 dB gain at $10^{-4}$), but is more complex and expensive. Non-coherent FSK is simpler, cheaper, and is preferred in low-cost applications like RFID and paging. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Module 2]` **Q: (a)** Explain the generation and detection of Binary Frequency Shift Keying (BFSK) with neat block diagrams. Discuss coherent and non-coherent detection methods. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A BFSK system uses mark frequency $f_1 = 1200$ Hz and space frequency $f_0 = 800$ Hz. The bit rate is $R_b = 400$ bits/sec. Compute: (i) Modulation index $h$ (ii) Minimum transmission bandwidth (Carson's rule) (iii) Peak frequency deviation $\Delta f$ (iv) Is the signal orthogonal? Justify. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:** **Generation (3 marks):** - The binary NRZ data stream is applied to a **frequency selector** (switch or logic circuit). - The selector routes a **VCO** to oscillate at $f_1$ when input is '1' and at $f_0$ when input is '0'. - The two frequencies are produced from a single reference $f_c$ with deviation $\Delta f = \vert f_1 - f_0 \vert / 2$. - A **bandpass filter** removes harmonics from the VCO output, producing a clean sinusoidal FSK signal. **Coherent Detection (2 marks):** - The received BFSK is multiplied by locally generated carriers $\cos(2\pi f_1 t)$ and $\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$. - Each product is **integrated over one bit period** $T_b$. - The two integrator outputs are sampled and compared. - A **synchronization circuit** (PLL or Costas loop) maintains phase coherence. **Non-Coherent Detection (2 marks):** - The received signal is filtered by two BPFs centered at $f_1$ and $f_0$. - Each filter output is passed through an **envelope detector** (diode + RC). - The two envelopes are sampled and compared in the decision device. - **No phase reference is needed**, making the receiver simpler and cheaper. --- **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** $f_1 = 1200$ Hz, $f_0 = 800$ Hz, $R_b = 400$ bps. **(i) Modulation index (2 marks):** $$ h = \frac{\vert f_1 - f_0 \vert}{R_b} = \frac{\vert 1200 - 800 \vert}{400} = \frac{400}{400} = 1.0 $$ **(ii) Minimum bandwidth using Carson's Rule (2 marks):** $$ BW = 2(\Delta f + R_b) $$ Compute $\Delta f = \vert 1200 - 800 \vert / 2 = 200$ Hz. $$ BW = 2(200 + 400) = 2 \times 600 = 1200 \text{ Hz} $$ **(iii) Peak frequency deviation (1 mark):** $$ \boxed{\Delta f = \frac{\vert f_1 - f_0 \vert}{2} = \frac{400}{2} = 200 \text{ Hz}} $$ **(iv) Orthogonality check (2 marks):** For orthogonality: $$ \vert f_1 - f_0 \vert = \frac{n R_b}{2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots $$ $$ \frac{n \times 400}{2} = 200n $$ For $n = 1$: Required spacing = $200$ Hz. Actual spacing = $400$ Hz. Since $400 = 2 \times 200$ (i.e., $n = 2$), the signal is **orthogonal** with $n = 2$. $$ \boxed{\text{Yes, the BFSK signal is orthogonal.}} $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Module 2]` **Q: (a)** Derive the expression for the probability of error $P_e$ of a coherent BFSK system operating over an AWGN channel. Show all the steps. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An M-ary FSK system with $M = 8$ uses orthogonal signaling over an AWGN channel with $E_b / N_0 = 10$ dB. Compute the approximate bit error probability assuming non-coherent detection. Compare it with coherent BPSK at the same SNR. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Two equiprobable signals $s_1(t) = \sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_1 t)$ and $s_0(t) = \sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ of equal energy $E_b$ are transmitted over AWGN with PSD $N_0/2$. **Step 2 (1 mark):** At the receiver, two correlators with reference signals $\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_1 t)$ and $\phi_0(t) = \sqrt{2/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ are used. **Step 3 (1 mark):** The correlator outputs are: $r_1 = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt$ and $r_0 = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\phi_0(t)\,dt$, where $r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t)$. **Step 4 (1 mark):** Decision rule: choose $s_1$ if $r_1 > r_0$, else choose $s_0$. Define decision variable $D = r_1 - r_0$. **Step 5 (1 mark):** If $s_1$ is sent, $D$ is Gaussian with mean $E_b$ and variance $N_0 E_b$. If $s_0$ is sent, $D$ is Gaussian with mean $-E_b$ and the same variance. **Step 6 (1 mark):** $$ P_e = P(D < 0 \mid s_1) = Q\!\left( \frac{E_b}{\sqrt{N_0 E_b}} \right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right) $$ **Step 7 (1 mark):** $$ \boxed{P_e(\text{CFSK}) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\dfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mark-Loss Traps:** > 1. Forgetting the **square root** inside the Q-function — losing 1 mark. > 2. Writing variance as $N_0/2$ instead of $N_0 E_b$ — losing 1 mark. > 3. Failing to state the **orthogonality condition** $\int s_1 s_0\,dt = 0$ — losing 1 mark. --- **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:** **Given:** $M = 8$, $E_b/N_0 = 10$ dB = $10^{1} = 10$ (in linear). **Step 1 (1 mark):** Non-coherent MFSK approximation: $$ P_e \approx \frac{M-1}{2} \exp\!\left( -\frac{E_b \log_2 M}{2 N_0} \right) $$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute $\log_2 8 = 3$ bits/symbol. Then: $$ \frac{E_b \log_2 M}{2 N_0} = \frac{10 \times 3}{2} = 15 $$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Substitute into the formula: $$ P_e \approx \frac{8 - 1}{2} \exp(-15) = 3.5 \times 3.059 \times 10^{-7} $$ $$ P_e \approx 1.07 \times 10^{-6} $$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** For coherent BPSK at the same SNR: $$ P_e(\text{BPSK}) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2 E_b}{N_0}}\right) = Q(\sqrt{20}) = Q(4.47) $$ Using Q-function table: $Q(4.47) \approx 3.9 \times 10^{-6}$. **Step 5 (1 mark):** Comparison: $$ \frac{P_e(\text{BPSK})}{P_e(\text{MFSK})} = \frac{3.9 \times 10^{-6}}{1.07 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 3.6 $$ **Step 6 (2 marks):** Conclusion: **Non-coherent MFSK (M=8) outperforms coherent BPSK by a factor of ~3.6 at $E_b/N_0 = 10$ dB.** This demonstrates the power-efficiency advantage of M-ary orthogonal signaling despite its larger bandwidth. $$ \boxed{P_e(\text{NCFSK, M=8}) \approx 1.07 \times 10^{-6} < P_e(\text{BPSK}) \approx 3.9 \times 10^{-6}} $$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Universal Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Reasons KTU Students Lose Marks in FSK Questions:** > 1. **Confusing $\Delta f$ with $\vert f_1 - f_0 \vert$:** $\Delta f$ is the *half-spacing*; the frequency difference is the *full spacing*. Examiners WILL deduct 1 mark for this. > 2. **Mixing up Coherent and Non-Coherent BER formulas:** Memorize both — they are tested in the same module. > 3. **Forgetting units in Carson's Rule:** Always write "Hz" — many students lose 0.5 marks for missing units. > 4. **Wrong orthogonality condition:** It is $\vert f_1 - f_0 \vert = n R_b / 2$, not $n R_b$. > 5. **Not drawing the block diagram in the answer:** Even in numerical problems, a quick block diagram earns 1–2 extra marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **FSK = frequency of carrier changes with binary data; amplitude and phase stay constant.** - **BFSK** uses two frequencies $f_1$ (mark) and $f_0$ (space). - **MFSK** uses $M$ frequencies; each symbol carries $\log_2 M$ bits. - **Modulation index $h = \vert f_1 - f_0 \vert / R_b$** is the master parameter. - **MSK** is the special case $h = 0.5$ — most compact spectrum. - **Carson's Rule** $BW = 2(\Delta f + R_b) = R_b(h + 2)$ — memorize this formula. - **Orthogonality condition:** $\vert f_1 - f_0 \vert = n R_b / 2$ for integer $n$. - **Coherent BFSK BER:** $P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{E_b/N_0}\right)$. - **Non-Coherent BFSK BER:** $P_e = \frac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/2N_0)$. - **Coherent FSK** is ~3 dB better than NCFSK and ~3 dB worse than BPSK. - **MFSK** is more power-efficient but less bandwidth-efficient than BPSK. - **NCFSK** is used in low-cost systems (RFID, pagers, Caller ID). - **CFSK** is used in high-fidelity radio and microwave links. - **Bandwidth Efficiency:** BPSK (1) > BFSK (0.5) > QFSK (0.25). - **Power Efficiency:** QFSK > BPSK > BFSK > NCFSK (asymptotically at low $P_e$). - **Common KTU trap:** $\Delta f$ is **half** the frequency separation, not the full separation. - **Practical implementations:** Bell 202 (1200 bps caller ID), POCSAG paging, GSM's GMSK, RFID tags.

topics.title: Phase Shift Keying (PSK)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Phase Shift Keying (PSK)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Phase Shift Keying (PSK) - Module 2

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phase Shift Keying (PSK)** is a **digital modulation technique** in which the **phase of a constant-amplitude, constant-frequency sinusoidal carrier wave** is discretely varied (shifted) in accordance with the digital baseband (message) signal, while the **amplitude and frequency of the carrier remain strictly constant**.

Mathematically, a general PSK-modulated signal is represented as:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\left(2\pi f_c t + \phi_k(t)\right)$$

where:
- $A_c$ = carrier amplitude (held constant)
- $f_c$ = carrier frequency (held constant)
- $\phi_k(t)$ = discrete phase state corresponding to the $k^{th}$ symbol
- $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, M-1$ for an M-ary PSK system

The set of allowed discrete phase values is given by:

$$\phi_k = \frac{2\pi k}{M} \quad ; \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, M-1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** PSK is listed under "Digital Modulation Techniques" along with ASK and FSK. The KTU paper often asks for the *signal expression*, *constellation diagram*, *bandwidth*, and *probability of error* for BPSK and QPSK.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **clock with a single red hand** (the carrier wave). The hand spins continuously at a constant speed (constant frequency) and reaches a fixed length from the center (constant amplitude). Now, instead of letting the hand rotate freely, you grab the hand and **snap it to one of two positions** at every tick of the second:

- If the incoming bit is **'0'** → snap the hand to point to **3 o'clock** (phase = $0^\circ$).
- If the incoming bit is **'1'** → snap the hand to point to **9 o'clock** (phase = $180^\circ$).

The receiver simply looks at **where the hand is pointing** at each tick and decodes the bit accordingly. This is exactly how **Binary PSK (BPSK)** works — the hand is the carrier, and the "snapping" is the phase shift.

> [!NOTE]
> The **direction the clock hand points** = **Phase** (this carries the information).
> The **length of the hand** = **Amplitude** (kept constant, hence PSK is immune to amplitude noise).
> The **speed of rotation** = **Frequency** (kept constant).

---

### 1.3 Why PSK? — Engineering Motivation

| Property | Value / Implication |
|----------|---------------------|
| Constant envelope | Resistant to **non-linear amplification** (ideal for satellite/RF links) |
| Bandwidth efficiency | Higher than ASK for the same bit rate |
| Noise immunity | Better BER than ASK, comparable to FSK |
| Spectral containment | No discrete carrier spike; energy is distributed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU examinations, students must clearly differentiate that **PSK varies PHASE**, **ASK varies AMPLITUDE**, and **FSK varies FREQUENCY**, while the other two parameters remain constant.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Variants of PSK Covered in KTU Module 2

PSK is implemented in several forms, each differing in the **number of discrete phase states (M)** and the **method of reference** (absolute vs. differential). The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly covers:

1. **BPSK (Binary PSK)** — $M = 2$
2. **QPSK (Quadrature PSK)** — $M = 4$
3. **DPSK (Differential PSK)** — Non-coherent variant
4. **M-ary PSK** — General $M$-level PSK

---

## 2.2 Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK)

### 2.2.1 Operational Logic

In BPSK, the digital data stream $b(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ is mapped to **two antipodal phase states**:

- **Bit '0'** → Phase $\phi = 0$ radians
- **Bit '1'** → Phase $\phi = \pi$ radians

The transmitted signal for the $k^{th}$ bit interval is:

$$s_k(t) = \begin{cases} \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{for bit } 0 \ (\phi_0 = 0) \\[10pt] \sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t + \pi) & \text{for bit } 1 \ (\phi_1 = \pi) \end{cases}$$

Using the identity $\cos(\theta + \pi) = -\cos(\theta)$, this simplifies to:

$$s_k(t) = \begin{cases} +\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{bit } 0 \\ -\sqrt{\dfrac{2E_b}{T_b}} \cos(2\pi f_c t) & \text{bit } 1 \end{cases}$$

Equivalently, in compact form:

$$\boxed{s_k(t) = m(t)\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)}$$

where $m(t) = +1$ for bit 0 and $m(t) = -1$ for bit 1, i.e., $m(t) \in \{+1, -1\}$.

### 2.2.2 Key Engineering Properties of BPSK

- **Anti-podal signaling**: The two signals are exact negatives of each other, giving the **maximum Euclidean distance** in the signal space.
- **Bandwidth**: Minimum Nyquist bandwidth = $f_b / 2$ if baseband filtering is ideal; in practice with raised-cosine filtering, the null-to-null bandwidth is $2/T_b = 2R_b$.
- **Bit Error Probability** (under AWGN, coherent detection):

$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

- **Spectral Efficiency**: $\eta = 0.5 \text{ bits/Hz}$ (since $R_b$ bits/s require $\approx R_b$ Hz of bandwidth, giving $\approx 0.5$ bits/Hz after accounting for the null-to-null bandwidth of $2R_b$).

---

## 2.3 Quadrature Phase Shift Keying (QPSK)

### 2.3.1 Operational Logic

QPSK uses **four phase states** separated by $90^\circ$ ($\pi/2$ rad). The input bit stream is **split into two parallel bit streams** (in-phase "I" and quadrature-phase "Q"), each of half the original bit rate. Each pair of bits (a **dibit**) selects one of the four phases.

Mapping (Gray coding for minimum BER):

| Dibit $(b_1 b_0)$ | Phase $\phi_k$ |
|:-----------------:|:--------------:|
| 00                | $\pi/4$        |
| 01                | $3\pi/4$       |
| 11                | $5\pi/4$       |
| 10                | $7\pi/4$       |

### 2.3.2 Signal Representation

$$s_k(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \frac{(2k+1)\pi}{4}\right), \quad k = 0,1,2,3$$

Using the cosine-sum identity, this is expanded as:

$$s_k(t) = \underbrace{\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\cos\phi_k}_{I\text{-component}}\cos(2\pi f_c t) - \underbrace{\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\sin\phi_k}_{Q\text{-component}}\sin(2\pi f_c t)$$

The relationship to BPSK: **QPSK is essentially two BPSK signals transmitted in parallel on orthogonal carriers (cosine and sine)**, each carrying one bit of the dibit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The symbol duration $T_s = 2T_b$ because each symbol carries **2 bits**. Since the symbol rate is halved, the **bandwidth is halved** compared to BPSK for the same bit rate.

### 2.3.3 Key Properties of QPSK

- **Bandwidth**: $B = R_b/2$ (half of BPSK for same $R_b$)
- **Spectral Efficiency**: $2 \text{ bits/Hz}$ (double BPSK)
- **Bit Error Rate** (coherent, AWGN):

$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Surprising result:** The BER of QPSK is **identical** to BPSK at the same $E_b/N_0$, but QPSK transmits **twice the data rate in half the bandwidth**. This is precisely why QPSK is used in satellite, Wi-Fi (older 802.11), and 3G systems.

---

## 2.4 Differential Phase Shift Keying (DPSK)

### 2.4.1 Operational Logic

In DPSK, **no reference carrier is required** at the receiver. Information is encoded in the **phase difference between successive symbols** rather than in the absolute phase.

Encoding rule:
- **Bit '0'** → **No phase change** ($\Delta\phi = 0$)
- **Bit '1'** → **Phase change of $\pi$** ($\Delta\phi = \pi$)

Mathematically:

$$s_k(t) = s_{k-1}(t) \cdot d_k$$

where $d_k = +1$ for bit 0 and $d_k = -1$ for bit 1.

### 2.4.2 Key Properties

- **No carrier recovery circuit needed** → simpler, cheaper receiver
- **Performance penalty**: $\approx 1$ dB worse than coherent BPSK at the same BER
- **Bit Error Rate**:

$$P_{e,\text{DPSK}} = \frac{1}{2}\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_b}{N_0}\right)$$

- Robust against **phase jitter** and **random phase rotations** in the channel.

---

## 2.5 M-ary PSK (General Case)

For general M-ary PSK, the signal set is:

$$s_k(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \frac{2\pi k}{M}\right), \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, M-1$$

Each symbol carries $\log_2 M$ bits, with symbol energy $E_s = E_b \log_2 M$.

The probability of symbol error is upper-bounded by:

$$P_s \leq 2\,Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{N_0}}\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{M}\right)\right)$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** As $M$ increases, the **phase separation** $\Delta\phi = 2\pi/M$ **decreases**, so the signal points in the constellation move closer together, making the system more vulnerable to noise. **Higher M = higher spectral efficiency but worse BER** (or higher power required for same BER).

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Parameter | BPSK | QPSK | DPSK | M-ary PSK |
|---|-----------|------|------|------|-----------|
| 1 | Number of phase levels $M$ | 2 | 4 | 2 (diff) | $M$ |
| 2 | Bits per symbol | 1 | 2 | 1 | $\log_2 M$ |
| 3 | Phase separation | $\pi$ | $\pi/2$ | $\pi$ (rel.) | $2\pi/M$ |
| 4 | Symbol duration | $T_b$ | $2T_b$ | $T_b$ | $T_b\log_2 M$ |
| 5 | Bandwidth (null-to-null) | $2/T_b$ | $1/T_b$ | $2/T_b$ | $2/(T_b\log_2 M)$ |
| 6 | Spectral efficiency | $0.5$ bps/Hz | $1$ bps/Hz | $0.5$ bps/Hz | $\log_2 M / 2$ bps/Hz |
| 7 | $E_b/N_0$ for $P_e = 10^{-5}$ | $9.6$ dB | $9.6$ dB | $\approx 10.3$ dB | Increases with M |
| 8 | BER (coherent, AWGN) | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | $\tfrac{1}{2}e^{-E_b/N_0}$ | see bound above |
| 9 | Detection type | Coherent | Coherent | Non-coherent | Coherent |

> [!NOTE]
> **$Q(x)$ function:** $Q(x) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}\int_x^{\infty}e^{-u^2/2}\,du$ — the tail probability of the standard normal distribution. It is closely related to the complementary error function: $Q(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(x/\sqrt{2})$.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

PSK and its derivatives are ubiquitous in modern communications:

- **BPSK**: GPS L1 civilian signal, deep-space telemetry (Voyager, Cassini), RFID.
- **QPSK**: DVB-S (satellite TV), 3G WCDMA, older Wi-Fi (802.11b/g), LTE control channels.
- **DQPSK / $\pi/4$-DQPSK**: Bluetooth, cellular (IS-136, PHS).
- **8-PSK**: EDGE (2.5G cellular), satellite modems.
- **Higher-order PSK (16, 64-PSK)**: Modems (V.29, V.32), DVB-S2 (combined with QAM and LDPC).

The choice of M is driven by the **Shannon capacity trade-off** — more bits per symbol at the cost of more required SNR and tighter linearity requirements on the power amplifier.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: BPSK Signal and its Signal-Space Representation

### Step 1: Start from the general PSK expression

A general PSK signal is defined as:

$$s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi_k)$$

where $\phi_k$ is one of the allowed discrete phases. For BPSK, there are two possibilities: $\phi_0 = 0$ and $\phi_1 = \pi$.

### Step 2: Write the two BPSK waveforms explicitly

$$s_0(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + 0) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

$$s_1(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t + \pi) = -A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

### Step 3: Normalize the energy per bit

We choose $A_c$ so that the energy of $s_0(t)$ over one bit interval $[0, T_b]$ equals $E_b$:

$$E_b = \int_0^{T_b} s_0^2(t)\,dt = \int_0^{T_b} A_c^2 \cos^2(2\pi f_c t)\,dt$$

Assuming $f_c \gg 1/T_b$ (carrier frequency much larger than bit rate), the average of $\cos^2$ over many cycles is $\tfrac{1}{2}$:

$$E_b \approx A_c^2 \cdot \frac{T_b}{2}$$

Solving for $A_c$:

$$A_c = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}$$

### Step 4: Substitute the normalized amplitude

$$s_0(t) = +\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

$$s_1(t) = -\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

### Step 5: Define the basis function

Choose the orthonormal basis:

$$\phi_1(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t), \quad 0 \le t \le T_b$$

The signal-space coordinates (using the orthonormal inner product $s_{i1} = \int_0^{T_b} s_i(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt$) are:

$$s_{01} = +\sqrt{E_b}, \qquad s_{11} = -\sqrt{E_b}$$

### Step 6: Construct the constellation diagram

The two signal points are placed on the real axis at $\pm\sqrt{E_b}$. The **distance between the points** is:

$$d = s_{01} - s_{11} = 2\sqrt{E_b}$$

This is the **maximum possible distance** for two equal-energy signals in a 1-D signal space — making BPSK **optimal** in terms of noise immunity among all binary schemes.

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation justifies why BPSK is called **antipodal** — the two signal vectors point in **opposite directions** on the signal space.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: BER of BPSK under AWGN

### Step 1: Set up the received signal model

After passing through an AWGN channel, the received signal is:

$$r(t) = s_i(t) + n(t), \quad i \in \{0, 1\}$$

where $n(t)$ is a zero-mean Gaussian noise process with two-sided PSD $N_0/2$.

### Step 2: Apply the optimal (matched-filter / correlator) receiver

The correlator receiver computes the decision variable:

$$r_1 = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\,\phi_1(t)\,dt = \int_0^{T_b}\left[s_i(t) + n(t)\right]\phi_1(t)\,dt$$

$$r_1 = s_{i1} + n_1$$

where $n_1 = \int_0^{T_b} n(t)\phi_1(t)\,dt$ is a **Gaussian random variable** with:
- Mean: $\mathbb{E}\{n_1\} = 0$
- Variance: $\sigma^2 = N_0/2$

### Step 3: Decision rule

Since the two signal points are symmetric about zero ($\pm\sqrt{E_b}$), the **optimal threshold is zero**:

$$\text{Decide } s_0(t) \text{ if } r_1 > 0; \quad \text{Decide } s_1(t) \text{ if } r_1 < 0$$

### Step 4: Compute probability of error

Assuming $s_0(t)$ was sent (i.e., $r_1 = +\sqrt{E_b} + n_1$), an error occurs if $r_1 < 0$:

$$P_e = P(r_1 < 0 \mid s_0 \text{ sent}) = P\!\left(n_1 < -\sqrt{E_b}\right)$$

Since $n_1 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, N_0/2)$:

$$P_e = \int_{-\infty}^{-\sqrt{E_b}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{\pi N_0}}\exp\!\left(-\frac{u^2}{N_0}\right)du$$

### Step 5: Substitute and simplify

Let $u = \sqrt{N_0/2}\,x$, so $du = \sqrt{N_0/2}\,dx$ and the lower limit becomes $-\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}$:

$$P_e = \int_{-\infty}^{-\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}} \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}}e^{-x^2/2}\,dx = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

### Final Result:

$$\boxed{P_{e,\text{BPSK}} = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\,\mathrm{erfc}\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most-tested formula in the KTU 2024 Data Communication exam** for Module 2. Memorize it and know how to read off the corresponding $E_b/N_0$ in dB from the standard Q-function table.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: QPSK Bandwidth & Bit Rate Relationship

### Step 1: Bit-to-symbol grouping

The input bit stream of rate $R_b$ bits/second is **grouped in pairs** to form dibits. Each dibit represents one QPSK symbol.

Number of dibits per second = $R_b / 2$ → **Symbol rate** = $R_s = R_b/2$ symbols/second.

### Step 2: Symbol duration

$$T_s = \frac{1}{R_s} = \frac{2}{R_b} = 2T_b$$

### Step 3: Bandwidth (null-to-null, raised-cosine)

The minimum null-to-null bandwidth of an M-ary PSK signal is:

$$B = \frac{2}{T_s} = \frac{2}{2T_b} = \frac{1}{T_b} = R_b$$

So for the **same bit rate $R_b$**, QPSK requires **half the bandwidth of BPSK**.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **central reason** QPSK is preferred over BPSK in bandwidth-limited applications like satellite and mobile communications.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Relation between QPSK and Two Parallel BPSK

Starting from the QPSK signal:

$$s_k(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \frac{(2k+1)\pi}{4}\right)$$

Apply the cosine-of-sum identity $\cos(A+B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$:

$$s_k(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_s}{T_s}}\left[\cos\!\left(\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{4}\right)\cos(2\pi f_c t) - \sin\!\left(\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{4}\right)\sin(2\pi f_c t)\right]$$

Define the I and Q data symbols:

$$I_k = \sqrt{E_s}\cos\!\left(\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{4}\right), \qquad Q_k = \sqrt{E_s}\sin\!\left(\frac{(2k+1)\pi}{4}\right)$$

Then:

$$s_k(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2}{T_s}}\left[I_k \cos(2\pi f_c t) - Q_k \sin(2\pi f_c t)\right]$$

The pair $(I_k, Q_k)$ for the four possible dibits takes the values:

| $k$ | Dibit | $I_k$ | $Q_k$ |
|:---:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|
| 0   | 00    | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$  | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$  |
| 1   | 01    | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$  | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$  |
| 2   | 11    | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$  | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$  |
| 3   | 10    | $+\sqrt{E_s/2}$  | $-\sqrt{E_s/2}$  |

Each of the I and Q arms is essentially a **BPSK signal** with $\pm\sqrt{E_s/2}$ amplitude. This proves:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **A QPSK modulator is the superposition of two BPSK modulators operating on orthogonal carriers (cosine and sine).** This is why QPSK has the same BER as BPSK at the same $E_b/N_0$.

---

## 3.5 Numerical Example — BPSK BER Calculation

**Problem:** A BPSK system operates with $E_b/N_0 = 8 \text{ dB}$. Compute the bit error probability.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Convert dB to linear scale:

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0}\bigg|_{\text{linear}} = 10^{8/10} = 10^{0.8} = 6.31$$

Step 2 — Substitute into the BER formula:

$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \times 6.31}\right) = Q\!\left(\sqrt{12.62}\right) = Q(3.55)$$

Step 3 — Read the Q-function value from tables:

$$P_e \approx 1.9 \times 10^{-4}$$

> [!NOTE]
> A similar numerical example is **almost always asked** in the 14-mark KTU questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 BPSK Transmitter — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[BITSTREAM\nInput Bits b t] --> node2[NRZ LEVEL\nCONVERTER\n0 to +1\n1 to -1]
    node2 --> node3[CARRIER\nGENERATOR\nA cos 2 pi fc t]
    node3 --> node4[PRODUCT\nMODULATOR\nBPSK Output]
    node4 --> node5[BPSK SIGNAL\ns t = +- A cos 2 pi fc t]
    node1 --> node4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the flow:** The input NRZ data multiplies the local carrier in the product modulator. The output is a constant-amplitude, constant-frequency carrier whose **polarity flips by $\pi$ radians** whenever the input bit changes.

---

## 4.2 BPSK Coherent Receiver — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[RECEIVED\nSIGNAL r t] --> nodeB[BANDPASS\nFILTER\nReject Out of Band Noise]
    nodeB --> nodeC[CORRELATOR\nMultiply by Local Carrier]
    nodeD[LOCAL\nCARRIER\ncos 2 pi fc t] --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeE[INTEGRATOR\nIntegrate over 0 to Tb]
    nodeE --> nodeF[DECISION\nDEVICE\nThreshold at 0]
    nodeF --> nodeG[OUTPUT\nBITSTREAM]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **local carrier at the receiver MUST be phase-coherent** with the transmitter carrier. Any phase error causes a reduction in the decision variable. This is the biggest engineering challenge of coherent PSK.

---

## 4.3 QPSK Transmitter and Receiver Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TX[QPSK TRANSMITTER]
        node1X[BITSTREAM] --> node2X[SERIAL TO\nPARALLEL\nSPLITTER]
        node2X -->|I bit| node3X[NRZ\nENCODER I]
        node2X -->|Q bit| node4X[NRZ\nENCODER Q]
        node3X --> node5X[PRODUCT\nMODULATOR I]
        node4X --> node6X[PRODUCT\nMODULATOR Q]
        node7X[CARRIER\nCOS 2 pi fc t] --> node5X
        node8X[CARRIER\nSIN 2 pi fc t] --> node6X
        node5X --> node9X[SUMMER\nAdder]
        node6X --> node9X
        node9X --> node10X[QPSK\nOUTPUT s t]
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RX[QPSK COHERENT RECEIVER]
        nodeR1[RECEIVED\nQPSK SIGNAL] --> nodeR2I[I-CHANNEL\nCORRELATOR\nMultiply by cos]
        nodeR1 --> nodeR2Q[Q-CHANNEL\nCORRELATOR\nMultiply by sin]
        nodeR3I[LOCAL CARRIER\ncos 2 pi fc t] --> nodeR2I
        nodeR3Q[LOCAL CARRIER\nsin 2 pi fc t] --> nodeR2Q
        nodeR2I --> nodeR4I[I-CHANNEL\nINTEGRATOR]
        nodeR2Q --> nodeR4Q[Q-CHANNEL\nINTEGRATOR]
        nodeR4I --> nodeR5I[I-CHANNEL\nDECISION\nThreshold at 0]
        nodeR4Q --> nodeR5Q[Q-CHANNEL\nDECISION\nThreshold at 0]
        nodeR5I --> nodeR6[PARALLEL TO\nSERIAL CONVERTER]
        nodeR5Q --> nodeR6
        nodeR6 --> nodeR7[OUTPUT\nBITSTREAM]
    end
```

---

## 4.4 Constellation Diagram (Logical / Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ConstellationDiagram
        direction TB
        node00[BIT 0\nPHASE 0 deg]:::bpskPt
        node01[BIT 1\nPHASE 180 deg]:::bpskPt
    end
    classDef bpskPt fill:#1f6feb,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **BPSK Constellation:** Two points on the real axis at $\pm\sqrt{E_b}$, separated by $180^\circ$.
> **QPSK Constellation:** Four points uniformly distributed on a circle at $45^\circ, 135^\circ, 225^\circ, 315^\circ$ (or any rotated version, depending on the carrier reference).
> **M-PSK Constellation:** M points uniformly distributed on a circle of radius $\sqrt{E_s}$ at angles $2\pi k / M$.

The **decision regions** are pie-slice sectors of $2\pi/M$ radians each.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Phase Shift Keying (PSK). How does it differ from ASK and FSK?**

**Model Answer:**

Phase Shift Keying (PSK) is a digital modulation technique in which the **phase of a constant-amplitude, constant-frequency carrier wave is varied in discrete steps** in accordance with the digital modulating signal, while the amplitude and frequency remain unchanged.

| Parameter | ASK | FSK | PSK |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|
| Varies | Amplitude | Frequency | **Phase** |
| Constant | Frequency, Phase | Amplitude, Phase | **Amplitude, Frequency** |
| Bandwidth | Low | High | Moderate |
| Noise immunity | Poor | Better | **Best (among ASK/FSK)** |

**Examiner's note (3 marks breakdown):** Definition 1 mark, table 1 mark, conclusion 1 mark.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**What is the difference between coherent and non-coherent detection of PSK signals? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

In **coherent detection**, the receiver uses a **locally generated carrier that is phase-synchronized** with the incoming carrier. This allows absolute phase reference — example: **BPSK and QPSK coherent receivers**.

In **non-coherent detection**, the receiver does **not require knowledge of the absolute phase** of the carrier; information is extracted from phase *differences* between consecutive symbols — example: **DPSK**.

Coherent detection provides better BER (about 1 dB superior), but requires complex carrier recovery circuitry. Non-coherent detection is simpler and more robust to phase jitter.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the generation and detection of **Binary Phase Shift Keying (BPSK)**. Derive the expression for its bit error probability under AWGN. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BPSK system transmits data at a rate of $R_b = 1 \text{ Mbps}$ over an AWGN channel. The received signal has $E_b/N_0 = 10 \text{ dB}$. Calculate the **probability of bit error** and the **minimum transmission bandwidth**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (a)**

**Block Diagram of BPSK Modulator** (see Section 4.1 above for the functional flow).

**Generation:** The input NRZ bitstream $b(t) \in \{0, 1\}$ is first level-converted to $m(t) \in \{+1, -1\}$. This signal is multiplied with a carrier $\sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ in a product modulator. The output is:

$$s(t) = m(t)\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\cos(2\pi f_c t)$$

When $m(t) = +1$ (bit 0): carrier has phase $0^\circ$.
When $m(t) = -1$ (bit 1): carrier has phase $180^\circ$.

**Detection (Coherent):** The received signal $r(t) = s(t) + n(t)$ is first bandpass-filtered, then multiplied by a locally generated coherent carrier $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and integrated over one bit duration $T_b$:

$$r_1 = \int_0^{T_b} r(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)\,dt = \pm\sqrt{E_b} + n_1$$

where $n_1$ is a Gaussian RV with zero mean and variance $N_0/2$. The decision device compares $r_1$ against the threshold $0$ and outputs the corresponding bit.

**BER Derivation** (full derivation in Section 3.2):

$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A (b)**

**Step 1:** Convert $E_b/N_0$ from dB to linear:

$$\frac{E_b}{N_0} = 10^{10/10} = 10$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the BER formula:

$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 \times 10}\right) = Q(\sqrt{20}) = Q(4.472)$$

**Step 3:** From standard Q-tables (or $\text{erfc}$ tables):

$$P_e \approx 3.87 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 4:** Minimum transmission bandwidth (null-to-null):

$$B_{\min} = 2R_b = 2 \times 10^6 = 2 \text{ MHz}$$

**Final Answers:** $P_e \approx 3.87 \times 10^{-6}$ and $B_{\min} = 2$ MHz.

---

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — *Internal Choice Alternative*

**(a)** Explain the principle of **Quadrature Phase Shift Keying (QPSK)**. Draw its **constellation diagram** and derive the **probability of bit error**. Compare it with BPSK. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A QPSK system uses a carrier frequency of $70 \text{ MHz}$ and a bit rate of $R_b = 20 \text{ Mbps}$. Compute the **(i) symbol rate**, **(ii) symbol duration**, **(iii) minimum bandwidth**, and **(iv) bandwidth efficiency**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (a)**

In QPSK, two bits (a dibit) are transmitted simultaneously by using **four phase states** of the carrier: $\phi_k = (2k+1)\pi/4$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3$.

The input bit stream is split into two parallel streams (I and Q) each of rate $R_b/2$. These modulate two **orthogonal carriers** $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ and $\sin(2\pi f_c t)$ respectively, and the two BPSK-modulated signals are summed:

$$s(t) = \sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{T_b}}\left[I(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t) - Q(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)\right]$$

**Constellation Diagram:** Four points on a circle at $45^\circ, 135^\circ, 225^\circ, 315^\circ$, each at radius $\sqrt{2E_b}$. The **decision boundaries** are the I-axis and Q-axis.

**BER Derivation:** The correlator outputs on the I and Q arms are:

$$r_I = \pm\sqrt{E_b/2} + n_I, \qquad r_Q = \pm\sqrt{E_b/2} + n_Q$$

with $n_I, n_Q$ independent Gaussian RVs of variance $N_0/2$. The probability that the I-channel makes an error is $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$; similarly for Q. Since bits in I and Q are independent, the overall bit error rate is:

$$P_e = Q\!\left(\sqrt{\frac{2E_b}{N_0}}\right)$$

**Comparison with BPSK:**

| Property | BPSK | QPSK |
|----------|------|------|
| Phase states | 2 | 4 |
| Bits per symbol | 1 | 2 |
| Bandwidth | $2R_b$ | $R_b$ |
| Spectral efficiency | $0.5$ bps/Hz | $1$ bps/Hz |
| BER (same $E_b/N_0$) | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ | $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$ — **same** |
| Symbol rate | $R_b$ | $R_b/2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for KTU:** QPSK and BPSK have identical BER performance, but QPSK uses **half the bandwidth** for the same bit rate, making it the **preferred choice for bandwidth-limited channels**.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B (b)**

**Given:** $f_c = 70$ MHz, $R_b = 20$ Mbps.

**(i) Symbol Rate:**

$$R_s = \frac{R_b}{\log_2 M} = \frac{20 \times 10^6}{2} = 10 \times 10^6 \text{ symbols/sec} = 10 \text{ Msps}$$

**(ii) Symbol Duration:**

$$T_s = \frac{1}{R_s} = \frac{1}{10 \times 10^6} = 0.1 \ \mu\text{s} = 100 \text{ ns}$$

**(iii) Minimum Bandwidth** (null-to-null):

$$B_{\min} = \frac{2}{T_s} = 2R_s = 20 \text{ MHz}$$

**(iv) Bandwidth Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{R_b}{B_{\min}} = \frac{20 \text{ Mbps}}{20 \text{ MHz}} = 1 \text{ bit/sec/Hz}$$

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in the KTU University Exam:**
> 1. **Confusing symbol rate and bit rate.** QPSK transmits 2 bits per symbol. Always write $R_s = R_b/\log_2 M$ explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the $E_b/N_0$ to linear conversion** in numerical problems. KTU examiners deduct a full mark if you write $E_b/N_0 = 8$ dB directly inside the Q-function.
> 3. **Wrong $Q(x)$ values from tables.** $Q(0) = 0.5$, $Q(\infty) = 0$. Never write $Q(x)$ for negative $x$ without using the identity $Q(-x) = 1 - Q(x)$.
> 4. **Mixing up "null-to-null bandwidth" ($2/T_s$) with "first-null bandwidth" ($R_s$) and "half-power bandwidth" ($0.886 R_s$).** KTU Module 2 typically uses the null-to-null definition.
> 5. **Missing the dibit grouping step** in QPSK derivations. Many students forget to mention that the bit stream is first serial-to-parallel converted.
> 6. **Writing BER for DPSK as $Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0})$** — that is the formula for coherent BPSK. DPSK uses $\frac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/N_0)$.
> 7. **Not stating assumptions** (e.g., $f_c \gg R_b$, equal a priori probabilities, AWGN channel) — the KTU rubric often awards 0.5 marks for explicit assumption statements.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for PSK (Module 2):**

- **PSK** modulates the **PHASE** of a constant-envelope carrier; amplitude and frequency are unchanged.
- **BPSK** has **2 phase states** ($0^\circ, 180^\circ$); it is **antipodal**, gives the **lowest BER** among binary schemes.
- BPSK signal: $s(t) = \sqrt{2E_b/T_b}\cos(2\pi f_c t + \phi_k)$ with $\phi_k \in \{0, \pi\}$.
- BPSK BER: $P_e = Q(\sqrt{2E_b/N_0}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(\sqrt{E_b/N_0})$.
- **QPSK** has **4 phase states** ($45^\circ, 135^\circ, 225^\circ, 315^\circ$); each symbol carries **2 bits (dibit)**.
- QPSK is **two BPSK signals on orthogonal carriers**; it has the **same BER as BPSK** but **half the bandwidth**.
- QPSK bandwidth (null-to-null) = $R_b$; QPSK symbol rate = $R_b/2$.
- **DPSK** is non-coherent: data is in **phase differences** between consecutive symbols; BER is $\tfrac{1}{2}\exp(-E_b/N_0)$, about **1 dB worse** than coherent BPSK.
- **M-ary PSK** has M phases at $2\pi k/M$, $k = 0, 1, \ldots, M-1$, with $\log_2 M$ bits per symbol.
- **Higher M → higher spectral efficiency, but tighter phase spacing → higher SNR required** for the same BER.
- **Coherent detection** requires phase-locked carrier recovery; **non-coherent (DPSK)** does not.
- **Constellation diagram** is a plot of $I = \int s(t)\cos(2\pi f_c t)\,dt$ vs. $Q = -\int s(t)\sin(2\pi f_c t)\,dt$.
- **Decision regions** in the constellation are pie-slice sectors of $2\pi/M$ radians.
- **Assumptions for all derived formulas:** AWGN channel, coherent detection, equiprobable symbols, matched-filter reception, perfect synchronization.
- **Real-world deployments:** BPSK → GPS, deep-space; QPSK → satellite TV, 3G; 8-PSK → EDGE; DQPSK → Bluetooth; M-PSK → legacy modems.
- **Quick numerical tip:** $E_b/N_0$ in dB = $10\log_{10}(E_b/N_0)_{\text{linear}}$. Always convert before using in formulas.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analog Data to Analog Signal - Amplitude Modulation (AM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Analog Data to Analog Signal - Amplitude Modulation (AM) # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Amplitude Modulation (AM)** is an analog-to-analog conversion technique in which the **amplitude** of a high-frequency sinusoidal **carrier signal** is varied in direct proportion to the instantaneous amplitude of a low-frequency **message (modulating) signal**, while the frequency and phase of the carrier remain constant. Mathematically, an AM signal is expressed as: $$ s(t) = A_c \left[1 + m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t)\right] \cos(2\pi f_c t) $$ Where: - $A_c$ = Amplitude of the **carrier signal** (Volts) - $f_c$ = Frequency of the **carrier signal** (Hz) - $f_m$ = Frequency of the **message signal** (Hz) - $m_a$ = **Modulation Index** (dimensionless, usually $0 \le m_a \le 1$) - $t$ = Time (seconds) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Analog-to-Analog conversion has only **three fundamental techniques**: **Amplitude Modulation (AM)**, **Frequency Modulation (FM)**, and **Phase Modulation (PM)**. AM is the oldest and simplest of all modulation schemes, dating back to the 1900s for radio broadcasting. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crane Lifting a Box" Analogy Imagine a construction crane: - The **crane's boom (arm)** swings back and forth at a fixed, constant speed — this is the **carrier signal**, oscillating at a fixed radio frequency. - A **worker places a heavy box** on the hook — the *weight* (height/depth) of the box is the **message signal** (your voice, music). - The crane's arm still moves at the same constant speed, but the **height of its tip now varies** based on the weight of the box. - An observer watching the crane's tip sees a wave whose *envelope* (the outer shape) carries the information of the box's weight. This is exactly how AM works: the **carrier oscillates fast and steady**, but the **envelope (outer boundary) of the wave follows the message signal** — your voice literally rides on top of the radio wave. ## 1.3 Key Terminology Standardized for KTU 2024 | Term | Standard Definition | | :--- | :--- | | **Baseband Signal** | The original low-frequency information signal (e.g., voice: 300 Hz – 3.4 kHz). | | **Carrier Signal** | A high-frequency sine wave used as the transport vehicle. For AM broadcast: 540 kHz – 1700 kHz. | | **Modulating Signal** | Another name for the baseband message signal. | | **Modulation Index ($m_a$)** | Ratio of message amplitude to carrier amplitude. | | **Envelope** | The smooth curve tracing the peaks of the AM waveform. | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Distinction:** In AM, *only* the **amplitude** changes. Frequency and phase of the carrier are *never* altered. If they change, the technique is no longer pure AM. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Let us break down how an AM signal is constructed and interpreted: **Step 1 — Choose the Carrier** A high-frequency pure sine wave is generated by a stable oscillator (e.g., a crystal oscillator) at the transmitter. **Step 2 — Generate the Message** The microphone converts sound pressure variations into a low-frequency electrical signal called the baseband signal. **Step 3 — Mixing (Modulation)** The message signal is added to a DC offset (so the total signal is always positive) and then multiplied with the carrier in a device called a **modulator** (e.g., a square-law modulator or balanced modulator). **Step 4 — Transmission** The resulting AM signal is amplified and sent via an antenna. The antenna's size is now feasible because the wavelength corresponds to the high carrier frequency. **Step 5 — Reception (Demodulation)** At the receiver, an **envelope detector** (a simple diode + RC circuit) strips away the high-frequency carrier and recovers the envelope, which is the original message. ## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step - **Why a high-frequency carrier?** Because efficient radiation of electromagnetic waves requires antenna lengths comparable to $\lambda/4$, where $\lambda = c/f$. Voice frequencies (~3 kHz) would need antennas ~25 km long — impossible. A 1 MHz carrier needs only a 75-meter antenna. - **Why a DC offset?** To make $1 + m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t)$ always positive, so the envelope never crosses zero (which would cause **over-modulation distortion**). - **Why an envelope detector?** Because the message is embedded in the amplitude's *envelope*, a simple diode-RC circuit suffices — this is why AM receivers are cheap and gave rise to pocket radios. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) | # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $s(t) = A_c[1 + m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ | Standard AM signal | Volts | | 2 | $m_a = A_m / A_c$ | Modulation index (from amplitudes) | Dimensionless, $\le 1$ | | 3 | $m_a = (A_{\max} - A_{\min}) / (A_{\max} + A_{\min})$ | Modulation index (from envelope peaks) | Dimensionless | | 4 | $s(t) = A_c\cos(2\pi f_c t) + \tfrac{A_m}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c-f_m)t] + \tfrac{A_m}{2}\cos[2\pi(f_c+f_m)t]$ | Expanded AM spectrum form | Volts | | 5 | $B_{AM} = 2 f_m$ | Bandwidth of AM signal | Hertz (Hz) | | 6 | $P_c = A_c^{\,2} / (2R)$ | Carrier power (across resistor R) | Watts | | 7 | $P_{USB} = P_{LSB} = A_m^{\,2} / (8R) = m_a^{\,2} P_c / 4$ | Sideband power (each sideband) | Watts | | 8 | $P_t = P_c \left(1 + \tfrac{m_a^{\,2}}{2}\right)$ | **Total transmitted power** | Watts | | 9 | $\eta = \tfrac{m_a^{\,2}}{2 + m_a^{\,2}}$ | **Transmission efficiency** | Percent (max = 33.3% at $m_a = 1$) | | 10 | $A_{\max} = A_c(1 + m_a)$, $A_{\min} = A_c(1 - m_a)$ | Peak/valley of envelope | Volts | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Tip:** The single most tested formula is $P_t = P_c(1 + m_a^{\,2}/2)$. Memorize it along with the efficiency formula. They are *always* asked together in 14-mark problems. ## 2.4 Real-World Utility - **AM Radio Broadcasting (540 kHz – 1700 kHz):** Long-range, ground-wave propagation. - **Airband Communication (108–137 MHz):** AM is used in aircraft VHF because it allows simultaneous voice transmission and simple homing (direction-finding) via the carrier. - **Citizens Band (CB) Radio:** Legacy AM usage at 27 MHz. - **Single-Sideband (SSB) and DSB-SC Variants:** AM is the parent of these more spectrum-efficient techniques used in HF military and maritime communication. > [!NOTE] > **Spectrum Visualization:** A pure AM signal consists of exactly **three frequency components**: the carrier at $f_c$, the Lower Sideband (LSB) at $f_c - f_m$, and the Upper Sideband (USB) at $f_c + f_m$. This is the foundation for understanding DSB-SC, SSB, and VSB later in the syllabus. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1 — From Time Domain to Frequency Domain (Spectrum) **Starting Equation:** $$ s(t) = A_c [1 + m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t) $$ **Step 1 — Expand the bracket:** $$ s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + A_c \, m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t) \cos(2\pi f_c t) $$ The first term is the carrier alone; the second term is the modulated portion. **Step 2 — Apply the trigonometric product-to-sum identity:** $$ \cos(\alpha) \cos(\beta) = \tfrac{1}{2} [\cos(\alpha - \beta) + \cos(\alpha + \beta)] $$ Here, $\alpha = 2\pi f_c t$ and $\beta = 2\pi f_m t$. Substituting: $$ A_c \, m_a \cos(2\pi f_c t) \cos(2\pi f_m t) = \tfrac{A_c \, m_a}{2} [\cos(2\pi(f_c - f_m)t) + \cos(2\pi(f_c + f_m)t)] $$ **Step 3 — Combine to get the final spectrum form:** $$ s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \tfrac{A_c \, m_a}{2} \cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \tfrac{A_c \, m_a}{2} \cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t] $$ Since $A_m = m_a A_c$, we can also write: $$ s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \tfrac{A_m}{2} \cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \tfrac{A_m}{2} \cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t] $$ **Interpretation:** The AM spectrum contains exactly three spectral lines — carrier, LSB, USB — confirming the bandwidth of $2 f_m$. --- ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2 — Total Transmitted Power Assume the AM signal is delivered to a resistive load $R$ ohms. The instantaneous power is $p(t) = s^2(t) / R$. The average power over one period is computed as follows. **Step 1 — Start with the expanded AM expression:** $$ s(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t) + \tfrac{A_m}{2} \cos[2\pi(f_c - f_m)t] + \tfrac{A_m}{2} \cos[2\pi(f_c + f_m)t] $$ **Step 2 — Compute average power of each component using $P = A^2 / (2R)$:** $$ P_c = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R} $$ $$ P_{LSB} = \frac{(A_m/2)^2}{2R} = \frac{A_m^{\,2}}{8R} $$ $$ P_{USB} = \frac{(A_m/2)^2}{2R} = \frac{A_m^{\,2}}{8R} $$ **Step 3 — Sum the three powers (cross-terms average to zero over time):** $$ P_t = P_c + P_{LSB} + P_{USB} = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R} + \frac{A_m^{\,2}}{8R} + \frac{A_m^{\,2}}{8R} $$ $$ P_t = \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{2R} + \frac{A_m^{\,2}}{4R} $$ **Step 4 — Express in terms of $P_c$ and $m_a$** (using $A_m = m_a A_c$): $$ P_t = P_c + \frac{(m_a A_c)^2}{4R} = P_c + m_a^{\,2} \cdot \frac{A_c^{\,2}}{4R} = P_c + \frac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} P_c $$ $$ \boxed{P_t = P_c \left(1 + \frac{m_a^{\,2}}{2}\right)} $$ --- ## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation 3 — Transmission Efficiency Efficiency is defined as the fraction of total power that actually carries information (i.e., sideband power) versus wasted carrier power: $$ \eta = \frac{P_{sidebands}}{P_t} = \frac{P_{LSB} + P_{USB}}{P_c + P_{LSB} + P_{USB}} $$ **Step 1 — Substitute known values:** $$ \eta = \frac{A_m^{\,2}/4R}{A_c^{\,2}/2R + A_m^{\,2}/4R} = \frac{A_m^{\,2}}{2A_c^{\,2} + A_m^{\,2}} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute $A_m = m_a A_c$:** $$ \eta = \frac{m_a^{\,2} A_c^{\,2}}{2A_c^{\,2} + m_a^{\,2} A_c^{\,2}} = \frac{m_a^{\,2}}{2 + m_a^{\,2}} $$ **Step 3 — Maximum efficiency** occurs at $m_a = 1$ (100% modulation, the limit before distortion): $$ \eta_{\max} = \frac{1}{2 + 1} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 33.33\% $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** Even at perfect modulation, AM wastes **two-thirds of its transmitted power** in the carrier — which carries *no information*. This is the principal motivation for **DSB-SC** and **SSB** systems. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical 7-Mark Sub-Question) **Problem:** A 600 W carrier is modulated by a sinusoidal message to a depth of 80%.
