Calculate: (a) Total transmitted power (b) Power in each sideband (c) Transmission efficiency **Given:** $P_c = 600$ W, $m_a = 0.8$ **Part (a):** Using the total power formula: $$ P_t = P_c \left(1 + \frac{m_a^{\,2}}{2}\right) = 600 \left(1 + \frac{0.64}{2}\right) = 600 \times 1.32 = 792 \text{ W} $$ **Part (b):** Each sideband carries: $$ P_{LSB} = P_{USB} = \frac{m_a^{\,2} P_c}{4} = \frac{0.64 \times 600}{4} = 96 \text{ W} $$ **Part (c):** Efficiency: $$ \eta = \frac{m_a^{\,2}}{2 + m_a^{\,2}} = \frac{0.64}{2.64} = 0.2424 = 24.24\% $$ **Verification:** $P_c + 2 P_{USB} = 600 + 192 = 792$ W = $P_t$ ✓ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — AM Transmitter (Modulator) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeMsg[Message Signal
m of t - 300 Hz to 3.4 kHz] nodeOsc[Carrier Oscillator
cos 2 pi fc t] nodeDC[DC Bias Adder
Adds 1 to message] nodeMult[Multiplier / Modulator
Balanced Modulator] nodePA[Power Amplifier
Boosts signal] nodeAnt[Antenna
Radiates EM wave] nodeOut[AM Signal in Space
sidebands + carrier] nodeMsg --> nodeDC nodeDC --> nodeMult nodeOsc --> nodeMult nodeMult --> nodePA nodePA --> nodeAnt nodeAnt --> nodeOut ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — AM Receiver (Envelope Detector) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeIn[Antenna Input
Weak AM signal] nodeRF[RF Amplifier
Tunes to fc] nodeDet[Envelope Detector
Diode + RC Circuit] nodeAF[Audio Amplifier
Boosts baseband] nodeSpk[Speaker
Recovers sound] nodeIn --> nodeRF nodeRF --> nodeDet nodeDet --> nodeAF nodeAF --> nodeSpk ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Frequency-Domain Spectrum Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeAxis[Frequency Axis f in Hz] nodeLSB[LSB Spectral Line
fc minus fm
Amplitude Am by 2] nodeCarrier[Carrier Spike
fc
Amplitude Ac] nodeUSB[USB Spectral Line
fc plus fm
Amplitude Am by 2] nodeLSB --- nodeCarrier nodeCarrier --- nodeUSB ``` **Spectrum Reading Guide:** - Horizontal axis → Frequency (Hz) - Vertical axis → Amplitude (Volts) - Distance between LSB and USB = $2 f_m$ = **AM Bandwidth** - Carrier amplitude = $A_c$ - Sideband amplitude = $A_m / 2 = m_a A_c / 2$ - **The carrier carries NO information; only the sidebands do.** ## 4.4 Mermaid Envelope Visualization (Conceptual) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph A[Fast Carrier Inside Envelope] node1[High frequency cosine wave - the carrier] end subgraph B[Slow Envelope on Outside] node2[Low frequency message signal shape] end A --- B node2 --> node3[Envelope = 1 plus ma times cos 2 pi fm t] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define Amplitude Modulation. Write the mathematical expression for an AM signal.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Amplitude Modulation is the process by which the amplitude of a high-frequency carrier signal is varied in accordance with the instantaneous amplitude of the low-frequency message signal, keeping the carrier's frequency and phase constant. $$ s(t) = A_c [1 + m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t)] \cos(2\pi f_c t) $$ where $A_c$ is the carrier amplitude, $f_c$ the carrier frequency, $f_m$ the message frequency, and $m_a$ the modulation index. **(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for variable explanation.)** --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]` **What is modulation index? Write its significance.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Modulation index $m_a$ is defined as the ratio of the peak amplitude of the message signal to the peak amplitude of the carrier: $$ m_a = \frac{A_m}{A_c} = \frac{A_{\max} - A_{\min}}{A_{\max} + A_{\min}} $$ **Significance:** (1) It indicates the depth of modulation. (2) For distortion-free AM, $m_a \le 1$. (3) It determines transmission efficiency, sideband power, and bandwidth occupancy. **(1 mark for formula, 2 marks for significance.)** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` **(a)** Derive the expression for the total transmitted power of an AM wave. Show that even with 100% modulation, only one-third of the total power is useful. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An AM transmitter radiates 10 kW when the carrier is unmodulated. When modulated sinusoidally, it radiates 12 kW. Find the modulation index and the peak amplitude of the message signal if the carrier amplitude is 200 V. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) We have: $$ P_t = P_c \left(1 + \frac{m_a^{\,2}}{2}\right) $$ Derivation is shown exhaustively in SECTION 3.2 above. **(Full 7 marks.)** For 100% modulation, $m_a = 1$: $$ P_t = P_c \left(1 + \frac{1}{2}\right) = 1.5 \, P_c $$ Useful sideband power = $P_t - P_c = 0.5 P_c$. Efficiency: $$ \eta = \frac{0.5 P_c}{1.5 P_c} = \frac{1}{3} = 33.33\% $$ Hence only one-third of the total power is useful, even at maximum modulation. **(Conclusion fetches the last mark.)** #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — Unmodulated power = carrier power:** $$ P_c = 10 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 2 — Total modulated power:** $$ P_t = 12 \text{ kW} $$ **Step 3 — Apply total power formula:** $$ 12 = 10 \left(1 + \frac{m_a^{\,2}}{2}\right) \Rightarrow 1.2 = 1 + \frac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} $$ $$ \frac{m_a^{\,2}}{2} = 0.2 \Rightarrow m_a^{\,2} = 0.4 $$ $$ \boxed{m_a = \sqrt{0.4} \approx 0.6325} $$ **Step 4 — Peak message amplitude** (using $m_a = A_m / A_c$): $$ A_m = m_a \times A_c = 0.6325 \times 200 = 126.5 \text{ V} $$ $$\boxed{A_m \approx 126.5 \text{ V}}$$ --- ### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Sample QP 2024]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the generation and detection of an AM signal. Define modulation index and explain what happens when $m_a > 1$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A carrier of 1 MHz and amplitude 50 V is amplitude modulated by a 5 kHz sinusoidal message to a depth of 60%. Determine: (i) modulation index, (ii) sideband frequencies, (iii) bandwidth, (iv) amplitude of each sideband. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Block diagrams:** Refer to SECTION 4.1 (Modulator) and SECTION 4.2 (Envelope Detector) of these notes. **(3 marks for diagrams.)** **Modulation index definition** (1 mark): $$ m_a = \frac{A_m}{A_c} $$ **What happens when $m_a > 1$:** When $m_a > 1$ (over-modulation), the term $[1 + m_a \cos(2\pi f_m t)]$ becomes *negative* during part of the cycle. This causes the **envelope to cross zero**, resulting in **envelope distortion** and **phase reversal** at the zero-crossing. The demodulated signal no longer represents the original message faithfully. **(3 marks.)** #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** $f_c = 1$ MHz, $A_c = 50$ V, $f_m = 5$ kHz, $m_a = 0.6$ **(i) Modulation index:** $m_a = 0.6$ **(0.5 mark)** **(ii) Sideband frequencies:** $$ f_{LSB} = f_c - f_m = 1{,}000{,}000 - 5{,}000 = 995 \text{ kHz} $$ $$ f_{USB} = f_c + f_m = 1{,}000{,}000 + 5{,}000 = 1{,}005 \text{ kHz} $$ **(1.5 marks)** **(iii) Bandwidth:** $$ B_{AM} = 2 f_m = 2 \times 5{,}000 = 10 \text{ kHz} $$ **(1.5 marks)** **(iv) Amplitude of each sideband:** $$ A_{LSB} = A_{USB} = \frac{m_a A_c}{2} = \frac{0.6 \times 50}{2} = 15 \text{ V} $$ **(2 marks)** **Total marks: 5.5 + 1.5 = 7 (with rounding and presentation grace marks.)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Units Mistake:** Writing bandwidth as "$2f_c$" instead of "$2f_m$" — a classic 1-mark loss. The bandwidth depends on the **message** frequency, not the carrier. > 2. **Confusing Carrier Amplitude with Sideband Amplitude:** Sideband amplitude is $m_a A_c / 2$, NOT $A_c / 2$. Many students lose 2 marks here. > 3. **Forgetting DC Bias:** When explaining the AM waveform, students often forget that the message is added to a DC level of 1 to prevent over-modulation. Worth 1 mark in 7-mark questions. > 4. **Sign Error in Efficiency:** Writing $\eta = m_a^{\,2} / (1 + m_a^{\,2}/2)$ instead of $m_a^{\,2}/(2 + m_a^{\,2})$. Always derive from $P_{sidebands}/P_t$ to avoid this. > 5. **Skipping Units:** KTU strictly deducts 0.5 marks for missing units in the final numerical answer. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ **AM signal** is given by $s(t) = A_c[1 + m_a\cos(2\pi f_m t)]\cos(2\pi f_c t)$. - ✅ **Modulation index** $m_a$ must satisfy $0 \le m_a \le 1$ for distortion-free AM. - ✅ **AM consists of three spectral components**: carrier, LSB ($f_c - f_m$), USB ($f_c + f_m$). - ✅ **Bandwidth of AM** is $B_{AM} = 2 f_m$ (twice the highest message frequency). - ✅ **Total power** $P_t = P_c(1 + m_a^{\,2}/2)$; Carrier is always present in AM. - ✅ **Maximum efficiency** of standard AM is only **33.33%** (at $m_a = 1$). - ✅ **Sideband power** (each) = $m_a^{\,2} P_c / 4$. - ✅ **AM is inefficient** because the carrier carries no information — motivating DSB-SC and SSB. - ✅ **Envelope detector** = diode + RC low-pass filter — the simplest demodulator. - ✅ **Over-modulation** ($m_a > 1$) → envelope distortion → information loss. - ✅ **Block diagrams** of modulator and envelope detector are **must-draw** items in 7-mark questions. - ✅ KTU's favorite 14-mark question: **Power + efficiency + sideband calculation** combined into a single numerical problem.

topics.title: Frequency Modulation (FM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Frequency Modulation (FM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 2 — FREQUENCY MODULATION (FM)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> **Frequency Modulation (FM)** is an analog modulation technique in which the **instantaneous frequency** of the carrier signal is varied in direct proportion to the **instantaneous amplitude** of the message (modulating) signal, while the **amplitude and phase** of the carrier remain constant.

Mathematically, the FM signal is expressed as:

$$
s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right)
$$

where $A_c$ = carrier amplitude, $f_c$ = carrier frequency, $k_f$ = frequency sensitivity (Hz/volt), and $m(t)$ = message signal.

### 🧠 Intuitive Analogy — "The Siren and the Road"

Imagine a police siren wailing as it approaches you and then recedes. The **loudness (amplitude)** of the siren is roughly constant, but the **pitch (frequency)** changes — high pitched when approaching, low pitched when moving away.

- The **carrier** = the steady hum of the siren at its rest pitch ($f_c$).
- The **message signal** = the speed/distance of the vehicle, which physically changes the pitch you hear.
- The **FM transmitter** = the vehicle's engine, intentionally modulating its siren's pitch with information (voice, music, data).
- The **FM receiver** = your ear/brain, decoding the pitch variations back into meaningful information.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2):**
> Students must master — (i) FM mathematical model, (ii) Modulation index $\beta$, (iii) Carson's Rule for Bandwidth, (iv) Bessel function spectrum analysis, (v) Narrowband vs Wideband FM, and (vi) FM generation/demodulation methods.

### 🧩 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Meaning | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Instantaneous Frequency | Frequency at any given instant of FM wave | $f_i(t)$ |
| Frequency Deviation | Maximum shift of instantaneous frequency from $f_c$ | $\Delta f$ |
| Modulation Index | Ratio of frequency deviation to message frequency | $\beta$ |
| Phase Deviation | Maximum phase shift in radians | $\Delta\phi = \beta$ |
| Carson's Bandwidth | Approximate spectral width of FM signal | $BW$ |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The FM Concept — Step by Step Logic

**Step 1 — Start with the message signal.**
The baseband (modulating) signal is $m(t)$ with amplitude $A_m$ and frequency $f_m$.

**Step 2 — Define instantaneous frequency.**
The instantaneous frequency of the FM wave at time $t$ is shifted from the carrier frequency by an amount proportional to $m(t)$:

$$
f_i(t) = f_c + k_f \cdot m(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Find frequency deviation.**
The maximum deviation occurs when $m(t)$ reaches its peak $|m(t)|_{\max} = A_m$:

$$
\Delta f = k_f \cdot A_m
$$

**Step 4 — Define the modulation index $\beta$.**
This is the *most-tested* quantity in KTU exams:

$$
\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{k_f \cdot A_m}{f_m}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why does $\beta$ matter?**
> $\beta$ controls everything: the spectral shape (Bessel coefficients), the bandwidth, the number of significant sidebands, and whether the FM is "narrow" or "wide."

**Step 5 — Convert frequency to phase.**
Since $\omega_i(t) = 2\pi f_i(t) = \frac{d\theta(t)}{dt}$, integrate to get the phase:

$$
\theta(t) = 2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

**Step 6 — Construct the FM signal.**
Substituting phase into a cosine carrier:

$$
s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right)
$$

For a single-tone message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$:

$$
s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right)
$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | FM Signal | $s(t) = A_c \cos\!\big(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\big)$ | Volts |
| 2 | Instantaneous Frequency | $f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)$ | Hz |
| 3 | Frequency Deviation | $\Delta f = k_f A_m$ | Hz |
| 4 | Modulation Index | $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \dfrac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Carson's Rule (Bandwidth) | $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2f_m(\beta + 1)$ | Hz |
| 6 | Highest-order Sideband | $n_{\max} = \lceil \beta \rceil$ | — |
| 7 | Transmission Power (FM) | $P_t = \dfrac{A_c^2}{2}$ | Watts (constant!) |
| 8 | NBFM Bandwidth (approx.) | $BW \approx 2f_m$ | $\beta \ll 1$ |
| 9 | WBFM Bandwidth (approx.) | $BW \approx 2\Delta f$ | $\beta \gg 1$ |
| 10 | Pre-emphasis Time Constant | $\tau = 75\ \mu s$ | Standard for FM broadcast |
| 11 | SNR Improvement (FM vs AM) | $\dfrac{(S/N)_{FM}}{(S/N)_{AM}} = \dfrac{3}{2}\beta^2$ | Wideband FM |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> FM is the workhorse of **high-fidelity audio broadcasting (88–108 MHz)**, **two-way radio**, **telemetry**, and **space communication** because of its excellent noise immunity — captured in the figure-of-merit $\frac{3}{2}\beta^2$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — FM Equation from Instantaneous Frequency

**Given:** $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, carrier $c(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$, frequency sensitivity $k_f$.

**Step 1:** By definition of FM, instantaneous frequency is:

$$
f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t) = f_c + k_f A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)
$$

**Step 2:** Total phase is the integral of angular frequency:

$$
\theta(t) = \int_{0}^{t} 2\pi f_i(\tau)\, d\tau = 2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{0}^{t} A_m \cos(2\pi f_m \tau)\, d\tau
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the integral:

$$
\int_{0}^{t} A_m \cos(2\pi f_m \tau)\, d\tau = \frac{A_m}{2\pi f_m} \sin(2\pi f_m t)
$$

**Step 4:** Substitute back:

$$
\theta(t) = 2\pi f_c t + \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m} \sin(2\pi f_m t)
$$

**Step 5:** Recognize $\beta = \frac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$ and write:

$$
\theta(t) = 2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)
$$

**Step 6:** Substitute into cosine carrier:

$$
\boxed{\,s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\big(2\pi f_c t + \beta \sin(2\pi f_m t)\big)\,}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bessel Function Expansion of FM

**Step 1:** Use the identity $\cos(A+B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$:

$$
s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)\cos(\beta \sin 2\pi f_m t) - A_c \sin(2\pi f_c t)\sin(\beta \sin 2\pi f_m t)
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Jacobi–Anger expansion (this is the **KTU favourite**):

$$
\cos(\beta \sin \theta) = J_0(\beta) + 2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} J_{2n}(\beta) \cos(2n\theta)
$$

$$
\sin(\beta \sin \theta) = 2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} J_{2n-1}(\beta) \sin((2n-1)\theta)
$$

where $J_n(\beta)$ are **Bessel functions of the first kind of order $n$**.

**Step 3:** Expand term by term. After algebraic manipulation, we get the canonical FM spectrum:

$$
s_{FM}(t) = A_c \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) \cos\!\big(2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t\big)
$$

**Step 4:** Write explicitly as carrier + sidebands:

$$
s_{FM}(t) = A_c J_0(\beta)\cos(2\pi f_c t) + A_c \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} J_n(\beta) \Big[\cos 2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t + (-1)^n \cos 2\pi(f_c - n f_m)t\Big]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Board Exams:**
> The amplitude of the $n$-th order sideband is $A_c \vert J_n(\beta) \vert$. The **carrier amplitude changes** with $\beta$ — unlike AM where the carrier is fixed! When $J_0(\beta) = 0$ (e.g., $\beta = 2.405$), the carrier disappears entirely. This is asked frequently.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Carson's Rule for Bandwidth

**Step 1:** Bessel coefficients $J_n(\beta)$ become negligibly small when $n > \beta + 1$. Therefore, significant sidebands extend up to order $n = \beta + 1$.

**Step 2:** Total number of significant sideband pairs = $\beta + 1$ on each side of $f_c$.

**Step 3:** Each sideband is spaced $f_m$ Hz from its neighbour, so the spectral extent on **one side** is:

$$
\text{One-sided extent} = (\beta + 1) \cdot f_m
$$

**Step 4:** Total bandwidth (both sides) is doubled:

$$
\boxed{\,BW = 2(\beta + 1) f_m = 2(\Delta f + f_m)\,}
$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power in FM Signal

**Step 1:** Average power of an unmodulated carrier:

$$
P_c = \frac{A_c^2}{2}
$$

**Step 2:** Use Bessel identity $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n^2(\beta) = 1$.

**Step 3:** Total power of FM signal is the sum of squared amplitudes of all spectral components:

$$
P_t = \frac{A_c^2}{2} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} J_n^2(\beta) = \frac{A_c^2}{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_t = \frac{A_c^2}{2} = P_c\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The total transmitted power in FM is **constant and independent of $\beta$**. Power is merely redistributed between the carrier and sidebands as $\beta$ changes. This is a *fundamental* difference from AM and a guaranteed KTU exam question.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — NBFM vs WBFM

| Parameter | Narrowband FM (NBFM) | Wideband FM (WBFM) |
|---|---|---|
| Modulation Index | $\beta \ll 1$ (typically $< 0.5$) | $\beta \gg 1$ (typically $> 5$) |
| Bandwidth | $BW \approx 2f_m$ | $BW \approx 2\Delta f$ |
| Spectral Components | Carrier + 1 pair of sidebands | Carrier + many sidebands |
| Noise Performance | Marginal (similar to AM) | Excellent ($\propto \beta^2$) |
| Use Case | Mobile comms, voice | FM broadcast, Hi-Fi audio |
| Generation | Indirect (Armstrong method) | Direct (VCO method) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 FM Modulation & Demodulation System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Message Signal m of t] --> B[Pre emphasis Filter]
    B --> C[FM Modulator VCO or Armstrong]
    C --> D[Power Amplifier]
    D --> E[Channel with Noise n of t]
    E --> F[RF Amplifier and Limiter]
    F --> G[FM Demodulator PLL or Discriminator]
    G --> H[De emphasis Filter]
    H --> I[Audio Amplifier]
    I --> J[Output Signal m out of t]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Block Diagram — Armstrong Indirect FM Generator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    msg[Message Signal m of t] --> bal[Balanced Modulator]
    osc1[Carrier Oscillator f1] --> bal
    bal --> nbfm[NBFM Output beta is small]
    nbfm --> freq[Frequency Multiplier n times]
    freq --> mixer[Mixer]
    osc2[Local Oscillator f2] --> mixer
    mixer --> shift[Frequency Shifter]
    shift --> out[WBFM Output with high beta]
```

## 4.3 Bessel Function Spectrum — Visual Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FM Signal s of t] --> B[Bessel Expansion]
    B --> C[Carrier J0 of beta at fc]
    B --> D[Sidebands Jn of beta at fc plus or minus n fm]
    C --> E[Spectrum Lines]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Beta small: only J0 J1 significant]
    E --> G[Beta large: many Jn significant]
    F --> H[NBFM: BW approx 2 fm]
    G --> I[WBFM: BW approx 2 Delta f by Carsons Rule]
```

## 4.4 FM Demodulator Hierarchy (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    fmIn[FM Signal at Input] --> lim[Limiter Removes AM Noise]
    lim --> disc[Frequency Discriminator]
    disc --> out[Recovered Message Signal]
    disc --> d1[Slope Detector]
    disc --> d2[Foster Seeley]
    disc --> d3[Ratio Detector]
    disc --> d4[PLL Demodulator]
    d1 --> out
    d2 --> out
    d3 --> out
    d4 --> out
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Limiter?**
> The limiter strips off amplitude variations (caused by noise in the channel), ensuring the discriminator responds *only* to frequency changes — this is the source of FM's superior noise immunity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define Frequency Modulation. Derive the expression for the instantaneous frequency of an FM wave.**

**Model Answer:**
Frequency Modulation is a technique in which the instantaneous frequency of the carrier is varied in proportion to the message signal amplitude, keeping the carrier amplitude constant.

The FM signal is given by:

$$
s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left(2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int_{-\infty}^{t} m(\tau)\, d\tau\right)
$$

The instantaneous angular frequency is:

$$
\omega_i(t) = \frac{d}{dt}\!\left[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int m(\tau)\, d\tau\right] = 2\pi f_c + 2\pi k_f m(t)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)\,}
$$

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State Carson's rule for FM bandwidth. A commercial FM broadcaster uses $\Delta f = 75$ kHz and $f_m = 15$ kHz. Find the bandwidth.**

**Model Answer:**
**Carson's Rule:** $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$

Substituting:

$$
BW = 2(75\,000 + 15\,000) = 2 \times 90\,000 = 180\ \text{kHz}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,BW = 180\ \text{kHz}\,}
$$

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Module 2 carries a 14-mark question with **internal choice**. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### ✍️ **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** For an FM system with message signal $m(t) = 10\cos(2\pi \times 1000\,t)$ and frequency sensitivity $k_f = 25$ kHz/V, determine:
1. Frequency deviation $\Delta f$
2. Modulation index $\beta$
3. Bandwidth using Carson's rule
4. Highest-order significant sideband

**(b) [7 Marks]** Using the Bessel function table, sketch the FM spectrum for $\beta = 2$ and $f_m = 5$ kHz, labelling all significant components.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(a):**

**Given:** $A_m = 10$ V, $f_m = 1000$ Hz, $k_f = 25\,000$ Hz/V.

**Step 1 — Frequency deviation:**

$$
\Delta f = k_f \cdot A_m = 25\,000 \times 10 = 250\,000\ \text{Hz} = 250\ \text{kHz}
$$

**Step 2 — Modulation index:**

$$
\beta = \frac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \frac{250\,000}{1000} = 250
$$

**Step 3 — Carson's Bandwidth:**

$$
BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(250\,000 + 1000) = 502\,000\ \text{Hz} \approx 502\ \text{kHz}
$$

**Step 4 — Highest-order sideband:**

$$
n_{\max} = \lceil \beta \rceil = 250
$$

So significant sidebands exist from $n = 1$ to $n = 250$ on each side of $f_c$.

$$
\boxed{\Delta f = 250\ \text{kHz},\quad \beta = 250,\quad BW = 502\ \text{kHz},\quad n_{\max} = 250}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Bessel coefficients at $\beta = 2$:**

| $n$ | $J_n(2)$ |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.2239 |
| 1 | 0.5767 |
| 2 | 0.3528 |
| 3 | 0.1289 |
| 4 | 0.0340 |

Higher orders ($n \geq 5$) are negligible (drop below 0.01).

**FM Spectrum Components (assuming $A_c = 1$):**

| Frequency | Amplitude | Type |
|---|---|---|
| $f_c$ | $0.2239$ | Carrier |
| $f_c \pm 5$ kHz | $0.5767$ | 1st order sidebands |
| $f_c \pm 10$ kHz | $0.3528$ | 2nd order sidebands |
| $f_c \pm 15$ kHz | $0.1289$ | 3rd order sidebands |
| $f_c \pm 20$ kHz | $0.0340$ | 4th order sidebands |

```
                  CARRIER
                    | 0.224
                    ▼
       ──────|──────────────|──────  f_c
              \   /  \   /  \
               \ /    \ /    \ 
        0.577  /\    0.577  /\
              /  \  / 0.353\  \
        0.353/    \/        \   0.353
             \    /\        /\
              \  /  \      /  \
        0.129  \/    \    /    0.129
               |     |   |     |
        f_c-15 f_c-10 f_c-5  f_c  ...
```

Bandwidth from Bessel analysis: $BW = 2 \times n_{\max} \times f_m = 2 \times 4 \times 5\ \text{kHz} = 40\ \text{kHz}$.

**Verification by Carson's rule:** $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2(2 \times 5 + 5) = 30\ \text{kHz}$ (using $\Delta f = \beta f_m = 10$ kHz).

$$
\boxed{\,BW_{Bessel} = 40\ \text{kHz},\quad BW_{Carson} = 30\ \text{kHz}\,}
$$

The Carson approximation is conservative; the Bessel spectrum gives the more accurate figure.

---

### ✍️ **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With neat block diagrams, explain the **Armstrong indirect method** of FM generation. Why is frequency multiplication necessary?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the operation of a **Phase-Locked Loop (PLL) FM demodulator** with a neat block diagram. Derive the condition for lock.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Armstrong Indirect Method — Step-by-Step Operation:**

**Step 1 — Generation of NBFM:**
A balanced modulator multiplies the message $m(t)$ with a carrier $c(t) = \cos(2\pi f_1 t)$, producing a Double-Sideband Suppressed-Carrier (DSB-SC) signal. By adding a quadrature carrier component, a low-modulation-index NBFM is obtained:

$$
s_{NBFM}(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_1 t) - A_c \beta \sin(2\pi f_1 t) \sin(2\pi f_m t)
$$

**Step 2 — Frequency Multiplication:**
The NBFM has small $\beta_1$ (typically $< 0.5$). To obtain WBFM ($\beta \approx 5$), the frequency is multiplied by $n$ using a nonlinear device followed by a bandpass filter. After multiplication by $n$:

- New modulation index: $\beta_2 = n \beta_1$
- New frequency deviation: $\Delta f_2 = n \Delta f_1$
- New carrier frequency: $f_2 = n f_1$

**Step 3 — Frequency Translation:**
A mixer with local oscillator shifts the carrier to the desired broadcast frequency $f_c$ without altering $\Delta f$.

**Why is frequency multiplication necessary?**
- Indirect method inherently produces very small $\beta$ because the balanced modulator output amplitude is limited.
- Multiplication by $n$ boosts both carrier frequency and $\beta$ by factor $n$, achieving WBFM with $\beta \gg 1$ required for noise immunity.
- Example: If $\beta_1 = 0.05$ and we need $\beta = 5$, then $n = 100$ is required.

$$
\boxed{\,\beta_{out} = n \cdot \beta_{in},\quad \Delta f_{out} = n \cdot \Delta f_{in}\,}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B(b):**

**PLL FM Demodulator — Block Diagram:**

```
   FM Input ──► Phase Detector ──► Loop Filter ──► VCO ──► Output
                  ▲                                       │
                  │                                       │
                  └───────── Feedback Path ───────────────┘
```

**Operation Steps:**

**Step 1 — Phase Detector (PD):**
Multiplies input FM signal $s_{FM}(t)$ with VCO output $v_{VCO}(t)$, producing an error voltage $v_d(t)$ proportional to the phase difference:

$$
v_d(t) = K_d \sin(\phi_e(t))
$$

where $\phi_e(t) = \phi_{FM}(t) - \phi_{VCO}(t)$ and $K_d$ = PD gain (V/rad).

**Step 2 — Loop Filter (LF):**
A low-pass filter removes high-frequency components, giving the control voltage:

$$
v_c(t) = K_f \cdot v_d(t)
$$

**Step 3 — VCO:**
The control voltage $v_c(t)$ shifts VCO frequency:

$$
f_{VCO}(t) = f_0 + K_0 \cdot v_c(t)
$$

**Lock Condition:**
When locked, the VCO instantaneous frequency tracks the FM signal's instantaneous frequency:

$$
f_{VCO}(t) = f_{FM}(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)
$$

Therefore:

$$
v_c(t) = \frac{k_f}{K_0} m(t)
$$

The control voltage $v_c(t)$ is **directly proportional to the message signal** — this *is* the demodulated output.

**Lock range condition:** $\vert \Delta f \vert < K_d K_0 K_f$ (loop bandwidth product).

$$
\boxed{\,v_{out}(t) = \frac{k_f}{K_0}\,m(t)\,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Confusing NBFM and WBFM formulas** — use Carson's rule $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$ only for **WBFM**; for NBFM use $BW \approx 2f_m$.
> 2. **Forgetting units of $\Delta f$ and $f_m$** must match (both Hz) when computing $\beta$.
> 3. **Power calculation errors** — students often wrongly think power changes with $\beta$ in FM. It does **not**; total power is always $A_c^2/2$.
> 4. **Mixing up the role of the limiter** — it removes AM noise, *not* FM deviation. Removing the limiter destroys FM's noise immunity.
> 5. **Bessel table values** must be memorized for $\beta = 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 5$ at least. KTU often asks to draw the spectrum.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **FM = frequency varies with message; amplitude stays constant.**
- ✅ **Instantaneous frequency:** $f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m(t)$.
- ✅ **Frequency deviation:** $\Delta f = k_f A_m$ (a constant, in Hz).
- ✅ **Modulation index:** $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m}$ — the most important single number.
- ✅ **Carson's Rule:** $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m(\beta + 1)$ — universal FM bandwidth estimator.
- ✅ **Bessel expansion:** $s_{FM}(t) = A_c \sum J_n(\beta) \cos[2\pi(f_c + n f_m)t]$.
- ✅ **Total FM power is constant** = $A_c^2/2$, independent of $\beta$.
- ✅ **NBFM ($\beta \ll 1$):** $BW \approx 2 f_m$; **WBFM ($\beta \gg 1$):** $BW \approx 2 \Delta f$.
- ✅ **Carrier can vanish** when $J_0(\beta) = 0$ (e.g., $\beta = 2.405, 5.520, \ldots$).
- ✅ **Generation:** Direct (VCO) or Indirect (Armstrong with frequency multipliers).
- ✅ **Demodulation:** Slope detector, Foster-Seeley, Ratio detector, **PLL** (modern preferred).
- ✅ **Limiter** before discriminator is essential for noise immunity.
- ✅ **Pre-emphasis ($\tau = 75\ \mu s$) at TX, De-emphasis at RX** boosts SNR further.
- ✅ **SNR advantage of FM over AM:** $\dfrac{3}{2}\beta^2$ for WBFM — the famous FM figure of merit.
- ✅ **Application:** FM broadcast band **88–108 MHz** with $\Delta f = 75$ kHz, $f_m^{\max} = 15$ kHz, $\beta_{\max} = 5$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phase Modulation (PM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Phase Modulation (PM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2: PHASE MODULATION (PM)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Phase Modulation (PM)** is a sub-class of **Angle Modulation** (the other being Frequency Modulation) in which the **instantaneous phase** of a high-frequency sinusoidal carrier wave is varied in direct proportion to the amplitude of the baseband (message) signal $m(t)$, while keeping the carrier amplitude and frequency strictly constant.

Mathematically, a PM signal is expressed as:

$$s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + k_p \, m(t)\right]$$

where:
- $A_c$ = **Amplitude of the carrier** (volts)
- $f_c$ = **Carrier frequency** (Hz)
- $k_p$ = **Phase sensitivity / phase deviation constant** (radians per volt, rad/V)
- $m(t)$ = **Message signal** (volts)
- $k_p \cdot m(t)$ = **Instantaneous phase deviation**, denoted $\phi(t)$ in radians

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** PM belongs to the family of **Continuous-Wave (CW) Modulation** techniques. It is the dual of Frequency Modulation (FM) — instead of changing the *frequency* of the carrier, PM changes the *phase* of the carrier in step with the message.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are sitting on a swing in a playground. Your friend is pushing you at a regular rhythm (this rhythm is the **carrier**). Now, your friend is also reading a WhatsApp message on the phone. Every time the message becomes more important or louder in tone, your friend **delays the push** (i.e., shifts the *phase* of the push). The push is still as strong as ever (constant amplitude), still at the same nominal interval (constant frequency), but its **timing offset** keeps changing based on the message content.

**That timing-offset variation IS Phase Modulation.**

- Push strength → analogous to **carrier amplitude** (kept constant in PM)
- Push interval → analogous to **carrier frequency** (kept constant in PM)
- Delay in push timing → analogous to **phase deviation** (varies with $m(t)$)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Summary:** PM encodes information in *when* the carrier wave's peaks occur, not in *how tall* they are or *how often* they repeat. **Height of the wave = fixed**, **gap between waves = fixed**, **only the horizontal shift of peaks = variable.**

### 1.3 Why PM? — Engineering Motivation

- **Constant envelope** ⇒ robust against amplitude-distorting noise & non-linear amplifiers.
- Trades bandwidth for **noise immunity** (similar to FM, but with different spectral roll-off).
- Theoretically important because **PM and FM are mathematically interconvertible** — understanding PM automatically gives FM insight.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conceptual Decomposition of the PM Equation

A single sinusoid can be described by three independent parameters: **Amplitude ($A$)**, **Frequency ($f$)**, and **Phase ($\phi$)**. PM manipulates the third parameter, $\phi$.

Let us dissect $s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t)\right]$ step-by-step:

| Symbol | Meaning | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| $A_c$ | Carrier amplitude | **Held constant** in PM. Does not carry information. |
| $2\pi f_c t$ | Linear carrier phase | The unmodulated phase ramp of a pure sinusoid. |
| $k_p$ | Phase sensitivity | A designer-set constant that scales the message into radians. |
| $m(t)$ | Baseband message | The actual information-bearing signal. |
| $\theta_i(t) = 2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t)$ | Instantaneous total phase | Phase at any instant $t$. |
| $\phi(t) = k_p m(t)$ | Instantaneous phase deviation | Information-bearing component. |
| $\omega_i(t) = \dfrac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt}$ | Instantaneous angular frequency | $\omega_i(t) = 2\pi f_c + k_p \dfrac{dm(t)}{dt}$. |

### 2.2 Instantaneous Frequency in PM — The Hidden Link to FM

A crucial KTU-favourite observation: although PM keeps the *nominal* frequency $f_c$ fixed, the **instantaneous frequency** drifts in proportion to the *derivative* of the message:

$$f_i(t) = f_c + \frac{k_p}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{dm(t)}{dt}$$

This tells us that **PM is mathematically equivalent to FM by a pre-emphasis differentiator.** This is a recurring KTU 14-mark question.

### 2.3 Modulation Index for PM — $\beta$ (also called $\Delta\phi$ or $m_p$)

The **maximum phase deviation** of a PM system is:

$$\beta = k_p \cdot \max \vert m(t) \vert = k_p A_m$$

where $A_m$ is the peak amplitude of the message signal. The unit is **radians**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** For a *single-tone* message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$, the PM signal becomes
> $$s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + \beta \cos(2\pi f_m t)\right]$$
> which is structurally identical to the Bessel-function expansion of FM, with $\beta$ taking the role of the FM modulation index $m_f$.

### 2.4 Power Content of a PM Signal

Because the envelope of $s_{PM}(t)$ is identically $A_c$ at every instant, the **average transmitted power is constant** and equal to the unmodulated carrier power:

$$P_{PM} = \frac{A_c^2}{2}$$

This is independent of the modulation index $\beta$ — a key reason PM is favoured in nonlinear power amplifiers.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PM waveform | $s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t)\right]$ | Volts | Fundamental definition |
| 2 | Instantaneous phase deviation | $\phi(t) = k_p m(t)$ | rad | Information term |
| 3 | Phase sensitivity | $k_p$ | rad/V | System constant |
| 4 | Modulation index (PM) | $\beta = k_p \cdot A_m$ | rad | Peak phase deviation |
| 5 | Instantaneous angular frequency | $\omega_i(t) = 2\pi f_c + k_p \dfrac{dm(t)}{dt}$ | rad/s | Reveals FM link |
| 6 | Instantaneous frequency | $f_i(t) = f_c + \dfrac{k_p}{2\pi} \cdot \dfrac{dm(t)}{dt}$ | Hz | — |
| 7 | Frequency deviation (PM) | $\Delta f = \dfrac{k_p}{2\pi} \cdot A_m \, f_m$ | Hz | Depends on $f_m$ |
| 8 | Carson's Rule (PM bandwidth) | $BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$ | Hz | Upper bound |
| 9 | Average transmitted power | $P_t = A_c^2 / 2$ | W | Independent of $\beta$ |
| 10 | PM → FM equivalence | Differentiate $m(t)$ then apply FM modulator | — | Critical KTU relationship |
| 11 | FM → PM equivalence | Integrate $m(t)$ then apply PM modulator | — | Critical KTU relationship |
| 12 | Spectral components | $\cos\!\left[\beta \cos(2\pi f_m t)\right] = J_0(\beta) + 2\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} (-1)^n J_{2n}(\beta)\cos(4\pi n f_m t)$ | — | Bessel expansion |
| 13 | $\sin\!\left[\beta \cos(2\pi f_m t)\right] = -2\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (-1)^n J_{2n+1}(\beta)\cos\!\left[2\pi(2n+1) f_m t\right]$ | — | — | Bessel expansion |
| 14 | Narrowband PM bandwidth | $BW \approx 2 f_m$ | Hz | $\beta \ll 1$ rad |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip (KTU 2024):** In the above table, $\vert m(t) \vert$ has been deliberately written without vertical pipes to keep the markdown table valid. In your answer sheet, write it as $|m(t)|$.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Satellite communication** (constant-envelope signals pass cleanly through TWTA amplifiers).
- **Mobile radio** (GSM originally used GMSK, a derivative of PM).
- **Modem signalling** (PSK family: BPSK, QPSK, 8-PSK are digital descendants of PM).
- **Radar & telemetry** (where phase coherence between transmitter and reference is exploited).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — PM Waveform from First Principles

**Goal:** Start from a generic angle-modulated signal and specialize to PM.

**Step 1 — Start with the canonical angle-modulation form:**

A general angle-modulated signal is written as

$$s(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[\theta_i(t)\right]$$

where $\theta_i(t)$ is the **instantaneous total phase** in radians.

**Step 2 — Apply the fundamental relation between instantaneous frequency and phase:**

The instantaneous angular frequency is, by definition, the time-derivative of the total phase:

$$\omega_i(t) = \frac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — For a *pure* carrier (no modulation), $\theta_i(t) = 2\pi f_c t$, so $\omega_i(t) = 2\pi f_c$.** This is the baseline. Any modulation must add an extra phase term.

**Step 4 — Define the *phase deviation* $\phi(t)$ as the additional phase produced by the message:**

$$\theta_i(t) = 2\pi f_c t + \phi(t)$$

**Step 5 — Encode the message in the phase.** By the definition of Phase Modulation, the additional phase is **directly proportional to the instantaneous value of $m(t)$:**

$$\phi(t) = k_p \, m(t)$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back into the canonical form:**

$$\boxed{\,s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t)\right]\,}$$

**Sanity check:** If $m(t)=0$ (no message), the expression reduces to $A_c \cos(2\pi f_c t)$, which is the unmodulated carrier. ✔

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Instantaneous Frequency of a PM Signal

**Step 1 — Begin with the PM waveform:**

$$s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Identify the argument of the cosine as the instantaneous total phase:**

$$\theta_i(t) = 2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t)$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate to get the instantaneous angular frequency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_i(t) &= \frac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt} \\
            &= \frac{d}{dt}\!\left[2\pi f_c t\right] + \frac{d}{dt}\!\left[k_p m(t)\right] \\
            &= 2\pi f_c + k_p \, \frac{dm(t)}{dt}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to ordinary frequency in Hz by dividing by $2\pi$:**

$$f_i(t) = f_c + \frac{k_p}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{dm(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** The instantaneous frequency of a PM signal *deviates* from the carrier by an amount that is proportional to the *time-derivative* of the message. This is the central reason PM and FM are interconvertible.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Modulation Index for Single-Tone PM

**Step 1 — Take a sinusoidal message of peak amplitude $A_m$ and frequency $f_m$:**

$$m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the PM expression:**

$$s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + k_p A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Define the modulation index $\beta$ as the *peak* value of the phase deviation term:**

$$\beta \equiv k_p A_m \quad \text{(units: radians)}$$

**Step 4 — Re-write the signal compactly:**

$$s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + \beta \cos(2\pi f_m t)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Note the structural similarity to wideband FM (where the modulation index is $m_f = \Delta f / f_m$).** For PM, the role of $m_f$ is played by $\beta$, but the *Bessel-function spectral structure is identical in form.*

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Frequency Deviation $\Delta f$ for PM

**Step 1 — From the instantaneous frequency result:**

$$f_i(t) = f_c + \frac{k_p}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{dm(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Take a single-tone message $m(t) = A_m \cos(2\pi f_m t)$:**

$$\frac{dm(t)}{dt} = -2\pi f_m A_m \sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute:**

$$f_i(t) = f_c + \frac{k_p}{2\pi}\!\left[-2\pi f_m A_m \sin(2\pi f_m t)\right] = f_c - k_p A_m f_m \sin(2\pi f_m t)$$

**Step 4 — Identify the peak deviation (when $\sin(2\pi f_m t) = -1$):**

$$\boxed{\,\Delta f = k_p A_m f_m\,}$$

**Step 5 — This is a critical KTU distinction: for FM, $\Delta f$ is independent of $f_m$; for PM, $\Delta f$ grows linearly with $f_m$.**

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Carson's Rule for PM Bandwidth

**Step 1 — Carson's rule (universal form for any angle modulation):**

$$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the PM-specific $\Delta f = k_p A_m f_m$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
BW &= 2\!\left(k_p A_m f_m + f_m\right) \\
   &= 2 f_m (k_p A_m + 1) \\
   &= 2 f_m (\beta + 1)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Two limiting cases:**

- *Narrowband PM* ($\beta \ll 1$): $BW \to 2 f_m$ (same as AM — DSB-SC).
- *Wideband PM* ($\beta \gg 1$): $BW \to 2 \beta f_m$ (similar scaling to wideband FM).

---

### 3.6 Derivation 6 — Power of a PM Signal

**Step 1 — The instantaneous power dissipated in a 1-Ω load is:**

$$p(t) = \frac{s_{PM}^2(t)}{1} = A_c^2 \cos^2\!\left[2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Average over one carrier period $T_c = 1/f_c$:**

$$P = \langle p(t) \rangle = \frac{A_c^2}{T_c} \int_0^{T_c} \cos^2\!\left[2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t)\right] dt$$

**Step 3 — Use the identity $\cos^2(x) = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[1 + \cos(2x)\right]$:**

$$P = \frac{A_c^2}{2 T_c} \int_0^{T_c} \!\left[1 + \cos\!\left(4\pi f_c t + 2k_p m(t)\right)\right] dt$$

**Step 4 — The cosine term averages to zero over a complete carrier period (it is a high-frequency oscillation):**

$$P = \frac{A_c^2}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{T_c} \int_0^{T_c} 1 \, dt = \frac{A_c^2}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Crucial conclusion:** The average power is **independent** of $k_p$ and $m(t)$ — only carrier amplitude matters. This is why PM is so power-efficient.

---

### 3.7 Derivation 7 — PM and FM Equivalence (KTU Favourite)

**Claim:** A PM modulator with message $m(t)$ and phase sensitivity $k_p$ is *mathematically equivalent* to an FM modulator with message $m_1(t) = \dfrac{dm(t)}{dt}$ and frequency sensitivity $k_f = \dfrac{k_p}{2\pi}$.

**Proof:**

**Step 1 — Begin with PM form:**

$$s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t)\right]$$

**Step 2 — From Section 3.2, the instantaneous frequency is:**

$$f_i(t) = f_c + \frac{k_p}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{dm(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the general FM form:**

$$f_i(t) = f_c + k_f \, m_1(t)$$

**Step 4 — Matching coefficients:**

$$k_f \, m_1(t) \equiv \frac{k_p}{2\pi} \cdot \frac{dm(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 5 — The most natural choice is to set $m_1(t) = \dfrac{dm(t)}{dt}$ and $k_f = \dfrac{k_p}{2\pi}$:**

$$\boxed{\,\text{PM}\{m(t),\, k_p\} \;\equiv\; \text{FM}\!\left\{\frac{dm(t)}{dt},\, \frac{k_p}{2\pi}\right\}\,}$$

**Step 6 — Block-diagram consequence:**

> Differentiator → FM Modulator = PM Modulator.

The reverse is also true: an **integrator → PM modulator = FM modulator.** This duality is heavily tested in KTU 2024 module-2 questions.

---

### 3.8 Analytical Comparison Matrix: PM vs FM

| Property | Phase Modulation (PM) | Frequency Modulation (FM) |
|---|---|---|
| Information parameter | Phase $\phi(t) = k_p m(t)$ | Frequency deviation $\propto m(t)$ |
| Modulation index | $\beta = k_p A_m$ (rad) | $m_f = \dfrac{\Delta f}{f_m} = \dfrac{k_f A_m}{f_m}$ (dimensionless) |
| $\Delta f$ depends on $f_m$? | **Yes** — $\Delta f = k_p A_m f_m$ | **No** — $\Delta f = k_f A_m$ (constant) |
| Equivalent to | FM of $\dfrac{dm(t)}{dt}$ | PM of $\int m(t)\,dt$ |
| Bandwidth (Carson) | $2 f_m (\beta + 1)$ | $2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m (m_f + 1)$ |
| Average power | $\dfrac{A_c^2}{2}$ (constant) | $\dfrac{A_c^2}{2}$ (constant) |
| Envelope | **Constant** | **Constant** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 High-Level PM Transmitter Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Message Signal m of t] --> B[Phase Modulator]
    B --> C[Amplifier stage optional]
    C --> D[Antenna for transmission]
    B -.- E[Local Oscillator at f sub c] -.-> B
    classDef src fill:#FFD580,stroke:#A0522D,color:#000
    classDef mod fill:#7EC8E3,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef tx fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    class A,E src
    class B,C mod
    class D tx
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `A` (message source) feeds the Phase Modulator `B`.
- A stable local oscillator `E` at carrier frequency $f_c$ provides the carrier reference.
- The modulator produces $s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t)]$.
- A power amplifier `C` (often nonlinear, e.g., Class-C) raises the level.
- The antenna `D` radiates the constant-envelope PM waveform.

### 4.2 PM ↔ FM Equivalence Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EQUIVALENCE ["PM and FM Interconvertibility"]
        direction TB
        X1[Message m of t] --> Y1[Differentiator]
        Y1 --> Z1[FM Modulator with k sub f equals k sub p over 2 pi]
        Z1 --> OUT1[Output identical to PM signal]
        X2[Message m of t] --> Y2[Integrator]
        Y2 --> Z2[PM Modulator with k sub p]
        Z2 --> OUT2[Output identical to FM signal]
    end
    classDef blk fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#8B7500,color:#000
    class X1,X2,Y1,Y2,Z1,Z2,OUT1,OUT2 blk
```

### 4.3 PM Receiver (Coherent Demodulation) Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Received PM signal r of t] --> M[Multiplier / Mixer]
    M --> LPF[Low Pass Filter]
    LPF --> V[Envelope / Phase Detector output equals k sub p m of t]
    M -.- LO[Local oscillator locked to f sub c] -.-> M
    classDef rx fill:#D8BFD8,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef sys fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    class R,LO rx
    class M,LPF,V sys
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Architectural Note:** Because $k_p m(t)$ is *inside* the cosine, a simple envelope detector **cannot** recover the message. A **coherent (synchronous) detector** multiplying the received PM signal by a locally generated $\cos(2\pi f_c t)$ reference is required, followed by low-pass filtering.

### 4.4 PM vs FM — Decision Tree for Engineers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Choose Angle Modulation Scheme] --> Q1{Message has strong low frequency content?}
    Q1 -- YES --> PM[Use PM with integrator at receiver to boost low frequency SNR]
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Message spectrum roughly flat?}
    Q2 -- YES --> FM[Use FM for constant deviation and simpler demodulation]
    Q2 -- NO --> NB[Use Narrowband PM for bandwidth limited links]
    classDef step fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef dec fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#483D8B,color:#000
    class S,Q1,Q2 step
    class PM,FM,NB dec
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define Phase Modulation. Write the mathematical expression for a PM signal and identify the role of the phase sensitivity constant $k_p$.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Phase Modulation is a form of angle modulation in which the **instantaneous phase** of the carrier is varied linearly with the instantaneous amplitude of the message signal, keeping amplitude and frequency constant.

$$s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t)\right]$$

The constant $k_p$ is the **phase sensitivity** (units: rad/V). It converts the message signal's voltage into a proportional phase shift, i.e., $k_p$ m(t) is the instantaneous phase deviation in radians.

*Mark split: Definition (1) + equation (1) + role of k_p (1).*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

State **Carson's rule** for the transmission bandwidth of an angle-modulated signal. For a PM system with modulation index $\beta = 4$ and message frequency $f_m = 2$ kHz, compute the bandwidth.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Carson's rule:

$$BW = 2(\Delta f + f_m) = 2 f_m (\beta + 1)$$

Substituting $\beta = 4$ and $f_m = 2$ kHz:

$$BW = 2 \times 2\,\text{kHz} \times (4 + 1) = 20 \text{ kHz}$$

*Mark split: Statement (1) + substitution (1) + answer with unit (1).*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Internal Choice Note (KTU 2024 ESE):** Students must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B in full.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **instantaneous frequency** of a Phase Modulated signal. Show that PM is mathematically equivalent to Frequency Modulation of the *derivative* of the message signal. (7 marks)

**(b)** A PM modulator has a phase sensitivity $k_p = 5$ rad/V. The message signal is $m(t) = 3\cos(2000\pi t)$ volts.
   - (i) Determine the **modulation index** $\beta$.
   - (ii) Calculate the **maximum phase deviation** in degrees.
   - (iii) Compute the **frequency deviation** $\Delta f$.
   - (iv) Apply Carson's rule to find the **transmission bandwidth**. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of instantaneous frequency and PM–FM equivalence:**

Instantaneous total phase:

$$\theta_i(t) = 2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t)$$

Differentiating to obtain instantaneous angular frequency:

$$\begin{aligned}
\omega_i(t) &= \frac{d\theta_i(t)}{dt} \\
            &= 2\pi f_c + k_p \frac{dm(t)}{dt}
\end{aligned}$$

Dividing by $2\pi$ to obtain frequency in Hz:

$$f_i(t) = f_c + \frac{k_p}{2\pi}\frac{dm(t)}{dt}$$

Comparing with the FM definition $f_i(t) = f_c + k_f m_1(t)$:

$$k_f m_1(t) \equiv \frac{k_p}{2\pi}\frac{dm(t)}{dt} \;\Rightarrow\; m_1(t) = \frac{dm(t)}{dt}, \; k_f = \frac{k_p}{2\pi}$$

**Conclusion:** A PM modulator with input $m(t)$ and constant $k_p$ is mathematically equivalent to an FM modulator with input $\dfrac{dm(t)}{dt}$ and frequency sensitivity $k_f = k_p/(2\pi)$. **(7 marks: 3 for differentiation, 2 for FM comparison, 2 for conclusion.)**

---

**(b) Numerical computation:**

Given $k_p = 5$ rad/V, $m(t) = 3\cos(2000\pi t)$, hence $A_m = 3$ V, $f_m = 1000$ Hz.

**(i) Modulation index:**

$$\beta = k_p A_m = 5 \times 3 = 15 \text{ rad}$$

**(ii) Maximum phase deviation in degrees:**

$$\Delta\phi_{\max} = \beta = 15 \text{ rad} = 15 \times \frac{180°}{\pi} \approx 859.4°$$

**(iii) Frequency deviation:**

$$\Delta f = k_p A_m f_m = 5 \times 3 \times 1000 = 15{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 15 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iv) Carson's-rule bandwidth:**

$$BW = 2 f_m(\beta + 1) = 2 \times 1000 \times (15 + 1) = 32{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 32 \text{ kHz}$$

*Mark split: (i) 1.5, (ii) 1.5, (iii) 2, (iv) 2. (7 marks total.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain how a **PM modulator** can be implemented using a **differentiator followed by an FM modulator**. Justify the equivalence mathematically. (7 marks)

**(b)** A single-tone PM signal is described by

$$s(t) = 10 \cos\!\left[2\pi \times 10^6 t + 4 \cos(2000\pi t)\right]$$

Determine:
   - (i) Carrier amplitude and carrier frequency.
   - (ii) Modulation index $\beta$ and message frequency.
   - (iii) Maximum phase deviation (in radians and degrees).
   - (iv) Bandwidth using Carson's rule. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) PM generation via Differentiator + FM Modulator:**

**Block diagram:** Message $m(t) \rightarrow$ Differentiator $\rightarrow$ FM Modulator $\rightarrow$ PM Signal.

**Mathematical justification:** The FM modulator's output is:

$$s_{FM}(t) = A_c \cos\!\left[2\pi f_c t + 2\pi k_f \int m_1(t)\,dt\right]$$

If we feed $m_1(t) = \dfrac{dm(t)}{dt}$ into the FM input, then:

$$2\pi k_f \int \frac{dm(t)}{dt}\,dt = 2\pi k_f \, m(t)$$

Setting $2\pi k_f = k_p$ gives the standard PM expression. Hence a differentiator–FM chain is a perfect PM modulator. **(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 3 for mathematics, 2 for conclusion.)**

---

**(b) Numerical extraction from the given waveform:**

Given: $s(t) = 10 \cos\!\left[2\pi \times 10^6 t + 4 \cos(2000\pi t)\right]$

**(i) Carrier amplitude and frequency:**

$$A_c = 10 \text{ V}, \qquad f_c = 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ MHz}$$

**(ii) Modulation index and message frequency:**

The argument of the cosine is $2\pi f_c t + \beta\cos(2\pi f_m t)$, so by inspection:

$$\beta = 4 \text{ rad}, \qquad 2\pi f_m = 2000\pi \;\Rightarrow\; f_m = 1000 \text{ Hz}$$

**(iii) Maximum phase deviation:**

$$\Delta\phi_{\max} = \beta = 4 \text{ rad} = 4 \times \frac{180°}{\pi} \approx 229.18°$$

**(iv) Carson's-rule bandwidth:**

$$BW = 2 f_m(\beta + 1) = 2 \times 1000 \times (4 + 1) = 10{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 10 \text{ kHz}$$

*Mark split: (i) 1.5, (ii) 2, (iii) 1.5, (iv) 2. (7 marks total.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (as flagged by KTU board examiners):**
> 1. **Confusing PM modulation index with FM modulation index.** For PM: $\beta = k_p A_m$ (radians). For FM: $m_f = k_f A_m / f_m$ (dimensionless). Many students write the same formula for both — **lose 1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to express phase deviation in degrees** when the question demands it. Always convert: $\text{rad} \times 180/\pi = \text{degrees}$.
> 3. **Using $|m(t)|$ in a markdown table will break the table layout.** In answer sheets, this is fine, but in your rough notes always use $\vert m(t) \vert$.
> 4. **Assuming Carson's rule is only for FM** — it applies to *all* angle modulation including PM, with the appropriate $\Delta f$ substituted.
> 5. **Stating the PM waveform without the cosine brackets** (e.g., writing $A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t)]$ vs $A_c \cos 2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t)$) — the latter is **mathematically wrong** and costs 1 mark.
> 6. **Power calculation:** Students often write $P = A_c$ instead of $P = A_c^2/2$ — re-derive the cosine-squared identity if unsure.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PM definition:** $s_{PM}(t) = A_c \cos[2\pi f_c t + k_p m(t)]$.
- **Phase deviation:** $\phi(t) = k_p m(t)$ (the information carrier).
- **Modulation index:** $\beta = k_p A_m$ (radians).
- **Instantaneous frequency:** $f_i(t) = f_c + \dfrac{k_p}{2\pi}\dfrac{dm(t)}{dt}$.
- **Frequency deviation (PM):** $\Delta f = k_p A_m f_m$ — *depends on $f_m$* (unlike FM).
- **Carson's rule for PM:** $BW = 2 f_m (\beta + 1)$.
- **Average power:** $P = A_c^2/2$ — *independent of $k_p$ and $m(t)$*.
- **PM ↔ FM equivalence:** Differentiate message then FM = PM; Integrate message then PM = FM.
- **Constant envelope:** PM waveform envelope is always $A_c$ — robust against non-linear amplification.
- **Demodulation requires coherent (synchronous) detection**, not envelope detection.
- **Spectral structure (single-tone PM):** Identical Bessel-function pattern to wideband FM, with $f_c \pm n f_m$ components whose amplitudes are $J_n(\beta)$.
- **KTU 2024 exam focus:** Questions typically test (a) the PM–FM duality, (b) numerical evaluation of $\beta$, $\Delta f$ and $BW$, and (c) justification of constant-envelope behaviour.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiplexing - Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multiplexing - Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)** is an analog multiplexing technique that permits the simultaneous transmission of multiple independent baseband signals over a single physical transmission medium by assigning each signal a **distinct, non-overlapping frequency band** within the total available bandwidth of the channel. Each input signal is first modulated onto a separate **carrier frequency** (sub-carrier) so that its spectral content is translated into a unique slot of the frequency spectrum, and the modulated outputs are then linearly summed (combined) by a multiplexer before being launched into the common channel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Snapshot:** FDM is a channel-access method in which the bandwidth of the physical medium is divided into several **non-overlapping frequency sub-bands**, each carrying one independent information signal. The bandwidth of the composite channel must equal at least the **sum of bandwidths** of all input signals plus the **guard bands** required to prevent inter-channel interference.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Radio Highway" Model

Imagine a multi-lane highway where each lane is reserved for a particular type of vehicle. Cars travelling in Lane 1 (frequency band 1), Lane 2 (frequency band 2), etc., can all move simultaneously without colliding because they occupy **different physical positions along the width of the road**. In FDM, the "width of the highway" is the **bandwidth of the channel**, the "lanes" are **frequency slots**, and the "cars" are the **information signals**. As long as the lanes are clearly separated by a small empty strip (the **guard band**), the vehicles (signals) never crash into one another.

A second analogy: think of a classroom where the teacher asks 5 students to talk at the same time, but each student is given a **different musical pitch** (low, medium-low, medium, medium-high, high). A trained listener can filter out all but the pitch they want and hear only one student clearly. FDM works in exactly this manner — the receiver uses **band-pass filters** tuned to specific pitches (frequencies) to isolate one conversation from the rest.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Voice Channel Bandwidth:** **4 kHz** (3 kHz usable + 0.5 kHz guard on each side, ITU-T standard).
- **Typical Guard Band Width:** **0.5 kHz to 1 kHz** between adjacent voice channels in FDM telephony.
- **Carrier Frequency Stability Required:** within **±1 Hz** for voice-grade FDM links.
- **Composite Signal Bandwidth (general):** $B_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} B_i + (N-1) \cdot B_{guard}$

> [!NOTE]
> The phrase **"non-overlapping frequency bands"** is the heart of every FDM definition. If the bands overlap, the system collapses into ordinary frequency mixing and cross-talk dominates. Examiners often award the full 3 marks for a definition that uses the words *"non-overlapping"*, *"simultaneous"*, and *"separate carrier"* in the same sentence.

## 1.4 Why FDM Exists — The Motivation

In the early days of telephony (1940s–1960s), laying one copper pair per conversation was **economically ruinous**. AT&T's carrier systems (Type-A, Type-J, Type-K, Type-N) showed that by stacking 12, 24, or more voice channels onto **one physical pair** through FDM, the cost per voice channel dropped dramatically. The same principle now governs **FM radio broadcasting (88–108 MHz)**, **analog TV transmission**, and **satellite transponder allocation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The FDM Operational Pipeline (Step-by-Step Logic)

The complete FDM transmission process can be decomposed into **eight well-defined stages**. Understanding each stage is essential for KTU problems on block diagrams and for viva-voce questions.

1. **Source Signal Generation:** Each of the *N* sources produces an independent baseband message signal $m_i(t)$ with bandwidth $B_i$.
2. **Pre-filtering (Anti-alias / Anti-overlap):** Each $m_i(t)$ is passed through a **low-pass filter (LPF)** to strictly limit its bandwidth to $B_i$ and prevent spectral leakage into adjacent slots.
3. **Carrier Modulation:** Each filtered signal modulates a unique carrier $c_i(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_i t)$. The modulation may be **AM, DSB-SC, SSB, FM, or PM** — KTU typically assumes **SSB-SC** for telephony because of bandwidth efficiency.
4. **Frequency Translation:** The spectrum of $m_i(t)$ is shifted from baseband to the band $[f_i - B_i, \, f_i + B_i]$ (or $[f_i, f_i + B_i]$ for SSB).
5. **Guard Band Insertion:** A small unused frequency gap $B_g$ is reserved on either side of every translated band to absorb filter roll-off imperfections and prevent adjacent-channel interference (ACI).
6. **Linear Summation (Multiplexing):** The *N* modulated signals are added by a **summing amplifier** to form the composite FDM signal:
   $$s_{FDM}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} s_i(t)$$
7. **Channel Transmission:** The composite signal propagates through the shared medium (copper pair, coax, radio link, optical fibre — note that optical FDM is called WDM).
8. **Demultiplexing & Demodulation:** At the receiver, **band-pass filters (BPF)** centred on $\{f_1, f_2, \dots, f_N\}$ separate each sub-band, and individual demodulators recover the original messages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** Whenever a question asks "draw the block diagram of FDM transmitter/receiver," examiners expect **N parallel branches**, each containing an **LPF → Modulator → BPF** chain on the transmitter side, and a chain of **BPF → Demodulator → LPF** on the receiver side. Always label the carrier frequencies $f_1 < f_2 < \dots < f_N$.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Stage

- **Pre-filtering:** Real-world signals (speech, music) have **unbounded spectra**. Without LPF, the high-frequency tails would smear into neighbouring channels.
- **Modulation:** Translates every baseband signal to a higher, unique frequency slot. The mathematical operator is multiplication by $\cos(2\pi f_i t)$, which by the modulation theorem shifts the spectrum.
- **Guard Bands:** Practical BPFs have **finite roll-off** (typically 20–60 dB/decade). Guard bands absorb the residual energy of the filter skirts.
- **Linear Summation:** Required because FDM is a **linear** technique — the composite signal must preserve the individual spectra intact for separation at the receiver. (TDM, in contrast, is a **time-domain** linear sampling technique.)
- **BPF Demultiplexing:** The receiver exploits the **orthogonality** of sinusoids on disjoint frequency intervals — i.e., $\int \cos(2\pi f_i t)\cos(2\pi f_j t)dt = 0$ for $i \neq j$ over sufficiently long intervals.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are routinely tested in KTU University Examination Part A and Part B questions.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Remarks / Boundary Condition |
|---|-------------------|-----------|-------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Composite bandwidth of FDM system | $B_{total} = N \cdot B_{ch} + (N-1) \cdot B_g$ | Hz | $N$ = number of channels, $B_{ch}$ = per-channel BW, $B_g$ = guard band |
| 2 | Per-channel bandwidth for SSB voice | $B_{ch} = 4 \text{ kHz}$ | kHz | ITU-T standard voice channel |
| 3 | Carrier-frequency spacing | $\Delta f = B_{ch} + B_g$ | Hz | Minimum spacing between adjacent carriers |
| 4 | Highest carrier frequency | $f_N = f_1 + (N-1)\Delta f$ | Hz | Used to compute total bandwidth |
| 5 | Number of channels that fit in bandwidth $B$ | $N = \left\lfloor \dfrac{B - B_g}{B_{ch} + B_g} \right\rfloor$ | integer | Floor function ensures integer count |
| 6 | Hierarchical grouping (FDM telephony) | Group = 12 voice channels; Supergroup = 60; Mastergroup = 600; Jumbo group = 3600 | channels | Standard AT&T/Bell hierarchy |
| 7 | Required SNR for telephony | $\text{SNR}_{min} \approx 40 \text{ dB}$ | dB | For acceptable voice quality (ITU-T) |
| 8 | FDM guard-band to channel-BW ratio | $\dfrac{B_g}{B_{ch}} \approx 0.10$ to $0.25$ | dimensionless | Empirical design rule |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Symbol Trap:** The KTU platform renders markdown tables strictly. Never write $\vert x \vert$ inside a table row — always use `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert` in LaTeX form, otherwise the table breaks visually on the examiner's PDF.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Public Switched Telephone Network (PSTN):** Carrier systems still use FDM up to the local exchange, after which TDM takes over.
- **FM Radio Broadcasting (88–108 MHz):** Each station occupies a **200 kHz** slot — this is FDM applied to RF.
- **Analog Cable Television:** The 54–1000 MHz coax spectrum is divided into **6 MHz** channels (US NTSC) or **8 MHz** channels (PAL), each carrying one TV signal — textbook FDM.
- **Satellite Communication (C-band, Ku-band, Ka-band):** A single transponder's bandwidth (e.g., 36 MHz or 72 MHz) is sub-divided by FDM to carry thousands of voice circuits.
- **First-Generation (1G) Mobile Telephony:** Used FDMA (Frequency Division Multiple Access) — the multiple-access extension of FDM.
- **Optical Communications:** Wavelength Division Multiplexing (WDM) is the **optical-frequency analogue** of FDM.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Composite Bandwidth of an FDM System

**Problem Statement:** An FDM system multiplexes $N$ independent voice channels. Each voice channel has a usable bandwidth of $B_{ch} = 4$ kHz. To prevent adjacent-channel interference, a guard band of $B_g$ is inserted between every pair of channels. Derive a closed-form expression for the **total composite bandwidth** $B_{total}$ required at the output of the multiplexer.

### Step-by-Step Exhaustive Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the spectral layout.**
Place the first channel at the lowest frequency position, the second immediately above it (with a guard band), and so on. The number of *guard bands* equals the number of *gaps between channels*, which is $(N-1)$. There is **no** guard band required above the topmost channel or below the lowest for the purpose of this standard formula.

**Step 2 — Write the per-channel spectral width.**
Each channel occupies $B_{ch}$ Hz in the spectrum.

**Step 3 — Count the contributions.**
- Number of channels contributing bandwidth: $N$
- Number of guard bands contributing width: $(N-1)$
- Each guard band has width $B_g$.

**Step 4 — Form the algebraic sum.**
The total bandwidth is the sum of all channel bandwidths plus the sum of all guard band widths:

$$B_{total} = N \cdot B_{ch} + (N-1) \cdot B_g$$

**Step 5 — Simplify by expansion.**

$$\begin{aligned}
B_{total} &= N \cdot B_{ch} + (N-1) \cdot B_g \\
&= N \cdot B_{ch} + N \cdot B_g - B_g \\
&= N (B_{ch} + B_g) - B_g
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Boundary Check.**
- If $N = 1$ (no multiplexing), $B_{total} = B_{ch}$ ✓ (single-channel bandwidth, no guard band needed).
- If $B_g = 0$ (ideal filter with infinite roll-off), $B_{total} = N \cdot B_{ch}$ ✓ (channels can touch).
- As $N \to \infty$, $B_{total} \approx N(B_{ch} + B_g)$ — the $-B_g$ term becomes negligible.

**Step 7 — Final Compact Form.**

$$\boxed{\,B_{total} = N \cdot B_{ch} + (N-1) \cdot B_g\,}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Number of Channels in a Given Bandwidth

**Problem Statement:** A radio link has a usable total bandwidth of $B$ Hz. If each voice channel requires $B_{ch}$ Hz and each guard band is $B_g$ Hz, derive the maximum integer number of channels $N$ that can be multiplexed.

**Step 1 — Begin with the bandwidth equation.**

$$B \geq N \cdot B_{ch} + (N-1) \cdot B_g$$

**Step 2 — Expand and group terms in $N$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
B &\geq N \cdot B_{ch} + N \cdot B_g - B_g \\
B + B_g &\geq N (B_{ch} + B_g)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Isolate $N$.**

$$N \leq \frac{B + B_g}{B_{ch} + B_g}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the integer constraint.**
Because $N$ must be a whole number, take the floor:

$$N_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{B + B_g}{B_{ch} + B_g} \right\rfloor$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent alternative form.**
Many textbooks equivalently write:

$$N_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{B - B_g}{B_{ch} + B_g} \right\rfloor + 1$$

This is obtained by absorbing the $+1$ count of channels. Both are correct; choose the form that matches the data given in the problem.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — FDM Hierarchy (Telephony)

**Problem Statement:** A long-haul FDM telephone system stacks voice channels into progressively larger groups. Each voice channel occupies 4 kHz. Each group contains 12 voice channels. Each supergroup contains 5 groups. Derive the bandwidth of one group, one supergroup, and one mastergroup (10 supergroups), assuming a 0.5 kHz guard band between voice channels and a 1 kHz guard band between groups.

**Step 1 — Bandwidth of one voice channel (with its own guard band).**
Channel pitch = $B_{ch} + B_g = 4 + 0.5 = 4.5$ kHz per voice slot.

**Step 2 — Bandwidth of one group of 12 voice channels.**

$$B_{group} = 12 \times 4.5 - 0.5 = 54 - 0.5 = 53.5 \text{ kHz}$$

(Subtracting the last guard band gives the standard **48 kHz** for the 12 voice channels plus internal guards.)

Equivalently: $B_{group} = 12 \times 4 + 11 \times 0.5 = 48 + 5.5 = 53.5$ kHz. The usable **12-channel group** is conventionally quoted as occupying **48 kHz** (60–108 kHz in the spectrum).

**Step 3 — Bandwidth of one supergroup (5 groups).**

$$B_{supergroup} = 5 \times B_{group} + 4 \times B_{g,group}$$

With $B_{g,group} = 8$ kHz (standard inter-group guard):

$$B_{supergroup} = 5 \times 48 + 4 \times 8 = 240 + 32 = 272 \text{ kHz}$$

The conventional value is **240 kHz** (carrying 60 voice channels), occupying the band **312–552 kHz**.

**Step 4 — Bandwidth of one mastergroup (10 supergroups).**

$$B_{mastergroup} = 10 \times 240 = 2400 \text{ kHz} = 2.4 \text{ MHz}$$

This carries **600 voice channels**.

**Step 5 — Mastergroup bandwidth for a "jumbo group" of 6 mastergroups.**

$$B_{jumbo} = 6 \times 2400 = 14400 \text{ kHz} = 14.4 \text{ MHz}$$

This carries **3600 voice channels** — the largest standard FDM aggregate used in intercontinental submarine cable systems.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — FDM vs TDM vs WDM

| Parameter | FDM | TDM | WDM |
|-----------|-----|-----|-----|
| Domain | Frequency | Time | Wavelength (≈ Frequency at optical scale) |
| Signal Type | Analog (originally) | Digital | Optical |
| Synchronization | Not required | Strictly required | Not strictly required |
| Guard Mechanism | Frequency guard band | Time guard interval | Wavelength guard channel |
| Bandwidth Efficiency | Lower (due to large guard bands) | Higher | High |
| Crosstalk Susceptibility | High (filter non-ideal) | Low | Moderate |
| Hardware Cost | Modulators, BPFs | Fast switches, clock recovery | Lasers, prisms/gratings |
| KTU Mark Weightage | 7–14 marks | 7–14 marks | 3–7 marks (overview) |
