# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A carries **3 marks** per question and expects a crisp definition, a labelled sketch, or a short derivation. Answers should be 4–6 lines.

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper Set A]`

**Define Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM). Mention any two applications.**

**Model Answer (Expected Length: 4–5 lines):**

Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM) is a technique in which multiple baseband signals are transmitted simultaneously over a single communication channel by allocating **distinct, non-overlapping frequency bands** to each signal. Each input signal modulates a separate carrier frequency so that its spectrum is translated into a unique slot, and the modulated outputs are summed to form a composite FDM signal.

**Two applications:**
1. **FM Radio Broadcasting:** Stations in the 88–108 MHz band are spaced 200 kHz apart — a classic FDM application.
2. **Analog Cable Television Distribution:** The 54–1000 MHz coax spectrum is divided into 6 MHz (NTSC) or 8 MHz (PAL) slots, each carrying one TV channel.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part A):** Students frequently confuse FDM with TDM by writing "signals are sent at different times." Always state the keyword **"non-overlapping frequency bands"**. Forgetting applications costs 1 mark easily.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Supplementary]`

**Explain the need for a guard band in FDM.**

**Model Answer:**

A guard band is a small, unused frequency band inserted between two adjacent FDM channels. It is needed because practical band-pass filters have **finite roll-off** and cannot achieve the ideal brick-wall response. Without the guard band, the spectral tails (skirts) of one channel would leak into the adjacent channel, causing **adjacent-channel interference (crosstalk)**. Typical guard-band widths in telephony are **0.5 kHz** (between voice channels) and **8 kHz** (between groups).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B carries **14 marks** per question. Students must answer **one full question** (either OR option). Each question has two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### Q3. Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3 Choice-1]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM) system. Label all blocks and the carrier frequencies.

**(b)** An FDM system multiplexes 12 voice channels, each of bandwidth 4 kHz, with a 0.5 kHz guard band between adjacent channels. Calculate (i) the total composite bandwidth, (ii) the spacing between adjacent carriers, and (iii) the highest carrier frequency if the lowest is 60 kHz.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Block Diagram (textual representation for the answer script):**

```
Source 1 --> LPF 1 --> Modulator 1 (carrier f1) --> BPF 1 --.
Source 2 --> LPF 2 --> Modulator 2 (carrier f2) --> BPF 2 --+--> SUMMER --> Channel
Source 3 --> LPF 3 --> Modulator 3 (carrier f3) --> BPF 3 --'
... up to N sources ...
Source N --> LPF N --> Modulator N (carrier fN) --> BPF N --'
```

**Working Explanation:**

1. **Source Stage:** $N$ independent message signals $m_1(t), m_2(t), \ldots, m_N(t)$ are generated.
2. **Pre-filtering:** Each signal is passed through a low-pass filter to strictly limit its bandwidth to $B_i$ and prevent spectral leakage.
3. **Modulation:** Each filtered signal modulates a unique carrier $c_i(t) = A_c \cos(2\pi f_i t)$ with $f_1 < f_2 < \ldots < f_N$, shifting the spectrum to a higher frequency slot.
4. **Band-pass Filtering:** A BPF centred on $f_i$ removes unwanted sideband products of modulation.
5. **Summing:** The $N$ band-limited, modulated signals are linearly added by a summing amplifier to form the composite FDM signal.
6. **Channel Transmission:** The composite signal propagates through the common physical medium.

At the receiver, the **mirror process** is performed using $N$ parallel BPFs (one per carrier), followed by $N$ demodulators and $N$ LPFs to recover the original $N$ baseband signals.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $N = 12$, $B_{ch} = 4$ kHz, $B_g = 0.5$ kHz, $f_1 = 60$ kHz.

**(i) Total composite bandwidth:**

$$B_{total} = N \cdot B_{ch} + (N-1) \cdot B_g$$

$$\begin{aligned}
B_{total} &= 12 \times 4 + 11 \times 0.5 \\
&= 48 + 5.5 \\
&= 53.5 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Carrier spacing (pitch):**

$$\Delta f = B_{ch} + B_g = 4 + 0.5 = 4.5 \text{ kHz}$$

**(iii) Highest carrier frequency:**

$$\begin{aligned}
f_{12} &= f_1 + (N-1) \cdot \Delta f \\
&= 60 + 11 \times 4.5 \\
&= 60 + 49.5 \\
&= 109.5 \text{ kHz}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part B):** Students often forget to **label every block** in the diagram, including the carrier frequencies and the guard bands. A diagram without labels typically earns only 2 of 7 marks. Also, do not forget the factor $(N-1)$ — using $N$ guard bands instead of $(N-1)$ is a **common +1 mark deduction**.

---

### Q4. Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice-2]`

**(a)** Explain the FDM hierarchy used in telephony. State the number of voice channels and bandwidth at each level.

**(b)** A satellite transponder has a usable bandwidth of 36 MHz. Each voice channel uses 4 kHz with a 0.5 kHz guard band. How many voice channels can be carried? Also find the percentage of bandwidth wasted in guard bands.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**FDM Hierarchy Table:**

| Level | Composition | Voice Channels | Bandwidth |
|-------|-------------|---------------|-----------|
| Voice Channel | 1 baseband signal | 1 | 4 kHz |
| Group | 12 voice channels | 12 | 48 kHz (60–108 kHz) |
| Supergroup | 5 groups | 60 | 240 kHz (312–552 kHz) |
| Mastergroup | 10 supergroups | 600 | 2.4 MHz |
| Jumbo Group | 6 mastergroups | 3600 | 14.4 MHz |

**Explanation:**
- A **Group** is formed by stacking 12 voice channels, each shifted by a 4 kHz sub-carrier.
- Five such groups form a **Supergroup** with carrier frequencies between 312 kHz and 552 kHz.
- Ten supergroups combine to form a **Mastergroup**, used in long-distance terrestrial and submarine cable links.
- Six mastergroups stacked together form a **Jumbo Group**, the highest standard FDM aggregate.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $B = 36$ MHz $= 36000$ kHz, $B_{ch} = 4$ kHz, $B_g = 0.5$ kHz.

**Step 1 — Apply the channel-count formula:**

$$N_{max} = \left\lfloor \frac{B + B_g}{B_{ch} + B_g} \right\rfloor$$

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{max} &= \left\lfloor \frac{36000 + 0.5}{4 + 0.5} \right\rfloor \\
&= \left\lfloor \frac{36000.5}{4.5} \right\rfloor \\
&= \left\lfloor 8000.111\ldots \right\rfloor \\
&= 8000 \text{ voice channels}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Total bandwidth occupied by 8000 channels:**

$$B_{used} = N \cdot B_{ch} + (N-1) \cdot B_g = 8000 \times 4 + 7999 \times 0.5$$

$$\begin{aligned}
B_{used} &= 32000 + 3999.5 \\
&= 35999.5 \text{ kHz} \\
&\approx 36000 \text{ kHz} = 36 \text{ MHz} \quad \text{✓ (matches the transponder BW)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Bandwidth wasted in guard bands:**

$$B_{waste} = (N-1) \cdot B_g = 7999 \times 0.5 = 3999.5 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage waste:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ waste} &= \frac{B_{waste}}{B_{total}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{3999.5}{36000} \times 100 \\
&\approx 11.11\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** A very common error is to compute the **percentage of guard bands as $B_g / B_{ch} \times 100 = 12.5\%$** and stop. The actual percentage depends on **how many guard bands are used relative to the total bandwidth**, and this number scales with $N$. Always plug the values into the explicit waste formula.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (print this on the night before the exam):**

- ✅ FDM = **F**requency **D**ivision **M**ultiplexing — share the channel in the **frequency** domain.
- ✅ FDM works for **analog** signals; for digital, prefer TDM.
- ✅ Each input signal is **modulated onto a unique carrier** $f_i$.
- ✅ The bandwidth of the channel $\geq \sum B_i + \sum B_g$.
- ✅ Standard formula: $B_{total} = N \cdot B_{ch} + (N-1) \cdot B_g$.
- ✅ **Guard bands** are mandatory to absorb BPF roll-off and prevent crosstalk.
- ✅ FDM telephony hierarchy: **Voice (4 kHz) → Group (12) → Supergroup (60) → Mastergroup (600) → Jumbo (3600).**
- ✅ FDM transmitter chain: **Source → LPF → Modulator → BPF → Summer → Channel.**
- ✅ FDM receiver chain: **Channel → Amplifier → N × (BPF → Demodulator → LPF) → Outputs.**
- ✅ Optical counterpart of FDM is **WDM (Wavelength Division Multiplexing).**
- ✅ Multiple-access extension of FDM is **FDMA**, used in 1G cellular systems.
- ✅ Typical **guard band width:** 0.5 kHz (voice) and 8 kHz (group).
- ✅ FDM is **linear and synchronous-free**, but **bandwidth-inefficient** compared to TDM.
- ✅ **Real-world FDM deployments:** FM radio, analog TV, satellite transponders, PSTN carrier systems.
- ✅ **KTU buzzwords to use in answers:** *non-overlapping frequency bands*, *distinct carrier frequency*, *guard band*, *adjacent-channel interference*, *summing amplifier*, *band-pass filter*, *orthogonality of sinusoids*.

---

> **End of Module 3 — Multiplexing (FDM) Premium Study Notes | OECST612 — Data Communication | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Wavelength Division Multiplexing (WDM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Wavelength Division Multiplexing (WDM) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wavelength Division Multiplexing (WDM)** is a physical-layer optical-fiber multiplexing technique in which **two or more optical carrier signals** of **distinct wavelengths (λ)** are simultaneously transmitted over a **single-mode optical fiber** through the use of a **wavelength-selective optical multiplexer (MUX)** at the transmitting end and an **optical demultiplexer (DEMUX)** at the receiving end, thereby exploiting the **enormous bandwidth (~25 THz)** of the low-attenuation optical windows (O, E, S, C, L bands) of silica fiber. In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, WDM is the **optical-domain analog of Frequency Division Multiplexing (FDM)**, where the carrier frequencies are extremely high (≈ 193.1 THz around the 1550 nm C-band) and the channel spacings are measured in **nanometers (nm)** or **Gigahertz (GHz)** of optical frequency. WDM allows network operators to **multiply the capacity of an existing fiber strand by a factor equal to the number of wavelength channels** without laying new fiber — a process termed *capacity scaling* or *bandwidth multiplication*. The fundamental operating equation linking wavelength and optical frequency is the wave equation: $$c = \lambda \cdot f$$ where: - $c$ = **speed of light in vacuum = 3 × 10⁸ m/s** - $\lambda$ = wavelength in meters - $f$ = optical frequency in Hertz > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme emphasizes the distinction between **Coarse WDM (CWDM)**, **Dense WDM (DWDM)**, and the architecture of **optical add-drop multiplexers (OADM)** and **optical cross-connects (OXC)**. These are direct short-answer (3-mark) favorites in the End Semester Examination (ESE). --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition **The "Multi-Lane Light Highway" Analogy:** Imagine a **single two-lane rural road** (one fiber). During rush hour it gets jammed. Instead of building a brand-new highway (laying new fiber), the city planners decide to build a **multi-lane elevated expressway** directly on top of the existing road. Now, **red cars use lane 1, blue cars use lane 2, green cars use lane 3**, and so on. All cars travel on the *same physical road*, but they occupy *different "color lanes"* that do not collide. - The **existing fiber** = the original road. - Each **wavelength (color of light)** = one logical "lane." - The **optical multiplexer** = the on-ramp that merges different color cars onto the highway. - The **optical demultiplexer** = the off-ramp that sorts cars by color to their respective destinations. - The **optical amplifier (EDFA)** = the service station that re-fuels cars midway so they don't run out of "energy" (optical power). This is *precisely* what WDM does: it stacks **independent optical channels**, each carrying its own data stream (10 Gbps, 40 Gbps, 100 Gbps, 400 Gbps, or even 800 Gbps per channel), onto a single fiber by giving each channel its own unique wavelength (color) in the infrared region. --- ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following constants and metrics are *mandatory recall* for KTU 2024 ESE: | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | Speed of light | $c$ | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** | Wave-frequency conversions | | Planck's constant | $h$ | **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** | Photon-energy calculations | | Boltzmann constant | $k_B$ | **1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** | Noise-floor derivations | | Reference wavelength | $\lambda_0$ | **1550 nm** | C-band center for DWDM | | Channel spacing (DWDM) | $\Delta\lambda$ | **0.8 nm / 0.4 nm / 0.2 nm** | ITU-T grid | | Channel spacing (CWDM) | $\Delta\lambda$ | **20 nm** | ITU-T G.694.2 | | Optical attenuation | $\alpha$ | **0.2 dB/km** at 1550 nm | Link-budget analysis | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Mnemonic:** *"WDM = Wavelengths Divvied-up onto a single fiber-Multiple-xer."* Always anchor your definition to the words **wavelengths, fiber, multiplexer, simultaneous**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" and the "How" The operational logic of any WDM system is governed by a **five-stage chain**, and every KTU question is mapped to one of these stages. **Stage 1 — Transponders (Optical-Electrical-Optical Conversion):** Each incoming client signal (e.g., a 10 Gbps Ethernet or 100 Gbps OTU4 stream) modulates an *independent* laser transmitter. Each laser is tuned to a **specific ITU-T wavelength** (e.g., λ₁ = 1530.33 nm, λ₂ = 1531.12 nm, …, λₙ). The output of each laser is a *narrow-bandwidth* optical carrier carrying the data. *Why?* Because direct modulation of a single broadband source would smear wavelengths together and cause inter-channel interference. **Stage 2 — Optical Multiplexer (MUX):** The MUX is a **passive wavelength-selective combiner** (typically a Thin-Film Filter (TFF) array, Arrayed Waveguide Grating (AWG), or Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) tree). It combines the N independent wavelengths into a single fiber output with minimal **insertion loss (typically 3–5 dB for an 8-channel MUX)**. *How?* The MUX uses the principle of **constructive interference at each passband** and **destructive interference outside it** to filter and combine channels. **Stage 3 — Optical Amplification (EDFA / Raman):** Because the MUX and the fiber itself attenuate the combined signal, **Erbium-Doped Fiber Amplifiers (EDFAs)** are inserted inline. EDFAs provide *simultaneous* gain across the **C-band (1525 nm – 1565 nm)** for all channels — this is a *unique* advantage of WDM: a single amplifier boosts N channels at once. *Gain equation:* $$G_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)$$ **Stage 4 — Transmission Fiber (Single-Mode Fiber, SMF):** The combined multi-wavelength signal travels through standard **G.652 SMF** or low-water-peak **G.652.D** fiber, with chromatic dispersion compensated by **Dispersion-Shifted Fiber (DSF)** or **Non-Zero Dispersion-Shifted Fiber (NZ-DSF)**. **Stage 5 — Optical Demultiplexer (DEMUX) & Receivers:** At the receiver, a DEMUX separates the wavelengths back to their original spatial channels, and each is detected by a **photodiode (PIN or APD)** followed by clock-and-data recovery (CDR) and SERDES electronics. --- ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet The table below is the **definitive formula reference** for any numerical WDM question in the ESE. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $c = \lambda f$ | Wave-frequency relation | m/s, m, Hz | | 2 | $\Delta f = \frac{c \cdot \Delta\lambda}{\lambda^2}$ | Channel spacing in **frequency** given wavelength spacing | Hz | | 3 | $B_{total} = N \cdot B_{channel}$ | Total aggregate fiber bandwidth | bps | | 4 | $N = \frac{\Delta\lambda_{band}}{\Delta\lambda_{channel}}$ | Number of channels in a given band | dimensionless | | 5 | $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-(\alpha L + L_{MUX} + L_{DEMUX})/10}$ | Link-budget equation | Watts | | 6 | $OSNR_{out} = OSNR_{in} + 10\log_{10}(G) - NF$ | Optical SNR after EDFA | dB | | 7 | $\eta_{spectral} = \frac{R_b}{\Delta f}$ | Spectral efficiency | bps/Hz | | 8 | $E_{photon} = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Energy of a single photon | Joules | | 9 | $L_{max} = \frac{P_{TX} - P_{RX,min} - L_{system}}{\alpha}$ | Maximum reach before amplification | km | | 10 | $BER = Q\left(\frac{I_1 - I_0}{\sigma_1 + \sigma_0}\right)$ | Bit Error Rate (Q-function form) | dimensionless | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **channel spacing in nm** with **channel spacing in GHz**. They are *not* equal. Use the differential relation $\Delta f \approx \frac{c \cdot \Delta\lambda}{\lambda^2}$ to convert. At 1550 nm, a 0.8 nm spacing equals ≈ **100 GHz**. --- ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility WDM is the **backbone of every long-haul, metro, and undersea telecommunications network on Earth**. Practical deployments include: - **Submarine cables:** Modern trans-oceanic systems (e.g., MAREA, Dunant) carry **> 200 Tbps** per fiber pair using **C+L band DWDM** with **probabilistic constellation shaping (PCS)**. - **5G fronthaul / backhaul:** CWDM and DWDM carry CPRI/eCPRI traffic between radio units and baseband units. - **Data Center Interconnect (DCI):** Coherent 400G ZR / 400G ZR+ pluggables use DWDM to connect data centers across metro distances. - **Passive Optical Networks (PON):** NG-PON2 uses **TWDM (Time+WDM)** with 4–8 wavelengths, each carrying 10 Gbps downstream. > [!TIP] > **KTU 2024 Tip:** When the question asks *"Give two applications of WDM,"* always cite **(i) long-haul telecom backbone** and **(ii) CATV / Cable-TV distribution networks** — these are the textbook favorites. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Converting Wavelength Spacing to Frequency Spacing This is the **most common 7-mark derivation** in KTU Module 3 numerical problems. The examiner will give you Δλ and ask for Δf (or vice versa). **Problem Context (Simulated ESE):** A DWDM system operates in the C-band with a reference wavelength $\lambda_0 = 1550$ nm and a channel spacing $\Delta\lambda = 0.8$ nm. Determine the corresponding channel spacing in **GHz**. **Step-by-step derivation:** We start from the fundamental wave relation: $$c = \lambda \cdot f$$ Solving for frequency: $$f = \frac{c}{\lambda}$$ To find how a small change in wavelength $\Delta\lambda$ maps to a change in frequency $\Delta f$, we differentiate $f$ with respect to $\lambda$: $$\frac{df}{d\lambda} = \frac{d}{d\lambda}\left(\frac{c}{\lambda}\right) = -\frac{c}{\lambda^2}$$ The negative sign indicates that *increasing* wavelength *decreases* frequency. For small perturbations we use the magnitude: $$\Delta f \approx \left\vert \frac{df}{d\lambda} \right\vert \Delta\lambda = \frac{c \cdot \Delta\lambda}{\lambda^2}$$ **Numerical evaluation:** Convert the variables to SI base units: - $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s - $\lambda = 1550 \text{ nm} = 1550 \times 10^{-9}$ m - $\Delta\lambda = 0.8 \text{ nm} = 0.8 \times 10^{-9}$ m Substitute: $$\Delta f = \frac{(3 \times 10^8) \cdot (0.8 \times 10^{-9})}{(1550 \times 10^{-9})^2}$$ Compute the denominator: $$(1550 \times 10^{-9})^2 = 2.4025 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^2$$ Compute the numerator: $$(3 \times 10^8) \cdot (0.8 \times 10^{-9}) = 2.4 \times 10^{-1} = 0.24$$ Therefore: $$\Delta f = \frac{0.24}{2.4025 \times 10^{-12}} \text{ Hz}$$ $$\Delta f \approx 9.9896 \times 10^{10} \text{ Hz}$$ $$\Delta f \approx 99.9 \text{ GHz} \approx 100 \text{ GHz}$$ **Conclusion:** A 0.8 nm wavelength spacing at 1550 nm corresponds to a **100 GHz** frequency spacing on the ITU-T grid. This is the standard DWDM channel plan. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Number of Channels in a Given Optical Band **Problem Context (Simulated ESE):** A CWDM system operates over the full ITU-T G.694.2 band from 1270 nm to 1610 nm with a fixed channel spacing of $\Delta\lambda_{channel} = 20$ nm. Calculate the maximum number of multiplexed wavelengths. **Step-by-step derivation:** Total usable spectral window: $$\Delta\lambda_{band} = \lambda_{max} - \lambda_{min} = 1610 - 1270 = 340 \text{ nm}$$ Number of channels: $$N = \frac{\Delta\lambda_{band}}{\Delta\lambda_{channel}} = \frac{340 \text{ nm}}{20 \text{ nm}}$$ $$N = 17 \text{ channels}$$ **Verification by listing center wavelengths:** 1271, 1291, 1311, 1331, 1351, 1371, 1391, 1411, 1431, 1451, 1471, 1491, 1511, 1531, 1551, 1571, 1591 nm — exactly 17 nominal channels per ITU-T G.694.2. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Total Aggregate Capacity and Spectral Efficiency **Problem Context (Simulated ESE):** A DWDM link multiplexes 40 channels, each carrying a 100 Gbps QPSK-modulated data stream, across the C-band. The total usable optical bandwidth is 12.8 THz (≈ 32 nm at 1550 nm). Compute (a) the total capacity, and (b) the spectral efficiency. **Step-by-step derivation:** **(a) Total aggregate capacity:** $$B_{total} = N \cdot B_{channel} = 40 \times 100 \text{ Gbps}$$ $$B_{total} = 4000 \text{ Gbps} = 4 \text{ Tbps}$$ **(b) Spectral efficiency:** For QPSK, 2 bits are encoded per symbol. The symbol rate $R_s$ per channel: $$R_s = \frac{R_b}{\log_2(M)} = \frac{100 \text{ Gbps}}{2} = 50 \text{ Gbaud}$$ The optical frequency spacing is 100 GHz (from Derivation 1), so the per-channel spectral efficiency is: $$\eta_{s,channel} = \frac{R_b}{\Delta f} = \frac{100 \times 10^9}{100 \times 10^9} = 1 \text{ bit/s/Hz}$$ The **aggregate spectral efficiency** of the WDM super-channel: $$\eta_{s,total} = \frac{B_{total}}{\Delta f_{band}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{12}}{12.8 \times 10^{12}}$$ $$\eta_{s,total} = 0.3125 \text{ bits/s/Hz}$$ **Engineering interpretation:** With higher-order modulation (16-QAM, 64-QAM) and tighter channel spacing, modern coherent systems achieve 4–8 bits/s/Hz spectral efficiency per channel. --- ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Optical Link Budget (Power-Loss Calculation) **Problem Context (Simulated ESE):** A 50 km WDM link uses a transmitter with $P_{TX} = 0$ dBm, a receiver sensitivity of $P_{RX,min} = -28$ dBm, fiber attenuation $\alpha = 0.25$ dB/km, two MUX/DEMUX pairs each with 4 dB insertion loss, and two connector pairs each with 0.5 dB loss. Compute the system margin. **Step-by-step derivation:** Total fiber attenuation: $$L_{fiber} = \alpha \cdot L = 0.25 \times 50 = 12.5 \text{ dB}$$ Total component losses: $$L_{components} = 2 \times L_{MUX} + 2 \times L_{connector} = 2(4) + 2(0.5) = 9 \text{ dB}$$ Total system loss: $$L_{system} = L_{fiber} + L_{components} = 12.5 + 9 = 21.5 \text{ dB}$$ Power at the receiver input: $$P_{RX} = P_{TX} - L_{system} = 0 - 21.5 = -21.5 \text{ dBm}$$ System margin (Engineering safety margin): $$M = P_{RX} - P_{RX,min} = -21.5 - (-28) = 6.5 \text{ dB}$$ **Conclusion:** A healthy **6.5 dB margin** allows for aging of components, splices, and temperature-induced loss variations — a robust design by industry standards (which typically require ≥ 3 dB). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Bidirectional WDM Link The following Mermaid block diagram maps the end-to-end functional flow of a WDM transmission system, including all critical subsystems (TX, MUX, EDFA, fiber, DEMUX, RX, OADM, and OXC). ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Client Signal 1
10G Ethernet] --> B1[Transponder 1
Laser Lambda 1] A2[Client Signal 2
40G OTU3] --> B2[Transponder 2
Laser Lambda 2] A3[Client Signal 3
100G OTU4] --> B3[Transponder 3
Laser Lambda 3] A4[Client Signal N
OTU Signal] --> B4[Transponder N
Laser Lambda N] B1 --> C[Optical Multiplexer MUX
Thin Film Filter Array] B2 --> C B3 --> C B4 --> C C --> D[Optical Amplifier EDFA 1
C Band Gain 25 dB] D --> E[Single Mode Fiber SMF
50 km Span] E --> F[In Line Amplifier EDFA 2
Optional Repeater] F --> G[Optical Add Drop Multiplexer OADM
Drops Adds Specific Lambdas] G --> H[Optical Cross Connect OXC
Wavelength Routing] H --> I[Optical Demultiplexer DEMUX
Arrayed Waveguide Grating] I --> J1[Photodetector 1
PIN APD Receiver] I --> J2[Photodetector 2
PIN APD Receiver] I --> J3[Photodetector 3
PIN APD Receiver] I --> J4[Photodetector N
PIN APD Receiver] J1 --> K1[Client Output 1] J2 --> K2[Client Output 2] J3 --> K3[Client Output 3] J4 --> K4[Client Output N] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagram:** Client-side electrical signals (left) are converted to optical channels at distinct ITU-T wavelengths by transponders. These are *passively combined* by the MUX, *amplified* by the EDFA, transmitted over SMF, and *dynamically routed* by the OADM/OXC before passive DEMUX separation back to client-side electrical signals (right). ### 4.2 WDM System Taxonomy — Conceptual Map ```mermaid flowchart TD P[Wavelength Division Multiplexing WDM] --> Q[Coarse WDM CWDM] P --> R[Dense WDM DWDM] P --> S[Hybrid TWDM
Time plus WDM] Q --> Q1[Channel Spacing 20 nm] Q --> Q2[ITU T G 694 2 Standard] Q --> Q3[Uncooled Lasers Low Cost] Q --> Q4[Max 18 Channels] R --> R1[Channel Spacing 0.8 nm or 0.4 nm or 0.2 nm] R --> R2[ITU T G 694 1 Standard] R --> R3[Cooled DFB Lasers High Cost] R --> R4[Up to 96 plus Channels] R --> R5[EDFA Amplification Compatible] S --> S1[Used in NG PON2] S --> S2[Combines TDM and WDM] ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Visual Cue:** When drawing MUX/DEMUX in your answer script, label the components clearly as **MUX** (input side, N → 1) and **DEMUX** (output side, 1 → N) and use **λ₁, λ₂, …, λₙ** to label channels. Examiners reward block diagrams with 1–2 bonus marks even when not explicitly asked. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Define Wavelength Division Multiplexing (WDM). Mention any two advantages of WDM over TDM.* **Model Answer:** > **WDM Definition:** Wavelength Division Multiplexing is an optical multiplexing technique in which multiple optical signals at different wavelengths (λ₁, λ₂, …, λₙ) are transmitted simultaneously over a single optical fiber using an optical multiplexer and demultiplexer. **Two advantages over TDM:** 1. **Bandwidth scalability without new fiber:** WDM multiplies the capacity of an existing fiber by adding more wavelengths, whereas TDM requires a higher bit-rate electronics upgrade at both ends. 2. **Protocol and bit-rate transparency:** Each wavelength can carry an independent data format and bit-rate (e.g., 10G Ethernet on λ₁, 100G OTU4 on λ₂), whereas TDM forces all tributaries to share a single synchronized high-speed stream. 3. *(Optional third)* Lower latency because no time-slot framing is required. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Differentiate between Coarse WDM (CWDM) and Dense WDM (DWDM) in terms of channel spacing and number of channels.* **Model Answer (Tabular Form Recommended):** | Parameter | CWDM | DWDM | |---|---|---| | Channel spacing | **20 nm** | **0.8 nm / 0.4 nm / 0.2 nm** | | ITU-T standard | G.694.2 | G.694.1 | | Number of channels | Up to **18** | Up to **96 / 192 / 400+** | | Laser type | Uncooled (low cost) | Cooled, wavelength-locked (high cost) | | Amplification | Not EDFA-compatible | **EDFA compatible** (C-band) | | Typical reach | Metro / access (< 80 km) | Long-haul / ultra-long-haul | | Cost per channel | Low | High | --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Format) --- #### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) **Q3.A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the functional block diagram of a WDM system with neat sketches. List the key optical components and their functions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A DWDM system operates at a center wavelength of 1550 nm with a channel spacing of 0.4 nm. Calculate (i) the corresponding frequency spacing, and (ii) the maximum number of channels that can be placed in a 40 nm spectral window. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Functional Block Diagram Explanation:** A WDM system consists of the following **five key optical sub-systems**, each with a specific function: 1. **Optical Transmitters (Transponders):** Convert incoming electrical client signals into optical signals on assigned ITU-T wavelengths. They perform O/E/O conversion when necessary and provide 3R functions (Re-amplify, Re-shape, Re-time). 2. **Optical Multiplexer (MUX):** A *passive* wavelength-selective combiner that merges N wavelengths into one fiber. Implementations include **Thin-Film Filters (TFF)**, **Arrayed Waveguide Gratings (AWG)**, and **Fiber Bragg Gratings (FBG)**. Typical insertion loss: **3–5 dB**. 3. **Optical Amplifier (EDFA):** Boosts all WDM channels simultaneously in the **C-band (1525–1565 nm)** with a gain of ≈ **20–30 dB** and a noise figure of **4–5 dB**. 4. **Transmission Fiber:** Standard single-mode fiber G.652 with attenuation **0.2 dB/km** at 1550 nm and chromatic dispersion **17 ps/nm·km**. 5. **Optical Demultiplexer (DEMUX):** The reciprocal of the MUX; separates the combined wavelengths back to individual receivers. **Auxiliary components:** **OADM** (Optical Add-Drop Multiplexer) for adding/dropping specific channels at intermediate nodes, and **OXC** (Optical Cross-Connect) for wavelength-level switching in mesh networks. **Part (b) — Numerical Computation:** **(i) Frequency spacing:** Using the relation: $$\Delta f = \frac{c \cdot \Delta\lambda}{\lambda^2}$$ Substitute: - $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s - $\lambda = 1550 \times 10^{-9}$ m - $\Delta\lambda = 0.4 \times 10^{-9}$ m $$\Delta f = \frac{(3 \times 10^8)(0.4 \times 10^{-9})}{(1550 \times 10^{-9})^2}$$ $$\Delta f = \frac{0.12}{2.4025 \times 10^{-12}}$$ $$\Delta f \approx 4.9948 \times 10^{10} \text{ Hz} \approx 50 \text{ GHz}$$ **Answer:** The frequency spacing is approximately **50 GHz**, corresponding to the **ITU-T 50 GHz grid**. **(ii) Maximum number of channels in 40 nm:** $$N = \frac{\Delta\lambda_{band}}{\Delta\lambda_{channel}} = \frac{40 \text{ nm}}{0.4 \text{ nm}}$$ $$N = 100 \text{ channels}$$ **Answer:** A maximum of **100 channels** can be placed in a 40 nm window with 0.4 nm spacing. --- #### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative **Q3.B. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** **(a)** Describe the working of an Optical Add-Drop Multiplexer (OADM) and an Optical Cross-Connect (OXC). How do they enhance the flexibility of a WDM network? **(7 marks)** **(b)** With reference to the EDFA used in WDM systems, derive the expression for Optical Signal-to-Noise Ratio (OSNR) at the output, starting from the input OSNR and including the noise figure. Explain why EDFAs are uniquely suited to WDM. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — OADM and OXC:** An **Optical Add-Drop Multiplexer (OADM)** is a network element that *selectively drops* one or more wavelengths from a WDM composite signal and *adds* new wavelengths at the same ITU-T grid, all in the optical domain — *without* converting to electrical. OADMs come in two generations: **fixed OADMs (FOADM)** with hard-wavelength drop ports, and **reconfigurable OADMs (ROADMs)** with software-controlled wavelength selection via wavelength-selective switches (WSS). An **Optical Cross-Connect (OXC)** is a larger-scale device that *switches* optical channels between multiple input and output fiber ports. Modern OXCs use **MEMS mirror arrays** or **liquid-crystal switches** and are the building blocks of **all-optical mesh networks**, eliminating the need for O/E/O regeneration at every node. **Flexibility enhancement:** OADMs and OXCs allow operators to remotely reconfigure the logical topology, provision new wavelengths on demand, perform **wavelength routing**, and enable **wavelength-level protection switching** — drastically reducing OpEx. **Part (b) — OSNR Derivation:** Let the input optical signal power be $P_{in}$ and the input optical noise power (within a reference bandwidth $B_{ref} = 0.1$ nm) be $P_{N,in}$. The input OSNR is: $$OSNR_{in} = \frac{P_{in}}{P_{N,in}}$$ When the signal passes through an EDFA with linear gain $G$ and noise figure $NF$, the output signal power is: $$P_{out} = G \cdot P_{in}$$ The output noise power (in linear units) is: $$P_{N,out} = G \cdot P_{N,in} + P_{ASE}$$ where $P_{ASE}$ is the **amplified spontaneous emission (ASE)** noise added by the amplifier. The noise figure relates the input SNR to the output SNR: $$NF = \frac{SNR_{in}}{SNR_{out}} = \frac{P_{in}/P_{N,in}}{P_{out}/P_{N,out}}$$ Substituting $P_{out} = G P_{in}$ and solving for the output OSNR: $$OSNR_{out} = \frac{OSNR_{in}}{NF}$$ Expressed in decibels: $$OSNR_{out,dB} = OSNR_{in,dB} - NF_{dB}$$ In a *cascade* of $k$ identical EDFAs with the same gain $G$ and noise figure $NF$, the OSNR degrades as: $$OSNR_{out,dB} = P_{in,dBm} - 10\log_{10}(k \cdot h \nu \cdot NF \cdot B_{ref}) + G_{dB}$$ **Why EDFAs are uniquely suited to WDM:** Unlike electronic repeaters, an EDFA provides *simultaneous* gain across the entire **C-band (≈ 35 nm)**, boosting *all* WDM channels in a single device. This eliminates the need for per-channel regeneration, dramatically reduces cost, and enables transparent long-haul transmission. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students most commonly lose marks on WDM questions:** > 1. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing nm and m in the wavelength-to-frequency derivation. *Always convert to SI units (meters) before substituting into the wave equation.* > 2. **Mis-stating the direction of conversion:** Some students write $f = c\lambda$ (wrong!). The correct form is $f = c/\lambda$. > 3. **Forgetting the negative sign:** When differentiating $f$ with respect to $\lambda$, the slope is *negative*. Always take the *magnitude* to get a positive channel spacing. > 4. **Confusing CWDM with DWDM channel counts:** Writing "CWDM has 80 channels" is a 2-mark deduction. The correct count is **≤ 18** for CWDM. > 5. **Omitting the role of EDFAs:** In a "List the components" question, never list only the MUX and DEMUX. Examiners expect EDFA, transponder, and dispersion compensation as separate items. > 6. **Skipping the block diagram:** KTU examiners allocate **1–2 marks** for a labeled block diagram even when not explicitly requested. Always draw one. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Wavelength Division Multiplexing** - **Core definition:** WDM is the *simultaneous* transmission of multiple optical carriers at *different wavelengths* over a *single fiber*. - **Wave equation:** $c = \lambda f$. Channel spacing conversion: $\Delta f = c \cdot \Delta\lambda / \lambda^2$. - **Two flavors:** **CWDM** (20 nm spacing, ≤ 18 channels, uncooled lasers, no EDFA) and **DWDM** (0.8/0.4/0.2 nm spacing, up to 96+ channels, cooled lasers, EDFA-compatible). - **ITU-T grids:** **G.694.1** for DWDM, **G.694.2** for CWDM. - **The five functional blocks:** *Transponder → MUX → EDFA → Fiber → DEMUX → Receiver.* - **Auxiliary elements:** OADM (drops/adds specific λ), OXC (wavelength-level cross-connect). - **Standard C-band:** 1525 nm – 1565 nm (≈ 35 nm ≈ 4.4 THz). - **Standard L-band:** 1565 nm – 1625 nm (extends capacity). - **Key formula — channel count:** $N = \Delta\lambda_{band} / \Delta\lambda_{channel}$. - **Key formula — total capacity:** $B_{total} = N \cdot B_{channel}$. - **Key formula — OSNR cascade:** $OSNR_{out,dB} = P_{in,dBm} - 10\log_{10}(k \cdot h\nu \cdot NF \cdot B_{ref}) + G_{dB}$. - **EDFA advantage:** Single amplifier boosts *all* channels simultaneously in the C-band. - **Applications:** Long-haul telecom, submarine cables, 5G backhaul, DCI, NG-PON2 (TWDM), CATV. - **Mnemonic for KTU 2024:** *"CWDM = Coarse (20 nm), Cheap, Cooler-free, Capacity-limited. DWDM = Dense (0.8 nm), Demanding, Deployed everywhere, EDFA-driven."*

topics.title: Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)** is a digital multiplexing technique in which multiple low-bit-rate digital data streams (or digitized analog sources) are combined and transmitted over a single high-speed communication channel by allocating **distinct, non-overlapping time slots** to each input source within a repeating cyclic frame structure. The entire bandwidth of the physical channel is sequentially shared in time among all attached sources, and the multiplexer switches between input channels at a rate synchronized to the frame period so that each source transmits its data burst only during its pre-assigned slot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** TDM is the canonical answer to the question *"How do we carry multiple voice/data channels over a single digital link?"* and is the foundation of the **PDH (Plesiochronous Digital Hierarchy)** and **SDH/SONET (Synchronous Digital Hierarchy)** optical transport networks used globally.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **single narrow hallway (the transmission channel)** connecting two large offices. Ten students (input sources) in Office A need to reach Office B, but they cannot walk side-by-side. The corridor supervisor (the multiplexer) decides that **only one student may walk through the hallway at any given moment**, and each student gets a fixed 1-second window in a strict rotating order: Student 1 in second 1, Student 2 in second 2, …, Student 10 in second 10, then the cycle repeats.

- The **hallway** = the single physical channel.
- The **supervisor** = the TDM multiplexer / demultiplexer.
- Each student’s **1-second window** = a **time slot**.
- The full 10-second cycle = a **TDM frame**.
- The order is fixed and cyclic = **synchronous (deterministic)** scheduling.

Because the rotation is so fast (often millions of slots per second), every student feels as though they have the hallway "all to themselves," even though in reality only one is using it at any instant. This is the essence of **time-sharing** a channel.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning (Concise) |
|---|---|
| **Multiplexer (MUX)** | Device that interleaves multiple input signals into one composite stream. |
| **Demultiplexer (DEMUX)** | Device that separates the composite stream back into original inputs. |
| **Time Slot** | The dedicated time interval reserved for one specific source inside a frame. |
| **Frame** | One complete cycle of all time slots (plus framing/overhead bits). |
| **Frame Period (T<sub>f</sub>)** | Time taken to transmit one complete TDM frame. |
| **Frame Rate** | Number of frames transmitted per second = 1 / T<sub>f</sub>. |
| **Bit Rate (R<sub>b</sub>)** | Number of bits transmitted per second on the composite channel. |
| **Frame Bit / Sync Bit** | A special bit pattern used by the receiver to identify frame boundaries. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant / Standard Metric:** For **voice telephony** (sampled analog audio), the **Nyquist sampling rate** is **8 kHz** (samples/sec), which is why the **TDM frame rate** in classical telephony systems (T1, E1) is also **8 kHz** — exactly one voice frame every **125 µs**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — How TDM Works (Step by Step)

1. **Source Encoding:** Each analog source is passed through an **anti-aliasing filter** (Low-Pass, cutoff f<sub>m</sub>) and then sampled at **f<sub>s</sub> ≥ 2 f<sub>m</sub>** samples/sec. Each sample is quantized and encoded into a fixed number of bits (e.g., 8 bits for standard PCM voice).
2. **Sample Buffering:** The samples from all `n` sources are temporarily stored in their respective buffers so the MUX can read them in a deterministic sequence.
3. **Slot Allocation:** The MUX contains a high-speed electronic switch that rotates through the inputs in a fixed order. For each input it reads a defined number of bits and places them into the next available time slot.
4. **Framing:** A set of **framing bits** (a known bit pattern, e.g., `101010…`) is prepended to the set of `n` payload slots to form a complete **TDM frame**. The framing bits allow the receiver to detect the start of each new frame and thus align its demultiplexer.
5. **Transmission:** The composite serial bitstream is transmitted at a much higher bit rate than any individual source.
6. **Demultiplexing:** At the far end, the DEMUX detects the framing pattern, identifies slot boundaries, and routes each time slot's bits to the correct output channel buffer.
7. **Reconstruction:** The buffered samples are converted back to analog (via DAC + reconstruction filter) for each voice channel — exactly the inverse process.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Step 1:** Without sampling and quantization we cannot integrate analog sources into a digital TDM stream.
- **Step 2:** Sampling is usually done at a fixed rate; buffering decouples the source rate from the multiplexer's scanning rate.
- **Step 3:** Sequential switching enforces deterministic slot ownership — no collisions can occur.
- **Step 4:** Without framing bits, the receiver cannot know where frame `k` ends and frame `k+1` begins (no "start-of-frame" delimiter), causing **bit slip**.
- **Step 5:** The high bit rate is forced by combining `n` channels: if each carries `r` bps, the line must carry at least `n × r` bps.
- **Step 6:** Synchronization between MUX and DEMUX is **non-negotiable**; even a 1-bit phase error corrupts every channel.
- **Step 7:** Reconstruction is the standard PCM decoding process.

### 2.3 Synchronous TDM vs. Asynchronous (Statistical) TDM

| Property | **Synchronous TDM (STDM)** | **Statistical / Asynchronous TDM** |
|---|---|---|
| Slot assignment | Fixed, pre-assigned slot per source, even if source is idle. | Dynamic slots allocated only to **active** sources. |
| Required output rate | = n × source rate (worst case, always full). | ≤ n × source rate (lower, on average). |
| Addressing needed | Not needed — position in frame identifies the source. | Required — each slot must carry a source address. |
| Wasted capacity | High when sources are bursty / idle. | Low — bandwidth follows demand. |
| Complexity | Low. | Higher (controller + buffers). |
| Typical use | Legacy voice multiplexing (T1, E1). | Data networks, modern packet switches. |

### 2.4 TDM Framing Hierarchy — Real-World Examples

- **T1 (North America / Japan):** 24 voice channels × 8 bits + 1 framing bit = **193 bits/frame** at **8 kHz** frame rate → **1.544 Mbps** composite rate.
- **E1 (Europe / most of world):** 32 time slots (30 user + 2 signaling) × 8 bits = **256 bits/frame** at **8 kHz** → **2.048 Mbps** composite rate.
- **DS-2:** 4 × T1 = **6.312 Mbps**; **DS-3:** 28 × T1 = **44.736 Mbps**; etc. — these form the **PDH**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are standard, mathematically verifiable, and align with KTU/Forouzan/Tanenbaum textbook conventions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Composite bit rate (STDM) | $R_{c} = n \cdot r$ | bps | `n` channels each of rate `r`. |
| 2 | Frame duration | $T_{f} = \dfrac{1}{f_{s}}$ | seconds | For voice, $f_s = 8$ kHz ⇒ $T_f = 125\ \mu s$. |
| 3 | Bits per payload (excluding framing) | $B_{p} = n \cdot k$ | bits | `k` = bits per sample. |
| 4 | Total bits per frame | $B_{f} = B_{p} + F$ | bits | `F` = framing bits per frame. |
| 5 | Composite bit rate (general) | $R_{c} = B_{f} \cdot f_{s}$ | bps | Frames per second × bits per frame. |
| 6 | Frame efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{B_{p}}{B_{f}} = \dfrac{B_{p}}{B_{p} + F}$ | unitless (0–1) | Often expressed as a percentage. |
| 7 | Time-slot duration | $T_{slot} = \dfrac{k}{R_{c}}$ | seconds | Duration occupied by one source's sample. |
| 8 | Sampling rate for analog input | $f_{s} \geq 2 f_{m}$ | samples/sec | Nyquist-Shannon theorem. |
| 9 | Statistical TDM output rate | $R_{stat} \leq \dfrac{B_{p}}{T_{f}} \cdot \rho$ | bps | $\rho$ = average utilization (0–1). |

> **Worked-out numeric memory aid:** For 24 voice channels × 8 bits + 1 framing bit @ 8 kHz ⇒ composite rate = 193 × 8000 = **1,544,000 bps = 1.544 Mbps (T1)**.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

TDM is the bedrock of the **global digital telephone network** (PSTN), the **PDH/SDH transport hierarchy** of telecom carriers, the **internal TDM buses of early digital circuit switches**, the **PCM highway** connecting base-station equipment in legacy cellular systems (GSM uses 8-slot TDM on the air interface), and the **TDM-based backplanes of spacecraft telemetry systems**. Whenever multiple low-rate deterministic streams must be transported cheaply and reliably over one shared medium, TDM remains the go-to technique.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — Designing a Synchronous TDM Link

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark style):**

> *"Four voice-band analog sources, each with a maximum signal frequency of 4 kHz, are to be multiplexed using synchronous TDM. Each sample is quantized using 8-bit PCM. Add 1 framing bit per frame. Compute:
> (a) the sampling rate per source,
> (b) the bit duration of each sample,
> (c) the number of payload bits per frame,
> (d) the total bits per frame,
> (e) the composite (output) bit rate,
> (f) the frame duration,
> (g) the slot duration per source,
> (h) the frame efficiency."*

**Given Data:**

$$
n = 4 \text{ sources}, \quad f_{m} = 4\ \text{kHz}, \quad k = 8\ \text{bits/sample}, \quad F = 1\ \text{framing bit/frame}
$$

---

#### (a) Sampling rate per source — by the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem

To reconstruct a band-limited analog signal of maximum frequency $f_m$ without aliasing, the sampling frequency must satisfy:

$$
f_{s} \;\geq\; 2 f_{m}
$$

In practice we choose the equality, so:

$$
f_{s} = 2 f_{m} = 2 \times 4000 = 8000\ \text{samples/second} = 8\ \text{kHz}
$$

This matches the global telephony convention. **So the sampling rate per source = 8 kHz.**

---

#### (b) Bit duration of each sample

The bit duration (i.e., how long each PCM bit occupies the composite channel) is determined by the composite bit rate and the per-sample bit count. We must first compute the composite rate, so we defer the numeric evaluation. However, symbolically:

$$
T_{bit} = \frac{1}{R_{c}}
$$

We will compute this explicitly after (e).

---

#### (c) Number of payload bits per frame

Each source contributes one `k`-bit sample per frame. With `n` sources:

$$
B_{p} = n \times k = 4 \times 8 = 32\ \text{payload bits/frame}
$$

---

#### (d) Total bits per frame (payload + framing)

$$
B_{f} = B_{p} + F = 32 + 1 = 33\ \text{bits/frame}
$$

---

#### (e) Composite (output) bit rate

Since we transmit **one full frame per source sample interval**, the number of frames per second equals the sampling rate $f_s$. Therefore:

$$
R_{c} = B_{f} \times f_{s} = 33 \times 8000 = 264{,}000\ \text{bits/second} = 264\ \text{kbps}
$$

**Equivalently**, using the standard formula $R_c = n \cdot r$ where $r$ is each source's bit rate:

$$
r = k \times f_{s} = 8 \times 8000 = 64{,}000\ \text{bps} = 64\ \text{kbps}
$$

$$
R_{c} = n \times r = 4 \times 64{,}000 = 256{,}000\ \text{bps}
$$

You will note that this gives **256 kbps** — slightly *less* than the 264 kbps computed above. **The discrepancy is exactly the framing overhead**: the additional 1 framing bit per frame adds 1 × 8000 = 8 kbps. The exact formula must therefore include framing bits:

$$
R_{c} = (n \cdot k + F) \cdot f_{s} = 264\ \text{kbps}
$$

---

#### (f) Frame duration

The time available to transmit one complete frame equals the inter-sample interval for any source (because we must send one frame per sampling instant):

$$
T_{f} = \frac{1}{f_{s}} = \frac{1}{8000} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 125\ \mu\text{s}
$$

---

#### (g) Time-slot duration per source

The duration occupied by **one source's 8-bit sample** within the composite frame is:

$$
T_{slot} = \frac{k}{R_{c}} = \frac{8}{264{,}000} \approx 3.0303 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{s} = 30.30\ \mu\text{s}
$$

Equivalently, since there are `n` data slots of equal size plus one framing bit:

$$
T_{slot} = T_{bit} = \frac{T_{f}}{B_{f}} = \frac{125\ \mu\text{s}}{33} \approx 3.788\ \mu\text{s/bit}
$$

$$
T_{slot} = 8 \times T_{bit} \approx 8 \times 3.788 = 30.30\ \mu\text{s}
$$

Both agree. ✓

---

#### (h) Frame efficiency

Frame efficiency quantifies what fraction of the transmitted bits actually carry useful user data (excluding framing overhead):

$$
\eta = \frac{B_{p}}{B_{f}} = \frac{32}{33} \approx 0.9697 = 96.97\ \%
$$

---

### 3.2 Complete Results Summary Table

| Quantity | Symbol | Computed Value |
|---|---|---|
| Number of sources | $n$ | 4 |
| Bits per sample | $k$ | 8 |
| Sampling rate | $f_{s}$ | 8 kHz |
| Source bit rate | $r$ | 64 kbps |
| Payload bits/frame | $B_{p}$ | 32 bits |
| Framing bits/frame | $F$ | 1 bit |
| Total bits/frame | $B_{f}$ | 33 bits |
| Composite bit rate | $R_{c}$ | 264 kbps |
| Frame duration | $T_{f}$ | 125 µs |
| Bit duration | $T_{bit}$ | ≈ 3.788 µs |
| Slot duration | $T_{slot}$ | ≈ 30.30 µs |
| Frame efficiency | $\eta$ | 96.97 % |

---

### 3.3 Second Worked Example — T1 Link Numerology (Classic KTU Question)

**Given:** A T1 system carries 24 voice channels, each sampled at 8 kHz with 8 bits/sample, plus 1 framing bit/frame.

**Step 1 — Payload bits/frame:**

$$
B_{p} = 24 \times 8 = 192\ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Total bits/frame:**

$$
B_{f} = 192 + 1 = 193\ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Frame duration:**

$$
T_{f} = \frac{1}{f_{s}} = \frac{1}{8000} = 125\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Composite bit rate:**

$$
R_{c} = B_{f} \times f_{s} = 193 \times 8000 = 1{,}544{,}000\ \text{bps} = 1.544\ \text{Mbps}
$$

**Step 5 — Bit duration:**

$$
T_{bit} = \frac{1}{R_{c}} = \frac{1}{1.544 \times 10^{6}} \approx 0.6477\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 6 — Time slot per channel:**

$$
T_{slot} = 8 \times T_{bit} \approx 5.182\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**Step 7 — Frame efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{192}{193} \approx 0.9948 = 99.48\ \%
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Frame Efficiency from First Principles

Starting from definition — frame efficiency is the ratio of payload bits to total bits per frame:

$$
\eta \;=\; \frac{B_{p}}{B_{f}}
$$

Substitute $B_{p} = n \cdot k$ and $B_{f} = n \cdot k + F$:

$$
\eta \;=\; \frac{n \cdot k}{n \cdot k + F}
$$

Divide numerator and denominator by $n \cdot k$:

$$
\eta \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + \dfrac{F}{n \cdot k}} \;=\; \frac{1}{1 + \rho}
$$

where $\rho = \dfrac{F}{n \cdot k}$ is the **framing overhead ratio**.

**Engineering insight:**

- As $n \to \infty$ (many channels), $\rho \to 0$, so $\eta \to 1$ (framing overhead becomes negligible).
- As $k \to 0$ (e.g., 1-bit quantization), $\eta$ drops because framing becomes relatively large.

This is why high-capacity TDM trunks (E3, DS-3) carry hundreds of voice channels and have overhead ratios below 1 %.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Synchronous TDM Multiplexer / Demultiplexer

The figure below shows the canonical block-level architecture of a synchronous TDM system, mapping the interactions between encoders, the multiplexer, the channel, the demultiplexer, and the decoders.

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcA["SOURCE 1 ANALOG"]
    srcB["SOURCE 2 ANALOG"]
    srcC["SOURCE 3 ANALOG"]
    srcD["SOURCE 4 ANALOG"]
    filtA["LPF FILTER"]
    filtB["LPF FILTER"]
    filtC["LPF FILTER"]
    filtD["LPF FILTER"]
    sampA["SAMPLE AND HOLD"]
    sampB["SAMPLE AND HOLD"]
    sampC["SAMPLE AND HOLD"]
    sampD["SAMPLE AND HOLD"]
    encA["8 BIT ADC PCM ENCODER"]
    encB["8 BIT ADC PCM ENCODER"]
    encC["8 BIT ADC PCM ENCODER"]
    encD["8 BIT ADC PCM ENCODER"]
    bufA["BUFFER 1"]
    bufB["BUFFER 2"]
    bufC["BUFFER 3"]
    bufD["BUFFER 4"]
    muxclk["MUX ROTATING SWITCH"]
    combiner["FRAMING BIT INSERTER"]
    txline["COMPOSITE CHANNEL HIGH SPEED SERIAL LINE"]
    framdet["FRAME DETECTOR AND SYNC"]
    demuxclk["DEMUX ROTATING SWITCH"]
    dbufA["BUFFER 1 OUT"]
    dbufB["BUFFER 2 OUT"]
    dbufC["BUFFER 3 OUT"]
    dbufD["BUFFER 4 OUT"]
    decA["8 BIT DAC PCM DECODER"]
    decB["8 BIT DAC PCM DECODER"]
    decC["8 BIT DAC PCM DECODER"]
    decD["8 BIT DAC PCM DECODER"]
    outA["OUTPUT 1 ANALOG"]
    outB["OUTPUT 2 ANALOG"]
    outC["OUTPUT 3 ANALOG"]
    outD["OUTPUT 4 ANALOG"]

    srcA --> filtA --> sampA --> encA --> bufA
    srcB --> filtB --> sampB --> encB --> bufB
    srcC --> filtC --> sampC --> encC --> bufC
    srcD --> filtD --> sampD --> encD --> bufD

    bufA --> muxclk
    bufB --> muxclk
    bufC --> muxclk
    bufD --> muxclk
    muxclk --> combiner
    combiner --> txline

    txline --> framdet --> demuxclk
    demuxclk --> dbufA
    demuxclk --> dbufB
    demuxclk --> dbufC
    demuxclk --> dbufD

    dbufA --> decA --> outA
    dbufB --> decB --> outB
    dbufC --> decC --> outC
    dbufD --> decD --> outD
```

### 4.2 TDM Frame Structure (Bit-Level Timeline)

The figure below shows the time-slot layout of a single TDM frame for the 4-channel example derived in Section 3.1. The framing bit (F) is inserted at the start to mark frame boundaries.

```mermaid
graph LR
    F["F = FRAMING BIT 1 BIT"]
    S1["SOURCE 1 SLOT k = 8 BITS"]
    S2["SOURCE 2 SLOT k = 8 BITS"]
    S3["SOURCE 3 SLOT k = 8 BITS"]
    S4["SOURCE 4 SLOT k = 8 BITS"]
    NEXT["NEXT FRAME STARTS"]

    F --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4 --> NEXT
```

**Frame layout (33 bits total per 125 µs frame):**

- Bit 1 = Framing bit `F`
- Bits 2–9 = Source 1 sample (8 bits)
- Bits 10–17 = Source 2 sample (8 bits)
- Bits 18–25 = Source 3 sample (8 bits)
- Bits 26–33 = Source 4 sample (8 bits)
- Repeat.

### 4.3 Synchronous vs Statistical TDM — Slot Allocation Comparison

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph STDM["SYNCHRONOUS TDM FIXED SLOTS"]
        sA["SLOT 1 SOURCE 1 ALWAYS RESERVED"]
        sB["SLOT 2 SOURCE 2 ALWAYS RESERVED"]
        sC["SLOT 3 SOURCE 3 ALWAYS RESERVED"]
        sD["SLOT 4 SOURCE 4 ALWAYS RESERVED"]
    end
    subgraph ATDM["STATISTICAL TDM DYNAMIC SLOTS"]
        t1["SLOT 1 DATA FROM SOURCE 2"]
        t2["SLOT 2 DATA FROM SOURCE 4"]
        t3["SLOT 3 IDLE NO TRANSMISSION"]
        t4["SLOT 4 DATA FROM SOURCE 1"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> In **Synchronous TDM**, slots are reserved whether or not the source has data. In **Statistical TDM**, slots are dynamically assigned only to active sources, and an **address field** must accompany each slot so the receiver knows which source owns it.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**Q1.** *Define Time Division Multiplexing. Mention two advantages of TDM over FDM.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Time Division Multiplexing (TDM) is a digital multiplexing technique in which multiple data signals share a single high-speed transmission channel by being assigned **distinct, sequential time slots** in a repeating cyclic frame structure. Each input source transmits only during its allotted slot, and the multiplexer scans through all sources at a rate synchronized with the sampling clock.

**Advantages over FDM (1 mark — any two):**

1. **No inter-channel crosstalk / guard bands** required — because channels are separated in time, not frequency, eliminating the need for wide guard bands that waste spectrum in FDM.
2. **Easier digital integration** — TDM is a natural fit for digital logic, making it cheaper and more reliable to implement than FDM (which needs precision analog filters).
3. **Higher noise immunity** — digital signals are more robust against channel noise and attenuation than analog FDM sub-carriers.

---

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**Q2.** *What is frame efficiency in a TDM system? For a T1 system carrying 24 voice channels with 8 bits/sample and 1 framing bit/frame, compute the frame efficiency.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Frame efficiency $\eta$ is the ratio of the number of **useful payload bits** (carrying user information) to the **total number of bits** transmitted per TDM frame, expressed as a percentage.

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$
\eta = \frac{B_{p}}{B_{f}} = \frac{n \cdot k}{n \cdot k + F}
$$

**Numerical computation (1 mark):**

- $B_{p} = 24 \times 8 = 192$ bits
- $B_{f} = 192 + 1 = 193$ bits

$$
\eta = \frac{192}{193} \approx 0.9948 = 99.48\ \%
$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student answers **either Question A or Question B** (internal choice).

---

### 5.2.1 Question A — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of **Synchronous Time Division Multiplexing (TDM)** with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of **framing bits**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle (4 marks):**

In Synchronous TDM, `n` digital data streams share a single high-speed channel by being allocated **fixed, pre-assigned time slots** in a repeating frame.

1. Each source data stream is buffered into a register.
2. A high-speed **rotating switch (commutator)** in the multiplexer sequentially reads one byte (or one sample) from each source buffer in a fixed cyclic order.
3. A set of **framing bits** is prepended to the payload slots to form a complete frame.
4. The composite serial bitstream is transmitted on the channel at a bit rate equal to $R_c = B_f \times f_s$.
5. At the receiver, a **frame detector** identifies the framing bit pattern, locks the demultiplexer's rotation, and routes each slot's bits to the corresponding output buffer.
6. The buffered bits are then handed off to their respective destinations.

**Role of Framing Bits (3 marks):**

- **Frame synchronization:** Provide a known bit pattern (e.g., `10101011`) at the start of every frame so the receiver can detect frame boundaries unambiguously.
- **Bit-slip prevention:** Even a 1-bit timing drift at the receiver can shift every slot boundary, causing every channel to be mis-routed; framing bits allow continuous re-alignment.
- **Channel identification:** Indirectly identify slot ordering — receiver learns "the bit after the framing bit belongs to source 1, then source 2, …".
- **Overhead:** They are a small cost (typically 1 bit/frame in T1) that buys reliable synchronization.

**(b)** A TDM system multiplexes **4 analog voice sources**, each band-limited to **3.2 kHz**, using **8-bit PCM** with **2 framing bits** per frame. Compute the:
(i) sampling rate, (ii) bits per frame, (iii) composite bit rate, (iv) frame duration, (v) frame efficiency. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $n = 4$, $f_m = 3.2$ kHz, $k = 8$, $F = 2$.

**(i) Sampling rate:**

$$
f_s = 2 f_m = 2 \times 3.2 = 6.4\ \text{kHz}
$$

**(ii) Bits per frame:**

$$
B_f = n \cdot k + F = (4 \times 8) + 2 = 32 + 2 = 34\ \text{bits/frame}
$$

**(iii) Composite bit rate:**

$$
R_c = B_f \times f_s = 34 \times 6400 = 217{,}600\ \text{bps} = 217.6\ \text{kbps}
$$

**(iv) Frame duration:**

$$
T_f = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{6400} = 156.25\ \mu\text{s}
$$

**(v) Frame efficiency:**

$$
\eta = \frac{B_p}{B_f} = \frac{32}{34} = 0.9412 = 94.12\ \%
$$

---

### 5.2.2 Question B — (14 Marks, Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Compare **Synchronous TDM** and **Statistical (Asynchronous) TDM**. Under what conditions is each preferred? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | **Synchronous TDM** | **Statistical TDM** |
|---|---|---|
| Slot allocation | Fixed, pre-assigned | Dynamic, on demand |
| Addressing | Not required (position implies source) | Required in every slot |
| Output bit rate | = n × source rate (constant) | < n × source rate (varies with load) |
| Bandwidth efficiency | Low when sources are idle | High — only active sources use slots |
| Buffering | Minimal | Heavy buffering at MUX |
| Complexity | Simple | Complex (needs controller) |
| Latency | Predictable, fixed | Variable |
| Error sensitivity | Single sync error → all channels affected | Errors localized to one slot |

**Preferred Conditions (3 marks):**

- **Synchronous TDM is preferred when:** sources are **continuous, predictable, and equal-rate** (e.g., PCM voice trunks, real-time control signals). Latency must be deterministic. Example: T1/E1 voice multiplexing.
- **Statistical TDM is preferred when:** sources are **bursty, intermittent, and unequal-rate** (e.g., computer terminal traffic, IP packets). Bandwidth savings outweigh the added complexity. Example: data network packet multiplexers, statistical time-division multiplexers in leased-line modems.

**(b)** Three data sources with rates **50 kbps, 100 kbps, and 200 kbps** are multiplexed using **Statistical TDM**. If the average utilization is 0.6 and the frame rate is **10 kHz**, determine the **average output bit rate** and the **average number of bits per frame**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Total input rate (worst case, if all sources were always active in synchronous TDM):**

$$
R_{total} = 50 + 100 + 200 = 350\ \text{kbps}
$$

**Average output bit rate (with statistical multiplexing):**

$$
R_{avg} = R_{total} \times \rho = 350 \times 0.6 = 210\ \text{kbps}
$$

**Average number of bits per frame:**

$$
B_{avg} = \frac{R_{avg}}{f_{frame}} = \frac{210{,}000}{10{,}000} = 21\ \text{bits/frame (on average)}
$$

**Comparison (bonus insight):**

In synchronous TDM, every frame would need $B_f = 35$ bits (5 bits per source × 10 kHz frame rate, accounting for different bit rates via unequal slot allocation). Statistical TDM averages only 21 bits/frame, achieving a **40 % bandwidth saving** at this load.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse frame rate with bit rate.** Frame rate = sampling rate (e.g., 8 kHz). Bit rate = bits/frame × frame rate (e.g., 264 kbps).
> 2. **Always include framing bits** in the total bits/frame; omitting $F$ costs you the composite rate calculation — a guaranteed loss of 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Use the Nyquist rate** $f_s = 2 f_m$ for analog inputs; many students incorrectly write $f_s = f_m$.
> 4. **Synchronous TDM ≠ Statistical TDM** in marking — examiners often award partial credit only if you correctly identify which formula applies.
> 5. **Show unit conversions explicitly** (kHz ↔ Hz, µs ↔ s). KTU examiners deduct marks for ambiguous units.
> 6. **In Part B questions, label each sub-part clearly** with the formula used — board examiners value structured answers over prose.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **TDM Definition:** Multiple digital streams share **one channel in time**; each gets a **fixed slot** in a **cyclic frame**.
- **Frame = all slots of all sources + framing bits**, transmitted once per sampling interval.
- **Sampling rate for voice:** $f_s = 8$ kHz ⇒ frame duration $T_f = 125\ \mu s$ (industry standard, memorize it).
- **Voice PCM:** 8 bits/sample ⇒ 64 kbps per voice channel.
- **Composite bit rate:** $R_c = (n \cdot k + F) \times f_s$.
- **Frame efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{n \cdot k}{n \cdot k + F}$ — always less than 1.
- **T1 system:** 24 × 8 + 1 = 193 bits/frame @ 8 kHz ⇒ **1.544 Mbps**.
- **E1 system:** 32 × 8 = 256 bits/frame @ 8 kHz ⇒ **2.048 Mbps**.
- **Synchronous TDM:** fixed slots, simple, wastage when sources idle.
- **Statistical TDM:** dynamic slots, addressing needed, bandwidth-efficient for bursty traffic.
- **Framing bits** are essential — they enable **frame synchronization** at the demultiplexer.
- **Pulse stuffing (justification)** is used in PDH (e.g., DS-2) to reconcile slight rate mismatches between incoming tributaries.
- **TDM vs FDM:** TDM separates channels in **time**, FDM in **frequency**. TDM is digital-native; FDM is analog-native.
- **Nyquist sampling:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ is non-negotiable for any analog source feeding a TDM system.
- **Typical KTU 14-mark question pattern:** always asks for sampling rate, bits/frame, composite rate, frame duration, and efficiency — practice these five numbers.
- **Unit conversions to memorize:** 1 ms = $10^{-3}$ s; 1 µs = $10^{-6}$ s; 1 kbps = $10^3$ bps; 1 Mbps = $10^6$ bps.
- **KTU trap question to avoid:** *"If sampling rate doubles, does the composite bit rate double?"* — **YES**, because $R_c \propto f_s$. If `n` doubles, $R_c$ also doubles. If framing bits double, $R_c$ increases only slightly (sub-linearly).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
