<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: CHARACTERISTICS OF TRANSMISSION MEDIA

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Transmission Media** is the physical pathway or the **unbounded medium** through which electromagnetic signals propagate from a source to a destination in a data communication system. The **characteristics of transmission media** describe the physical, electrical, and performance properties—such as **bandwidth**, **attenuation**, **propagation delay**, **noise susceptibility**, and **interference immunity**—that determine the suitability of a medium for a given communication application.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** Transmission media are broadly classified into two categories:
> 1. **Guided Media (Bounded/Wired)** — Signals travel along a solid medium: Twisted-Pair Copper Cable, Coaxial Cable, Optical Fibre Cable.
> 2. **Unguided Media (Unbounded/Wireless)** — Signals propagate through free space using electromagnetic (EM) waves: Radio Waves, Microwaves, Infrared.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway System" 🛣️

Imagine data as **vehicles** carrying information, and the transmission medium as the **road** on which they travel.

- A **twisted-pair cable** is like a **narrow one-lane village road** — cheap to build, but only a few cars (bits) can pass per second, and the road is easily damaged.
- A **coaxial cable** is like a **well-paved two-lane highway** — wider, more reliable, and can carry more traffic with less "wear" (attenuation).
- An **optical fibre** is like a **multi-lane super-expressway** — extremely fast, almost no external interference, and supports massive volumes of traffic.
- A **wireless channel** is like **air transport** — no fixed route, but the signal weakens with distance and is affected by weather and obstacles.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **characteristics** of a medium tell us *how wide the road is* (bandwidth), *how fast cars arrive* (data rate), *how much the road degrades over distance* (attenuation), and *how immune the road is to rain/storms* (noise immunity). These four parameters govern **every** KTU board exam problem on this topic.

### 1.3 Fundamental Performance Metrics (All Highlighted for Board Exams)

| Performance Metric | SI Unit | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Bandwidth (BW)** | Hertz (Hz) | Range of frequencies the medium can pass without significant loss |
| **Attenuation ($\alpha$)** | Decibels per kilometre (dB/km) | Loss of signal power as it propagates through the medium |
| **Propagation Delay ($T_p$)** | Seconds (s) | Time taken by a signal bit to travel from source to destination |
| **Propagation Speed ($v$)** | Metres per second (m/s) | Speed of the EM signal inside the medium |
| **Noise (N)** | Watts (W) or dBm | Unwanted random energy added to the signal |
| **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** | Decibels (dB) | Ratio of signal power to noise power |
| **Bit Error Rate (BER)** | Dimensionless ratio | Probability of a bit being corrupted during transmission |
| **Crosstalk (XT)** | Decibels (dB) | Unwanted coupling of signals between adjacent channels |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners almost always quote the signal-noise ratio, attenuation, and bandwidth in a numerical problem. Memorize the **Shannon–Hartley theorem** and the **attenuation formula** — they are the two most frequently tested equations in this module.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Transmission Media

The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly requires students to explain the **characteristics, advantages, and disadvantages** of every category below.

### 2.1.1 GUIDED MEDIA (Bounded / Wired)

#### A. Twisted-Pair Copper Cable
A pair of insulated copper conductors twisted helically around each other. The twist **cancels electromagnetic interference (EMI)** from neighbouring pairs and external sources.

**Sub-Types:**
- **UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair)** — No metallic shielding; cheapest; used in Ethernet LANs and telephone lines.
- **STP (Shielded Twisted Pair)** — Wrapped in a foil shield; better noise immunity; used in token-ring and industrial networks.

| Characteristic | Typical Value / Description |
|---|---|
| Bandwidth | Up to **250 MHz** (Cat 6) |
| Attenuation | **High** — ~10 dB per 100 m at 100 MHz |
| Maximum Range | **100 m** (Ethernet limit) |
| Noise Immunity | **Low–Moderate** (UTP); Moderate (STP) |
| Cost | **Very Low** |
| Data Rate | 10 Mbps to 10 Gbps |

#### B. Coaxial Cable
A central copper conductor surrounded by a **dielectric insulator**, a **braided metallic shield**, and an outer **PVC jacket**. Used in cable TV, early Ethernet (10BASE-2, 10BASE-5), and broadband internet.

| Characteristic | Typical Value / Description |
|---|---|
| Bandwidth | Up to **1 GHz** |
| Attenuation | **Moderate** — ~5 dB per 100 m at 100 MHz |
| Maximum Range | **500 m** (10BASE-2) to several km (CATV) |
| Noise Immunity | **Good** (due to braided shield) |
| Cost | **Low–Moderate** |
| Data Rate | 10 Mbps to 1 Gbps |

#### C. Optical Fibre Cable
A glass or plastic strand that guides light via **Total Internal Reflection (TIR)**. Two principal variants:
- **SMF (Single-Mode Fibre)** — Core diameter ~9 µm; laser source; long-haul, very high bandwidth.
- **MMF (Multi-Mode Fibre)** — Core diameter 50/62.5 µm; LED source; short-range LANs.

| Characteristic | Typical Value / Description |
|---|---|
| Bandwidth | Up to **THz** (terabits/sec) |
| Attenuation | **Very Low** — ~0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm |
| Maximum Range | **> 100 km** without repeater |
| Noise Immunity | **Excellent** (immune to EMI) |
| Cost | **High** (transceivers, splicing) |
| Data Rate | 1 Gbps to 100+ Gbps |

---

### 2.1.2 UNGUIDED MEDIA (Unbounded / Wireless)

Electromagnetic waves are classified by frequency. The **electromagnetic spectrum** below shows the windows used for data communication.

| Band | Frequency | Wavelength | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| VLF | 3–30 kHz | 10–100 km | Submarine communication |
| LF | 30–300 kHz | 1–10 km | Navigation, RFID |
| MF | 300 kHz–3 MHz | 100–1000 m | AM radio |
| HF | 3–30 MHz | 10–100 m | Shortwave radio |
| VHF | 30–300 MHz | 1–10 m | FM radio, TV |
| UHF | 300 MHz–3 GHz | 10–100 cm | TV, cellular, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth |
| SHF | 3–30 GHz | 1–10 cm | Satellite, radar, microwave links |
| EHF | 30–300 GHz | 1–10 mm | Millimetre-wave 5G, radar |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU favourite: **Terrestrial microwave** uses **parabolic dish antennas** for point-to-point links; **satellite communication** uses a transponder in geostationary orbit at **35,786 km** altitude; **infrared (IR)** is line-of-sight, short range, and used in TV remotes.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)$ | Attenuation in decibels | $P_{in}, P_{out}$ in **W** or **mW** |
| 2 | $P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-A/10}$ | Output power from attenuation | $A$ in **dB** |
| 3 | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | Propagation delay | $d$ in **m**, $v$ in **m/s** |
| 4 | $v = \dfrac{c}{n}$ | Signal speed in dielectric | $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s, $n$ = refractive index |
| 5 | $C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | Shannon–Hartley channel capacity | $C$ in **bps**, $B$ in **Hz** |
| 6 | $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | SNR in decibels | $S,N$ linear power |
| 7 | $\lambda = \dfrac{v}{f}$ | Wavelength | $v$ propagation speed, $f$ frequency |
| 8 | $f_{max} = n \cdot BW$ | Maximum data rate (Nyquist, noiseless) | $n$ = signal levels |
| 9 | $d_{free} = 2 \cdot h_{t} \cdot h_{r}$ | LOS radio link range (km) | $h_t, h_r$ antenna heights (m) |
| 10 | $L = 32.4 + 20 \log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20 \log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ | Free-space path loss (dB) | $d$ distance, $f$ frequency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Table syntax note:** The vertical bar $\vert$ in the formulas is rendered with `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table structure. Always double-check your rendered PDF/HTML.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Optical fibre** is the **backbone of the internet** — undersea cables carry > 95% of intercontinental data traffic.
- **Twisted-pair Cat 6/7** dominates **enterprise LANs** because of low cost and PoE (Power over Ethernet) capability.
- **Microwave radio** is used by **mobile network operators (4G/5G backhaul)** in hilly terrain where fibre laying is impractical.
- **Satellite links** are indispensable for **maritime, aeronautical, and remote-area** connectivity.
- **Free-space path loss** governs link-budget design for every wireless system, from Wi-Fi routers to interplanetary probes (NASA Deep Space Network uses the same $L = 32.4 + 20\log(d) + 20\log(f)$ equation at S-band, X-band, and Ka-band).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Attenuation Equation in dB

The attenuation $A$ of a medium is defined as the **ratio of input signal power to output signal power**, expressed in decibels.

**Step 1 — Start with the linear power ratio.**

$$
R = \frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}
$$

where $P_{in}$ is the power injected at the transmitter end and $P_{out}$ is the power received at the far end.

**Step 2 — Convert the linear ratio to a logarithmic (decibel) scale.**

The decibel (dB) is defined for power ratios as 10 times the base-10 logarithm of the ratio.

$$
A_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the output power $P_{out}$.**

We rearrange the equation by isolating the logarithm.

$$
\frac{A_{dB}}{10} = \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right)
$$

Apply the inverse operation $10^{(\cdot)}$ on both sides.

$$
10^{A_{dB}/10} = \frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}
$$

Invert to obtain $P_{out}$.

$$
P_{out} = P_{in} \cdot 10^{-A_{dB}/10}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** If a medium has an attenuation of **3 dB**, the output power is **half** the input power; **10 dB** means the output is **one-tenth** of the input; **20 dB** corresponds to **one-hundredth**. A 3-dB drop is also called the **half-power point** and appears frequently in board problems involving cascaded amplifiers and repeater design.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Shannon–Hartley Channel Capacity Theorem

**Step 1 — Start with the Nyquist maximum data rate for a noiseless channel.**

For a noiseless channel of bandwidth $B$ Hertz using $M$ discrete signal levels, the maximum data rate is

$$
C_{Nyquist} = 2 B \log_{2}(M)
$$

**Step 2 — Account for the random thermal noise that is unavoidable in every real medium.**

Noise sets a statistical limit on the number of distinguishable signal levels. Shannon proved (1948) that the maximum number of distinguishable levels is bounded by

$$
M_{max} = \sqrt{1 + \frac{S}{N}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $M_{max}$ back into the Nyquist formula.**

$$
C = 2 B \log_{2}\!\left(\sqrt{1 + \frac{S}{N}}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the logarithm identity $\log_{2}(\sqrt{x}) = \tfrac{1}{2} \log_{2}(x)$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= 2 B \cdot \frac{1}{2} \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) \\
  &= B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the celebrated **Shannon–Hartley theorem**. The quantity $C$ is the theoretical **upper bound** on the bit rate; no coding scheme can exceed it for the given $(B, S/N)$ pair.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Insight:** A common follow-up question asks: *“If the SNR doubles, does the capacity double?”* The answer is **no** — capacity grows **logarithmically** with SNR, so to double the capacity we need the SNR to grow to $SNR^{2}$. This non-intuitive property is a favourite viva question.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Pattern)

**Problem:** A transmission medium has an input power of **200 mW** and an output power of **2 mW** after traversing **5 km**. Compute (a) the total attenuation in dB, (b) the attenuation per kilometre, (c) the SNR in dB if noise power is **0.02 mW**, and (d) the channel capacity for a bandwidth of **10 kHz**.

### Part (a) — Total attenuation

$$
A = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{in}}{P_{out}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{200}{2}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(100) = 10 \times 2 = 20 \text{ dB}
$$

### Part (b) — Attenuation per kilometre

$$
\alpha = \frac{20 \text{ dB}}{5 \text{ km}} = 4 \text{ dB/km}
$$

### Part (c) — SNR in dB

$$
SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2 \text{ mW}}{0.02 \text{ mW}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(100) = 20 \text{ dB}
$$

### Part (d) — Channel capacity (Shannon)

$$
C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) = 10\,000 \cdot \log_{2}\!\left(1 + 100\right) = 10\,000 \cdot \log_{2}(101)
$$

Computing $\log_{2}(101)$:

$$
\log_{2}(101) = \frac{\log_{10}(101)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{2.0043}{0.3010} \approx 6.658 \text{ bits/s/Hz}
$$

Therefore:

$$
C = 10\,000 \times 6.658 = 66\,580 \text{ bps} \approx 66.58 \text{ kbps}
$$

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Media Characteristics at a Glance

| Parameter | Twisted Pair (UTP) | Coaxial | Optical Fibre | Microwave / Radio |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Attenuation (dB/km) | 20–50 | 5–20 | 0.2–3 | 0.01–0.1 (free-space) |
| Bandwidth | Low–Moderate | Moderate | **Very High** | Moderate–High |
| EMI Susceptibility | High | Low | **None** | Moderate–High |
| Security | Low | Low | **Very High** | Low |
| Installation Cost | **Very Low** | Low | **Very High** | Moderate |
| Typical Range | 100 m | 500 m | > 100 km | 50 km (terrestrial) |
| Repeater Spacing | 100 m | 500 m | **50–100 km** | 50 km |
| KTU Reference Use | Ethernet LAN | Cable TV, BUS | Internet backbone | Cellular backhaul |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Classification of Transmission Media

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[TRANSMISSION MEDIA] --> B[GUIDED MEDIA - WIRED]
    A --> C[UNGUIDED MEDIA - WIRELESS]
    
    B --> B1[Twisted Pair Cable]
    B1 --> B1a[UTP - Unshielded]
    B1 --> B1b[STP - Shielded]
    
    B --> B2[Coaxial Cable]
    B2 --> B2a[Baseband Coax]
    B2 --> B2b[Broadband Coax]
    
    B --> B3[Optical Fibre]
    B3 --> B3a[Single Mode Fibre - SMF]
    B3 --> B3b[Multi Mode Fibre - MMF]
    
    C --> C1[Radio Waves]
    C1 --> C1a[VLF - LF - MF]
    C1 --> C1b[HF - VHF - UHF]
    
    C --> C2[Microwaves]
    C2 --> C2a[Terrestrial Microwave]
    C2 --> C2b[Satellite Microwave]
    
    C --> C3[Infrared]
    C3 --> C3a[Line of Sight IR]
    C3 --> C3b[Diffused IR]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Signal Flow through a Guided Medium

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC[Source] --> ENC[Encoder]
    ENC --> TX[Transmitter]
    TX --> MED[Transmission Medium]
    MED --> AMP[Repeater - if needed]
    AMP --> MED2[Continued Medium]
    MED2 --> RX[Receiver]
    RX --> DEC[Decoder]
    DEC --> DST[Destination]
    NOISE[External Noise Source] -.-> MED
    NOISE -.-> MED2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed arrows from the **External Noise Source** to the **Transmission Medium** blocks model the **additive noise coupling** that justifies the $S/N$ term in the Shannon–Hartley capacity formula. A repeater is inserted when the cumulative attenuation $A_{total}$ approaches the receiver sensitivity threshold.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Attenuation vs Distance Relationship

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN[Input Power - Pin in mW] --> ATT[Attenuation Block - alpha dB per km]
    ATT --> DIST[Distance d in km]
    DIST --> OUT[Output Power - Pout in mW]
    SHN[Shannon Hartley] --> CAP[Channel Capacity C in bps]
    CAP --> BW[Bandwidth B in Hz]
    CAP --> SNR[Signal to Noise Ratio S by N]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Mermaid rendering safeguard: every node ID above begins with an alphabetic character (e.g., `SRC`, `ENC`, `MED`) and contains **no** reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`). All labels are plain uppercase alphanumeric — no bold markers, italics, or HTML tags inside the quoted label text.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Jun 2023]**
**Q:** Define the term **attenuation** in a transmission medium. A signal of power **50 mW** is launched into a cable. The received power after 4 km is **0.5 mW**. Compute the attenuation in dB/km.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Definition (1 mark):** Attenuation is the gradual loss of signal power as it propagates through a transmission medium, expressed in decibels (dB) per unit length.
**Computation (2 marks):**

$$
A = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{50}{0.5}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(100) = 20 \text{ dB total}
$$

$$
\alpha = \frac{20 \text{ dB}}{4 \text{ km}} = 5 \text{ dB/km}
$$

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**Q:** Compare **UTP** and **Optical Fibre** on the basis of (i) noise immunity, (ii) bandwidth, and (iii) typical application.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(i) Noise immunity:** UTP is **susceptible** to EMI/RFI; Optical Fibre is **completely immune** (light, not current).
- **(ii) Bandwidth:** UTP supports up to ~**250 MHz** (Cat 6); Optical Fibre supports up to **THz** range.
- **(iii) Application:** UTP is used in **office LANs and telephone lines**; Optical Fibre is used in **internet backbones, long-haul telecom, and undersea cables**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Standard Pattern

**[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024 Model Paper]**
**Q:** *(a)* Explain the **characteristics of guided transmission media** with neat diagrams and mention any two advantages of optical fibre over copper cable. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* A channel has a bandwidth of **4 kHz** and a signal-to-noise ratio of **30 dB**. Calculate the maximum theoretical data rate using the **Shannon–Hartley theorem**. If the SNR improves to **40 dB**, find the percentage increase in capacity. *(7 marks)*

### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]

1. **Twisted-Pair Cable:** A pair of insulated copper wires twisted helically. The twist rate reduces crosstalk and EMI. Sub-types: UTP (cheap, common) and STP (shielded, industrial).
2. **Coaxial Cable:** Central conductor + dielectric + braided shield + jacket. Better shielding, higher bandwidth, used in CATV and older Ethernet.
3. **Optical Fibre:** Glass core carries light by **Total Internal Reflection**. Two types — Single-Mode (long-haul, laser) and Multi-Mode (short-range, LED).
4. **Advantages of optical fibre (any two):** (i) Extremely low attenuation (~0.2 dB/km) allowing long repeater spacings; (ii) Complete immunity to EMI; (iii) Enormous bandwidth supporting Tbps rates; (iv) Highly secure — difficult to tap.

**Diagram (1 mark):** Cross-section of fibre with **core, cladding, and buffer coating** labelled.

### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Convert SNR from dB to linear scale.**

$$
SNR_{linear} = 10^{SNR_{dB}/10} = 10^{30/10} = 10^{3} = 1000
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Shannon's formula.**

$$
C_{1} = B \log_{2}(1 + SNR) = 4000 \cdot \log_{2}(1 + 1000) = 4000 \cdot \log_{2}(1001)
$$

$$
\log_{2}(1001) = \frac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{3.0004}{0.3010} \approx 9.967 \text{ bits/s/Hz}
$$

$$
C_{1} = 4000 \times 9.967 = 39\,868 \text{ bps} \approx 39.87 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 3 — Recompute for SNR = 40 dB.**

$$
SNR_{2} = 10^{40/10} = 10\,000
$$

$$
C_{2} = 4000 \cdot \log_{2}(10\,001) = 4000 \cdot 13.289 = 53\,156 \text{ bps} \approx 53.16 \text{ kbps}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage increase.**

$$
\%\text{ increase} = \frac{53.16 - 39.87}{39.87} \times 100 \approx 33.3\%
$$

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper]**
**Q:** *(a)* Discuss the **characteristics of unguided transmission media**. Compare **terrestrial microwave** and **satellite communication**. *(7 marks)*
*(b)* Derive the **Shannon–Hartley theorem** and explain why capacity grows **logarithmically** and not linearly with SNR. *(7 marks)*

### Model Answer — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Unguided media** use electromagnetic waves propagating in free space. Key characteristics:
1. **Radio Waves (3 kHz–300 GHz):** Omni-directional, follow the curvature of the earth (ground wave at LF/MF), reflected by ionosphere (sky wave at HF). Used in AM/FM radio, maritime, shortwave.
2. **Microwaves (1–300 GHz):** Line-of-sight (LOS), unidirectional, used in point-to-point links and cellular backhaul. Affected by rain, fog, and atmospheric absorption.
3. **Infrared (300 GHz–400 THz):** Short-range, line-of-sight, used in TV remotes, IRDA, indoor WLAN. Cannot penetrate walls.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Feature | Terrestrial Microwave | Satellite Microwave |
|---|---|---|
| Repeater | Line-of-sight tower | Transponder at 35 786 km |
| Range | Up to 50 km per hop | Global coverage |
| Delay | Microseconds | ~ 250 ms (one way) |
| Cost | Moderate | High (launch + ground station) |
| Attenuation | Affected by weather | Affected by rain + free-space path loss |

### Model Answer — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Derivation (5 marks):** Begin with Nyquist capacity $C = 2B \log_{2}(M)$ for $M$ levels. Due to additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN), the maximum number of distinguishable levels is $M_{max} = \sqrt{1 + S/N}$. Substituting gives:

$$
C = B \log_{2}\!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right)
$$

**Logarithmic growth explanation (2 marks):** Because the logarithm function compresses large linear ranges, a tenfold increase in SNR increases $C$ by only $\log_{2}(10) \approx 3.32$ bits per second per Hz. Hence doubling the SNR does **not** double the capacity — to double the capacity the SNR must increase by $6 \text{ dB} \times$ (current SNR) — i.e., dramatically more power is required, which has profound implications for power-limited wireless systems (e.g., satellite, IoT).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit trap:** When SNR is given in dB, students often forget to convert to linear scale using $10^{SNR_{dB}/10}$. Always show both steps.
> 2. **Logarithm base trap:** Shannon's formula uses $\log_{2}$, not $\log_{10}$. If your calculator is in base-10 mode, divide by $0.3010$ to convert.
> 3. **Sign error in attenuation:** A negative sign is implied; the convention is $|A| = 10\log(P_{in}/P_{out})$. Writing $A = 10\log(P_{out}/P_{in})$ gives a *gain* and is a 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Confusing bandwidth with data rate:** Bandwidth is measured in **Hz**; data rate is measured in **bps**. Examiners penalise interchanging these.
> 5. **Forgetting the "1+" in Shannon's formula:** $\log_{2}(1 + S/N)$ is mandatory, not $\log_{2}(S/N)$. A 2-mark deduction is typical.
> 6. **Not labelling the fibre diagram:** Markers explicitly look for labels like *core*, *cladding*, *buffer*, and *jacket* on any cross-section. A diagram without labels scores zero.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Two families** of media: **Guided** (twisted pair, coaxial, fibre) and **Unguided** (radio, microwave, IR).
- [x] **Attenuation equation** $A_{dB} = 10\log_{10}(P_{in}/P_{out})$; remember negative sign convention.
- [x] **Optical fibre** has the **lowest attenuation** (~0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm) and **highest bandwidth** (THz).
- [x] **UTP** dominates LANs; **coaxial** is legacy; **fibre** is the future backbone.
- [x] **Wireless** spectrum: VLF/LF/MF/HF/VHF/UHF/SHF/EHF — remember the lower the frequency, the longer the wavelength, the better the diffraction, the worse the bandwidth.
- [x] **Shannon–Hartley** theorem: $C = B\log_{2}(1 + S/N)$ — capacity grows **logarithmically** with SNR.
- [x] **SNR in dB** = $10\log_{10}(S/N)$ — convert before substituting into Shannon.
- [x] **Free-space path loss** $L = 32.4 + 20\log_{10}(d_{km}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{MHz})$ — doubles (≈ +6 dB) when distance or frequency doubles.
- [x] **Satellite altitude** for geostationary orbit = **35 786 km**; one-way propagation delay ≈ **250 ms**.
- [x] **Nyquist (noiseless)** vs **Shannon (noisy)** — always quote both limits when designing a system.
- [x] **Total Internal Reflection** is the physical principle behind optical fibre; requires the angle of incidence to exceed the **critical angle** $\theta_c = \sin^{-1}(n_2/n_1)$.
- [x] **Crosstalk** is dominant in UTP; **attenuation distortion** is dominant in coaxial; **chromatic dispersion** is dominant in MMF; **rain fade** is dominant in microwave satellite links.
- [x] **Velocity factor** $v = c/n$: for copper $v \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s; for fibre $v \approx 2 \times 10^{8}$ m/s (with $n \approx 1.5$).
- [x] **Wavelength** $\lambda = v/f$ — used for antenna sizing (half-wave dipole = $\lambda/2$).
- [x] **Microwave frequencies** are reserved in the **SHF band (3–30 GHz)** for point-to-point links to avoid interference with lower-frequency broadcast services.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synchronous TDM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Synchronous TDM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Synchronous Time Division Multiplexing (Synchronous TDM)** is a digital multiplexing technique in which multiple lower-bit-rate digital input signals are combined and transmitted over a single higher-bit-rate communication channel by allocating **fixed, pre-determined time slots** to each input source in a recurring sequence (a *frame*). The multiplexer assigns one time slot per source per frame, **regardless of whether the source actually has data to transmit**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (OECST612 - Module 3):**
> Synchronous TDM is a foundational concept in the "Digital Multiplexing & Spread Spectrum" unit. The 2024 Scheme places heavy emphasis on (1) frame structure, (2) bit-rate calculation, and (3) the role of synchronization (framing) bits. Questions of 7–14 marks are routinely asked linking frame mathematics to the link bit rate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition in KTU Board Examiner's Words:**
> "Synchronous TDM is a method of multiplexing in which the time slots are pre-assigned to the sources, fixed in position, and are transmitted in a definite cyclic order, irrespective of whether the source has data or remains idle."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **rotating 4-spoke Ferris wheel** (the *frame*) at a busy railway ticket counter:

- 🧍 Person A, Person B, Person C, and Person D stand in fixed positions on the wheel (the **4 input sources**).
- The wheel rotates every **5 seconds** (the **frame period**). Each person gets exactly **5 seconds** at the counter — even if Person C has no ticket to buy, his slot still rotates around and is sent out (an *empty slot*).
- The ticket clerk (the **high-speed link**) processes one person per slot in a strict, predictable order: A → B → C → D → A → B → C → D ...

That predictable, slot-per-source-every-cycle behavior is **Synchronous TDM**. The "synchronous" here means the multiplexer clock and the demultiplexer clock are **synchronized** (locked in time), so the demultiplexer always knows which slot belongs to which source without needing any addressing header.

### Contrast in One Line
| Feature | Synchronous TDM | Statistical TDM |
|---|---|---|
| Slot allocation | Fixed, pre-assigned | Dynamic, on demand |
| Wasted slots | Yes, if source is idle | No |
| Addressing bits | Not needed | Required |
| Complexity | Lower | Higher |

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

- **Frame Rate (framing rate)** = Number of TDM frames transmitted per second.
- **Slot Duration** = Time occupied by one source's data in a frame.
- **Frame Duration** = Total time to transmit all slots of one frame.
- **Link Bit Rate (R)** = Sum of source bit rates + framing overhead.
- **Duty Cycle** = Fraction of time a source is actually using its assigned slot.

> [!TIP]
> Remember the **golden rule of Synchronous TDM**: *If N sources are multiplexed and each contributes n bits per slot, the link bit rate must be at least N × n × (frame rate)*, plus a small percentage for synchronization/framing bits.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Working Principle

The Synchronous TDM system can be understood in the following structured sequence:

1. **Input Sampling Phase** — The TDM multiplexer contains a high-speed electronic switch (rotating selector) that sequentially samples each of the *N* input digital sources.
2. **Time Slot Allocation** — During each **slot duration (t_slot)**, a fixed number of bits (n bits) from a specific source is placed onto the high-speed link.
3. **Frame Assembly** — After collecting one slot from each of the *N* sources (plus possibly framing bits), a complete **TDM frame** is formed.
4. **Synchronization (Framing) Bits** — One or more bits are added at the start/end of each frame to allow the demultiplexer to identify the **start of a new frame** reliably.
5. **Transmission** — The assembled frame is transmitted at the link bit rate **R** over the shared channel.
6. **Demultiplexing** — At the receiver, a synchronized demultiplexer (rotating in lockstep with the multiplexer) extracts the slots in the same order and routes each to its correct output line.
7. **Idle Handling** — If a source has **no data**, the multiplexer still places a **"dummy" or null pattern** in that source's slot. The slot is *never* skipped.

## 2.2 Detailed Frame Anatomy

A standard Synchronous TDM frame is laid out as follows:

```
| F | S1 | S2 | S3 | ... | SN |
```

Where:
- **F** = Framing bit(s) / Synchronization pattern
- **S_i** = Slot i, holding n bits from source i

The frame repeats **F_frame** times per second (the **frame rate**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why framing bits?** In long streams of identical bit patterns, the receiver can lose track of where one frame ends and the next begins. A unique framing bit pattern (e.g., `010101...` or a specific marker) provides a "frame start" reference.

## 2.3 Interleaving Levels in Synchronous TDM

There are **two granularities** of interleaving used in exam questions:

| Interleaving Type | Slot Size | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Bit Interleaving** | 1 bit per slot per source | High-speed telecom backbones (E1/T1) |
| **Character (Byte) Interleaving** | 1 character (8 bits) per slot per source | Computer/terminal multiplexing |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most-tested formula set** in KTU University Exams for Synchronous TDM. Memorize the relationships.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Frame size (total bits/frame) | $F_b = N \cdot n + F_{ov}$ | bits | $F_{ov}$ = framing overhead bits |
| 2 | Slot duration | $t_{slot} = \dfrac{1}{R}$ | seconds | $R$ = link bit rate in bps |
| 3 | Frame duration | $T_f = N \cdot t_{slot} = \dfrac{N \cdot n}{R}$ | seconds | Time for one full frame |
| 4 | Frame rate | $f_f = \dfrac{1}{T_f} = \dfrac{R}{N \cdot n}$ | frames/sec | Inversely related to N |
| 5 | Source bit rate (equal sources) | $R_{src} = \dfrac{R}{N}$ | bps | When all sources are identical |
| 6 | Link bit rate (no overhead) | $R = \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_{src,i}$ | bps | Sum of source bit rates |
| 7 | Link bit rate (with framing) | $R = (N \cdot n + F_{ov}) \cdot f_f$ | bps | Most accurate form |
| 8 | Time to transmit 1 source bit | $t_{bit\_src} = \dfrac{1}{R_{src}} = \dfrac{N}{R}$ | seconds | Source sees a 1/N duty cycle |
| 9 | Sampling rate of one source | $f_s = f_f$ | samples/sec | Equals frame rate |
| 10 | Throughput utilization (η) | $\eta = \dfrac{N \cdot n}{N \cdot n + F_{ov}}$ | ratio (0–1) | Efficiency of link |

> [!TIP]
> The **CRITICAL exam relation** to drill into your head:
> $$\boxed{R_{link} = N \times R_{source} \quad \text{(when all sources identical, ignoring framing bits)}}$$

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Telephony (T1, E1, DS1, DS3 lines)** — The classic Synchronous TDM application. A T1 line multiplexes 24 voice channels of 64 kbps each onto a 1.544 Mbps link (1.544 = 24 × 0.064 + 0.008 framing).
- **PSTN Backbone Networks** — Long-distance carriers aggregate hundreds of voice calls onto fiber using Synchronous TDM hierarchies.
- **SONET/SDH** — High-capacity optical networks use synchronous byte-interleaved multiplexing at much higher rates (STM-1 = 155.52 Mbps, etc.).
- **Industrial Control Buses** — Synchronous TDM-style slot assignment is used in field buses for deterministic, real-time sensor data delivery.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Link Bit Rate from Source Parameters

**Problem Setup:** $N$ identical digital sources, each transmitting at $R_{src}$ bps, are multiplexed using Synchronous TDM with $n$ bits per source per frame and $F_{ov}$ framing bits per frame. Derive the link bit rate $R$.

**Step-by-Step:**

- **Step 1 — Bits per source per second:**
  Since each source transmits at $R_{src}$ bps, in one second each source emits $R_{src}$ bits.

- **Step 2 — Bits per frame from one source:**
  If the multiplexer visits each source once per frame, then in one frame a source contributes $n$ bits. Therefore the number of frames per second (frame rate) is:
  $$f_f = \frac{R_{src}}{n} \quad \text{frames per second}$$

- **Step 3 — Bits per frame from all sources:**
  With $N$ sources each contributing $n$ bits, plus $F_{ov}$ framing bits:
  $$B_{frame} = N \cdot n + F_{ov} \quad \text{bits per frame}$$

- **Step 4 — Link bit rate:**
  The link transmits $B_{frame}$ bits once every $\dfrac{1}{f_f}$ seconds. Hence:
  $$R = B_{frame} \cdot f_f = (N \cdot n + F_{ov}) \cdot \frac{R_{src}}{n}$$
  
  For the common exam assumption $F_{ov} \ll N \cdot n$ (overhead negligible), this simplifies to:
  $$R \approx N \cdot R_{src}$$

- **Step 5 — Sanity check:**
  Plugging $N = 24$, $R_{src} = 64{,}000$ bps, $n = 8$ bits (1 byte/frame for voice PCM):
  $$f_f = \frac{64{,}000}{8} = 8{,}000 \text{ frames/sec}$$
  $$R \approx 24 \times 64{,}000 = 1{,}536{,}000 \text{ bps}$$
  This is the well-known T1 line rate (1.544 Mbps once the 8 kbps framing bit is added — exactly matching reality). ✅

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Frame Duration, Slot Duration, and Synchronizer Frequency

**Problem Setup:** Given link bit rate $R = 1.544$ Mbps, $N = 24$ sources, $n = 8$ bits, find (a) slot duration, (b) frame duration, (c) time to transmit 1 bit of a single source.

**Step-by-Step:**

- **Step 1 — Slot duration:**
  $$\begin{aligned}
  t_{slot} &= \frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{1.544 \times 10^6} \\
  t_{slot} &\approx 647.67 \text{ ns} \quad \text{(1 bit time on the link)}
  \end{aligned}$$

- **Step 2 — Frame duration:**
  Each frame holds $N \cdot n = 24 \times 8 = 192$ data bits. Plus $F_{ov} = 1$ framing bit = 193 bits/frame.
  $$\begin{aligned}
  T_f &= \frac{193}{1.544 \times 10^6} \\
  T_f &= 125 \text{ microseconds} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s}
  \end{aligned}$$

- **Step 3 — Time to transmit 1 source bit:**
  Since one source gets 8 bits in $T_f = 125$ μs, one source bit occupies:
  $$\begin{aligned}
  t_{bit\_src} &= \frac{T_f}{8} = \frac{125 \text{ μs}}{8} \\
  t_{bit\_src} &= 15.625 \text{ μs} = \frac{1}{64{,}000} \text{ s}
  \end{aligned}$$
  This corresponds to a source bit rate of 64 kbps — the standard PCM voice channel. ✅

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Synchronization Problem (Why Framing Bits Are Mandatory)

**Problem Setup:** Show that without framing bits, a long string of identical bits from idle sources could cause frame misalignment at the demultiplexer.

**Analytical Walkthrough:**

- **Step 1 — Failure scenario:**
  Suppose all sources are idle and each source outputs continuous `0` bits. The TDM frame becomes `000...0` (length $N \cdot n$) repeated endlessly.

- **Step 2 — Demultiplexer ambiguity:**
  The demultiplexer must identify the **start of each frame** to correctly assign slots. With an unbroken stream of zeros, the demultiplexer has **no edge or marker** to know "frame 1 starts here."

- **Step 3 — The role of framing bit:**
  By inserting a known unique bit pattern (e.g., `1010...` or a specific flag bit) at the start of each frame, the demultiplexer can:
  - **Detect** the start of frame via bit-pattern matching.
  - **Recover clock** from the regular appearance of this pattern.
  - **Realign** if a slip occurs (loss of synchronization).

- **Step 4 — Overhead cost:**
  Adding 1 framing bit to a 193-bit frame yields an efficiency:
  $$\eta = \frac{192}{193} \approx 99.48\%$$
  This is the *efficiency penalty* paid for synchronization — usually negligible at high $N$, but significant for small $N$.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Problem-Solving Decision Tree

| Exam Question Pattern | First Formula to Apply | Final Answer Form |
|---|---|---|
| "Find link bit rate" | $R = N \cdot R_{src}$ | bps / kbps / Mbps |
| "Find frame duration" | $T_f = (N \cdot n + F_{ov}) / R$ | seconds / μs |
| "Find frame rate" | $f_f = R_{src} / n$ | frames/sec |
| "Find slot duration" | $t_{slot} = 1/R$ | seconds |
| "Find efficiency" | $\eta = N \cdot n / (N \cdot n + F_{ov})$ | percentage |
| "How long to transmit X bytes of one source?" | $t = X \cdot 8 \cdot N / R$ | seconds |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.3 Synchronization & Demultiplexer Locking Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram

    participant CLK as Common Clock
    participant DMX as TDM DMUX

    CLK-->>DMX: Distribute clock (synchronization channel)
```

# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]
**Define Synchronous TDM. How is it different from Asynchronous (Statistical) TDM?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Synchronous Time Division Multiplexing is a digital multiplexing technique in which multiple input sources share a common high-speed channel by means of **fixed, pre-assigned time slots** in a repeating frame structure. Each source is given one time slot per frame, and the slot is transmitted **regardless of whether the source has data** — idle sources still occupy their slot with a null pattern.

| Aspect | Synchronous TDM | Asynchronous TDM |
|---|---|---|
| Slot allocation | Fixed | Dynamic |
| Idle source behavior | Slot still transmitted | Slot skipped or shared |
| Addressing bits | Not required | Required in each slot |
| Link efficiency | Lower (wasted idle slots) | Higher |

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for the key contrast, 1 for the efficiency implication.)**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What are framing bits in Synchronous TDM? Why are they necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Framing bits** (also called synchronization or flag bits) are special bits added at the start (or end) of every TDM frame. They form a **unique, known bit pattern** (e.g., alternating `1010...`) that the demultiplexer can detect to identify the start of each frame.

They are **necessary** for the following reasons:
1. **Frame Synchronization** — The demultiplexer must know exactly where one frame ends and the next begins. Without framing bits, a long stream of identical bits (e.g., all zeros from idle sources) would cause the demultiplexer to **lose alignment**.
2. **Clock Recovery** — The periodic appearance of the framing pattern allows the receiver to maintain bit/frame synchronization with the transmitter.
3. **Error Detection / Resync** — A corrupted or missing framing pattern signals the demultiplexer to initiate a resynchronization procedure.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 each for two reasons.)**

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**
**Four voice channels, each sampled at 8 kHz and quantized to 8 bits, are multiplexed using Synchronous TDM with 1 framing bit per frame. Calculate:**
(i) Bit duration on the link
(ii) Frame duration
(iii) Total link bit rate
(iv) Frame efficiency

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

- **Given:**
  - $N = 4$ sources
  - Sampling rate $f_s = 8 \text{ kHz}$ → frame rate = 8,000 frames/sec
  - $n = 8$ bits per source per frame
  - $F_{ov} = 1$ framing bit per frame

- **(i) Bit duration on the link:**
  First, compute link bit rate:
  $$R = (N \cdot n + F_{ov}) \cdot f_f = (4 \times 8 + 1) \times 8000 = 33 \times 8000 = 264{,}000 \text{ bps}$$
  Bit duration:
  $$t_{bit} = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{264{,}000} \approx 3.788 \text{ μs}$$

- **(ii) Frame duration:**
  $$T_f = \frac{1}{f_f} = \frac{1}{8000} = 125 \text{ μs}$$

- **(iii) Total link bit rate:**
  $$R = 264 \text{ kbps}$$ (as computed above)

- **(iv) Frame efficiency:**
  $$\eta = \frac{N \cdot n}{N \cdot n + F_{ov}} = \frac{32}{33} \approx 0.9697 = 96.97\%$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**
**A T1 carrier system multiplexes 24 voice channels, each of 64 kbps, using Synchronous TDM with 1 framing bit per frame and 8 bits per channel per frame. Determine:**
(i) Frame rate
(ii) Number of bits per frame
(iii) Line bit rate
(iv) Duration of one bit

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

- **Given:**
  - $N = 24$, $R_{src} = 64 \text{ kbps}$, $n = 8$ bits, $F_{ov} = 1$ bit/frame

- **(i) Frame rate:**
  $$f_f = \frac{R_{src}}{n} = \frac{64{,}000}{8} = 8000 \text{ frames/sec}$$

- **(ii) Number of bits per frame:**
  $$B_{frame} = N \cdot n + F_{ov} = 24 \times 8 + 1 = 193 \text{ bits/frame}$$

- **(iii) Line bit rate:**
  $$R = B_{frame} \times f_f = 193 \times 8000 = 1{,}544{,}000 \text{ bps} = 1.544 \text{ Mbps}$$

- **(iv) Duration of one bit:**
  $$t_{bit} = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{1.544 \times 10^6} \approx 0.6477 \text{ μs} = 647.7 \text{ ns}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> A **very common mistake** is forgetting to include the **framing bit** when computing the line bit rate. Students often write 1.536 Mbps (= 24 × 64 kbps) instead of the correct 1.544 Mbps. The framing bit is **mandatory** and contributes the extra 8 kbps (= 1 bit × 8000 frames/sec). Also, mixing up *frame rate* with *bit rate* costs full marks — keep them strictly separate.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**
**Explain with a neat block diagram the operation of a Synchronous TDM system. How does the demultiplexer maintain synchronization with the multiplexer?**

**Model Answer Outline:**

- **Block Diagram:** Multiplexer (rotary switch) — High-speed link — Demultiplexer (rotary switch) with frame detector and synchronizer.
- **Operation:**
  1. Multiplexer rotates at frame rate $f_f$, sampling each input for slot duration $t_{slot} = 1/R$.
  2. Data is assembled into frames with framing bits.
  3. Frames are transmitted on the high-speed link.
  4. Demultiplexer uses a phase-locked loop (PLL) locked to the framing bit pattern.
  5. The demultiplexer switch rotates in **phase synchronism** with the multiplexer.
  6. Each extracted slot is routed to its corresponding output.
- **Synchronization Mechanisms:**
  1. **Framing bit detection** — Pattern recognition.
  2. **PLL-based clock recovery** — Recovering the bit clock from incoming bit stream.
  3. **Dedicated clock channel** (in some systems).
  4. **Re-sync procedure** — If pattern is lost for $K$ consecutive frames, demultiplexer enters search mode.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified]**
**Three sources with bit rates 1 kbps, 2 kbps, and 3 kbps are to be multiplexed using Synchronous TDM. Design a scheme to handle the unequal bit rates. Calculate the link bit rate assuming 1 framing bit per output frame and 8 bits per source per output frame.**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

- **Strategy for unequal bit rates:** Use the **lowest bit rate source** as the reference. Faster sources are allocated **multiple slots** per frame proportional to their bit rate.

- **Step 1 — Bit-rate ratio:**
  - Source 1: 1 kbps → 1 slot per frame
  - Source 2: 2 kbps → 2 slots per frame
  - Source 3: 3 kbps → 3 slots per frame
  - Total slots per frame: $1 + 2 + 3 = 6$ slots

- **Step 2 — Frame rate:**
  Reference (slowest) source: $R_1 = 1$ kbps, $n = 8$ bits/slot
  $$f_f = \frac{R_1}{n} = \frac{1000}{8} = 125 \text{ frames/sec}$$

- **Step 3 — Bits per frame:**
  $$B_{frame} = (\text{total slots}) \cdot n + F_{ov} = 6 \times 8 + 1 = 49 \text{ bits/frame}$$

- **Step 4 — Link bit rate:**
  $$R = 49 \times 125 = 6125 \text{ bps} \approx 6.125 \text{ kbps}$$

- **Verification:**
  - Source 1 effective rate: 1 slot × 8 bits × 125 = 1000 bps ✅
  - Source 2 effective rate: 2 slots × 8 bits × 125 = 2000 bps ✅
  - Source 3 effective rate: 3 slots × 8 bits × 125 = 3000 bps ✅
  - Total source rate: 6 kbps; overhead: 0.125 kbps; link: 6.125 kbps ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> For unequal-bit-rate problems, students frequently **forget to scale the number of slots per source** and just assign 1 slot to each. This yields incorrect source bit rates. The golden rule: *Number of slots per source = (Source bit rate) / (Lowest source bit rate) × base slot count.* Failing to add the framing bit in the final link bit rate is the **second most common** error.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Save this checklist and review it the night before your KTU exam.

- ✅ **Synchronous TDM** = Fixed time slots, pre-assigned to sources, repeated cyclically.
- ✅ **Frame** = 1 set of slots, one per source, plus framing bits.
- ✅ **Slot duration** = $\dfrac{1}{R_{link}}$ seconds.
- ✅ **Frame duration** = $N \cdot t_{slot} = \dfrac{N \cdot n}{R_{link}}$ seconds.
- ✅ **Frame rate** = $\dfrac{1}{T_f}$ = source sampling rate = $\dfrac{R_{src}}{n}$.
- ✅ **Link bit rate** (no overhead) = $N \times R_{src}$.
- ✅ **Link bit rate** (with framing) = $(N \cdot n + F_{ov}) \times f_f$.
- ✅ **T1 line** = 24 × 64 kbps + 8 kbps framing = **1.544 Mbps** (memorize this number).
- ✅ **E1 line** = 32 × 64 kbps (30 voice + 2 signaling) = **2.048 Mbps**.
- ✅ **Framing bits are essential** — they prevent demultiplexer drift in long idle streams.
- ✅ **Bit interleaving** → used in high-speed telecom; **character interleaving** → used in computer/terminal multiplexing.
- ✅ **Idle source** = still gets its slot, filled with dummy/null bits (wasted capacity).
- ✅ **Synchronization** achieved by framing bit pattern detection + PLL-based clock recovery.
- ✅ **Efficiency** $\eta = \dfrac{\text{data bits}}{\text{data bits + framing bits}}$ — always less than 100%.
- ✅ **For unequal-rate sources** → multiple slots per frame, scaled by the bit-rate ratio.
- ✅ **Comparison with Statistical TDM** → Synchronous wastes slots but is simpler; Statistical uses dynamic allocation with addressing overhead.

---

> **End of Notes — Synchronous TDM (Module 3, OECST612 — KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Statistical Time Division Multiplexing (Statistical TDM)**, also known as **Asynchronous TDM** or **Intelligent TDM**, is a dynamic channel-access method in which the multiplexer (MUX) does **not pre-assign** fixed time slots to each input source. Instead, time slots on the shared high-speed output link are **allocated on demand** to those input lines that actually have data ready to transmit.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** In Statistical TDM, the total available bandwidth of the link is divided among the active sources only. The multiplexer scans the input lines cyclically, but appends a data segment to the output frame **only if the input line has a data buffer that is non-empty**. This dramatically improves bandwidth utilization when the sources are "bursty" (i.e., they transmit data intermittently rather than continuously).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Shared Taxi Stand at a Railway Station**
>
> Imagine a busy railway station with **100 passengers** (input sources) who need to reach the city center. In **Synchronous TDM**, you book **100 separate taxis** (one per passenger) — 80 of them drive **empty** because those 80 passengers haven't arrived at the stand yet. Wastage!
>
> In **Statistical TDM**, you have only **20 taxis** waiting at the stand. As soon as a passenger arrives, the dispatcher puts them into the next available taxi. A small "address tag" is written on the passenger's bag saying **"Passenger 57"**, so when the taxi reaches the city, the system knows which destination the passenger belongs to. You save fuel, save money, and deliver everyone who actually needs a ride — instantly!
>
> The **address tag** corresponds to the **source address field** added inside the Statistical TDM frame.

### 1.3 Why Statistical TDM is Needed — Core Problem Statement

In Synchronous TDM, even when a source has **no data to send**, its dedicated slot in the frame is transmitted as **empty/idle padding**. For bursty traffic (typical of computer networks, terminals, and IoT devices), the line efficiency drops below **30%**, which is unacceptable for high-cost wide-area links.

Statistical TDM solves this by transmitting **only the active data**, raising the line efficiency to typically **80%–100%**.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Standard Parameters (Memorize for Exam):**
> - **Maximum Number of Input Sources:** $N$ (where $N$ is typically much larger than the output slot capacity $k$)
> - **Number of Output Time Slots per Frame:** $k$, where $k < N$
> - **Output Frame Length (in bits):** $(D + A) \times k$ bits, where $D$ = data bits per slot, $A$ = address bits per slot
> - **Typical line efficiency:** $\approx 0.8$ to $1.0$ (80% – 100%)
> - **Synchronous TDM efficiency baseline:** $\approx 0.25$ to $0.30$ for bursty sources
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture — How Statistical TDM Works

The Statistical TDM system can be broken down into a sequence of well-defined operational steps:

1. **Input Buffer Pool:** Each of the $N$ input sources is connected to a small **First-In-First-Out (FIFO) buffer** inside the multiplexer. The buffers store incoming data while the MUX is busy serving other lines.
2. **Cyclic Scan:** The MUX continuously scans the $N$ input lines in a fixed round-robin order (Source 1 → Source 2 → … → Source $N$ → Source 1 …).
3. **Conditional Capture:** During the scan, the MUX checks if the current source's buffer is **non-empty**. If empty, the MUX **skips** that source and moves to the next. If non-empty, it copies a chunk of data (typically a byte or a few bytes) into the next available output slot.
4. **Address Tagging:** Because the output frame is dynamically packed, the MUX **prefixes each data chunk with an address field** ($A$ bits) identifying which source it came from. The demultiplexer at the receiving end uses this address to route the data back to the correct output line.
5. **Frame Transmission:** The packed output frame is transmitted across the high-speed shared link.
6. **Demultiplexing:** At the receiver, the DEMUX reads the address field of each slot and delivers the data to the corresponding output port.

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Crucial Differences vs. Synchronous TDM:**
> 1. **Slot Allocation:** Synchronous TDM = **pre-assigned, fixed-position** slots. Statistical TDM = **on-demand, dynamic-position** slots.
> 2. **Addressing Required:** Statistical TDM **must** carry an address field in every slot. Synchronous TDM does **not**, because slot position is the implicit address.

## 2.2 The Role of $k$ vs. $N$ — Why $k < N$

The output frame of Statistical TDM contains only $k$ data slots, while the multiplexer handles $N$ possible input sources where $k \leq N$. This is because, statistically, **at any given instant, only a fraction of $N$ sources will be active**. The MUX designer chooses $k$ based on the **expected peak number of simultaneously active sources**, using Erlang B / Erlang C traffic engineering models (beyond KTU scope, but referenced in advanced courses).

If traffic suddenly exceeds $k$ (overload), buffers fill up and **data loss (overflow)** occurs. The system must therefore be dimensioned for the expected average plus a safety margin.

## 2.3 Frame Structure of Statistical TDM

A Statistical TDM output frame typically has the following structure:

| Field | Bits | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Flag / Sync | $F$ | Marks frame start (e.g., 8 bits) |
| Slot 1 Address | $A$ | Identifies source of slot 1 data (e.g., $\log_2 N$ bits) |
| Slot 1 Data | $D$ | Payload from source 1 (e.g., 8 bits) |
| Slot 2 Address | $A$ | Identifies source of slot 2 data |
| Slot 2 Data | $D$ | Payload from source 2 |
| ... | ... | ... |
| Slot $k$ Address | $A$ | Identifies source of slot $k$ data |
| Slot $k$ Data | $D$ | Payload from source $k$ |
| Trailer / CRC | $T$ | Error checking (e.g., 16 or 32 bits) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they are KTU exam favorites:**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Output Frame Length | $L = F + k(A + D) + T$ | Total bits per output frame |
| 2 | Output Data Rate Required | $R_{out} = k \times R_{in}$ | Where $R_{in}$ = input rate per active source |
| 3 | Average Output Frame Rate | $f_{frame} = \dfrac{N \times \text{activity factor}}{k}$ | Frames per second needed |
| 4 | Line Efficiency (Statistical) | $\eta_{stat} = \dfrac{k \cdot D}{L} = \dfrac{k \cdot D}{F + k(A+D) + T}$ | Fraction of useful data bits |
| 5 | Line Efficiency (Synchronous, for comparison) | $\eta_{sync} = \dfrac{k \cdot D}{N \cdot D} = \dfrac{k}{N}$ | If $N$ inputs each get a $D$-bit slot |
| 6 | Number of Address Bits Needed | $A = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ | To uniquely identify each of $N$ sources |
| 7 | Bandwidth Gain (approximate) | $G \approx \dfrac{N}{k} \times \dfrac{1}{1 + A/D}$ | Relative saving over synchronous TDM |

> **Engineering Utility:** Statistical TDM is the conceptual ancestor of **packet switching**, **statistical multiplexing in ATM**, and **dynamic slot allocation in DOCSIS cable modems**. Modern routers, switches, and 4G/5G base stations all rely on statistical multiplexing to handle bursty IP traffic economically.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Number of Output Slots $k$

We want to find the **minimum value of $k$** that satisfies a given Quality of Service (overflow probability $\leq P_{overflow}$), and then compute the resulting **line efficiency**.

### Step 1 — Define the Load Offered to the Multiplexer

Let there be $N$ input sources. Each source is in the "active" state with probability $p$ (the activity factor). The number of active sources at any instant, $X$, follows a **Binomial distribution**:

$$P(X = i) = \binom{N}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{N-i}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Expected Number of Active Sources

The average number of simultaneously active sources is:

$$E[X] = \sum_{i=0}^{N} i \cdot P(X=i) = N \cdot p$$

This is the **average load** offered to the multiplexer in terms of number of active sources.

### Step 3 — Choose $k$ to Bound the Overflow Probability

We need $k$ such that:

$$P(X > k) = \sum_{i=k+1}^{N} \binom{N}{i} p^{i} (1-p)^{N-i} \leq P_{overflow}^{target}$$

In KTU problems, $k$ is usually given directly, or you are asked to compute efficiency for a given $k$.

### Step 4 — Derive the Line Efficiency

Line efficiency is the ratio of **useful payload bits per frame** to **total transmitted bits per frame**:

Useful payload per frame = $k \cdot D$ bits (data only — addresses are overhead).

Total bits per frame = $F + k(A + D) + T$.

Therefore:

$$\eta_{stat} = \frac{k \cdot D}{F + k(A + D) + T}$$

> **Step 4 explanation:** We count only the data bits ($k \cdot D$) as "useful" because the address, flag, and trailer bits are *overhead* required to make the system work. Forgetting to include the address bits is the most common KTU mistake.

### Step 5 — Compare with Synchronous TDM Efficiency

In Synchronous TDM, the frame has $N$ slots (one per source) with no address bits:

$$\eta_{sync} = \frac{k \cdot D}{N \cdot D} = \frac{k}{N}$$

The **efficiency gain** is therefore:

$$G = \frac{\eta_{stat}}{\eta_{sync}} = \frac{N \cdot D}{F + k(A+D) + T}$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Pattern)

> **Problem (KTU 2024 Pattern):**
> 4 input data sources each operate at **1 kbps**. Only **2 sources are active at any time on average** (statistical assumption). Each source generates data in **8-bit chunks**. Each Statistical TDM output frame must carry **2 data slots**, an **8-bit flag**, a **16-bit trailer**, and a **2-bit source address per slot**. Compute:
> (a) Output frame length
> (b) Output data rate
> (c) Line efficiency

### Part (a) — Output Frame Length

Given: $N = 4$, $k = 2$, $D = 8$ bits, $A = 2$ bits, $F = 8$ bits, $T = 16$ bits.

$$L = F + k(A + D) + T$$

Substitute:

$$L = 8 + 2 \times (2 + 8) + 16$$

$$L = 8 + 2 \times 10 + 16$$

$$L = 8 + 20 + 16 = 44 \text{ bits per frame}$$

### Part (b) — Output Data Rate

Each input source produces 1 kbps = 1000 bits/sec. With 2 active sources on average, the aggregate input rate is:

$$R_{agg} = 2 \times 1000 = 2000 \text{ bits/sec}$$

Since each output frame carries 2 data slots × 8 bits = 16 data bits, the frame transmission rate must be:

$$f_{frame} = \frac{R_{agg}}{k \cdot D} = \frac{2000}{2 \times 8} = \frac{2000}{16} = 125 \text{ frames/sec}$$

Total output data rate (including overhead):

$$R_{out} = f_{frame} \times L = 125 \times 44 = 5500 \text{ bits/sec} = 5.5 \text{ kbps}$$

### Part (c) — Line Efficiency

$$\eta_{stat} = \frac{k \cdot D}{L} = \frac{2 \times 8}{44} = \frac{16}{44} = 0.3636$$

$$\eta_{stat} \approx 36.36\%$$

**Comparison with Synchronous TDM** (no addressing, 4 slots):

$$\eta_{sync} = \frac{k}{N} = \frac{2}{4} = 50\%$$

> **Counter-intuitive insight:** In this specific case, the overhead of the address and trailer fields in Statistical TDM actually *reduces* efficiency compared to a "perfect" Synchronous TDM with no addressing. This is why the design of $k$ matters — Statistical TDM only wins when $k \ll N$ and source activity $p \ll 1$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Statistical TDM vs. Synchronous TDM

| Parameter | Synchronous TDM | Statistical TDM |
|---|---|---|
| Slot allocation | Fixed, pre-assigned | Dynamic, on-demand |
| Source addressing needed? | No (positional) | Yes (explicit address field) |
| Wasted slots when source idle | Yes — always transmitted empty | None — only active sources served |
| Line efficiency for bursty traffic | Low (≈ 25–30%) | High (≈ 80–100%) |
| Buffer overflow risk | None (slot is guaranteed) | Yes — when more than $k$ sources active |
| Hardware complexity | Lower | Higher (need buffers + scanner logic) |
| Best suited for | Constant-bit-rate voice circuits | Bursty computer/terminal data |
| Conceptual successor | — | Packet switching, ATM, IP |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Statistical TDM Multiplexer + Demultiplexer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Source 1"] --> B1["Buffer 1 FIFO"]
    S2["Source 2"] --> B2["Buffer 2 FIFO"]
    S3["Source 3"] --> B3["Buffer 3 FIFO"]
    S4["Source 4"] --> B4["Buffer 4 FIFO"]
    S5["Source 5"] --> B5["Buffer 5 FIFO"]

    B1 --> SCN["Cyclic Scanner and MUX Controller"]
    B2 --> SCN
    B3 --> SCN
    B4 --> SCN
    B5 --> SCN

    SCN -->|"Packs data only from non-empty buffers"| FRM["Output Frame Builder: Flag plus Slot1 Addr Data plus Slot2 Addr Data plus Trailer"]

    FRM -->|"High speed shared link"| CH["Transmission Channel"]

    CH --> DMX["DEMUX with Address Decoder"]

    DMX -->|"Addr 01"| R1["Output Port 1"]
    DMX -->|"Addr 10"| R2["Output Port 2"]
    DMX -->|"Addr 11"| R3["Output Port 3"]
    DMX -->|"Other addr"| R4["Output Port 4"]
    DMX -->|"Default drop"| R5["Output Port 5"]
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram: Statistical TDM Operation (Decision Tree)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start of frame build cycle"] --> B["Set slot counter i equals 1"]
    B --> C{"Is source i buffer non-empty?"}
    C -->|"Yes"| D["Append A-bit address of source i to output frame"]
    D --> E["Append D-bit data chunk from source i to output frame"]
    E --> F["Increment slot counter i"]
    C -->|"No"| F
    F --> G{"Have we built k slots OR scanned all N sources?"}
    G -->|"No"| C
    G -->|"Yes"| H["Append F-bit flag at frame start and T-bit trailer at frame end"]
    H --> I["Transmit completed output frame on shared link"]
    I --> J["Return to start of next frame cycle"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Architecture: Synchronous TDM vs. Statistical TDM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SYNC["SYNCHRONOUS TDM - FIXED SLOTS"]
        direction LR
        S1A["Src 1"] --> M1["Slot always reserved"]
        S2A["Src 2"] --> M2["Slot always reserved"]
        S3A["Src 3 - IDLE"] --> M3["Slot transmitted EMPTY - WASTED"]
        S4A["Src 4 - IDLE"] --> M4["Slot transmitted EMPTY - WASTED"]
    end

    subgraph STAT["STATISTICAL TDM - DYNAMIC SLOTS"]
        direction LR
        S1B["Src 1 active"] --> N1["Slot 1: Addr 01 plus Data"]
        S2B["Src 3 active"] --> N2["Slot 2: Addr 11 plus Data"]
        S3B["Src 2 - IDLE"] --> SK["SKIPPED - no slot used"]
        S4B["Src 4 - IDLE"] --> SK2["SKIPPED - no slot used"]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, OECST612]**

### Q1. Define Statistical TDM. How does it differ from Synchronous TDM? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

Statistical Time Division Multiplexing (Statistical TDM) is a multiplexing technique in which the multiplexer dynamically allocates time slots on the shared output link **only to those input sources that have data to send**, rather than pre-assigning fixed slots to every source. The multiplexer scans input buffers cyclically and packs a data chunk into the next output slot only if the corresponding buffer is non-empty.

**Key differences from Synchronous TDM:**
1. **Slot assignment:** Synchronous TDM uses fixed, pre-assigned slots; Statistical TDM uses dynamic, on-demand slots.
2. **Addressing:** Statistical TDM requires an explicit **address field** in each output slot to identify the source, since slot position is no longer a reliable indicator.
3. **Efficiency:** Statistical TDM achieves much higher line efficiency (≈ 80–100%) for bursty traffic because no slots are wasted on idle sources.

---

### Q2. Why is an address field mandatory in Statistical TDM output frames? (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

In Synchronous TDM, the **position** of a slot within the frame is sufficient to identify its source (e.g., slot 3 always belongs to source 3). However, in Statistical TDM, the slot order is determined **dynamically** based on which sources are active. Therefore, the demultiplexer at the receiver cannot rely on slot position to identify the source.

To solve this, the multiplexer **prefixes each data slot with an address field** of $A = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ bits, where $N$ is the number of input sources. The demultiplexer reads this address and routes the data to the correct output port. Without the address field, the receiver would be unable to determine the source of each data block, making correct demultiplexing impossible.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Pattern, ESE Pattern, 14 Marks]**

---

### ✅ OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

**Statement:** Explain the architecture and operation of a Statistical TDM multiplexer with a neat block diagram. A Statistical TDM system multiplexes **4 input sources**, each operating at **2 kbps** with a **40% activity factor**. The output frame uses a **16-bit flag**, a **2-bit address per slot**, **8-bit data chunks**, and a **16-bit trailer**. The MUX packs **2 data slots per output frame**. Compute:
**(a)** The number of address bits required and justify your answer. **The output frame length in bits. The output line efficiency.** **(7 marks)**
**(b)** The **aggregate output data rate** in kbps, and **compare the efficiency with an equivalent Synchronous TDM** that does not use addressing. **State two situations where Statistical TDM is clearly preferred over Synchronous TDM.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Number of address bits:**

$$A = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil = \lceil \log_2 4 \rceil = \lceil 2 \rceil = 2 \text{ bits}$$

**Justification:** With 2 bits, we can address $2^2 = 4$ unique sources (00, 01, 10, 11), exactly matching our $N = 4$ sources.

**Step 2 — Output frame length:**

Given: $F = 16$ bits, $k = 2$ slots, $A = 2$ bits, $D = 8$ bits, $T = 16$ bits.

$$L = F + k(A + D) + T$$

$$L = 16 + 2 \times (2 + 8) + 16$$

$$L = 16 + 2 \times 10 + 16$$

$$L = 16 + 20 + 16 = 52 \text{ bits per frame}$$

**Step 3 — Line efficiency:**

$$\eta_{stat} = \frac{k \cdot D}{L} = \frac{2 \times 8}{52} = \frac{16}{52} = 0.3077$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{stat} \approx 30.77\%}$$

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Average aggregate input rate:**

The 40% activity factor means each source is active 40% of the time on average.

$$R_{avg,\text{per source}} = 2 \text{ kbps} \times 0.40 = 0.8 \text{ kbps}$$

$$R_{agg,\text{avg}} = 4 \times 0.8 = 3.2 \text{ kbps}$$

**Step 2 — Frame transmission rate:**

Each output frame carries $k \cdot D = 2 \times 8 = 16$ data bits.

$$f_{frame} = \frac{R_{agg,\text{avg}}}{k \cdot D} = \frac{3200 \text{ bits/sec}}{16 \text{ bits/frame}} = 200 \text{ frames/sec}$$

**Step 3 — Aggregate output data rate (including all overhead):**

$$R_{out} = f_{frame} \times L = 200 \times 52 = 10400 \text{ bits/sec}$$

$$\boxed{R_{out} = 10.4 \text{ kbps}}$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with Synchronous TDM:**

Synchronous TDM assigns one slot per source (4 slots) with no addressing.

$$\eta_{sync} = \frac{4 \times 8}{4 \times 8} = 1.0 = 100\%$$

Wait — the synchronous TDM frame has $N$ slots, each of $D$ bits, with no addressing or flag overhead (in the ideal model). Therefore:

$$\eta_{sync} = 1.0 \text{ (100%)}$$

**Comparison:** $\eta_{stat} = 30.77\%$ vs. $\eta_{sync} = 100\%$. In this case, Statistical TDM is **less efficient** due to address/flag/trailer overhead. However, **bandwidth saved on the link is real**: the synchronous TDM would need to transmit 4 slots per frame × 8 bits = 32 bits of data, while the statistical TDM only transmits 16 data bits per frame — half the link capacity, since only 2 of 4 sources are active on average.

**Step 5 — Two situations where Statistical TDM is clearly preferred:**

1. **Bursty traffic:** When input sources transmit data intermittently (e.g., computer terminals, web browsers, IoT sensors), most sources are idle most of the time. Statistical TDM avoids transmitting empty slots, drastically increasing link utilization.
2. **Asymmetric / unpredictable load:** When the number of active sources varies widely over time, statistical multiplexing adapts automatically without requiring manual reconfiguration of slot assignments.

---

### ✅ OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Statement:** With the help of a block diagram, describe the working of a **Statistical Time Division Multiplexer**. A system has **8 input sources**, each producing **500 bps** with a **25% activity factor**. The MUX allocates **2 slots per output frame**, each slot carries **4 bits of data** and a **3-bit address**. The frame also includes an **8-bit synchronization flag** and a **24-bit CRC trailer**.
**(a)** Calculate the **total number of bits in one output frame**, the **output bit rate** in bps, and the **line efficiency**. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** If the MUX is replaced by a **Synchronous TDM** with the same 8 inputs, what would be its **line efficiency**? Justify why Statistical TDM is preferred in this scenario. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $N = 8$, $k = 2$, $D = 4$ bits, $A = 3$ bits, $F = 8$ bits, $T = 24$ bits, $R_{source} = 500$ bps, $p = 0.25$.

**Step 1 — Total bits per output frame:**

$$L = F + k(A + D) + T$$

$$L = 8 + 2 \times (3 + 4) + 24$$

$$L = 8 + 2 \times 7 + 24$$

$$L = 8 + 14 + 24 = 46 \text{ bits per frame}$$

**Step 2 — Average number of active sources:**

$$E[\text{active}] = N \times p = 8 \times 0.25 = 2 \text{ active sources}$$

This matches the chosen $k = 2$ slots perfectly — an ideal design.

**Step 3 — Output bit rate:**

Each frame carries $k \cdot D = 2 \times 4 = 8$ data bits.

$$f_{frame} = \frac{N \times p \times R_{source}}{k \cdot D} = \frac{2 \times 500}{8} = \frac{1000}{8} = 125 \text{ frames/sec}$$

$$R_{out} = f_{frame} \times L = 125 \times 46 = 5750 \text{ bps}$$

$$\boxed{R_{out} = 5750 \text{ bps} = 5.75 \text{ kbps}}$$

**Step 4 — Line efficiency:**

$$\eta_{stat} = \frac{k \cdot D}{L} = \frac{2 \times 4}{46} = \frac{8}{46} = 0.1739$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{stat} \approx 17.39\%}$$

> **Discussion:** The efficiency appears low *as a fraction of transmitted bits*, but the **link capacity used is only 5.75 kbps** vs. the **8 kbps that synchronous TDM would require** (8 sources × 500 bps, 2 slots of 4 bits each = 8 kbps link). So real bandwidth is saved; the efficiency formula is penalising the protocol overhead.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Synchronous TDM line efficiency:**

In Synchronous TDM, the frame has $N = 8$ slots, each of $D = 4$ bits, with no addressing, flag, or trailer overhead in the ideal model.

$$\eta_{sync} = \frac{N \cdot D}{N \cdot D} = 1.0 = 100\%$$

**Step 2 — Why Statistical TDM is preferred despite lower bit efficiency:**

Statistical TDM is preferred because of **actual bandwidth conservation on the physical link**:

| Metric | Synchronous TDM | Statistical TDM |
|---|---|---|
| Output link rate required | 8 kbps (always) | 5.75 kbps (only active data) |
| Idle-source capacity | Wasted (transmitted as empty) | Not transmitted (skipped) |
| Effective data per kbps of link | 0.5 kbps data per 1 kbps link | 0.696 kbps data per 1 kbps link |
| Link utilization (data/total link time) | 50% (since 2 of 4 slots have data) | 100% (no empty slots) |

The **statistical gain** is therefore:

$$G = \frac{\eta_{stat,\text{link}}}{\eta_{sync,\text{link}}} = \frac{5.75 \text{ kbps achieved data}}{8 \text{ kbps required link}} \approx 71.9\% \text{ link utilization}$$

In this bursty scenario (only 25% activity), Statistical TDM uses **~28% less link capacity** for the same amount of user data, which is the key economic and engineering advantage.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the address field in the frame length formula** — many students compute $L = F + kD + T$, missing the $kA$ term. The address bits are real overhead that must be counted.
> 2. **Confusing "number of address bits" with "number of data bits"** — $A$ is for source identification, $D$ is for the actual payload.
> 3. **Mixing up input rate with output rate** — the output rate includes *all* overhead (flag, addresses, data, trailer), not just the data payload.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert units** — if input rates are in kbps and frame size in bits, be careful with seconds vs. milliseconds.
> 5. **Ignoring the activity factor** — Statistical TDM analysis is meaningless without specifying how often each source is active.
> 6. **Not justifying the value of $A$** — you must show $A = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ with reasoning; otherwise the examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 7. **Forgetting that $k \leq N$** — in Statistical TDM, the number of output slots per frame must be **less than or equal to** the number of input sources. If $k = N$, it degenerates to Synchronous TDM.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Statistical TDM = Asynchronous TDM = Intelligent TDM** — all three names refer to the same technique.
- ⭐ Slots are **dynamically allocated** to active sources only — no empty slots are transmitted.
- ⭐ **Address field is mandatory** in every output slot because slot positions no longer map to fixed sources.
- ⭐ Address bits: $A = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ to uniquely address $N$ input sources.
- ⭐ Frame length: $L = F + k(A + D) + T$ where $F$ = flag, $k$ = slots, $A$ = address bits, $D$ = data bits, $T$ = trailer.
- ⭐ Line efficiency: $\eta_{stat} = \dfrac{k \cdot D}{L}$.
- ⭐ Comparison: Synchronous TDM efficiency = $\dfrac{k}{N}$ (no overhead). Statistical TDM is preferred for **bursty traffic** because the link is not wasted on idle sources.
- ⭐ **Key trade-off:** Statistical TDM saves link bandwidth but introduces **buffer overflow risk** if more than $k$ sources become active simultaneously.
- ⭐ **Conceptual descendants:** Packet switching, ATM, MPLS, and modern IP routers all implement statistical multiplexing principles.
- ⭐ **Activity factor $p$** must always be considered in Statistical TDM problems; it determines the average load $N \cdot p$.
- ⭐ Statistical TDM is **less efficient per bit** than synchronous TDM when $k$ is close to $N$, but **saves link bandwidth** when $k \ll N$ and sources are bursty.
- ⭐ Always draw the **block diagram** in 14-mark questions — it is worth 2–3 marks by itself.
- ⭐ KTU loves questions that ask you to **compute output frame length, output data rate, and line efficiency** together — practice all three for the same problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spread Spectrum Techniques - Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Spread Spectrum Techniques - Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Spread Spectrum Techniques: Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum (DSSS)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Spread Spectrum?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spread Spectrum (KTU Syllabus Definition):**
> Spread Spectrum is a modulation technique in which the transmitted signal occupies a **bandwidth much greater than the minimum bandwidth necessary** to send the information. This bandwidth expansion is achieved using a **pseudo-random spreading code** that is independent of the original data signal, making the signal resistant to interference, jamming, and unauthorized interception.

The bandwidth expansion factor — called the **Processing Gain (Gp)** — is the fundamental metric that distinguishes a spread spectrum system from a conventional narrowband communication system.

### 1.2 Two Principal Variants of Spread Spectrum

| Variant | Full Form | Spreading Mechanism |
|---------|-----------|---------------------|
| **DSSS** | Direct Sequence Spread Spectrum | Multiplies data with high-rate PN chip sequence |
| **FHSS** | Frequency Hopping Spread Spectrum | Rapidly switches carrier among many pseudo-random frequencies |

> [!NOTE]
> In this module we focus exclusively on **DSSS**, which is the technique used in **CDMA (IS-95)**, **GPS**, **Wi-Fi (legacy 802.11b)**, and **3G WCDMA/UMTS**.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Whispering in a Noisy Restaurant"

Imagine Alice wants to whisper a secret to Bob across a noisy restaurant:

- **Narrowband approach** (normal communication): Alice whispers loudly at one frequency → easily overheard by Eve (eavesdropper) and drowned by jammer noise.
- **DSSS approach**: Alice and Bob agree on a **shared codebook** (pseudo-random sequence) beforehand. Alice **chops her whisper into tiny fragments (chips)** and spreads each fragment across the entire noisy room in a pre-agreed pseudo-random pattern. Bob, knowing the pattern, **reassembles** the fragments. An eavesdropper (Eve) hears a low-energy hiss everywhere and cannot reconstruct the message without the codebook.

**Key Insight:** DSSS does **not** increase the *power* of the signal — it spreads the *energy* across a much wider bandwidth, making the signal look like **noise** to anyone without the spreading code.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Used

- **Processing Gain (Gp)** — measured in **dB**; typically **10 dB to 60 dB** in commercial systems
- **Chip Rate (Rc)** — measured in **chips/second**; e.g., 1.228 Mcps in IS-95
- **Spreading Factor (SF)** — dimensionless integer; ratio of chips to bits
- **Jamming Margin (Mj)** — measured in **dB**; typically **15 dB to 40 dB**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examination Hook:** Whenever a question says *"spread the signal"*, the answer must explicitly mention the role of the **Pseudo-Noise (PN) sequence** — not random noise, but a *deterministic, periodic* sequence known to transmitter and receiver.
