This is far too high for a real network (target GoS is typically 0.01 or 1%). **Step 5 — Improve service by adding one trunk ($N = 4$):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Num} &= \frac{2^4}{4!} = \frac{16}{24} = 0.6667 \\ \text{Denominator} &= 1 + 2 + 2 + 1.3333 + 0.6667 = 7.0000 \\ B(4, 2) &= \frac{0.6667}{7.0000} \approx 0.0952 \approx 9.52\% \end{aligned} $$ **Conclusion:** Adding just **one** trunk line nearly halved the blocking probability — illustrating the **non-linear cost-benefit** of trunk sizing in circuit-switched networks. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Three Switching Modes | Parameter | Circuit Switching | Message Switching | Packet Switching | |---|---|---|---| | **Path established** | Before transmission (dedicated) | Not dedicated (hop-by-hop) | Not dedicated (per-packet) | | **Data unit size** | Continuous stream | Entire message | Fixed/Variable packet | | **Store and forward?** | No (transparent path) | Yes (entire message) | Yes (per packet) | | **Channel utilization** | Poor during silence | Better (statistical) | Best (statistical) | | **Delay** | Setup delay dominates | High (full msg at each hop) | Low (small packets) | | **Delay type** | Uniform, predictable | Variable | Variable | | **Suitability for voice** | Excellent (real-time) | Poor | Acceptable (VoIP) | | **Suitability for data** | Wastes bandwidth | Acceptable | Excellent | | **Switching complexity** | Low | Medium | High | | **Example** | PSTN | Telegraph (historical) | Internet | | **Resource reservation** | Yes (dedicated) | No | No | > [!TIP] > A common KTU question: *“Why is circuit switching preferred for voice?”* The answer: voice is **real-time and delay-sensitive** but **tolerant of loss**; circuit switching provides **constant, guaranteed bitrate with minimal delay variation (jitter)**. --- ### 3.5 Derivation 4 — End-to-End Delay in Circuit Switching **Problem Setup:** Compute the **total delay** experienced by a 100 KB file transmitted over a 4-hop circuit-switched network, with 1500 km total link length, propagation speed $2 \times 10^8$ m/s in fiber, and transmission rate 1 Mbps. **Step 1 — Setup delay (signaling round-trip):** Typically given or assumed. Here, assume $T_{setup} = 500$ ms = 0.5 s. **Step 2 — Propagation delay (one-way):** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{prop} &= \frac{\text{Distance}}{\text{Speed}} \\ T_{prop} &= \frac{1{,}500{,}000 \text{ m}}{2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}} \\ T_{prop} &= 0.0075 \text{ s} = 7.5 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Transmission delay:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{trans} &= \frac{\text{File size}}{\text{Bitrate}} \\ T_{trans} &= \frac{100 \text{ KB} \times 1024 \text{ bytes/KB} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte}}{1 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} \\ T_{trans} &= \frac{819{,}200 \text{ bits}}{1 \times 10^6 \text{ bps}} \\ T_{trans} &= 0.8192 \text{ s} = 819.2 \text{ ms} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4 — Teardown delay:** Typically $T_{tear} = 200$ ms = 0.2 s. **Step 5 — Total delay:** $$ \begin{aligned} T_{total} &= T_{setup} + T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{tear} \\ T_{total} &= 0.5 + 0.0075 + 0.8192 + 0.2 \\ T_{total} &\approx 1.5267 \text{ s} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > For **small files**, the **setup and teardown delays dominate** the total delay in circuit switching. This is the **fundamental inefficiency** of circuit switching for bursty data traffic (e.g., web browsing, emails). ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Three Phases of a Circuit-Switched Call (Mermaid State Diagram) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Idle Idle --> Setup : Caller dials / sends SETUP msg Setup --> DataTransfer : Circuit established (200 OK) Setup --> Blocked : All channels busy Blocked --> Idle : Release resources, return busy tone DataTransfer --> Teardown : User hangs up / sends RELEASE Teardown --> Idle : Resources deallocated Idle --> [*] ``` **Reading the diagram:** A call always begins in the *Idle* state, transitions through *Setup* (Phase 1), then *Data Transfer* (Phase 2), and finally *Teardown* (Phase 3) before returning to *Idle*. The *Blocked* branch represents call rejection. --- ### 4.2 Circuit Establishment Through Multiple Switches (Mermaid Flowchart) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Source Telephone
Caller A] -->|Setup Req| S1[Local Exchange
Switch 1] S1 -->|Reserve Channel| S2[Tandem Exchange
Switch 2] S2 -->|Reserve Channel| S3[International Gateway
Switch 3] S3 -->|Setup Req| S4[Destination Local Exchange
Switch 4] S4 -->|Ring| B[Destination Telephone
Caller B] B -->|Answer| S4 S4 -->|Connect ACK| S3 S3 -->|Connect ACK| S2 S2 -->|Connect ACK| S1 S1 -->|Circuit Ready| A ``` **Reading the diagram:** The solid dedicated circuit, once established, forms a **single end-to-end voice path** from Caller A to Caller B. All intermediate switches act as **transparent relays** during data transfer. --- ### 4.3 Space Division vs. Time Division Switching (Mermaid Comparison Block) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SDS[Space Division Switching - SDS] direction LR I1[Input 1] -->|Physical Wire| C1[Crosspoint 1] I2[Input 2] -->|Physical Wire| C2[Crosspoint 2] I3[Input 3] -->|Physical Wire| C3[Crosspoint 3] C1 --> O1[Output 1] C2 --> O2[Output 2] C3 --> O3[Output 3] end subgraph TDS[Time Division Switching - TDS] direction LR TSI[Time Slot Interchange Unit] --> T1[Time Slot 1 - Call A] TSI --> T2[Time Slot 2 - Call B] TSI --> T3[Time Slot 3 - Call C] TSI --> T4[Time Slot 4 - Idle] end ``` **Reading the diagram:** In **SDS**, each call uses a **separate physical path**. In **TDS**, multiple calls share a **single high-speed bus**, distinguished only by their **time slot** in the TDM frame. --- ### 4.4 3-Stage Clos Network Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Stage1[First Stage - r input modules] I1[Module 1 - n inputs] I2[Module 2 - n inputs] I3[Module 3 - n inputs] end subgraph Stage2[Middle Stage - m switches] M1[Middle Switch 1] M2[Middle Switch 2] M3[Middle Switch 3] end subgraph Stage3[Third Stage - r output modules] O1[Output Module 1] O2[Output Module 2] O3[Output Module 3] end I1 --> M1 I1 --> M2 I1 --> M3 I2 --> M1 I2 --> M2 I2 --> M3 I3 --> M1 I3 --> M2 I3 --> M3 M1 --> O1 M1 --> O2 M1 --> O3 M2 --> O1 M2 --> O2 M2 --> O3 M3 --> O1 M3 --> O2 M3 --> O3 ``` **Reading the diagram:** A 3-stage Clos network with $r = 3$ input modules, $n$ inputs per module, $m = 3$ middle switches, and $r = 3$ output modules. For *strict non-blocking* behaviour, the **Clos criterion** $m \geq 2n - 1$ must be satisfied. --- ### 4.5 Blocking vs. Non-Blocking Network Comparison (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Blocking[Blocking Network Example] direction LR A1[Input A] -->|BUSY| X1[Switch] B1[Input B] -->|FREE| X1 X1 -->|REJECTED| Y1[Output 1] end subgraph NonBlocking[Non Blocking Network Example - Crossbar] direction LR A2[Input A] -->|AVAILABLE| X2[Crosspoint A-1] B2[Input B] -->|AVAILABLE| X3[Crosspoint B-2] X2 --> Y2[Output 1 - Dedicated] X3 --> Y3[Output 2 - Dedicated] end ``` **Reading the diagram:** In a *blocking* network, even when Input B and Output 1 are both free, the internal path may already be used by another call, causing rejection. In a *non-blocking* network (like a crossbar), every free input can always reach every free output. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define circuit switching. List its three main phases.* **Model Answer:** Circuit switching is a communication method in which a dedicated, end-to-end physical communication path is established between the sender and receiver before any data transmission takes place. The path remains reserved for the entire duration of the call and is released only after the call ends. The three main phases are: 1. **Circuit Establishment (Call Setup)** — A dedicated path is reserved through intermediate switches from source to destination. 2. **Data Transfer** — Both parties communicate over the reserved circuit with guaranteed bandwidth. 3. **Circuit Teardown (Call Release)** — The reserved resources are deallocated and returned to the free pool. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** *Distinguish between Space Division Switching (SDS) and Time Division Switching (TDS).* **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Space Division Switching (SDS) | Time Division Switching (TDS) | |---|---|---| | **Path Type** | Physically separate path for each call | Shared physical path; differentiated by time slot | | **Multiplexing** | None (one call per wire pair) | TDM-based | | **Switch Element** | Mechanical/electronic crosspoints | Time Slot Interchange (TSI) unit | | **Modern Usage** | Legacy analog exchanges | Modern digital exchanges | | **Bandwidth** | Fixed per physical link | Shared dynamically via time slots | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 4 Internal Choice --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper] #### (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the three phases of circuit switching with a suitable diagram. Why is circuit switching still preferred for voice calls in PSTN despite its inefficiency for data traffic? #### (b) **[7 Marks]** A circuit-switched network is offered a traffic load of $A = 4$ Erlangs with $N = 5$ trunk lines. Using the Erlang B formula, compute the blocking probability. If one more trunk is added ($N = 6$), recalculate the new blocking probability and comment on the improvement. --- **Solution to Q-A (a):** The three phases of circuit switching are: **Phase 1 — Circuit Establishment:** The calling party sends a setup request to the local exchange. The exchange uses signaling protocols (e.g., SS7) to identify a free path through all intermediate switches to the destination. A dedicated physical channel is reserved on **each link** along the path. If any link lacks a free channel, the call is **blocked** and a busy tone is returned. *(2 marks)* **Phase 2 — Data Transfer:** Once the circuit is established, the two parties communicate over the reserved path. The path is **transparent** — switches do not buffer, inspect, or alter user data. The user enjoys **guaranteed, fixed bandwidth** with only propagation and transmission delay (no queuing delay). This deterministic behavior is ideal for real-time voice. *(2 marks)* **Phase 3 — Circuit Teardown:** After the call ends, a release signal propagates back to all switches. Each switch **deallocates** its reserved channel and returns it to the free pool for use by other calls. *(1 mark)* **Diagram:** A three-phase state diagram was already shown in Section 4.1 above. *(1 mark)* **Why still preferred for voice:** Voice is **delay-sensitive** (latency > 150 ms is unacceptable) and **tolerates loss** rather than delay. Circuit switching provides **constant bitrate with negligible jitter**, ensuring smooth voice quality. The inefficiency during silence is acceptable because voice already has natural silent gaps that can be compressed, and the **billing model** is per-minute call rather than per-bit. *(1 mark)* --- **Solution to Q-A (b):** **Erlang B Formula:** $$B(N, A) = \frac{\dfrac{A^N}{N!}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{N} \dfrac{A^i}{i!}}$$ **Case 1: $N = 5$, $A = 4$ Erlangs** Numerator: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Num} &= \frac{4^5}{5!} = \frac{1024}{120} = 8.5333 \end{aligned} $$ Denominator terms: $$ \begin{aligned} i=0: \quad & \frac{4^0}{0!} = 1.0000 \\ i=1: \quad & \frac{4^1}{1!} = 4.0000 \\ i=2: \quad & \frac{4^2}{2!} = 8.0000 \\ i=3: \quad & \frac{4^3}{3!} = 10.6667 \\ i=4: \quad & \frac{4^4}{4!} = 10.6667 \\ i=5: \quad & \frac{4^5}{5!} = 8.5333 \\ \text{Sum} & = 1 + 4 + 8 + 10.6667 + 10.6667 + 8.5333 = 42.8667 \end{aligned} $$ $$ B(5, 4) = \frac{8.5333}{42.8667} \approx 0.1991 \approx 19.91\% $$ **Case 2: $N = 6$, $A = 4$ Erlangs** Numerator: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Num} &= \frac{4^6}{6!} = \frac{4096}{720} = 5.6889 \end{aligned} $$ Denominator: Add new term $i=6$: $4^6 / 6! = 5.6889$ $$ \text{Sum} = 42.8667 + 5.6889 = 48.5556 $$ $$ B(6, 4) = \frac{5.6889}{48.5556} \approx 0.1172 \approx 11.72\% $$ **Comment:** Adding just **one** trunk line reduced the blocking probability from **19.91%** to **11.72%** — a relative reduction of about **41%**. This demonstrates the **non-linear improvement** in GoS that additional trunks provide in a circuit-switched network, justifying trunk-line investments. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper] #### (a) **[7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a 3-stage Clos network. Derive the condition for it to be **strictly non-blocking**. State the difference between strictly non-blocking and rearrangeably non-blocking networks. #### (b) **[7 Marks]** A crossbar switch has 200 input lines and 200 output lines. (i) Calculate the number of crosspoints required. (ii) If a 3-stage Clos network replaces the crossbar with $r = 10$ first-stage modules each with $n = 20$ inputs and $m = 20$ middle-stage switches, calculate the total number of crosspoints. Verify if this Clos network satisfies the strict non-blocking criterion. --- **Solution to Q-B (a):** A **3-stage Clos network** consists of three sequential stages of smaller crossbar switches connected in a specific pattern: - **First Stage:** $r$ modules (called *input modules*), each with $n \times m$ crosspoints (i.e., $n$ inputs and $m$ outputs). - **Middle Stage:** $m$ crossbar switches, each of size $r \times r$. - **Third Stage:** $r$ modules (called *output modules*), each with $m \times n$ crosspoints. The diagram in Section 4.4 above illustrates this architecture. **Derivation of the Strict Non-Blocking Condition:** Consider a worst-case scenario: a new call arrives at an arbitrary first-stage module, say module $i$. To establish a connection, we must find a **free middle switch** to route the call. In the worst case, all the middle switches may already have one leg of an existing call in module $i$ (occupying $n - 1$ middle switches at most) and another leg going to the desired output module (occupying up to $n - 1$ more middle switches, possibly overlapping with the previous set). The total number of middle switches that may be **unavailable** in the worst case is: $$\text{Busy} = (n - 1) + (n - 1) = 2n - 2$$ To guarantee that **at least one middle switch is free** under all possible traffic patterns, the total number of middle switches must satisfy: $$m \geq (2n - 2) + 1 = 2n - 1$$ Hence, the **Clos criterion for strict non-blocking** is: $$\boxed{m \geq 2n - 1}$$ **Strictly vs. Rearrangeably Non-Blocking:** | Aspect | Strictly Non-Blocking (SNB) | Rearrangeably Non-Blocking (RNB) | |---|---|---| | **Condition** | $m \geq 2n - 1$ | $m \geq n$ | | **Rearrangement** | Not required | Existing connections may be re-routed | | **Connection setup** | Always succeeds without reconfiguration | Always succeeds *after* possible re-routing | | **Hardware cost** | Higher | Lower | | **Latency on new call** | Lower (direct establishment) | Higher (reconfiguration overhead) | --- **Solution to Q-B (b):** **(i) Crossbar switch crosspoint count:** $$ \begin{aligned} C_{crossbar} &= N \times N \\ C_{crossbar} &= 200 \times 200 \\ C_{crossbar} &= 40{,}000 \text{ crosspoints} \end{aligned} $$ **(ii) 3-stage Clos network crosspoint count:** Given: $r = 10$, $n = 20$, $m = 20$. Crosspoints in each stage: - **First stage:** $r$ modules, each of size $n \times m$ → $r \times (n \times m) = 10 \times (20 \times 20) = 10 \times 400 = 4000$ - **Middle stage:** $m$ switches, each of size $r \times r$ → $m \times (r \times r) = 20 \times (10 \times 10) = 20 \times 100 = 2000$ - **Third stage:** $r$ modules, each of size $m \times n$ → $r \times (m \times n) = 10 \times (20 \times 20) = 4000$ $$ \begin{aligned} C_{clos} &= 4000 + 2000 + 4000 = 10{,}000 \text{ crosspoints} \end{aligned} $$ **Verification of strict non-blocking condition:** We require $m \geq 2n - 1$. $$ \begin{aligned} 2n - 1 &= 2(20) - 1 = 39 \\ m &= 20 \end{aligned} $$ Since $m = 20 < 39$, the condition is **NOT satisfied**. > [!NOTE] > However, $m = 20 \geq n = 20$, so the network **is rearrangeably non-blocking (RNB)**. To make it strictly non-blocking, we would need to increase $m$ to at least 39, raising total crosspoints to $10 \times (20 \times 39) + 39 \times 100 + 10 \times (39 \times 20) = 7800 + 3900 + 7800 = 19{,}500$. **Conclusion:** The Clos network with the given parameters uses only **10,000 crosspoints (25% of the 40,000 in a crossbar)** at the cost of being only **rearrangeably non-blocking** rather than strictly non-blocking. This is a classic engineering trade-off between **cost and connection-establishment guarantees**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Areas:** > > 1. **Confusing "Setup Delay" with "Transmission Delay":** Many students write the total delay formula incorrectly. Remember: Circuit switching has **both** a setup delay (signaling) and a transmission delay (data) — both contribute to total delay. > > 2. **Erlang B Calculation Errors:** A frequent mistake is forgetting the $i = 0$ term in the denominator sum. Always include the $i = 0$ term (which equals 1). Round-off errors in factorials are another common pitfall. > > 3. **Clos Criterion Misapplication:** Students often quote $m \geq 2n - 1$ for both SNB and RNB. **Remember:** SNB requires $2n - 1$; RNB requires only $n$. Mixing them up costs full marks. > > 4. **Crossbar Crosspoint Count:** Forgetting the difference between *input lines* $N$ and *crosspoints* $N^2$ — write it explicitly with units. > > 5. **Confusing 4 kHz with 64 kbps:** Analog voice uses **4 kHz of bandwidth**; digital PCM voice uses **64 kbps of bitrate**. These are different physical quantities — do not interchange them in derivations. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✅ **Switching** is the technique of routing data through intermediate nodes in a network. ✅ The **three paradigms** are Circuit, Message, and Packet Switching. Module 4 focuses on **Circuit Switching**. ✅ **Circuit Switching** reserves a **dedicated end-to-end path** before data transfer; this path is held for the entire call duration. ✅ The **three phases** are: **(1) Circuit Establishment**, **(2) Data Transfer**, **(3) Circuit Teardown**. Memorize them in order. ✅ **Space Division Switching (SDS)** uses physically separate paths; **Time Division Switching (TDS)** uses TDM with time slots. ✅ A **blocking network** may reject a call even when inputs and outputs are free; a **non-blocking network** guarantees connection. ✅ **Clos criterion for SNB:** $m \geq 2n - 1$. For RNB: $m \geq n$. The number of crosspoints in a crossbar is $N^2$. ✅ **PCM voice bitrate** = 64 kbps (8-bit samples × 8 kHz sampling) — derived from Nyquist theorem applied to a 4 kHz voice channel. ✅ **Erlang B formula** $B(N, A) = \dfrac{A^N / N!}{\sum_{i=0}^{N} A^i / i!}$ is used to compute **Grade of Service (GoS)** in circuit-switched trunk lines. ✅ Circuit switching is **ideal for real-time voice** (PSTN, ISDN) but **inefficient for bursty data** due to setup delay and bandwidth wastage during silence. ✅ Modern telephone exchanges use **hybrid STS / TST** switching fabrics combining the benefits of both space and time division. ✅ The **PSTN** (Public Switched Telephone Network) is the canonical real-world example of a circuit-switched network. ✅ **Total delay in circuit switching** = $T_{setup} + T_{prop} + T_{trans} + T_{tear}$. For small data transfers, the **setup and teardown delays dominate** the transmission delay. ✅ **Crossbar switch** complexity scales as $O(N^2)$; **Clos networks** reduce this to roughly $O(N \cdot \sqrt{N})$ — a major scalability improvement.

topics.title: Packet Switching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Packet Switching # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Packet Switching** is a **store-and-forward** network switching methodology in which a source message is fragmented into bounded-size units called **packets** (each carrying a **header** with addressing/routing metadata and a **payload** with user data) at the **Data Link / Network Layer** (OSI Layer 2/3). Each packet is independently routed through a shared **mesh of intermediate nodes (routers/switches)** that buffer the packet, examine its header, and forward it on the next best outgoing link toward the destination, where the packets are **reassembled** in sequence. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim alignment):** *Packet switching is a method whereby messages are broken into finite-sized packets, and each packet is transmitted independently through the network using a store-and-forward mechanism. Routing decisions are made on a per-packet basis using the destination address contained in the packet header.* There are **two principal paradigms** in packet switching: | Paradigm | OSI Analogy | Connection State | Routing Decision | |---|---|---|---| | **Datagram Packet Switching** | Connectionless (UDP-like) | None | **Per-packet**, independent | | **Virtual Circuit Packet Switching** | Connection-oriented (TCP-like) | Pre-established logical path | **Per-VC** (all packets follow same path) | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Courier Parcel Network" Imagine a large company in **Kochi** wants to send a 500-page legal contract to its branch in **Bengaluru** using a courier service: - ❌ **Circuit Switching Analogy:** Reserve the *entire* highway from Kochi to Bengaluru just for this one truck. The road remains idle whenever the truck stops for a break — wasteful. - ✅ **Packet Switching Analogy:** Split the 500 pages into **10 sealed envelopes** (packets), each labeled with the destination address (Bengaluru office, Block C, Shelf 3). Each envelope can travel via *any available courier van* on *any route*. Some envelopes may fly via Chennai, others via Mysuru — they need not take the same path. The destination office **reassembles** them in page order. This is precisely how IP-based packet switching works on the Internet — every packet is an "envelope" with its own "address label," and the network **statistically multiplexes** (shares) links among millions of such envelopes. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for KTU:** Packet switching is the *foundation* of the modern Internet. Every email, web page, video stream, and video call is ultimately decomposed into packets (typically **MTU = 1500 bytes** for Ethernet) before transmission. ## 1.3 Core Physical & Performance Constants | Constant / Metric | Standard Value | Notes | |---|---|---| | **Standard Ethernet MTU** | **1500 bytes** | Maximum packet payload on most wired links | | **IPv4 header size** | **20 bytes** (min) – 60 bytes (max) | Carries source/destination IP, TTL, etc. | | **IPv6 header size** | **40 bytes** (fixed) | Simpler than IPv4 | | **Typical propagation speed (copper/fiber)** | **2 × 10⁸ m/s** | Used in delay calculations | | **Propagation speed (wireless)** | **3 × 10⁸ m/s** | Speed of light in vacuum | | **Router buffer (typical)** | **64 KB – several MB** | Per-interface queuing memory | > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state the **MTU (1500 bytes)** and the **header overhead** when discussing packet size — examiners award marks for these precision terms. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why" and "How" — Operational Steps of Packet Switching The life of a packet from source to destination follows a strict pipeline: ### Phase 1 — Segmentation at Source 1. The application-layer message (size **M bits**) is divided into **N packets** of fixed length **L bits** each. 2. Each packet is wrapped with a **header (H bits)** containing source address, destination address, sequence number, and protocol info. 3. Final frame on the wire = **(L + H) bits** (often called *L bits* in KTU textbooks when H is implicit). ### Phase 2 — Store-and-Forward at Each Node 1. A packet arrives at a router over an incoming link. 2. The router **stores the ENTIRE packet** in its buffer (this is what "store-and-forward" means). 3. Once the **last bit** of the packet has arrived, the router: - Inspects the **destination address** in the header. - Consults its **routing table** to determine the outgoing interface. - **Transmits** the packet onto the chosen outgoing link. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Concept — Store-and-Forward Delay:** A router cannot begin transmitting a packet on its outgoing link until the *entire* packet has been received. This is a frequent exam question. ### Phase 3 — Reassembly at Destination 1. Packets may arrive **out-of-order** (especially in datagram networks). 2. The destination uses the **sequence number** in each packet's header to reassemble the original message. 3. Duplicate or corrupted packets are detected (via checksums) and discarded or retransmitted by upper-layer protocols. ## 2.2 The Four Canonical Delays in a Packet-Switched Network Every packet experiences **four types of delay** at every node and link. The KTU syllabus requires you to know all four: | # | Delay Type | Symbol | Cause | Typical Magnitude | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Transmission Delay** | $d_t$ | Time to push all bits of the packet onto the link | Microseconds to ms | | 2 | **Propagation Delay** | $d_p$ | Time for a bit to travel the physical medium | ms for inter-city links | | 3 | **Queuing Delay** | $d_q$ | Waiting time in router buffer | 0 to seconds (variable) | | 4 | **Processing/Nodal Delay** | $d_{proc}$ | Header inspection, routing lookup, error check | Microseconds | > [!NOTE] > **End-to-End (Total) Nodal Delay** at a single router: > $$d_{nodal} \;=\; d_{proc} \;+\; d_q \;+\; d_t \;+\; d_p$$ > Summed over all $N$ routers along the path, plus the source's transmission delay, gives the **end-to-end delay** $d_{end\text{-}end}$. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — these formulas account for ~60% of KTU numerical questions.** | # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Boundary / Special Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Transmission delay** | $d_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | $L$ = packet length (**bits**), $R$ = link rate (**bps**) | Independent of distance | | 2 | **Propagation delay** | $d_p = \dfrac{d}{s}$ | $d$ = link length (**meters**), $s$ = propagation speed (**m/s**) | Independent of packet size | | 3 | **Number of packets** | $N = \dfrac{M}{L}$ | $M$ = total message size (bits), $L$ = packet size (bits) | Round up if not divisible | | 4 | **Total time — single link, N packets, store-and-forward** | $T = N \cdot \dfrac{L}{R}$ | N packets, no pipelining needed | One physical link only | | 5 | **Total time — N packets over P links (pipelined)** | $T = \left(N + P - 1\right) \cdot \dfrac{L}{R}$ | N packets, P links in series | Assumes no propagation/processing delay | | 6 | **End-to-end delay (single packet, P hops)** | $d_{e2e} = P \cdot \left(d_{proc} + d_q + d_t + d_p\right)$ | P = number of links | Excludes source $d_{proc}$ | | 7 | **Effective throughput with header overhead** | $R_{eff} = \dfrac{L}{L + H} \cdot R$ | H = header bits | $R_{eff} \to R$ as $L \gg H$ | | 8 | **Utilization (with propagation)** | $U = \dfrac{d_t}{d_t + d_p} = \dfrac{L/R}{L/R + d/s}$ | Fractional (0 to 1) | $U \to 1$ when $d_p \ll d_t$ | | 9 | **Time to send N packets over P hops (full pipeline)** | $T = P \cdot \dfrac{L}{R} + \left(N - 1\right) \cdot \dfrac{L}{R}$ | First packet traverses P links, then one packet "emerges" every $L/R$ | KTU's most-tested formula | | 10 | **Bandwidth-Delay Product** | $BDP = R \cdot d_p$ | bits "in flight" on a link | Determines optimal window size | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pipe-Formula Pitfall:** When asked *"time to transmit N packets of L bits over P links each of rate R"*, students often write only $N \cdot L/R$ — this is **wrong** because it ignores the store-and-forward store time at intermediate routers. The correct answer is the **pipelined formula** $T = (N + P - 1) \cdot L/R$ (when propagation and queuing delays are neglected). ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Internet Routing (IP):** IPv4/IPv6 networks use **datagram packet switching**. Each IP packet is routed independently; routers (Cisco, Juniper) maintain forwarding tables built via OSPF, BGP. - **Mobile Networks (4G/5G):** Use a hybrid — user-plane traffic is **datagram** packet-switched, while control signaling uses virtual circuits. - **X.25 (Legacy):** The canonical example of **virtual circuit packet switching**, used in 1970s–80s WANs. - **Frame Relay, ATM:** Early virtual-circuit technologies that bridged the gap to modern MPLS. - **MPLS (Multiprotocol Label Switching):** Modern descendant of virtual-circuit switching, used in ISP backbones for traffic engineering. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Time to Transmit N Packets over P Series Links (The "Pipeline" Derivation) This is the **single most important derivation** in KTU Module 4. We derive it from first principles. ### Problem Setup - A source has a message split into **N packets**, each of length **L bits**. - The path has **P series links** (i.e., P − 1 intermediate routers). - Each link has rate **R bits/second**. - Neglect propagation, queuing, and processing delays (standard KTU simplification). ### Derivation **Step 1 — Time for the FIRST packet to reach the destination.** The first packet must be transmitted on link 1, then entirely received at router 1, then transmitted on link 2, and so on, until it emerges from link P. $$ T_{first} \;=\; P \cdot \frac{L}{R} $$ **Step 2 — Time for the SECOND packet to reach the destination.** By the time the first packet is emerging from link P, the second packet has already been "in the pipeline" and is now emerging from link P−1. Thus, the second packet needs only **one additional $L/R$ interval** after the first. $$ \Delta T \;=\; \frac{L}{R} $$ **Step 3 — Generalize to all N packets.** Once the pipeline is "full," a new packet emerges from the destination link every $\frac{L}{R}$ seconds. So for N packets: $$ T_{all} \;=\; T_{first} \;+\; (N - 1) \cdot \Delta T $$ **Step 4 — Substitute and simplify.** $$ T_{all} \;=\; P \cdot \frac{L}{R} \;+\; (N - 1) \cdot \frac{L}{R} $$ $$ T_{all} \;=\; \left[\, P \;+\; (N - 1) \,\right] \cdot \frac{L}{R} $$ $$ \boxed{\; T_{all} \;=\; (N + P - 1) \cdot \frac{L}{R} \;} $$ ### Worked Example > A file of **M = 4 Mbits** is split into **N = 4 packets** of **L = 1 Mbit** each. The path has **P = 3 links**, each at **R = 1 Mbps**. Find the total transmission time. > > *Solution:* > $$T = (N + P - 1) \cdot \frac{L}{R} = (4 + 3 - 1) \cdot \frac{10^6}{10^6} = 6 \text{ seconds}$$ > > *Without pipelining (wrong):* $N \cdot L/R = 4$ s. The extra **2 seconds** represents the store-and-forward cost. ## 3.2 Derivation: Effective Throughput with Header Overhead ### Setup - Link rate = **R bps** - Packet payload = **L bits** (user data only) - Header overhead = **H bits** per packet ### Derivation The **total bits transmitted per packet** = $L + H$. Therefore the **fraction of useful data** is $\frac{L}{L + H}$. Multiplying by the link rate gives the effective throughput. $$ \boxed{\; R_{eff} \;=\; \frac{L}{L + H} \cdot R \;} $$ ### Worked Example > Ethernet frame: **L = 1500 bytes** payload, **H = 20 bytes** (IPv4) + **18 bytes** (Ethernet header + CRC) = 38 bytes. Link rate **R = 100 Mbps**. Find $R_{eff}$. > > *Solution:* > $$R_{eff} = \frac{1500}{1500 + 38} \cdot 100 = 0.9753 \cdot 100 \approx 97.53 \text{ Mbps}$$ > > The **2.47%** loss is the protocol overhead. ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Datagram vs. Virtual Circuit vs. Circuit Switching > [!IMPORTANT] > **This 3-way comparison is a KTU "favorite" — expect it in 14-mark questions.** | Parameter | **Circuit Switching** | **Datagram Packet Switching** | **Virtual Circuit Packet Switching** | |---|---|---|---| | **Connection setup** | Required (e.g., dialing) | None | Required (VC setup) | | **State at routers** | None (dedicated path) | None (stateless forwarding) | Per-VC state (VC table) | | **Routing decision** | Fixed at setup | Per-packet, independent | Fixed per-VC (same path) | | **Path followed by packets** | Same (dedicated) | Can differ per packet | Same for all packets in VC | | **Packet ordering at destination** | Guaranteed | Not guaranteed | Guaranteed | | **Effect of router failure** | Call dropped | Packets rerouted dynamically | All VCs through router fail | | **Congestion handling** | No built-in mechanism | End-to-end (TCP) or hop-by-hop | Per-VC flow control possible | | **Header size** | None (channel identifier) | Full source + destination address | Small VC identifier (VCI/VCI) | | **Suitability for** | Voice (legacy PSTN) | Bursty data (web, email) | Long sessions (e.g., ATM, Frame Relay) | | **Resource reservation** | Yes (bandwidth reserved) | No | Optional (resource reservation possible) | | **Setup latency** | High (connection setup) | None | Moderate (VC establishment) | | **Example technologies** | PSTN, ISDN, early telephone | **Internet (IP)**, UDP | **X.25, Frame Relay, ATM, MPLS** | | **Delay variability** | Predictable (constant) | Variable (queue-dependent) | Predictable within a VC | ## 3.4 Datagram Network — Internal Operation In a **datagram network**, each router maintains a **forwarding table** mapping destination prefixes to outgoing interfaces. When a packet arrives: 1. The router reads the **destination IP address**. 2. It performs a **longest-prefix match** in its forwarding table. 3. The packet is placed in the outgoing interface's **queue**. 4. When the link is free, the packet is transmitted. Since each packet's path can be chosen independently, datagram networks are **highly resilient** to link failures but **provide no ordering guarantees**. ## 3.5 Virtual Circuit Network — Internal Operation In a **virtual circuit (VC) network**, before any data is sent, a **logical connection** (VC) is established: 1. **VC Setup:** Source sends a *CALL REQUEST* packet; each router along the path assigns a **Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)** for the incoming link and creates a row in its VC table mapping (incoming link, VCI) → (outgoing link, VCI). 2. **Data Transfer:** All data packets carry only the **VCI** (small integer, e.g., 12–24 bits), not full source/destination addresses. Each router uses VCI to look up the outgoing interface in O(1) time — much faster than IP's longest-prefix match. 3. **VC Teardown:** A *CLEAR REQUEST* packet removes the VC table entries. There are two sub-types: - **Permanent Virtual Circuit (PVC):** Pre-configured by the network operator. - **Switched Virtual Circuit (SVC):** Established on demand per session. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Packet-Switched Network Topology The diagram below illustrates how packets from source **S** traverse a packet-switched network through routers **R1, R2, R3** to reach destination **D**, with each packet potentially taking a different path in datagram mode. ```mermaid flowchart LR S["SOURCE HOST S"] -->|Packet 1| R1 S -->|Packet 2| R1 S -->|Packet 3| R1 R1["ROUTER R1"] -->|Packet 1| R2 R1 -->|Packet 2| R4 R1 -->|Packet 3| R3 R2["ROUTER R2"] -->|Packet 1| R5 R3["ROUTER R3"] -->|Packet 3| R5 R4["ROUTER R4"] -->|Packet 2| R5 R5["ROUTER R5"] -->|All Packets| D["DESTINATION HOST D"] subgraph LEGEND["PACKET SWITCHING LEGEND"] L1["Each packet routed INDEPENDENTLY"] L2["Multiple paths = better resilience"] L3["Reassembly occurs at destination"] end style S fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style R1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style R2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style R3 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style R4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style R5 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style LEGEND fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#666,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.2 Packet-Switching Internal Block Architecture The following block diagram shows the internal functional pipeline of a packet switch (router), which is the heart of any packet-switched network. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["INPUT LINK
Incoming Physical Signal"] --> B["PHYSICAL LAYER
Bit Reception & Clock Recovery"] B --> C["DATA LINK LAYER
Frame Decapsulation & CRC Check"] C --> D["BUFFER / QUEUE
Store-and-Forward Memory"] D --> E["ROUTING ENGINE
Header Inspection & Lookup"] E --> F{"ROUTING DECISION
Forwarding Table Lookup"} F -->|Match Found| G["OUTPUT QUEUE
Per-Interface Buffer"] F -->|No Match| H["DROP / ICMP ERROR
Packet Discarded"] G --> I["DATA LINK LAYER
Frame Encapsulation"] I --> J["PHYSICAL LAYER
Signal Transmission"] J --> K["OUTPUT LINK
Outgoing Physical Signal"] style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000 style B fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000 style C fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000 style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000 style F fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px style H fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000 style I fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000 style J fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#000 style K fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Datagram vs. Virtual Circuit — Operational Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph DG["DATAGRAM PACKET SWITCHING CONNECTIONLESS"] DG1["Source breaks message into packets"] --> DG2["Each packet has FULL source destination address"] DG2 --> DG3["Routers examine destination address"] DG3 --> DG4{"Routing decision per packet"} DG4 --> DG5["Packets may take DIFFERENT paths"] DG5 --> DG6["Destination REASSEMBLES in order"] end subgraph VC["VIRTUAL CIRCUIT PACKET SWITCHING CONNECTION ORIENTED"] VC1["VC SETUP call request packet"] --> VC2["Each router assigns VCI and builds VC table"] VC2 --> VC3["Data transfer: each packet carries only VCI"] VC3 --> VC4["All packets follow SAME path"] VC4 --> VC5["VC TEARDOWN clear request"] end style DG1 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000 style DG2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000 style DG3 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000 style DG4 fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#000 style DG5 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000 style DG6 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000 style VC1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style VC2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style VC3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style VC4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 style VC5 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Pipelined Transmission Timing Diagram The diagram below shows the store-and-forward pipeline for **N = 3 packets** over **P = 3 links** (R1, R2, R3). The first packet takes $3 \cdot L/R$ seconds to reach the destination; subsequent packets emerge every $L/R$ seconds. ```mermaid gantt title Pipelined Packet Transmission: 3 Packets over 3 Links dateFormat X axisFormat %S section Link 1 (Source to R1) Packet 1 transmission :a1, 0, 1s Packet 2 transmission :a2, 1, 2s Packet 3 transmission :a3, 2, 3s section Link 2 (R1 to R2) Packet 1 transmission :b1, 1, 2s Packet 2 transmission :b2, 2, 3s Packet 3 transmission :b3, 3, 4s section Link 3 (R2 to Destination) Packet 1 transmission :c1, 2, 3s Packet 2 transmission :c2, 3, 4s Packet 3 transmission :c3, 4, 5s ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Gantt chart:** The first packet arrives at the destination at **t = 3·L/R**, the second at **t = 4·L/R**, and the third at **t = 5·L/R**. Total time = $(N + P - 1) \cdot L/R = (3 + 3 - 1) \cdot 1 = 5$ seconds — matching our formula exactly. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Define packet switching. List its two types.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Packet switching** is a store-and-forward networking technique in which a source message is divided into fixed-size units called **packets**, each containing a **header** (with source/destination address) and a **payload** (user data). These packets are independently routed through intermediate nodes (routers) toward the destination, where they are reassembled. > > **Two types:** > 1. **Datagram Packet Switching** (connectionless — e.g., the Internet using IP) > 2. **Virtual Circuit Packet Switching** (connection-oriented — e.g., X.25, ATM, MPLS) > > *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for listing the two types.)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between datagram and virtual circuit packet switching (any three points).** **Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per valid point):** | # | Datagram | Virtual Circuit | |---|---|---| | 1 | No connection setup; each packet routed independently | Requires VC setup; logical path established first | | 2 | Packets may follow **different paths** | All packets follow the **same path** (VCI) | | 3 | Routers are **stateless** (no per-flow info) | Routers maintain **VC tables** (per-flow state) | | 4 | Packets may arrive **out of order** | Packets arrive **in order** | *(Any three correct points fetch full 3 marks.)* --- ## 📕 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Rule:** Module-end questions carry **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### ✅ QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` **A. (a) Explain the internal operation of a datagram packet-switched network with a neat diagram. List two advantages and two disadvantages.** **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** In a **datagram packet-switched network**, each packet is treated as an **independent entity** routed on a per-packet basis. The internal operation proceeds as follows: **Step 1 — Segmentation:** The source application generates a message which the **network layer** divides into packets of fixed size, each prefixed with a header containing: - Source IP address - Destination IP address - Sequence number - Time-to-Live (TTL) - Protocol type - Header checksum **Step 2 — Transmission onto first link:** The packet is transmitted onto the access link. Each bit takes $L/R$ seconds to be pushed (transmission delay), and propagates at speed $s$ (propagation delay). **Step 3 — Store-and-Forward at each router:** At every intermediate router: 1. The **entire packet is buffered** (store). 2. The header is examined; the destination address is extracted. 3. The **forwarding table** is consulted (longest-prefix match). 4. The packet is **forwarded** to the next link (forward). 5. This is repeated at every hop. **Step 4 — Independent routing:** Because each packet's path is chosen *independently* based on the current network state, two packets from the same message may take **completely different routes**. **Step 5 — Reassembly at destination:** Packets may arrive **out of order**; the destination reorders them using the **sequence number** in the header. **Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 — same flowchart earns full marks) **Advantages (2 marks):** 1. **Robustness / Resilience:** Failure of a single link or router does not stop communication — packets are dynamically rerouted. 2. **Efficient resource utilization:** Links are **statistically multiplexed**; idle bandwidth on one path can be used by other flows. 3. **No call setup delay:** Suitable for short, bursty data transfers (e.g., web browsing, DNS). 4. **Simpler router implementation** (stateless — no per-flow state to maintain). **Disadvantages (2 marks):** 1. **Variable / unpredictable delays** due to queuing at routers — unsuitable for real-time voice/video without QoS. 2. **Packets may arrive out of order**; reassembly and reordering logic is required at the destination. 3. **Header overhead is high** — every packet carries full source + destination address (unlike VCs which use a short VCI). 4. **No built-in congestion control** — must be implemented at transport layer (e.g., TCP). > *(Award 2 marks for the operational explanation, 2 marks for the diagram, 1.5 marks each for advantages and disadvantages. Total = 7 marks.)* --- **A. (b) A 2 Mbit message is to be transmitted over a packet-switched network. The message is broken into 4 equal-sized packets of 500 kbits each. The network path consists of 3 links, each of rate 1 Mbps. Using the store-and-forward pipeline model, calculate the total time required to deliver the entire message. Neglect propagation, queuing, and processing delays.** **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Given Data:** - Message size = 2 Mbits - Number of packets, **N = 4** - Packet size, **L = 500 kbits = 5 × 10⁵ bits** - Number of links (hops), **P = 3** - Link rate, **R = 1 Mbps = 10⁶ bps** **Step 1 — Recall the pipelined store-and-forward formula.** For N packets transmitted over P series links (store-and-forward), the total transmission time is: $$ T = (N + P - 1) \cdot \frac{L}{R} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the values.** $$ T = (4 + 3 - 1) \cdot \frac{5 \times 10^{5}}{10^{6}} $$ **Step 3 — Simplify.** $$ T = 6 \cdot 0.5 \;=\; 3.0 \text{ seconds} $$ **Step 4 — Verification by direct computation (for clarity):** - First packet: traverses 3 links → $3 \cdot L/R = 3 \times 0.5 = 1.5$ s - Each subsequent packet: emerges every $L/R = 0.5$ s - Total = $1.5 + (4 - 1) \times 0.5 = 1.5 + 1.5 = 3.0$ s ✓ **Final Answer:** $T = \mathbf{3.0 \text{ seconds}}$ > *(Award 2 marks for the correct formula statement, 2 marks for substitution, 2 marks for arithmetic, 1 mark for unit + final answer. Total = 7 marks.)* --- ### ✅ QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)* **B. (a) Explain with a diagram how a virtual circuit is established, used, and torn down in a packet-switched network. What are the advantages of virtual circuit switching over datagram switching?** **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** A **Virtual Circuit (VC)** is a logical connection established between source and destination before data transfer. It mimics the reliability of circuit switching while retaining the efficiency of packet switching. **Phase 1 — VC Establishment (Setup Phase):** 1. The source sends a **CALL REQUEST** packet to the destination via a chosen route (e.g., S → R1 → R2 → R3 → D). 2. At each intermediate router: - A **Virtual Circuit Identifier (VCI)** is assigned for the incoming link. - A row is added to the router's **VC table** of the form: `(incoming interface, incoming VCI) → (outgoing interface, outgoing VCI)`. 3. The destination responds with a **CALL ACCEPTED** packet traversing the reverse path, confirming the VC. **Phase 2 — Data Transfer:** 1. The source sends data packets. **Each packet carries only the VCI** (a small integer, typically 12–24 bits) — *not* the full source/destination address. 2. At each router, the VCI is used to look up the outgoing interface in O(1) — much faster than IP's longest-prefix match. 3. The outgoing packet is given a new VCI for the next link. 4. All packets follow the **same physical path** for the duration of the VC. **Phase 3 — VC Termination (Teardown):** 1. Either side sends a **CLEAR REQUEST** packet. 2. Each router deletes its VC table entry. 3. Resources (buffers, bandwidth reservations) are released. **Diagram (text representation):** ``` S -----[VCI=5]----> R1 -----[VCI=11]----> R2 -----[VCI=7]----> D CALL REQ FWD CALL ACC D <----[VCI=6]----- R1 <----[VCI=12]----- R2 <----[VCI=8]----- S CLEAR REQ ``` **Advantages of VC over Datagram (any 4):** 1. **Faster forwarding:** VCI lookup is O(1) vs. O(log n) for IP longest-prefix match. 2. **Guaranteed in-order delivery:** All packets follow the same path. 3. **Smaller headers:** Only VCI carried, not full source/destination. 4. **Resource reservation possible:** Bandwidth and buffer can be reserved at setup → better QoS. 5. **Simpler congestion control** on a per-VC basis. 6. **Predictable performance** within a VC. > *(Award 2 marks for the 3 phases, 2 marks for the diagram, 1 mark per advantage × 2 = 2 marks. Total = 7 marks.)* --- **B. (b) Consider a packet-switched network with a message of size 8 Mbits. The message is divided into packets of 1 Mbit each. The path consists of 2 links, each of rate 2 Mbps. Each packet has a 100-bit header. Calculate:** **(i) The total transmission time for the message (using store-and-forward pipelining).** **(ii) The effective throughput accounting for header overhead.** **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Given Data:** - Message size, **M = 8 Mbits** - Number of packets, **N = M / L = 8 / 1 = 8 packets** - Payload per packet, **L = 1 Mbit = 10⁶ bits** - Header per packet, **H = 100 bits = 10² bits** - Number of links, **P = 2** - Link rate, **R = 2 Mbps = 2 × 10⁶ bps** **Part (i) — Total transmission time (3.5 marks):** $$ T = (N + P - 1) \cdot \frac{L}{R} $$ $$ T = (8 + 2 - 1) \cdot \frac{10^{6}}{2 \times 10^{6}} $$ $$ T = 9 \cdot 0.5 \;=\; 4.5 \text{ seconds} $$ **Part (ii) — Effective throughput (3.5 marks):** Total bits transmitted per packet (payload + header): $$ L_{total} = L + H = 10^{6} + 10^{2} = 1{,}000{,}100 \text{ bits} $$ Effective throughput: $$ R_{eff} = \frac{L}{L + H} \cdot R = \frac{10^{6}}{1{,}000{,}100} \cdot (2 \times 10^{6}) $$ $$ R_{eff} = 0.9999 \cdot 2{,}000{,}000 \;\approx\; 1{,}999{,}800 \text{ bps} \;\approx\; 1.9998 \text{ Mbps} $$ **Final Answers:** - (i) **T = 4.5 seconds** - (ii) **R_eff ≈ 1.9998 Mbps** (≈ 99.99% of the raw link rate) > *(Award 3.5 marks for (i) with full substitution, 3.5 marks for (ii) with correct formula application. Total = 7 marks.)* --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common ways students LOSE marks on Packet Switching questions:** > 1. **Wrong pipeline formula:** Writing $T = N \cdot L/R$ instead of $T = (N + P - 1) \cdot L/R$ — costs **3 marks instantly**. Always check if the path has multiple links. > 2. **Confusing transmission vs. propagation delay:** $L/R$ is *transmission* (function of packet size & rate); $d/s$ is *propagation* (function of distance & medium speed). They are **not interchangeable**. > 3. **Forgetting to convert units:** KTU problems use **Mbps, kbits, Mbits** — convert to a single base unit (**bits and bps**) before plugging into formulas. > 4. **Omitting headers in effective throughput:** If the problem mentions "100-bit header," students often ignore it. This costs **2–3 marks**. > 5. **Mislabelling datagram vs. virtual circuit features:** VC switching **does** require setup and **does** maintain state at routers — saying otherwise loses marks. > 6. **Missing the diagram:** Even a hand-drawn sketch in a 14-mark question earns at least **2 marks**. **Always draw a diagram**, even if the question doesn't explicitly ask. > 7. **No sequence numbers mentioned in datagram explanation:** Datagram networks **require** sequence numbers in headers for reassembly. Forgetting this is a frequent mark loss. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### Rapid-Revision Checklist - ✅ **Packet switching = store-and-forward** technique; message split into bounded packets. - ✅ Two paradigms: **Datagram (connectionless, IP)** and **Virtual Circuit (connection-oriented, X.25/ATM/MPLS)**. - ✅ **Four delays** at every node: transmission ($L/R$), propagation ($d/s$), queuing ($d_q$), processing ($d_{proc}$). - ✅ **End-to-end nodal delay** = sum of all four, multiplied by number of hops. - ✅ **Pipelined transmission formula** (the most important): $$T = (N + P - 1) \cdot \frac{L}{R}$$ - ✅ **Effective throughput** with header overhead: $$R_{eff} = \frac{L}{L + H} \cdot R$$ - ✅ **MTU** on Ethernet = **1500 bytes**; IPv4 header = **20 bytes**; IPv6 header = **40 bytes**. - ✅ **Datagram pros:** resilience, no setup, statistical multiplexing. **Cons:** variable delay, out-of-order, large headers. - ✅ **VC pros:** fast O(1) lookup, ordered delivery, small header, QoS-friendly. **Cons:** setup delay, state at routers, single point of failure. - ✅ **Bandwidth-Delay Product** $BDP = R \cdot d_p$ gives the number of bits "in flight." - ✅ **MPLS** is the modern descendant of virtual-circuit switching used in ISP backbones. - ✅ Always state the **store-and-forward** assumption explicitly when using the pipeline formula. - ✅ Always include a **diagram** in 14-mark answers — even a rough sketch earns marks. - ✅ For numericals, **convert all units to bits and bps** before substituting. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Exam Mantra for Packet Switching:** *Draw the diagram, state the four delays, write the pipeline formula, mention MTU = 1500 bytes, distinguish datagram from VC in a small table, and you will score 13+/14 on any related question.*

topics.title: Message Switching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECST612 - DATA COMMUNICATION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Message Switching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Message Switching** is a *store-and-forward* network switching technique in which the **entire message** is treated as a single, indivisible unit of data. The complete message is transmitted in full from the source node to the next intermediate (routing) node, where it is **temporarily stored** (buffered) in its entirety. Only after the full message has been received and stored does the intermediate node begin forwarding it along the next available link toward the destination.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Verbatim:** "In Message Switching, the source node sends the complete message to the next switching office, which stores it in its entirety before forwarding it to the next switch. No dedicated physical path is established between sender and receiver."

This is fundamentally different from **Circuit Switching** (which reserves an end-to-end dedicated path before transmission) and **Packet Switching** (which fragments the message into smaller *packets*).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Post Office Parcel System" Intuition

Imagine you want to send a **large parcel** from Kochi to Delhi through India Post:

1. You hand over the **entire parcel** to your local post office (Source node).
2. The local post office holds the **complete parcel** in its warehouse (Store operation).
3. When a truck/flight is available, it ships the **whole parcel** to the next regional hub (Forward operation).
4. The next regional hub again stores the **entire parcel**, then forwards it.
5. This **hop-by-hop** shipping continues until the parcel reaches the Delhi post office (Destination).

| Real-World Analogy | Message Switching Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Complete parcel | Whole message (fixed size `L` bits) |
| Local post office | Source node / Router |
| Regional hub | Intermediate switching node |
| Warehouse storage | **Buffer / Memory storage** |
| Truck availability | Link bandwidth `R` bps |
| Hop-by-hop transit | Hop-by-hop forwarding |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Like the parcel, the message is **never split or fragmented**. It travels as one atomic block — making it simple but slow for large messages.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Link Bandwidth (R):** Measured in **bits per second (bps)**, typical values: **56 kbps (dial-up) to 100 Gbps (modern fiber)**.
- **Message Length (L):** Measured in **bits** or **bytes**.
- **Propagation Delay (Tp):** Speed of signal in medium ≈ **2 × 10⁸ m/s** (copper) to **3 × 10⁸ m/s** (vacuum/fiber).
- **Switching Delay (Ts):** Time to store + process header ≈ **microseconds to milliseconds**.

## 1.4 Why Message Switching Exists — Historical Context

Before the dominance of the Internet (which uses **packet switching**), networks like **telegraph networks, store-and-forward message systems (e.g., AUTODIN, ARPANET's original 1969 IMPs)**, and early military communication systems relied on **message switching** because:
- It efficiently utilized **bursty traffic** without wasting bandwidth on idle dedicated circuits.
- It provided **natural buffering** during congestion.
- It allowed **different transmission rates** between heterogeneous links.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — The Four Logical Phases

Message switching follows a strict **sequential, hop-by-hop procedure**. Let us break it down for a message traversing `N` nodes (i.e., `N-1` links) from source `S` to destination `D`.

### Phase 1: Message Creation & Encapsulation
- The source node constructs the entire message of length `L` bits.
- A **header** is appended containing destination address, source address, and routing/control information.
- The total unit transmitted is `L` bits (sometimes denoted `L_total`).

### Phase 2: First-Hop Transmission (Transmission Delay = L/R)
- Source transmits the full `L` bits onto the first link at rate `R` bps.
- Time taken = **Transmission Delay (Tt) = L / R** seconds.
- During this phase, the source cannot send any other message on the same link (blocking).

### Phase 3: Store-and-Forward at Intermediate Node (Switching Delay Ts)
- The intermediate node **must receive ALL L bits** before forwarding.
- Reception time = `L / R` (same as transmission time on the incoming link).
- The node stores the message in its **buffer (memory)**.
- Processing/routing table lookup time = `Ts` (switching delay).
- Total time at each intermediate node ≈ **`L / R + Ts`**.

### Phase 4: Forward to Next Link
- The node now transmits the complete `L` bits onto the next outgoing link.
- This cycle repeats at every intermediate node.

## 2.2 The Core "Why" Behind Message Switching

> [!NOTE]
> **WHY message switching?** It was designed to **eliminate the setup time and idle bandwidth waste** of circuit switching. Since the physical path is **NOT pre-established**, the network can dynamically route each message through whatever links are available — maximizing **statistical multiplexing** efficiency.

> **HOW does it achieve this?** By accepting that messages will experience **variable delays** (depending on queue lengths at intermediate nodes) in exchange for higher overall link utilization.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL:** All formulas below are **must-memorize** for KTU University Exam. Practice numerical problems using these.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Message Transmission Delay per hop | $T_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$ | seconds | Time to push `L` bits onto a link of rate `R` |
| 2 | Propagation Delay per link | $T_p = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | seconds | Time for signal to travel distance `d` at speed `v` (≈ 2×10⁸ m/s) |
| 3 | Switching / Processing Delay per node | $T_s$ | seconds | Header inspection + routing decision (≈ 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻³ s) |
| 4 | Queuing Delay per node | $T_q$ | seconds | Waiting time in buffer (variable, depends on traffic) |
| 5 | **Total Delay for `N` nodes (without Tq)** | $T_{total} = (N-1) \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{R} + T_s\right) + (N-1) \cdot T_p$ | seconds | End-to-end delay through `N-1` links |
| 6 | **Simplified Total Delay (ignoring Ts, Tp)** | $T_{total} \approx (N-1) \cdot \dfrac{L}{R}$ | seconds | Dominant term in long-message, low-speed networks |
| 7 | Number of Hops | $H = N - 1$ | dimensionless | Links traversed from S to D |
| 8 | Bandwidth-Delay Product per link | $BDP = R \cdot T_p$ | bits | Max bits "in flight" on a link |
| 9 | Link Utilization (Message Switching) | $\eta = \dfrac{T_t}{T_t + T_q}$ | ratio (0 to 1) | Fraction of time link is busy transmitting message |

> **Important notation rule:** In the table above, all absolute-value / division operators use LaTeX `\dfrac` to ensure compatibility. When writing these formulas in your KTU answer sheet, always use the same notation.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Defense & Tactical Networks:** Military message-switched systems (e.g., old **AFDAT** systems) provide reliable store-and-forward communication when real-time links are unavailable.
- **Email Systems (SMTP):** Internally, many older mail servers used message-switched paradigms to relay complete emails between MTAs (Mail Transfer Agents).
- **Store-and-Forward Satellite Networks:** Satellites in LEO store complete data bursts before forwarding — a modern form of message switching.
- **SMS Routing Centers:** Telecom SMSCs historically used message switching principles.
- **Comparison Baseline:** In KTU exams, message switching is taught primarily as a **conceptual predecessor** to packet switching, allowing comparison of delay, efficiency, and complexity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total End-to-End Delay for Message Switching

**Problem Setup:** A message of length `L` bits must travel from source `S` to destination `D` through `N` nodes (hence `N - 1` links). Each link has bandwidth `R` bps, propagation speed `v` m/s, and link length `d` meters. Each intermediate node introduces a switching delay `Ts` and queuing delay `Tq`.

**Objective:** Derive the total time `T_total` elapsed from "first bit sent by S" to "last bit received by D".

### Step 1: Time for Source to Transmit the First Bit (t = 0)
The first bit leaves the source at time `t = 0`. It must travel through `N - 1` links, so the **first bit arrives at D** at time:
$$t_1 = (N - 1) \cdot T_p = (N - 1) \cdot \dfrac{d}{v}$$

### Step 2: Time for Source to Transmit the Last Bit
The last bit must first be pushed onto the first link. This takes:
$$T_t = \dfrac{L}{R}$$
So the last bit leaves the source at time `t = L / R`.

### Step 3: Last Bit Arrives at Destination
The last bit, after leaving the source, must still propagate through the remaining `N - 1` links (it traverses them in sequence at near-light speed). It reaches `D` at:
$$t_2 = \dfrac{L}{R} + (N - 1) \cdot T_p$$

### Step 4: Account for Store-and-Forward Delay at Each Intermediate Node
At **every intermediate node**, the full message must be received before it can be forwarded. This effectively adds a transmission-time worth of delay at each intermediate node.

There are `N - 2` intermediate nodes (excluding source and destination). At each, the message waits an additional `L / R` to be re-transmitted. Switching delay `Ts` is added at each of the `N - 2` intermediate nodes.

**Conversion logic:** Each intermediate node "consumes" one full transmission-time `L/R` because it cannot begin forwarding until the entire message is received.

### Step 5: Combine All Delays
$$T_{total} = \underbrace{\dfrac{L}{R}}_{\text{Source transmit}} + \underbrace{(N - 2) \cdot \dfrac{L}{R}}_{\text{Store-and-forward at intermediate nodes}} + \underbrace{(N - 1) \cdot T_p}_{\text{Propagation on all links}} + \underbrace{(N - 2) \cdot T_s}_{\text{Switching at intermediate nodes}} + \underbrace{T_q}_{\text{Queuing}}$$

### Step 6: Final Simplified Form (often used in KTU problems)
If we combine the first two terms and assume `N - 1 ≈ N - 2 ≈ N` for large networks:
$$T_{total} \approx (N - 1) \cdot \dfrac{L}{R} + (N - 1) \cdot T_p + (N - 1) \cdot T_s + T_q$$

Or, in the most exam-friendly form:
$$\boxed{T_{total} = (N - 1) \left( \dfrac{L}{R} + T_s + T_p \right) + T_q}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Comparison with Circuit Switching Setup Time

**Context:** In circuit switching, a dedicated path of `N` switches is established, taking `S` seconds of setup time. After setup, the message transmits in `L / R` time, plus `T_p` propagation.

$$T_{CS} = S + \dfrac{L}{R} + T_p$$

**Message Switching Total:**
$$T_{MS} = (N - 1) \left( \dfrac{L}{R} + T_s + T_p \right) + T_q$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight for KTU:** Message switching is **slower than circuit switching for long, steady streams** (because of `N-1` store-and-forward cycles), but **faster for short, bursty messages** (because no `S` setup time).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Question:** A message of length **L = 10,000 bits** is sent through a message-switched network with **N = 4 nodes** (3 links). Each link has **R = 1000 bps** and **propagation delay Tp = 0.05 s**. Switching delay **Ts = 0.001 s** at each intermediate node. Ignore queuing.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Transmission delay per link:
$$T_t = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{1000} = 10 \text{ seconds}$$

Step 2 — Number of store-and-forward cycles = N - 1 = 3:
$$\text{Store-and-forward total} = 3 \times 10 = 30 \text{ s}$$

Step 3 — Total propagation delay:
$$T_p^{total} = (N - 1) \times T_p = 3 \times 0.05 = 0.15 \text{ s}$$

Step 4 — Switching delay (at N - 2 = 2 intermediate nodes):
$$T_s^{total} = 2 \times 0.001 = 0.002 \text{ s}$$

Step 5 — Total delay:
$$T_{total} = 30 + 0.15 + 0.002 = 30.152 \text{ s}$$

**Conversion logic:** The dominant term is `(N-1) × L/R` = 30 s, which is `>99%` of total delay. This is why message switching is inefficient for long messages.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Circuit Switching | Message Switching | Packet Switching |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dedicated Path | Yes (pre-established) | No (dynamic) | No (dynamic) |
| Setup Time `S` | Required | None | None |
| Message Unit | Continuous stream | Whole message | Small packets (≤ 1500 B) |
| Store-and-Forward | No | **Yes (full message)** | **Yes (per packet)** |
| Delay Variability | Constant | **High (variable Tq)** | Moderate |
| Best for | Voice (constant rate) | **Short bursty messages** | General data + interactive |
| Resource Utilization | Low (idle if no data) | High | High |
| Complexity at Node | Low (just switch) | **High (large buffers)** | Moderate |
| Header Overhead | None | Per message | **Per packet (large)** |
| KTU Exam Weight | High | **High (this topic)** | Very High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Message Switching Hop-by-Hop Flow

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** Each numbered arrow represents a single transmission of the **entire message**. Notice how the message travels **one hop at a time**, being stored in between.

```mermaid
graph LR
    S[Node S: Source] -->|1: Send Full Message L bits| N1[Node 1: Intermediate]
    N1 -->|2: Store L bits in Buffer| B1[Buffer at Node 1]
    B1 -->|3: Forward Full Message L bits| N2[Node 2: Intermediate]
    N2 -->|4: Store L bits in Buffer| B2[Buffer at Node 2]
    B2 -->|5: Forward Full Message L bits| D[Node D: Destination]
    style S fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc
    style D fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#009900
    style N1 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style N2 fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style B1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
    style B2 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture

This block diagram explains the **internal processing** at an intermediate node in message switching.

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN[Incoming Link: Receives Full Message] --> RX[Receiver Module: L/R seconds]
    RX --> STORE[Storage Buffer: Holds Entire Message L bits]
    STORE --> PROC[Header Processor: Reads Destination Address]
    PROC --> ROUTE[Routing Table Lookup: Ts seconds]
    ROUTE --> QUEUE[Output Queue: Waits for Link Availability]
    QUEUE --> TX[Transmitter Module: L/R seconds]
    TX --> OUT[Outgoing Link: Forwards Full Message]
    style IN fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#003366
    style STORE fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#990000
    style PROC fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600
    style QUEUE fill:#e6ccff,stroke:#330099
    style OUT fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Timeline of Message Propagation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU:** Timing diagrams are a **favorite KTU 14-mark question**. The teacher expects you to draw a **hop-time vs. time chart** showing L/R intervals. Use the diagram below as a model.

```mermaid
gantt
    title Message Switching Timeline (4 nodes, 3 links)
    dateFormat ss.SSS
    axisFormat %S.%L
    
    section Source Node S
    Transmit message on Link 1 (L/R) :a1, 00.000, 10s
    section Node 1 Intermediate
    Receive + Store + Forward :a2, 10.000, 10s
    section Node 2 Intermediate
    Receive + Store + Forward :a3, 20.000, 10s
    section Destination D
    Receive full message :a4, 30.000, 0.5s
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Comparison Flowchart (When to Use What)

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q{Network Traffic Type?}
    Q -->|Continuous Voice| CS[Circuit Switching]
    Q -->|Short Bursty Messages| MS[Message Switching]
    Q -->|Mixed Interactive Data| PS[Packet Switching]
    MS --> ADV1[No Setup Time]
    MS --> ADV2[Efficient Bandwidth Use]
    MS --> DIS1[High Variable Delay]
    MS --> DIS2[Large Buffer Memory Needed]
    style CS fill:#cce5ff
    style MS fill:#ffe0b3
    style PS fill:#ccffcc
    style Q fill:#fff2cc
```

## 4.5 Schematic Description — Physical Interpretation

If we were to draw the **physical flow of bits** on a time axis (commonly asked in KTU 14-mark questions), the picture is:

- **Horizontal axis:** Time (in seconds).
- **Vertical axis:** Distance along the network (Source at top, Destination at bottom).
- **Diagonal lines:** Bits in flight on each link.
- **Horizontal segments:** Bits stored at intermediate nodes (this is **unique to message switching** — the "store" half of store-and-forward).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often draw the diagram with **continuous diagonal lines through the nodes** (like circuit switching). Remember: in **message switching**, the line must go **horizontal (flat)** at each intermediate node during the storage period, then resume at a slope.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define Message Switching. List any two advantages and two disadvantages.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Message switching is a store-and-forward technique in which the **entire message** is transmitted as a single unit from node to node. At each intermediate node, the complete message is **received, stored in a buffer, and then forwarded** to the next node. No dedicated physical path is established in advance.

**Advantages (1 mark — any two):**
1. **No setup time** is required, unlike circuit switching.
2. **Efficient bandwidth utilization** — links are used only when data is actually transmitted.
3. Traffic can be **rerouted dynamically** if a link fails.

**Disadvantages (1 mark — any two):**
1. **High variable delay** due to queuing and store-and-forward at each hop.
2. **Large buffer memory** is required at each node to hold the entire message.
3. **Not suitable for real-time applications** (voice, video) due to unpredictable delays.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Compare Circuit Switching and Message Switching based on (i) Path establishment, (ii) Delay, (iii) Resource utilization.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Circuit Switching | Message Switching |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Path establishment (1 mark) | A **dedicated physical path** is established between source and destination **before** data transfer. | **No dedicated path** is established; message is routed **hop-by-hop** dynamically. |
| (ii) Delay (1 mark) | **Fixed delay** after setup; suitable for real-time voice. | **Variable delay** due to store-and-forward + queuing at each hop. |
| (iii) Resource utilization (1 mark) | **Poor** — resources remain reserved even during silent periods. | **Better** — links are used only when data is being transmitted. |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Rule:** Every Part B question has an **internal choice**. You must answer **either Option A or Option B**. Both questions below are equally weighted and **independently valid**.

---

### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Numerical + Conceptual
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]**

**A (a)** A message of length **L = 8000 bits** is transmitted from source `S` to destination `D` through a message-switched network with **N = 5 nodes** (i.e., 4 links). Each link has a bandwidth of **R = 2000 bps** and a propagation delay of **Tp = 0.02 s**. Each intermediate switching node has a switching delay of **Ts = 0.005 s**. Assume queuing delay is negligible.

**Calculate:**
(i) The transmission delay per link.
(ii) The total store-and-forward delay.
(iii) The total end-to-end delay.

**(7 marks)**

**A (b)** Explain the **store-and-forward** mechanism in message switching with a neat block diagram. Discuss why this mechanism causes **higher delay** in message switching compared to circuit switching. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

**A(a) — Step-by-Step Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
$$L = 8000 \text{ bits}, \quad N = 5 \text{ nodes}, \quad H = N-1 = 4 \text{ links}, \quad R = 2000 \text{ bps}, \quad T_p = 0.02 \text{ s}, \quad T_s = 0.005 \text{ s}$$

**(i) Transmission delay per link (1 mark):**
$$T_t = \dfrac{L}{R} = \dfrac{8000}{2000} = 4 \text{ seconds}$$

**(ii) Total store-and-forward delay (3 marks):**
The message must be stored and forwarded at every intermediate node. There are `N - 1 = 4` store-and-forward cycles (one per link). Each cycle involves transmission of `L` bits at rate `R`.

$$\text{Store-and-forward delay} = (N-1) \times \dfrac{L}{R} = 4 \times 4 = 16 \text{ seconds}$$

**Conversion logic:** At node 1, the source transmits the first 8000 bits in 4 s. Node 1 then forwards to node 2 in another 4 s, and so on. Hence 4 cycles × 4 s = 16 s.

**(iii) Total end-to-end delay (3 marks):**
Using the standard KTU formula:
$$T_{total} = (N-1) \cdot \dfrac{L}{R} + (N-1) \cdot T_p + (N-2) \cdot T_s$$

$$T_{total} = 4 \times 4 + 4 \times 0.02 + 3 \times 0.005$$

$$T_{total} = 16 + 0.08 + 0.015 = \boxed{16.095 \text{ seconds}}$$

**Observation (for full marks):** The store-and-forward transmission delay (16 s) **dominates** the total delay, contributing `>99%` of the end-to-end time. This illustrates why message switching is inefficient for long messages.

---

**A(b) — Block Diagram + Explanation (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the functional architecture of an intermediate node with the following blocks: **Input Link → Receiver → Buffer Storage → Header Processor → Routing Decision → Output Queue → Transmitter → Output Link**. (Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes.)

**Explanation (4 marks):**

1. **Store-and-Forward Mechanism (2 marks):** In message switching, an intermediate node must receive the **complete message** in its buffer before it can begin forwarding. The node cannot perform "cut-through" forwarding (as in some packet switches). The full message is stored in memory, the header is inspected, a routing decision is made, and only then is the message placed on the outgoing link.

2. **Why Delay is Higher than Circuit Switching (2 marks):**
   - In **circuit switching**, bits flow **continuously** through the established path with no intermediate storage.
   - In **message switching**, the message is **paused at every intermediate node** for a duration of `L / R` (plus processing time).
   - For `N` nodes, this adds `(N-1) × L/R` extra delay compared to a single continuous transmission.
   - Additionally, **queuing delay** at busy nodes introduces **variable, unpredictable latency** — making message switching unsuitable for real-time voice/video.

---

### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Conceptual + Comparison
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]**

**B (a)** With a neat timing diagram, explain how a message of length `L` bits is transmitted through **3 intermediate nodes** (i.e., 4 links) in a message-switched network. Label the **transmission delay, propagation delay**, and **total delay** clearly. **(7 marks)**

**B (b)** Compare **Message Switching** and **Packet Switching** in terms of (i) Message unit, (ii) Delay, (iii) Header overhead, (iv) Suitability for real-time applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

**B(a) — Timing Diagram + Explanation (7 marks):**

**Timing Diagram (4 marks):** Draw a graph with:
- **X-axis:** Time (in units of `L/R`).
- **Y-axis:** Distance from source to destination (mark nodes S, N1, N2, N3, D).
- **Diagonal segments** on each link with slope = `1 / propagation_speed`.
- **Flat (horizontal) segments** at each intermediate node representing the **store-and-forward** period (duration = `L/R`).
- **Total delay** = from `t = 0` (first bit sent) to `t = (N-1) × L/R + (N-1) × Tp` (last bit received).

**Explanation (3 marks):**
- At `t = 0`, the source begins transmitting the first bit. It reaches node N1 after `Tp` seconds (propagation).
- The source finishes transmitting the last bit at `t = L/R`.
- Node N1 has now received the entire message. It stores it, processes the header, and begins re-transmitting at `t = L/R`. The first bit of N1's transmission reaches N2 at `t = L/R + Tp`.
- This pattern repeats. The **last bit** reaches the destination at `t = (N-1) × L/R + (N-1) × Tp`.

---

**B(b) — Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Parameter | Message Switching | Packet Switching |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Message unit (2 marks) | The **entire message** is treated as one unit. | Message is **broken into smaller packets** (typically ≤ 1500 bytes). |
| (ii) Delay (2 marks) | **High end-to-end delay** of `(N-1) × L/R` because full message must arrive at each node before forwarding. | **Lower delay** — only `packet_size / R` per hop; also packets can take **parallel paths**. |
| (iii) Header overhead (1 mark) | **One header per message** — low overhead. | **One header per packet** — higher total overhead, especially for short packets. |
| (iv) Suitability for real-time (2 marks) | **Not suitable** — variable queuing delay makes it unreliable for voice/video. | **More suitable** — small packets have predictable, low delay. |

**Conclusion (for full marks):** Packet switching is a **logical evolution** of message switching that resolves the high-delay problem by **breaking the message into smaller, independently-routable units**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing packet switching with message switching** (most common error: writing "packet" instead of "message" in the definition — costs 1 full mark).
> 2. **Forgetting propagation delay** in numerical problems — examiners often give 1–2 marks specifically for `Tp` inclusion.
> 3. **Using `N` instead of `N-1` links** — always verify whether the question gives "nodes" or "links".
> 4. **Skipping the unit** in the final answer (e.g., writing `16` instead of `16 seconds`) — 0.5 mark penalty in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Forgetting queuing delay** when explicitly asked — read the problem statement carefully.
> 6. **Drawing the timing diagram with continuous slopes** through nodes — must show **flat segments** at intermediate nodes.
> 7. **Not defining store-and-forward** in part (b) of conceptual questions — KTU expects this term explicitly.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision guide — read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

- ✅ Message switching uses **store-and-forward** at every intermediate node.
- ✅ The **entire message** is treated as **one unit** — never fragmented.
- ✅ **No dedicated path** is established; routing is **dynamic** and **hop-by-hop**.
- ✅ **No setup time** is required (unlike circuit switching).
- ✅ **Total delay formula:** $T_{total} = (N - 1) \cdot \dfrac{L}{R} + (N - 1) \cdot T_p + (N - 2) \cdot T_s + T_q$
- ✅ **Transmission delay per hop:** $T_t = L / R$ (this is the dominant term).
- ✅ **Propagation delay per link:** $T_p = d / v$ where $v \approx 2 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- ✅ **Switching delay $T_s$** applies at **N - 2** intermediate nodes (excluding source and destination).
- ✅ **Number of links = N - 1** when N nodes are given.
- ✅ **Advantages:** no setup, efficient bandwidth, dynamic routing, fault tolerance.
- ✅ **Disadvantages:** high delay, large buffers needed, unsuitable for real-time traffic, variable queuing delay.
- ✅ **Best use case:** short, bursty, non-real-time messages (email, telemetry, store-and-forward satellite).
- ✅ **Predecessor to:** Packet switching (which solves the high-delay problem by fragmentation).
- ✅ **Key comparison points** for KTU: setup time, delay, path type, buffer size, real-time suitability.
- ✅ **In exam:** always mention **store-and-forward** by name — it is a KTU buzzword.
- ✅ **Timing diagrams must show flat (horizontal) segments** at intermediate nodes — never continuous slopes.
- ✅ **Sanity check numerical answers:** the answer should be `> (N-1) × L/R` (the store-and-forward floor) and `< few seconds` for reasonable message sizes.
